The new facility will eventually employ at least 1,500 people.
Construction on the facility is expected to begin as early as January 2023.
Production at the new facility is expected to begin in 2024.
The plant is located at the Belfast Commerce Park in Richmond Hill.

Governor Kemp noted that the groundbreaking of Hyundai’s new electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facility in northern Bryan County would unleash transformational job creation and investment in the region.
This announcement marks the second supplier to locate in the area in just two weeks.
Richmond Hill Mayor Russ Carpenter called the project by far the single largest investment into the economy of his city.
Mayor Carpenter stated that Richmond Hill residents know how special their small town is and that the world is catching on, expressing excitement to work with corporate partners from South Korea.
The EV Power Electric system plant will expand Hyundai Mobis’ current footprint in Georgia.
Hyundai Mobis has operated a manufacturing facility with almost 1,200 employees in West Point since 2009.
The West Point facility is the largest Tier 1 supplier to Kia Georgia.
The West Point plant supplies automotive manufacturers with complete cockpit modules, chassis modules, and bumper assemblies.
H.S. Oh, Vice President of Hyundai Mobis Electric Powertrain Business Unit, stated that the investment reflects an acceleration in the development of the EV supply chain in Georgia’s auto industry.
Oh noted that Hyundai Mobis will be a major production player in the EV market, which will trigger more growth within the sector.
Oh added that Mobis is looking forward to providing high-quality work opportunities to the growing local workforce.
Once it reaches full production, the 1,200,000-square-foot facility will be able to supply over 900,000 EV Power Electric systems and 450,000 Integrated Charging Control Units annually.
The plant will supply the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) factory in Bryan County, the Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama (HMMA) factory in Montgomery, and the Kia Georgia plant.

Development Authority of Bryan County Chairman Jon Seagraves welcomed Mobis to the community, citing close proximity of Belfast Commerce Park to available housing in nearby Heartwood, a top-performing public school system, interstate access, and rail.
Seagraves noted that the site was purchased about two years ago and had the potential to attract a special project.
Seagraves highlighted nearly $1 billion of investment and the creation of over 1,500 good-paying jobs.
Seagraves thanked Bryan County, the City of Richmond Hill, and Raydient Places for their support and partnership on the project.
Bryan County Commission Chairman Carter Infinger hailed the announcement and stated the county is working with Richmond Hill on infrastructure.
Infinger said Bryan County is working in unison with Richmond Hill to improve the Belfast Keller corridor in the coming years to ease traffic backups and improve safety.
The first improvement expected to begin next year aims at improving and widening intersections with Cranston Bluff Road and the new high school entrances.
Later projects will improve Belfast Keller Road from Belfast River Road to Belfast Commerce Center Drive.
Alyce Thornhill, Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Development Authority of Bryan County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that suppliers like Hyundai Mobis are transitioning to the jobs of the future and becoming part of Georgia’s growing EV ecosystem.
In May, GDEcD predicted that non-affiliated HMGMA suppliers in connection with the project would invest an additional $1 billion into Georgia.
Wilson noted that this prediction was blown past in about two weeks.
Wilson expressed excitement to prepare Georgia’s workforce of tomorrow for the jobs being created now with support from partners like Georgia Quick Start and sister agencies.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry, building on assets that make the automotive industry successful.
Since 2020, more than 30 EV-related projects have contributed over $13.3 billion in investment and announced more than 18,100 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Offsite suppliers connected to the HMGMA project are predicted to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around the state.
source_title: EV supplier to make Richmond Hill home and employ 1,500 - Bryan County News

facts: South Korean battery manufacturers LG and SK Innovation reached a settlement allowing SK to complete its $2.6 billion factory in Georgia.

The settlement removes the need for President Joe Biden and U.S. Trade Representative Katherine Tai to decide whether to overturn an International Trade Commission (ITC) ruling that had prompted SK to threaten abandoning the plant and its 2,600 jobs.
Sunday served as the deadline for the Biden administration to issue a decision on the ITC ruling.
The $1.8 billion agreement enables SK Innovation to complete its Commerce, Georgia facility, which will supply batteries for a suite of Volkswagen vehicles and the electric Ford F-150.
President Joe Biden publicly praised the settlement in a White House statement on Sunday.

Biden stated that a strong, diversified, and resilient U.S.-based EV battery supply chain is needed to meet growing global demand, create good-paying domestic jobs, and lay the groundwork for future jobs.
Biden added that the settlement is a positive step toward building the EV battery supply chain, bringing relief to Georgia workers and new opportunities nationwide.
In February, the ITC ruled that SK Innovation stole trade secrets from LG and ordered a blockade on shipments of materials needed to make batteries at the Georgia plant.
Despite an ITC grace period allowing automakers time to switch battery suppliers, SK Innovation threatened to abandon the project if the ruling was not overturned.
The ITC ruling posed a potential setback to President Biden's ambitious electric vehicle manufacturing goals.

The ITC granted Volkswagen two years and Ford four years to find alternative battery suppliers, but both automakers indicated it would take at least that long, risking a shift to imported batteries or delays in new model releases.
Federal law grants the president and U.S. Trade Representative 60 days to overrule an ITC decision, prompting an intense lobbying campaign directed at the White House.
SK Innovation hired former EPA Administrator Carol Browner and former Deputy Attorney General Sally Yates to lobby President Biden and USTR Katherine Tai to overturn the ruling.
SK Innovation warned that abandoning the Georgia plant would allow Chinese battery suppliers to capture the resulting market demand.
LG maintained that SK Innovation was bluffing about abandoning the project and would likely complete the factory regardless.
LG argued that even if SK abandoned the facility, another supplier would quickly acquire it to meet automaker demand.
Throughout the dispute, LG stated it was open to a settlement allowing SK to build its factory in exchange for compensation for stolen intellectual property.
On Sunday, the companies announced that SK Innovation would pay LG $1.8 billion plus a running royalty on batteries produced at the Georgia facility.
The companies did not disclose the size of the running royalty payments or their duration.

In exchange, LG agreed to resolve the ITC dispute, a parallel D.C. Circuit Court case, and all related legal issues in Korea, while promising not to sue SK Innovation over the intellectual property disputes for 10 years.
The settlement provides relief to the Biden White House by avoiding a difficult decision between protecting swing-state jobs and upholding U.S. trade laws.
Had President Biden upheld the ITC ruling, Republicans were prepared to criticize the administration over potential job losses.
Conversely, had Biden overturned the ruling, progressives would have argued for waiving intellectual property protections for COVID-19 vaccines, a stance the White House has resisted.
USTR Katherine Tai pushed both companies to reach a settlement through more than a dozen phone calls with their CEOs and executives in recent weeks.
Tai also consulted with senior Biden administration officials and Georgia lawmakers, prompting Biden to thank her for her "tireless work to resolve this dispute."
Katherine Tai congratulated both companies on resolving their differences, stating it builds confidence in their reliability and responsibility as U.S. auto industry suppliers.
Georgia's two newly elected Democratic senators welcomed the deal after publicly urging the companies to negotiate rather than pressuring the Biden administration to overturn the ruling.
Senators Raphael Warnock and Jon Ossoff claimed credit for the settlement, noting they had repeatedly contacted both the battery company leaders and the Biden administration.
Senator Raphael Warnock stated that negotiating a good-faith settlement was the best way to protect workers in Commerce, Georgia, and fulfill job promises made to Georgians.
source_title: Deal reached to allow Georgia battery plant completion - POLITICO

facts: Anovion Technologies, a startup, said Monday it plans to build an $800 million factory in Bainbridge, Georgia.
The facility will be located in the southwest corner of the state.
It will be the largest synthetic graphite production facility in North America.
The facility is vital to the state’s burgeoning electric vehicle sector.
The plant will supply synthetic graphite for electric battery production.
The project will occupy a 1.5 million-square-foot site.
The plant will employ more than 400 workers.
The plant further strengthens Georgia’s standing within the emerging U.S. lithium-ion battery supply chain, from parts suppliers for batteries to electric vehicles.
Gov. Brian Kemp said Anovion joins the wave of momentum Georgia has within the electric battery ecosystem.
Gov. Kemp added that the state “is fast becoming the e-mobility capital of the nation.”
The details were provided in a news release.
source_title: $800M electric battery materials facility announced for South Georgia

facts: Hyundai Motor Group plans to support sustainable manufacturing at its $7.6 billion Georgia electric-vehicle plant and related manufacturing facilities by buying enough solar power annually to electrify 31,000 typical American homes for a year.
The South Korean automaker announced a deal with Spanish energy developer Matrix Renewables.
Hyundai committed to purchasing 378 gigawatt hours of electricity annually through the agreement.

Maggie Shober, research director at the Southern Alliance for Clean Energy, explained that Texas operates on a self-contained energy grid while Georgia is part of the Eastern Interconnection, so the power will not physically flow from the Stillhouse Solar Project in Bell County, Texas, to Hyundai's EV factory in Bryan County, Georgia.
Hyundai will accumulate renewable energy credits for its share of the electricity over a 15-year agreement via a virtual power purchase agreement.
Under the arrangement, Hyundai will sell the generated electricity to a utility or other provider on the Texas grid.
Company spokeswoman Dain Kang confirmed the details of the virtual power purchase agreement.

Shober noted that buying power from another state illustrates a hierarchy in corporate energy goals, adding that the best scenario for offsetting load is on-site or within the same service territory and state.
Shober stated that Hyundai must ensure its outside energy purchases do not make Georgia Power's generation mix dirtier.
Shober observed that Hyundai did not consider potential increases in emissions from Georgia Power due to its load when announcing that the project would decrease emissions.

Georgia Power is responding to projected increased electricity demand from a flood of new manufacturing facilities, including Hyundai, and energy-intensive data centers by building three new gas-fired power plants recently approved by the state Public Services Commission.
The combined capacity of the three new gas-fired power plant units will be as much as 1,400 megawatts.
New federal emissions guidelines require new gas-fired power plants to capture 90% of carbon emissions, but utilities are not required to fully employ the technology until 2032.
Hyundai's share of the Stillhouse Solar Project will prevent an estimated 140 tons of heat-trapping carbon dioxide from entering the atmosphere in Texas annually.
The prevented emissions are equivalent to what 84,000 typical American cars release into the atmosphere in one year.

Codi Norred, executive director of Georgia Interfaith Power and Light, stated that buying clean energy from across the country is backward and inequitable, allowing Georgia Power to burn more fossil fuels while Hyundai calls its energy clean.
Hyundai has pledged to reach net-zero in climate-impacting pollution by 2045.

The Texas solar deal allows Hyundai to shrink its emissions footprint without impacting its actual energy mix, which is significant because 60% of Georgia's energy is still produced by burning fossil fuels.
Critics argue that Georgia should have demanded large-scale in-state solar investment in exchange for providing Hyundai with the state's largest-ever incentive package.
Jen Hilburn, north coast advocate for One Hundred Miles, stated that Georgia gave Hyundai $2 billion in tax abatements rather than asking them to invest in community infrastructure.
Hyundai is installing a solar array on a rooftop over a covered parking lot on its campus, but the panels will supply only a fraction of the plant's electricity.
Heavy energy users including Walmart and Google asked the Public Service Commission to reject Georgia Power's request for more natural gas capacity, as did the U.S. Department of Defense.

Of Georgia Power's four rate hikes since the beginning of last year, the largest led to a nearly $16 spike in the average residential customer's monthly bill, which was tied to soaring fuel prices in 2022.
Two additional rate increases have been approved for the next eight months, which will add $44 a month to the average residential bill.
Spain-based Matrix Renewables purchased the Texas solar project from developer OCI Solar Power.
Hyundai will purchase 70% of the Stillhouse Solar Project's capacity.
source_title: Hyundai taps Texas for solar power 'credit' at Georgia EV facility

facts: The first half of 2023 was better but far from good for the automotive year.
Vehicle sales were up in many markets, with Europe sales about 20 percent higher in the first quarter than in the same period last year.
The current market is still nowhere near the size it was back in 2019 due to supply bottlenecks, inflation, and a weak Chinese market.
The Chinese market presents the biggest challenges for both OEMs and suppliers, with volumes currently falling and the growth curve expected to be significantly flatter upon recovery.
China is getting closer to the kind of dynamics seen in Western automotive markets.
Automotive manufacturers focusing on high-margin models have weathered the storm well, and this strategy is expected to continue.
Lockdowns left manufacturers with a significant demand backlog while supply was limited, but these effects have now normalized to some extent.
Supply is gradually improving while demand is decreasing mainly due to recent inflation, putting the market back in familiar territory where price competition will rise as volumes stagnate.
High margins can currently only be realized on vehicles with internal combustion engines, while electric models are still nowhere near as profitable.
Some car manufacturers have already taken products out of their portfolio, with premium OEMs especially focusing on luxury and exiting the low-margin volume segment.
All OEMs are currently faced with the challenge of having a complex product portfolio, making it necessary to streamline as more electric models join combustion engine vehicles in coming years.
The low-margin segment may tend to be where cuts happen, but this strategy is only available to a small number of OEMs.
Suppliers are still navigating difficult waters, representing not an automotive industry crisis but a supplier crisis.
EBIT margins were already below five percent in 2022 and are expected to fall by up to one percentage point in 2023.
Production volumes are still below pre-pandemic levels almost everywhere, resulting in significant overcapacity, reduced plant utilization, and pressure on operational efficiency.
Discussions around OEMs sharing suppliers' costs have become more challenging than last year, as many compensations were one-time payments that suppliers need to renegotiate.
Suppliers have often agreed to long-term price increases with their upstream suppliers, compounding the ongoing crisis.
Smaller suppliers are particularly vulnerable, as getting through the crisis requires a certain size and financial stability.
Sustainability and transparency in the supply chain add maximum demands on suppliers that can lead to overwhelm.
Hella and Faurecia have formed the top-10 supplier Forvia through a merger.

Two-thirds of all acquisitions ultimately do not create significantly more value, making partnerships or collaborations potentially easier and more promising than complex integration processes for acquiring technological expertise.
Consolidation in the supplier industry is expected to be driven more by OEMs concentrating on a few select suppliers when awarding contracts rather than massive merger movements.
Growth areas for automotive suppliers include key technologies such as batteries, electric powertrains, and driver assistance systems and electronics.
Only suppliers that are large, financially strong, and have technological expertise have a chance of gaining a relevant share of the market in these growth areas.
Competition is extremely intense in these areas with significant price pressure already present even in the early stages of innovation.
The development of new technologies is almost always more complicated, time-consuming, and expensive than originally planned, making it challenging to present a financially stable business case.
Some car manufacturers are establishing their own capacities for components in the electric mobility sphere to master technologies, understand business impact, or protect jobs.
Insourcing in the electric mobility sphere is not expected to increase significantly and will probably decrease long-term, as electric mobility remains a suppliers' market.
Suppliers are generally more effective at generating cost advantages and working towards standardization than any single OEM would be.

Many mechanical component suppliers will find things harder in an electrified world and may no longer do business directly with OEMs, instead becoming Tier-2 suppliers for big system suppliers instead.
Big system suppliers are likely to emerge as winners from the transformation to electric mobility.
A collaborative partnership between OEMs and suppliers is going to be more necessary than ever due to volatility and uncertainty that neither party can manage alone.
OEMs need suppliers as a source of product innovations and to keep costs under control, though they are often still far from being a partnership of equals.
source_title: Automotive suppliers steer through turbulent waters  | Roland Berger

facts: The Georgia Supreme Court declined to hear an appeal contesting the legitimacy of Rivian’s expected property tax breaks for its new $5 billion EV facility in the state.
Rivian first announced plans for a 2,000-acre, $5 billion electric vehicle plant in Georgia in December 2021.
Once fully operational, the Georgia facility will be capable of producing up to 400,000 EVs annually.
Rivian's existing plant in Normal, Illinois, has a maximum production capacity of 150,000 EVs per year.
Initial plans stated the Georgia facility would be home to Rivian's next generation of products.
Initial plans called for construction to begin in summer 2021 and for the start of production (SOP) by 2024.
Rivian has not yet officially broken ground on the Georgia facility.
Five residents sued the Joint Development Authority (JDA) of Jasper, Morgan, Newton, and Walton Counties and the grading contractor in October over opposition to the facility.
Seven members of the opposition group Morgan County Land, Sky & Water Preservation previously challenged aspects of the local property tax incentives for Rivian.

In September, a Morgan County judge rejected roughly $700 million in local property tax breaks for Rivian, which was a central part of an over $1.5 billion incentive package that also included grants, free land, and infrastructure.

Rivian's incentive package is the second largest in Georgia state history, behind The Hyundai Motor Group's $1.8 billion in benefits for its $5.5 billion, 3,000-acre EV assembly plant in Bryan County.
In April, a panel of judges ruled in favor of the JDA and the State of Georgia on four of five aspects of the legal controversy over the majority of Rivian's incentive package.
The April panel declined to validate the bonds underlying the $700 million in local tax breaks Rivian expected to receive.
In May, the court threatened to halt the factory project and asked Rivian if it wanted to terminate its agreement with the state and JDA if the tax breaks were not included.
Rivian CEO RJ Scaringe told the Atlanta Journal-Constitution that the company is "committed to this state and this project."
Georgia's highest court ruled to let stand an appellate court decision favoring the state and JDA, paving the way for reinstating hundreds of millions of dollars in tax savings for Rivian.
A formal groundbreaking ceremony has not occurred, but the site is currently being graded.
Production at the Georgia facility is now expected to begin in 2026 and will build Rivian's next-generation R2 series products.
According to Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson, the Georgia facility is expected to employ 7,500 workers and spur thousands of supplier and related jobs.

Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that the project's scale will attract suppliers, build a thriving community to support local businesses, and benefit the e-mobility ecosystem and dozens of other supply chain industries.
Rivian delivered 12,640 EVs in the second quarter and produced nearly 14,000, shattering analyst expectations.
Rivian introduced in-house Enduro drive units and LFP battery packs this past quarter, which the company says will help streamline production.
Rivian aims to produce a total of 50,000 EVs for the year, which is more than double its 2022 production.
source_title: Rivian wins in Georgia, EV maker gets green light for $5B facility

facts: Kia Georgia's West Point plant marks 5 million vehicles with the 2027 Telluride.
The 2027 Telluride is the first hybrid electric vehicle built in Georgia.
The 2027 Telluride is the first hybrid electric vehicle built in the U.S.
source_title: Kia Georgia celebrates major milestones

facts: Designs, builds, and operates EV charging infrastructure that delivers whether the company funds it or the client does.

Offers a 'Funding By EnviroSpark and Others' model with often zero upfront or operating costs, where they install, maintain, and operate electric vehicle charging stations to allow amenities to drive value for core business.

Provides a 'Charging-as-a-Service' model featuring a monthly subscription fee with no upfront installation and operating costs, allowing clients to keep all EV charging revenue and maintain control of pricing and access.

Offers a 'Customer-Owned' model providing full ownership and control, where clients purchase the electrical infrastructure and equipment to take full control of usage, access, pricing, and EV charging revenue.
Provides specialty construction services from conduit to concrete, offering full turnkey installation and operations to support specialty EV infrastructure projects for private and public sectors.
Delivers custom support for commercial EV charging deployments regardless of size.
Has 11 years of experience with both Level 2 chargers and Level 3 EV chargers.
Operates a network and partnerships including Tesla, Georgia Power, and Shell.
Utilizes an in-house team for operational excellence, handling site acquisition, electrical design, permitting, and project management.
Provides real-time reporting, clear milestones, and progress tracking during deployment.
Offers proactive EV charger maintenance, performance monitoring, and remote diagnostics long after installation.
Builds EV charging solutions to match client businesses, covering driver access to backend management.
Promises industry-leading uptime, seamless integrations, and reliable service across every touchpoint.
Provides direct support from real people with fast responses and clear answers, avoiding ticket queues.
Operates across the US and Canada, handling EV charger installation services from design to driver-ready deployment.
Conducts site evaluation by an electrical engineering team that includes revenue projections and jurisdiction-specific considerations.
Handles custom EV charger site design and permitting with regular progress tracking and updates.
Has installed over 10,000 charging ports from coast to coast nationwide.
source_title: EV Charging Infrastructure & Installation | EnviroSpark

canonical_name: Joon Georgia, Inc., d/b/a Ajin Georgia
facts: Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Joon Georgia, Inc., d/b/a Ajin Georgia, an automotive parts manufacturer, will create 630 new jobs and invest $317 million in Bulloch County.
Plans for the company’s new manufacturing facility mark the first confirmed supplier for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America in Bryan County.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that less than two weeks after the groundbreaking of Hyundai Motor Group’s innovative facility, they are already seeing regional impact.
Governor Brian Kemp noted Joon Georgia will be the first of many companies drawn to Georgia to support the Metaplant, creating jobs and opportunity for generations of Georgians.
Joon Georgia is part of Ajin USA, a current supplier for multiple Hyundai Motor Group facilities.
The company produces a variety of automotive body parts and electronics for original equipment manufacturers (OEMs).

Jung Ho Sea, CEO of Ajin USA, expressed excitement about being one of the first Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America automotive suppliers to build an automotive parts manufacturing facility in Bulloch County and their first plant in Georgia.
Jung Ho Sea stated Joon Georgia will create approximately 630 new jobs with competitive benefits in Bulloch County and the State of Georgia.

Jung Ho Sea committed to being the best parts supplier for automotive and EV companies like Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, and the best employer and corporate citizen giving back to Bulloch County and the City of Statesboro.
Joon Georgia’s new facility will be located at Bruce Yawn Commerce Park, previously known as the Southern Gateway Commerce Park at I-16 and Hwy. 301.
Operations are expected to begin in mid-2024.
The company will begin hiring mid-2023.
Billy Allen, Chair of the Development Authority of Bulloch County, welcomed Joon Georgia to the Statesboro-Bulloch County community and region.
Billy Allen stated that their site in Bruce Yawn Commerce Park and proximity to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America will serve the company well.
Billy Allen noted that education resources, including Georgia Southern University and Ogeechee Technical College, will provide critical workforce support for Joon Georgia.
Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The GDEcD partnered with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia EMC for this project.
Workforce recruitment for the new facility will be supported by Quick Start, the top-ranked workforce training program in the nation.

By partnering with the Technical College System of Georgia, Quick Start helps assess workers, train new employees on unique processes, and develop customized job-specific training using current techniques and media.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson called Joon Georgia’s announcement a landmark moment driving Georgia’s automotive industry into the future.
Pat Wilson stated Hyundai Motor Group, its suppliers, and the Savannah area JDA are creating generational opportunities for communities in Georgia.
Pat Wilson emphasized that supporting tomorrow's jobs is a team effort and regional collaboration ensures benefits are shared across county lines.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry.
Since 2020, more than 30 EV-related projects have contributed over $13.3 billion in investment and announced more than 18,100 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Non-affiliated suppliers connected to the Hyundai project are predicted to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around the state.
Ajin Georgia will acquire the remaining land left in the Bruce Yawn Industrial park.
They will join Aspen Aerogels whose plant is already under construction.
The two-story Aspen plant construction can be seen from I-16.
These two new industries could bring over 1,000 new, high demand jobs in Bulloch.
This is in addition to the 8,500 new jobs that will be created at the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America in Bryan County which is just five miles from the Bulloch County line.
In the November 1, 2022 Bulloch County Commissioners meeting they approved rezoning two tracks of land totaling over 100 acres to light industrial.
During the presentation, Stephen Rushing, Bulloch County Development Authority attorney, said that multiple Hyundai suppliers are showing high interest in the land.
There could be two to three new industries located on these sites.
source_title: Bulloch YES (AGAIN) | First Hyundai Metaplant America Supplier Chooses Bulloch  - Grice Connect

facts: Georgia Tech researchers developed a new iron chloride cathode that could slash lithium-ion battery costs and revolutionize electric vehicles and energy storage.
A research team from multiple institutions, led by Hailong Chen of Georgia Tech, developed the cost-effective cathode with potential to significantly enhance lithium-ion batteries.
Hailong Chen is an associate professor with appointments in the George W. Woodruff School of Mechanical Engineering and the School of Materials Science and Engineering at Georgia Tech.
The new iron chloride (FeCl3) cathode costs a mere 1-2% of typical cathode materials and can store the same amount of electricity.
Cathode materials affect capacity, energy, efficiency, performance, lifespan, and affordability of a battery.
Hailong Chen stated that their cathode could be a game-changer that would greatly improve the EV market and the whole lithium-ion battery market.
Batteries are currently responsible for about 50% of an EV’s total cost, which makes clean-energy cars more expensive than internal combustion vehicles.
Only four types of cathodes have been successfully commercialized for lithium-ion batteries so far; Chen's would be the fifth.
The development represents a big step forward in battery technology toward creating an all-solid-state lithium-ion battery (LIB).

Conventional LIBs use liquid electrolytes that have hard limits on energy storage and can leak or catch fire, while all-solid-state LIBs use solid electrolytes to dramatically boost efficiency, reliability, safety, and energy capacity.
All-solid-state LIB technology is currently still in the development and testing phase.
With the FeCl3 cathode, a solid electrolyte, and a lithium metal anode, the cost of their whole battery system is 30-40% of current LIBs.

Hailong Chen noted that this technology could make EVs much cheaper than internal combustion cars, provide large-scale energy storage to enhance grid resilience, and improve sustainability and supply chain stability for the EV market.
Chen's interest in FeCl3 as a cathode material originated with his lab's research into solid electrolyte materials starting in 2019.

The lab initially tried making solid-state batteries using chloride-based solid electrolytes with traditional commercial oxide-based cathodes, but the cathode and electrolyte materials were incompatible.
Researchers determined that a chloride-based cathode could provide a better pairing with the chloride electrolyte to offer better battery performance.
Hailong Chen explained that FeCl3 was chosen because its crystal structure is potentially suitable for storing and transporting lithium ions, and it functioned as expected.
The most popularly used EV cathodes are oxides requiring a gigantic amount of costly nickel and cobalt, which are heavy elements that can be toxic and pose an environmental challenge.
The Chen team’s cathode contains only iron (Fe) and chlorine (Cl), which are abundant, affordable, widely used elements found in steel and table salt.
Initial tests showed FeCl3 performed as well as or better than other, much more expensive cathodes.
The new cathode has a higher operational voltage than the popularly used LiFePO4 (lithium iron phosphate) cathode.
This technology may be less than five years from commercial viability in EVs.
The research work was led by Chen and postdoc Zhantao Liu, who is the lead author of the study.
Collaborators included Ting Zhu and Yuanzhi Tang from Georgia Tech, Jue Liu from Oak Ridge National Laboratory, and Shuo Chen from the University of Houston.

Hailong Chen stated that while they want to perfect the materials in the lab and understand their underlying functioning mechanisms, they are open to opportunities to scale up the technology toward commercial applications.
The study titled “Low-cost iron trichloride cathode for all-solid-state lithium-ion batteries” was published on 23 September 2024 in Nature Sustainability.
The study was funded by the National Science Foundation under Grant Nos. 1706723 and 2108688.
source_title: Georgia Tech’s New Cathode Technology Could Revolutionize Electric Vehicles and Energy Storage

facts: Anovion Technologies, based in Chicago, announced plans to invest $800 million to build a factory in Bainbridge, Georgia.
The company will hire 400 workers for the new facility.
Anovion will manufacture synthetic graphite anode for lithium-ion batteries at the plant.
Production of the synthetic graphite anode is scheduled to start in 2025.
The factory supports Georgia's broader initiative to recruit electric vehicle and supplier manufacturers.
Republican Governor Brian Kemp stated the announcement furthers his goal of making Georgia the nation's electric mobility capital.
Most battery graphite is currently manufactured outside the United States.
Georgia economic development officials report that more than 40 electric vehicle-related projects have been announced in Georgia since 2020, pledging $22 billion in investment and 28,000 jobs.
Anovion was formed last year by combining the graphite businesses of Pyrotek and Amsted Graphite Materials, along with new investment from private equity firm Monomyth Group.
The company operates existing facilities in Sanborn, New York, and Clarksburg, West Virginia.
Anovion previously announced plans to build a graphite factory with a 35,000-ton yearly capacity near Muscle Shoals, Alabama.
The company won a $117 million grant under the new federal infrastructure law last year to help finance the Alabama plant and improvements at its New York facility.
CEO Eric Stopka cited Georgia's workforce, research universities, and growing solar energy production as key attractions for the project.
Stopka stated, "We are looking forward to providing solutions that will further secure the country’s electrified future with the support of our partners in Georgia and the federal government."
Anovion executives aim to reach 150,000 tons of production capacity in coming years, starting from near zero.
Synthetic graphite is produced by heating carbon products like petroleum coke (an oil refining byproduct) to super-high temperatures for extended periods.
The manufacturing process requires furnaces that consume huge amounts of electricity, making power costs a critical factor in site selection.
Anovion may qualify for a Georgia state income tax credit allowing an annual deduction of $3,000 per job from state taxes for five years, contingent on workers earning at least $31,300 annually.
The potential state income tax incentive could be worth up to $6 million.
The company may also qualify for property tax breaks from the city of Bainbridge and Decatur County.
This announcement marks the second major industrial project in two years for Bainbridge, located in southwest Georgia.
In 2021, Danimer Scientific announced plans to build a $700 million factory in the area, also hiring 400 employees.
Decatur County Commission Chairman Pete Stephens stated, "We have always known that in order to remain competitive and to grow, we needed to diversify our industry mix."
source_title: Electric battery component maker plans $800M Georgia factory, hiring hundreds | FOX 5 Atlanta

facts: A new fabrication technique could allow solid-state automotive lithium-ion batteries to adopt nonflammable ceramic electrolytes using the same production processes as in batteries made with conventional liquid electrolytes.
The cathode material NMC111 used in this research was sourced from MSE Supplies.
A new Georgia Tech manufacturing process could enable battery makers to produce lighter, safer, and more energy-dense batteries.

The melt-infiltration technology developed by materials science researchers at the Georgia Institute of Technology uses electrolyte materials that can be infiltrated into porous yet densely packed, thermally stable electrodes.
Ampcera solid electrolyte materials (sulfide, LLZO, etc.) are available for order from MSE Supplies.

The one-step process produces high-density composites based on pressure-less, capillary-driven infiltration of a molten solid electrolyte into porous bodies, including multilayered electrode-separator stacks.

The research was published as "Electrolyte melt infiltration for scalable manufacturing of inorganic all-solid-state lithium-ion batteries" by Xiao, Y., Turcheniuk, K., Narla, A. et al. in Nat. Mater. (2021).
The cathode material NMC111 used in this work is from MSE Supplies.

Gleb Yushin stated: "While the melting point of traditional solid state electrolytes can range from 700 degrees Celsius to over 1,000 degrees Celsius, we operate at a much lower temperature range, depending on the electrolyte composition, roughly from 200 to 300 degrees Celsius."
At these lower temperatures, fabrication is much faster and easier, materials do not react, and standard electrode assemblies including polymer binders or glue can be stable in these conditions.
Gleb Yushin is a professor in the School of Materials Science and Engineering at Georgia Tech.
The new technique is to be reported March 8 in the journal Nature Materials.

The technique could allow large automotive Li-ion batteries to be made safer with 100% solid-state nonflammable ceramic rather than liquid electrolytes using the same manufacturing processes of conventional liquid electrolyte battery production.

The patent-pending manufacturing technology mimics low-cost fabrication of commercial Li-ion cells with liquid electrolytes, but instead uses solid state electrolytes with low melting points that are melted and infiltrated into dense electrodes.
High-quality multi-layered cells of any size or shape could be rapidly manufactured at scale using proven tools and processes developed and optimized over the last 30 years for Li-ion.

Georgia Tech graduate student Yiran Xiao explained: "Melt-infiltration technology is the key advance. The cycle life and stability of Li-ion batteries depend strongly on the operating conditions, particularly temperature."
If batteries are overheated for a prolonged period, they commonly begin to degrade prematurely, and overheated batteries may catch on fire.
Nearly all electric vehicles currently include sophisticated and rather expensive cooling systems due to overheating risks.
In contrast, solid-state batteries may only require heaters, which are significantly less expensive than cooling systems.
Yushin and Xiao are encouraged by the potential of this manufacturing process to enable battery makers to produce lighter, safer, and more energy-dense batteries.

Co-author and Georgia Tech research scientist Kostiantyn Turcheniuk stated: "The developed melt-infiltration technology is compatible with a broad range of material chemistries, including so-called conversion-type electrodes."
Conversion-type electrodes have been demonstrated to increase automotive cell energy density by over 20% now and by more than 100% in the future.
Higher density cells support longer driving ranges and require high-capacity electrodes for that performance leap.
Georgia Tech's technique is not yet commercially ready.

Gleb Yushin predicts: "If a significant portion of the future EV market embraces solid-state batteries, this would probably be the only way to go," since it will allow manufacturers to use their existing production facilities and infrastructure.
Georgia Tech Professor Gleb Yushin sees melt-infiltration technology opening the floodgates to more Li-ion battery innovation.
Gleb Yushin stated: "That's why we focused on this project—it was one of the most commercially viable areas of innovation for our lab to pursue."
Battery cell prices hit $100 per kilowatt hour for the first time in 2020.
According to Gleb Yushin, battery cell prices will need to drop below $70 per kilowatt hour before the consumer EV market can fully open.
Battery innovation is critical to that occurring.
The Materials Science lab team currently is focused on developing other electrolytes that will have lower melting points and higher conductivities using the same technique proven in the lab.

Gleb Yushin explained: "So many incredibly smart scientists are focused on solving very challenging scientific problems, while completely ignoring economic and technical practicality. They are studying and optimizing very high-temperature electrolytes that are not only dramatically more expensive to use in cells but are also up to five times heavier compared with liquid electrolytes."
Gleb Yushin's goal is to push the research community to look outside that chemical box.

links.context: DOI for the published research paper

source_title: MSE Supplies Battery Material was Used by Georgia Tech Researchers in

facts: Auto parts manufacturer Joon Georgia plans to build a production facility in southeast Georgia to supply components to Hyundai Motor Group’s first U.S. electric vehicle plant.
Joon Georgia will invest $317 million to manufacture parts in Bullock County.
Joon Georgia is the first confirmed supplier for Hyundai’s incoming EV campus in Savannah.
Hyundai’s EV campus in Savannah broke ground in October.
The new plant will begin hiring for approximately 630 jobs mid-next year.
Production at the new plant is slated to begin in mid-2024.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that Joon Georgia is the “first of many companies” expected to open shop in Georgia to support Hyundai’s project.
Hyundai’s Savannah campus represents a $5.5 billion investment.
Hyundai’s Savannah campus is set to start production of up to 300,000 EVs per year in 2025.
Joon Georgia is a subsidiary of Alabama-headquartered Ajin USA.
Ajin USA currently supplies other Hyundai facilities with auto body parts and electronics.
The new facility will be Ajin’s first in Georgia.
Ajin USA CEO Jung Ho Sea confirmed the new facility's status in a statement.
The facility will be located in Statesboro.
The Statesboro location is in close proximity to the Port of Savannah, granting it access to “global markets quickly and efficiently.”
More parts manufacturers are growing their presence in Georgia to match their OEM customers.
Combined, Hyundai and Rivian have invested more than $10.5 billion in Georgia over the past year.
The combined investments mark the two biggest economic development projects in Georgia.
Polymer manufacturer Solvay Specialty Polymers announced plans to construct an $850 million plant to manufacture key lithium-ion battery parts in Augusta.
Copper foil manufacturer Denkai America is set to locate its North America headquarters and new facility in Augusta.
Georgia officials predict non-affiliated suppliers connected to Hyundai’s project will invest an estimated $1 billion in other locations around the state.
source_title: Hyundai supplier to build factory near automaker’s Georgia EV campus | Manufacturing Dive

facts: The push to increase the Savannah area's water supply grew from efforts to satisfy the demands of the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America site.
The Bryan County electric vehicle manufacturing complex will need 4 million gallons of water a day when it’s fully up and running.
Nearby industrial and residential developments are expected to guzzle another 2.6 million gallons daily.
An on-site well covers Hyundai’s needs for its construction and launch.
Wells in Bulloch County are expected to provide the water needed for the plant until more surface water is available.
Bulloch was chosen because it’s the so-called green zone where additional groundwater use is not limited.
The state capped or reduced the total amounts that can be withdrawn from the aquifer in Chatham, Bryan and part of Effingham counties.
The Bulloch wells were initially planned to provide water to Hyundai for its first 25 years.
Bulloch County residents objected over fears for the continued productivity of their own agricultural wells, and the timeline was shortened to 15 years.
Savannah’s chief of water resources is Ron Feldner.
In 1945 the General Assembly created the Industrial and Domestic Water Supply Commission to administer the construction of the water filtration plant in what was then Port Wentworth.
Using water drawn from 10 miles away in the Abercorn Creek in Effingham County, the plant could treat 35 million gallons a day when it opened.
The facility gave Savannah greater ability to supply not just its own needs, but also to sell water to smaller municipalities in the three-county area.

City Manager Jay Melder told Savannah City Council at a workshop in the fall that the City of Savannah has been a regional water supplier at least since 1947 when the Industrial & Domestic water plant was constructed.
Upgrades over the years have increased the Industrial & Domestic plant’s capacity to its current flow of 58 million gallons a day, or MGD.
The last major upgrade to the facility was in 1995 and the aging facility is strained by increasing demand.
One day last August, the plant treated 57.5 million gallons.

Water infrastructure consultant Jonathan Ladd of Black & Veatch told Savannah City Council in September that the plant is rated for 58 MGD with not a whole lot of fluff left, and additional standby units are needed for routine maintenance or unexpected repairs.
Savannah plans to increase the facility’s capacity to 65 MGD as quickly as possible.
The planned upgrades include a new settling basin plus filtration system improvements at the water treatment plant as well as new water storage tanks there.
At the water intake on Abercorn Creek, the city plans to install an oxygen injection system to meet state regulatory requirements.
The governor’s mid-year budget authorized $146 million for Savannah’s water issues, enough to cover this initial phase of upgrades, which is expected to be completed over the next two years.
The city ultimately expects to increase the capacity at its surface water plant to 100 million gallons per day to meet long-term demand.
Effingham County currently buys 4 million gallons of water a day from Savannah, all of it treated surface water.
Effingham also operates two wells that provide about 370,000 gallons a day.
Cities and private systems in Effingham serve their customers with groundwater totaling almost 4 million gallons a day.
The county population more than doubled over the last three decades to an estimated 73,000 last year, and growth continues.
Effingham officials expect half of Hyundai’s more than 8,000 employees to reside in the county by 2035.
Funded by $365 million in loans and grants from the state, Effingham plans a water treatment plant that can process 14 million gallons a day, and could eventually expand to 28 MGD.
Effingham County Manager Tim Callanan said the idea for the water treatment facility surfaced in January 2024 when the county updated its water and sewer master plan.
Callanan said they didn’t bring it up related to Hyundai, but looking regionally, it’s safer to have two intakes and one further away from the coast to avoid salt water.
Effingham is planning to pull water directly from the Savannah River at Georgia Power’s Plant McIntosh property.
That intake location is about 8 miles inland from Savannah’s intake on Abercorn Creek.
In April 2024, the county hired a consultant, GMC, to do a feasibility study.
Feldner said the city welcomed the partnership with Effingham County that evolved in early 2024.
Callanan welcomed the chance to cooperate with Savannah, his county’s main water purveyor.
In a November memo to state water regulators at the Environmental Protection Division, Callanan stressed the benefits of its proposed surface water treatment plant.

He cited redundancy as a benefit, noting they could provide a backup water supply for the City of Savannah and vice versa, as well as potential to provide water to the western portion of Savannah and to Bulloch County.
Environmentalists have been calling for better water planning.
Savannah Riverkeeper Tonya Bonitatibus supports the Bryan, Effingham and Savannah collaboration but is uneasy about how long the good feelings will last.

She criticized the governor for giving money to three different entities without any significant agreement on a regional entity to figure out how to get along, calling it a red flag for just throwing money at the problem.
Ogeechee Riverkeeper Damon Mullis advocates for diverting surface water to industry to preserve more pristine aquifer water for drinking.
He sees the plan and its $500 million in state funding as a good start and a foundational step in future regional water planning.
The move should benefit the Floridan Aquifer by helping to wean industrial water users off it and allow it to begin recovering.
The Savannah River provides drinking water to more than 60,000 retail customers in South Carolina’s Beaufort and Jasper counties.
Its harbor is dredged to allow massive ships to call on the port, and it’s a waste receptacle for industrial pollution.
Observers say the 313-mile-long Savannah can handle the additional withdrawals.
The closing of Georgia Power’s coal-fired plants Riverside in 2005 and Kraft in 2015 removed almost 400 million gallons a day of demand on the river.
In 2019, Georgia Power closed its coal unit at Plant McIntosh in Effingham, reducing its need to withdraw river water for cooling.
The plant closures on the lower Savannah River give suppliers like Savannah and now Effingham more wiggle room to request new or expanded water withdrawal permits.
Bonitatibus compared the water demand to one more straw in a camel’s back of an overloaded camel, warning that things will start breaking at some point.
She pointed out that International Paper’s two facilities in Chatham County last year used more than 20 million gallons a day of water from the aquifer.
Using aquifer water saves the paper mill money because it would have to pay to treat river water to a manufacturing standard, even if it had a permit.
Bonitatibus questioned why industries should get access to that water when International Paper is the linchpin of the pollution source in the lower river.
source_title: State funding reshapes Savannah-area water market - The Current

facts: Hyundai Mobis will invest $926 million in an EV Power Electric system plant in Bryan County.
The new plant will eventually create at least 1,500 jobs.
Gov. Brian Kemp announced the investment on Tuesday.
Hyundai Mobis is the second EV supplier to unveil plans near the Hyundai EV plant since the Korean automaker broke ground on its $5.5 billion project late last month.

Auto parts manufacturer Joon Georgia announced Nov. 7 it will invest $317 million in a new manufacturing plant in Bulloch County that will create 630 jobs, making it the first confirmed supplier for the new Hyundai EV plant.

Gov. Brian Kemp said, “When we celebrated the groundbreaking of Hyundai’s new electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facility in Bryan County, we knew it would unleash transformational job creation and investment in that entire region of our state.”

Kemp added, “As we announce the second supplier in just two weeks to locate in that area, we’re excited to see their impact on the surrounding communities and the growing list of other job creators that will soon follow.”
The EV Power Electric system plant will expand Hyundai Mobis’ footprint in Georgia.
Hyundai Mobis has operated a manufacturing facility with about 1,200 employees in West Point since 2009 as a supplier to the nearby Kia plant.

H.S. Oh, vice president of Hyundai Mobis’ Electric Powertrain Business Unit, said, “Hyundai Mobis’ investment project in Bryan County reflects an acceleration in the development of the EV supply chain in Georgia’s auto industry.”
Oh stated, “We’re going to be a major production player in the EV market, and that’s going to trigger more growth within the sector.”
Construction on the 1.2 million-square-foot Hyundai Mobis plant in Richmond Hill is expected to start as early as January.
Production at the new facility is due to begin in 2024.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development worked on the project in partnership with the Development Authority of Bryan County, the Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Power and the Technical College System of Georgia’s Quick Start program.
source_title: New Hyundai plant in Bryan County draws second supplier |

facts: Georgia leaders announced the biggest economic development site purchase in state history.
The development covers more than 2,000 acres located in Bryan County.
The site is intended to attract companies seeking speed to market for new business operations.
The state is working in partnership with the Georgia Ports Authority and the Department of Economic Development to attract investors.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that few states can assemble a site ready to go to market as quickly as this property.
Governor Kemp credited local elected leaders, taxpayers, and economic development teams for advancing the site to its current stage.
Governor Kemp called on major original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) to set up operations at the site.
The site is positioned as one of the premier locations in the country for speed to market, proximity to a port, and access to a highly skilled workforce.
Governor Kemp envisions a company investing billions of dollars at the site, drawing a comparison to SK Battery's investment in northeast Georgia.
The initial funding required to prepare the site is considered minimal compared to the long-term economic impact it will generate for the region over generations.
The sale of the property is expected to close within the coming weeks.
source_title: Governor Brian Kemp visits Bryan County mega-site, calls on companies to buy in

facts: Ajin Industrial is a South Korean auto parts manufacturer.
Ajin Industrial plans to develop a $317 million plant in eastern Georgia.
The plant will supply the nearby Hyundai electric vehicle factory that is now underway.
The planned facility site is located at 1500 Genesis Dr, Ellabell, GA.
source_title: News | Electric Vehicle Parts Supplier To Locate Near Hyundai Georgia Plant

canonical_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution Battery Plant at Ellabell, Georgia (Metaplant America)
facts: In early September, U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) raided the Hyundai-LG Energy Solution’s battery factory in Ellabell, Georgia.
Approximately 300 South Korean workers were on site during the raid, which involved choppers, drones, armored vehicles, and ICE agents.
Most of the detained South Korean workers were automation programmers, mechanical engineers, and information technology specialists.
At the time of the raid, construction of the electric vehicle battery factory was 98 percent completed.
The South Korean workforce had been dispatched to fine-tune machinery and train local workers.
ICE shackled the ankles and waists of the South Korean workers at gunpoint and forced them onto transportation vehicles.
ICE alleged that the South Korean workers had overstayed their visas or held permits that excluded manual labor.
Most of the workers arrived in Georgia with an ESTA visa waiver or a B-1 business visa intended for installing/maintaining imported machinery and educating Americans.
Hyundai, LG Energy Solution, and their subcontractors insist they followed U.S. Department of State guidelines and communicated with the U.S. Embassy in South Korea regarding visa compliance.
ICE’s Georgia raid was Homeland Security’s largest single worksite operation ever conducted in its history.
Georgia and Savannah local officials viewed Hyundai and LG as key to uplifting rural coastal counties.
Governor Brian Kemp and his economic development team visited Seoul to entice investment to Georgia.
In 2022, Georgia secured a manufacturing deal with Hyundai.
Hyundai’s total investment in Georgia has grown to $12.6 billion.
The Hyundai-LG Energy Solution battery cell plant is valued at $4.3 billion on its own.
The plant was slated to annually produce enough battery cells to power approximately 300,000 electric vehicles.
Trip Tollison, head of the Savannah Economic Development Authority (SEDA), stated Hyundai’s presence brings more economic benefit to Georgia than the Atlanta Olympics did.
Georgia state and local governments have invested more than $350 million in road improvements around the production site.
The area’s ports have been enlarged to facilitate shipment of manufacturing parts and cars.
South Korea is Georgia’s third-largest international trading partner.
Governor Kemp entered crisis management mode, conveying embarrassment and an apology regarding the raid.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development and SEDA met with Hyundai Motor executives to discuss returning deported workers.
Trip Tollison told the Savannah Morning News that no other entity in the world possesses the proprietary technology required to install the battery equipment, emphasizing reliance on South Korea.
Governor Kemp planned to visit South Korea again to meet with Hyundai executives for damage control.
The Ellabell battery plant is losing $2.3 million per day due to the forced construction stall.
On September 14, U.S. Deputy Secretary of State Christopher Landau visited Seoul and expressed “deep regrets” over the detention incident.
Landau assured that deported workers would face no disadvantages upon returning and that no further similar incidents would occur.
President Donald Trump posted that he does not want to frighten off or disincentivize foreign investment into America, welcoming companies and their employees.

The Congressional Research Service noted the raid raised concerns in South Korea about bilateral relations and whether U.S. immigration policy conflicts with increasing U.S. manufacturing jobs through foreign investment.

The raid instilled fears among South Korean companies with U.S. projects, including Samsung Electronics ($37 billion semiconductor foundry in Texas) and SK Hynix ($3.8 billion Indiana plant for high-bandwidth memory chips by 2028).
Weeks before the raid, Hyundai pledged to increase its total U.S. investment to $26 billion.
Since the raid, South Korean companies have repatriated workers, and specialized workers postponed or canceled U.S. trips.
Workers developed a collective phobia of U.S. business trips after seeing colleagues shackled and forced to survive on bread and canned beans for days.
Trump offered detained workers the chance to stay and continue work, but only one opted to remain.
Completion dates for South Korean plants in the U.S. are being delayed, causing economic loss for companies and local communities.
South Korean President Lee Jae-myung noted that businesses investing in the U.S. will be very hesitant following the raid.
Seoul and Washington are negotiating a verbal commitment to invest $350 billion into America, with the Georgia detention trauma looming large.
The incident prompted multinational companies to flood U.S. legal firms with requests for visa advice for employees on similar visas.
Non-Korean companies halted some planned U.S. trips after seeing South Korean employees treated “like criminals.”

It is common industry practice for foreign companies to bring specialized workers on short-term business visitor visas (ESTA, B-1/B-2), especially in battery manufacturing, shipbuilding, and semiconductors where U.S. workers lack technical expertise.
L-1 visas are for nonimmigrant intracompany transferees, but most specialized workers are subcontractors without access to this category.
H-1B visas have a low annual cap of 85,000, are exorbitant to apply for, highly in demand, and the lottery system prevents quick acquisition for short-notice technicians.
Previous U.S. administrations allowed high-skilled foreign nationals under visitors’ visas (ESTA for business activities, B-1 for equipment installment/training).

ICE under the Trump administration took a more restrictive view of business visitor allowances than regulations permit, claiming individuals at Hyundai’s plant were “fraudulently using visitors’ visas.”
U.S. officials have shown an apologetic stance and willingness to accommodate South Korea’s visa requirements following the raid.
Deputy Secretary of State Landau proposed talks on issuing proper visas for South Korean workers, citing a need for Washington’s institutional support.
Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick stated Trump will address tensions caused by the raid.

The most likely outcome is passage of the Partner with Korea Act (proposed in 2013), which intends to create an E-4 visa category for nonimmigrant specialized South Korean workers to build U.S. manufacturing capacity.
Experts believe at least 20,000 E-4 visas should be issued annually given investment size and the “Make America’s Shipbuilding Great Again (MASGA)” commitment.
The Foreign Ministry in Seoul noted the general public in South Korea “felt deeply shocked by the crackdown.”
The raid engendered rare political unity in South Korea, with politicians across partisan lines deploring images of people carted off in chains.
Almost 40 percent of South Korean conservatives said they were “deeply disappointed” by the Trump administration.
South Korean workers remain reluctant to set foot in the U.S., with trust shattered and a lasting sense of humiliation despite Washington's visa offers.
source_title: South Korea-US Relations Suffer the Aftermath of ICE’s Georgia Raid – The Diplomat

facts: HL-GA Battery Company LLC is a joint venture between Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution.
The company drives the EV revolution in North America with advanced lithium-ion battery manufacturing in Georgia.
HL-GA Battery Company is ramping up operations, finalizing facility construction, and preparing for large-scale battery production to meet surging EV demand in North America.
Major construction phases at the Georgia manufacturing facility are being completed.
The company is making strategic efforts to expand its market presence and reach new customer segments.
The company is implementing advanced technologies to improve service delivery and customer experience.
HL-GA Battery Company LLC is dedicated to advancing electric vehicle technology by producing high-capacity lithium-ion battery cells.
The company’s manufacturing plant is located in Ellabell, Georgia.
The facility is one of the largest battery projects in the state’s history.
The facility is expected to begin production in early 2026.
The facility has an annual capacity of 30GWh.
The facility will supply batteries for up to 300,000 EVs per year.
The batteries will support Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis models.
HL-GA Battery Company is committed to sustainability, innovation, and supporting the rapid growth of the EV market in North America.
The company was founded in 2023.
HL-GA Battery Company LLC is a private company.
source_title: HL-GA Battery Company LLC | EV Battery Manufacturing in G...

facts: Hyundai Motor America recorded its strongest first quarter in company history, selling 205,388 vehicles in Q1 2026, a 1 percent increase year-over-year.

The headline result was driven in part by record hybrid-electric sales, though the performance of Hyundai’s dedicated battery-electric lineup was divided: the Ioniq 5 continued to grow, the all-new Ioniq 9 began its sales ramp, and the Ioniq 6 posted declines severe enough that Hyundai has effectively withdrawn the standard model from the U.S. market for 2026.
Hybrid-electric vehicle sales rose 50 percent in March and 61 percent for the full first quarter compared to the same periods in 2025, setting records for Hyundai in the U.S.
The gains were spread across multiple nameplates.
The Sonata HEV increased by 150 percent in March and by 107 percent in Q1.
The Elantra HEV was up 92 percent in March and 141 percent for the quarter.
The Santa Fe HEV rose 31 percent in March and 47 percent for Q1.

Hyundai’s sales table does not break out powertrain variants within individual nameplates, so the specific contribution of hybrid trims to those totals cannot be fully isolated from the overall model numbers.
Among pure battery-electric vehicles, the Ioniq 5 was the standout performer.
The crossover sold 4,425 units in March, a 13 percent increase year-over-year and the model’s best-ever single month in the U.S.
For the full first quarter, Ioniq 5 sales reached 9,790 units, up 14 percent from 8,611 units in Q1 2025.

The Ioniq 5 is assembled at Hyundai Motor Group’s Metaplant America facility in Ellabell, Georgia, which means it is not subject to import tariffs on foreign-made vehicles and retains domestic manufacturing status that can benefit buyers and fleet operators navigating the current trade environment.
The Ioniq 9, Hyundai’s new three-row electric SUV, recorded 905 units in March and 1,990 units year-to-date.
There is no prior-year comparison available, as the model was not on sale in the first quarter of 2025.
The Ioniq 9 is also assembled at Metaplant America and starts at $60,555, including destination.
It offers up to 335 miles of range in its rear-wheel-drive configuration and up to 320 miles in standard all-wheel-drive trims, with 311 miles for the AWD Performance variants.
The vehicle uses an 800-volt architecture and supports DC fast charging at up to 350 kW, with a claimed charge time of approximately 24 minutes from 10 to 80 percent.
It comes standard with a NACS port, providing compatibility with Tesla’s Supercharger network.
With three rows and seating for up to seven passengers, the Ioniq 9 positions Hyundai’s electric lineup in the full-size family SUV segment for the first time.
The Ioniq 6 tells a sharply different story.
The electric sedan sold just 256 units in March 2026, down 82% from 1,435 in March 2025.
Year-to-date, the model has moved 829 units, down from 3,318 in the same period last year — a 75 percent decline.
The collapse in Ioniq 6 volume is not incidental.
Hyundai has discontinued the standard Ioniq 6 for the 2026 model year in the United States, except for the high-performance Ioniq 6 N, which is expected to arrive in limited quantities.
The standard model’s cancellation results from a combination of factors: the expiration of the $7,500 federal EV tax credit in September 2025 and the Ioniq 6’s exposure to import tariffs.
Unlike the Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9, the Ioniq 6 is manufactured in South Korea, which subjects it to a 25 percent tariff on imported vehicles.
That tariff, layered on top of the loss of the consumer tax credit, made it difficult to price the sedan competitively against domestically assembled alternatives.
The Q1 sales figures largely reflect clearance of the remaining 2025 inventory rather than active 2026 sales activity.

The divergence between the Ioniq 5’s growth and the Ioniq 6’s effective exit illustrates how consequential the combination of domestic production and tax credit eligibility has been in reshaping U.S. EV market dynamics.
Vehicles assembled in the United States are insulated from the import tariff exposure that has undercut models built overseas, and that structural advantage is now directly visible in the sales data.
On the autonomous vehicle front, Uber and Motional — which is majority-owned by Hyundai Motor Group — launched a commercial robotaxi service in Las Vegas.
Riders on the Uber platform can now be matched with an all-electric Motional Ioniq 5 robotaxi along Las Vegas Boulevard, with plans to expand the service area over time.
The deployment marks a meaningful step toward commercial autonomous EV operation, though it remains limited in geographic scope at this stage.
Hyundai is also one of the eight automakers behind Ionna, the joint-venture EV charging network that recently marked its second anniversary.
As of late March 2026, Ionna has more than 4,700 charging bays contracted nationwide, with nearly 1,500 in construction or beyond initial planning.
Approximately 100 sites are operational with close to 1,000 bays currently active, and the network has a stated goal of deploying more than 30,000 ultra-fast charging points by 2030.

The continued buildout of Ionna infrastructure is directly relevant to the long-term adoption of Hyundai’s EV lineup, particularly as the Ioniq 9 targets road-trip and family use cases where charging availability carries significant weight.
Hyundai Motor Group has committed $26 billion in U.S. investment for 2025-2028.

The Metaplant America facility in Georgia, which assembles the Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9, is a central part of that strategy, and its domestic production status has taken on added significance as tariff policy has made the cost gap between imported and U.S.-built EVs more visible to consumers.

Hyundai’s Q1 results mirror the pattern seen across its Korean stablemate Kia: hybrid models are growing at a rate that substantially outpaces battery-electric vehicles in the near term, and within the BEV category, domestically built models are weathering the current market environment far better than those relying on imports.

Whether Hyundai can sustain Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9 momentum through the remainder of 2026 — and whether the Ioniq 6 N generates meaningful volume as the sole remaining sedan variant — will be clearer by mid-year.
source_title: Hyundai's record Q1 was built on hybrids, not battery-electric vehicles - Destination Charged

facts: HL-GA Battery Company is excited to call Bryan County home and drive the EV transition in America!
Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution committed an investment of more than $4.3 billion to establish HL-GA Battery Company.
HL-GA Battery Company operates as a battery cell manufacturing joint venture.
The facility is strategically located outside Savannah, Georgia.
The facility is scheduled to start production early 2026.
HL-GA Battery Company is one of the largest projects in the state of Georgia history.
HL-GA Battery Company will supply next-generation battery technology to manufacturing facilities for production of Hyundai, Kia and Genesis EV models.
The new facility will help create a stable supply of batteries in the region and allow for a quick response to the global EV demand.
LG Energy Solution and Hyundai Motor Group have long been partners in the field of electrification.
LG Energy Solution worked with Hyundai Motor Group to supply EV batteries for vehicles, including Elantra Hybrid, Kona Electric, and IONIQ 6 dedicated EV.
The Hyundai Elantra Hybrid, LPi vehicle introduced in 2009, was HMG’s first electrified model.
In 2021, the two power houses started a battery cell joint venture in Indonesia which started production in early 2024.
Hyundai Motor Group is a global enterprise that has created a value chain based on mobility, steel, and construction, as well as logistics, finance, IT, and service.
Hyundai Motor Group has about 250,000 employees worldwide.
The Group’s mobility brands include Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis.
LG Energy Solution is a leading global manufacturer of lithium-ion batteries for electric vehicles, mobility, IT, and energy storage systems.
LG Energy Solution has 30 years of experience in revolutionary battery technology and extensive research and development (R&D).
LG Energy Solution is the top battery-related patent holder in the world with over 58,000 patents.
LG Energy Solution's robust global network spans North America, Europe, Asia, and Australia.

LG Energy Solution has established battery manufacturing facilities through joint ventures with major automakers such as General Motors, Stellantis N.V., Hyundai Motor Group, and Honda Motor Co., Ltd.
HL-GA Battery is transforming the automotive space and leading the way for sustainable automotive energy with over 30 years of research behind its lithium ion battery technology.
source_title: About -

facts: Bojangles has opened its first EV charging station at 4401 Ogeechee Road in Savannah, Georgia.
The Savannah site serves as the opening location in a planned national rollout developed with XLR8 America and Energy and Environmental Design Services.
Bojangles operates more than 870 restaurants across 23 states.
The company is positioning the Savannah site as the first step in extending its footprint into mobility infrastructure.
The Savannah hub combines Level 2 and Level 3 charging with dine-in service, framing the stop as a “charge-and-dine” occasion.
The site mixes Level 2 (medium-speed) and Level 3 (high-speed) chargers to give drivers a choice between longer meal sessions and shorter stops.
Bojangles reports targeted uptime of more than 97 percent across the installed equipment.
The company calls the format “charge-and-dine,” designed to give drivers a reason to stay during a charging session rather than treating it as transactional.
Bojangles is approaching the rollout as a category move rather than a one-off pilot, with additional sites planned in key markets.
The partnership brings XLR8 America as a turnkey infrastructure provider, alongside Energy and Environmental Design Services handling installation and engineering work.
XLR8 America operates on a host-side model in which the charging developer funds, owns, and operates the equipment.
Richard Del Valle, Chief Information Officer at Bojangles, stated that driving trends and expectations are changing, and EV charging turns waiting time into an asset for relaxation and dining.

Frank O’Connor, CEO of XLR8 America, added that their philosophy is to charge where you park, and together they are building a national network that makes EV charging effortless by combining it with dining stops.
Bojangles has not disclosed a specific site count, deployment timeline, or the next markets for the rollout.
source_title: Bojangles Opens First EV Charging Station in Savannah - The EV Report

facts: Ecoplastic Corp. will build a new automotive parts plant in Bulloch County.
The project represents a $205 million investment that will create 456 jobs, as announced by Gov. Brian Kemp on Thursday.
The plant will supply the huge Hyundai electric vehicle manufacturing facility being built near Savannah.
Ecoplastic was established in 1984 and produces plastic automobile parts for original equipment manufacturers and other suppliers.
Products include bumpers, consoles, trims, and plastic molds.
The Korean company expects to begin operations by October of next year.
The facility will be filling positions in human resources, finance, parts development, quality control, facility maintenance, injection, and paint production.

Gov. Brian Kemp stated: “We’re proud to welcome Ecoplastic to our growing ecosystem of manufacturers, logistics professionals, suppliers, and more, building on the record-breaking jobs and investments that are on their way to the Peach State.”
Gov. Brian Kemp added: “This great company … will find a welcoming community of hardworking Georgians and close proximity to our ports system.”

The Georgia Department of Economic Development worked with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, the Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia EMC, and the Technical College System of Georgia to land the project.

Two months ago, auto parts manufacturer Joon Georgia announced it will invest $317 million in a new manufacturing plant in Bulloch County that will create 630 jobs, making it the new Hyundai EV plant’s first confirmed supplier.
source_title: Bulloch County lands second auto parts supplier for new Hyundai EV plant - The Augusta Press

canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group Georgia EV Manufacturing Facility
facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp and Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung announced that Hyundai Motor Group will open its first fully dedicated electric vehicle (EV) and battery manufacturing facility in the State of Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group will invest $5.54 billion in opening a state-of-the-art U.S. smart factory at the Bryan County Megasite.
Non-affiliated Hyundai Motor Group suppliers will invest approximately another $1 billion in the project.
The project will deliver approximately 8,100 new jobs to Georgia’s coastal region.

Governor Kemp was joined by Jay Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, and José Muñoz, President and COO of Hyundai Motor Company, along with Hyundai Motor Group executives and state and local economic development leaders at the premier 2,923-acre site.
The megasite was purchased jointly by the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority (JDA) and the State less than a year ago.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that the announcement marks the largest economic development project in Georgia's history and aims to make the state a premier destination for quality companies creating jobs.

Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung shared plans to open their first dedicated full EV and battery manufacturing facilities in the U.S., partnering with Georgia to achieve shared goals of electrified mobility and sustainability.
Hyundai Motor Group expects to begin construction on the new facility in January 2023.
Full production is expected in the first half of 2025, with an annual capacity of 300,000 units.
Wages throughout the project will be well-paying advanced manufacturing positions competitive in the local market.
