Jaehoon (Jay) Chang stated that America embraces change and drives innovation, and the new EV plant will help meet growing demands of U.S. customers for leading edge design and zero-emissions vehicles.
Hyundai Motor Group cited speed-to-market, workforce, and Georgia’s ability to meet the company’s carbon neutrality standards as reasons for choosing the state.
Georgia is home to an existing network of Hyundai subsidiaries and suppliers.

The Bryan County Megasite was developed by the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor JDA over several years in partnership with the state to offer speed-to-market requirements that reduce barriers to speedy operations.
Industrial utilities are adjacent to the site, and extensive due diligence reports have been completed.

President and COO of Hyundai Motor Company José Muñoz expressed thrill at partnering with Governor Kemp and the Savannah Joint Economic Development Authority to bring a flexible EV smart factory to Bryan County.
Hyundai Motor Group is headquartered in Seoul, Korea, employs 250,000 people worldwide, and sells vehicles in 193 countries through 5,000 dealerships and showrooms.
The new Georgia facility will be highly connected, automated, and flexible, with plans to produce a diverse range of innovative full electric vehicles.
Details of production models will be shared at later dates.
Through the battery manufacturing facility, Hyundai Motor Group aims to establish a stable supply chain for EV battery and other EV components in the U.S. market.
When operations near the planned 2025 start, Hyundai Motor Group will seek local vendors and suppliers.

Carter Infinger, Chairman of the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority Board of Directors, called the announcement transformative, noting the jobs and investment will reverberate for years across Bryan, Bulloch, Chatham, and Effingham counties.
Bryan County is the fastest-growing county in Georgia, and the strategic purchase of the Bryan County Megasite in 2021 was the largest in state history.
The site is adjacent to Interstate 16 with immediate access from I-95 and I-16 to 250 major metro areas.

The site is less than 30 miles from the Port of Savannah, the single-largest and fastest-growing container terminal in the U.S., with two Class I rail facilities on-site provided by Georgia Central Railway.

Georgia Department of Transportation Commissioner Russell McMurry stated the news exemplifies Georgia’s collective focus on supporting the electric mobility future and the power of partnership and preparation.

Georgia Ports Authority Executive Director Griff Lynch noted the new location provides stellar access to global and domestic markets for both assembly components and finished vehicle marketing, endorsing Georgia as a dynamic center for cutting-edge manufacturing.
Georgia is the Southeastern leader for EV registrations per 1,000 registered automobiles and offers more EV charging outlets per capita than anywhere else in the Southeast.

Georgia’s Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance is a public-private-nonprofit initiative focused on growing the electric mobility ecosystem in the state and strengthening Georgia’s position in electrification-related manufacturing and innovation.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) was represented by Project Manager Taylor Walden and Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim, alongside the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor JDA, Georgia Department of Transportation, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that under Governor Kemp’s leadership, Georgia is at the forefront of electric mobility, and the announcement solidifies the state's spot at the vanguard of the EV transition.

Hyundai Motor Group plans to implement advanced intelligent manufacturing technologies currently under test at its innovation hub, including intelligent A.I.-equipped manufacturing and data-driven processes.
The plant will support Hyundai Motor Group’s efforts to achieve its carbon neutrality target through the active use of renewable energy for the facility.
Since 2020 alone, Georgia has announced more than 20 EV-related projects that will invest more than $13.54 billion and create nearly 18,000 jobs in the state.

links.context: Project RFIs, RFPs listing, and supply chain outreach information

source_title: Hyundai to invest $5.54B in Georgia at first fully dedicated EV manufacturing facility  - Grice Connect

entity_name: Bulloch County EV & Battery Supply Chain Projects
canonical_name: Development Authority of Bulloch County (DABC) & Partner Companies
facts: In 11 months from mid-February 2022 to the first week of 2023, four companies revealed plans to build manufacturing plants in Bulloch County.
Together, if all fulfill their promises to the county and state, they will eventually create more than 1,400 jobs.
Benjy Thompson is chief executive officer of the Development Authority of Bulloch County (DABC).
On Thursday, Jan. 5, the DABC board approved a memorandum of understanding for Ecoplastic America Corporation to build a $205 million plant.
The Ecoplastic plant promises to create 456 jobs, with both investment and job creation phased in over eight years.
The Ecoplastic plant will be built on a DABC-owned site south of Statesboro.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp made the official announcement from Atlanta while the DABC met in Statesboro.
Thompson stated that since February 17 of last year, announced projects include 1,407 jobs and $897 million in capital investments.
The Ecoplastic Corporation plant will make injection-molded plastic exterior and interior parts for Hyundai Motor Group’s electric vehicles.
Two of Bulloch County’s four new industries announced in the past 11 months will be direct suppliers to Hyundai Motor Group’s EV and battery “meta plant.”
Hyundai’s “mega site” is being built in Bryan County about five miles from Bulloch’s southeastern boundary.
The other two industries are not directly connected to the Hyundai plant but will make materials that can be used in battery or vehicle production.
Bulloch is one of four counties that teamed up to land the Hyundai plant itself, projected to employ 8,100 people to make Hyundai, Kia and Genesis branded vehicles.
Bryan, Bulloch, Effingham and Chatham counties together participate in the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority (JDA).
The JDA, in partnership with the state, closed on the purchase of the 2,284-acre site in July 2021.

Five years before that, regional officials had made preliminary deals for the tracts that eventually became the mega site, originally in an effort to attract a Volvo vehicle factory that instead went to Charleston, South Carolina.
Thompson said Bulloch County is, for the mega site, in a very good location for suppliers, having been just far enough away but just close enough.
This has allowed the DABC to sell land at a discount to the latest announced industries instead of giving it away as an incentive for them to locate here.
Aspen Aerogels was announced Feb. 17, 2022.
Construction is now well underway on a 90-acre site in the DSDA’s Bruce Yawn Commerce Park along U.S. Highway 301 at the I-16 interchange.
The company’s commitment is for a $325 million investment and at least 250 manufacturing jobs.
Aspen’s plant will make aerogel insulation, which is used in and around electric vehicle batteries.
Joon Georgia is a unit of established metal auto body parts manufacturer Ajin USA, a supplier to Hyundai Motor Group.
Ajin has committed to invest $317 million and eventually employ 630 people at a plant to be built on an 83-acre site, also at Bruce Yawn Commerce Park.
Gov. Kemp made the announcement Nov. 7.
The DABC itself revealed plans Dec. 21 for revalyu Resources, headquartered in Germany, to build a PET plastic-bottle recycling plant on a 43-acre site in Gateway Regional Industrial Park.
It is expected to create 71 jobs and require a $50 million investment in Phase 1, the only phase counted in the totals.
Automotive materials are one of the many applications revalyu lists for recycled PET, also known as polyester.
Ecoplastic’s site is a 78-acre tract along U.S. Highway 301 but not in either of the named industrial parks.
Instead it takes most of a tract measuring a little over 100 acres, previously farmland, that the DABC bought as a 52-acre parcel in the summer and a separate almost 53-acre parcel in November.
The same month the Bulloch County Board of Commissioners rezoned the combined tract for industrial uses.
It is 1.3 miles north of the interchange but lies along the Statesboro city water, sewer and natural gas lines that extend to the interchange and commerce park.

The development authority had the commissioners rezone a couple more, adjoining parcels for light industry this week as the authority acts to purchase them to have more land available for further prospects.
Under a lease that would eventually become a purchase, Ecoplastic will pay $2,032,000 for its site, with this price considered a $2.2 million discount.
Steve Rushing is attorney to the DABC.

In exchange for the phased-in job creation and its investment in the factory, the company will receive a 100% abatement from the county government portion of property tax for 10 years, followed by 50% abatement for five years.
However, Ecoplastic Corporation would be expected to pay the school system and fire protection taxes from the beginning.
Both the discounted land price and the partial tax abatement are linked to phased goals for job creation and investment over each of the first eight years.
If the company did not meet those goals, it could owe the DABC or the county more money under a “clawback” provision.
With many of the jobs among the 1,400 total being phased in over a period of years, not all of the demand for workers will be immediate.
But with the larger meta plant also in commuting distance, the development authority is part of discussions for where the workers will come from and where they will live.

DABC Chair Billy Allen said the area is going to grow because of the opportunities that are being presented to the general public, achieving the mission of having industry come with capital investment and good-paying jobs.
Allen is both a contractor with Snella Enterprises and a real estate agent with Berkshire Hathaway Kennedy Realty.
He reported hearing firsthand in his work lots of interest in folks wanting to move to Statesboro and the surrounding areas.
source_title: Four new industries now promise 1,400-plus jobs in Bulloch County - Statesboro Herald

facts: On July 28, 2023, VinFast officially broke ground on its electric vehicle manufacturing site in Raleigh, North Carolina.
The manufacturing site is located within the Triangle Innovation Point in Chatham County, North Carolina.
This project marks the first electric vehicle manufacturing facility in North Carolina and the largest economic development initiative in the state's history.

The groundbreaking ceremony was attended by North Carolina Governor Roy Cooper, Vietnamese Ambassador to the US Nguyen Quoc Dzung, representatives from state and federal governments, and VinFast’s partners, customers, industry experts, and journalists.
Phase 1 of the plant involves a total investment of up to $2 billion USD.
The facility spans an area of approximately 1,800 acres.
The plant is divided into five main production areas: a body shop, general assembly, press shop, paint shop, and an energy center.
Other functional facilities will also be located on the factory premises.
In its initial phase, the factory will focus on building VinFast’s VF 7, VF 8, and VF 9 electric vehicles.
The expected production capacity for the facility is 150,000 vehicles per year.
The supplier base for components and materials will be primarily concentrated in the US, Vietnam, and a few other countries.
Future expansion and updates for the factory will be identified in the next phase.
VinFast has appointed Clayco as the general contractor and construction management company to lead the project.
VinFast will begin the bidding process soon to select subcontractors for the construction project.
North Carolina Governor Roy Cooper stated that North Carolina’s skilled and diverse workforce is the greatest strength and that the state has the largest manufacturing workforce in the Southeast.
Governor Roy Cooper noted that the VinFast factory will create thousands of good-paying jobs in the state, along with a healthier environment as more electric vehicles take to the road.
Madam Le Thi Thu Thuy, CEO of VinFast, stated that the event marks a new milestone affirming VinFast’s commitment to the North American market.

Madam Le Thi Thu Thuy added that the project will create thousands of jobs, contribute to North Carolina’s economic development, bring additional economic benefits to consumers, and help develop the North Carolina community during production and business operations.
The VinFast electric vehicle manufacturing plant is expected to commence production in 2025.
The facility will create an ecosystem of suppliers and thousands of jobs.
This manufacturing complex is VinFast’s first electric vehicle factory outside of Vietnam.
The project plays an important role as VinFast strives to reach world-class levels of sustainable mobility on a global scale.
VinFast is a member of Vingroup, established in 2017, and envisions driving the global smart electric vehicle revolution.
VinFast owns a state-of-the-art automotive manufacturing complex with up to 90% automation in Hai Phong, Vietnam.
source_title: VINFAST BREAKS GROUND AT NORTH CAROLINA ELECTRIC VEHICLE FACILITY | VinFast

facts: Provided counsel to Panasonic Energy of North America in locating its first stand-alone lithium-ion battery cell manufacturing facility in De Soto, Kansas.
The Panasonic facility is expected to cost over $4 billion to construct and equip.
At full production, the Panasonic factory is expected to employ approximately 4,000 people.
Provided counsel to Tesla Motors, Inc., an electric automobile manufacturer, in locating a lithium-ion battery manufacturing facility in Storey County, Nevada.
The Tesla Gigafactory will cost approximately $10 billion to fully construct and equip.
At full production, the Tesla Gigafactory will employ approximately 6,500 people.
Provided incentives and real estate counsel to EnerSys for its industrial and defense lithium-ion battery cell manufacturing facility outside of Greenville, South Carolina.
The EnerSys facility was projected to create 500 jobs and generate capital investment of $500 million.
Provided counsel to several solar developers on utility-scale projects around the southeastern United States regarding tax incentives, site control, land use, and financing matters.

Provided counsel to Canoo Technologies in the identification and selection of locations for its manufacturing, research and development, customer service, and headquarter facilities in Oklahoma and Arkansas.
Canoo estimates investing approximately $1.5 billion and creating over 2,500 jobs for its facilities in Oklahoma and Arkansas.
Provided counsel to VinFast in negotiating incentives for its electric vehicle assembly and battery manufacturing campus in Chatham County, North Carolina, outside of Raleigh.
Provided incentives counsel to Volkswagen Group of America for its $600 million expansion of the Volkswagen Chattanooga manufacturing plant.
The Volkswagen Chattanooga expansion project will add 2,000 jobs to the plant.
Provided counsel to Spanish automotive supplier Truck & Wheel Group in locating a $30 million tire and wheel assembly facility in Tuscaloosa County, Alabama, which is their first facility in the U.S.
The Truck & Wheel Group facility is one of the most technologically advanced assembly facilities of its kind in the world.
source_title: Experience | Matthew A. Hinshaw | People | Bradley

facts: Vietnamese automaker announced plans to build a plant in North Carolina to manufacture electric vehicles.
The company promises to bring 7,500 jobs and end the state’s streak of near-misses for landing carmakers.
VinFast will build its first North American plant in Chatham County southwest of Raleigh.
Production is expected to start in 2024.
The company expects to employ 7,500 workers by 2027 with average salaries of $51,000, according to the state Commerce Department.
Gov. Roy Cooper stated that VinFast’s transformative project will bring many good jobs to the state and help reduce greenhouse gas emissions as more electric vehicles take to the road.
The company is expected to invest $4 billion in building the plant on a nearly 2,000-acre (800-hectare) site about a 30-minute drive southwest of Raleigh.
The plant is planned to have a capacity to manufacture 150,000 cars per year.
The state has agreed to use job development grants to reimburse $316 million over 32 years if the company hits job-creation and investment goals.
The state is also planning to provide as much as $450 million for site preparation, road improvements, and other infrastructure work.
A release from the state Commerce Department said the manufacturer considered sites in 12 states before narrowing its search to North Carolina and a site in Savannah, Georgia.
Workforce, incentives, and site preparation were key factors in VinFast's choice of location.

VinFast Global CEO Le Thi Thu Thuy stated that North Carolina’s strong commitments in building a clean energy economy, fighting climate change, and reducing greenhouse gas emissions in transportation make it an ideal location for developing premium, smart, and environmentally friendly EVs.

The United Auto Workers (UAW) union plans to organize workers at the new VinFast plant, as well as other electric vehicle and battery factories locating in Southern states, according to union President Ray Curry.
The UAW has funding available now to start organizing efforts for these plants.
The UAW already has a presence in North Carolina.
Union President Ray Curry stated, “We believe that we are the preferred union,” and noted their goal is to represent any auto work assembly in the country, especially EVs transforming right now.
The UAW will also try to organize Ford and General Motors battery plants in Kentucky and Tennessee, as well as factories run by Tesla and electric vehicle startup Rivian.
Both Tesla and Rivian have plants that were represented by the union under previous owners.
Such organizing campaigns could cost tens of millions of dollars depending on their duration.

Landing the car manufacturing plant is considered a major accomplishment for Gov. Roy Cooper, legislative leaders, and economic recruiters following a series of disappointments stretching back decades.
In the early 1990s, North Carolina lost out to South Carolina for BMW’s first full-service factory outside of Germany.
Later, North Carolina lost out to Alabama for Mercedes-Benz’s SUV plant.
More recently, the state fell short in competing for new plants by Tesla and Rivian Automotive.
Freightliner already operates truck production plants in North Carolina.
Volvo Trucks’ North America headquarters is based in Greensboro.
The VinFast plant acquisition is the latest in a string of significant economic victories for the state over the past 12 months.
Apple announced plans last April to build its first East Coast campus in Research Triangle Park between Raleigh and Durham, creating 3,000 new jobs over the next decade.

Toyota revealed in December it will build a $1.3 billion battery plant near Greensboro that will employ at least 1,750 people and help fulfill plans to drastically increase electric vehicle sales in the U.S.

Boom Supersonic announced in January that Greensboro would be the home for its first full-scale manufacturing facility for next-generation supersonic passenger jets, with a goal of more than 2,400 new jobs by 2032.
VinFast is part of Vietnamese conglomerate Vingroup, which is providing capital to expand electric vehicle sales to the U.S. and eventually Europe.
The company already sells vehicles with internal combustion engines and electric powertrains in Vietnam.
In November, the company introduced the VF e35 midsize and VF e36 large electric SUVs at the Los Angeles Auto Show without revealing prices.
The company’s former chief executive said last year that VinFast plans to start taking orders in the spring and delivering vehicles in the U.S. in the fall.
Initially, the SUVs would be built at a new factory in Vietnam, but VinFast was planning to build a U.S. factory that would start producing in the second half of 2024.
VinFast, with a U.S. headquarters in Los Angeles, plans to sell vehicles through its own network of stores, with much of the order process done online.
The company already has plans for 60 stores in California alone, along with some service centers and mobile service.
source_title: Vietnamese carmaker to build electric vehicles in Chatham County | Salisbury Post

facts: The Corridors of Opportunity event was hosted by the Triangle Business Journal on June 23rd.

Panelists included Karen Howard, chair of the Chatham County Board of Commissioners; Michael Cline, North Carolina’s state demographer; Michael Smith, president of Chatham Economic Development Corp.; and Julian “Bubba” Rawl, co-owner of Chatham Park and Preston Development Company.
The event was sponsored by MOSAIC and Chatham Park.
The event was held at the Chatham County Agriculture & Conference Center in Pittsboro.
Growth over the past decade now accounts for 40% of the total Chatham County population.
Major companies such as Vietnamese carmaker VinFast plan to build substantial manufacturing facilities in the area.
North Carolina-based silicon carbide company Wolfspeed plans to build a substantial manufacturing facility in the area.
Margaret Chadwick, chief human resources officer at Wolfspeed, stated there is much more opportunity for collaborations and partnerships than competition.

Julian “Bubba” Rawl and partner Tim Smith were attracted to the Pittsboro region by its beauty, landscape, downtown, culture, and history, aiming to create amenities that make Chatham Park a special place.
Social media and local news media amplified buzz from the stage and audience at the event.
The Chatham News & Record reported on Michael Cline’s facts and figures regarding the county’s population growth.
source_title: Smart Growth Stressed at Corridors of Opportunity Event - MOSAIC at Chatham Park

facts: Volkswagen recommits to a 2022 electric vehicle assembly launch in Chattanooga despite a ruling against its supplier.
A Volkswagen of America spokesman says a ruling by a trade panel against a battery supplier for electric vehicles to be built in Chattanooga won't impact a planned 2022 production launch.
Company spokesman Mike Tolbert said the company is continuing to analyze the ruling from the U.S. International Trade Commission (ITC) and its impact on the automaker.
source_title: Volkswagen recommits to 2022 electric vehicle assembly in Chattanooga despite ruling against suppler | Chattanooga Times Free Press

facts: SK Battery America Inc. laid off nearly 1,000 workers at a manufacturing plant northeast of Atlanta on Friday.
The layoffs occurred amid automakers’ changing electrification plans and uncertain consumer demand for EVs.
Friday marked the last working day for 958 plant employees, representing about 37% of its workforce.
The reduction was reported via a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) notice filed by human resources chief Chuck Moore.
Impacted workers will be paid through May 6.
The Commerce plant will continue to employ approximately 1,600 workers.
SK opened the $2.6 billion battery plant in Commerce, Georgia, in January 2022.
The facility notably supplied batteries for the Ford F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck.
Ford announced plans to cancel the fully electric version of the F-150 Lightning in December.
The U.S. electric vehicle market is currently at a standstill due to the Trump administration steering federal support away from electrification.
Federal policy is shifting toward more lax automotive emissions rules and broader support for oil and gas industries.
SK Americas spokesperson Joe Guy Collier stated the workforce reduction aligns operations with current market conditions.
Collier affirmed SK Battery America remains committed to Georgia and building a robust U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing.
SK is actively pursuing future customers, including opportunities in the Battery Energy Storage System sector.
The City of Commerce and Jackson County commission chair did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
Ford scrapped the fully-electric F-150 Lightning in December in favor of an extended-range version.
A Ford spokesperson declined to comment on supplier personnel actions.
SK and Ford previously invested a combined $11.4 billion in joint battery plants across the U.S.
SK ended its joint venture with Ford in December.
SK also supplies batteries to Volkswagen.
Georgia U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff stated that the layoffs represent lost battery manufacturing jobs and blamed Trump’s policies for hurting Georgia’s economy.
SK has invested significantly in Jackson County, Georgia, as automakers developed EV plans supported by the Biden administration's domestic supply chain efforts.
In June 2020, SK announced plans to invest $940 million to expand its battery manufacturing presence in Atlanta.
Gov. Brian Kemp’s office stated the 2020 expansion would create 600 jobs.
SK and Hyundai are jointly building a $5 billion battery factory near Cartersville, northwest of Atlanta.
Georgia has attracted other major EV manufacturing investments, including Rivian’s $5 billion factory and Hyundai’s $7.6 billion factory complex.
Few states benefited more than Georgia from Biden’s Inflation Reduction Act, which accelerated green energy project development.
Georgia announced 33 additional green energy projects by the end of 2024, the most nationwide according to environmental group E2.
Total investments in Georgia's green energy projects topped $20 billion, pledging more than 25,000 jobs.
Qcells, a unit of South Korea’s Hanwha Solutions, resumed normal production on Friday after temporarily reducing hours and pay due to U.S. customs detaining imported solar panel components.
A spokesperson for Gov. Kemp’s office expressed gratitude for the remaining workforce at SK’s factory.
Georgia has announced over 219,700 new jobs since 2019, according to a state spokesperson.
EV demand is still growing but has not met automakers’ recent ambitious expectations.
EVs accounted for about 8% of new vehicle sales in the U.S. in 2025, matching the previous year's rate.
Automakers are reevaluating multibillion-dollar electrification plans as financial losses mount and consumer demand shifts.
Manufacturers including Ford, General Motors, Stellantis, and others have canceled factory and investment plans, laid off workers, and pivoted to hybrid and plug-in hybrid vehicles.
Consumers are increasingly favoring hybrids and efficient gasoline vehicles due to concerns over EV driving range and charging infrastructure availability.
Under President Donald Trump, Congress eliminated tax credits of up to $7,500 for consumers purchasing new or used EVs.
The administration announced plans to weaken fuel economy and greenhouse gas emissions rules for automakers, removing federal incentives for cleaner vehicle fleets.
source_title: SK lays off nearly 1,000 workers at Commerce plant amid cooling automaker EV plans

facts: SK Battery America terminated 958 positions at its Commerce facility on Friday.
The layoffs were cited due to a cooling EV market and changing manufacturer priorities.
The Commerce plant represents a $2.6 billion investment.
The plant previously supplied batteries for the Ford F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck.
Ford announced plans to cancel the fully electric version of the F-150 Lightning in December.
Impacted workers will receive severance pay through May 6.
The final day of work for the laid-off employees was Friday.
A Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) notice detailing the layoffs was filed by human resources chief Chuck Moore.
SK Battery America opened the Commerce battery facility in January 2022.
Federal policy shifts include the Trump administration steering support away from electrification earlier this year.
The Trump administration has opted for more lax automotive emissions policies and a broader agenda supporting oil and gas industries.
Congress eliminated consumer tax credits of up to $7,500 for the purchase of new or used EVs.
The administration announced plans to weaken fuel economy and greenhouse gas emissions rules.
These regulatory changes essentially eliminate federal incentives for auto companies to prioritize cleaner vehicle fleets.
SK Battery America stated it remains committed to Georgia and its U.S. operations despite the layoffs.
SK Battery America is pursuing future customers, including in the Battery Energy Storage System arena.
Sen. Jon Ossoff blamed the current White House administration for the job losses, stating Trump's war on electric vehicles is hurting Georgia's economy.
Sen. Raphael Warnock criticized the administration for slashing clean energy investments and imposing harmful tariff taxes that make manufacturing in Georgia more expensive.
source_title: SK Battery America lays off nearly 1,000 workers at Commerce plant - AOL

facts: SK Innovation plans to invest 1.98 trillion South Korean won (US$1.01 billion) to establish an electric vehicle (EV) batteries production base in Jackson County, Georgia.
The project aims to establish SK Innovation in the US, which is described as “the largest battleground of global automakers”.
The plant will be built on a 278-acre site in Commerce City.
The facility is expected to have an annual production capacity of 9.8 gigawatt-hours.
The announcement confirms speculation of such a project reported earlier this year.
The state of Georgia will provide administrative support, including education, training, and employment programmes.
SK Innovation is part of the South Korean conglomerate, the SK Group.
SK Innovation will establish SK Battery America (working title) as a subsidiary to handle its US investments, both current and future.
Establishing the subsidiary should enable the SK Group to avoid a potential 25% US auto tariff that the Trump White House has threatened to impose.
The likelihood of the tariff was made more likely by General Motors’ recent decision to idle five factories in North America.
Chief executive Kim Joon stated that SK Innovation has been actively seeking a production base to secure competitiveness and increase orders in the battery business, a major global market.
Kim Joon added that with their business at the hub of the global auto industry, they will grow as a top player in the battery business.

Georgia Governor Nathan Deal commented that SK Innovation’s decision to make this significant investment and create more than 2,000 jobs in Jackson County reflects Georgia’s reputation as a top destination for international businesses, especially those in the automotive supply sector.
The project is expected to create more than 2,000 jobs in Jackson County.
Construction is expected to start on the plant in early 2019.
Mass supply from the facility is expected from 2022.
source_title: SK Innovation battery project targets US automotive market - Best Magazine

facts: Korean automotive supplier Dongwon Autopart Technology plans to invest $30 million into a new production facility in South Georgia.
The project promises to create 200 jobs.
Dongwon Autopart Technology manufactures parts including door, seat, and battery frames.
The new facility will be Dongwon's second Georgia location, situated in Emanuel County.
Emanuel County is located approximately 175 miles southeast of Atlanta.
Gov. Brian Kemp announced the project on Wednesday.
Ken Warnock, CEO of the Swainsboro-Emanuel County Chamber of Commerce and Development Authority, stated that it is literally the biggest new industrial development project ever in Emanuel County.
Emanuel County has historically been known as a hub for metal working.
Dongwon Autopart Technology was drawn to the county specifically because of its local metal-working expertise.
source_title: Auto supplier to invest $30M in rural Georgia county

facts: A South Korean auto parts supplier will invest more than $240 million to build a transmission assembly plant in West Point, GA.
Hyundai Transys plans to build the new plant next to its existing factory in West Point.
The site is also home to Kia Motors’ assembly plant.
Kia and Hyundai Transys are both parts of Hyundai Motor Group, the Korean auto conglomerate that sells cars under the Kia and Hyundai brands.
The new Hyundai Transys plant will build eight-speed transmissions.
The 620,000-square-foot plant is expected to create 165 new jobs.
The new facility will boost the supplier’s employment in Georgia to more than 1,700 people.
Many supplier plants are clustered near Kia plant in West Point and the Hyundai Motor Manufacturing plant in Montgomery, AL.
In Georgia's Troup County alone, suppliers to the two OEM facilities employ more than 4,300 people.
source_title: Hyundai Supplier Plans $240 Million Assembly Plant in Georgia | 2020-12-08  | ASSEMBLY

facts: Hyundai is constructing an electric vehicle assembly factory near Savannah, Georgia.
Company officials broke ground on the facility in October 2022.
The project is on pace to begin rolling finished vehicles off the assembly line by fall, roughly two years after the start.
Test production is already underway in the main plant as construction finishes, according to a July 22 report by the Atlanta Journal-Constitution.
Hyundai has hired and is training its initial shift of assembly workers.
Hyundai has a stated goal of introducing 17 new electric vehicle models and manufacturing 1.44 million battery-powered cars annually by 2030.
Company officials refer to the facility as the "Metaplant," reflecting their view of "the future of mobility."
Carter Infinger, an elected leader in Bryan County, Georgia, where the plant is located, stated, "Our friends from Korea only know one speed: Full throttle."
Hyundai is investing $7.6 billion at the 2,906-acre site.
The assembly facility plans to produce up to 300,000 vehicles per year.
An EV battery manufacturing plant and a worker training center are also under construction on the site and are scheduled to open in 2025.
Altogether, the project is the largest in Georgia economic development history.
The property is located along Interstate 16 behind a truck weigh station near the town of Ellabell, approximately 25 miles west of downtown Savannah.
A highway interchange is located just west of the site, and another exit is planned immediately to the east with a projected opening date in 2027.
The property was formerly known as the Bryan County Megasite and was first prepared for development a decade ago when Volvo considered opening a factory in the Savannah area.
State officials expanded the size of the site in 2021 after selling part of another nearby industrial parcel to Amazon.
Hyundai inquired about the property in January 2022 and announced plans to build the factory in southeast Georgia within five months.
Site preparation began in August 2022, and vertical construction started in early 2023.
The Metaplant will launch operations with Hyundai's best-selling IONIQ 5 EV model.
The assembly facility is designed for flexibility, moving vehicles through the factory on a conveyor belt.
Automated guided vehicles will deliver parts and perform tasks alongside workers, whom Hyundai refers to as "metapros."
The facility's design allows for the production of more than one vehicle model on the same assembly line.
The plant will eventually manufacture vehicles for Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis brands.
Hyundai already operates two other factories in the region: one producing Hyundais in Montgomery, Alabama, and another producing Kias in West Point, Georgia.
The West Point, Georgia facility recently began production of the Kia EV9 automobile.
Hyundai is also building a second battery factory near Cartersville, Georgia.
The metro Savannah facility could produce hybrid and plug-in hybrid cars in addition to EVs.
The Bryan County onsite battery manufacturing facility is intended to shorten the supply chain.
As part of a $1.8 billion incentives package signed with state and local officials, Hyundai agreed to employ 8,500 workers at the Savannah factory.
The battery plant near Cartersville is expected to hire an additional 3,500 people.
Hyundai began hiring within months of announcing the project.
The company reached 500 production workers as of February and has since grown to over 850 total employees.
More than 80 percent of the initial hires and 96 percent of production line employees live within 60 miles of the factory site.
This hiring occurred despite a historically low unemployment rate of 3 percent for the greater Savannah area, according to the Georgia Department of Labor.
Hyundai recently paused recruitment of assembly workers but continues to seek engineers and other professionals.
The automaker will resume hiring production personnel once it expands manufacturing beyond one shift.
Hyundai is training its workers through Georgia Quick Start, a state program specializing in advanced manufacturing.
Training also occurs at coastal Georgia technical colleges, including Savannah Tech and Ogeechee Tech in Statesboro.
Georgia Quick Start operates a training center in the Savannah suburb of Pooler and has leased additional space to handle Hyundai hires.
Hyundai's supplier network spans southeast Georgia and other areas, including metro Atlanta.
Seventeen suppliers manufacturing components ranging from seats to climate control parts have been announced, with 11 located near the Metaplant.
The supplier firms are projected to employ about 7,000 workers and invest $2.7 billion in new facilities.
Hyundai maintained its rapid construction pace despite EV sales underperforming expectations, partly due to federal electric vehicle incentive restrictions.
Only EVs manufactured in the United States qualify for the $7,500 purchase rebate incentive, though there is a loophole for leased vehicles.
The IONIQ 5s manufactured in Georgia will be eligible for the rebates once the battery factory becomes operational next year.
The Georgia-made Hyundais are intended for domestic sale rather than export.
The Georgia Ports Authority auto terminal in Brunswick, the second-busiest in the country, does not expect a Hyundai-related increase in business once the factory comes online.
The Georgia Ports Authority is handling imports of parts and materials for the Hyundai plant through its Savannah terminals.
source_title: Construction Nearing Its Finish at Hyundai EV Metaplant Near Savannah : CEG

facts: Hyundai’s $7.6 billion EV plant in Georgia gained its 18th supplier as the automaker builds an extensive US supply chain.
The plant is slated to open as early as October and will produce US-made Hyundai electric vehicles, starting with the updated 2025 IONIQ 5.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp announced that Shinsung Petrochemical will invest $11.2 million in a new manufacturing facility in Toombs County.
Shinsung is a leading auto sealant company and will be a key supplier for Hyundai’s new Metaplant in Bryan County.
Governor Kemp stated, “We are excited that Shinsung is joining that growing network of suppliers locating in rural communities.”
The Shinsung facility is scheduled to open in 2025 and will be the first in Toombs County, creating over 30 new jobs.
Shinsung is the 18th supplier to invest in Hyundai’s new Georgia EV plant.
Hyundai is investing $7.6 billion to develop the facility, directly creating 8,500 jobs in the state.
Another $5 billion EV battery plant with SK On is being built in Bartow County, GA, creating an additional 3,500 positions.
Since 2018, Georgia has attracted over $28 billion in investments to build electric vehicles, batteries, and other clean energy initiatives.
The funding has already attracted over 36,000 new jobs in the state.
According to a recent Center for Automotive Research study, Hyundai’s plant will create more than 58,200 new jobs while attracting over $12.6 billion in investments in Georgia.
The first model to roll off the assembly line will be the updated 2025 IONIQ 5, which will have more range, a sleek new design, and a Tesla NACS charging port.
Hyundai will also launch its first three-row electric SUV, the IONIQ 9, which will also be built at the facility.
Initially, vehicles built at the facility will only be eligible for a partial $3,750 tax credit.
Hyundai expects its US-made EVs to qualify for the full $7,500 once the battery unit comes online.
Hyundai’s 2025 IONIQ 5 gained a new XRT variant designed for off-road use with “XRT-exclusive” front and rear bumpers, side skirts, and other aggressive design elements.
The AWD Limited SE/SEL XRT trim has a targeted new range of +250 to +280 miles.
The RWD SE/SEL/Limited Standard Range trims have targeted new ranges of +310 miles and +240 miles, respectively.
Hyundai topped Ford and GM in US electric vehicle sales in the second quarter.
According to Motor Intelligence, Hyundai and Kia accounted for 10% of US electric vehicle sales in Q2, outpacing Ford (7.4%) and GM (6.3%).
With US production and advanced new EVs on the way, Hyundai wants an even bigger share of the market.
source_title: Hyundai's massive new Georgia EV plant adds its 18th supplier

facts: On Nov 14, Toyota Industries announced an expansion of its Georgia presence in Pendergrass, creating 250 new jobs with a $69 million investment.
On Nov 01, 100 new jobs and a $24 million investment were announced in Georgia.
On Oct 31, 460 new jobs and a $176 million investment were announced in Georgia.
On Oct 11, 240 new jobs were announced with no specified investment amount in Georgia.
On Aug 31, Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution announced an additional $2 billion investment in Bryan County, creating 400 new jobs.
On Aug 29, automotive supplier Daesol Ausys Georgia announced a $72 million investment in Harris County (West Point), creating 140 new jobs.
On Jul 12, a $200 million investment was announced with no specified job count in Georgia.
On Jun 23, 160 new jobs and a $72 million investment were announced in Georgia.
On Jun 07, Woory Industrial Co. announced plans to open an EV parts manufacturing and R&D facility in Laurens County (Dublin), creating 130 jobs with an $18 million investment.
On May 23, Gov. Kemp announced that automotive supplier Hanon Systems will create 160 new jobs in Bulloch County (Statesboro) with a $40 million investment.
On May 18, Anovion Technologies announced its $800 million Georgia plant in Bainbridge, aimed at securing the U.S. graphite supply, creating 400 jobs.
On Apr 12, a facility with 500 MW capacity was announced with no specified job count or investment amount in Georgia.
On Apr 11, 180 new jobs and a $72 million investment were announced in Georgia.
On Mar 22, Gov. Kemp announced that Qcells supplier Hanwha Advanced Materials Georgia will invest $147 million in a Cartersville manufacturing facility, creating 160 jobs.
On Mar 07, 402 new jobs and a $67 million investment were announced in Georgia.
On Feb 21, 740 new jobs and a $300 million investment were announced in Georgia.
On Feb 01, Gov. Kemp announced that Seoyon E-HWA joins the list of Hyundai suppliers, nearly doubling its Georgia employment to 740 jobs with a $76 million investment in Bloomingdale.
On Jan 26, SK Battery America announced plans to open a U.S. IT center in Roswell, creating 200 high-tech jobs with a $19 million investment.
On Jan 11, Qcells announced bringing 2,000 jobs and a solar manufacturing campus to Bartow County (Dalton) as part of a $2.5 billion expansion, with an initial phase in Dalton creating 510 jobs.
On Jan 11, Qcells announced a $2.5 billion investment for a solar manufacturing campus in Cartersville, Georgia, creating 2,000 jobs with a capacity of 3.3 GW.

On Jan 05, Gov. Kemp announced that longtime Kia and HMG supplier Ecoplastics Corporation will build a manufacturing facility in Bulloch County (Register), creating 456 jobs with a $205 million investment.
On Dec 08, Hyundai Motor Group and SK On announced plans to build an EV battery facility in Bartow County, creating 3,500 jobs with a $4 billion investment.
On Nov 23, 1,500 new jobs and a $926 million investment were announced in Georgia.
On Nov 14, FREYR Battery announced plans for a manufacturing center in Coweta County, creating 723 jobs with a $2.57 billion investment.
On Nov 09, Joon Georgia, Inc announced plans for a $317 million investment in Statesboro to supply the Hyundai EV plant, creating 630 jobs.
source_title: Georgia | E2

facts: Georgia Power unveiled plans to invest significantly in new generation capacity and battery energy storage systems (BESS) across the state, particularly in Floyd and Bartow counties.
Georgia Power is constructing a standalone Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) in Floyd County known as the Hammond BESS.
The Hammond BESS facility has a capacity of 57.5 MW and is being developed on the site of the retired Plant Hammond, repurposing its transmission capacity for grid support.
The Hammond BESS project is expected to go into commercial operation by November 2026.
Georgia Power plans two new battery storage installations in Bartow County called Bowen Phases 1 & 2, totaling 500 MW.
The Bowen Phases 1 & 2 BESS projects are designed to enhance grid stability during peak usage by storing energy during off-peak hours.
Georgia Power proposes adding two combined-cycle natural gas units with a combined capacity of 1,482 MW in Bartow County.
These new natural gas plants are part of a larger initiative to deliver nearly 3,700 MW of natural gas generation statewide for improved reliability.
In filings with the Georgia Public Service Commission, Georgia Power requested certification for 10 additional BESS facilities across the state totaling approximately 3,022.5 MW.
The 10 additional BESS facilities are being sited in counties including Bibb, Lowndes, Floyd, Cherokee, and Laurens.
Alongside the new BESS facilities, Georgia Power is pursuing additional solar-plus-storage proposals and natural gas generation contracts amounting to 2,821 MW.
The company projects about 8,200 MW of electrical load growth by 2030.
Peak demand is expected to rise by 2,200 MW versus the company's 2023 forecast.
Plant Hammond in Floyd County had an 800 MW capacity and shut down in 2019.
Plant Bowen, located in Bartow County, remains in operation with around 3,376–3,499 MW of coal generation.
Plant Bowen is recognized as one of the dirtiest plants in the nation.
Regulators will revisit Plant Bowen's future and potential retirement timeline by 2025.
Georgia Power has already built and operates 16 solar farms across Georgia, including numerous projects in North Georgia.
Georgia Power also operates solar facilities at military installations across the state.
More utility-scale solar capacity is still being sought through bids and contracts to support the utility's clean energy goals.
source_title: Georgia Power Projects Coming to Bartow & Floyd Counties – What You Need to Know

facts: HSAGP Energy LLC is a joint venture company between Hyundai Motor Group and SK On.
The joint venture has established an electric vehicle battery cell manufacturer located in Kingston, Georgia.
source_title: HSBMA Battery Manufacturing America | Manufacturers - General

facts: Georgia leads the country in post-Inflation Reduction Act clean energy factory investments with over $17 billion, according to a Canary Media analysis.
South Carolina ranks second with $9.3 billion in clean energy factory investments, followed by Michigan with $8.1 billion.
The Biden administration is betting on renewing the American industrial machine to help decarbonize the U.S. electric grid by 2035 and power a mass transition to electric vehicles.
Incentives in the Inflation Reduction Act spurred the emergence of a new Battery Belt spanning Michigan, Indiana, Ohio, Kentucky, Tennessee, Georgia, and the Carolinas.
Former Michigan Governor Jennifer Granholm picked EV and battery production as a strategic growth industry over a decade ago to help Michigan recover from the Great Recession.
The Kings Mountain Mine in North Carolina was opened by Foote Mineral Company in 1938, tapping the Carolina Tin-Spodumene Belt across Cleveland and Gaston counties.
Historically, the Kings Mountain mine produced more hard-rock lithium than pretty much anywhere else in the world before shutting down in 1988.
Livent's Hallman-Beam Mine near Bessemer City, North Carolina, shuttered in 1996.
Albemarle, which now owns the Kings Mountain mine, is conducting economic-feasibility studies to determine whether to revive the long-dormant operation.

Newcomer Piedmont Lithium hopes to open a brand-new mine in the area, but its initial public outreach campaign failed due to neighbor concerns over mining explosions and potential destabilization of local water wells.
Global lithium suppliers Albemarle and Livent still process lithium near Charlotte, North Carolina.

Energy Secretary Jennifer Granholm stated that the U.S. should do more to extract critical minerals domestically but emphasized that new mining operations need reforms for better environmental practices and respect for local residents, especially tribal communities.

Former Charlotte Mayor Jennifer Roberts noted that state regulators often lack resources to police industrial polluters but highlighted Vulcan Materials as a good neighbor that set aside wildlife habitat and coordinated blasting with residents.
South Korea's SK Corporation opened a $2.6 billion battery factory in Commerce, Georgia, in early 2022, before the Inflation Reduction Act was passed.
SK ships batteries from its Georgia plant to Volkswagen's electric ID.4 production site in Chattanooga, Tennessee, and supplies Ford's F-150 Lightning.
SK makes enough batteries at its Georgia facility to power 300,000 EVs annually, according to company statements.

Georgia Governor Brian Kemp praised SK in January for hitting employment targets two years ahead of schedule, with 2,600 people already working there and the number expected to hit 3,000 by year's end.
SK is one of the largest single-site employers in Georgia.

Norwegian company Freyr opted to fast-track development of a second factory in Coweta County, southwest of Atlanta, to take advantage of Inflation Reduction Act benefits for U.S.-based battery production.
Freyr is making a $2.6 billion investment in Georgia in partnership with Koch Industries.
Freyr expects to produce 2.5 gigawatt-hours of batteries in 2025, ramping to 38 gigawatt-hours shortly thereafter, and should employ more than 700 people.
Freyr licensed battery technology developed by MIT spinout 24M, which advertises a streamlined production technique that lowers factory construction costs.
SK will team up with Hyundai to build a $5 billion factory in northern Georgia.
SK is also partnering with Ford to build $5.7 billion factories near Memphis and in southern Kentucky.

Georgia emerged as a hub of U.S. battery manufacturing by courting SK and other Korean investors, including appointing an economic development lead dedicated specifically to attracting South Korean business.
Yoonie Kim started her job as Georgia's economic development lead for South Korea in 2006 and helped facilitate $11.5 billion of Korean investment in the state in 2022 alone.
The Kemp administration courts EV employers with tax incentives and Georgia's state-funded Quick Start worker training program.
Volkswagen ramped up ID.4 assembly last year, hiring 1,000 new employees and adding a third shift at its Chattanooga plant, with another 500 hires expected that year.

The VW ID.4 electric SUV flagship is largely sourced from within the U.S., with steel from Alabama and Ohio, interior parts from Indiana and South Carolina, electronics from Kentucky and North Carolina, and batteries from SK's Georgia base.
Tennessee is home to approximately 900 auto parts suppliers, a network that increasingly includes batteries to reduce global shipping disruption risks.
Roughly half the U.S. population lives within 650 miles of Nashville, giving Tennessee a geographical logistics advantage.
Memphis serves as a historic logistics hub halfway up the Mississippi River and has evolved into a railroad, trucking, and global air freight hub thanks to FedEx.
GM makes electric Cadillacs at a facility in Spring Hill, Tennessee, near Ford's BlueOval City.
BMW added EV production to its plant in Spartanburg, South Carolina.
Hyundai and Kia are building new sites in Georgia.
Electric truck startup Rivian looked to set up shop outside Atlanta, but the state's incentive package has been tied up in court due to a case brought by residents opposing the project.
Central North Carolina has attracted new factories from Toyota and Vietnamese firm VinFast.
Tennessee recently passed a constitutional amendment to perpetuate right-to-work status, with voters approving it by a nearly 40-point margin.
Newly elected United Auto Workers President Shawn Fain has spoken about the need to ensure union representation at new EV plants in the region.
The first operational battery recycler in the Southeastern Battery Belt, Ascend Elements, opened in spring 2023 in Covington, Georgia.
Ascend Elements uses a two-story-tall wet shredder to process used battery modules and scrap materials from nearby factories like SK's.
The wet shredder drops batteries through an airlock into an oxygen-purged space to prevent sparks from grinding metal while batteries contain flammable liquid electrolyte.
Ascend cooks off the liquid electrolyte in a rotating kiln, condenses it, cleans it up, and sells it back to battery makers.
Ascend separates remnants by density and particle size, producing aluminum, copper, magnetic bits, and black mass containing electrode metals in 1,000-kilogram sacks.
Ascend sells its black mass back to battery manufacturers for processing into proprietary recipes.
Ascend is breaking ground on a $1 billion factory in southern Kentucky that will use a hydro-to-cathode technique to pull out exact element ratios for each battery maker.
Ascend just announced a $1 billion deal to supply an unnamed U.S. battery manufacturer.
Redwood Materials, launched by Tesla co-founder JB Straubel, is building a $3.5 billion recycling plant near Charleston, South Carolina.

Redwood plans to produce 100 gigawatt-hours per year of cathode active materials and anode foil annually across its U.S. operations by 2025, which would supply approximately 1 million electric vehicles.
Nearly all anode and cathode production supplying U.S. battery cell manufacturers currently occurs overseas in Asia, according to Redwood Materials spokesperson Daniel Zotos.
Localizing the supply chain lowers the environmental footprint of manufacturing batteries and helps mitigate supply-chain risks, which will drive down EV costs.
Recycling cannot catch up to soaring battery demand for at least the next decade, so fresh mining will be needed to keep EV production going.
The Southeastern Battery Belt already produces scrap from manufacturing lines and will generate waste from retired EV batteries, making recycling a solvable ecological opportunity.

Southeastern states have become indispensable to the national quest to clean up transportation-related emissions by locking in value-add parts of the EV supply chain, including gigafactories, EV production locations, and advanced lithium-ion recycling leadership.
source_title: The South is building the most vibrant EV and battery… | Shenyang Alpine Adventures Group

facts: General Motors and Ford slammed the brakes on electric vehicle production late last year as consumer adoption lagged behind expectations.
GM scrapped plans to produce 400,000 electric vehicles through mid-2024 due to lower demand.
Ford cut back production on its electric pickup.
Electrical machinery construction, which includes EV battery plants, hit $35.2 billion in 2023, according to Dodge Construction Network.
Electrical machinery construction accounted for roughly 47% of overall manufacturing construction in 2023.
Economists anticipate the manufacturing boom to continue in 2024 despite automaker pullbacks.
Dodge Construction Network expects a 15% increase in overall manufacturing construction in 2024.
American consumers bought 15.6 million vehicles in 2023.
About 9% of 2023 vehicle sales came from EVs.
GM expects EV sales to increase to at least 10% in 2024, according to CEO Mary Barra's recent earnings call statements.
S&P Global Mobility forecasts EV market share for new vehicles will jump to 40% by 2030.
Meeting the projected 2030 demand would require about 60 billion batteries and 40 large-scale gigafactories, according to Anthony Johnson.
Kansas City, Missouri-based JE Dunn plans to remain busy with manufacturing-related work in 2024, particularly in the EV-related space.
JE Dunn will deliver Dongwha Electrolyte’s first project in North America: a $70 million electric vehicle battery production facility in Clarksville, Tennessee.
Brent Strength serves as senior vice president and manufacturing market leader at JE Dunn.
Strength expects manufacturing construction in 2024 to surpass its 2023 levels.
Contractors currently have a healthy backlog from previous years.
There is significant spending of Inflation Reduction Act, CHIPS Act, and similar dollars right now.
Chicago-based Clayco expects to remain busy with advanced manufacturing construction in 2024.
Clayco recently won the contract to build Rivian’s $5 billion EV plant in Stanton Springs, Georgia.
Anthony Johnson serves as president of the industrial business unit at Clayco.
Johnson expects the advanced manufacturing segment to see significant investment expanding at very high rates in 2024.
Contractors acknowledge maintaining record activity levels indefinitely is not guaranteed due to factors outside the onshoring boom.
Hurdles to sustained growth include changes to EV tax breaks for consumers and persistently high interest rates, according to Strength.
The overall growth trajectory might not be as steep as previously projected, given slower consumer adoption, higher interest rates and changes to tax credits, said Strength.
The number of EV types qualifying for clean vehicle tax credits up to $7,500 under the Inflation Reduction Act dropped from 35 to 14 this year, per the U.S. Department of Energy and IRS.
The tax credit change stipulates that vehicles sourcing EV battery components from foreign-based entities, such as firms in China or Korea, will no longer be eligible for the credit in 2024.
Without the incentive, 2024 will provide a clear sense of where EV demand truly is in the United States, according to Dodge chief economist Richard Branch.
Johnson expects EV adoption to continue making slow headway, which will translate into a longer-term boom of work for contractors in the sector.
The 2024 election year has made EVs a political topic regarding climate change and government subsidies, creating segment volatility, according to Johnson.
Individual automakers will experience ups and downs along the way, but the overall EV growth curve continues to go upwards over time.
source_title: EV battery plant construction booms even as automakers hit the brakes | Construction Dive

facts: Atlanta Mayor Andres Dickens' official city car is a Rivian electric vehicle.
Rivian is building a $5 billion EV and battery plant in Georgia.
source_title: Rivian is now official vehicle of Atlanta Mayor Andre Dickens - Atlanta Business Chronicle

facts: More than a hundred people packed a Social Circle High School to tell economic development leaders they do not want an electric vehicle company to build a manufacturing plant in their community.
Residents say they feel planning was done in private, while project leaders said they had monthly public meetings about the site as early as last May that few people attended.
Tommy Wasendorf, who has lived in the area for more than 20 years, stated at a Joint Development Authority (JDA) town hall: "We're not getting any say in this, this was done at the state capitol."
The planned factory will stand at the Stanton Springs Industrial Park, located an hour east of Atlanta.
Walton County Economic Development director Shane Short stated that everything was done through protocol nondisclosure agreements to prevent competing companies from interfering with plans.
Other JDA members confirmed there have been monthly public meetings to outline the process since mid-2021.
The JDA recognizes public outrage began when Gov. Brian Kemp announced the project at the end of the year.
Tommy Wasendorf, a manufacturing worker, noted that non-disclosure agreements protect companies but do not protect neighborhoods and communities.
Gov. Brian Kemp announced plans for a multi-billion-dollar Rivian plant last month (December 2021).
Rivian produces Amazon electric vehicles.
The project promises $5 billion in investments and 7,500 jobs with a starting salary of $56,000 a year.
Construction on the 2,000-acre facility is scheduled to begin this summer (2022), with first cars being built in 2024.
The recurring question at the town hall was whether the development can be stopped.
Shane Short stated that stopping the project during the rezoning process is possible but will be difficult.
About 26 property owners willingly sold their land for the development, and no eminent domain was used to finalize the project.
Residents at the town hall plan to research ways to prevent the plant's construction.
source_title: Social Circle residents upset over upset over planned electric vehicle plant | FOX 5 Atlanta

facts: Woory Industrial Co., Ltd. is an EV parts manufacturer and Hyundai supplier coming to South Georgia.
The company will build a new manufacturing facility in Dublin.
Woory Industrial manufactures heating, ventilation, and air conditioning components and systems.
The company will invest $18 million into the new manufacturing facility.
The project will create more than 130 jobs.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp welcomed Woory Industrial as a job creator helping the state become the "e-mobility capital of the nation."

Governor Kemp stated that the emerging industry continues to bring historic levels of new jobs and investment to communities across the state, thanks to a highly-skilled workforce and reliable infrastructure network.
Governor Kemp expressed confidence that Woory will have a long and beneficial partnership with Dublin-Laurens County.
source_title: EV parts manufacturer to supply Hyundai factory in Bryan County

canonical_name: Georgia Alternative Fuel Corridors and EV Charging Network
facts: The Federal Highway Administration has designated two Georgia highways as “alternative fuel corridors” for the purpose of facilitating the construction of electric vehicle charging stations.
U.S. 441 from Cornelia in Northeast Georgia south to Dublin and U.S. 82 from Brunswick west to Albany will add 25%, or approximately 330 miles, to the state’s EV charging network.

State Commissioner of Transportation Russell McMurry stated on Thursday that convenient access to electric vehicle charging stations is critical to innovating and expanding Georgia’s transportation network.
These federal designations are important because they signify Georgia’s commitment to alternative fuel options in every part of the state, according to Commissioner McMurry.

The two highways were selected based on such factors as their location near major job clusters, access to tourism sites, the high share of EV sales in nearby counties, and their proximity to hurricane evacuation routes.
The designations also are expected to enhance Georgia’s chances of landing federal funds from the $1.2 trillion bipartisan infrastructure spending bill Congress passed last fall.
The legislation earmarked $5 billion in grants to support EV charging stations.
The Georgia Department of Transportation announced last week that it is working with Chicago-based commercial real estate company JLL to plan a network of EV charging stations around the state.
About 30,000 electric vehicles are on Georgia roads today being serviced by more than 1,300 publicly available charging stations with an estimated 3,400 individual outlets.
A legislative study committee created by the General Assembly during this year’s session is expected to begin working soon toward developing a plan for expanding the supply of EV charging stations.
source_title: Two Georgia highways chosen as corridors for EV charging stations - The Christian Index

facts: OSHA found serious safety violations at the SK Battery America plant following a battery fire.
A federal investigation uncovered repeated safety lapses at the Georgia plant.
The US Department of Labor's Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) uncovered alarming safety breaches at the SK Battery America plant in the aftermath of a lithium battery fire.
The findings have raised urgent concerns about safety standards within the electric vehicle battery manufacturing sector.
The OSHA investigation was prompted by an October 2023 lithium battery fire at the Commerce, GA, plant.
OSHA inspectors found SK Battery America culpable of failing to adequately train its workforce to navigate the hazardous aftermath of the incident.
Multiple workers suffered potentially irreversible respiratory damage due to exposure to toxic fumes.
The company was cited for exposing workers to inhalation hazards, including hydrofluoric acid vapors, by neglecting to establish a comprehensive emergency response plan.
SK Battery America failed to ensure that staffing agency Moveret Inc. informed its workers about the dangers associated with lithium battery fires.
There was inadequate training for on-site emergency responders and fire brigade members regarding lithium battery fire hazards and treatment procedures for exposed employees.
Employees lacked training on hazardous chemicals in their work areas and the means to protect themselves during lithium battery fires.
The company neglected to conduct annual fit-testing for workers required to utilize tight-fitting facepiece respirators.
OSHA imposed fines totaling over $77,000 on SK Battery America for the safety infractions.
This marks the second instance within a year where the company has been found wanting in adhering to federal safety standards.
The safety lapses underscore broader concerns within the battery manufacturing sector regarding employee safety and regulatory compliance.

The regulatory framework governing environmental standards in this sector has yet to catch up with the rapid pace of development, posing significant challenges for EV manufacturers, as described by US law firm Troutman Pepper.
Joshua Turner, OSHA's Area Office Director in Atlanta-East, underscored the importance of prioritizing worker safety, particularly in emerging industries like battery manufacturing.
Turner emphasized that innovation must not come at the expense of employee well-being.
Safety advocates and industry stakeholders are calling for stringent safety measures to be implemented without delay.
SK Battery America Inc. is a subsidiary of SK On, a key player in South Korea's SK Group.
The company employs approximately 3,100 workers at its Commerce plants.
BlueOval SK is a joint venture between Ford and SK On to manufacture batteries in Glendale, KY, and Stanton, TN, for Ford and Lincoln EVs.
SK On and Hyundai Motor Group planned a joint venture in April in Bartow County, GA, for Hyundai and Kia EVs.
SK On and Westwater Resources signed an agreement in 2023 to develop anode materials tailored to batteries produced at SK On’s US plants.
Industry stakeholders emphasize that worker safety must take precedence over all other considerations and that concerted efforts are needed for industry-wide reforms to prevent future tragedies.
source_title: OSHA Finds Serious Safety Violations at SK Battery America Plant Following Battery Fire

facts: Rivian held a groundbreaking ceremony for its $5 billion electric vehicle manufacturing plant.
The plant is located at Stanton Springs North in Social Circle (Walton County), Georgia.
The site falls within the Joint Development Authority (JDA) region of Jasper, Morgan, Newton & Walton Counties.
When first announced in 2021, the project was the largest single economic development investment in Georgia’s history.
The project is expected to create 7,500 jobs.
Shane Short, President and CEO of the Development Authority of Walton County, led the recruitment efforts on behalf of the JDA.
Serra Hall, Executive Director of the Newton County Industrial Development Authority, led the recruitment efforts on behalf of the JDA.
Rivian’s decision highlights Georgia’s strength as a hub for advanced manufacturing and the importance of regional collaboration in securing transformational projects.
The groundbreaking marks the start of construction and the beginning of new opportunities for workforce development, supplier growth, and long-term prosperity across the four-county region.
source_title: Rivian Groundbreaking in Georgia - Southern Economic Development Council

facts: Norwegian battery firm Freyr confirmed plans for a Georgia factory that will supply batteries for energy storage.
The factory is dubbed "Giga America."
The factory will be located on a 368-acre tract in Coweta County on the southwestern edge of the Atlanta metropolitan area.
Production will be stepped up in phases.
The first phase targets 34 gigawatt-hours from a $1.7 billion investment.
Freyr did not confirm when the first phase would be completed.
A second phase will add more cell production lines.
The second phase will bring total investment to $2.6 billion by 2029.
Freyr expects an incentive package from the State of Georgia and Coweta County.
Freyr expects tax credits under the Inflation Reduction Act.
The company is applying with the U.S. Department of Energy for a federal incentive package.
The potential federal package could include a grant and/or direct loans.
The factory will use Boston-based 24M's SemiSolid manufacturing process.
The SemiSolid process is billed as boosting efficiency in the manufacture of lithium-ion cells.
Technicians from 24M will collaborate with Freyr's Norway-based staff.
Freyr considers the U.S. energy storage market to be the world's largest.
Citing Rystad Energy, Freyr expects combined U.S. and European energy storage demand to reach 1.4 terawatt-hours by 2030.
This expected demand represents a roughly 34-fold increase from 2022.
Raw material prices have kept battery prices inflated despite growing interest in battery-based energy storage from companies, utilities, and residences.
Battery prices have steadily decreased over the past decade.
A recent raw material price surge is expected to delay further affordability gains until 2024 at the earliest.
The battery plant is part of a cluster of new manufacturing projects in Georgia.
Rivian's Georgia plant is scheduled to start vehicle production in 2024.
Rivian's smaller R2 model is due to debut at the Georgia plant later.
An upcoming Hyundai/Kia "Metaplant" in Georgia is expected to reach full capacity around 2025.
The Hyundai/Kia Metaplant will produce up to 500,000 EVs at full capacity.
SK Innovation's Georgia plants will produce batteries for up to 300,000 EVs annually.
SK Innovation's Georgia plants will supply batteries for the Ford F-150 Lightning.
source_title: Freyr's Georgia battery factory will focus on energy storage

facts: Kia will invest more than $200 million at its West Point plant.
The investment will create nearly 200 jobs.
The plant will produce the Korean automaker’s new electric vehicle sports-utility model, the EV9.
Gov. Brian Kemp announced the project on Wednesday.
The EV9 will become the fifth model assembled at the Georgia plant since it opened in 2009.
The plant currently assembles the Telluride, Sorento and Sportage SUVs, and the K5 mid-size sedan.
More than 40% of Kia vehicles sold in the U.S. are manufactured in West Point.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that Georgia’s longstanding partnership with Kia has led to generational job creation and growth for the West Point area.
Kemp said the project will bring more opportunity to hardworking Georgians and help propel the state toward its goal of becoming the e-mobility capital.
Sean Yoon, president & CEO of Kia Motors North America and Kia Motors America, stated that like the Telluride, the EV9 has the potential to be another change catalyst for Kia.
Yoon added that the EV9 will be the most innovative vehicle Kia has ever built and a standout in the EV market and on the road.
To date, Kia has invested more than $1.9 billion in Georgia.
Kia Georgia is responsible for more than 14,000 plant and supplier jobs in the region.
Hyundai is building a $5.5 billion plant west of Savannah that will generate 8,100 jobs.
The Hyundai project is described as the biggest economic development project in Georgia’s history.
Rivian has under construction a $5 billion EV plant in the Covington area expected to create 7,500 jobs.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team worked on the Kia EV project with the West Point Development Authority.
The project involved collaboration with the city of West Point and Troup County.
The Georgia Center of Innovation’s manufacturing and energy teams assisted with the project.
The Technical College System of Georgia’s Quick Start program was involved in workforce training for the project.
The EV9 plant is expected to begin operations in the second quarter of next year.
source_title: Kia to bring EV manufacturing to West Point | News | times-herald.com

facts: In 2018, 6,000 electric vehicles were sold in Georgia, an increase of 147 percent compared to the prior year.
The majority of EV charging stations are located in dense, urban areas like Atlanta where there are hundreds of stations dotting the streets.
EV charging stations are also popping up in more rural areas like Coweta County.
Newnan currently has 16 EV charging stations, with 13 of them providing a free charge.
Drivers can find EV charging stations by City Hall, Carl Miller Park, and the Target off Bullsboro Drive.
Other cities in Coweta County don’t have any EV charging stations yet.
Most charging stations are located near major highways, interstates or throughout urban areas, according to a 2019 report by the Center for Rural Affairs.

The Center for Rural Affairs stated that the lack of charging stations throughout many rural areas restricts the viability of electric vehicles for people who live in rural communities or tourists who wish to visit.
The Center for Rural Affairs stated that any plans to expand charging infrastructure must include rural areas.

A Jan. 7 state news release stated that Georgia is committed to strengthening its position as a leader in the EV market with support of cutting-edge innovations emerging out of universities, a highly skilled workforce, and a relationship approach to economic development.

International companies SK Innovations, TEKLAS, and GEDIA Automotive Group have partnered with the Georgia Department of Economic Development to invest in EV supply chain-related production in Georgia.

Georgia companies and universities are researching projects including improving driving range, increasing recharging speeds, enhancing connectivity systems, improving AI, and adding nanotech materials into production.

Coweta-Fayette EMC provides a Drive Free for A Year Plug-in Electric Vehicle Program where qualified members who lease or purchase a new plug-in 100 percent EV on or after April 22, 2019 may qualify for a monthly credit on their power bill to cover the cost of charging the EV for one year.

The Coweta-Fayette EMC website states that today’s EVs offer longer driving ranges, have extended warranties, and can use charging points located along major roadways, places of employment, shopping, and entertainment.
General Motors is planning to become an all-electric vehicle company by 2035.
GM plans to release two Chevrolet Bolt models starting under $34,000 this summer.
GM will release an electric version of their iconic Hummer amounting to $113,000 later this year.
Volkswagen plans to build 28 million electric vehicles by 2028.
Volkswagen Group is investing nearly $37 into its electric car program.

The Yale School of Environment predicts that if the private sector and the U.S. government make a serious commitment to EVs, the majority of new automobiles sold in the U.S. will be electric within the next two decades.
A Yale article claims that a more extensive nationwide network of charging stations and improved EV batteries are needed before EVs become more prevalent.
Trae Westmoreland with the Coweta County Development Authority stated that Coweta has been a finalist for several potential battery manufacturers.
Westmoreland stated that Coweta is getting more looks from the industry and has the attributes that fit the model for what they are trying to do.
Westmoreland stated that Coweta has good sites, workforce, and skill set, with local employees well positioned.
Westmoreland stated that through the German apprenticeship model, Coweta is a hub for advanced manufacturing.
Westmoreland stated that when someone needs 400 acres within driving distance of Atlanta and the airport, Coweta is really the only game in town right now.
LG Batteries was the last company to seriously consider Coweta County but is likely to set up shop in Tennessee due to General Motors’ desire to utilize an incentive package provided by the state.
source_title: The future of electric cars in Coweta | Local News | times-herald.com

facts: E-Z-GO Certified Pre-Owned RXV vehicles are tested, reconditioned, and certified to the company's highest standards.
CPO vehicles are refurbished in Augusta, Georgia, with component replacements and a comprehensive inspection.
Customers can now build and price a Certified Pre-Owned RXV vehicle online to explore available options and understand pricing upfront.

The CPO inspection covers the body, cowl, bumper, tires, steering wheel, tops, struts, windshield, front and rear facing seats, floormats, headlights, tail lights, turn signals, trim, logos, and hardware.
The re-certification process also includes checking the steering unit, alignment, axels, floorboards, dash, battery amps, state of charge meter, and 14 other key areas.
Certified Pre-Owned E-Z-GO vehicles provide access to an industry-leading service network comprising over 80 E-Z-GO-certified technicians nationwide.
The service network includes a call center team with a 35-second wait time.
Every E-Z-GO Certified Pre-Owned vehicle comes with a 1-year parts and accessory warranty.
Extended warranty coverage is available for CPO vehicles.
Lease terms of up to 4 years are available for these vehicles.
E-Z-GO has handcrafted every vehicle in Augusta, Georgia since 1954.
source_title: E-Z-GO Certified Pre-Owned Fleet Vehicles

facts: Varchea understands how batteries, motors, controllers, and software work together as a system to help customers avoid compatibility issues and performance bottlenecks.
The company supports custom voltage, capacity, enclosure design, BMS configuration, and branding to enable partners to differentiate their EV products while maintaining reliability.
