Products are designed with durability, thermal stability, and long service life in mind for fleet, dealer, and commercial EV applications where downtime is costly.
By combining in-house engineering with mature supply chains, Varchea delivers competitive pricing without sacrificing performance or safety to help customers improve total cost of ownership.
Varchea works closely with international partners, offering technical support, documentation, and after-sales service tailored for overseas markets.
source_title: varchea

facts: Mister Sparky of Augusta specializes in EV charger installation for homeowners in Augusta, GA.
The company provides Level 2 charger installation, service panel upgrades, and troubleshooting or repair services for faulty EV chargers.
Mister Sparky offers transparent pricing, on-time service, a 100% satisfaction guarantee, and 24/7 emergency services.
Home EV charging speed depends on the home's electrical capacity and the type of EV charging unit selected.
Level 1 chargers typically come standard with most EVs, utilize a regular 120-volt household outlet, add only a few miles of range per hour, and are best suited for overnight charging.

Level 2 chargers require a dedicated 240-volt circuit and professional installation by a qualified electrician, offering significantly faster charging speeds that can add around 70 miles of range per hour.

Level 3 chargers are the fastest available, capable of recharging an EV battery to 80% in about 30 minutes, but require high-powered industrial electrical connections and are generally not feasible for residential installation.
Installing a Level 2 charger typically requires a home to have a 150–200 amp electrical service.
Homes with a 100-amp electrical panel may need an upgrade to safely support the new load and avoid overloading the system.
Mister Sparky technicians are trained, licensed, insured, and local to the area.
Authority Brands is the parent company of Mister Sparky and operates as a leading provider of in-home services supporting franchisees and homeowners.
Mister Sparky holds a 4.9-star rating based on 1,410 customer reviews.

Customer reviews highlight specific technicians (Rod, Dylan, Stuart) performing electrical work including smoke detector replacement, generator installation, whole-house surge protector installation, and RFID installation for community projects.
One customer received a whole-house surge protector installation awarded through a prize drawing at the 2026 Home and Garden Show in Augusta, Georgia.
A technician from Mister Sparky offered to contact GA Power to address electrical questions during a service call.
source_title: EV Charger Installation in Augusta, GA | Mister Sparky

facts: Solar manufacturer QCells has started making the key components that go into solar panels at its new factory in Cartersville, Georgia.
This production start marks a major milestone in moving solar production to the US.
QCells has been assembling solar modules in Dalton, Georgia, since 2019.
Until now, the ingots, wafers and cells that make up those panels have been manufactured overseas.

Scott Moskowitz, vice president of industry affairs for QCells, explained that ingots, wafers, and cells are where the critical technology of a solar panel comes from and what actually powers and creates electricity from it.
Making those parts abroad can create problems with customs, tariffs, and other supply chain issues.
Last year, QCells slowed down production and implemented furloughs due to such delays.
Making the cells locally should prevent those kinds of problems in the future, Moskowitz said.
Moskowitz called the milestone a really meaningful one for QCells and the US solar industry.
Moskowitz stated that this development more than doubles the available capacity of US-made cells in the United States.
The solar, battery, and electric vehicle industries are all trying to move more manufacturing to the US.
These industries aim to address supply chain issues and take full advantage of tax credits that favor domestic production.
Georgia has been a leader in that transition, Moskowitz said.
Moskowitz noted that Georgia has recognized manufacturing is important to its citizens and economy.
QCells plans to fully ramp up production at the new factory later this year.
The factory is the first of its kind in the US, where all the major parts of a solar module will be made under one roof.
Once both facilities are at full capacity, the two QCells facilities in Georgia are expected to produce 47,000 panels a day.
This output is enough to power more than one million homes for a year, according to the company.
source_title: July 3 - Georgia manufacturer QCells moves toward fully US-made solar panels | Manufacturing | savannahbusinessjournal.com

facts: Robert Howey has worked at the Hanwha Qcells solar manufacturing plant in Dalton, Georgia since it opened in 2019.
The Qcells plant is located in what Georgians affectionately call “the carpet capital of the world.”
For two years, Howey was a creeler, knotting together rolls of yarn, so that as one roll finished getting fed into the weaving machine, the next could seamlessly follow.
Before Howey started at Qcells, he had no idea about solar.
He was hired to be a “tabber operator” on the production line, where his role would be to watch over machines that solder silicon wafers together, an early stage in the making of a solar module.
Georgia Quick Start, a state-funded workforce training program, gave him the guidance he needed.
In a week of on-ramping, trainers walked him through all the steps of making a solar panel, instruction that gave him the confidence to start his new career.

Clean energy manufacturing in the U.S. is poised for explosive growth; the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 will infuse more than $47 billion in the buildout of clean energy technologies and spur an estimated 900,000 manufacturing jobs over the next decade.
Georgia Quick Start provides customized job training for companies free of charge.
The program has a track record of tackling workforce training challenges and could serve as a model for other states preparing their workers for jobs in clean energy manufacturing.

Quick Start was founded in 1967 with the goal of attracting manufacturers from northern Rust Belt states to Georgia in order to diversify the state’s agriculture-based economy, according to Rodger Brown, executive director of Quick Start.
The program was designed to prepare a workforce accustomed to seasonally dependent, sunup-to-sundown farm labor to industry’s regimented 8-hour workdays.
Over the following decades, Quick Start steadily grew to serve a broad range of industries.

By the 1980s, for example, the program was training workers for automobile manufacturer Ford Motor Company and aircraft companies Lockheed Corporation (now Lockheed Martin), Boeing and Gulfstream Aerospace Corporation.
In the 1990s, it scaled up to serve the booming carpeting industry around Dalton, Georgia.

Today, Quick Start continues to train workers for jobs in these industries as well as for ones in the manufacturing of food, pharmaceuticals, medical devices, vinyl flooring — and, increasingly, clean energy technologies such as EVs, solar panels and batteries.
A division of the state’s technical college system, Quick Start has achieved national recognition for its effectiveness.
A panel of experts surveyed by Area Development Magazine has named it the top state workforce development program in the country for nine years running.
Since its founding, the program has helped prepare more than 1.8 million Georgians for new jobs, according to Brown.
Clean energy companies and their suppliers currently make up the majority of Quick Start’s clients, Brown said.

Cleantech companies are setting up shop in Georgia for many reasons — generous tax incentives and the fact that Georgia is a “right-to-work” state with legislation that weakens labor unions are certainly among them.
But Quick Start is also a big part of the equation.
The program was a major factor for Qcells, said Lisa Nash, the company’s senior director of human resources.

Quick Start developed training materials for the South Korean manufacturer’s first U.S. plant in Dalton, the one Howey was hired to work in, which meant “we didn’t have to invest the thousands of hours of making classroom and video training that was necessary to get this factory started,” Nash said.
That was “a game-changer for us.”

Steven Jahng, director of external affairs of South Korea–based SK Battery America, echoed those sentiments in Site Selection’s 2023 Workforce Guide: “The key incentive for SK Battery is the Georgia Quick Start program. They know exactly how to train the average person off the street.”
Like most advanced manufacturing facilities, factories that produce clean energy technologies are filled with powerful, highly automated machines and robots.
Even a single wrong move, such as tripping a sensor that starts a machine whirring when it should stay off, could be dangerous.

In its weeklong Qcells training course and subsequent follow-up sessions, Quick Start teaches new hires how to stay safe, including requirements from the federal Occupational Safety and Health Administration.
That’s hugely important, said David Uribe, who started working as a Qcells equipment technician in February.
New employees could be straight out of high school or coming from service roles at McDonald’s or Walmart, said Uribe, who has been a mechanic for decades.
By acclimating people to this unfamiliar environment, Quick Start training actually helps retain new workers, said Howey, the former tabber operator who is now a training coordinator at Qcells.
He estimates that he was seeing about four out of 10 newly hired Qcells employees who didn’t receive Quick Start guidance dropping out “because they were overwhelmed.”
But with Quick Start, only one or two out of 10 new hires quit — “if that,” Howey said.
One reason Quick Start is so effective is because it tailors trainings to the needs of each company it works with, said Brown, the program’s executive director.
To begin, it closely studies every detail of a company’s manufacturing process.
For example, to develop training for SK Battery, a team of instructors flew to South Korea in 2019 to observe the company’s facilities firsthand.
“We took photographs and video, and took lots of notes and asked lots of questions,” Brown said. “It’s very rare to be allowed that kind of access.”
Based on that deep dive, Quick Start, which has 75 full-time staff members, designed a training program for the company’s U.S. operations within months.
As soon as SK Battery had recruited employees, Quick Start was able to begin training them, covering core modules on safety and SK Battery’s process, as well as providing job-specific training.
Quick Start’s trainees are able to gain direct, hands-on experience before they even step onto the factory floor.

In 2019, the program opened a 50,000-square-foot facility in Savannah so that trainees can practice using advanced-manufacturing equipment, including automated robotics, sensors and programmable logic controllers, which are computer systems for industrial processes.
As it trains clean-energy workers on its equipment, Quick Start has been able to draw from its decades of experience with other industries, Brown said.

For example, part of the lithium-ion battery production process involves slurries of graphite, cobalt, nickel and other metals, which are mixed in enormous vessels that are similar to those used in food manufacturing to mix milk, salt and sugar.
Workers monitor and troubleshoot the vats in the same way, he said.
It’s not all technical training, though, Brown noted.

Quick Start also develops trainees’ soft skills, offering programs in leadership training, data-driven process improvement methods (such as Six Sigma), and with Korean companies such as SK Battery and EV manufacturers Hyundai and Kia, “cross-cultural training, like different work and communication styles.”
Another factor in Quick Start’s success, according to Brown, is that it’s wholly state-funded.
Federal dollars “come with a lot of reporting and strings and complexity,” he noted.
Quick Start, by contrast, can adapt quickly to train workers however needed, he said.

“If you’re creating 1,000 jobs, we will train them until you have 1,000 people qualified and working in your plant — whether that means we only train a couple of hours and they go into the facility, or we do 60 hours,” Brown said.
“As long as [a company is] creating net new jobs, we will be their training partner.”

Federal funding does have its advantages, of course: It sends “a lot of dollars to programs that are absolutely necessary,” said Cynthia Finley, vice president of workforce strategy and innovation at the Interstate Renewable Energy Council, which has developed trainings for a multitude of clean-energy jobs, including in solar and energy efficiency.
Federal funding opportunities can funnel much-needed money to underserved communities or groups, such as women or veterans.
And because it requires quantitative benchmarks of success, Finley said, “You know you’re moving the needle.”
What’s clear is that Quick Start is able to grow rapidly to meet the demands of the clean energy boom.
The program, which currently operates five training centers, is expanding the one for advanced manufacturing and is also designing three new facilities for clean energy manufacturers and suppliers.

One of those training centers will be a $70 million facility for Hyundai in Bryan County, where the EV manufacturer has committed to the single-largest economic development project in Georgia’s history.
Hyundai broke ground on its new plant last October and is slated to open in January 2025, starting with a few hundred workers and, over the next several years, growing to more than 8,000.
Once its new center is ready, Quick Start plans to train about 300 to 400 new Hyundai hires a week, Brown said.
When companies need to go fast, “we go fast.”
source_title: This Georgia program is training a huge cleantech… | Canary Media

facts: Hanwha Qcells and LG Energy Solution Vertech signed a multiyear agreement to install reliable, affordable, American-made storage products for Qcells’ development projects across the country.
The agreement includes 5 GWh of lithium-ion energy storage projects for utility-scale sites.
The partnership includes lifecycle services from LG Energy Solution Vertech.
Qcells’ engineering, procurement, and construction (EPC) projects under this agreement are scheduled for 2028-2030.
In May 2024, Qcells entered into battery purchase agreements with LG Energy Solution Vertech totaling 4.8 GWh, which were installed in energy storage system (ESS) projects developed across the U.S.

The partnership secures ESS for Qcells that meet domestic content requirements because LG Energy Solution’s battery energy storage products are made in Michigan and Qcells’ solar panels are made in Georgia.
Leveraging made-in-America products supports U.S. manufacturing jobs, strengthens the nation’s clean-energy supply chains, enhances grid reliability, and reduces exposure to trade risks and tariffs.
Chris Hodrick, CEO of Qcells EPC, stated that Qcells is proud to partner with LG Energy Solution on a project showing what is possible when energy development supports U.S. manufacturing.

Hodrick noted the collaboration reflects a shared commitment to building clean energy the right way by creating American jobs, strengthening supply chains, and delivering reliable, affordable power for communities across the country.
Qcells operates the largest solar panel manufacturing facility of its kind in the U.S., located in Dalton, Georgia.
The company is building a second factory in Cartersville, Georgia, where it will manufacture ingots, wafers, cells and finished panels.
This second factory represents the largest investment in U.S. solar history to create an integrated, sustainable domestic supply chain.
Once fully operational, Qcells’ annual solar panel production capacity in the U.S. will reach 8.4 gigawatts, producing nearly 46,000 panels every day.
The produced panels will be enough to power more than 1.3 million American homes each year.

Jaehong Park, CEO and President of LG Energy Solution Vertech, expressed excitement to continue the partnership and emphasized a commitment to supporting projects throughout their lifecycles for long-term customer success.
The ESS included in this commitment are built using the latest LG Energy Solution containerized lithium-ion battery solutions with LFP cells manufactured at U.S. facilities.
LG Energy Solution is manufacturing up to 16.5 GWh of battery energy storage cells in Holland, Michigan.
LG Energy Solution production is set to ramp up to around 50 GWh at sites across North America by the end of 2026.

Qcells EPC provides complete utility-scale turnkey solutions through the entire project lifecycle, including modules, solar and energy storage project development, and engineering, procurement, and construction (EPC) services to the U.S. utility-scale market.
Qcells USA has a track record that includes 8.4 GW of sustainably produced module manufacturing capabilities in Georgia and nearly 2 GW of developed, constructed, and operating projects.
Qcells EPC is a flagship company of Hanwha Group, a FORTUNE Global 500 firm and a Top 7 business enterprise in South Korea.

LG Energy Solution Vertech is an energy storage systems integrator backed by LG Energy Solution that delivers integrated, reliable, and bankable grid-scale energy storage systems supported by in-house services and software.
In 2022, LG Energy Solution purchased NEC Energy Solutions and established LG Energy Solution Vertech Inc. to vertically integrate the battery manufacturer and integrator.
Via a single contract, LG Energy Solution Vertech provides customers with systems, warranties, services, and software through a system-level contracting process.
The combination of technology excellence and nearly two decades of energy storage integration positions LG Energy Solution Vertech as a leader in the global energy storage market.

links.context: More information about Qcells EPC services

source_title: LG Energy Solution Vertech and Qcells partner to deliver energy storage projects

facts: Lithium-ion battery fires occupy a narrow but consequential space in Georgia product liability law.
Battery fire cases routinely involve manufacturing defect, design defect, and failure to warn theories simultaneously.
Manufacturing defect claims focus on contaminated electrodes, misaligned separators, or incomplete welds in the cell casing.
Design defect claims challenge the fundamental architecture of the battery management system and question whether thermal runaway protection was adequate under foreseeable operating conditions.
Failure to warn theory carries unusual weight because thermal runaway is invisible to consumers until it becomes catastrophic.
The supply chain for batteries in recalled hoverboards or e-bikes is global and deeply fragmented.
Cells may be manufactured in one country, assembled into a pack in another, imported by a distributor, and sold by a retailer.
Identifying which link in the supply chain caused a failure requires forensic analysis of the battery’s internal structure, manufacturing records, and quality control documentation.
Thermal runaway begins when a cell’s internal temperature rises past a threshold, triggering exothermic reactions that generate more heat than the cell can dissipate.
Within seconds, a single cell failure can cascade through an entire battery pack.
Post-fire evidence is often severely degraded because thermal runaway consumes much of the battery itself.
Battery fire experts typically include electrochemical engineers who distinguish between internal short circuits, overcharging caused by defective chargers, and mechanical damage.

Lithium-ion batteries sold in the United States are subject to oversight from the Consumer Product Safety Commission (CPSC), the Department of Transportation (DOT), and the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA).
The CPSC enforces safety standards under the Consumer Product Safety Act and has authority to issue recalls when a product presents a substantial product hazard.
A battery recall serves as powerful evidence of a manufacturer’s knowledge of a defect in Georgia civil litigation.
DOT regulations governing lithium battery shipment create liability avenues if improper packaging, labeling, or shipping violates federal hazardous materials regulations.
Atlanta is a major distribution hub where significant volumes of lithium-ion products move through warehouses and freight facilities in the metro area.
E-bikes and electric scooters are responsible for a significant and growing share of battery fire injuries in urban areas including Atlanta.
Hoverboards became subject to major CPSC scrutiny beginning in the mid-2010s.
Vaping devices, power tools, laptop computers, and home energy storage systems have also generated substantial litigation.

Under Georgia law (O.C.G.A. Section 51-1-11), any seller in the chain of distribution can face strict liability, including cell manufacturers, battery pack assemblers, finished product brands, importers, distributors, and retailers.
Georgia’s general personal injury statute of limitations requires claims to be filed within two years of the date of injury.
Georgia’s product liability statute of repose generally bars claims brought more than ten years after the product was first sold.
Atlanta is one of the largest distribution and logistics centers in the southeastern United States.
Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport handles enormous volumes of air freight, including lithium battery products subject to FAA and DOT shipping regulations.
The metro area’s concentration of warehouses, e-commerce fulfillment centers, and last-mile delivery operations means improperly handled or stored battery products are a real and recurring risk.
source_title: Atlanta Lithium-Ion Battery Fire Lawyer

facts: Georgia Green Energy Services is a locally owned and operated electrician company that has served Atlanta, Georgia, and surrounding counties since 2008.
The company offers EV charging station installation as part of its comprehensive electrical services for home and business owners.
Georgia Green Energy Services provides routine electrical inspections and emergency electrical repairs to ensure property safety and energy efficiency.
The company's electricians adhere to rigorous professional standards and back all work with exceptional warranties.
Hiring the company's professionals ensures electrical work is completed safely, up to code, and reduces risks of accidents like electrical fires or shock.
Georgia Green Energy Services prioritizes safety measures to prevent electrical accidents for residential and commercial properties.
The team consists of licensed and insured electricians with extensive experience handling complex electrical systems.
The company guarantees warranties and guarantees for a year to provide customer reassurance.
Professional installation by the company reduces the likelihood of costly DIY repairs down the road.
Georgia Green Energy Services delivers 24/7 emergency electrical repairs using fully stocked service vehicles ready to restore safety and comfort.
The company stays up to date on the latest codes and regulations, ensuring all work meets the highest industry standards.

Georgia Green Energy Services serves multiple Southeast locations including Athens, Augusta, Columbus, Macon, Savannah, Atlanta, and Sandy Springs in Georgia; Columbia and Charleston in South Carolina; and Mobile, Tuscaloosa, Birmingham, Huntsville, Montgomery, and North Charleston in Alabama.
The company is a certified Qmerit partner, providing trusted electrification installation solutions that meet high safety and service quality standards.
The partnership with Qmerit enables the company to provide the latest electrification technology for seamless residential and commercial transformations.
The exclusive Qmerit certification is only awarded to select electrical service providers who have demonstrated excellence in electrification.
As a Qmerit-certified partner, Georgia Green Energy Services delivers personalized installation solutions that exceed customer expectations.
All Qmerit Certified Solutions Partners serve as expert consultants and installers for all home electrification technologies.
Qmerit partners must be properly licensed, fully insured, and thoroughly background-checked.
Qmerit partners are required to comply with all applicable worker’s compensation guidelines.
Qmerit partners are required to secure permits to verify installations meet local building codes.
Qmerit partners receive continuous training on safety, industry best practices, and the latest technologies.
Qmerit partners are experienced in working with all major technology brands.
Georgia Green Energy Services offers financing options for its electrical services.
The company holds all employees to high standards of personal conduct and ethics.

facts: Duracell is a subsidiary of Berkshire Hathaway.
Duracell maintains manufacturing operations across multiple locations worldwide, including facilities in the United States, Europe, and Mexico.
The majority of Duracell’s manufacturing takes place in the United States.
Duracell's LaGrange, Georgia plant is responsible for creating most of the AA and AAA alkaline batteries.
US manufacturing plants are located in Cleveland, Tennessee; LaGrange, Georgia; and Lexington, North Carolina.
Duracell's US operations and headquarters are based in Bethel, Connecticut.
Duracell’s Lancaster, South Carolina plant was closed in 2016, shortly after Berkshire Hathaway’s acquisition of the company.
In Europe, Duracell operates a manufacturing plant in Aarschot, Belgium, recognized as its most innovative facility and transformed into a “factory of the future” in two years.
The company leases manufacturing plants in Mississauga, Ontario, Canada, and Wrexham, Wales.
In Mexico, Duracell has a manufacturing plant located in Mexico City.
The company collaborates with several third-party manufacturers to produce batteries in other countries.
Duracell has a distribution center located in Heist-op-den-Berg, Belgium, which exports products to Europe and Africa.
Duracell’s batteries are available in over 130 countries worldwide.
Berkshire Hathaway oversees Duracell’s production of its own alkaline batteries.
Duracell's manufacturing facilities have received ISO 9001 certification for their quality management systems.
Duracell developed the Duralock Power Preserve Technology, enabling longer-lasting batteries.
Duracell was originally founded as the P.R. Mallory Company in the 1920s by scientist Samuel Ruben and businessman Philip Rogers Mallory.
In 2014, Procter & Gamble announced plans to spin off Duracell.
Berkshire Hathaway purchased Duracell in an all-stock deal finalized in February 2016.
The acquisition involved a cash investment of $1.8 billion from Procter & Gamble directly into Duracell.
In 2016, Duracell announced that its executives and 60 other employees would relocate to Chicago.
Duracell manufactures non-rechargeable (alkaline) and rechargeable batteries for consumer devices.
Non-rechargeable batteries are available in AAA, AA, C, D, and 9V sizes with Simply Power, Plus, and Ultra Power variations.
Rechargeable batteries are available in standard (Recharge Plus) and high-capacity (Recharge Ultra) types using NiMH and lithium-ion technologies.
Duracell car batteries are manufactured by East Penn in Pennsylvania, USA.

There are 18 total Level 2 stations.
58% of the ports are level 3 charging ports.
There are 25 total Level 3 stations.

There are 10 total free stations.
The main charging networks operating in LaGrange are Tesla, Flo, and Ford Charge.

facts: The Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) is located in Ellabell, Georgia.
The plant produced its first car in 2024.
The facility consists of 11 buildings with 7.5 million square feet of space under roof.
Current output consists of two electric vehicles: the Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9.
The plant is set to eventually have 10 models on site, including hybrids and entries from Kia and Genesis.
A 2.5-million-square-foot LG joint venture battery plant is now under construction at the site.
The complex includes a training center and an ecological park.
Hyundai has made a $12.6 billion investment in Georgia.
In 2025, Hyundai announced it would expand the plant’s output from 300,000 annually to 500,000 vehicles.
The made-in-Korea Ioniq 6 sedan will not be produced at the Georgia plant.
Only the Ioniq 6N performance iteration will be available in the US market.
The Metaplant complements Hyundai Motor Manufacturing in Montgomery, Alabama.
The Alabama facility produces about 360,000 vehicles annually.
Hyundai is also building a steel mill in Louisiana.
The heavily robotized plant currently has 1,623 people directly working for HMGMA.
It will have 2,600 employees when fully staffed.
The total workforce at the 3,000-acre complex, including suppliers, will reach 8,500.
Salaries average $56,000 annually.
Average salaries are 25 percent higher than is common in the surrounding area.
The highest number of workers (32 percent) come from Chatham County, which includes Savannah.
At the time of the visit on April 23, 150 to 175 workers were at the on-site training center learning core skills.
The Hyundai Mobility Training Center is operated by Georgia Quick Start, the state’s workforce development program.
Guide Susan Williams of Quick Start noted that workers must repeat tasks like installing doors if they do not understand them.
One training task had workers standing on a moving conveyer traveling at line speed to identify key car parts.
Other training covered how to use a torque wrench, fasten grommets, and install fasteners at awkward angles.
Judging by first quarter 2026 sales figures, Hyundai produces approximately five times more Ioniq 5s (9,790 units) at the plant than Ioniq 9 SUVs (1,990 units).
The plant's size necessitates travel via golf cart.
A fleet of Hyundai Xcient hydrogen fuel-cell big rigs brings in parts from suppliers on service roads.
Rows of Ioniq 5s headed for distant locations are lined up at rail sidings.
Vehicles destined for less than 500 miles are shipped by truck.
Ioniq bodies in white start at stamping and move through welding, the paint shop, then general assembly on motorized autonomous carts with pre-programmed routes.
The plant eliminates traditional assembly lines in favor of automated guided vehicles.
Everything is described as sparkling clean with a noise level far from traditional auto plants.
Workers at HMGMA are called Meta Pros.
Line people work alongside huge robots that lift car bodies.
Boston Dynamics robotic quality control “dogs” named Spot roam around and check welds.
Robots handle back-breaking drudge work to keep head count down and do not add to the $497 million annual payroll.
HMGMA has its own Georgia Power substation.
The plant has 1,878 solar parking spots that contribute five percent of the plant’s energy needs.

Miles Johnson, Hyundai senior manager of public relations, stated the company signed a 15-year power purchase agreement with Matrix Renewables in 2024 to supplement the plant with renewable energy credits.
Sister company Kia recently completed installation of a 10-megawatt solar array at its West Point plant that doubles as hail protection.
Completed cars go through rigorous obstacle courses on a quality control test track to search for buzz, squeaks, and rattles.
The auto hold feature gets checked, as does the vehicle’s willingness to travel a straight line.
Tester Vanessa Walker tests maybe 35 to 38 cars daily on Impact and Hill Test Roads.
Ninety five percent of tested cars are fine, but the other five percent may have issues like loose clips in the tailgate, dropped bolts under carpet, or wiper blades needing replacement.
A known issue with the Ioniq 5 has been failure of the critical Integrated Charging Control Unit (ICCU).
Hyundai has resolved backorder issues with the ICCU part, which is replaced under warranty.
Due to the ICCU issue, Hyundai issued a warranty extension covering the part for 15 years or 180,000 miles.
Hyundai spokesman Michael Stewart said the company’s Q1 2026 sales in the US set a record.
This complements five years of retail sales increases.
Electrified sales were 33.8 percent of the Q1 volume.
Hyundai is celebrating 40 years in the US, beginning in 1986 with the $4,995 Excel.
Dan Hwang, senior manager for advanced vehicle technical planning, said more than half of Ioniq 9 owners use their third row at least once a month.
A subset of Ioniq 9 owners uses the third row a few times per week.
Hyundai has a robotaxi partnership with Waymo, with IONIQ 5-based vehicles now under testing in San Francisco.
The Waymo car is 90 to 95 percent made by Hyundai.
Its Motional-partnered robotaxi, equipped with more than 30 sensors, is a 100 percent company product.
The Motional robotaxi is in a program with Uber that has geofenced vehicles running on the Vegas strip and downtown.
The Ioniq 5 off-road GTX model was demonstrated in an autocross, showing EV capability beyond paved trails.
source_title: Inside Hyundai’s $12.6 Billion Georgia Metaplant

facts: The $7.6 billion Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) is one of the newest and most technologically advanced car factories in the world.
The plant is located near Savannah, Georgia, and opened its doors in March.
It will serve as a key production facility for Hyundai's EVs and PHEVs, as well as vehicles for its Genesis luxury brand and sister company Kia.
Genesis North America COO Tedros Mengiste cited the investment as an example of Hyundai's track record for 'visionary and strategic, and long-term thinking.'
The Hyundai Metaplant is situated on a 3,000-acre campus in the south Georgia town of Ellabell.
It is located just 20 miles from the Port of Savannah, one of the busiest ports in the US.
The location gives Hyundai much-needed US manufacturing capacity to avoid import tariffs and provides flexibility to export vehicles abroad.
It gives Hyundai the production footprint to compete against rivals like Tesla, GM, and Rivian, which is also building a new factory in Georgia.
It is Hyundai Group's second car factory in Georgia, alongside a $3.2 billion, 2,200-acre facility in West Point, Georgia, that builds Kia EV and ICE SUVs.
The 2026 Hyundai Ioniq 9 EV SUV is one of the vehicles assembled at the Metaplant.
The Hyundai Ioniq 5 EV is the only other model currently assembled at the plant.
Hyundai broke ground on the facility in the fall of 2022 and took just two years to complete construction on the main production buildings.
The Metaplant site consists of 11 buildings totalling 7.5 million square feet of space.
The facility aims for vertical integration by manufacturing as many key components, ranging from battery packs to seats, on-site.
Hyundai XCIENT hydrogen fuel cell semi trucks are used to transport parts and supplies, with 21 emission-free XCIENT trucks deployed around the site.
Production begins in a stamping shop where an on-site Hyundai Steel facility supplies sheet metal cut and stamped into frame parts.
Almost 300 automated guided vehicles (AGVs) are employed to shuttle spare parts and partially assembled cars.

Originally expected to produce up to 300,000 electrified vehicles annually, Hyundai announced at the March grand opening that capacity will expand to 500,000 units in the coming years as part of a new $21 billion US manufacturing investment.
The plant is expected to start production of its first Kia model next year.
Stamped metal pieces are welded into vehicle bodies by robots and inspected by human employees known as Meta Pros.
The plant employs more than 1,300 Meta Pros, nearly 90% of whom were hired locally.
An employee cafeteria with remote ordering capability is located in the main assembly building.
Vehicles are also inspected by a pair of Boston Dynamics robot dogs called Spot, following Hyundai's 2021 acquisition of an 80% stake in the company for $1.1 billion.
Outside visitors were denied access to the paint shop due to concerns that they may compromise paint application quality.
Painted vehicle bodies travel via a bridge to the assembly building, where they are combined with battery packs and skateboard chassis.
Hyundai Mobis produces the skateboard chassis in a building adjacent to the general assembly facility.

An on-site battery factory operated in a joint venture with LG is expected to come online next year, while the plant currently sources batteries from other Hyundai facilities, including a North Georgia joint venture with SK.
Vehicle interiors are assembled by hand.
Assembled vehicles are loaded onto AGVs that navigate via floor-embedded QR codes to shuttle them through quality control tests.

Completed EVs undergo testing at an on-site test track before moving to a vehicle preparation center (VPC); regional dealership vehicles use trucks, while shipments over 500 miles use the plant's on-site train terminal.
source_title: I Visited Hyundai's New $7.6 Billion EV Factory in the US — See Inside - Business Insider

facts: Hyundai Motor Group celebrated the grand opening of its Metaplant America (HMGMA) in Ellabell, GA.
The event marked the completion of the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history.
The project's groundbreaking occurred in 2022.
Hyundai’s supplier network has announced more than $2.5 billion in capital investment across 12 Georgia counties.
This supplier investment is more than double the initially projected $1 billion.
The supplier investments are bringing approximately 6,900 new jobs to the region.

Euisun Chung, executive chair of Hyundai Motor Group, stated that the Metaplant America represents the group’s advanced manufacturing capabilities and commitment to innovation, as well as investment in relationships with partners and communities in Georgia.
HMGMA is expected to play a central role in Hyundai’s long-term electrification strategy.
The plant began production in October 2024.
Initial production vehicles include the Hyundai IONIQ 5 and later the IONIQ 9.
The plant is equipped to manufacture both electric and hybrid vehicles for Hyundai, Genesis, and Kia.
Hyundai plans to add a Kia model to the production line by 2026.
Hyundai plans to increase annual production capacity at HMGMA from 300,000 to 500,000 units.
Hyundai and its joint ventures with LG Energy Solution and SK On have committed $12.6 billion to the HMGMA project.
According to the Center for Automotive Research, these investments are projected to generate nearly 40,000 direct and indirect jobs across Georgia.
These investments are projected to generate $4.6 billion in annual individual earnings across Georgia.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp stated that the Metaplant America is a once-in-a-generation opportunity.
Gov. Kemp noted that collaborations between HMGMA and Georgia’s universities and technical colleges are preparing Georgians for high-quality jobs of today and tomorrow.
Hyundai announced plans to invest $21 billion in the U.S. from 2025 to 2028.
$9 billion of that investment is aimed at scaling domestic production capacity to 1.2 million vehicles annually.
To date, Hyundai has invested over $20.5 billion in the U.S.
This investment has contributed to the creation or support of more than 570,000 jobs nationwide.
source_title: Hyundai Celebrates Grand Opening of Metaplant America - Autobody News

facts: The $7.6 billion Hyundai electric vehicle project in Georgia is nearing completion.
A Korean-speaking technician was trapped in a conveyor belt at the construction site for 15 excruciating minutes, bleeding uncontrollably from his left thigh and having skin stripped from his hand.
Emergency medical responders described the 40-year-old worker's injuries as a crushed chest, deformed hand, and mangled leg.
The injured technician was airlifted to Savannah’s trauma hospital, located 30 miles away.
On June 3, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) released a statement confirming a worker was treated for severe internal and external injuries and was in stable condition.
On June 5, the federal organization that oversees workplace safety finished an onsite inspection of the construction site.
More than two months after the June 5 inspection, the federal workplace safety organization had yet to assign any blame.

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) has started using hydrogen-powered trucks at its electric vehicle (EV) facility in Ellabell, Georgia.
The Hyundai XCIENT fuel-cell trucks will transport parts from suppliers to the plant.
This deployment is part of Hyundai’s efforts to create a more sustainable supply chain and cut carbon emissions.
HMGMA is starting with 21 XCIENT trucks for daily deliveries of parts.
At first, the trucks will move parts to a nearby consolidation center.
Later, they will work with a larger network of suppliers.
These trucks make up about one-third of the truck fleet managed by Glovis America at HMGMA.
Oscar Kwon serves as the CEO of HMGMA.
Oscar Kwon stated: “Our Hyundai XCIENT hydrogen fuel-cell trucks offer a cleaner alternative to traditional freight methods and support our goal of zero emissions in our operations.”
This project is part of a larger plan to use hydrogen in logistics.
Hyundai introduced its HTWO brand earlier this year to focus on hydrogen production, storage, and transportation.
HTWO stands for “Hydrogen” and “Humanity” and reflects Hyundai’s goal to make hydrogen energy a key part of its business.
HMGMA will set up a mobile hydrogen refueling station at the Georgia facility.
The company plans to build a permanent station for hydrogen production and refueling in the future.
Earlier this year, Hyundai shared its vision for hydrogen energy at the CES 2024 event in Las Vegas.
At CES 2024, the company highlighted how hydrogen can reduce greenhouse gas emissions and improve the way energy is stored and used.
Hyundai has committed to being carbon neutral by 2050.
Hyundai plans to use 100% renewable energy at all overseas factories by 2045.
By 2035, Hyundai aims to use 3 million tonnes of hydrogen per year across its operations.
The company believes hydrogen is an essential tool for creating a cleaner future.
The hydrogen trucks in Georgia are part of Hyundai’s broader U.S. efforts, which include the NorCAL ZERO initiative in California that uses hydrogen trucks to help reduce emissions at ports.
In Georgia, Hyundai’s hydrogen trucks will make the logistics process at the EV plant cleaner and more efficient.
These efforts also create jobs and promote the use of clean technology in the region.
source_title: Hyundai’s New Hydrogen Fuel Trucks Energize Georgia Facility

facts: The Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America is an all-new $7.6 billion EV factory.
HMGMA is located near Savannah, Georgia, and opened its doors in March of this year.
The factory will be able to build 500,000 EVs and hybrids for Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis every year.
The plant is one of the newest and most technologically advanced car factories in the world.
It serves as a key production facility for Hyundai's EVs and PHEVs, as well as those belonging to its Genesis luxury brand and sister company Kia.

In a recent interview with Business Insider, Genesis North America COO Tedros Mengiste cited the investment as an example of Hyundai's track record for "visionary and strategic, and long-term thinking."
The Hyundai Metaplant is situated on a 3,000-acre campus in the south Georgia town of Ellabell.
The plant is located just 20 miles from the Port of Savannah, one of the busiest ports in the US.
The location gives Hyundai much-needed US manufacturing capacity to avoid import tariffs and provides flexibility to export vehicles abroad.
The facility gives Hyundai the production footprint to compete against rivals like Tesla, GM, and Rivian, which is also building a new factory in Georgia.
It is Hyundai Group's second car factory in Georgia.
Hyundai Group also operates a $3.2 billion, 2,200-acre facility in West Point, Georgia, that builds Kia EV and ICE SUVs.
The 2026 Hyundai Ioniq 9 EV SUV is one of the vehicles assembled at the Metaplant.
The Hyundai Ioniq 5 EV is the only other model currently assembled at the plant.
Hyundai broke ground on the facility in the fall of 2022.
Construction on the main production buildings took just two years to complete.
The Metaplant site consists of 11 buildings totaling 7.5 million square feet of space.
The Metaplant aims for vertical integration by manufacturing as many key components as possible on-site, ranging from battery packs to seats.
There are 21 emission-free Hyundai XCIENT hydrogen fuel cell semi trucks deployed around the Metaplant site to transport parts and supplies.
The production process begins in a stamping shop where sheet metal is cut and stamped into parts for the car frame.
Sheet metal is supplied by an on-site Hyundai Steel facility.
Stamped parts are transported by automated guided vehicles (AGVs).
The plant employs almost 300 AGVs to shuttle items ranging from spare parts to partially assembled cars.
The Metaplant was originally expected to produce up to 300,000 electrified vehicles annually.
At the plant's grand opening in March, Hyundai announced that capacity will be expanded to 500,000 units in the coming years as part of a new $21 billion investment in US manufacturing.
The plant is expected to start production of its first Kia model next year.
In the welding shop, stamped metal pieces are welded together by robots to form the vehicle body.
The work done by welding robots is inspected by human employees known as Meta Pros.
The Metplant employs more than 1,300 Meta Pros, nearly 90% of whom were hired locally.
Vehicles are inspected by a pair of Boston Dynamics robot dogs called Spot.
In 2021, Hyundai acquired an 80% stake in Boston Dynamics in a deal valuing the company at $1.1 billion.
In the assembly building, painted bodies are combined with battery packs and skateboard chassis.
Hyundai Mobis produces the skateboard chassis in a building next door to the general assembly facility.
The Metaplant's on-site battery factory, operated in a joint venture with LG, is expected to come online next year.
The plant currently sources its batteries from Hyundai's other facilities, including one in North Georgia that is a joint venture with SK.
Assembled vehicles are loaded onto different AGVs that navigate the facility by reading QR codes embedded into the floor.
Completed EVs are tested at an on-site test track before being sent to the vehicle preparation center (VPC) to be readied for shipping.
Vehicles destined for regional dealerships are put on trucks, while those traveling more than 500 miles are shipped by rail at the Metaplant's on-site train terminal.
source_title: Hyundai just built a $7.6 billion EV factory in Georgia to compete with Tesla and GM — see inside - AOL

facts: Rivian is an electric vehicle maker.
Rivian broke ground on a $5 billion plant.
The new facility is located in Georgia.
Construction for the Georgia factory is scheduled ahead of 2026.
The company faces a 'Do or Die' phase as it breaks ground on the facility.
News coverage regarding the groundbreaking was published on September 16, 2025.
TechCrunch reported on the event with author Sean O'Kane.
source_title: Google News - News about Rivian - Overview

facts: California-based automaker Rivian announced Thursday it amended its multibillion-dollar federal construction loan, a deal that will reduce its borrowing by $2 billion.
The U.S. Department of Energy’s Office of Energy Dominance Financing agreed to reduce a previously awarded $6.6 billion loan to $4.5 billion.
The amended loan allows for the first phase of Rivian’s future plant an hour east of Atlanta to have an annual capacity of 300,000 vehicles, a 50% increase from prior plans.
CEO RJ Scaringe said in a Georgia press release: "We're committed to working with the U.S. government."
CEO RJ Scaringe stated: "I want to make this truck as appealing as possible to customers of several critical drivers of global sales profit."
The EV maker says the change will allow the company to expand the scope of its Georgia factory’s first phase.
Rivian announced the amended loan agreement via a news release.
source_title: Rivian signals it will increase production at planned plant east of Atlanta

facts: Rivian Automotive leaders say they’re confident as the company starts long-delayed work on a $5 billion facility in Georgia.
The money-losing California-based company breaks ground Tuesday east of Atlanta despite President Donald Trump’s successful push to roll back electric vehicle tax credits.
Starting Sept. 30, buyers will no longer qualify for savings of up to $7,500 per car.
Rivian Chief Policy Officer Alan Hoffman said the company believes it can sell electric vehicles not for environmental or tax incentive reasons, but because they’re superior.
Hoffman stated: “We did not build this company based upon federal tax incentives.”
Hoffman added: “And we’re going to prove that we’re going to be successful in the future.”
The Georgia plant was first announced in 2021.
The Georgia plant is Rivian’s key to reaching profitability.
Now the company makes the high-end R1T pickup truck and the R1S sport utility vehicle in Normal, Illinois, as well as delivery vans for Amazon and others.
Its truck prices start at $71,000.
The Illinois plant will begin making smaller R2 SUVs next year, with prices starting at $45,000.
An expanded Illinois plant will be able to assemble 215,000 vehicles yearly.
If the R2 is a hit and Rivian successfully produces an even smaller R3, it will need more capacity.
The company has said the Georgia operation will be able to make 200,000 vehicles yearly starting in 2028.
It plans another 200,000 in capacity in phase two, volume that would spread fixed costs over many more vehicles.
The projections would be a big leap from the 40,000 to 46,000 vehicles Rivian expects to deliver this year, down from 52,000 last year.
The company says it’s limiting production now in part to launch 2026 models.
Alex Oyler, North American director of auto research firm SBD Automotive, said: “For Rivian, it’s do-or-die time.”

Oyler noted: “We saw with Tesla that the key to profitability is scale, and you can’t scale if your cheapest vehicle is $70,000. So they need that plant online to achieve a level of scale of R2 and ultimately R3.”
Sales growth is slowing for electric vehicles in the United States, rising only 1.5% in 2025’s first half, according to Cox Automotive.
Tesla accounted for almost 45% of U.S. electric vehicle sales in that period, according to Cox.
General Motors’ slice of American EV sales has climbed to 13%.
Rivian had a 3% share in the first half of the year, behind Tesla and six traditional automakers.
Excluding Tesla, Rivian is the most successful of the startup automakers.
The company initially tapped a largely unfilled niche: demand for electric pickups and SUVs.
But the competition now includes Ford’s F-150 Lightning and the electric Chevrolet Silverado.
After an initial public offering in 2021, Rivian shares have fallen by more than 80%, while automaker shares overall have outpaced the broader stock market.
Rivian lost $1.66 billion in 2025’s first half.
Some automakers’ ardor for electric vehicles is cooling.
Stellantis last week canceled Ram’s electric truck program.
Ford has delayed production at a new Tennessee plant.
General Motors abandoned plans to build electric vehicles at a suburban Detroit plant.
Sam Fiorani, vice president at AutoForecast Solutions, said: “With all the competition out there in this market and the slowing growth of EVs, it does not play in Rivian’s favor.”
Fiorani added: “However, there still is an EV market out there.”
Georgia has pledged $1.5 billion of incentives to Rivian in exchange for 7,500 company jobs paying at least $56,000 a year on average.
Rivian can’t benefit from most incentives unless it meets employment goals, but the state is already spending $175 million to buy and grade land and improve roads.

Georgia Republican Gov. Brian Kemp has said he wants to make Georgia “the electric mobility capital of America,” acknowledges Rivian faces bumps, but says he remains confident the company can fulfill its promises.
While Tesla has thousands of employees in California and Texas, some new electric vehicle plants have sputtered.
Two separate EV makers that hoped to assemble vehicles in a former GM plant in Lordstown, Ohio, went bankrupt.
Georgia’s Hyundai complex near Savannah is faring better, with production underway.
However, a battery plant there has been delayed by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement arresting 475 people on site, including more than 300 South Koreans.
Rivian was supposed to be making trucks by now at the 2,000-acre (800-hectare) site near Social Circle, about 45 miles (70 kilometers) east of Atlanta.
As the company burned through cash in 2024, it paused construction.
German automaker Volkswagen agreed to invest $5.8 billion in Rivian in exchange for software and electrical technology.
Then-President Joe Biden’s administration in November agreed to loan Rivian $6.6 billion to build the Georgia plant.
Despite the Trump administration’s hostility toward EVs, Hoffman said Rivian hopes the U.S. Department of Energy will distribute the loan money, arguing it will boost domestic manufacturing.
Some neighbors still oppose the plant.
Rivian faces opposition from some residents who say the plant is an inappropriate neighbor to farms and will pollute the groundwater.
Eddie Clay, who lives less than a mile away, said: “I planned on dying and retiring on the front porch and the biggest project in Georgia has to go next door to me, of all places in the country?”
Clay says his well water turned mud-choked after excavation at the Rivian site.

There are other challenges for Rivian, including tariffs costing $2,000 per vehicle, the Trump administration ending a tax-credit program that will cost the company $140 million in revenue this year, and long-term threats from low-priced, cutting-edge Chinese EVs.
Hoffman says Rivian is “in this for the long haul.”
Hoffman stated: “We think that we can compete with anyone out there and that once given the opportunity, we’re going to excel,”
source_title: At a truly awful time to build an electric vehicle plant in the U.S., Tesla's distant rival breaks ground on $5b Georgia facility | Fortune

facts: Rivian announced it is increasing the initial production capacity of its future manufacturing facility in Stanton Springs North, Georgia to 300,000 vehicles per year.
The company has invested US$5bn in its new facility where it will manufacture the R2 SUV.
Rivian also operates a plant in Normal, Illinois where it works on commercial vans and the Rivian R1.
Progress on the Georgia facility will accelerate this summer as the facility’s primary buildings begin to take shape.
Rivian Founder and CEO RJ Scaringe told Reuters that the company is working on other variants of the R2 which are yet to be announced.
The increase to 300,000 vehicles in Stanton Springs represents a significant increase in capacity over the initially planned 200,000 units.
Rivian says it increased capacity to facilitate a lower cost per unit while providing significant room for future expansion of capacity in later phases.
In a press release, Rivian stated it remains on track for vehicle production to begin in late 2028.
RJ Scaringe said: “We’re very excited to partner with the US Department of Energy to grow our manufacturing footprint in Georgia.”

RJ Scaringe said: “R2 dramatically expands our market opportunity. The thousands of dedicated people who will soon work in our Georgia plant will be instrumental to Rivian’s growth as we scale American manufacturing and work to ensure that the US retains its leadership in innovation and technology.”
Rivian is working on an undisclosed variant of its R2, including a potential pickup variant.
RJ Scaringe told Reuters regarding the Georgia plant: "What we're building in Georgia allows for different variations."
Rivian’s R2, a mid-size electric SUV, is set to begin deliveries in Spring of 2026.
The company says that the R2 lineup will offer up to 656 horsepower and 0 to 60 mph in 3.6 seconds.
The R2 will offer an estimated range of up to 345 miles.
The vehicle is set to offer acceleration from 50 to 70 mph in as quick as 1.55 seconds on the highway.
Rivian has worked with the US Department of Energy’s Office of Energy Dominance Financing to update a loan central to its Georgia manufacturing plant.
As construction commences in 2026, Rivian now expects to first draw on the loan by early 2027.
The total loan is aligned with the updated facility design and roadmap at up to US$4.5bn.
Rivian’s loan amount was reduced from the initially projected figure of US$6.6bn.
RJ Scaringe told Reuters that the loan structure changed from two phases to a single phase: "whereas before the loan was across two phases, it's now the US$4.5bn loan across a single phase."
RJ Scaringe stated regarding the loan: "I look at it as it reflects the level of confidence we have on our midsize platform."
The Rivian R2 is central to Rivian’s partnership with Uber.
Rivian is expected to build up to 50,000 robotaxis at its Georgia plant beginning in late 2028.
Initial deployments of Rivian's Robotaxis are planned for San Francisco and Miami in 2028, scaling to 25 cities through 2031.
Uber plans to invest US$1.25bn in Rivian through 2031, subject to the achievement of milestones.
source_title: Rivian's Plans to Manufacture 300,000 EV SUVs in Georgia | EV Magazine

facts: The Kia production factory in West Point, Georgia has had a busy start to the year.
In the midst of supply chain issues and labor shortages, the plant introduced a new president and CEO.
Stuart Countess spoke to WABE’s “All Things Considered” Tuesday at the Georgia Chamber’s “Future of Georgia Summit” at Georgia Tech.

links.context: Newsletter signup and full story access

source_title: Kia Georgia CEO talks supply chain disruption and the coming electric vehicle age – WABE

facts: Operates as Kia Motors Corporation's first manufacturing site in North America.
Located in West Point, Georgia.
The facility sits on about 2,200 acres.
Began mass production on November 16, 2009.
Produces the Sorento, the Telluride, and the K5 mid-size sedan.
Has the capacity to assemble up to 360,000 vehicles per year from U.S.- and globally sourced parts.
Operates within the motor vehicle manufacturing industry.
Serves as a major production hub for Kia in North America.
Supports thousands of jobs.
Sits within Georgia's automotive ecosystem.
Recent developments in 2026 include a collaboration with The Ray C. Anderson Foundation to support infrastructure around the West Point test track.
source_title: Kia Georgia, Inc. Company Overview, Contact Details & Competitors | LeadIQ

facts: Groundbreaking celebrations were held on a Tuesday for the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant (HMGMA).
Production at the dedicated electric vehicle (EV) and battery plant facility is expected to begin in the first half of 2025.
Kia established a production facility in West Point, Georgia, in 2006.
The Kia West Point facility accounts for nearly 40 percent of all Kia sales in the U.S.
Kia was the first company to open an auto assembly plant in Georgia.
Sean Yoon is the president and CEO of Kia North America and Kia America.
Kia plans to produce EVs in the U.S., building on its success and growth in Georgia.
Together with its suppliers, Kia Georgia has helped create more than 14,000 jobs.
The HMGMA project was announced five months prior to the article's publication date.
HMGMA is projected to create more than 8,100 jobs over the next few years.
Hyundai Motor Group aims to establish a stable supply chain for EV batteries and other EV components in the U.S. market through this project.
Kia Georgia manufactures the Telluride, Sorento, and Sportage SUVs as well as the K5 midsize sedan.
More than 90 percent of the vehicles assembled at Kia Georgia are destined for dealerships across America.
The Kia West Point factory has provided over $10 million in community support to Georgia since 2009.
Over $6 million of that support has funded educational programs, including the THINC College & Career Academy in LaGrange and SAE’s “A World in Motion.”
source_title: Production of Electric Vehicles in Georgia to Begin in First Half of 2025, Officials Say • The Georgia Virtue

facts: SK Battery America, a subsidiary of South Korea-based SK On, is laying off hundreds of workers from its production facility in Commerce, Georgia.
The layoffs are occurring as electric vehicle battery demand slows and federal incentives dry up.
The producer of Nickel-Cobalt-Manganese lithium-ion battery cells filed a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification on Friday.
The notification notified the state of Georgia of job cuts affecting 958 people.
Impacted workers will receive pay and benefits for the next 60 days, confirmed by a spokesperson via email to Manufacturing Dive.
Friday, March 6 was the last day of work for the affected employees.
The Commerce site previously had 2,566 workers before reductions.
Workforce reductions brought the Commerce site headcount down to 1,600.
Joe Guy Collier serves as SK America’s spokesperson and stated the company will work with affected workers during the transition.
SK Battery America issued a statement affirming its commitment to Georgia and building a robust U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing.
The company is actively pursuing future customers in the Battery Energy Storage System arena.

The EV battery industry is currently navigating demand challenges, with many automakers and battery companies implementing cutbacks due to slower sales and reduced federal incentives under the current administration, according to the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas.
Automakers and battery companies are also shifting focus to cater to growing data center demand for backup power and energy storage systems.
In December, Ford Motor Co., a customer of SK, announced it was shifting its electrification plans to include hybrid vehicles and smaller, more affordable EVs.
Ford is simultaneously expanding its energy storage business.
The automaker expects to record a $19.5 billion write-down due to its strategic shift.
Ford dissolved its BlueOval SK battery joint venture with SK On as it moves away from electric vehicles.
Ford canceled the electric F-150 Lightning production due to sluggish sales.
In 2023, SK America announced plans to expand its Commerce site workforce by several hundred employees to meet rising EV demand, exceeding an initial goal of 2,600 employees.
The Occupational Safety and Health Administration previously fined the EV battery maker twice for exposing workers to respiratory hazards.
Law firm Strauss Borrelli announced an investigation into SK Battery for allegedly failing to notify the 958 employees in compliance with the WARN Act.
The WARN Act mandates that employers provide written notification to workers at least 60 days before a plant closure or job cuts take effect.
Spokesperson Joe Guy Collier stated that SK America’s workforce reduction complies with WARN guidelines.
source_title: SK Battery America lays off nearly 1,000 workers at Georgia plant | WardsAuto

facts: All electric vehicles manufactured by Hyundai Motor Group at its assembly plant near Savannah since April 1 qualify for a $7,500 federal tax credit now that their batteries are sourced from a northeast Georgia factory.
SK Battery America in Commerce is supplying the lithium battery packs to the Hyundai Metaplant, located along I-16 in Bryan County.
EVs are powered by batteries, not gasoline engines, and the battery pack is the largest component.
Before the deal with SK Battery, the IONIQ 5s coming off the Savannah assembly line failed to meet the tax credit’s requirement.
To be eligible for the full credit, 60% of an EV’s battery components must be made domestically.
source_title: Certain Georgia-made Hyundai EVs now qualify for federal tax credit

facts: Cut 958 jobs at its North Georgia plant.
Electric vehicle demand is slowing.
Federal support is declining.
source_title: SK Battery America cuts nearly 1,000 jobs at Georgia plant - Atlanta Business Chronicle

facts: The 2025 HL-GA Battery Company Industry Expo was held December 4, 2025 in the Engineering Research Building at Georgia Southern University.
HL-GA Battery Company LLC represents a collaboration between Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution.
HL-GA Battery Company manufactures state-of-the-art lithium-ion battery cells for electric vehicles.
The facility is strategically located at the HMGMA Metaplant site near Savannah, Georgia.
Upon completion, the factory will have a production capacity of 30GWh.
This capacity is enough to power 300,000 electric and hybrid vehicles annually.
HL-GA Battery Company LLC served as the Title Sponsor for the 2025 Industry Expo Project Report Out (PRO) Day.
The sponsorship underwrote the use of RocketJudge as the judging platform, lunch, and the Awards Reception.
Project winners were chosen in three categories: Computing; Electrical and Computer Engineering; Manufacturing Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, and Civil Engineering and Construction.
source_title: Industry Expo – College of Engineering & Computing - Georgia Southern University

facts: Federal agents arrested 475 workers at a construction site in Georgia on Sept. 5, the Department of Justice announced.
The Ellabell, Georgia, facility is the future home of a $4.3 billion electric vehicle battery facility co-owned by Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution.
The vast majority of the arrested workers were South Korean.
White House border czar Tom Homan told CNN that the administration will continue to ramp up raids on U.S. workplaces.
Estimates place the total share of undocumented construction workers at about 23%.

Immigration enforcement has affected about one in three construction firms, according to a survey released Aug. 28 by the Associated General Contractors of America and the National Center for Construction Education and Research.
Roughly nine in ten construction firms report having openings for craft construction workers.
The lack of skilled workforce has been the largest cause of project delays, per the AGC survey.
Hyundai and LG invested $4.3 billion to establish the HL-GA Battery Company plant.
Plans call for beginning production of EV batteries in early 2026, per the project website.
Both Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution are based in South Korea.
No Hyundai employees were arrested at the site.
Forty-seven LG employees were detained during the raid.
Most of the arrested workers were employed by subcontractors.
Hyundai stated it has a “zero tolerance” policy for those who do not follow the law and expects the same from project partners.
Hyundai is reviewing its processes to ensure all parties maintain high standards of legal compliance, including thorough vetting of employment practices by contractors and subcontractors.
LG is working closely with the Korean government regarding detained employees and is working for their release.
LG stated it will do its utmost to swiftly resolve the issue and ensure the safe and prompt return of its employees and partner company members.
Negotiations to return workers to South Korea moved quickly, per Reuters.
Three hundred detained workers will be processed, released, and returned via a chartered plane, according to the South Korean presidential office on Sunday.
The megaproject is an example of an onshoring push to benefit investments by foreign companies on U.S. soil.
Tom Homan claimed employers hire undocumented workers to work them harder, pay them less, and undercut competition that hires U.S. citizen employees, driving wages down.
President Donald Trump posted on Truth Social encouraging foreign companies to continue planning projects in the U.S. legally, bring technical talent, and hire and train American workers.
source_title: After 475 arrests at Georgia jobsite, feds vow more raids | Construction Dive

facts: Hyundai Mobis has secured $940M for electrification investments in North America.
The planned plants are located in Alabama and Georgia and will assemble battery systems and power electronics.
This funding marks Hyundai Mobis' first overseas Green Loan.
Hyundai Mobis issued green bonds domestically in 2021 to advance eco-friendly projects.
This is the first occasion Hyundai Mobis has utilized the Green Loan method for environmentally-friendly business investments abroad.
Green Loans refer to funding methods for eco-friendly businesses, such as electric vehicles and renewable energy.
Hyundai Mobis currently operates electrification production hubs in nine locations globally (six domestic and three overseas).
Hyundai Mobis is establishing six new electrification production hubs in South Korea, North America, and Indonesia.
This $940M investment follows the company's successful funding for a battery cell joint venture factory in Indonesia last year.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis Secures $940M to Assemble EV Batteries and Power Electronics in Alabama and Georgia - EV Tech Insider

facts: Georgia Governor Brian Kemp announced that Hyundai Mobis would invest $926 million in an Electric Vehicle Power Electric system plant in Bryan County.
The project will hire at least 1,500 people.
Gov. Kemp stated the groundbreaking of Hyundai’s new electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facility in Bryan County would unleash transformational job creation and investment in that region.
This announcement marks the second supplier to locate in the area within two weeks.
Hyundai Mobis has operated a manufacturing factory in West Point with almost 1,200 employees since 2009.
The West Point factory is the largest Tier 1 supplier to Kia Georgia.
The West Point plant supplies car manufacturers with complete cockpit modules, chassis modules, and bumper assemblies as part of the Hyundai Mobis family.
H.S. Oh, Vice President of Hyundai Mobis Electric Powertrain Business Unit, stated the investment reflects an acceleration in the development of the EV supply chain in Georgia’s auto industry.
H.S. Oh noted Hyundai Mobis will be a major production player in the EV market and will trigger more growth within the sector.
H.S. Oh added that Mobis is looking forward to providing high-quality work opportunities to the growing local workforce.
The new 1,200,000-square-foot building is expected to provide over 900,000 EV Power Electric systems and 450,000 Integrated Charging Control units annually.
Production will be supplied to several factories in Georgia and one factory in Alabama.
Construction is expected to begin as early as January 2023 at the Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development Certified Belfast Commerce Park in Richmond Hill.
Construction officials predict production will begin sometime in 2024.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated suppliers like Hyundai Mobis are transitioning to the jobs of the future and becoming part of Georgia’s growing EV ecosystem.
In May, GDEcD predicted that non-affiliated HMGMA suppliers in connection with the project would invest an additional $1 billion into Georgia.
GDEcD reported that the $1 billion prediction had already been blown past in about two weeks.
GDEcD mentioned utilizing partners like Georgia Quick Start and sister agencies to prepare Georgia’s workforce for the jobs being created.
Officials congratulated Bryan County, the City of Richmond Hill, the Georgia Ports Authority, and Hyundai Mobis on the project.
source_title: New Hyundai Metaplant to bring 1,500 jobs to Bryan Co., Gov. Kemp says

facts: Hyundai MOBIS Georgia is located at 7001 Kia Parkway, West Point, GA 31833, United States.
Established on May 17, 2007, the facility operates as a three-shift manufacturing operation.
The plant functions as a Tier 1 supplier to Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia, LLC (KMMG).
MOBIS Georgia assembles four major vehicle modules: Front Chassis, Rear Chassis, Cockpit, and Front End.
The facility also produces front and rear bumpers for the three vehicles manufactured by KMMG.
Using components from domestic and overseas suppliers, the plant utilizes a just-in-time, just-in-sequence production system.
MOBIS Georgia supplies parts for over 1,400 vehicles per day.
The facility tour agenda includes registration and networking from 10:00 to 10:30 am, followed by a corporate presentation and safety briefing from 10:30 to 10:45 am.
A plant tour runs from 10:45 am to 12:00 pm, concluding with a wrap-up and summary from 12:00 to 12:30 pm.
Closed-toe shoes are mandatory for attendees, while high-heeled shoes, press coverage, and photos are prohibited.
Registration is capped at two people per company to allow space for other attendees.
A pre-tour networking session, GMA LaGrange Coffee & Connections, takes place from 7:30 am to 9:00 am at THINC Academy and requires separate registration via the organization's events page.
source_title: Hyundai MOBIS Tour - West Point -  Georgia Manufacturing Alliance

facts: Hyundai Mobis, a global leader in advanced automotive technology, introduced its revolutionary Holographic Windshield Display at CES 2025.
The holographic windshield display was developed in collaboration with ZEISS, a world-renowned German optics company.
The technology transforms the entire windshield into an immersive, transparent screen that blends safety, convenience, and aesthetics.
The system was demonstrated in a Kia EV9 electric vehicle.
It projects critical driving information, including navigation prompts, speed, and multimedia options, directly onto the windshield.
The display eliminates the need for physical dashboard screens to offer a seamless and distraction-free interface.
From the outside, the display looks like an ordinary piece of glass.
The system delivers crystal-clear visuals in bright sunlight due to high brightness and precise colour reproduction.
A specially embedded Holographic Optical Element (HOE) uses light diffraction to project images straight to the driver’s eyes.
Hyundai Mobis CEO Lee Gyu Suk and ZEISS CEO Karl Lamprecht held a high-profile meeting at CES to discuss the future of their partnership.
Both CEOs expressed optimism about scaling the technology for mass production.
The partners aim to roll out the holographic windshield display globally by 2027.
Lee Gyu Suk remarked that the synergy between Hyundai Mobis’s innovative vision and ZEISS’s optical precision will bring the futuristic concept to the roads.
The technology is currently in the pre-production phase.
Hyundai Mobis aims to complete the final stages of development by mid-2026.
A full-scale market launch is planned for 2027.
Hyundai Mobis also showcased other next-generation innovations at CES 2025, including advanced electrification components for EVs and hybrid vehicles.
Other showcased technologies include vehicle electronics for smarter driving, chassis systems for improved performance and comfort, and innovative lighting solutions.
The holographic display reduces driver distraction by keeping critical information in the driver’s line of sight.
The innovation aligns with the broader push for autonomous and semi-autonomous vehicles requiring clear, real-time information displays.
Experts have hailed the holographic windshield as a game-changer with potential applications extending to public transport and aviation.
Developing the technology for mass production faces challenges related to manufacturing scalability and cost efficiency.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis and ZEISS Unveil Holographic Windshield Display - Highways Today

facts: Hyundai Glovis operates a logistics base in West Point, Georgia.
The West Point, Georgia base is crucial for the company's involvement in the US automotive supply chain, supporting inland transportation and complete knock-down (CKD) parts distribution.
Hyundai Glovis primarily serves its parent group affiliates, Hyundai Motor Co. and Kia Corp., managing logistics for their finished vehicles and CKD/SKD parts for international production facilities.
The company is targeting Chinese electric vehicle manufacturers, exemplified by a September 2024 memorandum of understanding with BYD Co. to optimize vessel capacity.
Hyundai Glovis aims to expand non-affiliated sales to over 40% by 2030, moving beyond its historical reliance on the Hyundai Motor Group.
The company is exploring collaborations with other global automakers, such as Volkswagen AG.
Hyundai Glovis handles specialized transportation for finished vehicles, raw materials, industrial equipment, and breakbulk cargo like heavy machinery.
In 2024, the distribution segment (including used car trading and CKD parts) generated KRW 13.3 trillion in revenue.
The logistics segment reported Q1 2025 revenue of KRW 2.458 trillion, an 8.1% increase.
SKD-related revenue increased by 75% between 2020 and 2024.
Overseas logistics revenue surged by 93% during the same period (2020-2024).
Hyundai Glovis is investing approximately $750 million for six new LNG dual-fuel vessels to be delivered by the end of 2028.
The company made a $110 million investment in Asiana Airlines' cargo division in 2024 to expand air logistics capabilities.
Hyundai Glovis operates a global network with 55 bases in Korea and 97 overseas bases, supporting integrated logistics across America, Asia, Europe, the Middle East, and Oceania.
The company is venturing into waste EV battery businesses to cater to market trends towards sustainability.
Hyundai Glovis supports increased overseas production by Hyundai Motor Group, particularly in the US, which boosts CKD and inland transportation volumes.
source_title: What is Customer Demographics and Target Market of Hyundai Glovis Company? – MatrixBCG.com

facts: Hyundai Motor Group is hiring locally for its automobile assembly facility and neighboring electric vehicle battery plant in the Savannah area.
The automobile assembly facility and neighboring electric vehicle battery plant will directly employ 8,100 workers.
Suppliers have already committed to hiring nearly 5,000 more workers to make bumpers, consoles, and other components.
Hyundai officials are determined to hire locally as much as possible and will offer competitive wages and compensation packages to attract workers away from existing employers.

State officials envision the initial labor cannibalization being offset in the long term as job demand coaxes Savannah young adults to stay in the local market rather than move to Atlanta or other employment centers.
The Hyundai Metaplant site will host multiple Hyundai affiliates, including the EV assembly plant, the battery factory, and onsite subsidiaries that manufacture key parts and materials.
Hyundai's logistics arm, Hyundai Glovis, will operate from the Metaplant site.
The assembly facility alone will employ 2,600 workers, which includes 2,000 hourly and assembly line employees.
Georgia House Rep. Ron Stephens represents parts of Savannah's southside, West Chatham, and South Bryan county.
Georgia's Workforce Ready Career Pathway and Georgia Quick Start programs are well positioned to produce a significant number of local laborers over time.
Per an incentives agreement with the state, Hyundai must create 8,100 jobs by December 2031.
