Hyundai intends to hire its first 500 employees before the end of this year and another 700 in 2024 ahead of the assembly plant's opening in early 2025.

Existing Savannah employers are beefing up their compensation packages and offering new incentives to retain employees, according to Maria Whitfield, director of workforce for the Savannah Joint Development Authority.
Maria Whitfield stated that laborers are free to choose where they want to work and companies cannot discriminate against applicants from other local employers like Gulfstream.
Tal Loos, vice president of economic development at Savannah Tech, noted that companies may entice workers to stay by promoting flexible work hours or other incentives.

Rep. Stephens, a retired pharmacist, observed that existing industries may consider additional incentives to keep talent flowing, though likely not as aggressively as past chain drugstore signing bonuses.
Hyundai's Park hopes the company's hiring will have a limited impact on other local manufacturers like Gulfstream, noting significant differences between automobile and aviation industry expertise.
Park stated that while they can hire some good engineers from the aviation sector, the expertise is quite different, resulting in no cannibalization between the two large companies.
Georgia House Rep. Jesse Petrea represents Chatham's islands and coastal Bryan County.
Rep. Petrea stated that workers developing skills and work ethic who receive new opportunities with greater economic potential are benefiting communities and society by securing good jobs.
Small businesses are likely to feel the ripple effects of Hyundai's hiring as blue-collar workers transition to new sectors.
Industry reports indicate that almost half of all employees working in the U.S. are employed by a small business, making them vulnerable to worker poaching.
Workers leaving other manufacturers or warehousing roles to move up will create openings for others in hourly jobs within hospitality and retail sectors.
Hyundai's Park stressed that hiring locally promotes a sense of community within the workforce, emphasizing that collaboration, cooperation, and close communication are vital.
Brent Stubbs, senior manager of learning and development for the Hyundai Metaplant, is utilizing creative strategies to find talent within the state.
Stubbs stated that efforts are 100% focused locally to ensure the community benefits from the new jobs.
source_title: Hyundai to focus hiring on Savannah area. The local labor market is bracing for impact. - AOL

facts: Hyundai Motor Group is expanding its energy-saving measures across its affiliates amid ongoing tensions in the Middle East.
The auto giant is expanding its five-day vehicle rotation system, which was previously imposed on Hyundai Motor and Kia headquarters, to all Hyundai Motor Group affiliates.
The company will increase the number of commuter shuttle services to discourage the use of personal vehicles.
Hyundai Motor Group’s measure comes amid continued conflict in the Middle East, which is affecting global supply chains, and rising fuel prices.

The company will implement a new AI-based system that cuts off power and lighting in unused spaces like corridors, parking lots and empty rooms, based on feeds from closed-circuit cameras and motion sensors.
Heating, cooling and computer power usage will also be regulated under the AI system.
Hyundai Motor Group will minimize business trips within Korea, replacing them with online meetings to reduce car usage.
When business trips are unavoidable, electric and hydrogen fuel cell vehicles will be prioritized.
The firm will focus on improving the efficiency of various machines and upgrading its aging facilities, including expanded inspections for electrical or oil leaks on site.
Logistics affiliate Hyundai Glovis will optimize maritime routes and reduce vessel speeds to conserve fuel.
As part of long-term measures, Hyundai Motor Group will install solar panels on its production sites, parking lots and car maintenance shops to improve its energy storage system.
The firm will expand its power purchase agreement, which allows Hyundai to purchase renewable energy at a fixed price.
The automaker will gradually replace its commuter buses with hydrogen fuel-cell buses.

Hyundai Motor Group stated in a press release on Sunday: “We have established practical energy-saving measures to help overcome the national energy crisis, and we plan to actively implement the plans across all our affiliates.”
Hyundai Motor Group added: “Both the company and our employees will work together to participate in the government’s energy conservation policies.”
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group expands energy-saving measures across affiliates

facts: LG Energy Solution is moving ahead with construction at U.S. plants despite an immigration raid this month at a Georgia battery plant it’s building with Hyundai Motor Group.
source_title: LG Energy working to minimize impact after U.S. immigration raid - Automotive News

facts: South Korean EV battery maker LG Energy Solution faced the largest single-site immigration raid in United States history.
Operation Low Voltage swept more than 300 Korean workers accused of being in the U.S. illegally.
The raid undercut LG Energy Solution’s efforts to open a factory to supply Hyundai’s electric vehicle manufacturing facility near Savannah.
Operation Low Voltage and its sweep won’t deter LG from doing business in the country in the future.
An LG official said Thursday the company still plans to locate half its total global battery cell-making capacity in the U.S.
LG’s Philip Lienert said in an email: “The U.S. is an extremely important market for us.”
source_title: LG stays committed to U.S., Georgia factory despite ICE raid

facts: South Korean company LG Energy Solution raised the stakes in its fight with SK Innovation, saying it could build its own factory in Georgia to make batteries for electric vehicles.
SK is building a plant in Commerce.
The U.S. International Trade Commission ruled last month that technology the company plans to use there was stolen from LG.
Because of that, the commission said, SK can make batteries in Georgia for a limited time before it must halt production.
Since the ruling, LG has pressured SK to reach a financial settlement.
Now LG has suggested it can open a plant in Georgia to make the electric vehicle batteries.
source_title: LG Energy Solution could manufacture batteries for electric vehicles in Georgia

facts: Solvay Specialty Polymers received a $178 million federal grant to build a companion facility to produce battery components for electric vehicles in Augusta.
The proposed new plant could bring as many as 500 construction jobs and 100 manufacturing jobs to the Augusta area.
U.S. Sen Raphael Warnock's office announced the job projections in a statement on Wednesday.
Solvay won the grant through the Battery Materials Processing and Battery Component Manufacturing and Recycling program.
The program is funded through the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law enacted in 2021, according to the U.S. Department of Energy.

Cal Wray, executive director of the Augusta Economic Development Authority, stated that the real economic impact numbers for the Augusta-Richmond County Metropolitan Statistical Area will likely end up being higher than the grant amount.
The Augusta Economic Development Authority wrote a letter to the DOE earlier this year in support of Solvay's grant application.
Cal Wray noted that between a couple-hundred million investment and a couple-hundred jobs that are well-paid, he thinks it is great for the area.
The Augusta-Richmond County Metropolitan Statistical Area has a concentration of about 23,000 people who work in manufacturing.
Solvay provides some of the best paid manufacturing jobs in the area.
The factory is expected to manufacture electric battery-grade binders and separator coatings.
These components are crucial for lithium-ion batteries that power EVs.
Belgium-headquartered Solvay acquired its current Augusta facility from BP Amoco in 2000.
Since acquiring the facility, Solvay has regularly invested in increasing its product lines and capacity.
In 2015, Solvay announced an $85 million local expansion at the Augusta facility.
Cal Wray noted that Aurubis and Denkai are already in the same industry in Augusta alongside Solvay's continually growing facility.
In June, German company Aurubis AG broke ground in south Richmond County on a $340 million copper recycling and smelting plant dubbed Aurubis Richmond.
The Aurubis Richmond plant will process about 90,000 tons of complex recycling materials annually to extract about 35,000 tons of blister copper.
Copper is one of the most in-demand finished products that goes into making EVs.
In July, Japanese manufacturer Denkai America announced the construction of a $430 million south Augusta manufacturing plant.
Denkai produces copper foil used in printed electronics such as circuit boards.
U.S. Sen Raphael Warnock stated that Georgia is open for business and today's announcement is good news for both Georgia's growing clean energy economy and Augusta workers.

Mike Finelli, president of Solvay Growth Platforms and chief North American officer, said the company is proud to receive the federal government's support for manufacturing technologies that will provide the U.S. with critical raw materials for building an independent, sustainable EV value chain.
Finelli stated the grant reaffirms Solvay's leadership in developing a robust and resilient domestic battery supply chain that brings important clean energy jobs to the U.S.
source_title: Augusta polymers plant wins $178 million grant to build electric-vehicle battery parts

entity_name: U.S. E-Waste and Critical Mineral Recycling Market
canonical_name: U.S. E-Waste and Critical Mineral Recycling Market
facts: The U.S. is recycling old electronics to reduce its dependence on China for rare earth metals.
Companies like Glencore, Illumynt, and Cyclic Materials are expanding operations to extract metals from e-waste.
Tariffs, trade war tensions, and long mining timelines are pushing the U.S. toward faster recycling solutions.
The Pentagon grabbed a big equity stake in MP Materials, the only company currently digging rare earths out of U.S. soil.
President Trump tightened his grip on global copper supplies with a 50% import tariff, pushing prices to an all-time high.
The U.S. is targeting dead phones, broken EV batteries, trashed servers, and ancient laptops piling up in warehouses and landfills for recycling.
The goal is to recycle metals like neodymium, praseodymium, and terbium out of e-waste instead of waiting 30 years for new mines.
Companies like Glencore, Full Circle Electronics, Cyclic Materials, and startups like Illumynt are fighting for e-waste as raw material in a geopolitical fight against China.
These metals are needed for fighter jets, power tools, MRI machines, wind turbines, and high-end tech used by the military or industry.
Kunal Sinha, global head of recycling at Glencore, stated that recycling was not taken seriously until recently.
Kunal Sinha noted, “A lot of people are still sleeping at the wheel and don’t realize how big this can be.”
Glencore has been processing old electronics at its smelter in Quebec for decades, turning waste into copper slabs and extracting gold, silver, palladium, platinum, and more.
Fifteen percent of Glencore’s Quebec smelter feedstock now comes from recycled electronics.
Glencore signed a multi-year deal with Cyclic to supply them with recycled copper pulled from EV motors, data center parts, and wind turbines.
Cyclic is spending over $20 million building a new U.S. plant in Mesa, Arizona.
Germany’s Wieland started a $100 million recycling facility in Kentucky in 2022.
Aurubis followed with an $800 million multi-metal plant in Georgia.
Aurubis CEO Toralf Haag said the Aurubis Richmond facility “will allow us to keep strategically important metals in the economy,” aiming to cut down dependence on China.
John Mitchell, president of the Global Electronics Association, pointed out that most electronics imported into the U.S. are loaded with metals like copper, gold, and steel.
John Mitchell stated, “There’s a great opportunity to actually have the tariffs be an impetus for greater recycling in this country.”
Recycling is fast because waste flows daily, unlike new mines which take decades to scale.
Full Circle Electronics CEO Dave Daily said they have seen a spike in dumped electronics because businesses are rushing upgrades ahead of rising prices.
Dave Daily noted, “We’re definitely seeing a bit of an influx of [e-waste] coming into our warehouses.”
Full Circle tears old gear into 40 to 50 material types and sells them off to recyclers hunting metals for resale.
Lithium-ion batteries have become a hot feedstock, packed with lithium, cobalt, nickel, manganese, and aluminum.
Recyclers like Ascend Elements, Redwood Materials, Cirba Solutions, and American Battery Technology are trying to tap battery materials to feed EV battery production.
These recyclers rely on the 45X tax credit created under Biden, a program that could get slashed in Trump’s new spending plan.
Kunal Sinha warned, “Be careful not to build a recycling company on the back of one tax credit, because it can be short-lived.”
Illumynt is harvesting rare earths from dead hard drives removed from data centers.
In April, Western Digital teamed up with Microsoft, Critical Materials Recycling, and PedalPoint Recycling to pull out copper, gold, aluminum, steel, and rare earths from old drives.

The ITAD industry is booming, with waste disposal firms like Waste Management, Clean Harbors, and specialists like Sims Lifecycle Services and Electronic Recyclers International wiping data and breaking down devices.
In 2022, nearly 62 million metric tons of e-waste were produced worldwide, up 82% since 2010.
The U.S. made almost 8 million metric tons of e-waste, but only 15–20% was recycled.
IBISWorld said the U.S. e-waste recycling market generated $28.1 billion last year, growing 8% a year.
In April 2025, China restricted exports of rare earth magnets in retaliation for new Trump tariffs.
Ford had to shut down some factories due to magnet shortages.
In June, China granted a few six-month export licenses to ease the squeeze, but shipments are still far from normal.
China still controls 90% of the market, from mining to refining to processing.
Biden gave MP Materials $45 million before the Pentagon move.
In April, the Interior Department greenlit development on the Colosseum rare earth project, deep inside California’s Mojave National Preserve.
The site, owned by Australia’s Dateline Resources, could become the second U.S. rare earth mine.
Glencore put $327.5 million into Li-Cycle, a battery recycler based in Toronto.
Li-Cycle filed for Chapter 15 bankruptcy in May after starting construction on a plant in Rochester, New York.
Glencore is trying to scoop up the failed project with a $40 million bid.
Kunal Sinha said the current environment will lead to more startups and investments, adding, “We are investing ourselves.”
Sinha warned against betting too much on hype or just new tech, stating, “Don’t invest on the hype,” but rather “on the fundamentals.”
source_title: U.S. eyes old tech as ammo in rare earth fight with China - Cryptopolitan

facts: Aurubis AG marked the official ribbon-cutting ceremony for its state-of-the-art recycling facility in Augusta, Georgia on Saturday, September 21, 2024.
The facility, named Aurubis Richmond, is the first-ever secondary smelter of its kind for multimetal recycling in the United States.
Construction of the plant took over two years, following a groundbreaking in June 2022.
At the end of 2022, Aurubis made a strategic decision to fast-track the expansion of the plant and invest a total of around $800 million.
The expanded facility will be able to process up to 180,000 tons of complex recycling materials annually.
The processed materials include circuit boards, copper cables, and other metal-containing products.
Aurubis Richmond is expected to provide over 230 high-quality jobs in the greater Augusta area.
The company pledged a community benefit agreement with Augusta Technical College, Aiken Technical College, and the Richmond County School System.
As part of the community benefit agreement, Aurubis Richmond will invest in scholarships and job training programs for young workers.
Aurubis Richmond's recycled metals are essential for key industries including renewable energy, electric vehicles, and battery manufacturing.
The facility is equipped with advanced technology that meets environmental standards set by both the State of Georgia and federal regulators.
Aurubis has a strategic vision to be carbon-neutral well before 2050.
Governor Brian Kemp and First Lady Marty Kemp attended the ribbon-cutting ceremony alongside Aurubis leaders and local officials.
Aurubis CEO Toralf Haag stated that Aurubis Richmond will keep strategically important metals in the economy, making US supply chains more independent.
David Schultheis, President and Managing Director of Aurubis Richmond, described the facility as a major player in the North American multimetal supply chain.
Governor Brian Kemp noted that Aurubis's goals aligned with Georgia's to create good-paying jobs and position the state as a leader in an emerging electric industry.
German-based parent company Aurubis AG is a leading global provider of non-ferrous metals and one of the largest copper recyclers worldwide for over 150 years.
COO Inge Hofkens participated in conversations with Governor Brian Kemp during the event.
source_title: Aurubis celebrates ribbon-cutting at the first multimetal recycling plant in the United States

entity_name: Korea Trade-Investment Promotion Agency (KOTRA) Atlanta Office
facts: Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) Commissioner Pat Wilson joined Mr. Soo Deuk Sohn, Senior Executive Vice President of KOTRA, to announce the opening of the new KOTRA overseas office in Atlanta.
KOTRA is the Government of Korea’s trade and investment organization.

Both parties signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) affirming their dedication to support each other’s services and networks and to promote cooperation in economic relations, trade, and investment between Georgia and Korea.
Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Korea is an established strategic market for investment, trade, and tourism for Georgia, and the new Atlanta office will strengthen these relationships.
Existing connectivity includes daily direct flights between Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport and Incheon International Airport.
Long-standing agreements exist between the Georgia ports of Savannah and Brunswick and five Korean ports to facilitate joint efforts to create an innovative electric mobility ecosystem in Georgia.
On a mission to Korea in early March, Commissioner Wilson met with KOTRA President and CEO Mr. Jeoung Yeol Yu and key business representatives including those from Kia and SK Group.
The mission re-confirmed the State of Georgia’s commitment to its Korean companies and international partners.

Mr. Soo Deuk Sohn stated that KOTRA Atlanta will serve as an outpost to support Korean business in the Southeastern United States and expand cooperation in many industrial fields between Korea and the U.S.

KOTRA helps Korean companies settle in new markets like Georgia by acting as the first point of contact and providing investment and support resources, including information on programs from state or local agencies like GDEcD.
The new KOTRA office is located at 950 East Paces Ferry Road NE, Suite 999 in Atlanta.
Atlanta will host the eighth overseas KOTRA office in the United States and the 10th in North America.
The Republic of Korea has maintained a consular presence in Georgia since 1971, with Mr. Yoonjoo Park serving as Consul General since June 2021.
Georgia has maintained continuous representation in Korea since 1985 with an office in Seoul headed by Managing Director Peter Underwood.
The state also has a dedicated Georgia-based director of Korean Investment, Yoonie Kim.
Several Georgia companies including American Megatrends, First Advantage Corporation, InComm, Novelis, Unicoi Systems, and UPS have operations in Korea.
Georgia is home to at least 100 Korean facilities that currently employ more than 9,400 Georgians.
Korean-affiliated companies in Georgia include Kia, SK Battery America, Sangsin Technology America, and Q CELLS Korea.

Since 2020, numerous Korean companies have announced projects in Georgia, including SK Group subsidiary SKC manufacturing glass-based substrates for semiconductor chips in Covington with several business partners.
SUNGDO ENG USA announced its corporate U.S. headquarters in the state to provide highly technical construction services to Georgia-based clients.
Electric mobility parts and storage systems supplier for SK Group, Duckyang, is opening a new facility in Jackson County.
In west Georgia, brake pad supplier KB Autosys will open its first U.S. advanced manufacturing facility to better serve regional customers such as GM, Hyundai, and Kia.
Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport offers daily direct flights from Atlanta to Seoul.
The Georgia Ports Authority (GPA) in Savannah has an established relationship with Korean ports and offers seven regular shipping services to the Port of Busan.
GPA’s Brunswick Port also offers shipping services to Incheon, Masan, Pyongtaek, and Kunsan.
In 2021, Georgia exports to Korea totaled $860.59 million, making Korea the 10th largest export market for Georgia.
Top exports from Georgia to Korea include gas turbines, aircraft, medical instruments, chemical woodpulp, and chemical analysis instruments.
Georgia imports from Korea totaled $8.83 billion in 2021, with Korea ranking third among Georgia’s importer nations.
Top imports from Korea to Georgia include motor vehicles, tractor parts, bulldozers, engines, and fork-lift trucks.

KOTRA was established in 1962 to contribute to the development of the national economy by promoting trade, facilitating investment between domestic and foreign companies, and supporting industrial technology cooperation.
KOTRA is headquartered in Seoul but operates over 127 Overseas Trade Centers in 84 countries.
On March 31, 2022, leaders from GDEcD and KOTRA announced the opening of the new KOTRA overseas office in Atlanta and signed an MOU affirming dedication to cooperation between Georgia and Korea.
source_title: Korea Trade-Investment Promotion Agency Opens First Atlanta Office - Expansion Solutions

facts: Manufacturer of automotive interior products such as seats, door trim, consoles, luggage-space side trim, headlining, etc.; as well as exterior parts and equipment such as bumpers.
The Company provides the majority of its products to Hyundai Motor and Kia Motors.
Percentage of sales by destination as of December 31, 2024: Hyundai Motor accounted for 53%, while Kia Motors comprised 34%.
Main shareholder Seoyon Co., Ltd. holds a 48.70% interest ratio as of Dec. 31, 2024.
Main shareholder Yang-Seok Yu holds a 5.45% interest ratio as of Dec. 31, 2024.
Product line includes door trim assemblies with arm rests, map pockets, inside handles, and speaker grilles.
Product line includes bumpers with front/rear bumper covers and radiator grilles.
Product line includes console assemblies with upper covers, floor consoles, arm rests, and rear covers.
Product line includes package tray assemblies with speaker grilles, package trim boards, and child anchor covers.
Product line includes pillar trim assemblies.
Product line includes seat assemblies with seat belt buckles, cushions, headrests, and back seats.
Product line includes screen assemblies with sunshades and housing.
Established Hanil E-Hwa Interior Systems Alabama LLC in the USA in May 2005.
Established Hanil E-Hwa Interior Systems Georgia LLC in the U.S.A. in April 2010.
Established Seoyon E-Hwa Savannah in the U.S. in October 2022.
Incorporated Seoyon E-Hwa Auburn in the U.S. in April 2023.
Established Seoyon E-hwa Texas in the U.S. in March 2024.
The company was established in April 1972 and changed its name to Seoyon E-Hwa Co., Ltd. in January 2016.
source_title: Seoyon E-Hwa Co., Ltd. (Formerly Hanil E-Hwa Co., Ltd.) - MarkLines Automotive Industry Portal

facts: Seoyon E-HWA announced plans to invest $76 million in a new manufacturing plant near Savannah, Georgia.
The facility will manufacture interior and exterior parts including door trim, headlining, and tailgate trim for Hyundai’s upcoming assembly plant in Ellabell.
Production at the new plant is scheduled to begin in October 2024.
The company plans to hire more than 500 workers for the new facility.
Seoyon E-HWA currently operates a plant with 630 employees in LaGrange, Georgia, located near Hyundai’s Montgomery, Alabama plant and Kia’s West Point plant.
The company operates 32 manufacturing units worldwide.
Hyundai announced plans in May to build a $5.5 billion plant to assemble electric vehicles and batteries.
Hyundai’s Ellabell plant could eventually employ up to 8,100 workers and is scheduled to begin vehicle production in 2025.
Seoyon E-HWA is the fourth Hyundai supplier to announce plans for a southeast Georgia facility since Hyundai's May announcement.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp proclaimed his second-term goal is to make Georgia the leading state for electric-powered vehicles.
Trip Tollison, president and CEO of the Savannah Economic Development Authority, described the new jobs as “well paying” but did not disclose specific wage details.
Seoyon E-HWA already imports parts through Savannah’s port to supply its LaGrange facility.
The state will cover worker training costs, though the total incentive package from state and local governments was not immediately clear at the time of the announcement.
Seoyon E-HWA could qualify for more than $3.1 million in state income tax credits valued at $1,250 per job over five years, contingent on workers earning at least $31,300 annually.
Hyundai Mobis previously announced plans for a $926 million powertrain plant in Richmond Hill that will employ 1,500 workers.
Joon Georgia announced plans for a $317 million plant near Statesboro that will employ 630 workers.
Ecoplastic announced plans for a $205 million plant in Register that will employ 456 workers.
source_title: Hyundai supplier plans $76M Georgia plant, hiring 500 – WSB-TV Channel 2 - Atlanta

facts: Sewon America is a subsidiary of Korean-based Sewon Precision Industry Co.
Sewon America announced plans to build a $300 million facility in Rincon, Ga.
The new facility will create 740 jobs.
The plant will produce electric vehicle body parts for OEMs.
Operations at the new Rincon facility are expected to begin in 2025.
Sewon America opened a manufacturing facility in LaGrange, Ga., in 2008.
The LaGrange facility has since doubled in size.
Following the completion of the Rincon facility, Sewon is expected to support 1,600 jobs in Georgia.
source_title: Sewon America to build $300 million facility in Georgia

facts: Sewon America expects to create 100 new jobs by expanding its facility in Rincon.
The company is a Hyundai supplier planning a $50 million expansion project for its Georgia facility.
The Sewon America plant is located in Rincon, Georgia.
The facility produces electric vehicle body parts.
The plant supplies components to companies like Hyundai.
source_title: Auto parts supplier Sewon America to expand Georgia manufacturing - Atlanta Business Chronicle

facts: Hanon Systems Statesboro Corp. delivers advanced automotive thermal and energy management solutions from its new Georgia facility, supporting the U.S. electric vehicle market.
The company is ramping up operations to meet increasing demand for electric vehicle components, focusing on advanced thermal management solutions and supporting the regional automotive supply chain.
A new 112,000 sq. ft. manufacturing facility was launched in Statesboro, Georgia.
The company is making strategic efforts to expand market presence and reach new customer segments.
The company is implementing advanced technologies to improve service delivery and customer experience.
Hanon Systems Statesboro Corp. manufactures advanced thermal management components, including heating and cooling systems for battery boxes and vehicle interiors.
The facility in Statesboro, Georgia, spans 112,000 square feet with expansion potential to 330,000 square feet.
The facility is strategically positioned to serve the nearby Hyundai Meta Plant and other automotive manufacturers.
As part of Hanon Systems’ global network, the Statesboro operation enhances the company’s ability to supply innovative solutions for electric and conventional vehicles.
The company is committed to supporting the automotive industry’s shift toward electrification and sustainability.
The facility is located in Statesboro, Georgia, USA.
The facility was established in 2023.
Hanon Systems Statesboro Corp. operates as a private company.
source_title: Hanon Systems Statesboro Corp. | Automotive Thermal Solut...

facts: Hanon Systems Corp. is a South Korean thermal management systems provider for automakers.
Hanon Systems Corp. is set to inject $40 million to establish a plant in the US state of Georgia.
The new facility is located in Bulloch County, Georgia.
The plant will assemble automotive thermal solutions and create 160 new jobs.
The facility is set to begin operations in May 2024.
The plant will also be expected to begin operations as early as the third quarter of 2024.
The facility will accelerate Hanon’s supplies for electric vehicles (EVs) to Hyundai Motor Group, Ford Motor Co., and other global clients.
There is a possible expansion in annual production at the facility to 500,000 EVs.
The investment aligns with Hanon’s strategy to support the industry shift toward electrification while growing the company’s footprint in North America.
Min Sung serves as the president and chief executive of Hanon.
Hanon will intensify its heat management systems production in the US as global automakers increase manufacturing under the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA).
The USMCA took effect in July 2020 to incentivize automotive parts made in North America.
Hanon offers a variety of solutions in heating ventilation and air conditioning; powertrain cooling; compressor; fluid transport; and electronics and fluid pressure.
Hanon was named Supplier of the Year by General Motors two months prior to the announcement.
Hanon earned the Supplier of the Year title from GM for six consecutive years by exceeding requirements and providing innovative technologies with high quality.
The office of Georgia Governor Brian Kemp announced the plant details on a Tuesday.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia’s automotive and e-mobility industries continue to grow as they welcome Hanon Systems to Georgia.
Governor Brian Kemp noted that Georgia is furthering its position as the EV capital of America and that new jobs and opportunities are coming to Georgians across the state.
Other Korean car parts suppliers are also set to expand their production in the US as global automakers accelerate EV manufacturing in the region.
Ajin Industrial Corp., which directly supplies auto parts such as fenders to Hyundai, will inject 440 billion won ($334.1 million) to establish an EV components plant in Georgia.

Ecoplastic Corp., a leading supplier of plastic parts like exterior bumpers and interior consoles, will build a $205 million plant in the same state to supply Hyundai Motor’s EV and battery plant to be built nearby.
Seoyon E-hwa Co., which provides car interior parts such as door panels, bumpers and seats to Hyundai Motor, announced it will invest $4.2 million to expand production in Alabama state.
source_title: Hanon to build $40 mn heat pump systems plant in US state of Georgia - KED Global

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Hanon Systems will create 160 new jobs and invest more than $40 million in a new manufacturing facility in Bulloch County.
The announcement was made on May 31, 2023, while Governor Kemp was on an economic development trip to Israel.
Hanon Systems is a global provider of automotive thermal and energy management solutions for electrified and conventional vehicles.
The company's offerings include heating ventilation and air conditioning, powertrain cooling, compressors, fluid transport, electronics, and fluid pressure solutions.
Hanon Systems will build its new facility at Gateway Regional Industrial Park in Bulloch County to assemble automotive thermal solutions.
Operations at the new facility are expected to begin in May 2024.
Governor Brian P. Kemp stated that Georgia’s automotive and e-mobility industries continue to grow as the state welcomes Hanon Systems.
Governor Kemp noted that Georgia is furthering its position as the EV capital of America, with new jobs and opportunities coming to Georgians statewide.
Min Sung, President and Chief Executive Officer of Hanon Systems, said the company is pleased to contribute to the Bulloch County economy through job creation.
Min Sung thanked the State of Georgia and the cooperation between its agencies for guiding the project.
Min Sung stated that the announcement aligns with Hanon Systems' strategy to support the industry shift toward electrification while growing its North America footprint.
Billy Allen, Chair of the Development Authority of Bulloch County, said Statesboro-Bulloch County is honored by Hanon Systems’ decision to locate its new facility there.
Billy Allen expressed excitement about welcoming an automotive supplier that will work closely with the Hyundai Metaplant and the electric vehicle industry.
Billy Allen cited the decision as an example of the resources offered by the community and region to help companies succeed.
Griff Lynch, Executive Director of the Georgia Ports Authority (GPA), said the announcement further solidifies Georgia’s position as a growing hub for auto manufacturing.
Griff Lynch stated that GPA is proud to support the operations of Hyundai Motor Group and its suppliers in delivering jobs and opportunity for Georgia residents.

Alyce Thornhill, Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy for the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team, represented GDEcD on this competitive project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.

Pat Wilson, GDEcD Commissioner, stated that suppliers like Hanon Systems successfully support larger industry ecosystems through partnership-driven solutions ranging from workforce training to logistics systems efficiencies.

Pat Wilson noted that Bulloch County is part of the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority and has been part of Georgia’s growing success story in automotive manufacturing.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry, building on assets that make the automotive industry successful.
Since 2020, over 40 EV-related projects have announced more than $22.7 billion in investment and 28,400 jobs in Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground on October 25 for its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility in neighboring Bryan County.
Hanon Systems was formed in 1986 and has grown into a leading supplier of automotive thermal and energy management solutions.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: Automotive Supplier Hanon Systems to Create 160 N

facts: South Korean automaker Hyundai has announced a second large supplier for its new electric car plant on the Georgia coast.
Hyundai Mobis plans to invest nearly $1 billion in a new facility in Bryan County.
The new Hyundai Mobis facility could employ around 1,500 workers.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp announced the Hyundai Mobis investment and job details in a news release.
The Hyundai Mobis plant will be located in Richmond Hill.
The facility will make powertrains for Hyundai’s electric vehicles manufactured at its new plant scheduled to open in 2025.
Hyundai’s new EV plant is located about 30 miles (48 kilometers) west of Savannah.
The Hyundai Mobis plant will supply components for a Kia plant in West Point.
The Hyundai Mobis plant will also supply components for another Hyundai plant in Montgomery, Alabama.
Earlier this month, Gov. Brian Kemp announced that Joon Georgia would invest $317 million.
Joon Georgia will hire 630 workers for a Hyundai parts plant in Statesboro.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that Hyundai’s new electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facility is located in Bryan County.
Kemp described the project as unleashing transformational job creation and investment in that region of Georgia.
Gov. Brian Kemp attended a groundbreaking ceremony for the new Hyundai plant in Bryan County in October.
The new Hyundai plant is projected to make up to 300,000 vehicles a year.
Georgia officials describe the Hyundai project as the largest economic development project the state has ever seen.
source_title: Hyundai announces new Georgia plant's $1B parts supplier | AP News

facts: Auto parts maker Seohan Auto Georgia plans to invest $72 million in a new manufacturing plant in Midway, Georgia.
The South Korea-based auto parts manufacturer will produce H/shafts, axles and brake systems for OEMs.
Seohan Auto Georgia's products will be supplied to Hyundai’s nearby EV and battery manufacturing hub.
Seohan Auto Georgia’s upcoming facility is expected to create 180 jobs.
Production at the new facility is slated to start in late 2024.
Seohan Auto Georgia is the seventh Hyundai supplier to locate in Georgia since the automaker broke ground on its upcoming $5.5 billion EV campus in October.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp called Hyundai’s plant the “largest economic development project in state history.”
Gov. Kemp estimated the site would bring at least 8,100 jobs to Georgia’s coastal region.
Georgia officials had predicted Hyundai’s Metaplant would attract offsite suppliers to invest an estimated $1 billion.
Hyundai’s suppliers have outpaced that estimate, with plans to invest nearly $2 billion in the state, according to an April 11 announcement.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that the Hyundai Metaplant is and will continue to be an economic driver for the entire region, creating more jobs for hardworking Georgians in communities like Liberty County.
In November, Joon Georgia became the first supplier to announce plans for the state, with a $317 million production facility in Statesboro.
Later that month, Hyundai Mobis became the second Hyundai supplier, with its plans for a $926 million EV power electric systems plant.
Since January, Ecoplastic Corporation, Seoyon E-Hwa, Sewon America and PHA have each announced plans for manufacturing plants of their own to supply Hyundai’s manufacturing hub.
source_title: Seventh auto supplier follows Hyundai to Georgia | WardsAuto

facts: Steel columns are up at the Bryan County mega site, marking another significant turn in the development of Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Construction of the paint shop is underway at the facility.
The paint shop building is a critical part of the manufacturing process where various paint colors are applied to the vehicles being assembled on the site.
Eric Johnson serves as the project director for the Savannah Joint Development Authority.
source_title: First building vertical at Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America in GA

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Joon Georgia, Inc., an automotive parts manufacturer, will create 630 new jobs and invest $317 million in Bulloch County.
Plans for the company’s new manufacturing facility mark the first confirmed supplier for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America in Bryan County.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that less than two weeks after the groundbreaking of Hyundai Motor Group’s innovative facility, they are already seeing the regional impact it will have.
Governor Brian Kemp said Joon Georgia will be the first of many companies drawn to the Peach State to support the Metaplant, creating jobs and opportunity for generations of hardworking Georgians.
Joon Georgia is part of Ajin USA, a current supplier for multiple Hyundai Motor Group facilities.
The company produces a variety of automotive body parts and electronics for original equipment manufacturers.

Jung Ho Sea, CEO of Ajin USA, stated they are excited to be one of the first Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America automotive suppliers to build an automotive parts manufacturing facility in Bulloch County and for their first plant in Georgia.
Jung Ho Sea said Joon Georgia, Inc. will create approximately 630 new jobs with competitive benefits in Bulloch County and the State of Georgia.

Jung Ho Sea stated their commitment is to be the best parts supplier for automotive and EV companies like Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, and to be the best employer and corporate citizen giving back to the community of Bulloch County and the City of Statesboro.
Joon Georgia’s new facility will be located at Bruce Yawn Commerce Park, previously known as the Southern Gateway Commerce Park, near Statesboro.
The site is in close proximity to the Port of Savannah.
With connections to the port, both Hyundai and Joon Georgia will be able to access global markets quickly and efficiently.
Operations at the Bruce Yawn Commerce Park are expected to begin in mid-2024.
The company will begin hiring mid-2023.
Billy Allen, Chair of the Development Authority of Bulloch County, stated they are honored and proud to welcome Joon Georgia to the Statesboro-Bulloch County community and region.
Billy Allen believes their great site in the Bruce Yawn Commerce Park and proximity to the new Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America will serve the company well.
Billy Allen noted that education resources, including Georgia Southern University and Ogeechee Technical College, will provide critical workforce support to help Joon Georgia succeed.
Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was in partnership with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Quick Start, Georgia Ports Authority, and Georgia EMC.
Workforce development for the new facility will be supported by Quick Start, the top-ranked workforce training program in the nation.

By partnering with the Technical College System of Georgia, the program helps assess workers, train new employees on unique processes, and develop customized job-specific training using the most current techniques and media.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated Joon Georgia’s announcement is a landmark moment as they drive Georgia’s automotive industry into the future.
Pat Wilson said Hyundai Motor Group, its suppliers, and the Savannah area JDA are creating generational opportunities for communities in Georgia.
Pat Wilson noted that supporting the jobs of tomorrow is a team effort and regional collaboration ensures benefits are shared across county lines.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry.
Since 2020, more than 30 EV-related projects have contributed over $13.3 billion in investment and announced more than 18,100 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Non-affiliated suppliers connected to the Hyundai project are predicted to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around the state.
source_title: Read more about Gov. Kemp: First Hyundai Motor Group Supplier to Build Facility in Bulloch County - EIN Presswire

facts: As of its public grand opening, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America directly employs a few more than 1,200 people.
The promised total of 8,500 jobs at the main plant and its five onsite suppliers is expected to be realized by 2031.
Company officials announced a future expansion of capacity from the original 300,000 to 500,000 cars a year.
A top executive said the new goal will eventually boost direct employment at the site to 14,000 jobs.
The 14,000 direct employment estimate includes two battery plants.
The extra capacity at the Metaplant is located on the nearly 3,000-acre Mega Site in the Ellabell and Black Creek area of northern Bryan County.
The expanded capacity will result mostly from the addition of a production line for hybrid vehicles.
The plant will not be limited to manufacturing all-electric vehicles (EVs) as first announced.
About 50 reporters and photographers were given a tour Wednesday in motorized carts through the massive complex, which is now mostly finished.
A battery plant being built in a joint venture between Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution is still taking shape.
A ceremony was attended by Gov. Brian Kemp, U.S. Rep. Buddy Carter, Republic of Korea Ambassador to the U.S. Hyundong Cho, and Hyundai corporate officials.
The event celebrated the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history.
Groundbreaking for the project occurred two and a half years prior to the ceremony.
A couple hundred “Meta Pros” (employees of the Metaplant) wore blue company shirts and white HMG caps at the ceremony.
The plant utilizes hundreds of robots, including small floor-level materials movers, dog-like quadrupeds, and giant robotic cranes.
Robots and employees are currently assembling two all-electric vehicle types: the compact Ioniq 5 and the larger Ioniq 9 SUV.
Hyundai Motor Company President and CEO Jose’ Muñoz indicated plans for other production lines during his remarks.
HMG also makes vehicles with the Kia, Genesis, and Ioniq labels, as well as Hyundais.
Hyundai Motor Group is investing an additional $21 billion in the United States, according to CEO Jose’ Muñoz.
Nine billion of this additional investment is designated to expand the group’s automotive operations.
Part of the new investment impacts the operation at HMGMA.
The company is expanding HMGMA from 300,000 units annually to 500,000 units.
The expanded production at the Ellabell plant will not account for all of the $9 billion additional investment in automotive operations.
South Korea-based HMG’s planned $21 billion total new U.S. investment over four years will also include a $5.8 billion steel plant in Louisiana.
Altogether, this new commitment doubles the $20.5 billion Muñoz said HMG has invested in production in the United States since first expanding to America in 1986.
The corporation’s originally announced investment to establish the Metaplant was $7.5 billion.
Expanding in second and third phases to 500,000 units annually represents a more than 66% increase in the production goal.
CEO Jose’ Muñoz stated that adding 200,000 additional units is like building a new plant.
The expansion announcement was made on the day of the plant's official opening.
Governor Brian Kemp remarked that the new news requires another ribbon cutting and groundbreaking for the new plant.
Kemp noted that hardworking Georgians will either need to be hired or see a lot of overtime to produce another 200,000 vehicles.
CEO Jose’ Muñoz confirmed in a roundtable interview that the expansion would require a larger workforce.
This confirmation led to the “14,000 direct employment” estimate.
HMG officials do not expect to add any more buildings at the site to reach the new production goal.
A substantial portion of the production will be of hybrid vehicles.
Significant investment is required on equipment, with most dedicated to hybrids.
Originally, the project was thought to be a 100% EV plant based on trends and information at the time.
The company is now moving more into hybrids, with a minimum of one-third going to hybrids.
The shift to hybrids requires additional significant investment on equipment, people, and systems, but no additional buildings.
Euisun Chung, executive chair of Hyundai Motor Group, met with President Donald Trump on Monday at the White House.
Louisiana Gov. Jeff Landry also met with Trump to announce plans for Hyundai’s steel plant in Louisiana and overall $21 billion U.S. investment.
Trump called Hyundai’s plans clear evidence that “tariffs very strongly work.”
Trump reportedly said that Hyundai’s products will not be subject to tariffs because of its level of U.S. investment.
It remains to be seen whether the tariff exemption will actually apply to the company’s vehicles or materials imported from Korea.
CEO Jose’ Muñoz stated it would be nice if President Trump’s statement was confirmed regarding tariff exemptions.
In an email, Bianca Johnson, the Metaplant’s assistant manager for communications and public relations, said the plant currently has 1,232 employees.
The current employee count does not include Korean expatriates.
Among the 1,232 workers, 1,077 are from Georgia.
Of the Georgia employees, 185 are Bulloch County residents.
895 employees are from what Hyundai calls the Greater Savannah area, a 60-mile radius from the plant.
Bulloch County has the second-highest level of employment in the plant among area counties, with 185 Bulloch residents working there thus far, or 15% of the total.
Chatham County is first with 413 employees at the plant, or 33% of its workforce.
Effingham County is third, with 103 employees, or 8%.
Bryan County is fourth, with 91 employees at the plant, or 7% of the HMGMA workforce.
The initial commitment of HMG alone was for about 2,600 jobs.
Right now, there are about 1,200 of that 2,600 for HMGMA, according to Chief Administrative Officer Brent Stubbs.
The remainder of the 8,500 predicted jobs are with onsite suppliers and the battery joint venture.
Hyundai broke ground on the site in October 2022.
Production operations commenced at the plant last fall with the first vehicle, a Hyundai IONIQ 5, rolling off the assembly line Oct. 4.
In a release Wednesday, Hyundai Motor Group said its investment in Georgia related to the Metaplant and joint battery ventures with LG Energy Solution and SK On is $12.6 billion.
This is the largest single business investment in Georgia history.
The company expects to reach its previously stated goal of 8,500 total jobs at the Metaplant by 2031.
A report from the Center for Automotive Research cites that Hyundai’s total investments in Georgia are expected to create nearly 40,000 direct and indirect jobs.
These investments are expected to generate $4.6 billion in individual earnings every year.
As of the end of 2024, more than $2.5 billion in capital investment and approximately 6,900 jobs have been announced by Metaplant suppliers in 12 counties across the state.
In Bulloch County, three tier-one, direct suppliers to Hyundai are currently operating or completing manufacturing plants.
AJIN Georgia manufactures stamped-metal parts that comprise much of the framework of passenger vehicles.
Hanon Systems is a maker of "automotive thermal and energy management" equipment, which includes air-conditioning components.
Ecoplastic America Corporation will manufacture injection-molded plastic automotive body parts for Hyundai Motor Group vehicles.
source_title: Not 300,000 cars a year, but 500,000 - Statesboro Herald

facts: DQS Inc., an independent certification body, certified Ascend Elements’ electric vehicle (EV) battery recycling facility in Covington, Ga. to the ISO 9001:2015 standard for Quality Management Systems on July 15, 2024.
The ISO 9001:2015 standard is globally recognized for helping organizations improve performance, meet customer expectations, and demonstrate a commitment to quality.
The certification process includes onsite audits and extensive policy and documentation reviews to establish, implement, maintain, and continually improve a quality management system (QMS).

Andrew Aberdale, CFO/COO at Ascend Elements, stated that achieving ISO certification is a major achievement for any manufacturing company, especially for a startup organization in an emerging field like battery recycling.
Aberdale noted that the ISO certification signals the company's commitment to quality, customer service, and rigorous safety procedures.

The Ascend Elements facility in Covington is one of the largest EV battery recycling facilities in North America with capacity to process 30,000 metric tons of used lithium-ion batteries and scrap per year.
The 154,000 square foot facility in Newton County contains battery discharge and disassembly processes, battery shredding, lithium extraction and analytical testing capabilities.

Ascend Elements is a North America- and Europe-based battery materials leader converting spent lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing scrap into premium precursor cathode material (pCAM) and battery-grade lithium carbonate through its patented Hydro-to-Cathode® process.
The company operates plants at scale with a proven technology platform to deliver circular supply for EV and energy storage markets.
source_title: Ascend Elements | Ascend Elements EV Battery Recycling Facility Certified to ISO 9001: 2015 Quality Management Standard by DQS Inc.

facts: Ascend Elements is a pioneering climate tech company founded with the mission to transform the lithium-ion battery supply chain through sustainable manufacturing and advanced recycling solutions.
The company is headquartered in Westborough, Massachusetts.
Ascend Elements specializes in converting spent lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing scrap into high-performance cathode active materials (CAM) and precursor cathode active materials (pCAM).
The produced CAM and pCAM are for electric vehicle (EV) batteries.
The company's work drives a transition to a circular economy and reduces environmental impact.
source_title: Ascend Elements - Battery-Tech Network

facts: Ascend Elements is the global leader in circular battery materials.
The company announced a series of year-end milestones that cap a transformational 2025 and position it for accelerated growth through 2026 and beyond.
Ascend Elements stands alone as the proven, US- and EU-based answer to delivering circularity and security from core battery material recycling to engineered-component production at scale.
Linh Austin serves as President and CEO of Ascend Elements.

In 2025, the company proved circularity works at commercial scale, establishing itself as North America’s most advanced platform for integrated, domestic production of essential battery ingredients with industry-leading cost and carbon footprint reduction.
In May, Ascend Elements secured a $340M non-dilutive grant commitment from the Republic of Poland to support development of advanced pCAM and lithium processing capacity in Europe.
In September, Ascend Elements became America’s first commercial-scale producer of recycled lithium carbonate with greater than 99% purity.
The company has production operations in Georgia.
Near-term expansion is planned for Poland.
The company plans to deliver more than 15,000 tons of recycled Li₂CO₃ annually by 2027.
This output directly addresses critical mineral shortages in the US and Europe.
In November, Ascend Elements executed a 15,000 metric ton take-or-pay lithium carbonate agreement with Trafigura.
The Trafigura agreement assures multi-year revenue and reinforces market confidence in its lithium-first strategy.
The company executed a multi-year, nearly $1B supply contract with a leading global automaker.
This contract demonstrates clear and compelling OEM demand for certified, circular, domestic supply at scale.
Ascend is the only Western firm delivering full, commercial-scale vertical integration from recycling to cathode-active pCAM and battery-grade lithium carbonate today, not in pilot.
The company utilizes a patented Hydro-to-Cathode® Process that upgrades recycled feedstock straight into high-value components, including Lithium Carbonate and pCAM.
This process cuts cost, steps, and reduces CO₂e by up to 90% over mining-based approaches.
Commercial production of recycled lithium carbonate is already operational in the U.S.
The company's model eliminates dependency on imported, high-impact materials.
Active U.S. and EU sites, rapid expansion, and long-term offtakes ensure locally sourced, clean, and transparent supply for electric vehicles and grid storage.
Upon financial close expected in Q1/Q2 2026, Ascend will scale its Kentucky and Poland facilities.
The company's growth plan will power robust revenue and EBITDA expansion through the decade.
The company supports national security, energy transition, and climate targets.
Ascend Elements converts spent lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing scrap into premium precursor cathode material (pCAM) and battery-grade lithium carbonate.
The company delivers circular, sustainable supply for EV and energy storage markets through plants operating at scale and a proven technology platform.
source_title: Ascend Elements Closes 2025 with Strong Business Momentum and Commercial Scale Production of Critical Minerals for Battery Production | The AI Journal

facts: Anovion is the only commercial producer of synthetic graphite in North America.
The company announced an $800 million investment to build a plant in Bainbridge, Georgia.
The 1.5 million-square-foot plant will employ 400 people.
Anovion is scaling up from an existing single plant near Niagara Falls, New York, which is one of the only U.S. facilities producing synthetic graphite commercially today.
The company was selected for a $117 million Department of Energy grant funded by the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law to support the Georgia project.
The investment aligns with automakers' efforts to secure battery minerals and components in the U.S. or free-trade partner countries to comply with the Inflation Reduction Act.
Under current rules, half of the $7,500 EV tax incentive depends on at least 40 percent of critical minerals like graphite being sourced from compliant countries.
The critical mineral sourcing threshold for the EV tax incentive increases by 10 percent annually, reaching 80 percent in 2027.
Synthetic graphite can be produced from a petroleum byproduct called needle coke.
Graphite is a key anode material and represents the most plentiful material in a lithium-ion battery.

Anovion CEO Eric Stopka stated that graphite demand is expected to outstrip supply by more than 1.2 million tons by 2030 based on roughly one terawatt of American battery production already announced.
Mr. Stopka noted that today there is almost a negligible amount of synthetic graphite being produced in the United States.
Once operational, the Bainbridge plant will produce 40,000 metric tons of graphite per year.
Georgia already has one operational EV battery plant: SK On’s $2.6 billion investment in Commerce.
A joint-venture plant between SK On and Hyundai Motor is currently being built in Cartersville, Georgia.
Hyundai Motor is building an EV plant near Savannah, known as "Meta Plant America," which will start production in 2025 and create 8,000 jobs.
Rivian plans to manufacture its electric trucks in Covington, Georgia.
CEO Eric Stopka declined to name specific customer prospects but confirmed discussions have been held with automotive OEMs and niche users like the Department of Defense.
The company's primary growth focus is on the electric vehicle space and grid storage systems to support renewable energy.
Anovion's corporate motto is "to secure the supply chain of the electrified future."
Most global graphite processing currently resides in China and uses older methods that do not meet modern stakeholder demands for reduced carbon footprints.
Anovion was launched in March 2022 as a spinoff from Pyrotek Inc. and Amsted Graphite Materials.
The company utilizes proprietary furnace technology and controls to avoid outdated incumbent technology used in China, which controls over 90 percent of global graphite processing and production.
Internal lifecycle assessments indicate that Anovion's materials have lower emissions intensity compared to both mined graphite and rival producers.

Most graphite used for U.S. cell production is currently shipped from Asia, and locating in Bainbridge will drastically reduce logistics-related emissions by shortening delivery times to U.S. customers.
Anovion gathered site proposals from 15 states toward the end of the previous year before narrowing them to 10 and finally four prime candidates visited in person.
Bainbridge was selected for its available land for the 1.5 million square foot plant, good rail lines and barge access, and a workforce described as "technically capable, trainable or upskill-able."
The company identified sufficient available workers in Decatur County who are currently seeking employment outside the county, allowing Anovion to provide local job opportunities.

Most plant jobs require computer literacy and skills in operating and maintaining technical equipment, with a long-term goal of building a sustainable employment pipeline through partnerships with local high schools and technical colleges.
Georgia's expanding clean-energy infrastructure, including new nuclear reactors in Augusta and large-scale solar farms in the south, influenced the company's decision.
CEO Eric Stopka noted Georgia's grid decarbonization efforts were impressive and important for the company's commitment to climate tech processes.
Anovion has long-term plans to become carbon neutral.
The local community and the Georgia Department of Economic Development provided strong support and made the company feel welcome during site visits.
State officials were well-represented at every visit to Bainbridge, demonstrating strong cross-level government support for the project.
Governor Brian Kemp welcomed the company, citing his goal to make Georgia the electric mobility capital of the U.S.
Governor Brian Kemp stated in a release that job creators recognize Georgia's statewide resources and workforce, and he looks forward to partnering with Decatur County residents.
Anovion is simultaneously expanding its Center of Excellence and hydro-powered facility in New York to scale up production and meet demand.
source_title: Anovion’s $800 Million Georgia Plant Aimed at Securing U.S. Graphite Supply - Global Atlanta

facts: A manufacturer of materials for lithium-ion batteries used to power electric vehicles will invest $800 million in a plant in Southwest Georgia.
The new plant will create more than 400 jobs.
Gov. Brian Kemp announced the investment on Monday.
Chicago-based Anovion Technologies supplies premium synthetic graphite.
Synthetic graphite is the largest battery material used in EVs by mass, exceeding copper, nickel, manganese, cobalt, and lithium.
The new plant will be located in Bainbridge.
The plant will span 1.5 million square feet.
The plant will be the first of its size and scale in North America.
Anovion CEO Eric Stopka stated that this is the company's first large-scale expansion manufacturing facility in Georgia.
The company cites Georgia's strong technical workforce and top-tier schools as factors that will help advance research partnerships and contribute to a highly skilled talent pipeline.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that Georgia is fast becoming the e-mobility capital of the nation.
Gov. Brian Kemp noted that job creators are recognizing that every corner of Georgia has the resources and workforce needed for success.
The two largest economic development projects in Georgia history are electric vehicle manufacturing plants.
Hyundai is investing $5.5 billion in a plant near Savannah in Bryan County.
The Hyundai plant is expected to generate 8,100 jobs when fully built out.
Rivian is building a $5 billion plant east of Atlanta.
The Rivian plant will create 7,500 jobs.
Suppliers of electric vehicle batteries are springing up across Georgia to serve the two major EV manufacturing plants.
Anovion will be hiring for technical, production, and engineering jobs, as well as administrative and management positions.
The Bainbridge plant is expected to begin operations in late 2025.
source_title: Maker of EV battery materials coming to Southwest Georgia with $800 million investment

facts: An Augusta polymers plant has received a $178 million federal grant to build a companion facility to produce battery components for electric vehicles.
The proposed new plant could bring as many as 500 construction jobs and 100 manufacturing jobs to the Augusta area, according to U.S. Sen Raphael Warnock’s office.

Solvay Specialty Polymers won the grant through the Battery Materials Processing and Battery Component Manufacturing and Recycling program, a program funded through the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law enacted in 2021.

Cal Wray, executive director of the Augusta Economic Development Authority, stated that the real economic impact numbers for the Augusta-Richmond County Metropolitan Statistical Area will likely end up being higher than the grant amount.
The Augusta Economic Development Authority wrote a letter to the U.S. Department of Energy earlier in the year in support of Solvay’s grant application.
Cal Wray said: "Between a couple-hundred million investment, a couple-hundred jobs that are well-paid, I think it’s great for the area."
The Augusta-Richmond County Metropolitan Statistical Area has a concentration of about 23,000 people who work in manufacturing.
Solvay has some of the best paid manufacturing jobs in the area, and Wray assumed that would continue to be the case with the new facility.
The factory is expected to manufacture electric battery-grade binders and separator coatings, which are crucial components for lithium-ion batteries that power EVs.
Belgium-headquartered Solvay acquired its current Augusta facility from BP Amoco in 2000.
In 2015, Solvay announced an $85 million local expansion at the Augusta facility.
Cal Wray noted that EV companies are looking at the Southeast and specifically Augusta, pointing to Aurubis and Denkai as existing players in that same industry there.
In June 2022, German company Aurubis AG broke ground in south Richmond County on a $340 million copper recycling and smelting plant dubbed Aurubis Richmond.
The Aurubis Richmond plant will process about 90,000 tons of complex recycling materials annually to extract about 35,000 tons of blister copper.
Copper is one of the most in-demand finished products that goes into making EVs.
In July 2022, Japanese manufacturer Denkai America announced the construction of a $430 million south Augusta manufacturing plant.
Denkai produces copper foil used in printed electronics such as circuit boards.
U.S. Sen Raphael Warnock said: "Georgia is open for business, and today’s announcement is good news for both Georgia’s growing clean energy economy and Augusta workers."

Mike Finelli, president of Solvay Growth Platforms and chief North American officer, said the company is proud to receive federal government support for manufacturing technologies that will provide the U.S. with critical raw materials for building an independent, sustainable EV value chain.
Mike Finelli stated: "This grant reaffirms Solvay’s leadership in developing a robust and resilient domestic battery supply chain that brings important clean energy jobs to the U.S."
source_title: Solvay to build Augusta battery-parts plant with $178 million grant

facts: The U.S. government is considering limiting Chinese imports of a plastic used in electric vehicle batteries.
Resin maker Solvay SA said this move is needed to protect sizable investments in a new polyvinylidene fluoride plant.
The new polyvinylidene fluoride plant is being built in Georgia.
source_title: Solvay pushes US to probe Chinese rivals, protect Georgia EV plastic plant - Plastics News

entity_name: Made in Georgia: the EV Energy Supply Chain roundtable
canonical_name: Made in Georgia: the EV Energy Supply Chain roundtable
facts: LG Energy Solution North America Executive Vice President Robert Lee could not find an electric vehicle charging station on the Savannah Technical College campus after visiting twice.
LG Energy Solution operates a leading-edge EV plant and is the number one global battery maker located near Savannah Tech.
Savannah Technical College has partnered with Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America on an EV Production certification program.
Robert Lee served as the keynote speaker for the 'Made in Georgia: the EV Energy Supply Chain' roundtable held Tuesday afternoon.

The Zero Emission Transportation Association (ZETA) Education Fund organized the event, bringing together representatives from major regional, national, and global partners to support Georgia's goal of becoming the U.S. EV manufacturing epicenter.
ZETA Executive Director Albert Gore, son of former U.S. Vice President Al Gore, led the roundtable discussion.
In 2023, 74% of electric vehicles sold in the United States were made in the United States.
The most recent quarterly data showed 79% of EVs sold in the U.S. were made in North America.
China still produces nearly 40% of all types of motor vehicles.
Worldwide EV and plug-in hybrid vehicle sales reached 17 million.
That represents a 25% year-over-year increase from the previous year.
Consumers purchased more than one million fully battery electric vehicles for the second consecutive year.

A ZETA press release stated that across battery manufacturing, charging deployment, and automotive assembly, the EV and battery supply chain supports over $27.3 billion in investment and tens of thousands of jobs across Georgia.
The average cost of an EV is around $56,000, compared to an average of $48,000 for internal combustion engine cars.
EV costs declined by 10% between 2023 and 2024.
Internal combustion engine car costs increased by 3% during the same period.
Robert Lee foresees EVs costing less on average in roughly two years.
Lee wants the industry to produce $30,000 EVs with 300-mile ranges in the near future.
Georgia Public Service Commissioner Tim Echols moderated a panel discussion with industry partners at the event.

Panelists included Colton Hotary (LG Energy Solution senior director of government and corporate affairs), Corey Ershow (Rivian Automotive director of federal affairs), Mickey Daniel (Georgia Power area manager), Scott Moskowitz (QCells vice president of market strategy and industry affairs), and Job van Campen (Heliox, a Siemens Company, head of sales).
The roundtable occurred six days after Massachusetts-based Aspen Aerogels, Inc. demobilized its Statesboro, Georgia plant project, exposing the company to incentive agreement clawbacks or penalties.

McKinsey & Company released a report on December 19, 2024, identifying persistent long-term challenges for the industry into 2030, including a regional raw battery materials supply and demand imbalance.
On February 6, the Federal Highway Administration suspended $5 billion allocated for states' EV charging infrastructure deployment.
The suspension followed the Trump Administration's January 20 'Unleashing American Energy' order, which announced a desire to terminate the electric vehicle mandate.
The order claims unfair subsidies and other government-imposed market distortions favored EVs over other technologies.
Georgia's District 1 Representative Buddy Carter participated in a fireside chat with Savannah Area Chamber of Commerce President and CEO Bert Brantley at the event.

Carter stated he was one of 18 congressional members who wrote a letter urging Republican leaders not to dismantle key provisions of the Inflation Reduction Act that bolster domestic manufacturing economic security.

In a post-event interview, Carter noted that Trump is 'just trying to shake things up' and emphasized communicating the importance of EV production to Georgia's 1st District, U.S. economic security, and national security.

Lee acknowledged uncertainties regarding shifting government EV subsidy policies but remains cautiously optimistic due to private sector investment interest in EV charging infrastructure at universities, malls, and hotels.
Gore remarked that 'a bit of shuffling' is common with new presidential administrations and the industry will adjust.
Gore cited agreements Hyundai and Rivian have with mineral producers and refiners that have chosen to invest in America.
Gore stated long-term EV growth trends are phenomenal, particularly because three of the five most popular EVs now cost below $45,000.
source_title: It's electric! EV industry event at Savannah Tech asserts Georgia's role in global market

entity_name: Georgia Clean Energy and EV Manufacturing Projects
canonical_name: Georgia Battery, Solar, and EV Supply Chain Projects
facts: Two South Korean companies announced a multibillion-dollar investment to build solar panel and electric battery factories in northwest Georgia.
Federal subsidies helped close a deal to diversify the local economy of Cartersville.
The factories promised thousands of new jobs, transforming the manufacturing base in Cartersville.
Cartersville was once a cotton mill town before an Anheuser-Busch brewery arrived in the 1990s and a tire plant in 2006.
Republicans in Congress want to gut subsidies for projects across the country in a tax cut bill likely days from final passage.

President Donald Trump's signature legislation could harm Cartersville despite it being in overwhelmingly Republican Bartow County, which backed Trump with 75% of the vote all three times he appeared on the ballot.
Both companies say they're continuing their buildout plans despite the legislative threat.
Steve Taylor, a Republican who is Bartow County's lone elected commissioner, says ending the tax credits would be "a little concerning."
Taylor said: "Those companies came and it gave us a completely different type of industry and manufacturing for our community."
No state may have more to lose than Georgia from cuts in Trump's "Big Beautiful Bill."
Top Georgia Republicans have been mostly silent on the issue.
Georgia's two Democratic U.S. senators are staunchly opposed to the bill.
U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff told The Associated Press: "A vote for this bill is a vote against Georgia's economy and a vote that will put so much of what we've worked so hard to achieve at risk."
Cartersville is the Bartow County seat about 35 miles (55 kilometers) northwest of Atlanta.
Bartow County is transforming from rural to suburban.
Leaders foresee an economic boost from the $5 billion battery factory that Hyundai Motor Group and SK On are building.
Leaders foresee an economic boost from the $2.3 billion solar panel plant belonging to Qcells, a unit of Hanwha Solutions.
Both plants pledge to pay workers an average of $53,000 a year.
Clean energy projects in Georgia had already taken off before 2022, when then-President Joe Biden signed the Inflation Reduction Act.
The rush of clean energy projects accelerated after the IRA was passed.
33 additional clean energy projects were announced by the end of 2024, the most nationwide according to E2, an environmental business group.
Projects in Georgia top $20 billion, pledging more than 25,000 jobs, though exact figures differ.
Buyers of Qcells solar panels get a 40% federal tax credit, including a 10% bonus for domestic content.
The 40% tax credit and 10% domestic content bonus would go away under the proposed bill.
Qcells itself would still get production tax credits for panels it started producing last year in Cartersville.
The bill would also tax companies that buy panels or components from some foreign countries including China.
Taxing Chinese imports could help Qcells, but wouldn't aid domestic producers as much as the domestic content bonus.
When complete, the 1,900-job plant will take refined polysilicon, cast it into ingots, and thinly slice ingots into wafers that become solar cells.
Qcells says controlling its own supply chain will let it work more efficiently.
Those additional steps would earn the company additional tax credits.
Scott Moskowitz is vice president of market strategy and industry affairs for Qcells.
Moskowitz said the company built its first American factory up the road in Dalton during the first Trump administration in response to Trump's protectionist trade policy.
Moskowitz argues that a quick curtailment of federal subsidies undercuts Trump's goal of bolstering domestic manufacturing, pushing buyers back to Chinese-controlled producers.
16 GOP state legislators sent a June 17 letter imploring Congress to preserve tax breaks for solar panels.

Georgia lawmakers, led by Republican state Rep. Matthew Gambill of Cartersville, wrote: "We urge you not to weaken the tax credits, as doing so would only harm the manufacturing renaissance in Georgia while creating opportunities for Chinese companies to take over the solar industry."
Some argue it's unfair for Congress to pull the rug out after companies relied on the promise of federal support to invest huge sums.
Cartersville Mayor Matt Santini said: "I would like to think that from a business perspective that when you have agreements in place that you carry those out to fulfillment."
Clean energy projects have overwhelmingly located in Republican-held congressional districts.
A report by Atlas Public Policy found GOP districts host 77% of planned spending on these projects.
Republican U.S. Rep. Barry Loudermilk, who lives in Bartow County, addressed the local impact in a Monday statement to the AP.
Loudermilk said the cuts will push Qcells and Hyundai-SK On to become "independent of government subsidies."
Loudermilk said: "It is important to keep industry as free from government control as possible, and not dependent on government."
Loudermilk labeled the Biden incentives as "the Democrats' Green New scam" in May.
Republican Gov. Brian Kemp says he's staying out of the debate.
Kemp spokesperson Garrison Douglas said: "Our position is that Congress needs to be the one to decide the future of the IRA."
Kemp loves green energy investments and jobs, and declared his goal is to make Georgia the "electric mobility capital of America."
Kemp and Ossoff clash over who should get credit for Georgia's green energy boom.
Kemp sharply disputes that the Biden-era incentives spurred the flood of investment, saying industries were already coming to Georgia before the Inflation Reduction Act was passed.
Kemp vociferously opposed some domestic content requirements that made it hard for Hyundai to access the same tax credits as unionized U.S.-based automakers.
Douglas said: "Just generally speaking, the Inflation Reduction Act picked winners and losers, and we saw that negatively impact our partners."
All nine of Georgia's Republican House members voted to support the bill.
U.S. Rep Buddy Carter earlier signed a letter supporting green energy subsidies.
Carter is seeking the GOP nomination to oppose Ossoff for Senate in 2026.
Carter represents a coastal district that includes a $7.6 billion Hyundai plant in Ellabell that started production last year.
Hyundai wants to make batteries at what would be a 3,500-employee plant near Cartersville.
The battery plant is intended so Hyundai and Kia buyers can fully take advantage of the $7,500 tax credit for electric vehicles.
Those EV tax credits would end six months after the bill is enacted under the current version.
Hyundai is publicly sidestepping the current legislative fight.
With American demand for electric vehicles slow to take off, Hyundai now says it will also build gas-electric hybrid vehicles in Ellabell.
The Ellabell plant was projected to make only electric vehicles before this shift.
