Hyundai spokesperson Michael Stewart said: "We remain focused on electrification because we believe it represents a significant long-term opportunity."
Stewart added: "At the same time, our business is driven by consumer demand, which is why we continue to offer a full range of powertrains."
Bartow County leaders say it's in everyone's interest to keep the projects on solid footing and that jobs should outweigh politics.
Santini said: "I don't know that people are lining up along party lines over this topic."
Ossoff says partisanship is motivating many Georgia Republicans to turn their backs on the state's economic interests.
Ossoff said: "For national Republicans right now, loyalty to Trump is more important than anything else, and this is what Trump says he wants."
source_title: A Georgia town that solidly backed Trump could fall victim to his tax bill's green energy cuts - AOL

facts: Two workers were killed this week in separate workplace accidents at two of Georgia’s most high-profile manufacturing sites: the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America battery plant near Savannah and the Hanwha Qcells solar panel plant in Cartersville.
The deaths occurred within six weeks of the death of Ronald Adams, Sr. at the Stellantis Dundee Engine Complex in Michigan.
Ronald Adams, a 63-year-old worker, was killed on April 7 when an overhead gantry engaged, pinning him to a conveyor and causing fatal crushing injuries to his upper torso.
On Tuesday, May 20, a construction worker was killed at the HL-GA Battery facility, part of the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America complex in Ellabell, Georgia.
According to reports from the Bryan County sheriff’s office, the worker was fatally injured when an unsecured load fell from a forklift during loading operations.
The incident occurred shortly before 11:00 a.m., and the identity of the worker, who was employed by a subcontractor involved in the construction of the battery plant, has not been released.
The HL-GA Battery plant is not yet operational and is a key part of Hyundai’s $12.6 billion investment in Georgia, slated to supply batteries for the company’s new electric vehicle assembly plant.
This is not the first fatality at the Hyundai Metaplant construction site.
In March of this year, Sunbok You, a 67-year-old worker, was struck and dragged by a forklift, resulting in fatal injuries.
In April 2023, Victor Gamboa, a 43-year-old contractor, died after falling 60 feet when his safety line failed.
According to an OSHA investigation that cited the contractors, the workers had not been provided with adequate safety gear.
On Monday, May 19, a fatality occurred at the Hanwha Qcells solar panel plant in Cartersville, Georgia.
Firefighters responded to a call at 7:15 p.m. and found Marion Jose Rugama, a 33-year-old resident of Norcross, dead on top of a tank.
It has not yet been established if Rugama was a direct employee of Hanwha Qcells or a contractor, but the use of contract labor is widespread at the facility.
According to local authorities, the oxygen level at the top of the tank was only 15 percent, far below the minimum required to sustain life.
A nitrogen leak is suspected as the cause, though an autopsy and toxicology screening are pending.
The Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) in Ellabell, Georgia is the largest single economic development project in Georgia’s history.

Backed by Hyundai and its partners, including LG Energy Solution and SK On, the sprawling complex is expected to employ 8,500 workers by 2031 and includes both vehicle assembly and battery manufacturing operations.
The facility is being constructed at breakneck speed, with the goal of making Georgia a hub for electric vehicle and battery production.
The Hanwha Qcells plant in Cartersville is part of a $2.5 billion investment by the South Korean clean energy giant, which also operates a major facility in Dalton, Georgia.
Together, the two plants form the largest solar manufacturing operation in the Western Hemisphere, with plans to expand the Cartersville facility even further in 2025.
Neither the workforce at Hyundai Metaplant nor the Hanwha Qcells Cartersville facility are unionized.
The use of contract labor allows companies to cut costs, evade responsibility and fragment the workforce.
Contract workers are often paid less, receive fewer benefits and have less job security than direct employees.
Contract workers are also less likely to receive adequate safety training.
The automotive and manufacturing sectors remain among the most dangerous for workers in the United States.
According to the Bureau of Labor Statistics, 5,486 workers died on the job nationwide in 2022, with transportation, construction and manufacturing consistently ranking among the deadliest industries.
In February 2024, OSHA reported an investigation of the Columbus, Georgia facility of Aludyne Columbus LLC, a prominent global manufacturer of auto parts with 30 production facilities worldwide.
OSHA’s findings uncovered 22 serious violations and were followed by proposed penalties of $182,344.

Violations included failure to provide protective gear and enforce safety protocols where employees worked dangerously close to energized power lines without insulated tools and were exposed to airborne silica at levels up to 15 times higher than the permissible limit.
Workers also lacked proper respiratory equipment, and the company failed to conduct fit testing for those exposed to silicosis hazards.

OSHA Atlanta-West Area Office Director Jeffery Stawowy stated that Aludyne Columbus LLC’s failure to prioritize employee safety nearly cost a worker their life and allowed overexposure to silica well above the permissible limit.
Workers at StarPlus Energy, a joint venture between Stellantis and Samsung SDI in Kokomo, Indiana, took to social media to expose dangerous working conditions inside the facility.
The workers reported no medical emergency response, no safety data sheets, no workers’ comp doctors, and that injured workers must climb four flights of stairs to seek assistance.
The International Workers Alliance of Rank-and-File Committees (IWA-RFC) has launched an independent investigation into the death of Ronald Adams at the Dundee Engine plant.
Stellantis, the UAW apparatus, and MIOSHA have refused to release information about the circumstances or official investigation into Adams’s fatality.
The campaign for an independent investigation has resonated with workers across the auto industry who have witnessed catastrophic conditions created by speedup, understaffing, and profit drives.
source_title: 2 workers killed in separate fatal workplace accidents in Georgia - World Socialist Web Site

facts: Qcells has announced it has begun manufacturing solar cells at its Cartersville, Georgia factory.
The Cartersville facility will be the first and only U.S. factory to produce major parts of a solar photovoltaic module under one roof, from ingot to finished panel.
The company expects to be at full production by Q3 2026, making it the largest operating solar cell factory in U.S. history.
Cartersville module assembly is now at full capacity, building 16,700 panels per day.
Qcells' Cartersville facility will add 3.3 GW of vertically integrated ingot, wafer, cell, and 3.5 GW of module capacity when fully operational.
By Q3 2026, Cartersville will make 3.3 GW each of ingots, wafers, cells, and 3.5 GW of modules a year.
Together with the expanded Dalton factory (which tripled module capacity to 5.1 GW in late 2023), Qcells' total module capacity in Georgia will reach 8.6 GW a year, or 47,000 panels a day.
The combined output is approximately the energy needed to power roughly 1.3 million U.S. homes for a year.
Andy Park, Global CEO of Qcells, stated that producing the first solar cells at Cartersville is a milestone for Qcells and American manufacturing.
As ingot, wafer, and cell lines reach full capacity, Qcells will make the major components of a solar panel right here in Georgia.
A dependable domestic supply chain creates thousands of good-paying jobs and gives customers greater certainty on price, supply, and tariffs.
The Cartersville investment is bringing thousands of skilled manufacturing jobs to Northwest Georgia.
Together with the expanded Dalton campus, Qcells' Georgia operations are expected to employ nearly 4,000 people — an estimated 3,800 direct jobs across Bartow and Whitfield Counties.
Modules produced in Cartersville will help project developers and asset owners qualify for the 10% Domestic Content Bonus under the Investment Tax Credit.
Because major components of each module are made domestically, customers can pursue qualifying projects with greater confidence on pricing, supply, and sourcing.
With demand for fully domestic solar equipment growing, Qcells expects strong interest in Cartersville-produced modules.
Qcells' fully integrated U.S. manufacturing platform helps shield customers from supply chain disruptions and trade uncertainty.

By producing major solar components domestically, the company gives customers greater visibility into sourcing, pricing, and delivery timelines, reducing exposure to international supply constraints and tariff volatility.
For developers and energy buyers focused on reliable, long-term project execution, that domestic supply chain is an increasingly decisive advantage.
The domestic manufacturing footprint positions Qcells to capture the full value of the Section 45X Advanced Manufacturing Production Tax Credit across the solar value chain.
By producing ingots, wafers, cells, and modules domestically, the company can claim credits at each stage of production — something no other U.S. solar manufacturer can currently do.
That competitive advantage supports continued investment in American manufacturing, workforce development, and long-term domestic supply chain growth.
The Cartersville factory is the first such operation built in the United States in more than a decade.
The facility will be home to the largest ingot and wafer plant ever constructed in the country.
Qcells is the largest silicon-based solar manufacturer in the United States.
Qcells operates a global manufacturing network spanning the United States, Malaysia, and South Korea.
Qcells' Complete Energy Solutions combine high-performance solar technology, advanced energy storage, proprietary energy management software, and flexible financing options.
Qcells advances long-term sustainability and system performance through solar recycling operations and next-generation cell technology R&D.
source_title: Qcells begins solar cell manufacturing in Georgia

facts: Freyr Battery US LLC officially announced on Thursday that it will not build its proposed $2.6 billion electric battery factory in Coweta County.

Climbing interest rates, falling battery prices and a change in leadership at the company all contributed to the decision, according to Jason Peace, Freyr’s senior vice president of business development.
Jason Peace told Coweta County Development Authority members at their meeting that morning he was super ambitious about building a battery plant there two years ago but now cannot support it.
The move was confirmed in a letter to the authority dated Jan. 21, citing a realignment of near-term strategic goals.
Peace stated Freyr has been holding the site for two years and suggested releasing it to someone else.
Freyr’s decision to abandon the project marks a major shift from its initial plans to establish the Giga America facility.
The Giga America facility was expected to create 723 jobs over seven years.
Initially announced in November 2022, the facility was to produce energy storage systems using lithium-ion batteries to store renewable energy from wind and solar power.
Freyr’s CEO, Tom Einar Jensen, had described the project as transformative for the energy grid, enabling renewable energy to replace coal- and gas-fired power without disruptions.
In its communication, Freyr praised the support it received from Georgia and Coweta County.
The company described assistance from state and local officials as "stellar."

To formally exit the project, Freyr submitted several documents, including an executed notice of prepayment and purchase option for the property incentive bond, a draft redemption notice for the county jobs grant bond, and a draft quitclaim deed release related to the project site.
The company requested assistance from the authority to determine payoff amounts for the bonds, terminate related leases and debt documents, and finalize the transfer of the project site.
The authority members approved the documents at their meeting.
According to the letter, Freyr will also work with the Georgia Department of Community Affairs to confirm the repayment of grants and incentives tied to the project.
Sarah Jacobs, executive director of the authority, said the failed project illustrates the effectiveness of performance-based incentives.
Jacobs stated that the approach ensures taxpayers were protected through built-in clawbacks and safeguards.
In its third-quarter 2024 financial report, Freyr disclosed a net loss of $27.5 million, or $0.20 per diluted share.
This compared to a $9.8 million loss in the same period in 2023.
Year-to-date losses totaled $83 million, driven by restructuring charges and declining warrant liability fair value adjustments.
Freyr reported $184.1 million in cash reserves and no debt as of Sept. 30, 2024.
This marked a significant drop from $275.7 million in cash reserves at the start of the year.
Peace told authority members that the company is still committed to green energy and has acquired a nearly finished facility in Texas that will build solar panels.
The fact that the Texas plant is almost ready to go represented “a fast path to cash” for the company, according to Peace.
Peace stated the company was burning cash for two years trying to build a battery plant without success.
Freyr’s 2022 announcement initially positioned Georgia as a growing hub for clean energy and electrification projects.
The planned Coweta facility joined a wave of electrification initiatives in Georgia, including SK Battery in Jackson County and the future Rivian and Hyundai electric vehicle plants.
The planned Coweta facility was to be near Amazon and Shopify distribution centers off Interstate 85 near Newnan.
The site was expected to offer average salaries exceeding $60,000.
Peace said after the meeting that the property will be sold and there have been interested parties.
A local Georgia-area developer is looking to buy the site.
If everything goes well, the sale could close in a couple of weeks.
Peace was unsure of the developer’s plans for the property.
source_title: Freyr Battery abandons proposed $2.6 billion Georgia factory | News | times-herald.com

facts: Norwegian battery firm Freyr has canceled plans for a Georgia factory that would have supplied batteries for energy storage.
The cancellation was first reported by the Newman Times-Herald of Newnan, Georgia.
Freyr confirmed plans to cancel the factory in a letter to the local Coweta County Development Authority dated Jan. 21.
Freyr also confirmed the cancellation during a Thursday meeting with the authority.
Freyr told the Georgia Department of Community Affairs that it would repay grants and incentives tied to the factory project.
The factory was announced in November 2022.
The proposed facility was branded "Giga America".
The factory would have sat on a 368-acre site in Coweta County.
Coweta County is located on the southwestern edge of the Atlanta metropolitan area.
Production at the facility was due to be stepped up in phases.
Phase one involved a $1.7 billion investment to build out 34 gigawatt-hours of annual production capacity.
A second phase was planned to add more cell production lines.
The second phase would bring the total project investment to $2.6 billion by 2029.
Rising interest rates contributed to the decision to cancel the factory.
Falling battery prices contributed to the decision to cancel the factory.
A change in leadership at Freyr contributed to the decision to cancel the factory.
Jason Peace, the company's senior vice president of business development, cited these factors in an interview with The Newman-Times Herald.
Freyr reported a net loss of $27.5 million in its third-quarter 2024 financial results.
Freyr reported a net loss of $9.8 million for the same period in 2023.
Peace told authority members that Freyr had been burning cash to get the battery plant built.
Freyr is now looking at a nearly-finished solar-panel plant in Texas as an alternative, quicker way to generate revenue.
Rivian continues to move ahead with plans for an EV assembly plant about an hour from downtown Atlanta.
Rivian's plant was announced in 2021 and originally scheduled to open in 2024.
Rivian paused construction on its Georgia plant, pushing the opening first to 2027 and then to 2028.
A $6.6 billion conditional loan from the Department of Energy may help complete Rivian's plant.
source_title: Freyr Battery cancels $2.6 billion Georgia battery factory plans

facts: The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) ends federal tax credits for electric vehicles (EVs) and related battery manufacturing, impacting the growing EV market.
The OBBBA's removal of incentives is expected to slow EV sales growth and increase reliance on manufacturer discounts and deals.
Building a domestic battery supply chain and expanding charging infrastructure are key challenges for the EV industry's future.
President Donald Trump bought an $80,000 red Tesla from Elon Musk at the White House lawn on March 11, stating he would pay full price.
The OBBBA was enacted July 4 and ends federal tax credits for electric vehicles by the end of September and tax breaks for American battery manufacturers in 2030.
More clean energy projects were cancelled in the first quarter of 2025 than the past two years combined, according to research group Atlas Public Policy.
Since January, cancellations and downsized projects have amounted to roughly $14 billion in abandoned investments.
The abandoned investments include a $1 billion factory planned to produce thermal barriers for batteries in Statesboro.
Georgia rolled out billions of dollars in tax breaks for EV manufacturing plants with the goal of becoming the 'electric vehicle capital of America' by the end of Gov. Brian Kemp's second term.
Industry leaders are reluctant to show signs of defeat and boast that the industry is growing towards an independent supply chain that will rely less on federal support.
Georgia Public Service Commissioner Tim Echols stated that tax credits played a purpose but that purpose may be over, suggesting the state may not need subsidies in the future.

Electrification Coalition Policy Manager Skye Golann emphasized the need to develop an American critical mineral supply chain during the opening speech of an Aug. 7 press briefing titled 'How EV Investments Are Powering Prosperity in Georgia.'
The Biden administration's Inflation Reduction Act (IRA), passed in August 2022, provided tax credits, subsidies, and incentives for purchasing and manufacturing EVs and their charging facilities.
A 2024 report by the Rhodium Group and MIT found that investments in batteries, fueling equipment, and critical minerals refining and processing have each grown at least three-fold since the IRA.
In 2022, the U.S. Department of Energy granted $178 million for the construction of Solvay Specialty Polymers' battery-grade polyvinylidene fluoride facility in Augusta.
Last year, the DOE awarded $80 million to Fort Valley-based Blue Bird for its electric school bus manufacturing and workforce development.

Cox Automotive Director of Industry Insights Stephanie Streaty noted that EV purchases ticked up in the U.S., spurred by an IRA credit offering a $7,500 discount for a new EV and $4,000 or 30% for a used EV.
Last year was a record year of 1.3 million EVs sold, according to Streaty.
The Georgia Clean Energy Roadshow hosted by Echols at Savannah Technical College on Aug. 12 featured over 60 vehicles, including one of the City of Savannah's Nissan Leafs starting at about $30,000.
When Echols first began the roadshow 15 years ago, only the Tesla Roadster was on display, worth more than $100,000 at the time.
The average EV transaction this year is $10,000 more expensive compared to a gas vehicle, according to Cox Automotive data.
Before the passing of the OBBBA, Cox Automotive predicted that EVs would make up 10% of all cars sold for 2025, up 2% from last year.
Cox adjusted its EV sales prediction to 8.5% due to tariffs, interest rates, and other headwinds, according to Streaty.
Skye Golann stated that taking away federal tax credits will dampen some growth and called the OBBBA a setback for EVs.
Lithium-ion batteries could make up 40% of the cost of a car, according to Streaty.
China still dominates the battery's mineral processing, admitted Golann.
The U.S. is home to just 1% of the world’s lithium reserves, according to the World Economic Forum.
On Aug. 6, GM announced plans to co-manufacture six EV models with Hyundai, including an electric van for the North American market.
A day later, GM inked a deal to import Chinese batteries that will function as a stopgap until it can build its own lithium batteries at lower cost in the U.S., according to the Detroit Free Press.
Kia's Head of Department Corporate Services and Information Technology Ted Arnold stated that about half of Kia’s manufacturing for EV and gas vehicles remains overseas.
Hyundai, a parent company of Kia, is in the process of bringing suppliers locally so they don't have to import, according to Arnold.

The OBBBA will end the 45X tax credit for U.S. battery manufacturers in five years, which will cede growth to other countries such as China, Mexico, Canada, and members of the European Union, according to think tank Energy Innovation.
In January, Norway-based Freyr Battery called off a battery manufacturing plant in Newnan, stating so in a letter to Coweta County.

In February, Aspen Aerogels told investors it halted construction of its second plant manufacturing thermal barriers for EV batteries in Statesboro and will instead increase production in China, according to Latitude Media.

In March, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America President José Muñoz announced an expansion focused on hybrid vehicles at its grand opening, marking a diversion from its expected all-electric operations.
Streaty noted that the IRA 45X reduction credit doesn't sunset until 2030, calling it a glimmer of light for manufacturers to take advantage of tax breaks before they end.
Dealers are getting aggressive with rebates, deals, and discounts now that the IRA is going away, according to Streaty.
Manufacturers have already spent money to build assembly lines and factories and will want to put incentives on those cars, said Echols.
EVs eventually need to be able to sell on their own without constant incentives, according to Streaty.
source_title: Georgia grapples with loss of federal funding for its EV industry

entity_name: Georgia EVTOL and Urban Transportation Initiative
canonical_name: Georgia Electric Vertical Takeoff and Landing (EVTOL) Program
facts: Beginning July 1, a new law gives the Georgia Department of Transportation authority to regulate electric vertical takeoff and landing, or EVTOL, aircraft.
Walmart will deliver products by drones from several northern arc Walmarts at the end of this year, with expansion statewide in 2026.
Tesla has kicked off their robo-taxis.
Waymo in Atlanta has kicked off autonomous vehicle operations.
Through the rest of 2025 and the rest of 2026, Georgia will see an explosion of autonomous vehicles hitting its streets.
EVTOL aircraft will initially move around 'lily pads' (formally called vertiports).
Alpharetta and Roswell have stepped up with plans in action to go along the 400 corridor.
Vertiports do not need a runway like Hartsfield, PDK, or Charlie Brown airports.
A vertiport will be about 43,000 square feet (about 1 acre) with four landing facilities.
All four landing facilities will have power generation from Georgia Power or one of the state's EMCs to charge EVTOL aircraft in 15 minutes.
Operators will be able to use any vertiport to create competition and a price structure along the lines of an Uber XL or an Uber Black.
Rep. Todd Jones hopes the first flight will kick off the World Cup here in Atlanta in July of 2026.
Republican Rep. Todd Jones of South Forsyth chairs the House Committee on Technology and Infrastructure Innovation.
source_title: Lawmakers Huddle: Georgia on the verge of 'transportation revolution' | Georgia Public Broadcasting

facts: Starting in 2025, Archer plans to build hundreds and eventually thousands of aircraft at a Georgia manufacturing plant.
source_title: Archer Aviation plans to build its air taxis near Atlanta - Silicon Valley Business Journal

facts: Electric vertical takeoff and landing (eVTOL) aircraft manufacturer Archer Aviation Inc. (Santa Clara, Calif., U.S.) has announced plans to locate its eVTOL manufacturing facility in Covington, Ga., U.S., at a site adjacent to the Covington Municipal Airport.
Archer plans to initially build out a 350,000-square-foot facility on a 96-acre site capable of producing up to 650 aircraft per year.
The planned eVTOL aircraft manufacturing facility is expected to create more than 1,000 jobs over the long term.
Construction is expected to be completed in the first half of 2024 with initial production expected to begin in the second half of 2024.
The facility will be capable of being expanded by an additional 550,000 square feet, which is estimated to support production of up to 2,300 aircraft per year.
In selecting the location for its first manufacturing facility, Archer completed a comprehensive survey of potential sites across the U.S.

Key considerations for site selection included the availability of talent in the local labor market, utility availability, ability to conduct seamless flight test operations, construction costs and logistics.
Archer will receive an incentive package that includes land conveyance, tax incentives and a Georgia REBA grant.
The company anticipates that financing for the project will be provided by Synovus, the largest bank headquartered in the state.
Adam Goldstein, Archer’s CEO, stated that the U.S. has long led the world in aerospace technology and Georgia has played a vital role in that industry.

Adam Goldstein noted that their eVTOL technology can transform how urban and rural communities live and commute and that the factory can create pathways to highly skilled manufacturing jobs and other ladders of social and economic opportunity.

Home to more than 800 aerospace companies, Georgia maintains a robust aerospace education pipeline with a dozen high schools that offer training in the field, five technical colleges with aviation programs and several universities offering degrees in aerospace engineering.

The Georgia Center of Innovation for Aerospace, a strategic arm of the Georgia Department of Economic Development, has been cultivating an ecosystem of opportunities for innovative companies like Archer for more than 10 years.
source_title: Archer Aviation selects site in Georgia to build eVTOL manufacturing facility | CompositesWorld

facts: Archer Aviation is building a nearly 400,000 square foot high-volume manufacturing facility in Covington, GA, adjacent to the Covington Municipal Airport.
Construction of the facility is on track to be completed by the end of 2024, reaffirming an expected 2024 completion date.
The first build phase, inclusive of grading and foundation work, was completed earlier this year.
Phase two, involving the erection of exterior walls and roof, is nearing completion.
Phase three (interior fit out) and phase four (equipment load in and power on) are on track to be completed in the coming months.
Once complete, the facility will support Archer’s planned production of up to 650 Midnight aircraft annually.
Archer is producing the Midnight aircraft in partnership with Stellantis.
Archer recently showcased its Midnight aircraft at Atlantic Aviation’s new terminal at DeKalb–Peachtree Municipal Airport.

Guests at the showcase included government officials from Georgia’s Office of the Governor, Georgia’s Congressional Offices, the City of Atlanta, Georgia’s State Legislature, the Federal Aviation Administration, and other local governments.
Adam Goldstein, CEO and founder of Archer, stated that the company is tracking towards completing the manufacturing facility by the end of the year.
Archer aims to ramp production up to 650 aircraft a year and be the first in the eVTOL industry to achieve scaled manufacturing.
The Midnight is an electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) aircraft built around Archer’s proprietary electric powertrain.
The Midnight is designed to travel at speeds up to 150 mph, turning hour-long ground commutes into minutes in the air.
The Midnight is designed for back-to-back flights of 20-50 miles with minimal charge time in between.
The Midnight operates at 100x quieter than a helicopter at cruising altitudes, making it nearly inaudible from streets below.
The Midnight features redundant systems allowing Archer to target safety levels similar to commercial airliners.
The Midnight is designed to carry a pilot, up to four passengers, and carry-on luggage.
source_title: Archer Aviation - Archer Aviation Showcases Midnight Aircraft In Atlanta As It Nears Completion Of Its High-Volume Manufacturing Facility In Covington, Georgia

facts: DUCKYANG AMERICA, INC. is located at 984 Broadway Ave, Braselton, GA 30517.
The company has a total of 145 bills of lading on file between August 30th, 2021 and May 1st, 2026.
Of the 145 bills of lading, 0 were within the past 30 days and 1 was within the past 90 days.
Records in the past year total 2.
The record distribution includes 0 shipper records, 144 consignee records, and 144 notify party records.
DUCKYANG AMERICA, INC. imports accumulators parts into the United States.
Shipments are primarily received at the Port of Savannah, Georgia, accounting for 137 of the total records.
The primary foreign port of lading is Pusan, South Korea, with 144 records.
DUCKYANG AMERICA, INC. has a recorded contact phone number of +1-706-983-1582.

Major associated companies in terms of total bills of lading include DUCKYANG IND. CO., LTD (139 records), DY DEOKYANG IND. (23 records), MOBASE ELECTRONICS CO., LTD. (377 records), and HYUNDAI ELECTRIC MACHINERY AMERICA (115 records).
Historical address records for the company also list the location as 984 Broadway Ave, Brasaton GA 30517.
Shipment data includes containerized cargo details, such as a 40ft container weighing 66,280 KG containing accumulators parts shipped from DUCKYANG IND. CO., LTD to DUCKYANG AMERICA, INC.
source_title: DUCKYANG AMERICA, INC. | U.S. Import Activity

facts: Georgia is emerging as a national leader in advanced manufacturing thanks to investment attracted through strong international relationships.
The Jackson Alliance for Economic Development serves as the central economic development organization for Jackson County, Georgia.
The Jackson Alliance is organized under the Jackson County Area Chamber of Commerce and has been successful in attracting investment.

Duckyang, a supplier of automotive battery modules and energy storage systems to SK Battery America (SKBA), announced it would invest US$10 million in its first US manufacturing facility in Jackson County.
South Korean companies are major investors in Georgia, with total investments from South Korean firms in 2020 reaching US$1.3 billion.
South Korean firms created over 2,700 jobs in Georgia in 2020.
In June 2020, SK Innovation increased its investment in its Georgia manufacturing plant by US$940 million.
In 2019, SK Innovation invested US$1.67 billion in a manufacturing plant in Georgia.
Total job creation by SK Innovation stands at 2,600.
Jun Kim is the CEO of SK Innovation.
South Enchem Ltd is spending US$61 million to build two new manufacturing plants in Jackson County.
Enchem specializes in the development and manufacturing of electrolytes for rechargeable batteries and electrostatic double-layer capacitors (ELDCs).
Enchem was the first supplier to follow SK Innovation, which broke ground in Jackson County in March 2019 on a US$1.67 billion battery manufacturing plant.
Since its founding in 2012, Enchem has developed multiple new electrolytes and functional additives to improve and extend battery life.
Governor Brian Kemp welcomed Enchem to Georgia, noting the investment follows their first international trip in June 2020.
Toyota Industries Compressor Parts America (TICA) announced in October 2020 that it would expand its Pendergrass, Georgia production facility to meet growing demand for onboard power electronics.
Toyota is investing US$5.5 million in a project that will create at least 27 new jobs in Jackson County over the next 18 months.
Toyota set up the TICA facility in 2012 with an initial investment of US$350 million to supply automotive compressors for Toyota Motor Corporation, Chrysler, Ford, General Motors, and others.
TICA works in concert with the adjacent TD Automotive Compressor Georgia facility, which assembles parts manufactured at the TICA plant.
The combined facilities encompass over one million square feet of production space and a total investment of more than US$1 billion.
Kota Otoshi is the executive officer for the electronics division of TICA’s parent company, Toyota Industries Corporation.
In 2018, GE Appliances invested US$55 million in a new Southeast Region Appliance Distribution Center in Commerce.

GE maintained a strong presence in Georgia with 1,960 employees at Roper Corporation, a wholly owned subsidiary in LaFayette, and 50 employees supporting Factory Service Operations based out of Atlanta.
John Scott is the Vice President and Director of Economic Development for the Jackson County Area Chamber of Commerce.
When complete, SK Innovation's Georgia location will give it a projected annual global capacity of 71 gigawatt hours together with facilities in Europe and Asia.
Jackson County is centrally located in the Southeast United States’ growing automotive industry.
The SK Group was among the first Korean investors in Georgia and has a longstanding history in the state.
Duckyang's investment helps Jackson County and Georgia secure a place in the automotive industry's future by leveraging strong relationships with SK Battery America and other Korean companies.

Jackson County's strategic location, strong workforce, quality of life, business-ready transportation infrastructure, and investments in workforce and logistics attract significant investment from recognized companies.
source_title: Jackson Alliance helping to establish Georgia as advanced manufacturing hub - AI Online -

facts: Georgia Power joined with Governor Brian Kemp and the Georgia Department of Economic Development to welcome Rivian to Georgia on December 16, 2021.
Rivian is a world-class electric sport utility vehicle and pickup company that places a focus on sustainability and positive environmental impact.
Rivian was named the 2022 MotorTrend Truck of the Year.
Rivian selected Georgia for a nearly 13 million square foot plant for vehicle and battery operations.
The plant is located along Interstate 20 near Social Circle.
source_title: Georgia Power welcomes Rivian to Georgia - Coastal Courier

facts: Kia Motors America dropped “Motors” from its name and is now known simply as “Kia America.”
The Korean automaker has a $25 billion plan to transition to electric vehicles, which prompted the rebranding.
Kia operates an assembly plant in Troup County producing 340,000 vehicles a year.
Gov. Brian Kemp announced July 20 a new statewide initiative aimed at strengthening Georgia’s status as a leader in the electric mobility industry.
The Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance (EMIA) will bring industry executives together with state leaders in government, education, utilities and nonprofits to build up the industry.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that Georgia has a proven track record of investing early in the resources and infrastructure needed to connect it to the world and develop jobs of the future.
Kemp said the Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance will ensure the state is positioned to continue leading the nation in the rapidly growing electric mobility industry.
Georgia already has two SK Innovation electric vehicle battery manufacturing facilities in Jackson County, representing a nearly $2.6 billion investment by the South Korean company.
Dutch electric-vehicle charging company Heliox decided to base its North American headquarters in Atlanta.
Turkish electric-vehicle parts manufacturer TEKLAS plans to open its first North American plant in Gordon County.
German-owned GEDIA, a manufacturer of lightweight auto-body parts, plans to open a plant in Whitfield County.
South Korea-based Duckyang, an electric mobility parts supplier, will build two plants near Braselton.

Chris Clark, president and CEO of the Georgia Chamber of Commerce, cited projections showing electric vehicles in Georgia will balloon from just 7% of the market in 2025 to 30% by 2030, 58% by 2040 and 96% by 2050.
Clark stated that Kia, SK and all these companies have to build out these vehicles and emphasized the desire for Georgia to do it.
Pat Wilson, commissioner of the state Department of Economic Development, said 55,000 jobs in Georgia are connected in some form to the auto industry.
Many of those 55,000 auto-connected jobs involve manufacturing vehicles with traditional internal-combustion engines in an industry that is rapidly moving to electric vehicles.
Wilson stated that the state will probably see more change in the next 20 years in the auto industry than in the past 100 years and is focused on public policy to help drive that transition.
Participants in the EMIA initiative will be divided into five committees that will set objectives in supply chain, infrastructure, workforce development, innovation and policy.
The Carl Vinson Institute at the University of Georgia will host committee meetings for the initiative.
The auto industry will be represented in the initiative by executives from Kia, Cox Automotive, Porsche and Sandy Springs-based Mercedes-Benz USA.
Porsche has its North American headquarters in Hapeville.
The initiative will also involve executives from Georgia Power and the state’s electric membership cooperatives (EMCs).
The public sector will be represented by the state Department of Transportation and the Technical College System of Georgia, which will take part in discussions on workforce development.
Wilson described the initiative as a true public-private partnership.
What emerges from the initiative could come in the form of legislation for the General Assembly to consider, new policy initiatives or a combination of the two.
Lawmakers previously approved a tax credit for purchasers of electric vehicles back in the 1990s when EVs were scarce, but eliminated it in 2015 as EV sales soared.
Georgia Rep. Chuck Martin, R-Alpharetta, the 2015 bill’s chief sponsor, said the tax credit was mainly generating sales for the Nissan Leaf electric vehicle and wasn't helping drive innovation.
Martin stated that the old tax credit was just subsidizing a purchase rather than driving innovation.
Clark agreed a tax credit to stimulate sales of EVs is no longer needed but expects some form of tax credit proposal could come out of the new initiative.
The expected new proposal will neither incentivize EV sales nor offer the jobs tax credit Georgia traditionally has provided industries the state hopes to attract.
Clark stated that the old jobs tax credit is not going to move the needle for this new industry and economy, and the state will have to listen to the industry.
Clark envisions new tax breaks that would incentivize gasoline stations to install charging stations for EVs or encourage homebuilders to put chargers in new houses.
Martin said state government needs to be careful not to get ahead of the industry by pushing too aggressively for a transition to all electric vehicles.
Martin stated that innovation will get the state there, but in some places there won’t be the electric grid or charging stations for that to happen.
Wilson said it’s uncertain what solutions the committees will hit upon and emphasized wanting to go into this with a blank slate and really listen to the professionals.
The EMIA committees will meet this fall with a goal of making recommendations in time for the 2022 General Assembly session starting in January.
source_title: Archives |

facts: Seojin Global secured a 222,618-square-foot industrial lease at 984 Broadway Ave. in Braselton, Georgia.
The company occupies the full Building 3 at 85 Braselton Logistics Center for a new distribution operation serving the greater Atlanta region.
Seojin Global manufactures aluminum cases and cabinet systems used across telecom, server, automotive, and energy storage applications.
Seojin Global operates as the U.S. subsidiary of South Korea-based Seojin System.
Craig Viergever, SIOR, of Lee & Associates – Atlanta and Jason Um of Lee & Associates – Detroit represented the tenant in the transaction.
Nathan Anderson and Darren Butler of NAI Brannen Goddard represented the landlord.
The lease was recognized as one of CoStar’s Power Broker Awards – Q4 2025 Top Industrial Leases.
The transaction reflects continued activity from international manufacturing groups expanding their logistics footprint within the Northeast Atlanta industrial corridor.
source_title: Lee & Associates Represents Seojin Global in 222,618 SF Lease in Braselton, Georgia - Atlanta

facts: Preciball USA plans to invest $17.6 million in a new production facility in Screven County.
The project will create 65 new jobs over the next several years.

Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the investment on February 06, 2026, describing Preciball USA as one of the small-to-medium-sized manufacturers whose products are critical to entire industries that call Georgia home.
Preciball USA designs and manufactures precision and industrial balls for industrial use.
The company’s products are used in applications such as bearings, pumps, valves, swivel joints, and more.
Preciball USA’s primary offices will remain open in Pooler.

Don Peak, Executive Vice President of Preciball USA, stated the new high-precision manufacturing facility will complement existing distribution operations and offer fully integrated, end-to-end domestic production.
The new manufacturing facility will be located at the Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD)-certified Screven County Industrial Park in Sylvania.
The company will hire for positions including management, engineering, quality technicians, industrial maintenance, and production roles.
Hiring is expected to begin in 2027.
Speaker Jon Burns stated the generational investment by Preciball USA will provide citizens of Screven County with employment opportunities for years to come.

Chairman Stephen Milner of the Screven County Development Authority noted the project reflects years of preparation at the Screven County Industrial Park and brings high-quality jobs and long-term economic value.

Chairman Will Boyd of the Screven County Board of Commissioners stated the investment strengthens the county's tax base, creates quality jobs, and ensures the county can continue to provide essential services while planning responsibly for future growth.

Senior Regional Project Manager Dorie Bacon represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team on this project in partnership with the Screven County Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, and Georgia Power.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson credited Governor Kemp, the Georgia General Assembly, and collaboration with the Department of Community Affairs for programs like the Rural Site Development Initiative benefiting communities statewide.
Preciball USA is part of the Precision Ball Group, a global organization rooted in Belgium’s Peltzer & Fils Group, whose origins trace back to the early 18th century.
source_title: Preciball USA Invests $17 Million in Screven County | Georgia

facts: Visteon Corporation announced an agreement to divest the majority of its global automotive interiors business to an affiliate of Cerberus Capital Management, L.P.
The transaction represents the second phase in a three-phase exit strategy, with 90 percent of the interiors business now under contract, offer, or sold.
The first transaction, involving the sale of Duckyang shares, has been completed.
The divestiture aligns with prior guidance to fully exit the interiors business with an aggregate neutral value impact to Visteon.
The move supports Visteon's strategic focus on core growth businesses: thermal management and cockpit electronic ecosystems.
The transaction is subject to regulatory reviews and other conditions, with completion expected by December 31, 2014.
Visteon completed the sale of its 50 percent ownership stake in a Korean automotive interiors joint venture, Duckyang Industry Co., Ltd., as part of the divestiture.
The agreement to sell the Duckyang stake was announced on March 19 for approximately $24.1 million, plus approximately $6 million in dividends.
The sold interiors operations generated revenues of approximately $1 billion in 2013.
These operations produce automotive interior products including cockpit modules, instrument panels, door panels, and floor consoles supplied to global vehicle manufacturers.
The divested operations encompass 15 manufacturing plants, five just-in-time assembly plants, and six engineering centers located in Europe, Asia, and South America.

As part of the transaction, the Cerberus affiliate made an irrevocable offer to purchase the interiors business in France, prompting Visteon France to begin consultation with employee representatives.
More than 4,000 manufacturing, engineering, and administrative employees are part of the business involved in the transaction.
Under the agreement terms, Visteon will divest the interiors operations for nominal consideration and contribute up to $95 million to the business.
The Cerberus affiliate will assume approximately $20 million of pension and other liabilities associated with the business.
Visteon will retain ownership in certain real estate and other assets in South America and Europe valued at approximately $35 million in aggregate.
Visteon will provide support services to the Cerberus affiliate following the transaction.

Visteon agreed to provide a seller-backed revolving credit facility to bridge any shortfall of a targeted $90 million in external credit facilities if not available by closing, repayable by the buyer once additional committed facilities are secured.
The $90 million financing is not anticipated to be utilized at closing and serves only to add liquidity to the business.
The potential amount of the Visteon-backed revolver, if drawn, is difficult to assess but is not expected to exceed $20 million once all external credit facilities are in place.

Timothy D. Leuliette, Visteon president and CEO, stated the transaction supports the intention to divest the interiors business and focus on core thermal management and cockpit electronic ecosystems businesses that enjoy market-leading positions in faster-growing segments.

Dev Kapadia, a Cerberus managing director, expressed pleasure in making a long-term investment in Visteon's interiors business, citing its strong products, skilled workforce, excellent customer relationships, and solid manufacturing operations, including in emerging markets.
Cerberus plans to use the acquired business as a platform to pursue additional acquisitions in the automotive interiors space after completing the transaction.
Visteon designs, engineers, and manufactures innovative components and systems for virtually every vehicle manufacturer worldwide.
Visteon maintains corporate offices in Van Buren Township, Michigan; Shanghai, China; and Chelmsford, UK.
Visteon operates facilities in 29 countries and employs approximately 24,000 people globally.
Visteon reported sales of $7.4 billion in 2013.
Cerberus Capital Management, L.P., established in 1992, manages over $25 billion across distressed securities, private equity, commercial lending, and real estate strategies.
Cerberus operates from headquarters in New York City with affiliate and advisory offices across the U.S., Europe, and Asia.

canonical_name: Alternative Fuel Vehicle Refueling Property Credit (30C)
facts: The Alternative Fuel Vehicle Refueling Property Credit is available for qualified AFV fueling property installed in qualified locations on or after January 1, 2023, and through June 30, 2026.

A single item of 30C property is each charging port or fuel dispenser, as well as each energy storage property for electricity, hydrogen, natural gas, propane, E85, or biodiesel blends of at least 20% (B20+).
Components and parts that are essential to the operation of the charging port or fuel dispenser, including labor costs for constructing and installing the property, are eligible for the 30C credit.
Businesses are eligible for a tax credit of 6% of the depreciable costs, up to $100,000 per item.
Businesses may receive a tax credit of 30% of the depreciable costs, up to $100,000 per item, if the installation meets U.S. Department of Labor prevailing wage and apprenticeship requirements.
Tax exempt entities, including state and local governments, may be eligible to receive this credit in the same amount as businesses via IRS elective pay provisions.

Consumers who purchase qualified alternative fueling equipment for installation at their principal residence in qualified locations on or after January 1, 2023, through June 30, 2026, may receive a tax credit of up to 30% of the cost, up to $1,000.
The program references Public Law 119-21, Public Law 117-169, and 26 U.S. Code 30C, 30D, 38, and 6417.
Covered technologies include Biodiesel, Ethanol, EVs, Hydrogen Fuel Cells, Natural Gas, PHEVs, and Propane (LPG).
The incentive is administered by the U.S. Internal Revenue Service under Federal jurisdiction as an Incentive type.
The program was enacted on Aug 8, 2005, and amended on Jul 4, 2025.
The credit expires on June 30, 2026.
source_title: Alternative Fuels Data Center: Alternative Fuel Infrastructure Tax Credit

facts: Enchem America Inc. is a leading electrolyte manufacturer selecting Tennessee for its second U.S. facility.
The company will invest $152.5 million to establish electrolyte manufacturing operations in Brownsville, Tennessee.
The new facility is located at the I-40 Advantage Industrial Park, a Select Tennessee Certified Site.
The project will create 190 new jobs in Tier 4, At-Risk Haywood County.
The Brownsville facility will operate similarly to Enchem’s U.S. headquarters in Commerce, Georgia.
The facility will manufacture electrolytes for electric vehicle (EV) batteries as well as other EV battery-related services.
Enchem America Inc. is a subsidiary of Enchem Co., Ltd., which is based in South Korea.
The company specializes in the development and manufacturing of electrolytes and other additives for rechargeable batteries.
With the additional Tennessee operations, Enchem will be able to further support its EV partners across the Southeast.
Since 2019, TNECD has supported more than 50 economic development projects in the Southwest Tennessee region, resulting in nearly 9,300 job commitments and $7.2 billion in capital investment.

Gov. Bill Lee stated that Enchem’s decision to invest more than $150 million in Tennessee is a testament to the state's unmatched business climate, highly skilled workforce, and position as a leader in the automotive industry.
TNECD Commissioner Stuart McWhorter noted that South Korea is one of Tennessee’s top 10 foreign direct investment partners and that Enchem is boosting the EV supply chain across Tennessee.
Enchem Co. Ltd. CEO and founder Jung Kang Oh expressed gratitude to Haywood County, the City of Brownsville, and professionals for assisting Enchem's expansion in North America.
Jung Kang Oh stated that the growth of the EV market has been remarkable and indications suggest it will continue at an accelerated pace over the coming decade.

Jung Kang Oh expressed confidence that Enchem is well positioned to capitalize on EV market growth and look forward to exploring innovation and partnership opportunities with Haywood County and the City of Brownsville.
Haywood County Mayor David Livingston thanked Enchem for picking Haywood County and Brownsville as home to its newest location.
Mayor Livingston stated the economic opportunity will help transform Haywood County and all West Tennessee with great, new high-paying jobs.
Brownsville Mayor Bill Rawls welcomed Enchem America to the City of Brownsville.
Mayor Rawls stated the announcement brings to fruition years of work and investment in I-40 Advantage Industrial Park fulfilling the goal of bringing high-quality job opportunities to the city.
TVA senior vice president of economic development John Bradley congratulated Enchem America on its decision to establish operations in Haywood County.

John Bradley stated TVA is proud to partner with HTL Advantage, Brownsville-Haywood County Industrial Development Corporation, City of Brownsville, and the Tennessee Department of Economic and Community Development to support companies creating jobs and investment in the region.
Sen. Page Walley (R-Savannah) welcomed Enchem America to Haywood County.
Sen. Walley stated the investment will bring many jobs, boost the regional economy, and further establish the community as a hub of innovation at the forefront of this rapidly growing industry.
Rep. Johnny Shaw (D-Bolivar) noted that the prospect of a more than $152 million investment is another example of unprecedented growth in Brownsville, Haywood County, and West Tennessee.
Rep. Shaw stated that BlueOval City lit the fuse for what continues to be an economic explosion for the area.
source_title: Governor Lee, Commissioner McWhorter Announce Enchem America Inc. to Locate US Manufacturing Operations in Brownsville – Haywood County, Tennessee

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Jack Link’s will create 800 jobs and invest $450 million for a new manufacturing facility in Perry, GA.
The announcement was made with company, state, and local officials at an event at the future home of Jack Link’s.
Governor Kemp stated he promised to bring opportunities to rural communities overlooked in the past and highlighted the 800 quality jobs and $450 million investment.
Governor Kemp noted the company will take advantage of Georgia’s award-winning workforce program, Quick Start, to quickly fill available positions with Georgians.
Jack Link’s was founded in 1985 in Minong, Wisconsin, and is a family-owned and operated company known for high-quality meat snacks and environmentally friendly manufacturing processes.
The company operates multiple sites throughout the Midwest and has facilities in New Zealand, Brazil, and Germany.
Troy Link, CEO of Jack Link’s, expressed excitement to partner with the State of Georgia and Houston County on a state-of-the-art facility and commitment to becoming a strong community advocate.
Jack Link’s selected a 120-acre site in the I-75 GRAD Certified Site in Houston County located at 720 Perry Parkway in Perry for the new production facility.
The facility is slated to be operational at the end of 2023.
Ben Hulbert, Chairman of the Development Authority of Houston County, praised the company's relationship-driven approach and alignment with community values.

Hulbert noted the project involves partners including Houston County, the City of Perry, the Houston County School District, Central Georgia Technical College, and other key stakeholders aiming to bring quality jobs to citizens.
Career opportunities at the facility will include positions in management, production and production supervision, quality, maintenance, engineering, and general office support.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) was represented by Global Commerce Senior Project Manager Fernanda Kirchner, partnering with the Development Authority of Houston County, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson welcomed Jack Link’s to Georgia, emphasizing that economic investment and job growth are crucial for the long-term health and prosperity of rural communities.

Houston County’s I-75 GRAD Site is a Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified park, requiring due diligence and specific certification requirements prior to business visits to catalyze economic growth.

Link Snacks, Inc. (Jack Link’s) is the #1 Meat Snack brand in the U.S., headquartered in Minong, Wisconsin, with a portfolio including Jack Link’s®, Lorissa’s Kitchen®, Wild River®, Golden Island®, MATADOR®, BiFi®, and Peperami®.
The company's market position is ranked by NielsenIQ TSR RMS for 52 weeks ending 3/26/2022 in Total US Convenience, Super Category: Salty Snacks by Brand Low.

links.context: Deputy Press Secretary for Governor Kemp

source_title: Jack Link’s to Create 800 Jobs with Facility in Houston County | Georgia

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp and GDEcD announced Georgia sustained momentum through economic development projects in key industries during the first half of Fiscal Year 2023 (July 1 through December 31, 2022).
The 218 locations or expansions supported by GDEcD’s Global Commerce division are expected to create approximately 17,500 new jobs and more than $13 billion in investment for the state.
Both the number of locations and total investment amount increased over the same period last year.
Areas outside the 10-county metro Atlanta region accounted for 85 percent of jobs created and 92 percent of investments from economic development projects during the period.
Four of Georgia’s largest industries – advanced manufacturing, automotive, aerospace, and food processing – accounted for nearly 75 percent of new jobs and 84 percent of investments.
Planned investments resulting from these projects increased by more than 38 percent compared to the same time last year.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson highlighted proven solutions such as the Georgia Quick Start job training program and GRAD certified sites that support accelerated project timelines.
The automotive industry was the second-largest source of new jobs and investment, after advanced manufacturing.

In October, state and local officials joined company representatives including Korean Ambassador to the U.S. Tae-yong Cho for the groundbreaking of the new Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) EV facility.
The HMGMA EV facility will support more than 8,100 direct jobs in Bryan, Bulloch, Chatham and Effingham Counties.
Three off-site HMGMA suppliers announced locations following HMGMA’s groundbreaking.
These supplier additions swiftly surpassed the initial $1 billion in investment predicted to come from them.
International companies have been valued partners in creating a closed-loop electric mobility supply chain and accounted for 23 percent of state projects since July 1, 2022.
Historic project examples include the partnership between SK On and Hyundai Motor Group in Bartow County.
Next-generation developer FREYR Battery’s Coweta County facility was also highlighted as a historic project example.
Both the SK On/Hyundai partnership and the FREYR Battery facility are in the top five largest projects by investment in the state’s history.
Battery recyclers Sung-Il Hitech and Denkai America joined Georgia’s growing electric mobility ecosystem.
GDEcD Deputy Commissioner for Global Commerce Kristi Brigman noted that companies use programs like Georgia Quick Start to build talent and workforce from the ground up, helping Georgians upskill.

links.context: Details of state-announced economic development projects

source_title: Key Industries Sustain Momentum for State's Economic Development | Georgia

facts: Clean Cities and Communities offers webinars for coalitions and stakeholders with information on transportation topics including alternative fuels, electric vehicles, efficiency improvement strategies, and web-based tools.
No upcoming webinars are scheduled at this time.
Webinars from the past 3 years are listed below.
The webinar 'Features, Capabilities, and Deployment of the Megawatt Charging System Standard' took place on Feb. 4, 2025, with a recording to be posted soon.
The webinar 'Heavy-Duty Battery Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Scenario Analysis Model (HEVISAM)' took place on April 3, 2024.
The webinar 'Amplifying Industry Investments in Transportation, Charging Infrastructure, and Energy Projects with the U.S. Department of Energy’s Loan Programs Office' took place on March 13, 2024.
The webinar 'Using JOBS EV 1.0 To Estimate Employment Impacts of EV Manufacturing' took place on Feb. 28, 2024.
source_title: Clean Cities and Communities: Webinars

canonical_name: Tennessee Valley Authority Small Modular Reactor
facts: The U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) accepted a construction permit application from the Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA) to build one of the nation’s first small modular reactors (SMR).
TVA is the first utility applying to build GE Vernova Hitachi’s BWRX-300 design in the United States.
The utility submitted its application to the NRC in late May to build the advanced nuclear plant at its Clinch River site near Oak Ridge, Tennessee.
TVA already secured the nation’s first and only early site permit for an SMR with support from the U.S. Department of Energy to clear the environmental review process.
The next step is a full safety review of the reactor design before it is cleared for construction.
TVA President and CEO Don Moul stated that this is an exciting step to bringing the nation’s first utility-led SMR online.

As the first utility in the United States to have a construction permit application under review by the NRC for the BWRX-300 reactor, this will create a path for other utilities to deploy the reactor and ensure energy security and reliable electricity.
The BWRX-300 is the only boiling water SMR design under development in the United States.
The 300-megawatt-electric advanced light water system is a smaller, simplified version of GE Vernova Hitachi’s licensed Economic Simplified Boiling Water Reactor.
The reactor will leverage established supply chains that could accelerate its deployment.
In 2023, TVA joined a global collaboration with Ontario Power Generation to expedite technology development of the SMR for use in the United States and Canada.
Four BWRX-300 reactors were recently cleared for construction in Clarington, Ontario, with commercial operation expected by the end of 2029.
TVA anticipates preliminary site preparation work could begin as early as next year while the NRC reviews the company’s construction permit application.

The U.S. Department of Energy is committed to unleashing commercial nuclear power in the United States and is working diligently to enable the deployment and export of next-generation nuclear power systems, like SMRs.
source_title: NRC Dockets Construction Permit Application for TVA Small Modular Reactor | Department of Energy

facts: Hitachi Automotive Systems Americas, Inc. is a Tier 1 supplier of world-class products to the global automotive market.
The company announced plans to create 100 jobs through an expansion at its existing facility in Monroe, Georgia.
The planned investment is at least $100 million, with potential up to $330 million.
Governor Brian P. Kemp highlighted that Hitachi has been a partner to the Walton County community by investing in employees and creating jobs for more than twenty years.
Governor Kemp expressed confidence that the investment will positively impact the local community and encourage continued success in Georgia’s strong automotive industry.
Hitachi Automotive Systems Americas, Inc. is a group company of Hitachi Automotive Systems, Ltd., which is headquartered in Tokyo.

Hitachi Automotive Systems, Ltd. manufactures and markets engine powertrain systems, electric powertrain systems, and integrated vehicle control systems for major automotive original equipment manufacturers (OEM) worldwide.
Hitachi Automotive Systems Americas, Inc. President and CEO Paul Carroll stated the company has been operating in Georgia for over twenty-two years.
The Georgia factory supplies advanced technology products for major automotive OEMs including Ford Motor Company, General Motors, Nissan, Subaru, and Honda.
Paul Carroll noted a great partnership with stakeholders, especially the State of Georgia, Walton County, and the City of Monroe.
Hitachi Automotive Systems Americas, Inc. opened its first facility in Georgia in 1997.
The company currently employs 900 Georgians.
The expansion is intended to increase production capacity in response to the growth of the automotive industry.
Walton County Commission Chairman Kevin Little stated that Hitachi has become Walton County's largest employer and provides quality jobs and investment.
City of Monroe Mayor John Howard praised Hitachi for continuing to invest in high technology and top-quality jobs for the community.
Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) Sr. Project Manager Tammy Caudell represented the Global Commerce Division on the project.
The GDEcD partnered with the Development Authority of Walton County, Walton EMC, and the Georgia Department of Community Affairs for this expansion.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that companies like Hitachi create jobs, take an active role in the community, and have driven success and interest from Japanese companies in Georgia.
Hitachi Automotive Systems, Ltd. is a wholly owned subsidiary of Hitachi, Ltd., headquartered in Tokyo, Japan.
The parent company develops, manufactures, sells, and services automotive components, transportation related components, industrial machines and systems.
source_title: Hitachi Automotive Systems America to Expand in Walton County | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: Eastman Chemical Company is a global advanced materials and specialty additives company headquartered in Kingsport, Tennessee.
The company operates 48 manufacturing facilities and holds equity interests in three manufacturing joint ventures across 14 countries.
Kingsport, Tennessee hosts the company's headquarters and largest manufacturing facility.
In 2018, Eastman reported sales revenue of $10.2 billion, earnings before interest and taxes (EBIT) of $1.6 billion, and net earnings attributable to Eastman of $1.1 billion.
Diluted earnings per share attributable to Eastman were $7.56 in 2018.
Cash provided by operating activities was $1.5 billion in 2018.
Excluding non-core and unusual items, adjusted EBIT was $1.6 billion and adjusted diluted earnings per share attributable to Eastman were $8.20.
Approximately 60 percent of 2018 sales revenue was generated from outside the United States and Canada region.

The company produces Impera™ high performance resins for tires, which enable tire manufacturers to improve safety and handling, balance performance and fuel economy needs, and achieve superior tack levels for tire construction.
Eastman manufactures Tetrashield™ performance polyester resins based on proprietary monomer technology for automotive coatings, industrial, and food packaging markets.
The company recently introduced a 72-inch version of automotive paint protection film marketed under established trademarks.

Eastman completed an expansion of its manufacturing facility in Kuantan, Malaysia for Crystex™ insoluble sulfur rubber additives in the second quarter of 2018, with commercial sales beginning in the first quarter of 2019.
A capacity expansion at the Kingsport, Tennessee manufacturing facility for specialty ketones became fully operational in the second quarter of 2018.

The Advanced Materials segment produces polymers, films, and plastics with differentiated performance properties for value-added end-uses in transportation, consumables, building and construction, durable goods, and health and wellness markets.

Key technology platforms utilized by the company include cellulose esters, copolyesters, polyvinyl butyral (PVB), polyester films, insoluble sulfur, hydrocarbon resins, alkylamine derivatives, and propylene derivatives.
source_title: Document

facts: The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) announced $70.8 million to support federal partnerships with state and local governments for small- and medium-sized manufacturers (SMMs) across the automotive, battery recycling, and smart manufacturing sectors.
The projects were selected by the Office of Manufacturing and Energy Supply Chains (MESC).
The funding will help SMMs upgrade manufacturing facilities to support advanced vehicles, increase the efficiency of battery recycling programs, and modernize state-level manufacturing bases.

The initiative aims to ensure benefits of federal investments in climate, clean energy, and other areas flow to disadvantaged communities marginalized by underinvestment and overburdened by pollution.

U.S. Secretary of Energy Jennifer M. Granholm stated that the projects cement the Administration’s state-based efforts to support the nation’s auto workforce and communities while strengthening domestic manufacturing and bolstering energy security.

Five states with significant automotive workforces were selected to receive $50 million from the Inflation Reduction Act to invest in upgrading auto facilities for growing vehicle markets, including electric, hybrid, or fuel cell vehicle supply chains.
This vehicle facility upgrade funding is made possible by the Department’s $2 billion Domestic Automotive Manufacturing Conversion Grant program.
Michigan was allocated $22,653,072 for automotive facility upgrades.
Indiana was allocated $10,793,685 for automotive facility upgrades.
Kentucky was allocated $6,001,534 for automotive facility upgrades.
Tennessee was allocated $5,555,067 for automotive facility upgrades.
Illinois was allocated $4,996,639 for automotive facility upgrades.

Seven states and local governments were selected for award negotiation to receive $11.5 million in Bipartisan Infrastructure Law funds as part of the second round of MESC’s State and Local Battery Recycling program.
Tennessee's Department of Environment and Conservation was selected for battery recycling funding.
Michigan's Department of Environment, Great Lakes and Energy was selected for battery recycling funding.
Nevada's Washoe County was selected for battery recycling funding.
New York's Erie County was selected for battery recycling funding.
Wisconsin's Department of Natural Resources was selected for battery recycling funding.
Iowa's Dubuque Metropolitan Area Solid Waste Agency was selected for battery recycling funding.
Indiana's Allen County was selected for battery recycling funding.
Five states were selected for award negotiation to receive $9.3 million in Bipartisan Infrastructure Law funds as part of the second round of the State Manufacturing Leadership Program.

The State Manufacturing Leadership Program partners with states to establish programs accelerating smart manufacturing and high-performance computing adoption across SMMs, building on twelve prior round 1 awards.
Illinois was selected under the Smart Manufacturing Lighthouse Program (SMLP).
Michigan was selected under Project DIAMOnD Statewide Expansion: Distributed, Independent, Agile, Manufacturing On Demand.
Oregon was selected under the Oregon Smart Manufacturing Initiative (OSMI).
Tennessee was selected under the Tennessee Partnership for Smart Manufacturing (TPSM).
Virginia was selected under the Virginia Smart Manufacturing Accelerator 2 (VSMA 2).

Smart manufacturing uses emerging and advanced technologies to increase traditional manufacturing efficiency, lower costs, increase energy efficiency and performance, reduce material demand, and improve product quality.
The program expands SMM access to technical assistance, training, facility assessments, apprenticeships, and financial assistance.
Selection for award negotiations does not constitute a commitment by the DOE to issue awards or provide funding.
Before funding issuance, applicants must undergo a negotiation process and complete an environmental review.
The DOE reserves the right to cancel negotiations and rescind selections at any time during the process.
source_title: Biden-Harris Administration Announces Nearly $71 Million to Continue Strengthening America's Auto Communities, Expanding Battery Recycling, and Modernizing Manufacturing | Department of Energy

facts: Georgia Tech and Hyundai Motor Group signed a memorandum of understanding on Sept. 19, 2023.
The MoU covers collaboration on research and applications to support sustainable mobility, developing a hydrogen economy, workforce development, and making cities smarter and more sustainable.
Hyundai Motor Group is investing $12.6 billion in new dedicated electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facilities in Georgia.
The investment includes the $7.59 billion Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) site in Bryan County, Georgia.
The HMGMA site will create more than 8,500 direct jobs.
The investment also includes a $5 billion battery manufacturing facility joint venture with SK On in Bartow County, Georgia.
The SK On joint venture facility will employ approximately 3,500 people.
Proximity to Georgia Tech was one of the important considerations in deciding the locations of these facilities.
Kia Georgia’s West Point assembly plant began vehicle production in 2009 under the leadership of Honorary Chairman Mong-Koo Chung and Executive Chair Euisun Chung.
University System of Georgia Chancellor Sonny Perdue, who was Governor of Georgia at the time of the Kia Georgia plant’s opening, participated in the HMG-Georgia Tech MOU ceremony.
A recent study by the Center for Automotive Research reveals Hyundai Motor’s automotive operations contributed $20.1 billion and 190,000 jobs to the U.S. economy in 2021 alone.
According to the study, Hyundai’s investments in Georgia are expected to create nearly 40,000 jobs and $4.6 billion in individual earnings every year.

Georgia Tech President Ángel Cabrera stated that Hyundai is a global brand synonymous with quality, innovation, and a commitment to advancing technology, and noted an alignment of values between the organizations.
Cabrera expressed gratitude for Hyundai's transformative investments in the state and pride that the Hyundai brand will feature prominently on campus.
Jay Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, said Georgia is like a second home to the company and emphasized their mission-driven focus on sustainable mobility and the hydrogen economy.

Chang stated the partnership with Georgia Tech includes research and applications for sustainable mobility, workforce development, and smart cities, calling it the beginning of a decades-long partnership and a reason for choosing Georgia for EV investments.

José Muñoz, President and Global COO of Hyundai Motor Company, and President and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America, said Hyundai’s investments will create tens of thousands of jobs and bring tens of billions of dollars in economic impact for decades to come.
Muñoz noted that proximity to institutions like Georgia Tech was one of the reasons Hyundai selected Georgia for its new EV manufacturing facility.
Muñoz envisioned zero emissions hydrogen-powered vehicles on campus, advanced air mobility shuttles to Hartsfield International Airport, and hands-free autonomous vehicles on I-75 and I-85.
The expanded relationship with Georgia Tech will include opportunities for student professional development and cooperative work programs.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Georgia stands at the forefront of the mobility sector's transformation toward sustainable transportation alternatives and is building an ecosystem to support it.
Hyundai will collaborate with Georgia Tech on joint research and development programs focused on sustainable mobility, battery technology, and expanding the hydrogen economy.
Georgia Tech and Hyundai will create technical training and leadership development programming for Hyundai employees.
The partnership will initiate engagement activities to stimulate interest in STEM degrees among students.
Hyundai will actively participate in career recruitment events at Georgia Tech.
Hyundai will create learning programs for both undergraduate and graduate students.
Hyundai’s partnership includes field-naming recognition at Bobby Dodd Stadium, which is now known as Bobby Dodd Stadium at Hyundai Field.
The partnership provides a pathway for Georgia Tech to expand its stature as a leading research entity in automotive electrification through world-class hydrogen research.
The partnership gives Georgia Tech an opportunity to contribute to the state of Georgia’s commitment as a hub for electric vehicle production.
The partnership brings new revenue for Georgia Tech Athletics to provide resources for student-athletes and teams.
source_title: Georgia Tech and Hyundai Motor Group Sign MoU for Future Mobility Collaboration | Hyundai Motor Group

facts: Hyundai Motor Group and Georgia Tech announced an expansion of their collaboration on April 30, 2026, to advance hydrogen-powered transportation, deepen applied research and education, and accelerate zero-emissions vehicles in Georgia.

The partnership brings hydrogen fuel cell vehicles and fueling infrastructure to the Georgia Tech campus, positioning it as one of the nation's most prominent campus-based examples for hydrogen mobility.

Ken Ramírez serves as Executive Vice President and Head of Global Energy and Hydrogen Business at Hyundai Motor Group; he is a 1991 Georgia Tech alumnus and currently sits on the Georgia Tech Advisory Board.

The President and CEO of Hyundai Motor North America announced that Hyundai donated four NEXO fuel cell electric SUVs to expand clean mobility on campus and provide real-world experience with zero-emissions transportation technology.
The partnership includes a hydrogen electrolyzer project to be installed at Georgia Tech’s North Avenue Research Area (NARA).
The donated vehicles and installed infrastructure will support campus operations and interdisciplinary research, using the university as a living laboratory for clean transportation technologies.
Faculty and students will study insights from this initiative to inform and accelerate the widespread use of hydrogen technology in campuses, fleets, cities, and freight corridors.
The initiative supports Georgia Tech’s strategic plan goal of expanding the use of zero-emissions vehicles powered by sustainable energy sources.
Beyond hydrogen energy, the partnership aims to advance innovation in broader mobility areas and create an ecosystem for collaboration, innovation, and education.

Hyundai's regional presence in Georgia includes the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) and HTWO Logistics, a clean logistics partnership operating Hyundai XCIENT fuel cell heavy-duty trucks near Savannah.
The collaboration reinforces the link between education, research, and Hyundai’s long-term goal of achieving carbon neutrality by 2045.
The partnership aims to generate workforce talent and position Georgia as a national leader in hydrogen and zero-emissions transportation.
