facts: Hitachi entered into an agreement with Honda to transfer a portion of its common shares in Astemo to Honda.
Hitachi will transfer 21% of its common shares in Astemo to Honda at a transaction price of approximately 152.3 billion yen.
Following the share transfer, Hitachi’s ownership ratio of voting rights for Astemo will change from Hitachi: Honda: JICC-01 = 40.0%: 40.0%: 20.0% to 19.0%: 61.0%: 20.0%.
The share transfer is expected to close in the first quarter of fiscal year ending March 31, 2027, subject to regulatory approvals and customary closing conditions.
Astemo will no longer be an equity-method associate of Hitachi following the transfer.
Hitachi will continue supporting Astemo’s sustainable growth and enhancement of corporate value as a shareholder, taking an IPO into consideration, through assistance focused on digital technologies.

Hitachi will utilize funds obtained through the share transfer in accordance with its capital allocation policy to maximize corporate value and accelerate growth toward achieving its management plan, “Inspire 2027.”
The impact of the share transfer on Hitachi’s consolidated financial statement is not material.
Hitachi operates globally in Digital Systems & Services, Energy, Mobility, and Connective Industries sectors through its Social Innovation Business (SIB) and Strategic SIB Business Unit.
Hitachi generates value from integrating data, technology, and domain knowledge via its Lumada platform to solve customer and social challenges.
Hitachi’s revenues for FY2024 (ended March 31, 2025) totaled 9,783.3 billion yen.
Hitachi operates 618 consolidated subsidiaries and employs approximately 280,000 people worldwide.

facts: Hollingsworth & Vose (H&V) is strategically investing in its Mysuru India plant, doubling manufacturing capacity to produce battery separators that serve the South Asia and Global markets.
In 2014, H&V acquired Raman FibreScience Pvt Ltd, a technology company based in Mysuru, India.
The facility specializes in developing innovations in fiber-based, wetlaid filtration media and battery separators.
Today in Mysuru, H&V produces battery separator products that are used in a variety of energy storage applications.

Nick Starita, president, Energy Solutions with H&V, says the investment will more than double capacity to serve the global market and is an exciting opportunity to strengthen on-the-ground presence in India.
The growth will enable H&V to further support the global transition to clean and sustainable energy storage solutions.
H&V manufactures Hi-Sep battery separators in Mysuru which are integral in industrial and automotive applications.
Hi-Sep technology also serves a niche in applications such as electric rickshaws, back-up power and industrial forklifts.
Electric rickshaws are popular across Asia and are a growing form of transportation.
H&V’s investment will expand capacity to manufacture Hi-Sep battery separators and Absorbent Glass Mat (AGM) separators for automotive and industrial applications.
The investment will enable production of HEPA filtration media for regional customers.
source_title: Hollingsworth & Vose Doubles Energy Storage Capacity in India - Nonwovens Industry

facts: US-based Hollingsworth & Vose Company (H&V) announced the acquisition of Mysore-based Raman FibreScience Pvt Ltd for an undisclosed amount.
Raman FibreScience is a technology and innovation-driven company specialising in wet-laid, fiber-based materials for filtration media and battery separator applications.
Hollingsworth & Vose Company is a global leader in these markets with operations worldwide.
H&V CEO Val Hollingsworth stated that Raman FibreScience has a highly professional team with considerable experience in fibre forming technology.
Raman FibreScience has a unique culture where research and development and manufacturing are highly integrated.
The company leveraged this capability to develop and take to international markets some very unique and innovative products.
India represents an exciting market for H&V, and the RFS Mysore location and team provide H&V a strong on-the-ground operational base with which to build and grow its India presence.
Aroon Raman, Managing Director of Raman Fibre Science, stated that both companies are privately held with world-class technical capabilities and a policy of investing in innovation for the long term.
Raman will continue on the board of the acquired company following the acquisition.
The acquisition is effective immediately.
Veda Corporate Advisors served as advisors to the transaction in India.

facts: Hollingsworth & Vose is a trusted supplier of energy and filtration solutions with 13 production facilities strategically located in North America, Asia, and Europe.
In 2014, U.S.-based Hollingsworth & Vose acquired Raman FibreScience Pvt Ltd, a technology company based in Mysuru, India.
Raman FibreScience specializes in developing innovations in fiber-based, wet-laid filtration media and battery separators and was a natural complement to H&V’s existing business.
H&V successfully integrated the India R&D and manufacturing operations extending the company’s reach to supply Mysuru-based battery products to global customers.
The acquisition has enabled H&V’s manufacturing facility in Mysuru to produce battery separator products that expand its offerings into energy storage.
Hi-Sep™ battery separators are produced in Mysuru and are commonly used in both industrial and automotive applications.
In 2022, H&V is expanding its manufacturing capacity in Mysuru, India.
The investment H&V is committing will more than double H&V’s existing capacity to serve the South Asia and Global market.
H&V’s investment will expand capacity to manufacture Hi-Sep battery separators and Absorbent Glass Mat (AGM) separators for automotive and industrial applications.

The strategic acquisition of Raman FibreScience, coupled with the commitment to expand South Asia production capacity, has enabled H&V to further support the global transition to clean and sustainable energy storage solutions.

facts: Hollingsworth & Vose (H&V) is a global leader in the lead-acid battery industry for decades, manufacturing advanced separator products.
H&V targets new technologies aligning with clean energy, decarbonization, electrification, and climate technology megatrends.
H&V develops separator products for emerging battery chemistries serving energy storage systems (ESS), data centers, and advanced lithium-ion and solid-state batteries.
H&V also produces separators for hydrogen production and CO2 capture applications.

H&V actively seeks partnerships with battery chemistry developers to optimize new battery chemistry performance early in the development process using material science, manufacturing at scale, and non-woven design architecture.

H&V is spearheading transformational change in emerging battery technologies including alternative aqueous-based batteries for high-power applications (e.g., data center UPS), flow batteries for long-duration storage, GEM technology (next-generation lead-acid batteries), and lithium-ion batteries.
These emerging battery technologies require durable, scalable, and robust electrolyte-compatible separators to meet application demands.
Lithium-ion (Li-ion) has become a dominant and preferred energy storage choice for many automotive and industrial applications.
Nickel-zinc and other battery technologies are receiving renewed attention due to increasing demand for commercial-scale storage and varied use cases.
H&V controls factors such as chemical compatibility, porosity, volume, and mechanics to deliver proper separator solutions for various battery chemistries.
H&V leads the market in battery separator innovation for emerging technologies including alkaline batteries, solid-state batteries, and synthetic and glass separators for other battery chemistries.
H&V is developing a nonwoven glass electrode material (GEM) that can replace lead plates found in lead-acid batteries.
GEM technology offers improved cycle life by eliminating failure mechanisms associated with lead plates.
GEM technology improves manufacturability by allowing engineering firms to replace stamping lead plate equipment with equipment for glass electrode materials.
Research and development in GEM technology is ongoing, with H&V aiming to bring safe and viable new battery technologies like GEM to global marketplaces.

Rising electrification and lithium-ion batteries throughout mobility, automotive, and energy storage industries are increasing demand for thermal barrier technologies to protect users from battery failure and thermal runaway.
Thermal barriers prevent thermal propagation and battery fires, giving passengers enough time to safely evacuate in automotive applications.
H&V is developing a physical thermal barrier material that goes between battery cells and between the battery pack and the vehicle cabin.
H&V leverages glass fiber and non-woven fiber production capabilities to deliver thermal management solutions for consumers and original equipment suppliers throughout the value chain.
Working with global partners, H&V is aiming for a soft launch of its thermal barrier in 2025.

H&V manufactures alkaline-stable, high-efficiency separators using comprehensive production and additive incorporation capabilities to support low greenhouse gas (GHG) electrolysis methods for hydrogen production.
H&V produces highly CO2-absorbing non-woven materials that improve run time efficiency and durability for various carbon capture systems.
H&V's current battery separator offerings surpass competitor products on the market due to unique engineering expertise and manufacturing capabilities.
H&V has decades of proven experience supplying innovative materials and solutions that shape the future of lithium-ion batteries, lead-acid batteries, and other emerging battery technologies.
source_title: Advanced Energy Solutions | H&V

facts: Hollingsworth & Vose Company is a globally recognized leader in technically advanced fibrous composite and non-woven materials for battery separators, filtration media, and industrial applications.
The company sells products in more than 80 countries.
H&V is privately held with headquarters in East Walpole, MA.
The company operates 12 manufacturing facilities across North America, Europe, and China.
H&V maintains sales offices in the Americas, Europe, and Asia.
H&V produces battery separator products at facilities in Easton, NY (Washington County), West Groton, MA, the UK, and China.
H&V has captured U.S. market share for absorbed glass mat (AGM) separators used in demanding lead-acid battery applications.
The company has 30 years of experience manufacturing AGM battery separators, positioning it for separator development, testing, validation, and commercial qualification with battery manufacturers.

Modern passenger cars have greatly increased electrical demand with additional systems such as GPS, displays, entertainment equipment, video cameras, collision avoidance systems, and other electronics.
H&V delivers AGM battery separators that ensure batteries perform at optimum levels in vehicles with high electrical demand.
The EnergyGuard® product provides 200% of the tensile strength and 320% greater puncture resistance versus standard glass mat separators.
These features make the EnergyGuard® product ideal for high-speed automated manufacturing and resilient in challenging environments.
H&V manufactures separators at regional mills around the world to enable fast delivery and local application.
Start-Stop micro-hybrids save fuel and reduce emissions by turning off the engine in place of idling.
This innovation places new demands on car batteries, particularly with respect to partial state of charge management and charge acceptance.
The duty cycles of a stop-start lead acid battery are much more demanding and require advanced design, manufacture and processing from suppliers to the industry.
H&V is positioned to supply separator technology to the automotive market based on already established industrial and telecommunications battery systems.
Advanced designs for these systems can now be applied to the emerging stop-start micro-hybrid automotive market.

H&V has been the recipient of a previous New York State Department of Energy & Development Authority (NYSERDA) grant for micro-hybrid vehicle battery separator research, which was conducted in their NY facilities.
H&V currently continues to work with NYSERDA and NY-BEST support on micro-hybrid battery separator research.
H&V serves as a supplier of key battery separators for this cutting-edge technology.

Kevin Porter, Director of Grants and Funding at H&V, stated that the company appreciates NYSERDA's support and is pleased to be a member of NY-BEST, participating in a forum to bring industry, academia and government together to foster innovation and research for the stakeholders of the State of New York.
Established in 1843, H&V is recognized as the technology leader in the manufacture of specialty fibrous composite and nonwoven materials.

The company has a heritage of investment in research and development as well as manufacturing processes, continually striving to advance the state of the art in technology, production, and quality by employing systems such as Lean and Six Sigma and an internal customized Stage-Gate process (InnoHVision).

For over 160 years, H&V has combined high-quality raw materials, leading technical expertise, and a broad array of wet and dry manufacturing processes to create materials and media with critical properties demanded by customers, from mechanical strength to precisely controlled pore structure.
source_title: Hollingsworth & Vose Member Spotlight

facts: Hollingsworth & Vose Company has been awarded the 2026 BCI Innovation Award by Battery Council International for its development of a unique glass fibrous matrix electrode.

The innovation, known as Glass Electrode Material (GEM), helps generate and support a unique microporous structure within the lead active mass of a battery to enhance chargeability performance and cycle life reliability without impacting water loss.

Roger Miksad, President and Executive Director of BCI, stated that battery innovation is thriving and Hollingsworth & Vose is a great example of technological leadership and product evolution that advances the capabilities of the storied lead battery.
GEM technology improves recharge efficiency through enhanced charge acceptance, reducing fuel-related emissions while improving battery performance, durability, and cycle life reliability.
With approximately three times higher Dynamic Charge Acceptance (DCA), GEM enables next-generation start-stop systems that supports fuel economy gains of up to 5%.
Its fast recharge response makes it well suited for advanced 12-V architectures in hybrid (HEV, PHEV) and electric vehicle auxiliary systems.
The technology platform is applicable across multiple energy storage applications, including automotive batteries, motive power for forklifts, and stationary backup power solutions.
Hollingsworth & Vose developed GEM in partnership with ArcActive.

Nicolas Clement, R&D Director at Hollingsworth & Vose, stated that GEM technology has true potential for creating a more sustainable future and will be a great tool for battery manufacturers for enhanced performance and to enable lead batteries to compete better with alternative chemistries.
Dr. Amogh Karwa serves as the Director of Product management at Hollingsworth & Vose.
East Penn Manufacturing and Silicon Joule received Honorable Mention recognition for their joint work on the first commercial bi-pole battery based on lead chemistry and treated silicon wafers.
Battery Council International annually presents this award to celebrate innovation in equipment, processes, services, and products that advance the lead battery industry.
The award was established to honor industry thought leader Sally Breidegam Miksiewicz, the former CEO of East Penn Manufacturing Co.
Hollingsworth & Vose is a global manufacturer of advanced materials used in filtration, battery, and industrial applications.
H&V’s advanced materials contribute to a cleaner world through their use in products that provide clean air, clean liquids, and energy storage.
The company is family-owned for seven generations and is headquartered in East Walpole, Massachusetts, U.S.A.
source_title: Hollingsworth & Vose wins 2026 BCI Innovation Award - AFV NEWS

facts: Hyundai Motor Group confirmed it will spend $5.5 billion on a huge electric vehicle plant near Savannah.
The plant will employ thousands of workers, with Georgia’s governor calling the deal the largest economic development project in the state’s history.
Hyundai Motor Group CEO Jaehoon Chang made the announcement with Gov. Brian Kemp at the site of the future factory in Bryan County.
State and local officials purchased a flat, sprawling tract for $61 million last year in hopes of luring a major manufacturer.
Gov. Brian Kemp said hard-working Georgians will have the opportunity to have a really high-paying, advanced manufacturing job with a great company.
Hyundai said it will employ at least 8,100 workers at the plant near the unincorporated town of Ellabell.
It will be Hyundai's first U.S. plant dedicated to assembling electric vehicles and will also produce vehicle batteries.
CEO Jaehoon Chang told more than 100 people that this new, high tech EV plant represents the future of their business.
Hyundai Motors plans to start construction early next year and in 2025 begin producing up to 300,000 vehicles per year.
The company did not specify which vehicle models the Georgia plant will make.
In a video shown at the announcement, Hyundai Motors Chairman Euisun Chung said it will produce “a wide range of exciting, innovative EVs for our American customers.”
The company and state officials expect suppliers to invest an additional $1 billion in the area.
Gov. Kemp predicted a ripple effect that will boost businesses from Savannah’s already booming seaport to restaurants and convenience stores.
The announcement came as President Joe Biden is visiting South Korea.
Biden was scheduled to meet with Hyundai’s CEO in Seoul on Sunday.
Jake Sullivan, the president’s national security adviser, told reporters Biden “will have the opportunity to say thank you for this significant investment that will occur in the United States.”
The timing was fortunate for Kemp, who is being challenged by former U.S. Sen. David Perdue in a Republican primary election that will be decided Tuesday.
It’s the second huge electric vehicle plant announced in Georgia in less than a year.
Rivian Automotive announced in December plans for a $5 billion electric truck plant east of Atlanta that’s expected to employ about 7,500 workers.
Kemp declined to discuss details of what incentives and tax breaks the company was offered for locating in Georgia.
Rivian received an incentive package worth $1.5 billion.
The state of Georgia and partner local governments bought 2,200 acres (890 acres) in Bryan County a year ago to lure a large manufacturer.
That initial tract wasn't large enough for Hyundai.
The state and its partners purchased two additional neighboring tracts to expand the site to more than 2,900 acres (1,170 hectares).
That's more than 4.5 square miles (11.6 square kilometers) dedicated to the Hyundai project.
Officials declined to give the sale price of the extra acreage, saying the property was under contract but the deal hadn't been finalized.
The plant site sits adjacent to Interstate 16 that links Savannah and Macon, not far from its intersection with Interstate 95 that spans the Eastern Seaboard.
It’s also near to the Port of Savannah, the fourth-busiest U.S. seaport.
Kemp predicted the Hyundai plant will become one of the biggest customers for Savannah’s port, which has already seen explosive growth in recent years.
Hyundai Motor Group sells cars under the Hyundai and Kia brands.
The South Korean automaker already operates two American assembly plants in Montgomery, Alabama, and in West Point, Georgia.
Perdue stopped Friday in Savannah to campaign with Sarah Palin, the former Alaska governor and onetime GOP vice presidential candidate.
Palin borrowed one of former President Donald Trump's slogans to praise Perdue as a Republican who “was America First before America First was cool.”
Perdue has accused Kemp of trying to buy his reelection with the Rivian deal and its big incentives.
Kemp brushed off the criticism, noting Perdue’s record of outsourcing U.S. jobs to overseas manufactures as a corporate executive.
The governor said incentives help Georgia compete for big projects with states such as South Carolina, Tennessee and Texas.
He pointed to the Kia plant in West Point as one that filled a void when the local textile industry shut down years earlier.
Kemp said, “You’ve seen downtowns that used to be ghost towns, they’re vibrant communities now.”
Kemp added, “These are investments that we’ll be reaping the benefits of 20 to 30 years down the road.”
source_title: Hyundai Announces $5.5B Electric Vehicle Plant in Georgia | Manufacturing.net

facts: The 2022 Digital FactBook is an annual report that provides an overview of Honda's global operations, as well as detailed historical and up-to-date information concerning Honda's sales, manufacturing and R&D operations in North America.
This report includes a general chronology, which highlights the significant milestones in Honda's corporate history.
Updates for the 2022 edition include updated contact details in the PR directory section.
Hybrid sales data is separated for respective Honda and Acura models.
Hybrid automobile production data is separate for respective Honda and Acura models.
The report covers a public relations directory, overview of worldwide operations, sales network and 2021 production.
It includes an overview of North America (N.A.) operations and subsidiaries.
It contains the history of Honda automobile introductions in the U.S., and Honda automobile and industry sales data for N.A.
It covers the history of Acura automobile introductions in the U.S., and Acura vehicle and industry sales data for N.A.
The report lists N.A. manufacturing facilities and information on automobile, powersports and power equipment production.
It summarizes N.A. R&D/Engineering facilities.
It includes a summary of exports of N.A.-made Honda and Acura products.
It provides overviews of operations, subsidiaries, R&D centers and manufacturing facilities in South America, Europe/Middle East/Africa, Japan, Asia/Oceania, and China.
Honda established operations in North America in 1959.

The company employs more than 40,000 associates in the region engaged in the development, manufacturing, sales and service support of Honda and Acura automobiles, Honda power equipment, Honda powersports products and the HondaJet Elite S advanced light jet.
Honda operates 18 major manufacturing plants in North America.
Honda works with nearly 800 suppliers in the region to produce a diverse range of quality products.
Honda has built automobiles in North America for 40 years.
In 2021, more than 95% of all Honda and Acura automobiles sold in the U.S. were produced in North America.
Honda conducts research and development activities at 23 facilities in North America where it fully designs, develops and engineers many of the company's North American-made products.
source_title: Honda 2022 Digital FactBook

entity_name: Hyundai TRANSYS Georgia Seating System, LLC
canonical_name: Hyundai TRANSYS Georgia Seating System, LLC
facts: Hyundai TRANSYS Georgia Seating System, LLC will invest $9 million in expanding seating manufacturing operations in West Point, Georgia.
The expansion will create more than 150 jobs in Troup County.
Governor Brian P. Kemp stated that Georgia’s highly skilled workforce continues to attract manufacturing jobs across the state.
Hyundai TRANSYS Georgia Seating System manufactures seats and seat foam pads for the Hyundai Santa Fe, KIA Sorento, and other models.
The company provides seating structures for automobile manufacturers.
The company supplies seats to leading electric vehicle makers.
Hyundai TRANSYS Georgia Seating System’s President and CEO Sangkil Jung expressed excitement about expanding their presence in the West Point community.
Sangkil Jung stated pride in the partnership with the State of Georgia.
The expansion will take Hyundai TRANSYS’ total employment in Georgia to more than 1,890 jobs.
Hyundai TRANSYS Georgia Seating System is continuing to hire for careers in production seat assembly, foam, quality, and logistics.
West Point Development Authority Chairman Kevin Patrick thanked Hyundai TRANSYS Georgia Seating System for its continued investment.
Kevin Patrick stated that automotive and existing industries are the backbone of West Point's economic success.
Senior Project Manager Amanda Fields represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce division on this competitive project.
The project was executed in partnership with the West Point Development Authority, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Hyundai TRANSYS contributes more than $3 billion to Georgia’s economy.
Pat Wilson noted the state is committed to growing Georgia’s robust automotive ecosystem and creating solid manufacturing jobs of the future.
Pat Wilson thanked partners at local, state, and federal levels for fostering a pro-business environment that gives automotive partners confidence to invest.
Georgia is home to more than 200 automotive-related facilities.
Georgia remains a national leader in advanced manufacturing, outpacing the United States in 10-year GDP growth in machinery, electrical equipment and components, and fabricated metals manufacture.
Hyundai TRANSYS is an automotive parts company that produces critical automobile parts including transmissions, axles, and seats.
Hyundai TRANSYS is the only company in the world specializing in transmissions.
Hyundai TRANSYS produces all types of automatic transmissions, manual transmissions, dual clutch transmissions (DCT), and continuously variable transmissions.
Hyundai TRANSYS develops and produces top quality seats for Genesis, Santa Fe, Sorento, and other models.
source_title: Hyundai TRANSYS Georgia Seating System, LLC Expands Manufacturing Operations in Georgia - Expansion Solutions

facts: Hyundai Motor Company confirmed Thursday it will press forward with a major expansion at its Georgia Metaplant, even after a federal raid earlier this month at the same site led to hundreds of worker arrests.
The company announced a $2.7 billion investment to add production capacity of 200,000 vehicles by 2028.
The move is expected to create 3,000 new jobs.
The expansion will bring the plant’s total output to 500,000 vehicles annually.
Production will focus on hybrid and electric models.
Hyundai unveiled the investment at its first CEO Investor Day outside Korea, held in New York City.
CEO José Muñoz described the company’s 2030 vision as a transformation into a global mobility leader through electrification, new products, and software-driven technology.
Hyundai aims to produce more than 80 percent of the vehicles it sells in the United States domestically by 2030, up from 60 percent today.
Globally, the automaker set a sales goal of 5.55 million vehicles by 2030, including 3.3 million electrified models.
The company’s roadmap includes launching region-specific EVs.
Hyundai plans to debut extended-range electric vehicles with more than 600 miles of driving range starting in 2027.
The company will introduce hybrid versions across its Genesis luxury lineup.
Hyundai also plans to expand manufacturing abroad, adding 1.2 million units of capacity in South Korea, India, Saudi Arabia, and other markets.
Financially, Hyundai raised its 2025 revenue growth forecast to 5–6 percent but trimmed its operating profit margin outlook to 6–7 percent, citing the impact of new U.S. tariffs.
A KRW 77.3 trillion investment plan from 2026 to 2030 will focus on research, new production facilities, and advanced technologies.
On Sept. 4, U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement raided the construction site of the plant’s battery facility in Ellabell, detaining about 475 workers, most of them South Korean nationals.
Hyundai acknowledged delays tied to the raid but said its expansion plan remains on track.
Hyundai officials said the Georgia expansion underscores their long-term commitment to U.S. production.
source_title: Hyundai expands Georgia plant hit by recent ICE raid | FOX 5 Atlanta

facts: Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia is suspending operations for a 2nd time through mid-April.
The West Point, Ga., plant shut down earlier this month for two days.
source_title: Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia is suspending operations for a 2nd time through mid-April - Atlanta Business Chronicle

facts: Rumoured since January, it now appears certain that Honda has chosen Ontario for the site of a new electric vehicle assembly plant, an EV battery gigafactory and other precursor production facilities.
According to multiple reports, Honda will announce in the coming days that it is building a new EV assembly plant and EV battery gigafactory in Ontario.
Editor’s Note (April 23): Honda Canada today sent invitations to media to attend an announcement at its Alliston, Ont., manufacturing plant on Thursday April 25 at 10:00 a.m.

Canada, specifically Ontario, is on the cusp of landing another major international electric vehicle investment — this one from Honda Motor Co. — according to multiple recent reports citing unnamed government sources.

The package may include all the pieces Honda will need to create a new North American hub for EV production: a new EV assembly plant, an EV battery gigafactory and facilities to produce battery precursor materials.
Honda also operates plants in two U.S. locations that will produce EVs.
Initial reports about the possibility of this deal published in January pegged its potential value at $18.4 billion.

On Monday, speaking at the annual First Nations Major Projects Coalition conference, Ontario Premier Doug Ford promised he’d be making an announcement later this week about “the largest deal in Canadian history.”
Ford didn’t provide any specifics or confirm it involved Honda.
In the past 24 hours major Canadian news organizations, citing unnamed government sources, say Honda will announce a massive, multiphase EV investment.
The first of these reports was published by Bloomberg over the weekend.
The new EV plant will be in Alliston, Ont., where Honda Canada already runs a Canadian vehicle assembly plant.
Electric Autonomy confirmed through public documents the Town of New Tecumseth is discussing the new Honda factory.
In 2022, the federal and provincial governments kicked in $131.6 million over a six-year period to help Honda retool that plant to make a new CR-V Hybrid crossover.
At that time, officials denied the deal was a precursor to additional investments to make EVs at the facility.
Several newly published accounts now indicate that deal was a door-opener to subsequent discussions leading to this week’s announcement.
Details about the battery gigafactory are less definitive.
Reports suggest it’s likely to be in close proximity to the new EV assembly plant.
Additional facilities to make cathode active materials (CAM) and other precursors for battery production will be located elsewhere.
Usually companies choose locations near to where they intend to source the critical minerals and other elements.
Any specific government investments to help secure the Honda project will likely only be public once the announcement is official.
In last week’s federal budget, the Liberal government unveiled a new EV Supply Chain Investment Tax Credit to support development of EV assembly, battery making and precursor materials factories.
That credit was immediately seen as being tied to a potential Honda investment.

In March, the Ontario government’s latest budget also contained what might have been a telltale provision: specifically, $2.8 billion over three years, beginning in 2024–25, for strategic investments in industrial support and land development, including Ontario’s auto manufacturing sector and electric vehicle battery production.
Will that funding form the package the province put together as part of Canada’s pitch to Honda?
If the latest reports are correct, we could find out as early as this Thursday.
source_title: Honda to announce Ontario EV, battery plants: reports

facts: Honda unveiled prototypes of an electric SUV and sedan at tech fair CES in Las Vegas, building upon the 0 Series line it debuted last year.

The Honda 0 Saloon and Honda 0 SUV are two of the first vehicles unveiled as part of Honda's 0 Series line, the brand's next-generation lineup of EVs that will feature a new operating system designed for "eyes-off" driving.
Both the Saloon and a version of the SUV were displayed at CES 2024 as concept cars to introduce the Honda 0 Series, which is focused on a "Thin, Light, and Wise" development strategy.

The Saloon prototype of CES 2025 reportedly received only minor changes since its 2024 concept debut, with the most major difference being its white-painted exterior versus the sleek black of the original.
The four-door "sporty" Saloon features a minimal design, with a gentle sloping hood that runs seamlessly into its tinted windshield.
Its back fender tilts slightly upwards, where it ends in an abrupt bumper.
A similar chip looks taken off the car's hood to create the front fender, which narrows and tilts downwards.
Slim headlights and tinted windows encircle the car.
"The prototype model features both a low height, sporty styling that sets the Saloon apart from other EVs at first glance," said Honda.
The SUV is marked by a similar rear as the Saloon, although the car body runs into a traditional mid-size SUV shape.
Both vehicles also feature rectangular taillights that encircle a simplified Honda logo.
Like other car brands such as Audi and Jaguar, Honda debuted the new word mark last year as part of its new EV line.
The car's interiors were updated to feel more spacious and feature a fully digitized dashboard and white seating offset with a predominantly purple lighting palette.
Both vehicles will run on the Honda-produced operating system Asimo OS, a semi-automated system named after the humanoid robot the brand created in 2000.
The software will enable autonomous and "eyes-off" driving features for the Series 0 system with the ultimate goal of making drivers "feel at one with the vehicle" according to the brand.

"Honda will rapidly expand global application of its eyes-off function through the Honda 0 Series, striving to become the world's first automaker to enable eyes-off driving in all driving situations to open up new possibilities for mobility," said Honda.
Although the vehicles were presented as prototypes at CES 2025, both vehicles are slated for 2026 production.
They will be introduced in the North American market and then in Japan and Europe.
CES 2025 took place in Las Vegas from 7 to 10 January 2025.
source_title: Honda debuts electric car prototypes at CES for future "eyes-off" driving

facts: Honda’s Jeffersonville plant has begun production of lithium batteries for electric grid and storage purposes, not for EV batteries, as stated Wednesday, July 1.
L-H Battery Co. Inc. launched lithium-ion battery cell mass production more than three years after construction began west of Ohio 729 and south of Interstate 71.
The plant spans more than 2 million square feet.
The plant has more than 1,000 employees.
L-H Battery stated it will remain flexible with its products and production as the market for battery cells continues to evolve.
The plant was originally envisioned to begin mass production to supply battery cells for electric vehicles.
The plant pivoted to support energy storage systems and future production of hybrid-electric vehicle battery cells in North America.
A spokeswoman for L-H Battery referred questions about when EV battery production will begin to Honda.

LG Energy Solution will sell its stake in the joint venture EV battery production site in Fayette County to Honda Development and Manufacturing of America for $2.85 billion, according to a December 2025 Korean regulatory filing.
The first battery cells produced at L-H Battery will be packaged into energy storage systems by LG Energy Solution Vertech.
LG Energy Solution Vertech is the U.S. energy storage division of LG Energy Solution.
The energy storage systems are delivered to customers for grid-scale, residential, commercial, industrial, and large-scale utility grid applications.
The systems capture and store electricity created by traditional energy sources like oil and gas as well as renewables like wind and solar.
L-H Battery Chief Executive Chahun Ku said these systems are an important part of L-H Battery’s future, along with production of battery cells for hybrid-electric vehicles.

L-H Battery Chief Operating Officer Rick Riggle stated that nearly four years after establishment, progress includes completing construction, establishing a new workforce, and delivering meaningful economic benefits to the Fayette County community.
Global lithium-ion battery deployment in 2025 was six times that of 2020, according to an International Energy Agency report cited by Reuters.
source_title: Honda battery plant has started full-time production | Local | springfieldnewssun.com

The industry publications are produced by Georgia Power’s award-winning research team of analysts and data scientists.

Available industry publications cover Aerospace, Automotive, Chemical Manufacturing, Digital Media & Entertainment, Electric Transportation, Metal Manufacturing, FinTech, Food Processing, Information & Communication Technology, Life Sciences, Plastics & Rubber Manufacturing, and Warehouse Distribution.

Base version expected to make around 350 horsepower, while Veloce will produce closer to 800 horsepower.

Van built in U.S. starting 2028; both brands offer versions sharing platform with unique interiors and exteriors.
Jaguar reinventing as exclusive high-end luxury EV brand with Type 01 four-door grand tourer revealed late 2026.
Tri-motor powertrain produces over 986 horsepower; targets EPA-estimated range at least 430 miles.
Ability to add 200 miles range in 15 minutes on fast-charger; costs around $130,000; sale sometime 2027.

entity_name: Georgia AI Data Center Tax Incentive Program
canonical_name: Georgia High Technology Data Center Equipment Sales and Use Tax Exemption
facts: Georgia does not have a statewide property tax exemption for data centers.
The main state incentive is a sales and use tax exemption on qualifying equipment, which expires at the end of 2031.
Local governments can offer property tax abatements using a bonds for title structure that satisfies a constitutional ban on direct grants.
These abatements reduce a project’s property tax bill but do not eliminate it.
The state level sales tax exemption preempts local sales taxes.
Several bills introduced in 2026 seek to curtail or repeal the exemption.
Georgia does not exempt AI data centers from ad valorem property taxes at the state level.
Ad valorem taxes are based on the assessed value of real property.
Georgia assesses taxable real property at 40 percent of its fair market value unless otherwise specified by law.
The state’s incentive code for AI data centers targets only sales and use tax on equipment.
Real property, including land, buildings, and permanently attached fixtures, is explicitly excluded from the exemption.
An AI data center in Georgia pays property tax on its real estate like any other commercial or industrial property.
The main incentive is the High Technology Data Center Equipment Sales and Use Tax Exemption under O.C.G.A. § 48-8-3(68.1).
It exempts qualifying equipment purchases and leases from both state and local sales tax.
The exemption began on July 1, 2018 and is scheduled to run through December 31, 2031.
To qualify, the facility must meet the rule’s definition of a high technology data center.
Qualification turns on investment and job thresholds, not building size.
The equipment that qualifies includes servers, generators, power distribution units, cooling systems, and other tangible personal property used directly in the AI data center.
The building shell, land, and permanently attached improvements are not covered.
The owner must apply for an exemption certificate from the Georgia Department of Revenue.
The revenue commissioner must find that the project is more likely than not to satisfy the required minimum investment thresholds.
Those thresholds depend on the county’s population under a tiered system adopted in 2022 for certificates issued on or after May 9, 2022.
For counties over 50,000 population, the minimum investment is $250 million and minimum new quality jobs is 25.
For counties with 30,001 to 50,000 population, the minimum investment is $75 million and minimum new quality jobs is 10.
For counties under 30,001 population, the minimum investment is $25 million and minimum new quality jobs is 5.
The investment must occur within a seven year period that the owner selects, starting on or after July 1, 2018 and ending no later than December 31, 2031.
The commissioner may also require a bond of up to $20 million, forfeitable if the owner fails to meet the minimum investment.
A New Quality Job is a position that requires at least 30 hours of work per week in the county where the data center sits.

The employee must regularly work 30 hours or more per week in the county on matters directly related to the data center, and the wages must equal at least 110 percent of the average wage for all industries in that county.

An AI data center that receives the sales tax exemption cannot also claim any other tax credits under Georgia’s jobs, quality jobs, mega project, or investment tax credit programs for the same project.
Developers must choose between the sales tax break and the other incentives.
Colocation customers can also benefit if they sign a contract of at least 36 months with a certified AI data center owner.
As of late 2025, 34 AI data centers had used or applied for the exemption.
The exemption has been a major driver of Georgia’s AI data center boom.
Local governments negotiate abatements through special development authority structures because the Georgia Constitution forbids direct grants.

In a bonds for title deal, the county’s development authority issues industrial development bonds to take legal title to the property and leases it back to the company at a rental payment equal to the debt service on the bonds.
Because the authority is a governmental entity that holds title, the property becomes exempt from ad valorem property taxes during the lease.
The company pays rent, often called a PILOT (payment in lieu of taxes), in place of property taxes.
After the agreed abatement period, title transfers back to the company, and full property taxes resume.
In Fulton County, the Board of Assessors approved appraisals for the Edged Energy AI data center at the former Tilford Yard in west Atlanta.
The deal is estimated to save the developer about $23 million in property taxes over a 10 year period.
The approval came in April 2024 after a months long freeze on AI data center abatement valuations.

In Clayton County, the Development Authority approved an incentive package in June 2025 for a $959 million, 180 megawatt AI data center being built by a TA Realty subsidiary along East Tanners Church Road.
In Butts County, the Development Authority offers bonds for title incentives, and AWS is building a large campus there.
The county’s combined property tax rate is 24.134 mills, a factor that determines the property tax bill both before and after abatement.
The Savannah Economic Development Authority (SEDA) can also grant real and personal property tax abatements for qualifying projects.
Each deal is negotiated individually with no uniform formula.
Local abatements reduce property taxes but do not zero them out.
The University of Georgia’s Carl Vinson Institute of Government studied four metro Atlanta AI data center projects for the state’s 2025 tax incentive evaluation.
For a representative three building campus valued at more than $2 billion, the annual property tax bill at average millage rates would be about $33.6 million.
After the typical bonds for title abatement, about $5.9 million would be abated.
That leaves roughly $27.8 million in taxes still due each year.
The abatement typically shaves off a portion, often under 20 percent, of the full liability.
The sales tax exemption on AI data center equipment applies to both the state’s 4 percent sales tax and any local option sales taxes.
The Georgia Department of Audits and Accounts found in 2025 that the exemption has a negative net fiscal effect.
The state recoups roughly 24 cents on each dollar of forgone revenue from the exempted projects.
The but for rate is only 30 percent, meaning 70 percent of the AI data center construction would have occurred anyway without the tax break.
The net fiscal loss is projected to reach $780.2 million by 2030.
Local governments feel this impact indirectly through lost sales tax collections.
In 2018, HB 696 created the sales tax exemption, with an original sunset of December 31, 2028.
In 2022, HB 1291 extended the sunset to December 31, 2031 and introduced the tiered investment and job thresholds.
In 2024, the General Assembly passed HB 1192, which would have halted the issuance of new exemption certificates from July 1, 2024 to June 30, 2026.
Governor Brian Kemp vetoed it on May 7, 2024, stating that it would undermine investments made in reliance on the 2022 extension.
In 2025, the state’s updated tax incentive evaluation found a negative net fiscal impact and a 30 percent but for rate.
Multiple bills were introduced in 2026 to curtail or eliminate the exemption.
SB 408 seeks to eliminate the exemption effective January 1, 2027.
SB 410 seeks to end the exemption for projects going forward and passed the Senate as of early 2026.
SB 436 seeks to suspend new certificates from July 1, 2026 to June 30, 2027.
HB 559 seeks to sunset the exemption on December 31, 2026.
HB 1012 seeks to pause new AI data center construction until March 1, 2027.
If any of these bills become law, the sales tax exemption could end abruptly before projects now in design or construction can capture the benefit.
Governor Kemp’s 2024 veto signals that full repeal might face resistance, but the 2026 bills represent a broader, bipartisan push.
Developers should monitor the session closely and plan for the possibility that the 2031 sunset could be pulled forward.
source_title: Georgia property tax and local tax breaks for AI data centers · Compute Law Blog

entity_name: Honda Precision Parts of Georgia LLC (HPPG)
canonical_name: Honda Precision Parts of Georgia LLC
facts: Associates at Honda Precision Parts of Georgia LLC reached a production milestone on October 4 at 2:30 p.m. with the completion of the 1 millionth Honda transmission built in Georgia.
Mass production at the facility began on May 12, 2006.
The Tallapoosa, Ga. facility cost $150 million.
Masaaki Miyaji, HPPG president, called the milestone a "happy and proud day for all HPPG associates, our Tallapoosa community, for Georgia and for Honda."
Miyaji stated that HPPG has established a strong culture of stable operations, timely delivery, and production of high quality products for customers.
Miyaji added that the foundation built at HPPG will be key to future success and exceeding customer expectations.
HPPG currently employs 400 associates.
The plant has a capacity to produce more than 300,000 automatic transmissions per year.
Honda's strategy bases powertrain production near vehicle production for synchronous assembly.

The Tallapoosa plant supports production of the Honda Odyssey minivan, Pilot sport utility vehicle, Ridgeline pickup truck, and Accord V6 sedan at Honda Manufacturing of Alabama LLC (HMA) in Lincoln, Ala.
HMA is located 60 miles west of the Tallapoosa plant.
HMA is the sole North American production source for the Pilot sport utility vehicle, Odyssey minivan, Ridgeline pickup, and the V6 engines that power each vehicle.
HMA shares production of the Accord V6 Sedan with the Marysville Auto Plant in Marysville, Ohio.
HMA has a capacity to produce 300,000 vehicles and V6 engines per year.
Honda has manufactured more than 11 million transmissions in America since starting production of automatic transmissions in Ohio in 1989.
source_title: Honda transmission plant in Georgia celebrates production milestone

facts: Two reports on claimed Apple Car production plans have emerged today.
One report suggests progress on a rumored $3.6B Hyundai deal to make up to 100,000 cars per year at a Kia plant in Georgia.
Other manufacturers remain potential partners for Apple's EV project.
Apple is reportedly in talks with at least six carmakers.
Japanese manufacturers saw their share prices rise following reports of discussions with Apple.
The WSJ reported that Hyundai is ramping up preparation for Apple Car production.
Hyundai has discussed investing more than $3 billion with Apple to have its subsidiary Kia build cars under Apple's brand as soon as 2024.
Under the potential agreement, up to 100,000 vehicles could be assembled in the first year at Kia's Georgia factory.
Hyundai-Kia officials have been reaching out to potential partners in preparation for a deal.
A person briefed on the matter in Georgia stated, "We are hearing encouraging things."
Bloomberg reported increased speculation about Apple talking with Japanese car companies.
Speculation that Apple is seeking an EV partner swept through South Korea and Japan, causing shares of major regional car companies to climb.
The Nikkei newspaper reported that Apple was in discussions with at least six automakers.
Tatsuo Yoshida, a senior analyst at Bloomberg Intelligence, noted Japanese automakers are typically too busy with internal development and sales to partner with Apple.
Yoshida suggested Nissan Motor Co. or Mitsubishi Motors Corp. might sign up because they "don’t have much work, and are somewhat idle."
When approached for comment, Mitsubishi and Subaru appeared to rule themselves out, while other companies offered no comment.
Ming-Chi Kuo suggested Apple would use Hyundai’s E-GMP chassis, which supports an 80% charge in 18 minutes and a 60-mile range boost from a five-minute charge.
Bloomberg suggests Nissan has a similar chassis that may be suitable for Apple Car production.
Among Japanese carmakers, Nissan likely has the right solution for a non-automaker entering the EV market due to its common EV platform developed with French partner Renault SA.
A CNBC report claimed the first version of the Apple car will "not be designed to have a driver."
The article notes that targeting a 2024 launch for a fully autonomous car would be extremely ambitious technically and legislatively, casting doubt on the claim's credibility.
source_title: Apple Car production: Reports of talks with Japanese companies - 9to5Mac

facts: Hyundai Motor Group announced a multi-year partnership with the Georgia Institute of Technology to advance hydrogen-powered transportation, applied research, education, and zero-emissions vehicles in Georgia.
Hyundai Motor North America is donating four NEXO fuel cell electric SUVs exclusively for campus use at Georgia Tech.
Hyundai Motor Group is donating a hydrogen electrolyzer project that will be installed at Georgia Tech's North Avenue Research Area (NARA).
The donated NEXO SUVs and hydrogen electrolyzer will support campus operations and interdisciplinary research across engineering, sustainability, energy systems, and public policy.

Ken Ramírez, Executive Vice President and Head of Global Energy and Hydrogen Business at Hyundai Motor Group, stated the collaboration combines Georgia Tech's research with Hyundai's global experience to drive the energy transition and inspire future mobility leaders.
Ken Ramírez is a Georgia Tech alumnus (class of 1991) and currently serves on the Georgia Tech Advisory Board.

Randy Parker, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor North America, stated the donation of NEXO fuel cell SUVs will expand the clean mobility footprint on campus and provide real-world experience with zero-emissions transportation technology.

Ángel Cabrera, President of the Georgia Institute of Technology, stated the expanded partnership advances hydrogen research, reduces campus emissions, and strengthens Georgia's role in the future of clean mobility.
The installation of the hydrogen electrolyzer at NARA positions Georgia Tech as one of the most visible real-world testbeds for hydrogen mobility in the U.S.
Research focus includes exploring hydrogen-based systems and mobility solutions within Engineering.
Research focus includes assessing environmental benefits of hydrogen technologies within Sustainability.
Research focus includes understanding integration of hydrogen fuel cells into current infrastructure within Energy systems.
Research focus includes evaluating regulatory and social implications of hydrogen adoption within Public policy.

The initiative uses Georgia Tech as a living laboratory for clean transportation, with faculty and students studying real-world hydrogen technology performance, infrastructure requirements for large-scale deployment, and environmental impacts of hydrogen energy systems.
Insights from the initiative aim to inform and accelerate widespread hydrogen technology use in campuses, fleets, cities, and freight corridors.
The partnership supports Georgia Tech's strategic plan goal of expanding zero-emissions vehicles powered by sustainable energy sources.
Beyond hydrogen energy, the collaboration aims to advance innovation in autonomous driving, electric vehicle batteries, charging infrastructure, materials science, and cybersecurity.

Hyundai Motor Group's regional presence in Georgia includes the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) and HTWO Logistics, a clean logistics partnership operating Hyundai XCIENT fuel cell heavy-duty trucks near Savannah.
The collaboration reinforces the link between education, research, and Hyundai Motor Group's long-term goal of achieving carbon neutrality by 2045.
The partnership aims to generate workforce talent and position Georgia as a national leader in hydrogen and zero-emissions transportation.
Additional announcements regarding research projects, educational programs, and vehicle deployment are expected in the coming months.
A new initiative brings NEXO hydrogen fuel cell SUVs and fueling infrastructure to campus to advance research, workforce development, and zero-emissions logistics.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group and Georgia Tech Sign MOU to Further Collaborate on Hydrogen Mobility Developmen

facts: Hyundai Motor Group opened its new $7.59 billion electric and hybrid vehicle manufacturing plant, Metaplant America, in Georgia.
The facility is Hyundai’s first state-of-the-art smart factory in the US and is described as North America’s largest and most advanced smart factory.
HMG Metaplant America (HMGMA) sits on 2,900 acres (11.7 square kilometres) of land in Ellabell, Bryan County.
Hyundai signed a $12.6 billion investment deal with the State of Georgia in May 2022.
Construction work at the site began in October 2022.
The first vehicle, a Hyundai IONIQ 5, rolled off the production line in late 2024.
HMGMA officially opened in early April 2025.
The facility currently has a headcount of 1,200 employees.
Current production capacity is 300,000 vehicles per annum.
HMGMA will produce both electric and hybrid vehicles for the US market.
The IONIQ 9 electric SUV is now in production at the facility.
Production of Kia models is scheduled to begin in 2026.
Production of Genesis models will follow thereafter.
Hyundai claims to have invested a total of $20.5 billion in US manufacturing since entering the country, creating 570,000 jobs.
Hyundai has committed to invest an additional $21 billion following the opening of HMGMA.
The plant aims to expand capacity by about 60 percent, from 300,000 to 500,000 vehicles per year.
The facility will create 8,500 full-time jobs at the site by 2031.
Hyundai targets producing 1.2 million electric and hybrid vehicles annually in the US by 2028 with its new $21 billion investment.
The facility incorporates cutting-edge automation, including autonomous robots, AI vision systems, and automated processes for all parts of vehicle assembly.
Automation rates in HMGMA’s assembly shop are nearly double that of Hyundai’s other facilities.
Private 5G elements in the factory were supplied by Georgia-based Industry 4.0 system integrator Future Technologies.
Future Technologies used Nokia’s private cellular core and radio (RAN) network systems for the 5G deployment.

Peter Cappiello, chief executive at Future Technologies, noted the company has been headquartered in Georgia for over 25 years and served as the lead system integrator for the facility’s private 5G solution.
The factory utilizes autonomous mobile robots (AMRs), automated guided vehicles (AGVs), and unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs/drones).
AI-based management and control systems oversee sensors, robots, machines, and processes, with human oversight and intervention in specialist functions.
Discriminative AI is used for camera vision and software analysis, though generative or agentic AI are not mentioned.
The facility features an AI automation and control system for vehicle parts management.
An AI drone system is used for inventory management planning and storage.
A ‘One-Kit’ AI vision system verifies component assemblies.
A Digital Command Centre (DCC) digitizes and integrates production data.
AI vision systems precisely and automatically align metal sheets.
A smart press controls power and speed for optimal metal forming.
An AI vision system detects holes or cracks in panels, marking a world first.
AI vision guides the precise positioning of the automatic loading system.
Unmanned transport vehicles carry pallets.
Automated warehouses efficiently store and manage pallets.
A ‘Robot Main Buck’ system integrates robots and vision technology.
An autonomous adjustment system adjusts vehicle door gaps, marking a world first.
A ‘Metal finish line flow-type AGV’ serves as a collaborative workspace, marking a world first.
An automated overhead robot grabs and moves heavy body-in-white parts.
An automated electrocoating scarf sanding system sands vehicle part defects.
An automated sealer system fills vehicle gaps for waterproofing and soundproofing.
An automated final inspection system inspects paint jobs and performs markings.
An automated ‘trim-IN’ robot lifts and lowers vehicles.
An automated body/chassis marriage system fits the vehicle body and chassis.
An automated door installation system assembles doors, marking a world first.
An automated headlamp adjustment system optimizes lamp brightness.
Hyundai’s $12.6 billion Georgia investment also covers a joint battery venture at HMGMA with LG Energy Solution and SK On.
The company states the $12.6 billion deal is the largest investment in Georgia’s history.
Hyundai’s other US sites in Alabama (Hyundai) and Georgia (Kia Autoland) will be made smarter.
A new $5.8 billion electric arc furnace steel mill in Louisiana will supply automotive steel plates to HMGMA when commercial production begins in 2029.

Euisun Chung, executive chair at Hyundai, stated that HMGMA represents the group’s advanced manufacturing capabilities and commitment to innovation, and highlighted building the future of mobility with America in America.
source_title: Hyundai opens the smartest factory in America – pumped full of AI and 5G

facts: Hyundai Motor Group and Georgia Tech announced an expansion of their collaboration to advance hydrogen-powered transportation, deepen applied research and education, and accelerate the use of zero-emissions vehicles in Georgia.
The partnership brings hydrogen fuel cell vehicles and fueling infrastructure to the Georgia Tech campus.
This initiative turns Georgia Tech into one of the nation’s most prominent campus-based examples for hydrogen mobility.

Ken Ramírez, executive vice president and head of Global Energy and Hydrogen Business at Hyundai Motor Group, stated that combining the university’s research with Hyundai’s global experience creates a foundation for real-world solutions to drive the energy transition and inspire future mobility leaders.
Ken Ramírez is a 1991 Georgia Tech graduate and a member of the Georgia Tech Advisory Board.
Randy Parker, president and CEO of Hyundai Motor North America, donated four NEXO fuel cell SUVs as part of the expanding relationship with Georgia Tech.
The hydrogen-powered NEXO vehicles will expand the clean mobility footprint on campus while providing real-world experiences with zero-emissions transportation technology.
Georgia Tech President Ángel Cabrera stated that expanding work with Hyundai advances hydrogen research, reduces emissions on campus, and strengthens Georgia’s role in the future of clean mobility.
The partnership includes a hydrogen electrolyzer project to be installed at Georgia Tech’s North Avenue Research Area.
The installed vehicles and infrastructure will serve as one of the most visible real-world test beds for hydrogen mobility in the U.S. and support campus operations and interdisciplinary research.

Key research focus areas include engineering (exploring hydrogen-based systems and mobility solutions), sustainability (assessing environmental benefits of hydrogen technologies), energy systems (understanding integration of hydrogen fuel cells into current infrastructure), and public policy (evaluating regulatory and social implications of hydrogen adoption).
The initiative uses Georgia Tech as a living laboratory for clean transportation technologies, connecting its research enterprise with campus operations.
Faculty and students will study the real-world performance of hydrogen technology, infrastructure requirements for large-scale deployment, and environmental impacts of hydrogen energy systems.
Insights from this initiative aim to inform and accelerate the widespread use of hydrogen technology in campuses, fleets, cities, and freight corridors.
The initiative supports Georgia Tech’s strategic plan goal of expanding the use of zero-emissions vehicles powered by sustainable energy sources.
Beyond hydrogen energy, the partnership seeks to advance innovation in autonomous driving, electric vehicle (EV) batteries, charging infrastructure, materials science, and cybersecurity.

Hyundai’s regional presence in Georgia includes the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America and HTWO Logistics, a clean logistics partnership operating Hyundai XCIENT fuel cell heavy-duty trucks near Savannah.
The collaboration reinforces the link between education, research, and Hyundai’s long-term goal of achieving carbon neutrality by 2045.
Additional announcements regarding the partnership’s research projects, educational programs, and vehicle deployment are expected in the coming months.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group, Georgia Tech Sign MOU on Hydrogen Mobility Development

facts: Georgia and local governments are on track to give $2.1 billion in tax breaks and other incentives to Hyundai Motor Group.
The South Korean automaker and a partner announced last month that it will invest an additional $2 billion at an electric vehicle complex it’s building in Georgia.
Projected incentives will rise by more than $290 million from the $1.8 billion deal originally announced last year.
Only $2.75 million of that represents additional cash from the state.
The rest of the incentive increase will come from increases in tax breaks.
The deal calls for Hyundai and battery maker LG Energy Solution to invest $7.6 billion in the Georgia plant.
The deal requires hiring 8,500 workers by the end of 2031 at the sprawling electric vehicle and battery complex being built in Ellabell, west of Savannah.
That job projection is up from the original projection of 8,100 jobs.
It is the largest economic development deal in Georgia history.
The deal comes with the largest incentive package in Georgia history.
Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson said Hyundai is projected to have a direct payroll of $4.7 billion over the next 10 years.
The company has promised to pay workers a yearly average of $58,105, plus benefits.
State leaders say benefits to Georgia outweigh the incentives.
Pat Wilson stated Hyundai will give back to the region by investing in schools, families, and communities.
Local officials released property tax projections to the AP on Tuesday.
The Hyundai package has been described as the largest subsidy package a U.S. state has ever promised a single automotive plant, according to Good Jobs First.
Good Jobs First is a group skeptical of subsidies to private companies.
Local governments have agreed to abate property taxes on the assembly plant through 2048.
Local governments have agreed to abate property taxes on the battery plant through 2049.
During the tax abatement period, Hyundai is projected to pay $523 million in taxes while saving $669 million.
The state projects it will waive an additional $81.8 million in sales taxes on construction materials and machinery.
These exemptions bring Hyundai’s savings from those exemptions to more than $478 million.
The company is projected to receive an additional $10.5 million in state income tax credits.
The income tax credits are calculated at $5,250 per job over five years due to the increase in jobs.
This brings Hyundai’s state income tax savings to $223 million.
If Hyundai didn’t owe that much state corporate income tax, Georgia would instead give the company personal income taxes collected from Hyundai workers.
The state will spend an additional $2.75 million to help fund construction, machinery and equipment.
This boosts the total funding for construction, machinery and equipment to nearly $53 million.
State and local governments will spend more than $112 million to buy and prepare 2,913 acres (1,179 hectares) for the plant.
The state will spend $175 million on water and sewer facilities.
The state will spend $210 million on road construction and improvements.
The state will spend more than $153 million to recruit and train workers.
The deal requires Hyundai to pay back a portion of the incentives if the company falls below 80% of promised investment or employment.
Hyundai plans to start EV production in 2025.
Initially, the plant will make 300,000 vehicles per year.
Production may possibly expand to 500,000 vehicles a year.
Since the company announced its first U.S. plant solely dedicated to electric vehicles, suppliers have pledged to invest $2.2 billion and hire 5,300 people.
The announcements are part of an electric vehicle and battery land rush across the United States.
Under the U.S. Inflation Reduction Act, EVs must be assembled in North America to qualify for a full $7,500 EV tax credit.
A certain percentage of battery parts and minerals must come from North America or a U.S. free trade partner to qualify for the full $7,500 EV tax credit.
source_title: Higher investment means Hyundai could get $2.1 billion in aid to make electric cars in Georgia | AP News

facts: Hyundai has not canceled plans to complete its electric vehicle battery plant in Georgia.
Hyundai has recommitted to the EV battery plant project and broadened its agreement significantly.
Hyundai announced a sweeping production expansion and an overall investment of $26 million over three years at its CEO Investor Day.
CEO José Muñoz outlined Hyundai Motor’s global growth goals while speaking in New York.
Hyundai announced a $5 billion battery joint venture in Cartersville, Georgia.
Hyundai announced expansions to the Kia Corp. plant in West Point, Georgia.
Hyundai announced plans for a steel mill in Donaldsonville, Louisiana.
Hyundai announced plans for a robotics hub and an expansion of its plant in Montgomery, Alabama.
Hyundai announced a reduction to its 2025 budget, citing a current tariff rate of 25%.
Hyundai's new operating profit margin will be approximately 7%, with a goal to return to approximately 9% by 2030.
The 25% tariff is directly affecting Hyundai’s profitability by raising costs on non-domestically made cars and components, as only about 40% of its U.S. sales are currently made domestically.
Hyundai announced a plan to increase U.S. car production and plans for rapid growth at its Ellabell Georgia “Metaplant.”
Hyundai targets producing 500,000 vehicles a year at the Georgia facility, including hybrids and EVs.
Hyundai plans to introduce a midsize truck for the first time ever.
Hyundai plans to offer 18 hybrid models globally.
Hyundai plans to launch extended-range EVs in 2027.
Hyundai will introduce several U.S.-specific vehicle offerings.
Hyundai plans to have a 10-model lineup at its Georgia facility.
In 2025, Hyundai raised its revenue growth target and adjusted its operating profit.
Hyundai announced an investment of KRW 77.3 trillion ($55.66 billion in USD) by 2030.
Hyundai plans to roll out a Software-Defined Vehicle (SDV) platform and a High-Performance Vehicle Computer.
In 2026, the Genesis brand will introduce hybrid models.
An all-new Palisade Hybrid will launch in 2026.
A cloud-based battery management system will launch in 2026.
Hyundai plans to build a plant in Saudi Arabia in 2026.
In 2027, Hyundai will debut its first Extended Range electric vehicle (EREV).
In 2028, Hyundai will expand at Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
The 2028 expansion adds 200,000 units of annual capacity.
The 2028 expansion will create 3,000 new jobs.
By 2028, Hyundai will reach production of 500,000 hybrid and EV vehicles per year.
By 2030, Hyundai aims to reach a target of 55.5 million vehicles sold globally.
By 2030, Hyundai's hybrid choices will expand to 18 or more models.
In 2030, Hyundai will announce its first midsize pickup truck.
Following an ICE raid at the Georgia plant, President Trump stated on Truth Social that foreign workers are “welcome” and that the U.S. should take its cues from other high-tech industries.
source_title: Hyundai doubles down on Georgia plant expansion plans | Columbus Ledger-Enquirer

facts: Hyundai Motor missed out on United States tax incentives for its new $7.6-billion manufacturing plant, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, in Bryan County, Georgia.
The missed credit did not affect Hyundai Motor's investment in the plant.

Industry sources reported Monday that Hyundai Motor was not included in the list of companies receiving tax incentives in the second round allocations of the Qualifying Advanced Energy Project Credit (48C).
The 48C program is an allocated tax credit funded by President Joe Biden’s Investing in America agenda through the Inflation Reduction Act.
The Biden administration has assigned a total of $10 billion to the 48C program.
Selected companies under the 48C program could receive up to a 30 percent tax credit for investments on American soil.
A local report citing unnamed industry sources estimated Hyundai Motor could have received up to 480 billion won ($344 million) in tax incentives.
Construction at the Georgia plant has proceeded smoothly despite missing the tax incentives.
Hyundai Motor began early-stage production of electric vehicles at HMGMA in October.
The new plant is expected to start full operation in the first quarter of next year.
A Hyundai Motor official stated that while the company applied for the 48C program, it did not expect benefits because those tax incentives were made for parts makers, not automakers.
The same official noted that the 48C program was not in consideration when Hyundai Motor invested in HMGMA.
The US Department of Energy announced a list of 35 projects across 20 states selected for $1.93 billion in the first round allocations of the 48C program in April this year.
The 48C projects were categorized into four areas: clean energy and clean vehicle manufacturing, critical minerals and materials, grid components and modernization, and industrial decarbonization.
Mobis North America electrified Powertrain, a US subsidiary of Hyundai Motor Group’s auto parts maker Hyundai Mobis, was awarded $57.6 million in tax credits.
Mobis North America electrified Powertrain is set to construct three different EV component plants that will provide parts for the production of Hyundai and Kia electric vehicles.
source_title: Hyundai Motor plays down missed IRA incentives for Georgia plant - THE INVESTOR

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Joint Venture
canonical_name: Hyundai-LG Battery Cell Manufacturing Joint Venture
facts: Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp announced Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution will invest an additional $2 billion in their battery cell manufacturing joint venture.
The additional investment brings the total project investment to more than $4.3 billion.
The expansion will add another 400 jobs, bringing the total regional headcount to 8,500.

José Muñoz, president and global COO of Hyundai Motor Company and president and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America, stated that vehicles produced at the mega plant will be 100% electric vehicles.
Muñoz explained the need for onsite battery production to support volume requirements and provide next-generation batteries with more range, lower weight, and higher technology.
In May, Hyundai and LG Energy Solution announced a partnership for the $4.3 billion plant designed to produce 300,000 batteries per year.
The facility is expected to open in late 2025, shortly after Hyundai's EV assembly factory comes online.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp stated that the project broke ground on the largest project in state history and welcomed LG Energy Solution to Bryan County.
Kemp emphasized efforts to make Georgia the e-mobility capital of the nation, noting investments will support paychecks, improved schools, and infrastructure.
Battery materials produced at the new facility will be assembled into battery packs by Hyundai Mobis, which is constructing its own facility in Richmond Hill.
Hyundai Mobis will distribute the finished battery packs to Hyundai Motor Group manufacturing facilities throughout the U.S. for Genesis EV models, Hyundai, and Kia vehicles.

Carter Infinger, chairman of the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority, stated the announcement will bring well-paying jobs to the Savannah region and described the continued investment as transformative.
Since 2018, Georgia's EV market job creation has driven more than $25 billion in investments and created more than 30,000 jobs statewide.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group announced plant expansion plans

facts: Hyundai Motor Group (HMG) celebrated the opening of its new manufacturing facility outside Savannah, Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) is described as the company's factory of the future for regional production of new vehicles, including the forthcoming Hyundai Ioniq 9 SUV.
HMGMA is part of a $7.59 billion investment made in Bryan County, Georgia, by HMG.
The facility includes a joint venture 30 gigawatt-hour battery plant with LG Energy Solution (LGES).
It joins the company's SK On battery plant in Commerce, Georgia, as a producer of batteries for HMG's electric vehicles.
The new site brings Hyundai Motor Group's total investment in Georgia to $12.6 billion.
The new site is the largest economic development project in Georgia's history.

Governor Brian Kemp attended the opening ceremony alongside Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Chung, U.S. Representative Buddy Carter, Hyundai Motor Group Vice Chair Jaehoon Chang, Hyundai Motor Company President and CEO José Muñoz, and Kia Corporation President and CEO Ho Sung Song.
HMG is the parent company of the Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis vehicle brands, Hyundai Mobis, Hyundai Transys, Hyundai Glovis, and Hyundai Steel.
It is Hyundai's second plant in Georgia; the other is Kia Georgia in West Point.
Kia Georgia in West Point makes the Telluride, Sorento, Sportage, EV9, and EV6 SUVs.

It is Hyundai's third plant in America's Southeast; Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama in Montgomery makes the Hyundai Tucson, Santa Fe, Santa Fe Hybrid SUVs, Hyundai Santa Cruz truck, and Genesis GV70 SUVs.
HMGMA features 16 million square feet of manufacturing space.
The facility will have the ability to manufacture 500,000 units annually once fully operational.
Construction of the facility began in January 2023.
The site is strategically located off Interstate 16, close to Interstate 95 and the Port of Savannah, and linked to Class 1 rail.
HMGMA features proof-of-concept manufacturing processes and technologies from Hyundai Motor Group Innovation Center Singapore, scaled to accommodate larger volume production.
The facility's processes (order collection, procurement, logistics, and production) are optimized using artificial intelligence and real-time data gathering.
Robots assist humans in the factory floor work, including Boston Dynamics' Spot robot which completes exterior inspection tasks.
Hyundai acquired Boston Dynamics at a cost of $1.1 billion in 2021.
The factory floor features open layouts with daylight streaming in via skylights and indoor landscaping to encourage communication and collaboration.
Vehicles travel by conveyor belt from the paint shop to the general assembly area, visible to travelers on the interstate.
HMGMA's parking lot generates solar energy via panels above its 1,878 spaces, accounting for 5 percent of the plant's total power consumption.
The site features a 41-acre park with trails, running tracks, sports fields, and picnic areas for workers called Meta Pros.
HMG will utilize 21 XCIENT Fuel Cell trucks, which run on hydrogen fuel and emit only water vapor, at the site for logistics operations.
This truck deployment marks the first step in reducing emissions from Hyundai's internal logistics operations.
The Ioniq 5 was the first vehicle produced at the factory, rolling off the assembly line on October 3, 2024.
HMG predicts its investment in Georgia will result in 8,500 new jobs at HMGMA by 2031.
These jobs are predicted to represent $4.6 billion in individual earnings annually, according to the Center for Automotive Research.
Through the end of 2024, 6,900 jobs and capital investments totaling $2.5 billion have been announced by HMGMA suppliers in 12 of Georgia's 159 counties.
The supplier job and investment numbers are more than double what HMG originally projected.
The factory opening follows a Monday announcement that Hyundai will invest $21 billion in the U.S. from 2025 to 2028.
This $21 billion amount is greater than the $20.5 billion Hyundai has invested in the U.S. to date since entering the market in 1986.
Of the $21 billion, $9 billion will be spent to increase production capacity to 1.2 million vehicles annually across its three brands.
Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama and Kia Georgia will receive improved production facilities as part of this $9 billion allocation.
The company's logistics operation will receive a $6 billion infusion to localize production of automotive components, including battery packs.
Hyundai Steel will build a mill in Louisiana capable of producing millions of tons of steel annually using steel scrap.
