Key alliances with tech leaders including Boston Dynamics (robotics), NVIDIA (artificial intelligence solutions), Waymo (robotaxis), and Supernal (eVTOL development) will cost $6 billion.
The final $6 billion of the $21 billion will go toward energy infrastructure investments linked to new business opportunities.
Hyundai projects that the $21 billion investment will create more than 100,000 direct and indirect job opportunities.
Stephanie Brinley, associate director of AutoIntelligence at S&P Global, stated that automakers and suppliers are expected to make investment announcements in this environment.
Brinley noted that the HMG announcements align with previously announced projects and expected direction, except for U.S. steel production.
She added that as Hyundai increases U.S. vehicle production, having access to its own regional steel gives it an advantage even without tariffs.

Brinley explained that increased production capacity allows for more flexible sourcing and responsiveness to U.S. demand changes, particularly for electrified vehicles enabled by previous U.S. battery manufacturing investments.
She concluded that these investments support building where volume increases is less costly and align with Hyundai's targeted growth for the U.S. market.
An update on 3/26/2025 at 2:25 p.m. ET noted that the total number of units the facility will manufacture annually has been updated.
source_title: Hyundai’s $7.6B Factory of the Future Opens in Georgia - Newsweek

facts: Hyundai Motor Group confirmed it is moving forward with plans to expand its Georgia auto plant just weeks after an immigration raid delayed the startup of an electric vehicle battery facility on site.
The Ellabell plant, located west of Savannah, will receive a $2.7 billion investment.
The investment will increase annual production by 200,000 vehicles over the next three years.
Total production capacity at the facility will reach 500,000 vehicles per year.
Hyundai plans to produce 10 models of electric and hybrid gas-electric vehicles in Georgia, increasing from the current two models.
The expansion supports Hyundai's broader goal to manufacture 5.6 million vehicles worldwide annually by 2030.
The facility will begin production of a mid-sized pickup truck for the first time.
The plant will also produce models like the Santa Cruz compact pickup, which Hyundai began selling in 2021.
Hyundai states that more than 80% of vehicles sold in the U.S. will be produced domestically by 2030.
This shift will increase domestic vehicle content from 60% to 80%.
An immigration raid resulted in the arrest of more than 300 South Korean nationals.
The raid delayed the opening of the on-site battery plant by at least two to three months.
The battery plant startup timeline has been pushed to the first half of 2026.
Hyundai CEO José Muñoz and company spokesperson Michael Stewart emphasized that the broader Georgia expansion remains on track despite the delay.

Georgia officials, including Governor Brian Kemp, have actively supported the company by emphasizing the state's business advantages and promising visa issues will be resolved to facilitate operations.
State and local governments have pledged $2.1 billion in tax breaks and incentives for the project.
Hyundai plans to increase its Ellabell workforce from 3,129 employees to at least 8,500 by 2031.
The Georgia expansion is the largest component of Hyundai's global growth strategy.
Hyundai's global growth strategy also includes production increases in India, South Korea, Saudi Arabia, Vietnam, and North Africa.
By 2027, Hyundai plans to introduce extended-range electric vehicles capable of traveling more than 600 miles on a single battery-gasoline hybrid system.
source_title: Hyundai moving forward with $2.7 billion Georgia plant expansion after ICE raid - CBS Atlanta

facts: Governor Brian Kemp and First Lady Marty Kemp joined President and Global COO of Hyundai Motor Company and President and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America José Muñoz to celebrate Hyundai Motor Group’s historic impact on Georgia and provide an update on Metaplant America.
The facility will officially start vehicle production in the fourth quarter of this year.
Governor Brian Kemp stated Hyundai has been an incredible partner to the state for decades and highlighted benefits brought by the project.
Governor Kemp noted Hyundai helps recruit businesses, gives back to communities, and works with world-class colleges and universities to connect Georgians with opportunities.

Hyundai and Genesis electric vehicles on display included the IONIQ 5, IONIQ 6, GV60, Electrified GV70, and the hydrogen-powered XCIENT Fuel Cell Truck used for logistics between Hyundai’s manufacturing facilities and suppliers.
Governor Kemp officially declared February 26 as Hyundai Day throughout the state.
The Georgia General Assembly passed a Resolution commending Hyundai Motor Group’s dedication to Georgia and recognizing February 26 as Hyundai Day at the State Capitol.
José Muñoz stated Hyundai’s investments in Georgia will create tens of thousands of great-paying American jobs and bring tens of billions of dollars in economic impact for decades to come.
Since the HMGMA project was announced in May 2022, LG Energy Solution was named as the joint venture partner in the manufacture of battery components.
An additional expansion of the LG Energy Solution battery facility was announced.
Hyundai and SK On are building an EV battery manufacturing facility in Bartow County that will supply Hyundai Motor Group’s U.S. plants.
Since the initial announcement, Hyundai Motor Group’s planned investment in Georgia has reached more than $12.6 billion.
The project brings with it the creation of 12,000 direct, long-term jobs.

Lieutenant Governor Burt Jones and House Speaker Jon Burns joined legislative leaders, GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson, local elected/economic officials from the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority, and company representatives at the celebration.
Lt. Governor Burt Jones applauded Governor Kemp, state agencies, and the University System of Georgia (especially Georgia Tech) for their partnership with Hyundai.
Up to 300,000 Hyundai, Genesis, and Kia EVs are expected to be produced annually at the Metaplant.
17 HMGMA suppliers have announced projects across 10 Georgia counties spanning beyond southeast Georgia.
Further supplier announcements are expected.
Supplier facilities are expected to create more than 6,700 new jobs and more than $2.5 billion in investment.
Supplier facilities will be operational prior to the opening of HMGMA.
Speaker Jon Burns praised Governor Kemp and the General Assembly for sound policy direction supporting Hyundai's Southeast Georgia investment.
Speaker Burns stated the Metaplant will have a generational impact as a force multiplier for investments in education, healthcare, housing, and transportation.
The nearly 3,000-acre Metaplant site already has four building exteriors complete.
Outfitting for internal operations is underway at the site.
The Metaplant’s architecture incorporates technologically advanced elements and design features representing the Savannah area, Hyundai’s brand identity, nature, and clean energy.
HMGMA’s full-time employees are called “Meta Pros” and are being hired for training and skills on machinery, office/clerical work, and numerous other employment roles.
HMGMA currently has 566 full-time employees.
Approximately 75 percent of the workforce is from the Greater Savannah area.
An additional 37 employees are from Georgia but outside the local area.
Georgia Quick Start broke ground on its customized training center at the Metaplant site on February 8.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated Georgians will be trained in high-skill careers as Meta Pros at one of the most advanced vehicle assembly and battery plants in the world.
HMGMA partnered with local technical colleges to prepare students for entry-level employment at the Metaplant.

Electrical Vehicle Professional Technical Certificate of Credit (EVP TCC) training is available at Coastal Pines Technical College’s Baxley and Waycross campuses, Southeastern Technical College’s Vidalia campus, Savannah Technical College, and Ogeechee Technical College.
TCSG Commissioner Greg Dozier stated technical colleges are providing comprehensive training programs tailored to the growing EV industry in Georgia.
In September 2023, Georgia Institute of Technology (Georgia Tech) and Hyundai Motor Company signed a memorandum of understanding to collaborate on research and applications for sustainable mobility.
The MOU covers developing a hydrogen economy, workforce development, and making cities smarter and more sustainable.
U.S. News & World Report ranked Georgia Tech as the top public university and No. 3 nationally in energy and fuel research.

Ángel Cabrera, President of Georgia Tech, stated the partnership represents one of their most significant corporate relationships to advance science for key sectors and produce the next-generation workforce.
Since 1998, Hyundai has directed over $6 million towards local Georgia communities through philanthropic programs and partnerships.
The company is promoting STEM programs in local K-12 schools.
Hyundai hosts educational programs on alternative energy sources.
Hyundai supports local organizations dedicated to affordable homeownership.
Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority’s Trip Tollison stated Hyundai has given more than $1 million to regional nonprofits.
Tollison noted Hyundai formed key partnerships with local educational institutions.
Hyundai committed to $7.59 billion in capital investment in Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
The project will create 8,500 jobs.
More than 550 Meta Pros have already been hired, with 75 percent coming from the Savannah region.
Beginning with mass production, electricity used to power the Metaplant will come from 100 percent clean power sources.
This aligns with Hyundai’s commitment to carbon neutrality by 2045.
A 41-acre park will be featured in front of the plant.
The park includes trails, running tracks, environmental art displays, sports fields, and other amenities for employee use.
source_title: State Leaders Celebrate Hyundai Motor Group’s Historic Impact on Georgia - Columbus CEO

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp and Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung announced that Hyundai Motor Group will open its first fully dedicated electric vehicle (EV) and battery manufacturing facility in Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group will invest $5.54 billion in opening a state-of-the-art U.S. smart factory at the Bryan County Megasite.
Non-affiliated Hyundai Motor Group suppliers will invest approximately another $1 billion in the project.
The project will deliver approximately 8,100 new jobs to Georgia’s coastal region.

Governor Kemp was joined by Jay Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, and José Muñoz, President and COO of Hyundai Motor Company, along with Hyundai Motor Group executives and state and local economic development leaders at the premier 2,923-acre site.
The megasite was purchased jointly by the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority (JDA) and the State less than a year ago.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that the announcement is now the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history.
Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung expressed pride in sharing plans to open the first dedicated full EV and battery manufacturing facilities in the U.S.
Hyundai Motor Group expects to begin construction on the new facility in January 2023.
Full production is expected in the first half of 2025.
The facility will have an annual capacity of 300,000 units.
Wages throughout the project will be well-paying advanced manufacturing positions competitive in the local market.
Jaehoon (Jay) Chang stated they decided to build their first dedicated EV plant in the U.S. because America embraces change and drives innovation.
Hyundai Motor Group cited speed-to-market, workforce, and the state’s ability to meet carbon neutrality standards as reasons for choosing Georgia.
Georgia is home to an existing network of Hyundai subsidiaries and suppliers.
The Bryan County Megasite was developed by the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor JDA over several years in partnership with the state.
Industrial utilities are adjacent to the site, and extensive due diligence reports have been completed.

President and COO of Hyundai Motor Company José Muñoz stated they are thrilled to partner with Governor Kemp and the Savannah Joint Economic Development Authority to bring a flexible EV smart factory to Bryan County.
Hyundai Motor Group is headquartered in Seoul, Korea, and employs 250,000 people worldwide.
The Group’s vehicles are sold in 193 countries through 5,000 dealerships and showrooms.
At the new Georgia highly connected, automated, and flexible EV smart factory, Hyundai Motor Group plans to produce a diverse range of innovative full electric vehicles.
Details of production models will be shared at later dates.
Through the battery manufacturing facility, the Group aims to establish a stable supply chain for EV battery and other EV components in the U.S. market.
When it nears planned 2025 start of operations, Hyundai Motor Group will seek local vendors and suppliers.

Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority Board of Directors Chairman Carter Infinger stated the announcement is transformative and the jobs/investment will reverberate for years and generations.
Bryan County is the fastest-growing county in the state.
The strategic purchase of the Bryan County Megasite in 2021 was the largest in state history.
The site is adjacent to Interstate 16 with immediate access from I-95 and I-16 to 250 major metro areas.
It is less than 30 miles from the Port of Savannah, the single-largest and fastest-growing container terminal in the U.S. with two Class I rail facilities on-site provided by Georgia Central Railway.

Georgia Department of Transportation Commissioner Russell McMurry stated Hyundai's news exemplifies Georgia’s collective focus on supporting the electric mobility future and the power of partnership/preparation.

Georgia Ports Authority Executive Director Griff Lynch stated Hyundai's new location provides stellar access to global and domestic markets for both assembly components and finished vehicle marketing.
Building on existing automotive assets and workforce, Georgia is consistently welcoming increased investment in the electric mobility ecosystem and supply chain across the state.
Georgia is the Southeastern leader for EV registrations per 1,000 registered automobiles.
Georgia offers more EV charging outlets per capita than anywhere else in the Southeast.

The Georgia Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance is a public-private-nonprofit initiative focused on growing the electric mobility ecosystem in the state and strengthening Georgia’s position in electrification-related manufacturing and innovation.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) was represented by Project Manager Taylor Walden in partnership with the Department’s Georgia-based Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim.
Partners included the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor JDA, Georgia Department of Transportation, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power.
Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated under Governor Kemp’s leadership, they have focused on putting Georgia at the forefront of electric mobility.
At the new facility, Hyundai Motor Group plans to implement many advanced intelligent manufacturing technologies currently under test at the Group’s innovation hub.
Technologies include intelligent A.I.-equipped manufacturing and data-driven processes to best meet customer demands.
The plant will help support Hyundai Motor Group’s efforts to achieve its carbon neutrality target through the active use of renewable energy for the facility.
Since 2020 alone, Georgia has announced more than 20 EV-related projects that will invest more than $13.54 billion and create nearly 18,000 jobs in the state.

links.context: Project RFIs, RFPs listing, and supply chain outreach inquiries

source_title: Hyundai Motor Group to invest $5.54 billion in Georgia at first fully dedicated electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facility - SEDA

facts: Hyundai Motor Group has reportedly begun early-stage production of electric vehicles at its new Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America facility, located near Savannah, Georgia.
According to a report on Saturday by the Atlanta Journal-Constitution, Hyundai commenced production at the facility earlier last week.
The company also reportedly held a private event on Thursday for employees to mark this achievement.
The official completion ceremony for the plant is still scheduled for early 2025, according to the newspaper.
The HMGMA plant represents a key part of Hyundai's long-term investment in the US market.
Hyundai Motor Group first announced its plans for the plant in April 2022, with the groundbreaking taking place in October of that year.
The company has committed $7.6 billion to develop the facility.
The facility is expected to produce a wide range of electric and hybrid vehicles.
Initial pilot production officially began earlier this year.
A Hyundai Seoul official confirmed the production but clarified that the plant is not fully operational yet.
The official stated, "We are aiming to start production in the fourth quarter. While we're making progress, we're still in the process of recruiting and training staff."
Although primarily marketed as a dedicated EV plant, Hyundai designed the facility to be flexible for producing both electric and hybrid vehicles.
At a Hyundai investor event in Seoul earlier in August, Hyundai Motor Company CEO Chang Jae-hoon emphasized the plant's flexibility.
Chang noted that the plant "goes beyond the conventional concept of an automotive manufacturing facility."
He added that HMGMA is capable of producing a mix of EVs and hybrids to adapt to market demand.
The first model to roll off the line at the Georgia plant is the 2025 Ioniq 5, an updated version of the electric crossover introduced in South Korea in March.
All trims of the Ioniq 5, except the high-performance Ioniq 5 N, will be produced at HMGMA.
The Ioniq 5 produced at HMGMA will benefit from the $7,500 tax credit provided under the Inflation Reduction Act.
Hyundai projects that the plant will have the capacity to manufacture between 300,000 and 500,000 vehicles per year when fully operational.
source_title: Hyundai reportedly begins EV production at Georgia plant - THE INVESTOR

facts: Hyundai has begun producing electric SUVs at its $7.6 billion plant in Georgia.
The plant is located west of Savannah, Georgia.
Production began less than two years after breaking ground on the sprawling manufacturing plant.
Groundbreaking for the plant occurred in late October 2022.
Hyundai’s factory held an “employee-focused celebration” on a Thursday when its first EV for commercial sale rolled off the assembly line.
Bianca Johnson is the spokesperson for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Production processes were validated to ensure vehicles meet Hyundai Motor Group’s high quality standards.
Initial production of customer vehicles started ahead of schedule.
A grand opening celebration at the Georgia plant is expected in the first quarter of 2025.
South Korean automaker Hyundai and battery partner LG Energy Solution plan to employ 8,500 total workers at the Bryan County site once fully operational.
The Bryan County site is located about 50 miles (80 kilometers) west of Savannah.
Hyundai plans to produce up to 300,000 EVs per year in Georgia.
Hyundai will also produce the batteries that power these vehicles at the Georgia site.
The plant’s vehicle production areas have been completed and are being staffed by more than 1,000 workers.
Battery-making facilities at the site remain under construction.
The first vehicles being produced at the site are 2025 models of Hyundai’s Ioniq 5 electric SUVs.
These American-made EVs will arrive at U.S. dealerships before the end of this year.
During the first half of 2024, the Ioniq 5 was America’s second-best-selling electric vehicle not made by Tesla.
The Georgia plant is the largest economic development project the state has ever seen.
The project came with $2.1 billion in tax breaks and other incentives from state and local governments.
Hyundai rushed to start making EVs in Georgia within two years of groundbreaking, spurred by federal electric vehicle incentives that reward domestic production.
The Inflation Reduction Act was passed in 2022 with provisions intended to combat climate change.
The Inflation Reduction Act includes a tax credit that saves EV buyers up to $7,500, but only on cars made in North America with domestic batteries.
Hyundai executives complained the law was unfair.
Hyundai President and Global Chief Operating Officer Jose Munoz said the law caused the automaker to push to open sooner in Georgia.
source_title: Hyundai has begun producing electric SUVs at its $7.6 billion plant in Georgia | AP News

facts: Hyundai Motor Group and SK On finalized the name of their North American battery production joint venture as HSBMA.
HSBMA stands for Hyundai SK Battery Manufacturing America.
The official corporate name was confirmed as HSBMA according to industry sources on Monday.
The joint venture's launch was announced on April 25, 2023.
HSBMA has completed more than 90% of construction.
HSBMA aims to begin commercial operations this year.
The facility is located in Bartow County, Georgia.
The facility is planned to have an annual production capacity of 35 gigawatt-hours (GWh).
The planned capacity is enough to supply battery cells for approximately 300,000 electric vehicles.
Hyundai Motor Group and SK On invested a total of $5 billion in the joint venture.
The two companies hold equal 50% stakes in HSBMA.
Battery cells produced at HSBMA will be assembled into battery packs by Hyundai Mobis.
The battery packs will be supplied entirely to Hyundai Motor, Kia, and Genesis electric vehicles manufactured in the United States.
Hyundai Motor's stock ticker is 005380.KS.
Kia's stock ticker is 000270.KS.
HSBMA is located near Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
HSBMA is located near Kia's Georgia plant.
HSBMA is located near Hyundai Motor's Alabama plant.
The possibility of expanding HSBMA's supply in line with future production increases at HMGMA has been discussed.
SK On plans to further strengthen its North American production capacity through its existing standalone plant in Georgia, SK Battery America (22 GWh).
SK On plans to further strengthen its North American production capacity through the upcoming HSBMA plant (35 GWh).
SK On plans to further strengthen its North American production capacity through the BlueOval SK Tennessee plant (45 GWh).
The BlueOval SK Tennessee plant is set to be converted into a standalone facility.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group, SK On Finalize U.S. Battery JV Name as 'HSBMA' - Seoul Economic Daily

facts: SQM, a Chilean lithium mining company, announced it reached a long-term agreement with Hyundai Motor and Kia to supply lithium hydroxide to the South Korean automakers.
The SQM deal marked Hyundai's third lithium hydroxide supply contract signed this year after contracts with Chinese companies Ganfeng Lithium and Chengxin Lithium Group announced in January.
Lithium hydroxide is used in high-priced ternary batteries, such as NCM (nickel-cobalt-manganese) batteries, which are high in energy density.
NCM batteries are relatively expensive but allow for a long driving range per charge for EVs compared with the more broadly used LFP (lithium iron phosphate) batteries.

Auto industry observers see Hyundai Motor Group's recent lithium hydroxide supply deals as a sign that the group is focusing on adopting high-capacity NCM batteries to boost the driving range of its future EV models.
Anxiety over the driving range of EVs is often cited as a key hindrance in the mass adoption of electric cars, with many drivers remaining unconvinced that EVs offer enough mileage per charge.
Hyundai's yet-unveiled sub-compact electric sport utility vehicle (SUV), the Casper Electric, boasts a maximum driving range of 315 kilometers on a single charge thanks to its NCM battery.
The comparable Ray EV by Kia has a range of 205 kms due to its LFP battery.
The Casper Electric, which will be badged as Inster in Europe, will debut at the Busan International Mobility Show this week.
Kia's new dedicated EV SUV, the EV3, will be sold starting in July and equipped with an NCM battery instead of an LFP battery.
The long-range trim of the EV3, fitted with a high-capacity 81.4 kWh NCM battery, has a driving range of up to 501 kms on a single charge.
Given its higher price tag, Hyundai Motor Group is seeking to lower its EV customers' NCM battery cost burden by internalising production.
This year, the South Korean auto giant began production at its battery plant in Indonesia established jointly with LG Energy Solution.
Kia will use the NCM batteries produced at the Indonesia plant in the upcoming EV3 units.
Song Ho-sung, CEO and president of Kia, last month stressed the importance of ensuring the long driving range of EVs, suggesting that Kia will expand its adoption of NCM batteries.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Doubles Down On Ensuring Longer Driving Range Of Evs

facts: A deal was reached between South Korea and the United States on Sunday to release more than 300 workers detained in an immigration enforcement raid at a massive Hyundai plant in Georgia.
During the Thursday raid, Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents arrested 475 immigrants suspected of living and working in the U.S. illegally.
According to South Korea's Foreign Minister Cho Hyun, more than 300 of the detained workers were South Korean nationals.
South Korea's presidential chief of staff Kang Hoon-sik said negotiations for the workers' releases had been finalized.
The detained workers will be returned to South Korea as soon as remaining administrative steps are completed.
South Korea plans to send a charter plane to transport the released workers back home.
Hundreds of U.S. federal agents raided Hyundai's sprawling manufacturing site in southern Georgia last week.
The targeted facility is where the Korean automaker manufactures electric vehicles.

Steven Schrank, special agent in charge of Homeland Security Investigations in Georgia and Alabama, stated at a Friday news conference that a majority of the detained individuals were Korean nationals.
Schrank noted he did not know the exact number of Korean nationals detained during the operation.
The detained workers were employed by different companies, including subcontractors, according to Schrank.
The raid was conducted as part of the Trump administration's mass deportation agenda.
State officials have long designated the targeted facility as Georgia's largest economic development project.
Steven Schrank confirmed the raid was executed as part of a month-long investigation into allegations of unlawful employment practices and other federal crimes.
Schrank described the operation as the largest enforcement action at a single site in the history of Homeland Security Investigations, an ICE unit.
Video released by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement on Saturday showed a caravan of vehicles arriving at the site with federal agents directing workers to line up outside.
Some detainees were ordered to place their hands against a bus while being frisked and shackled around their hands, ankles, and waist.
Agents specifically focused their operation on a plant that remains under construction.
Hyundai has partnered with LG Energy Solution at the facility to produce batteries for electric vehicles.
Most of the detained individuals were transported to an immigration detention center in Folkston, Georgia, near the Florida state line.
As of Friday, none of the detainees had been charged with any crimes, according to Schrank.
Schrank added that the underlying investigation remains ongoing.
The South Korean government expressed "concern and regret" over the raid targeting its citizens and sent diplomats to the site.
source_title: South Korea says it reached a deal with U.S. to release workers detained by ICE in Georgia Hyundai raid - CBS News

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (Ellabell Plant)
facts: For months, companies building Hyundai’s electric vehicles and batteries have trucked millions of gallons of wastewater from the Ellabell manufacturing facility to private treatment facilities in Georgia and beyond state lines.
Savannah city authorities found excessive levels of heavy metals from the plant’s wastewater that violated its permit.
Correspondence between Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America and Savannah officials shows struggles with wastewater processing and disposal since September 2024.
Permit violations forced the company to abandon treatment in Coastal Georgia and truck industrial wastewater to sites in Jacksonville and Goose Creek, South Carolina, among other places.
The difficulties illustrate challenges local communities face from Hyundai’s rapid pace to open Georgia’s largest economic development project.
Bryan County is building the $129 million North Bryan County Water Reclamation Facility across I-16 from the Hyundai site to accommodate the plant’s industrial waste.
The new facility will not be complete until the end of 2025.
HMGMA is the legal entity in charge of manufacturing and permitting at the Ellabell site, which started assembling vehicles last September.
Two temporary measures—first Savannah treating the wastewater, then a Richmond Hill facility handling it—have failed.
Ogeechee Riverkeeper Damon Mullis stated HMGMA’s wastewater issues are part of a larger pattern of neglect of state standards and good stewardship of natural resources.

Mullis noted there seems to be a gap in the system where contents of hauled wastewater are not properly vetted, resulting in industrial pollutants passing through facilities and polluting shared waters.
Savannah city government agreed to treat HMGMA’s industrial wastewater temporarily while the Bryan County facility was built.
On Sept. 3, the Travis Field Water Reclamation Facility near the airport received its first wastewater from the Hyundai site through 24 miles of pipe.
Within weeks, city technicians discovered Hyundai’s wastewater contained more than six times the allowable limit of copper and more than twice the allowable zinc concentration.
HMGMA failed to keep continuous oversight on wastewater conductivity, with data gaps sometimes reaching 19 hours.
Savannah has authority from the Georgia Environmental Protection Division (EPD) to operate and enforce an industrial pretreatment program.
The permit for HMGMA demanded self-reporting from the company.
City technicians found clear violations resulting in rising levels of phosphorus in discharge from Travis Field, which poses a risk to sensitive biological treatment processes.
On Sept. 26, the city shut down operations at Travis Field in anticipation of Hurricane Helene, which was also the last day it received wastewater from HMGMA.
On Oct. 1, the city sent a “Notice of Violation” to Hyundai detailing permit violations and demanding a detailed correction plan.
Savannah Chief of Water Resources Ron Feldner stated that inability to achieve compliance necessitated alternate approaches for wastewater disposal.
Rather than restart treatment in Savannah, HMGMA started trucking wastewater to various facilities in late September.
State regulators at the EPD stepped in on Dec. 6, sending a “Letter of Concern” to HMGMA requiring a permit for government-owned facilities and requesting a list of all receiving treatment plants.

In the last four months of 2024, HMGMA sent more than 5.2 million gallons of wastewater to private treatment plants operated by Liquid Environmental Solutions in Jacksonville, Fla.; ReWorld in Augusta and Asheboro, NC; Shamrock Environmental Corp in Browns Summit, NC and Oxford, Ga; and US Water Recovery in Goose Creek, SC.

Communications and PR Manager Joe LaMuraglia said Hyundai’s own investigation showed pipes within its onsite pretreatment system had elevated zinc levels affecting both domestic and industrial wastewater discharge.
The piping replacement process will not be complete until the fourth quarter of 2025.
HMGMA received a Notice of Violation on Jan. 27 from the EPD for not applying or receiving a permit prior to disposing wastewater at the Richmond Hill facility.
The EPD notice threatens fines for any continued noncompliance.
Correspondence shows companies from the Ellabell site stopped using the Richmond Hill facility in November.
LaMuraglia stated the company had not received any fines as of March 5.
LaMuraglia clarified that the wastewater is not solely from HMGMA.
HMGMA remains the permit holder and subject of both Savannah’s and EPD’s regulatory actions.
State EPD officials continue to talk with HMGMA to ensure proper wastewater management until the new Bryan County facility is ready later this year, according to EPD spokeswoman Sara Lips.
Savannah city water experts remain involved in working daily to return to a normal treatment process, according to Feldner.

The new Bryan County facility will use a membrane bioreactor process and have capacity to treat 5 million gallons of wastewater a day at its projected opening in the fourth quarter of this year, according to spokesman Nicholas Beard.
LaMuraglia stated the wastewater issues have had “no impact to production” at the megasite.
Hyundai will start releasing production numbers at the end of March.
LaMuraglia emphasized Hyundai’s vested interest in keeping local waterways healthy and adherence to all applicable laws and regulations regarding wastewater.

Ogeechee Riverkeeper Legal Director Ben Kirsch stated that Hyundai’s failure to properly treat industrial wastewater began impacting the biochemical balance at City facilities and could have polluted waterways if not caught.

Kirsch noted it is likely what happened at each of the smaller facilities Hyundai sent wastewater to, as without a pretreatment permit, they could not know if they could successfully treat the specific needs of Hyundai’s industrial wastewater.
source_title: Hyundai fails wastewater standards - The Current

facts: U.S. immigration authorities raided the sprawling site where Hyundai manufactures electric vehicles in southeast Georgia on Thursday.
The operation shut down construction on an adjacent factory being built to produce EV batteries.
The site is touted by the governor and other officials as one of Georgia’s largest and most high-profile manufacturing sites, and the largest economic development project in the state’s history.
Hyundai Motor Group began manufacturing EVs at the $7.6 billion plant a year ago.
The plant employs about 1,200 people.
U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement spokesman Lindsay Williams confirmed federal authorities were conducting an enforcement operation at the 3,000-acre (1,214-hectare) site west of Savannah.
Agents focused their search on the construction site for the battery plant.

The Department of Homeland Security stated agents executed a search warrant as part of an ongoing criminal investigation into allegations of unlawful employment practices and other serious federal crimes.
DHS did not disclose whether anyone was detained or arrested during the operation.
Georgia State Patrol troopers blocked roads to the Hyundai site to assist federal authorities dispatched by the Georgia Department of Public Safety.

Video posted to social media showed workers in yellow safety vests lined up while a man wearing a tactical vest with HSI (Homeland Security Investigations) letters ordered construction to cease immediately and all work to end on the site right now.
President Donald Trump’s administration has undertaken sweeping ICE operations as part of a mass deportation agenda, including raids on farms, construction sites, restaurants, and auto repair shops.

The Pew Research Center cited preliminary Census Bureau data indicating the U.S. labor force lost more than 1.2 million immigrants from January through July, including both undocumented individuals and legal residents.
In addition to making electric vehicles at the site facing Interstate 16 in Bryan County, Hyundai partnered with LG Energy Solution to build the adjacent battery plant.
The joint venture battery plant is slated to open sometime next year.
HL-GA Battery Company stated it is cooperating fully with appropriate authorities and has paused construction to assist their work.
Plant spokesperson Bianca Johnson confirmed that operations at Hyundai’s EV manufacturing plant were not interrupted by the raid.
Production and normal office hours had already begun for the day when authorities shut down access, and the operation did not impact employees getting to work.
source_title: US immigration officers raid Hyundai's electric vehicle site in Georgia | AP News

facts: Governor Brian Kemp and First Lady Marty Kemp joined President and Global COO of Hyundai Motor Company and President and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America José Muñoz to celebrate Hyundai Motor Group’s impact on Georgia and provide an update on Metaplant America on Tuesday, February 27th, 2024.
The facility will officially start vehicle production in the fourth quarter of 2024.

Governor Brian Kemp stated Hyundai has been an incredible partner for decades, helping recruit businesses, give back to communities, and work with colleges and universities to connect Georgians with opportunities.

Hyundai and Genesis electric vehicles on display included the IONIQ 5, IONIQ 6, GV60, Electrified GV70, and the hydrogen-powered XCIENT Fuel Cell Truck, which will be used for logistics between Hyundai’s manufacturing facilities and suppliers.
Governor Kemp officially declared February 26 as Hyundai Day throughout the state.
The General Assembly passed a Resolution commending Hyundai Motor Group’s dedication to Georgia and recognizing February 26 as Hyundai Day at the State Capitol.
José Muñoz stated Hyundai’s investments in Georgia will create tens of thousands of great-paying American jobs and bring tens of billions of dollars in economic impact for decades to come.

Since the HMGMA project was announced in May 2022, LG Energy Solution was named as the joint venture partner in the manufacture of battery components, with an additional expansion of the battery facility announced.
Hyundai and SK On are building an EV battery manufacturing facility in Bartow County that will supply Hyundai Motor Group’s U.S. plants.

Since the initial announcement of HMGMA’s location, Hyundai Motor Group’s planned investment in Georgia has reached more than $12.6 billion, bringing with it the creation of 12,000 direct, long-term jobs.

Lieutenant Governor Burt Jones and House Speaker Jon Burns, along with legislative leaders from both chambers, Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson, local elected and economic officials from the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority, and numerous company representatives joined the celebration.

Lt. Governor Burt Jones applauded Governor Kemp, state agencies, and the University System of Georgia (especially Georgia Tech) for their partnership with Hyundai, noting it enabled positive impact and thousands of jobs, supporting Georgia's position as the number one state to do business.
Up to 300,000 Hyundai, Genesis, and Kia EVs are expected to be produced annually at the Metaplant.
17 HMGMA suppliers have announced projects across 10 Georgia counties spanning beyond southeast Georgia, with further announcements expected.
Supplier facilities are expected to create more than 6,700 new jobs and more than $2.5 billion in investment, and will be operational prior to the opening of HMGMA.

Speaker of the House Jon Burns stated Hyundai's Metaplant will have a generational impact as a force multiplier for investments in education, healthcare, housing, and transportation, and praised the commitment to start vehicle production in Q4 2024.
At the nearly 3,000-acre Metaplant site, four building exteriors are complete and outfitting for internal operations is underway.
The Metaplant’s architecture incorporates technologically advanced elements plus design features representing the greater Savannah area, Hyundai’s brand identity, nature, and clean energy.
HMGMA’s full-time employees ("Meta Pros") are being hired at a rapid pace for training and skills on machinery, office and clerical work, and numerous other roles.
HMGMA currently has 566 full-time employees.
Of that workforce, approximately 75 percent are from the Greater Savannah area.
An additional 37 employees are from Georgia but outside the local area.
Georgia Quick Start broke ground on its customized training center at the Metaplant site on February 8.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that with the groundbreaking for the Georgia Quick Start Training Center, Georgia fulfilled a commitment to Hyundai and Georgians, who will be trained in high-skill careers as Meta Pros.
HMGMA partnered with local technical colleges to prepare students for entry-level employment at the Metaplant.

Electrical Vehicle Professional Technical Certificate of Credit (EVP TCC) training is available at Coastal Pines Technical College’s Baxley and Waycross campuses, Southeastern Technical College’s Vidalia campus, Savannah Technical College, and Ogeechee Technical College.

TCSG Commissioner Greg Dozier stated the Technical College System of Georgia is proud of its partnership with Hyundai, providing comprehensive training programs tailored to the growing EV industry to cultivate a highly skilled workforce.

In September 2023, Georgia Institute of Technology (Georgia Tech) and Hyundai Motor Company signed a memorandum of understanding to collaborate on research and applications for sustainable mobility, including developing a hydrogen economy, workforce development, and making cities smarter and more sustainable.
U.S. News & World Report ranked Georgia Tech as the top public university and No. 3 nationally in energy and fuel research.

Georgia Tech President Ángel Cabrera stated the strategic partnership represents a significant corporate relationship to advance science, emerging technologies, and produce the next-generation workforce.
Since 1998, Hyundai has directed over $6 million towards local Georgia communities through philanthropic programs and partnerships.

The company is promoting STEM programs in local K-12 schools, hosting educational programs on alternative energy sources, supporting affordable homeownership organizations, and focusing on environmental stewardship.

Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority’s Trip Tollison stated Hyundai has given more than $1 million to regional nonprofits, formed key partnerships with local educational institutions, committed to $7.59 billion in capital investment in HMGMA, will create 8,500 jobs, and hired more than 550 Meta Pros (75% from the Savannah region).
Beginning with mass production start, electricity used to power the Metaplant will come from 100 percent clean power sources, aligning with Hyundai’s commitment to carbon neutrality by 2045.
A 41-acre park will be featured in front of the plant including trails, running tracks, environmental art displays, sports fields, and other amenities for employee use.
source_title: State Leaders Celebrate Hyundai Motor Group’s Historic Impact on Georgia - Middle Georgia CEO

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group Georgia EV & Battery Manufacturing Facility
facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp and Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung announced that Hyundai Motor Group will open its first fully dedicated electric vehicle (EV) and battery manufacturing facility in the State of Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group will invest $5.54 billion in opening a state-of-the-art U.S. smart factory at the Bryan County Megasite.
Non-affiliated Hyundai Motor Group suppliers will invest approximately another $1 billion in the project.
The project will deliver approximately 8,100 new jobs to Georgia’s coastal region.

Governor Kemp was joined by Jay Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, and José Muñoz, President and COO of Hyundai Motor Company, along with Hyundai Motor Group executives and state and local economic development leaders at the premier 2,923-acre site.
The megasite was purchased jointly by the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority (JDA) and the State less than a year ago.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that the announcement is now the largest economic development project in Georgia's history.

Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung shared plans to open their first dedicated full EV and battery manufacturing facilities in the U.S. and partner with Georgia for electrified mobility and sustainability.
Hyundai Motor Group expects to begin construction on the new facility in January 2023.
Full production is expected in the first half of 2025.
The facility will have an annual capacity of 300,000 units.
Wages throughout the project will be well-paying advanced manufacturing positions competitive in the local market.
Jaehoon (Jay) Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, stated that America embraces change and drives innovation, prompting the decision to build their first dedicated EV plant in the U.S.
The new EV plant is described as the future of their business and will help meet growing demands of U.S. customers for leading edge design, safe, zero-emissions vehicles.
Hyundai Motor Group cited speed-to-market, workforce, and the state’s ability to meet the company’s carbon neutrality standards when choosing Georgia.
Georgia is home to an existing network of Hyundai subsidiaries and suppliers.
The Bryan County Megasite was developed by the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor JDA over the last several years in partnership with the state.
The site offers needed speed-to-market requirements that reduce barriers to speedy operations.
Industrial utilities are adjacent to the site, and extensive due diligence reports have been completed.

President and COO of Hyundai Motor Company José Muñoz stated they are partnering with Governor Kemp and the Savannah Joint Economic Development Authority to bring a flexible EV smart factory to Bryan County.
The plant will position them well to win in a competitive market with consumers demanding smart, sustainable mobility solutions.
Hyundai Motor Group is headquartered in Seoul, Korea, and employs 250,000 people worldwide.
The Group’s vehicles are sold in 193 countries through 5,000 dealerships and showrooms.
At the new Georgia highly connected, automated, and flexible EV smart factory, Hyundai Motor Group plans to produce a diverse range of innovative full electric vehicles.
Details of production models will be shared at later dates.
Through the battery manufacturing facility, the Group aims to establish a stable supply chain for EV battery and other EV components in the U.S. market.
When it nears planned 2025 start of operations, Hyundai Motor Group will seek local vendors and suppliers.
Information related to inquiries about project RFIs, RFPs listing, and supply chain outreach will be available when it becomes available.

Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority Board of Directors Chairman Carter Infinger stated the announcement is transformative and the jobs/investment will reverberate for years and generations to come.
The project is a great day for Bryan, Bulloch, Chatham and Effingham counties as well as this entire region and the State of Georgia.
Bryan County is the fastest-growing county in the state.
The strategic purchase of the Bryan County Megasite in 2021 was the largest in state history.
The site is adjacent to Interstate 16 with immediate access from I-95 and I-16 to 250 major metro areas.
It is less than 30 miles from the Port of Savannah, the single-largest and fastest-growing container terminal in the U.S. with two Class I rail facilities on-site provided by Georgia Central Railway.

Georgia Department of Transportation Commissioner Russell McMurry stated today’s news exemplifies the power of partnership and preparation, and that Georgia’s planning ensures logistics networks are safe, sustainable, innovative, and ready to support economic growth.

Georgia Ports Authority Executive Director Griff Lynch stated Hyundai Motor Group’s new location provides stellar access to global and domestic markets for both assembly components and finished vehicle marketing.
The decision to expand in Georgia is a clear endorsement of the Peach State as a dynamic center for cutting-edge manufacturing.

Building on the state’s existing automotive assets and developing workforce, Georgia is consistently welcoming increased investment in the electric mobility ecosystem and supply chain across the state.
Georgia is the Southeastern leader for EV registrations per 1,000 registered automobiles.
Georgia offers more EV charging outlets per capita than anywhere else in the Southeast.

The Georgia Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance is a public-private-nonprofit initiative focused on growing the electric mobility ecosystem in the state and strengthening Georgia’s position in electrification-related manufacturing and innovation.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) was represented by Project Manager Taylor Walden in partnership with the Department’s Georgia-based Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim.

Partners at the announcement included the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor JDA (Bryan, Bulloch, Chatham, and Effingham counties), the Georgia Department of Transportation, the Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power.
Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that under Governor Kemp’s leadership, they have been focused on putting Georgia at the forefront of electric mobility.
The announcement solidifies Georgia's spot at the vanguard of the EV transition.
The state-of-the-art facility will create exciting new possibilities for all Georgians and transform an entire region.
At the new facility, Hyundai Motor Group plans to implement many of its advanced intelligent manufacturing technologies currently under test at the Group’s innovation hub.
Technologies include intelligent A.I.-equipped manufacturing and data-driven processes to best meet demands of customers.
The plant will help support Hyundai Motor Group’s efforts to achieve its carbon neutrality target through the active use of renewable energy for the facility.
Since 2020 alone, Georgia has announced more than 20 EV-related projects that will invest more than $13.54 billion and create nearly 18,000 jobs in the state.

links.context: Project RFIs, RFPs listing, and supply chain outreach
More information about Hyundai Motor Group

source_title: More about Gov. Kemp: Hyundai Motor Group to Invest $5.54 Billion in Georgia at First Fully Dedicated Electric Vehicle and Battery Manufacturing Facility | Georgia Industry Online

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group Georgia EV Plant Project
facts: The state of Georgia and local governments will provide Hyundai Motor Group with $1.8 billion in tax breaks and other incentives.
The incentives are in exchange for Hyundai constructing its first U.S. plant specifically for electric vehicles.
The plant will be located close to the port city of Savannah.
Hyundai is required by the agreement to hire 8,100 people.
Hyundai is required by the agreement to invest $5.5 billion in its Georgia plant.
This represents the most significant economic investment in the state of Georgia.
A few months prior to this announcement, Georgia closed a deal with electric vehicle manufacturer Rivian to establish a factory.
Pat Wilson, the state’s Economic Development Commissioner, approves of both the Hyundai and Rivian deals and incentives.
Wilson stated the deals would boost interest in electric vehicles while providing Georgians across the state with reliable jobs in the future.
The project site is an expansive 2,900-acre location in Bryan County, west of Savannah.
Hyundai officials and Governor Brian Kemp announced the agreement at the site.
Hyundai intends to build the facility in the coming year.
The plant is scheduled to produce up to 300,000 vehicles annually in 2025.
EV batteries will also be manufactured at the new factory.
The deal was made public on Friday.
The Hyundai incentives are worth approximately $300 million more than the incentives Rivian received.
Georgia and four counties near Savannah are giving Hyundai $228,000 for each new job created.

entity_name: Georgia Electric Vehicle Market and Tax Credit Program
canonical_name: Georgia EV Market and Federal Clean Air Tax Credit Program
facts: Consumers have less than a week to claim the $7,500 federal tax credit for new clean air electric vehicles before the program ends.
The $4,000 federal tax credit for qualifying used EVs also ends on Sept. 30.
Local dealerships across Georgia are capitalizing on the countdown with targeted incentives and promotions.
Ed Voyles Honda has created a dedicated EV webpage featuring available incentives and a map of nearby charging stations.
Jim Ellis Volkswagen Kennesaw is highlighting which models still qualify for the credit, including the Volkswagen ID.4.
Costco’s Auto Program is offering members an additional $1,000 to $3,000 incentive on top of the federal tax credit when purchasing an EV through participating dealerships.
According to the U.S. Department of Energy, Georgia is home to approximately 92,000 electric vehicles, with the majority concentrated in the metro Atlanta area.
The concentration of EVs has led to the development of approximately 1,700 charging stations in the metro Atlanta region and 2,300 statewide.
Kia’s plant in West Point produces 40% of all Kia models sold nationwide, according to the Georgia Department of Economic Development.
Hyundai’s new Metaplant will support the production of 500,000 Hyundai, Kia and Genesis electric and hybrid vehicles annually at full production.
Last week, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America announced a $2.7 billion expansion that is set to create 3,000 more jobs.
Rivian has confirmed a $5 billion investment in a plant near Covington, which is expected to create 7,500 jobs.
source_title: Race against time: Georgia EV buyers scramble for $7,500 tax credit

facts: A forklift incident on May 20 resulted in the death of a construction worker at the Hyundai Metaplant complex near Savannah, Ga.
This fatality marks the third recorded at the electric vehicle production campus in just over two years.
The incident was the second jobsite death in the past two months.
Local media reports cite the Bryan County Sheriff’s Office regarding the incident location.

The plant is being constructed for HL-GA Battery Company, a joint venture formed by Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solutions to produce batteries for the automaker’s electric vehicle manufacturing operations at the site.
The victim was identified as Allen Kowalski, 27, who worked for a subcontractor at the project.
He reportedly died after being struck by a load that unexpectedly fell as it was being unloaded from the forklift.
The victim’s employer was not identified.
No other injuries were reported from this incident.
The U.S. Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) is investigating the incident.
LG Energy Solutions issued a statement expressing condolences to the worker’s family, friends and colleagues.
LG Energy Solutions stated that “the health and safety of our larger team is our highest priority, and we are committed to following all Occupational Industrial Safety and Health Policies.”
LG Energy Solutions noted they are fully supporting a thorough investigation with their subcontractor and relevant authorities to determine the root cause and prevent future accidents.
The fatality occurred less than two months after another forklift-related incident at the battery plant resulted in the death of a 45-year-old subcontractor employee.
That previous incident also remains under investigation.
Hyundai is listed as architect and general contractor for the $7.6-billion Metaplant’s 11 facilities.
Construction on the Metaplant broke ground in late 2022.

Approximately six months after groundbreaking, in April 2023, 34-year-old Victor Javier Cajija Gamboa died while accessing the top beam of an open-web structural steel frame for the 16-million-sq-ft EV assembly plant.
Gamboa was an employee of steel contractor Eastern Constructors Inc. of Geismar, La.
According to an OSHA investigation report, Gamboa lost his balance while walking along a steel I-beam.
The beam cut his personal fall arrest lanyard, causing him to fall 60 ft to the ground.
The OSHA report concluded Gamboa was killed due to blunt force trauma.
Eastern Constructors was penalized more than $20,000 for safety violations, which included one willful and one serious violation.
OSHA added Eastern Constructors to its Severe Violator Enforcement Program.
Local media report that OSHA has opened at least 15 investigations related to activities at the 2,906-acre site.

In addition to the three fatalities, incidents reportedly have included employees struck by moving objects, falls, an electrocution, and a pipe explosion in March of this year that caused serious but non-life-threatening injuries.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America held the facility's grand opening on March 26.
The first Hyundai vehicles have already rolled off the assembly line.
The facility has an initial annual production capacity of 300,000 units, plus the capability for further expansion.
The HL-GA battery plant is scheduled to begin full operation next year.
According to Hyundai, the Metaplant will be powered entirely by renewable energy, including 5.2 MW of solar power generated by solar panels positioned by the nearly 1,900-vehicle parking area.
source_title: Third Fatality Recorded at Hyundai’s $7.6B Metaplant in Georgia | Engineering News-Record

facts: Hundreds of federal officers raided the Hyundai Metaplant in Ellabell, Georgia, arresting 475 people.
The site is located about 25 miles west of Savannah, Georgia.
The majority of the arrested individuals are Korean nationals.
All 475 people taken into custody were suspected of living and working illegally in the US.
Some arrestees entered the US illegally, some had visa waivers prohibiting work, and some overstayed their visas.
Several people tried to flee during the raid, including some who ran into a sewage pond on the premises.
Agents used a boat to retrieve individuals from the water, and one individual swam under the boat trying to flip it over.
Some of the arrested workers may have been contractors or subcontractors rather than direct employees.
A Hyundai spokesperson stated he does not believe anyone arrested was a direct employee of Hyundai Motor Company.
Hyundai is committed to full compliance with all laws and regulations, including employment verification requirements and immigration laws.
Hyundai Motor North America Chief Manufacturing Officer Chris Susock will manage the megasite following the raid.
An investigation will be conducted to verify that suppliers and subcontractors comply with laws.
Hyundai is reviewing its processes to ensure contractors and subcontractors maintain high standards of legal compliance, including thorough vetting of employment practices.
Hyundai has zero tolerance for those who do not follow the law.
The sprawling 2,900-acre Hyundai Metaplant consists of two parts: a Hyundai electric vehicle manufacturing site and an EV battery plant that is a joint venture between Hyundai and LG.
The raid halted construction of the EV battery plant.
LG stated it is closely monitoring the situation, prioritizing the safety and well-being of employees and partners, and will fully cooperate with relevant authorities.
The operation was part of a multi-month criminal investigation involving evidence development, interviews, document gathering, and judicial search warrants.
ICE and Homeland Security Investigations executed the search warrant accompanied by the Georgia State Patrol, FBI, DEA, ATF, and other agencies.

A search warrant sought records related to violations of conspiracy to conceal, harbor or shield people in the US illegally, including employment/recruitment records, correspondence with federal officials, and identification/immigration documents.
The warrant identified four people specifically to be searched.
No charges have been filed yet, meaning no wrongdoing is being accused at this time.
A construction worker described the raid as a 'war zone' and said workers were lined up against a wall for about an hour before being processed.
Agents asked each worker for their Social Security number and date of birth, and cleared workers received a paper stating 'clear to depart' to show at the gate when leaving.
Another worker hid in an air duct to evade capture due to the heat.
Republican Gov. Brian Kemp has touted the Hyundai Metaplant as a boon for the Georgia economy.
In 2022, Hyundai announced an agreement with the state of Georgia to build its 'first dedicated fully electrified vehicle and battery manufacturing facilities in the United States' in Bryan County.
The Metaplant was expected to create 8,500 jobs.
Gov. Kemp noted that with the first 500 employees trained, it is a major milestone toward the full opening of the Hyundai Metaplant.
Kemp's office stated Georgia will always enforce state and federal immigration laws and that the Department of Public Safety coordinated with ICE to provide support for the operation.
A spokesperson for South Korea's Foreign Ministry said many Korean nationals were detained and urged special attention to ensure their legitimate rights and interests are not violated.
Seoul conveyed concerns and regret through the US Embassy, urging protection of citizens' rights during US law enforcement actions.

Georgia immigration attorney Charles Kuck stated two of his clients were detained after arriving from South Korea under a visa waiver program allowing travel for tourism or business for up to 90 days.
One client arrived last week and the other several weeks ago; both were authorized to work under the visa waiver pursuing activities consistent with it.
The detained clients are engineers who came to advise briefly on work as part of their assigned duties abroad and planned to return to South Korea shortly.
James Rim, president of the Korean American Association of Southeast Georgia, said many Koreans working at the site are skilled construction workers or technicians.
source_title: Immigration raid at Hyundai megaplant in Georgia leads to 475 arrests | CNN

facts: Hyundai Motor Group has shrunk the gap between itself and Tesla by 33% in the United States electric vehicle (EV) market.
The South Korean company’s achievement is attributed to Hyundai and Kia brand sales in the United States.
Hyundai Motor Group shrunk its gap with Tesla by 33% over the past five years.
While Tesla remains the undisputed king of EVs in the US market, Hyundai and Kia might be considered number two.
The Asian company’s recent January to May 2024 EV sales hints that Hyundai and Kia are working hard to cement their place in the US electric vehicle market.
In 2020, Tesla’s gap with Hyundai and Kia combined was 73.2% between January and May.
By 2022, the gap between Tesla and Hyundai Motor Group’s top US brands dropped to 52.9% in the first half of the year.
Last year, Hyundai and Kia managed to close the gap between Tesla by 40.5% in H1 2023.
In 2023, Hyundai and Kia combined sold the second-most EVs in the United States after Tesla.
Hyundai and Kia shares in the US EV market increased by 11.2% as of May 2024.

As per the Korea Automobile & Mobility Association (KAMA), between January and May 2024, Hyundai Motor Group’s top brands in the US sold 48,383 EVs out of 437,246 electric passenger cars in the United States.
Hyundai Motor of America reported that its EV sales increased by 42% last month, partly thanks to the IONIQ 5.
Randy Parker, CEO of Hyundai Motor America, stated: “We continue seeing great success in our eco-friendly lineup with an overall 50% increase year over year.”

Randy Parker noted that both EVs and Hybrids continue to gain popularity with Hyundai’s newest HEV, the 2024 Santa Fe, gaining 116% year over year and their award-winning IONIQ 5 family increasing 82%.
Randy Parker announced that Hyundai has America’s Most Awarded EV Lineup in the industry.
Tesla delivered 480,126 vehicles in the second quarter of 2026, a 25 percent year-over-year jump that crushed Wall Street estimates of roughly 400,000–408,000 units.
Tesla's Q2 2026 production reached 451,758 vehicles, with Model 3 and Model Y accounting for the vast majority.
Tesla's strong Q2 deliveries ended two years of annual delivery declines and drew down inventory, signaling demand that outpaced earlier production.
Bears had warned that the expiration of the U.S. federal EV tax credit would hammer demand, arguing that without the $7,500 incentive, American buyers would balk at higher effective prices.
Rising gas prices provided a powerful tailwind for Tesla, especially in the U.S., amplified by geopolitical tensions tied to the Iran conflict pushing fuel costs higher earlier in the year.
Advances in Full Self-Driving (FSD) supervised software appear to have boosted Tesla's appeal, with expanded FSD availability in select European markets and continued system refinement.
For tech-oriented buyers, the promise of future autonomy and enhanced driver-assistance features adds perceived value beyond the car itself.
Tesla introduced lower-cost versions of the Model 3 and Model Y during Q2, widening accessibility without sacrificing core margins.
Attractive financing and leasing options helped Tesla convert interest into actual orders more effectively than many analysts expected.
Supported by government incentives, corporate fleet electrification, and easing political headwinds around CEO Elon Musk, Tesla saw stronger registration numbers throughout Europe.
Strong exports from the Shanghai Gigafactory and a production ramp at Giga Berlin ensured supply met resurgent European demand.
Corporate buyers accelerated transitions to EVs to meet sustainability targets, providing a steady volume base for Tesla.
A Texas man, Michael Butler, was arrested and charged with manslaughter after his Tesla crashed into a home in Harris County, Texas, killing a woman inside.
Butler claimed the vehicle was in self-driving mode, but Tesla data showed he overrode the system.
Butler was booked at the Harris County, Texas, jail and remained in custody through Thursday and Friday without entering a plea; his next court hearing is scheduled for Monday.
The woman who was killed’s family, the Avilas, filed a wrongful death lawsuit against Tesla and Butler, seeking at least $1 million in damages.
Charging documents from the Harris County prosecutor show Butler had been using Full Self-Driving mode without incident through multiple DoorDash deliveries the evening of the accident.

In the moments leading up to the crash, while approaching a left turn in FSD, Butler pressed the accelerator pedal, overriding FSD’s speed control, and pushed it to 100 percent, causing rapid acceleration.
The brake pedal was never pressed before the crash, and there is no data to show that Butler aimed to turn away from the curb or house.
No mechanical error was detected or recorded by the vehicle before Butler and the Tesla struck the curb.
Forensic analysis of Butler’s phone showed he searched Google around the time of the crash with queries questioning why FSD was “too timid” or “not aggressive enough.”
Multiple Google searches related to Tesla were made from Butler’s phone in the months leading up to the crash, including May 2026 searches indicating frustration with Tesla’s FSD mode.

Tesla had claimed just after the crash that its internal data showed Butler had overridden the system’s speed control and pressed the accelerator completely, causing the vehicle to travel at an excessive rate of speed.
The car slammed into Avila’s house, killing her.
Butler has now been formally charged with Manslaughter, a felony.
A Tesla Semi was involved in a fatal collision on U.S. Highway 50 in Dayton, Nevada, on Sunday, June 28, 2026, marking the first known fatal crash involving the electric Class 8 truck.
The incident occurred around 7:20 a.m. at the intersection with Traditions Parkway, approximately 40 miles east of Reno and close to Tesla’s Gigafactory Nevada.
A semi-truck struck two passenger vehicles stopped at a traffic signal from behind; two people were pronounced dead at the scene, and a third person suffered life-threatening injuries.
Preliminary statements suggested the truck driver may have fallen asleep at the wheel, but the Nevada Highway Patrol stated the official cause has not yet been determined.
The Tesla Semi is Tesla’s battery-electric heavy-duty truck, produced at the nearby Gigafactory in Nevada.
Authorities initially described the vehicle as a semi-truck; its make was subsequently confirmed through reporting and scene identification.
The investigation remains active with no further official details on contributing factors or vehicle systems released as of early July 2026.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group closes the gap with Tesla

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) celebrated its grand opening on October 3, 2024.
The grand opening marks the completion of Georgia’s largest economic development project.
Construction at HMGMA began two and a half years prior to the grand opening.
HMGMA is Hyundai’s third manufacturing facility in the U.S.
It joins existing Hyundai and Kia plants in Alabama and Georgia, respectively.
Euisun Chung, Executive Chair of Hyundai Motor Group, stated that HMGMA showcases advanced manufacturing capabilities and strong investment in partnerships with local communities in Georgia.
Chung noted that a rich legacy of craftsmanship and a skilled workforce at HMGMA are shaping the future of mobility in America.
The first vehicle, a Hyundai Ioniq 5, rolled off the production line on October 3, 2024.
Production of the Hyundai Ioniq 9, a three-row electric SUV, is also underway at HMGMA.
The plant will begin producing its first Kia model in 2026.
HMGMA is designed to manufacture electric and hybrid vehicles for Hyundai, Genesis, and Kia.
The plant is initially capable of producing up to 300,000 vehicles annually.
Hyundai Motor Group’s total investment in Georgia, including HMGMA and joint battery ventures with LG Energy Solution and SK On, amounts to $12.6 billion.
This $12.6 billion investment is the largest in Georgia’s history.
The investment is expected to create 8,500 jobs at HMGMA by 2031.
Overall, the Group’s investments in Georgia are expected to generate nearly 40,000 direct and indirect jobs.
These investments are expected to generate an annual income impact of $4.6 billion, according to the Center for Automotive Research.
As of July 2024, more than $2.5 billion in capital investment has been committed by HMGMA suppliers across 12 counties in Georgia.
This supplier investment is generating about 6,900 jobs.
Hyundai has already invested over $20.5 billion in the U.S.
Hyundai recently announced a further $21 billion investment between 2025 and 2028.
This new investment will support an annual production capacity of 1.2 million vehicles in the U.S.
The Group plans to increase HMGMA’s production capacity to 500,000 units annually.
This expansion represents an increase of 200,000 units from the original plan.
Hyundai’s U.S. production facilities in Alabama and Georgia will undergo upgrades to support this growth.
HMGMA marks a major milestone as Hyundai’s first dedicated facility for mass-producing electrified vehicles.
The plant is located in Bryan County, Georgia.
It integrates a highly connected, automated, and adaptable manufacturing system designed to link every component of the EV and hybrid ecosystem.
All production stages at the facility, from order collection and procurement to logistics and assembly, are optimized through AI and data analytics.
The plant fosters a human-centered work environment where robots collaborate with human workers to enhance productivity and safety.
Hyundai Motor Group has a diverse value chain spanning mobility, steel and construction, logistics, finance, IT, and services.
The Group employs approximately 250,000 people worldwide.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group Inaugurates New U.S. Plant | Machine Maker - Latest Manufacturing News | Indian Manufacturing News - Latest Manufacturing News | Indian Manufacturing News - Machine Maker

facts: An expansion by Hyundai TRANSYS near LaGrange will include an investment of more than $240 million and the creation of 678 jobs.
Gov. Brian Kemp announced on Dec. 1 that Hyundai TRANSYS will invest more than $240 million to expand operations in Georgia.
The investment will create 678 full-time, long-term positions at a new manufacturing facility in the West Point area.
Gov. Brian Kemp cited Georgia's No. 1 business climate, robust logistics network, and top-ranked workforce training programs as key factors attracting and retaining global companies.
Gov. Brian Kemp thanked Hyundai TRANSYS for its long-term commitment to Georgia and expressed hope that the investment will continue to benefit the company and West Georgia residents.
Hyundai TRANSYS is the only automobile manufacturing company in the world that specializes in transmissions.
The new 620,000-square-foot West Point plant will be located near the company's current location on the Kia campus.
The new plant will produce eight-speed transmissions for a global automotive leader.

Hyundai TRANSYS Georgia Powertrain President Sangkil Jung cited years of capital investment, employment, training support, and unmatched opportunities as reasons Georgia was a natural choice for expansion.
Sangkil Jung expressed excitement about expanding Hyundai TRANSYS's presence in the West Point community.
Hyundai TRANSYS will bring hundreds of advanced manufacturing jobs, along with management and office staff positions, to the local community.
The company plans to create an additional 165 supplementary construction handling and inspection jobs.
The expansion will bring Hyundai TRANSYS's total employment in Georgia to 1,741 jobs.
Individuals interested in career opportunities with Hyundai TRANSYS are encouraged to check the company's website for updated information.
West Point Mayor Steve Tramell expressed thrill over Hyundai TRANSYS's long-term investment in West Point and the creation of new jobs.
Mayor Tramell thanked the West Point Development Authority, the State of Georgia, and Troup County for their contributions to making the project a reality.
Hyundai TRANSYS has been a community partner with West Point and Troup County for over a decade.
Troup County was chosen by Hyundai TRANSYS as the location for its new manufacturing facility.
Troup County Chairman Patrick Crews stated the significant capital investment will strengthen the local economy and create economic opportunities for residents.
Chairman Crews described Georgia as having the best state environment for business and a community with a history of great leadership.
Chairman Crews called the project a team win resulting from combining leadership with resources.
Senior Project Manager Amanda Fields represented the GDEcD Global Commerce division on the project.
The project was developed in partnership with the West Point Development Authority, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that companies investing in Georgia become Georgia companies and will be supported for long-term growth.
Commissioner Wilson celebrated the partnership leading to more Georgians being employed by Hyundai TRANSYS.
Commissioner Wilson expressed gratitude for Hyundai TRANSYS choosing to invest in Georgia again.
Commissioner Wilson thanked Gov. Kemp and the General Assembly for supporting business recruitment and market expansion efforts.
Communities across Georgia, both urban and rural, continue to benefit from the state's international trade and investment.
In 2020, Georgia's international relationships facilitated more than $1 billion in investment and over 2,000 jobs from South Korean companies.
Georgia is home to more than 200 automotive-related facilities.
Georgia remains a national leader in advanced manufacturing, outpacing the U.S. in 10-year GDP growth for machinery, electrical equipment and components, and fabricated metals.
Hyundai TRANSYS produces critical automobile parts, including transmissions, axles, and seats.
The company produces all types of automatic transmissions, manual transmissions, dual clutch transmissions (DCT), and continuously variable transmissions.
Hyundai TRANSYS develops and produces seats for Genesis, Santa Fe, Sorento, and other models.
source_title: Hyundai TRANSYS expansion to bring 678 jobs to west Georgia | The Citizen

facts: A South Korean auto parts supplier will invest more than $240 million to build a transmission factory in western Georgia with plans to hire 678 workers.
Hyundai Transys announced plans for the new factory in West Point, adjoining an existing Hyundai Transys factory and a Kia Motors Corp factory.
Both plants are part of Hyundai Motor Group, the Korean auto conglomerate that sells cars under the Kia and Hyundai brands.
The new plant will build eight-speed transmissions.
The 620,000-square foot (58,000-square meter) plant will be part of the Kia campus just off Interstate 85 in West Point near the state line with Alabama.
Hyundai Transys was created by a merger of two Hyundai affiliates last year and also makes axles and vehicle seats.
The company has operations in South Korea, the United States, and seven other countries.
The investment will create 165 other jobs.
Its total employees in Georgia will rise above 1,700 once the new plant is complete.
West Point Mayor Steve Tramell stated he is absolutely thrilled that Hyundai Transys will make this long-term investment in West Point, but most importantly for the new jobs being created.
Many supplier plants are clustered near the Hyundai plant in Montgomery, Alabama, and the Kia plant in West Point, with others scattered around and between the two plants.
Kia, affiliates and suppliers had more than 4,300 employees in Georgia's Troup County in 2018, according to county financial documents.
It is the largest industrial announcement, by value of the investment, made so far this year in Georgia.

Hyundai TRANSYS Georgia Powertrain President Sangkil Jung said the level of trust built through years of capital investment, employment, and training support – along with new opportunities unmatched by any other states – made Georgia a natural choice.
Final incentives for Hyundai Transys have not been agreed on yet, according to Marie Hodge Gordon, spokesperson for the Georgia Economic Development Department.
The company will qualify for a state income tax credit that allows it to deduct $3,500 per job from its Georgia income taxes for five years.
