If Hyundai Transys doesn't incur $2.37 million in state income taxes each year, it can use the credit to get cash payments redirected to it out of money withheld from employee paychecks for state income taxes, given its location in a less-developed area.
Workers must make at least $28,000 a year for Hyundai Transys to qualify for tax credits.
The state will pay to train Hyundai Transys workers.
The state could chip in money to pay for infrastructure.
Hyundai will automatically qualify for tax breaks that will save it millions in sales and use taxes on machinery.
Local officials could also abate property taxes.
West Point's financial statements show that in 2018 Kia, its affiliates and suppliers were receiving $2.85 million in property tax breaks from the city alone.

Kia got subsidies and tax breaks worth more than $450 million to locate its plant in West Point that opened in 2009, with the state aiming to revitalize a region hit hard by the closure of textile and apparel plants.
Because the plant is only miles from the Alabama state line, a significant number of its workers live in that state.
source_title: Hyundai affiliate plans $240M transmission plant in Georgia

facts: LG Energy Solution and SK Innovation resolved an intellectual property dispute via mutual agreement on April 11, 2021.
The dispute resolution occurred prior to a deadline that would have required intervention by the U.S. president.
Jong Hyun Kim is CEO and president of LG Energy Solution.
Jun Kim is CEO and president of SK Innovation.
LG Energy Solution pledged to invest over $4.5 billion in its own U.S. business by 2025.
LG Energy Solution is close to finalizing a second joint venture plant with GM.
SK Battery America is constructing a $1.67 billion manufacturing facility near Commerce, Georgia, in Jackson County.
SK Battery America announced an additional $940 million investment this year (2021) to accommodate increasing demand.
One of SK Battery America's primary customers is VW in Chattanooga.
VW announced plans to expand its production site to assemble all-electric SUVs in 2022.
The International Trade Commission ruled on February 10 that SK Innovation had stolen trade secrets from LG Energy Solutions.
SK Innovation has vigorously denied the ITC claim as without basis.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp sought relief from the new U.S. president regarding the ITC ruling.
By law, President Biden has 60 days to alter or block the ITC ruling.
If the ruling stands, SK could make batteries for VW for two years and for Ford for four years before being banned from importing necessary components for 10 years.
A representative of SK Innovation stated the company will immediately enter discussions with customers Ford Motor Company and Volkswagen regarding commercial and operational implications.
SK Innovation believes the ITC ruling could have a serious adverse impact on President Biden’s policies to combat climate change and expand U.S. auto fleet electrification.
Construction on the SK plant in Commerce began nearly two years ago (relative to April 2021).
The SK plant is slated to start producing samples in the first half of 2021.
Mass production at the SK plant is expected to begin in the latter half of 2021.
SK Battery America expects to employ nearly 2,000 people by 2025.
Construction of SK Battery America's second plant began in July 2020.
The second SK plant is expected to start mass production by 2023.
Upon completion of both plants, SK Battery America will supply enough batteries for 310,000 vehicles (21.5GWh) annually.
SK Battery America's site selection in Commerce was driven by three factors: location, workforce access, and support from Team Georgia.
The Jefferson micropolitan area (Jackson County) is ranked No. 2 in the nation in the year’s Top Micropolitan rankings.
Georgia is situated at the center of the growing automobile industry in the Southeast, surrounded by Tennessee, Alabama, and South Carolina.
Accessibility to Atlanta-Hartsfield International Airport, the busiest airport in the world, enhances SK's location choice.
Growth of the Savannah Port and Port of Brunswick, plus future development of the Gainesville Inland Port, will improve logistical capabilities.
North Georgia provides access to 1.6 million workers within a 45-minute drive from the Commerce plant.
Atlanta and Athens are within reach for workforce access.
SK Battery America partners with Lanier Technical College, North Georgia Technical College, Athens Technical College, and Gwinnett Technical College for workforce development.
The Georgia Quickstart program trains SK's initial workforce with precision for immediate job start.
Georgia ranked among the country’s top 10 exporting states for the first time last year (2020).
Total trade reached $137.7 billion in 2020, spanning 221 countries and territories.
Georgia’s top five export markets in 2020 were Canada, Germany, China, Mexico, and Singapore.
Motor vehicles are Georgia’s No. 2 top exported product, representing $1.9 billion.
Pat Wilson is the Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner.
In 2020, Georgia saw more than $1 billion in foreign investments and more than 2,000 jobs from South Korean companies.
KB Autosys announced a $38 million investment in Lone Oak in Meriwether County in February 2021.
KB Autosys will build its first U.S. manufacturing facility in Georgia to meet product demand from General Motors, Hyundai, and Kia Motors.
Kia Motors’ plant in West Point, Georgia, is located just 30 minutes south on I-85 from Lone Oak.
Richard A. Kaatz is vice president of KB Autosys America.
Hyundai TRANSYS announced plans to invest more than $240 million in its West Point, Georgia, operations.
Hyundai TRANSYS produces eight-speed transmissions at the West Point facility.
The Hyundai TRANSYS expansion will create 678 full-time, long-term positions at a new 620,000-sq.-ft. (57,598-sq.-m.) manufacturing facility near its current Kia campus location.
This expansion will take Hyundai TRANSYS’ employment total in Georgia to 1,741 jobs.
Sangkil Jung is the Hyundai TRANSYS Georgia Powertrain President.
GEDIA Automotive Group announced plans in August 2020 to build an advanced manufacturing facility focused on EV parts.
GEDIA will invest $85 million in the project, delivering 200 jobs to the greater Dalton area.
GEDIA will build and occupy a 180,000-sq.-ft. (16,722-sq.-m.) facility on a Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development Certified property in Carbondale Business Park.
GEDIA operations are expected to begin in the third quarter of 2021.
GEDIA supplies lightweight structural automotive parts to VW, BMW, and Mercedes-Benz.
This investment marks GEDIA’s first site in the Southeast and its second facility in the United States.
GEDIA’s new facility will be located just down I-75 from VW’s Chattanooga plant.
Markus Schaumburg is one of two GEDIA Automotive Group CEOs.
source_title: Georgia Poised to Power Up the Southeast’s Electric Vehicle Market – Site Selection Magazine

facts: A Hyundai affiliate is investing more than $240 million and bringing hundreds of jobs to the West Point area as part of a new manufacturing facility.
Hyundai TRANSYS is an automobile manufacturing company that specializes in transmissions.
The new factory is located in West Point, near an existing Hyundai TRANSYS factory and a Kia Motors Corp. factory.
Both facilities are part of Hyundai Motor Group, the Korean auto conglomerate that sells cars under the Kia and Hyundai brands.
The facility will create 678 full-time long-term positions, according to a release from Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp’s office.
It is the largest industrial announcement by value of the investment made so far this year in Georgia, according to the Associated Press.

Sangkil Jung, president of Hyundai TRANSYS Georgia Powertrain, stated that trust built through years of capital investment, employment and training support – along with new opportunities unmatched by any other states – made Georgia a natural choice.
Sangkil Jung added that the company is excited for the opportunities ahead as it expands its presence in the West Point community.
The company plans to create 165 additional construction handling and inspection jobs.
Following the expansion, Hyundai TRANSYS will employ a total of 1,741 people in Georgia.
The new plant will build eight-speed transmissions.
The facility will be a 620,000-square foot plant.
The plant will be part of the Kia campus just off Interstate 85 in West Point near the state line with Alabama.

Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp stated that the state's No. 1 business climate, alongside its robust logistics network and top-ranked workforce training programs, are attracting global companies to the Peach State while retaining existing industries and helping them grow.
source_title: Hyundai to expand operations, add jobs near Columbus, GA | Columbus Ledger-Enquirer

facts: Hyundai Motor Group will use solar energy to power its electric vehicle plant currently under construction in Georgia.
Hyundai has signed a 147-megawatt power purchase agreement (PPA) with Matrix Renewables.
The carmaker will receive electricity generated from Matrix Renewables' 210-megawatt solar power project located in Texas.
The Texas solar power project is slated to begin operations in October 2025.
This agreement represents the largest renewable deal a Korean company has ever signed in the United States.
Hyundai plans to procure 378 gigawatt hours of solar energy annually under the deal.
The procured solar energy is enough to power about 100,000 households each year.
The solar electricity will be used for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
HMGMA is scheduled to start commercial operations in October 2025.
The solar electricity will also be used for three affiliate local plants: Hyundai Mobis North America Electrified Powertrain, Hyundai Steel Georgia, and Hyundai Transys Georgia Powertrain.
The deal is expected to enable the automotive group to reduce 140,000 tons of carbon emissions.
This emission reduction is equivalent to the annual emissions from around 84,000 mid-sized sedans.
Hyundai stated in a press release that the agreement is significant as it represents the group's substantial move towards securing renewable energy sources.
source_title: Hyundai Motor, Matrix Renewables sign solar energy deal for EV plant in US | Aju Press

facts: Xtract One Technologies announced its SmartGateway security screening solution was selected to protect entrances at Hyundai Transys in West Point, Ga.
The SmartGateway will be deployed at the manufacturing plant to protect workers and staff from weapons entering the buildings and to prevent mass casualty events.
Jinho Kim, manager of GA & EHS at Hyundai Transys Georgia Powertrain Inc., stated they viewed and tested Xtract One’s Gateway at Kia Georgia Inc., which had great success with keeping weapons out.
Jinho Kim noted they were impressed by the quick and accurate detection of weapons and felt the customization aspect would fit seamlessly into their manufacturing facility.
SmartGateway is the latest iteration of Xtract One's Gateway screening solution.

It is a fast, frictionless entry screener used by manufacturing plants, casinos, and large arenas to unobtrusively detect guns, knives, and other potential weapons on people entering without requiring them to empty pockets of personal items.
Peter Evans, CEO of Xtract One, stated auto manufacturers, their supply chains, and large workplaces are well suited for the Gateway solution due to high worker throughput during shift changes.
This marks Xtract One's third customer in the auto manufacturing industry.
Modern walk-through metal detector systems are inefficient by modern standards, less secure, and more intrusive than the Gateway system.
SmartGateway uses AI-powered scanners to detect concealed guns and knives and alerts security personnel to conduct a secondary screening.
If people are not carrying weapons, they simply walk through without needing to empty pockets or other personal articles.
Upgrades to the system have made SmartGateway very easy to learn and use, meaning it requires fewer personnel to operate relative to other scanning systems.
The system is customizable to fit the security needs and requirements of individual facilities, including look and feel.
Ryan Smith, Program Manager – Security at Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia, Inc., recommended Xtract One to Hyundai Transys after being pleased with the Gateway solutions at their facilities.
Since implementing the solution at Kia Georgia, they have prevented a significant number of weapons from entering the facility.
Personnel screening time was drastically reduced from 21 seconds to 2.5 seconds.
The time reduction provides production flexibility to maintain work areas during crucial times in an industry based on just-in-time processes.
Xtract One Technologies is a leading technology-driven threat detection and security solution leveraging AI to provide seamless and secure patron access control experiences.
The company makes unobtrusive threat detection systems that enable venue building operators to prioritize and deliver improved patron experiences while providing unprecedented safety.
Xtract One’s multi-sensor Gateway product enables companies to covertly screen for weapons at points of entry without disrupting the flow of traffic.

AI-based Xtract Vision allows venue and building operators to identify weapons and other threats inside and outside of facilities, and Xtract One Insights provides valuable intelligence for optimizing operations.
source_title: Xtract One Technologies Selected by Hyundai Transys to Secure Facilities and Keep Weapons Out - Xtract One

facts: Hyundai has not canceled plans to complete the electric vehicle battery plant in Georgia but has recommitted to the project and broadened its agreement.
At its CEO Investor Day and a prior press release, Hyundai announced a sweeping expansion to production and an overall investment of $26 million over three years.
CEO José Muñoz outlined Hyundai Motor's goals to grow in the global market while speaking in New York.
Hyundai announced a $5 billion battery joint venture in Cartersville, Georgia.
Hyundai plans expansions to the Kia Corp. plant in West Point, Georgia.
Hyundai is building a steel mill in Donaldsonville, Louisiana.
Hyundai is establishing a robotics hub and expanding its plant in Montgomery, Alabama.
In a press release ahead of Investor Day, Hyundai announced a reduction to its 2025 budget citing a current tariff rate of 25%.
Hyundai's new operating profit margin will be approximately 7%, with a goal to raise it back to approximately 9% by 2030.
The 25% tariff is directly affecting Hyundai's profitability by raising costs on cars and components not made domestically, as only about 40% of its U.S. sales are currently made in the U.S.
Hyundai plans rapid growth at its Ellabell Georgia "Metaplant" with a target of producing 500,000 vehicles a year, including hybrids and EVs.
Hyundai plans to introduce a midsize truck for the first time ever.
Hyundai plans to offer 18 hybrid models globally.
Hyundai plans to launch extended-range EVs in 2027.
Hyundai plans to release several US specific offerings.
Hyundai will have a 10 model lineup at the Georgia facility.
For Phase 1 in 2025, Hyundai raised its revenue growth target and adjusted operating profit.
For Phase 1 in 2025, Hyundai announced a KRW 77.3 trillion ( $55.66 billion in USD) investment by 2030.
For Phase 1 in 2025, Hyundai plans to roll out a Software-Defined Vehicle (SDV) platform and High-Performance Vehicle Computer.
For Phase 1 in 2026, Hyundai's Genesis brand will introduce hybrid models.
For Phase 1 in 2026, Hyundai will launch an all-new Palisade Hybrid.
For Phase 1 in 2026, Hyundai will launch a cloud-based battery management system.
For Phase 1 in 2026, Hyundai will open a plant in Saudi Arabia.
For Phase 1 in 2027, Hyundai plans the debut of its first Extended Range electric vehicle (EREV).
For Phase 2 in 2028, Hyundai will expand at Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
For Phase 2 in 2028, Hyundai will add 200,000 units of annual capacity.
For Phase 2 in 2028, Hyundai will create 3,000 new jobs.
For Phase 2 in 2028, Hyundai will reach 500,000 a year hybrid and EV vehicles.
For Phase 2 in 2030, Hyundai aims to reach a target of 55.5 million vehicles sold globally.
For Phase 2 in 2030, Hyundai plans to expand hybrid choices to 18 or more.
For Phase 2 in 2030, Hyundai will announce its first midsize pickup truck.
Despite an ICE raid at the Georgia plant, President Trump stated on his Truth Social platform that foreign workers are "welcome" and that the U.S. should take cues from other high-tech industries.
source_title: Reality check: Is the GA Hyundai plant closing or expanding after ICE raid?

facts: UAE-based real estate giant EMAAR Group signed a memorandum of understanding to invest approximately $6 billion in Georgia.

The agreement was signed in the UAE by Levan Davitashvili, Georgia's Minister of Economy; Tornike Rijvadze, Chairman of the Government of Adjara; and Mohammed Ali Rashed Alabbar, Founder and Managing Director of EMAAR Group.
The investment will fund a multifunctional development project creating infrastructure in Tbilisi, Batumi, and Gonio.
Georgian Prime Minister Irakli Kobakhidze led the delegation for the signing.
Prime Minister Irakli Kobakhidze stated the project will significantly contribute to economic development, accelerate growth rates, create new jobs, and attract more investors.
Mohammed Ali Rashed Alabbar stated the investment will motivate many other investors and businessmen in Georgia, mirroring their track record in other countries.
Minister of Economy Levan Davitashvili stated EMAAR's entry will completely change the Georgian real estate market and elevate it to a qualitatively different level.
The $6 billion investment is expected to drive economic growth of approximately 2-3% for Tbilisi and Batumi within a year.
The historic investment represents about 20% of Georgia's economy.
The project is expected to attract additional investments and significantly boost the country's overall economic growth.
source_title: UAE's EMAAR Group to Invest $6 Billion in Georgia | EurAsia Gulf Business Platform

facts: Georgia Ports Authority hosted their 57th annual Georgia International Trade Conference bringing together customers, business partners and industry leaders to discuss the latest maritime and logistics developments.
Governor Brian Kemp met with Georgia Ports customers and logistics partners during the conference.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that innovation and port investments by the Georgia Ports Authority are helping to keep our state No. 1 for business while also positioning us as a true leader in global trade, and pledged to continue finding ways to further their success and reach.

Georgia Ports Chairman Alec Poitevint thanked the International Longshoremen’s Association, Gateway Terminals Savannah, the Georgia Department of Transportation, the Georgia Department of Economic Development, and other members in the Georgia supply chain for their valuable contributions.

Georgia Ports President and CEO Griff Lynch stated that customers require greater supply chain predictability and cost savings opportunities, and that the port has the capacity and connectivity to respond quickly to disruptions and market fluctuations around the world.

New findings from Georgia Tech researchers show using the Port of Savannah saves shippers more than $1,000 per container to Atlanta, Memphis and Nashville compared to West Coast gateways, along with more predictable transit times.

Chris Gaffney, managing director of the Supply Chain and Logistics Institute at Georgia Tech, noted that routing cargo through Savannah provides a clear economic advantage compared with West Coast routes by reducing congestion exposure, saving shippers money, and delivering more consistent, predictable transit performance.
80 percent of Savannah’s cargo moves inland by truck and 20 percent by rail.
Rail is increasingly attractive beyond 250 miles from the port due to the cost of diesel fuel.

Savannah’s supply chain velocity features 40 container ships a week calling Savannah, 42 doublestack trains a week connecting to inland markets, with the lowest rail dwell in the nation of 20 hours or less.
The port handles 14,000-16,000 truck gate moves a day with dual moves taking less than 50 minutes, and single moves 30 minutes.
Georgia Ports Authority will be opening the Gainesville Inland Port on May 4th with daily rail service via Norfolk Southern.
In the first year of operation at the Gainesville Inland Port, 26,000 truck moves are expected to transition to rail and off Atlanta highways.
Georgia Ports’ 10-year plan will see five new big ship berths added, reflecting a 54 percent growth in container throughput with Ocean Terminal and Savannah Container Terminal.
Rail service will be a major differentiator as a gateway port serving growth to inland consumer and manufacturing markets.
The Mason Mega Rail facility had a record year in 2025, handling 591,000 containers.

On April 15, the Georgia Ports Authority submitted a Letter of Intent to Adam Telle, the Assistant Secretary of the Army for Civil Works (ASACW), to formally begin the study process to further deepen and widen the Savannah Harbor.

The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers encourages non—federal sponsors to undertake feasibility studies themselves in line with Section 203 of the Water Resources Development Act of 1986 as a way to accelerate dredging timelines and save taxpayer funds.

Griff Lynch stated they are excited to kick off this first step with the Army Corps of Engineers on the Savannah Harbor deepening and widening feasibility study, with the goal of being the benchmark for Army Corps of Engineering deepening projects.

Port officials said the shipping channel needs to be improved to better serve the largest ships currently serving Savannah, and to prepare for even larger, more efficient vessels expected to serve the U.S. East Coast in the future.
The feasibility study will consider deepening the channel and adding passing lanes for two-way ship traffic.
The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers will provide technical assistance and will retain final review and approval authority, including oversight of environmental and economic analyses.

The Georgia Department of Transportation (GDOT) will begin construction on a cable replacement project for the Talmadge Bridge over the Savannah River, which will remain operational during the project.
The Talmadge Bridge work will shorten the bridge’s cables, increasing air draft beneath the bridge from 185 feet to about 205 feet, allowing larger vessels to pass, with completion expected in 2029.

In August 2026, GDOT will open the $126 million Brampton Road Connector, a four-lane highway providing a direct link between the Port of Savannah’s Garden City Terminal and the interstate system, removing at-grade rail crossings and truck traffic from local neighborhoods.
Separately, GDOT plans to widen Interstate 95 by one lane in each direction along a 22-mile stretch from Exit 90 to the South Carolina state line, with the project currently in preliminary design.
The department also plans to reconstruct and widen portions of Interstate 16 west of I-95 toward State Route 67 in Bulloch County.
This $522 million project will expand I-16 to three lanes in both directions, with construction expected to begin in 2027.

Griff Lynch stated that all the pieces are coming together in Savannah as the gateway of choice for the Southeast region, with five container berths in the pipeline, a new Gainesville Inland Port, multiple GDOT infrastructure improvements, and Georgia Ports ready for the next 10 years with its $5 billion investment plan as the fastest growing port in the Southeast.
The port serves 70% of the nation’s population east of the Mississippi River.
source_title: GA: Supply Chain Predictability, Cost-Saving Strategies Highlight Georgia Ports Trade Conference | Trade and Industry Development

facts: At least one Korean worker detained in a huge ICE immigration raid on a Hyundai Motor factory site in Georgia last week was living and working legally in the US with a valid visa.
ICE officials mandated that the legal worker agree to be removed from the US despite not violating his visa.
Immigration officials were aware that someone with a valid visa was among those arrested during the raid at the Hyundai factory and taken to ICE detention for removal proceedings.
The arrested detainees remained in ICE detention on Tuesday before expected deportation flights back to South Korea.
A leaked internal ICE agent document reports on the detained worker's case, obtained exclusively by the Guardian.
The detained man arrived in the US in June and was redacted from the document because he could not be reached directly and his legal representation status is unclear.
Immigration agents from Atlanta determined the man entered with a valid B1/B2 visa and was employed at HL-GA Battery Company LLC as a contractor from the South Korean company SFA.
Law enforcement database queries confirmed the man had not violated his visa, but the Atlanta Field Office Director mandated he be presented for Voluntary Departure.
The man accepted voluntary departure despite not violating B1/B2 visa requirements.

Georgia-based immigration attorney Charles Kuck, representing several arrested individuals, called detaining a valid visa holder illegal and described the situation as an 'actual crime' committed by the government.
Kuck called the detention 'outrageous.'
The leaked document contradicts ICE claims that all 475 people arrested during the raid were working illegally or violating their visas.
Attorneys representing detainees claimed immigrants with valid working status were swept up alongside those allegedly working unlawfully and placed in removal proceedings.
An anonymous agency official backed up the claim that legal immigrants were swept up, calling the arrest 'illegal.'
The government maintains the detainee acted unlawfully by admitting to unauthorized work on a B1/B2 visa.
It remains unclear whether other people with valid visas were detained or how many were actually alleged to be working illegally at the factory.
Approximately 300 arrested people could be put on flights back to South Korea as early as Wednesday, while non-Koreans arrested are expected to remain in ICE detention.
Last Thursday, ICE led a raid at the Hyundai battery factory under construction in Ellabell, near Savannah, Georgia, arresting nearly 500 people, the majority from South Korea.
The arrested workers were part of a $12.6bn investment program in Georgia by Hyundai.
Construction on the plant was halted due to the raid.
The plant is designed to supply batteries for electric vehicles.
A DHS spokesperson stated the individual admitted to unauthorized work and accepted voluntary departure, contradicting the leaked document's account.
DHS reiterated its statement when pressed for clarification, while ICE did not respond to requests for comment about legal workers being arrested.
The man entered the US with a valid B1/B2 visa, which allows for some business-related activities and tourism, and worked at the factory as a contractor for SFA.
Kuck noted that 'voluntarily' in immigration means leaving under ICE custody with consequences including loss of visa and possible inability to return to the US.
After the raid, additional ICE officers and agents were sent to the detention facility to process the sheer number of people arrested.

Officials say the arrested people violated immigration laws, but many with valid legal status were offered voluntary departure, with no clear legal mechanism to remove those who refuse if they are not in violation.
There is no suggestion that the Korean man in question has a criminal record in the US.
The official suggested the arrest might be a way to push up arrest numbers and cover up mistakes.
The raid angered the South Korean government, which had announced billions of dollars of investment in the US following a new trade deal between the countries.
On Sunday, the South Korean and US governments negotiated a deal to take the arrested workers home.

Samantha Hamilton, a litigation attorney with Asian Americans Advancing Justice-Atlanta, stated the workers were put in incredibly vulnerable positions simply by taking work opportunities on an ambitious international project.
DHS and ICE did not respond to requests for comment on allegations that they are pressuring the detainees to agree to be deported.
source_title: Leaked Ice document shows worker detained in Hyundai raid had valid visa | ICE (US Immigration and Customs Enforcement) | The Guardian

facts: Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp is visiting Germany on an economic development mission, his second official trip abroad since taking office last year.
The Republican governor departed on Tuesday and plans to return this weekend.
One of Kemp’s first planned stops is a ribbon cutting at the state’s new European office in Munich.
Kemp also plans to visit several German companies with a presence in Georgia.
Kemp is accompanied by his wife, first lady Marty Kemp.

Kemp stated: “Marty and I are honored to travel to Germany - Georgia’s fourth largest export market and home to our European office - to reaffirm our strategic partnership and strengthen business ties across multiple industry sectors.”
In 2018, exports to Germany from Georgia totaled $2.3 billion.
In 2018, imports from Germany to Georgia totaled $9.8 billion.

Georgia Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated: “Georgia’s official presence in Europe for more than 45 years has led to investments from some of Germany’s largest corporations, and these strategic investments have paid off.”
Kemp previously trekked to South Korea in June for his first economic development trip abroad as governor.
source_title: Kemp in Germany for economic development mission

facts: Immigration authorities detained 475 people during a raid on the sprawling manufacturing site in Georgia where Korean automaker Hyundai makes electric vehicles.
The raid was led by Steven Schrank, lead Georgia agent of Homeland Security Investigations, and resulted from a monthslong investigation into allegations of illegal hiring at the site.
The operation was described as the "largest single site enforcement operation" in the agency's two-decade history.
The raid targeted one of Georgia's largest manufacturing sites where Hyundai Motor Group began manufacturing electric vehicles a year ago at a $7.6 billion plant.
The site employs about 1,200 people in an area about 25 miles (40 kilometers) west of Savannah where bedroom communities bleed into farms.
Gov. Brian Kemp and other officials have touted the site as Georgia's largest economic development project.
Agents focused their operation on an adjacent plant still under construction where Hyundai has partnered with LG Energy Solution to produce batteries that power EVs.
The South Korean government expressed "concern and regret" over the operation targeting its citizens.
South Korean Foreign Ministry spokesperson Lee Jaewoong stated that business activities of investors and rights of nationals must not be unjustly infringed during U.S. law enforcement.
The South Korean ministry is dispatching diplomats from its embassy in Washington and consulate in Atlanta to the site and planning to form an on-site response team.

Immigration attorney Charles Kuck said two of his detained clients arrived from South Korea under a visa waiver program that enables them to travel for tourism or business for stays of 90 days or less without obtaining a visa.
One detained client had been in the U.S. for a couple of weeks, while the other had been in the country for about 45 days.

Schrank told reporters that while some detained workers illegally crossed the U.S. border, others had entered legally but had expired visas or had entered on a visa waiver that prohibited them from working.
Some of those detained worked for the battery manufacturer, while others were employed by contractors and subcontractors at the construction site.
Schrank said he didn't know precisely how many of the 475 detained were Korean nationals, but that they made up a majority.
No one has yet been charged with any crimes, and the investigation is ongoing.
Most of the detainees were taken to an immigration detention center in Folkston, Georgia, near the Florida state line.
The Hyundai site sits on 3,000 acres (1,214 hectares) in a largely rural area of Bryan County, drawing in workers from several surrounding counties and communities including Savannah.
Hyundai began producing electric vehicles at the site last September.

Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chairman Euisun Chung credited President Trump with the company's decision to create more American jobs by building an EV factory in Georgia during a White House appearance.
Chung stated the decision to invest in Savannah, Georgia, creating more than 8,500 American jobs, was initiated during his meeting with President Trump in Seoul in 2019.
The battery plant operated by HL-GA Battery Co., a joint venture by Hyundai and LG Energy Solution, is slated to open next year.
LG said it was closely monitoring the situation and gathering all relevant details, and couldn't immediately confirm how many of its employees or Hyundai workers had been detained.
LG stated its top priority is always ensuring the safety and well-being of its employees and partners, and that it will fully cooperate with the relevant authorities.
Operations at Hyundai's EV manufacturing plant were not interrupted by the raid, according to plant spokesperson Bianca Johnson.
Hyundai Motor Company stated it was working to understand the specific circumstances of the raid and detentions.
Hyundai Motor Company stated that as of the report date, none of those detained are directly employed by Hyundai Motor Company.
HL-GA Battery Co. did not immediately respond to a request for comment but previously stated it's cooperating fully with appropriate authorities.

The Democratic Party of Georgia condemned the raid, with chair Charlie Bailey calling it politically-motivated fear tactics designed to terrorize people who work hard for the economy and communities across Georgia.
Kemp and other Georgia Republican officials issued statements saying all employers in the state were expected to follow the law.
source_title: Homeland security official says 475 people were detained during an immigration raid in Georgia | National | smdailyjournal.com

facts: Kia provided a $1 million gift to the UGA College of Engineering and the Georgia Network for Electric Mobility.
The University of Georgia established the Georgia Network for Electric Mobility (GNEM) in early 2023.
GNEM focuses on enhancing research, education, and outreach opportunities in e-mobility across the state of Georgia.
GNEM brings together industry, academia, government, and communities to aid workforce development, economic development, and research.
The network takes special care to involve rural and underserved communities and small businesses in its initiatives.

The million-dollar commitment will provide endowed support for undergraduate scholarships, graduate research fellowships, community engagement programs, and experiential learning opportunities related to e-mobility.
Experiential learning opportunities supported by the gift include co-ops, internships, and capstone projects for students in the College of Engineering.
Kia established a state-of-the-art production facility in West Point, Georgia in 2009.
Since 2009, Kia Georgia has helped create more than 14,000 jobs at its West Point facility.
Since 2009, Kia Georgia has produced more than 4.2 million vehicles at its West Point facility.
One of the vehicles soon to be produced at the West Point facility will be Kia’s all-electric vehicle, the EV9.
As a founding partner of GNEM, Kia is providing support in perpetuity for educational pursuits, research productivity, and experiential learning efforts related to electric mobility careers.
Kia was a premier sponsor of the University of Georgia’s third annual Electric Mobility Summit, which took place April 23-24.
The Electric Mobility Summit focused on the adoption of electric mobility in Georgia, educational programs to support industry growth, and the future of e-mobility in Georgia and beyond.

UGA President Jere W. Morehead stated that the university is thrilled to partner with Kia on the electric mobility initiative to advance technology, help students become leaders, and strengthen Georgia’s economy.
Kia North America President and CEO Sean Yoon stated that Kia is committed to future e-mobility initiatives promoting sustainable transportation solutions in Georgia and across the nation.

Kia Georgia President and CEO Stuart Countess stated that programs like GNEM are timely and critical for establishing a cohesive strategy for development and implementation of new initiatives within Georgia.
College of Engineering Dean Donald Leo described Kia’s support as a testament to the power of collaboration between industry and academia.
The gift aligns with UGA’s land-grant mission to serve all populations within Georgia by expanding GNEM’s impact on rural and underserved communities.
source_title: Kia commits $1M to UGA's initiative on e-mobility

facts: South Korea's Kia Motors Corp said March 13 it would set up a $1.2 billion plant in Georgia, as its parent Hyundai Motor Group seeks to globalize its production base.
Located in West Point, Troup County, Georgia, Kia Motors' first U.S. plant will be completed by 2009 with an annual capacity of 300,00 units.
It will employ approximately 2,500 local workers.
Five or six suppliers are expected to set up operations in Troup County and the surrounding areas.
The supplier presence is expected to result in the creation of an additional 2,000 jobs.
Kia chose West Point over rival sites because of its proximity to a Hyundai plant in Alabama, some 135 kilometers (85 miles) away.
The Hyundai Motor plant in Alabama opened last year.
The new plant is part of Hyundai Motor Group's efforts to triple its overseas production to three million units per year, more than 40% of its total production.
Georgia promised various incentives worth $410 million including free land and infrastructure, subsidies for the creation of new jobs, financial support for training workers and tax breaks.
Kia expects its North American sales to climb 15% to 350,000 units this year.
Kia expects its North American sales to further grow to 800,000 by 2010.
Its total 2005 sales stood at 1.22 million units, including 266,000 in South Korea.
It has more than 32,500 employees at 12 manufacturing and assembly operations in seven countries.
Its network of distributors and dealers covers 160 countries.
Kia is building plants in China and Slovakia as well, with an annual production capacity of 300,000 units each.
source_title: Kia Motor To Build First U.S. Plant In Georgia | IndustryWeek

facts: Kia donated $1 million to the University of Georgia College of Engineering and the Georgia Network for Electric Mobility.

The donation will provide endowed support for undergraduate scholarships, graduate research fellowships, community engagement programs, and experiential learning opportunities such as co-ops, internships, and capstone projects related to e-mobility.
Kia's gift also supports the Georgia Network for Electric Mobility (GNEM), which was established by UGA in early 2023.
GNEM brings together industry, academia, government, and communities to aid workforce development, economic development, and research across the state.
The network takes special care to involve rural and underserved communities and small businesses in its initiatives.
As a founding partner of GNEM, Kia is providing support for educational pursuits, research productivity, and experiential learning efforts in perpetuity.
In 2009, Kia established a state-of-the-art production facility in West Point, Georgia.
Since opening in 2009, Kia Georgia has helped create more than 14,000 jobs at the West Point facility.
Since opening in 2009, Kia Georgia has produced more than 4.2 million vehicles at its West Point facility.
One of the vehicles soon to be produced at the West Point facility will be Kia’s all-electric vehicle, the EV9.

UGA President Jere W. Morehead stated that the university is thrilled to partner with Kia on the electric mobility initiative and grateful for support advancing the technology and strengthening Georgia's economy.
Kia North America President and CEO Sean Yoon said Kia is committed to future e-mobility initiatives promoting sustainable transportation solutions in Georgia and across the nation.

Kia Georgia President and CEO Stuart Countess noted that programs like GNEM are timely and critical for establishing a cohesive strategy for development and implementation of new initiatives within Georgia as Kia moves into a new era of electric vehicles.
College of Engineering Dean Donald Leo described Kia's support as a testament to the power of collaboration between industry and academia.
Kia was a premier sponsor of UGA’s third annual Electric Mobility Summit, which took place April 23-24.
The summit focused on the adoption of electric mobility in the state, educational programs to support industry growth, and the future of electric mobility in Georgia and beyond.
source_title: Kia commits $1M to UGA’s initiative on e-mobility - College of Engineering

facts: South Korean automaker Kia plans to invest over $200 million in its assembly plant in Georgia.
Kia will begin production of the electric EV9 SUV at the Georgia plant next year.
Foreign automakers are increasing North American production to qualify for federal EV tax credits.
Vehicle assembly in North America is required for eligibility for up to $7,500 in federal EV tax credits under the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA).
The Inflation Reduction Act was signed in August 2022.
Hyundai Motor Group, which owns Kia, is working with battery manufacturer SK On to build a new battery-manufacturing plant in Georgia.
SK Innovation has opened a $2.6 billion battery plant in Georgia to produce batteries for Ford.
Kia will add about 200 jobs at the Georgia plant to build the EV9 SUV and four other models.
The EV9 will be Kia's first EV assembled in the United States.
Kia aims to hit $122 billion in gross revenue by 2030 as part of its push to increase EV sales.
Kia's electrification strategy is backed by a $24 billion investment.
Kia aims to sell 1 million EVs by 2026 as part of its electrification strategy.
Kia aims to begin operating its first dedicated EV factory in Gwangmyeong in 2024.
Kia plans to release 15 new EV models by 2027.
Kia first revealed the EV9 SUV in March.
The EV9 is considered well-suited for the North American market due to its affordability, size, and range.
The EV9 can act as a mobile power source.
The EV9 features an array of sensors including cameras, lidar, and radar to enable an advanced driver assistance system.
The EV9 represents a brand shift for Kia from a reputation for cheap airport rentals to a high-tech company.
The EV9's infotainment and software suite enables over-the-air updates for nearly every function on the car.
Over-the-air updates for nearly every function are a first for Kia.
source_title: Kia to invest $200M in Georgia plant to build EV9 SUV | TechCrunch

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp and the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) announced sustained momentum for business recruitment and expansions in Fiscal Year 2024.
Between July 1, 2023, and June 30, 2024, GDEcD’s Global Commerce team supported 429 facility expansions and new locations statewide.
These efforts resulted in more than $20.3 billion in investment and the commitment of 26,900 new private sector jobs statewide.
83% of expansions and new locations are located in communities outside the 10-county Atlanta region.
Rural Georgia expansions account for more than 18,300 new jobs and $12.5 billion in investment.
Metro Atlanta attracted nearly $7.8 billion in investments as a logistics and business hub.
Statewide, new locations account for $12.8 billion in investments, while expansions are responsible for 9,800 new jobs.

Lt. Governor Burt Jones stated that the Technical College System of Georgia, University System of Georgia, College and Career Academies, and private-public partnerships are building Georgia’s workforce pipeline with their workforce training programs.
During FY2024, Governor and First Lady Kemp led economic development missions to Korea and Switzerland focusing on strengthening relationships with Hyundai Motor Group and its supplier network.
In the prior year, Governor and First Lady Kemp led similar missions to Israel, France, and Switzerland.
Georgia landed more than 8,100 new jobs and $5.9 billion in investments from international companies expanding or locating in the U.S. market during FY2024.
The State of Georgia celebrated more than 50 years of continuous business and trade representation in Europe and Japan, which remain among the top sources of foreign direct investment.
The coming fiscal year will mark 40 years of continuous representation in Korea, a partnership that earned GDEcD the James A. Van Fleet Award from the Korea Society.
Markets where Georgia maintains business and trade representation accounted for 29% of total new jobs and investments in FY2024.
Manufacturing, including aerospace, automotive, and e-mobility sectors, accounted for over 50% of expansions or new locations in FY2024.
Kia Georgia added the EV9 to its lineup at its facility in Troup County.
Toyota Industries Corporation established a campus in Jackson County.
StandardAero operates facilities in Richmond County.
International life sciences company Gerresheimer announced a recent expansion in Fayette County.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America suppliers announced new locations in Bryan, Candler, Chatham, Coweta, Effingham, Harris, and Laurens Counties.
SOLARCYCLE, an innovator in solar panel recycling, announced plans for a new location in Polk County to support Qcells’ operations.
Investments in food processing and agribusiness totaled more than $1.3 billion in FY2024.
Japanese probiotic beverage company Yakult located in Bartow County.
Korean bakery CJ Foodville located in Hall County.
Walmart will source milk from local dairy farmers at a future dairy processing facility in Lowndes County.
Technology and corporate hubs are creating at least 4,400 new jobs as a result of FY2024 wins.
Cargill Corporation is establishing a new office hub in the City of Atlanta.
U.S. Soccer’s future headquarters and national training center will be located in Fayette County.
Georgia has been ranked the No. 1 state for business by Area Development magazine for 10 consecutive years.
Georgia earned the No. 1 spot in Site Selection magazine’s annual Prosperity Cup rankings for successful investment recruitment in 2023.
Georgia was awarded a Gold Shovel by Area Development for achieving significant success in attracting new business investment and job creation in 2023.
Business Facilities magazine ranked Georgia as the No. 1 state for EV investments.
Business Facilities magazine ranked Georgia as a top 10 state for food processing, cybersecurity, aerospace and defense, and AI growth hubs.

source_title: Georgia Job Creation Remains Strong | AM 1180 Radio

facts: Hyundai Motor Group is strengthening its push into the US market by adding Kia's (000270.KS) Sportage to production at its American flagship plant, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
Kia plans to expand its annual US production to 550,000 units by 2030.
Kia held a ceremony on Tuesday local time at the Metaplant in Ellabell, Georgia, to mark the start of Sportage Hybrid production.
HMGMA is a plant Hyundai Motor (005380.KS) Group completed in March last year to target the US market.
The Metaplant had been producing Hyundai electric vehicles such as the Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9 before this expansion.
This marks the first time a Kia hybrid model has been added to HMGMA's production mix.
After the Donald Trump administration scaled back EV support, HMGMA's output fell sharply.
The group broadened the plant's product mix with a hybrid model in response to reduced EV support.
HMGMA's utilization rate dropped from 65.3% at the end of last year to 38.2% in the first quarter of this year.
Hyundai Motor plans to lift productivity by raising the level of automation at the HMGMA plant.
At the ceremony, Hyundai showcased its automation capabilities by using an autonomous mobile robot (AMR) to move the first Sportage produced at the plant onto the stage.
HMGMA expands Kia's US manufacturing footprint beyond its existing Georgia plant.
Through the two plants, Kia plans to raise its local production capacity to 550,000 units by 2030.
The planned capacity represents more than 57% above the current level of about 350,000 units a year.

Yoon Seung-kyu, president of Kia's North American Region and Kia America, stated that the Metaplant is Kia's second major investment in Georgia and that they highly value Georgia's continued growth potential as a hub of the auto industry.

Yoon Seung-kyu noted that the Sportage Hybrid, whose enhanced model was unveiled last year, is Kia's best-selling model and expects production at HMGMA to further strengthen Kia's growth in the United States.
source_title: Kia to Build Sportage at Metaplant, Targeting 550,000 US Output by 2030 - Seoul Economic Daily

facts: Kia Georgia team members marked a historic day at its West Point facility on Tuesday with the start of production of the all-new 2027 Kia Telluride.
The ceremonial vehicle represented two other major milestones: the first hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) assembled in the state of Georgia and the 5 millionth vehicle produced at Kia Georgia.
A ceremony for the milestone was attended by Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp, team members, and state/community leaders.

Sean Yoon, President and CEO of Kia North America and Kia America, stated that as the only plant in the world that builds Telluride, the launch reflects the strength of the West Point team, customer trust, and Kia's commitment to building the future of mobility in Georgia.
Reaching five million vehicles and introducing Kia's first US-assembled HEV is a testament to Kia's history and direction going forward, according to Yoon.
Since beginning production in 2009, Kia Georgia has grown into one of the region’s largest employers and a cornerstone of automotive manufacturing in the Southeast.
The Telluride is one of the industry’s most awarded vehicles, has become a flagship model for the brand, and is a symbol of quality built in West Point.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp stated that since its introduction in 2019, the Telluride has been exclusively Georgia-made and has become one of the most successful vehicles in Kia’s lineup.
Governor Kemp attributed the Telluride's success to a proven partnership approach creating long-lasting relationships between Kia Georgia, the local community, and the entire state.
Stuart Countess, Kia Georgia’s President and CEO, emphasized that the day’s milestones were made possible by the dedication of team members at Kia Georgia.
Countess stated that five million vehicles are the result of teamwork and that producing world-class gasoline-powered vehicles, EVs, and now HEVs speaks to the versatility and talent in the plant.
Kia Georgia, Inc. is the first manufacturing site in North America for Kia Corporation based in Seoul, Korea.
Kia Georgia has an annual capacity of 350,000 units.
Kia Georgia is located on 2,200 acres in West Point, Ga.
Kia Georgia began mass production on Nov. 16, 2009.
Kia Georgia produces the Telluride, Sorento (excludes HEV and PHEV models), Sportage (excludes HEV and PHEV models) SUVs, and the all-electric EV9 and EV6.
source_title: Kia Georgia celebrates trio of major milestones, including Start of Production for 2027 Kia Telluride | NY Business Times

facts: Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia (KMMG) is Kia Motors’ first North American manufacturing plant.
KMMG has created more than 15,000 local jobs.
KMMG has produced more than three million vehicles.

Governor Brian Kemp and Hyundai Motor Group Executive Vice Chairman Euisun Chung attended a ceremony to mark the facility's 10th anniversary of vehicle production in West Point, Georgia on November 18, 2019.
KMMG became Kia’s third overseas manufacturing plant when it held its ground-breaking ceremony in October 2006.
The site underwent over three years of construction before producing its first vehicle, a Sorento, in November 2009.
The facility is located on a 2,200-acre site in West Point, Georgia.
KMMG currently has a production capacity of 340,000 vehicles per year.
Most output at KMMG is for the brand’s popular SUV models, the Telluride and Sorento, as well as the Optima mid-size sedan.
Kia Motors plans to increase KMMG’s annual production target for the Telluride to 80,000 units from the current 60,000.
Euisun Chung was president of Kia Motors Corporation when it made the decision to locate in Georgia.
Kia Motors expressed appreciation for support and cooperation from Georgia’s Department of Economic Development, the Department of Community Affairs, and the Georgia Ports Authority.
Georgia’s Technical College System and Georgia Quick Start played a role in training and developing KMMG’s workforce during its first decade.
Georgia Quick Start trained over 3,000 employees at KMMG.
EVC Chung stated that support from state and local governments, civic organizations, and various partners has been critical to the success of Kia’s first American plant.
EVC Chung emphasized that Georgia and the larger U.S. market will be the crown jewel of Hyundai Motor Group's global operations.
EVC Chung said Hyundai Motor Group and Kia Motors would become paradigm-shifters and trailblazers in the rapidly changing automotive industry.
EVC Chung stated that KMMG will continue to play an important part in the company’s future strategy.
EVC Chung added that Kia will offer the world’s best smart mobility services, expanding beyond vehicle production.
EVC Chung stated that in the future Kia will manufacture personal air vehicles and robots, as well as offer related services.
Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp lauded a successful first decade of production and a promising future for Kia in West Point.
Kia Motors recently announced with affiliate Hyundai Motor Company that it will establish an autonomous driving joint venture with Aptiv in the U.S. to lead the future of autonomous driving.
KMMG contract signing occurred in March 2006.
KMMG ground-breaking ceremony occurred in October 2006.
Production of the Sorento began at KMMG in November 2009.
KMMG grand opening occurred in February 2010.
Production of the Optima began at KMMG in September 2011.
Production of the Telluride began at KMMG in January 2019.
KMMG produced its three-millionth vehicle in September 2019.
KMMG began mass production on Nov. 16, 2009.
source_title: Kia Motors celebrates decade of manufacturing in Georgia

facts: Kia America announced the commencement of production of its Sportage Hybrid SUV at Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) in Georgia.
The Sportage Hybrid is the first hybrid vehicle to be manufactured at the new Georgia facility.
The Georgia facility began producing electric vehicles in early 2025.
The launch is regarded as a significant advancement for the Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis brands.
The launch underscores the plant's role as a transformative hub in Hyundai Motor Group's electrification strategy.
Kia stated that the Sportage Hybrid is the first hybrid-electric vehicle (HEV) to be manufactured at the facility.
The facility was initially established for the production of battery electric vehicles (BEVs) under an EV-focused strategy.
Kia operates a dedicated plant in West Point, Georgia.
The West Point plant assembles the Telluride SUV, the EV9 and EV6 BEVs, and internal combustion engine (ICE) variants of the Sportage and Sorento SUVs.
The Metaplant produces the Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Hyundai Ioniq 9 electric vehicles for Hyundai Motor Group.
The facility was designed with the capacity to manufacture up to ten different models across Hyundai Motor Group's three brands.
The facility's design potentially includes capacity for future body-on-frame vehicles, extended-range hybrid systems, and robotics applications.
U.S.-built versions of the Sportage Hybrid are scheduled to be available from the 2027 model year.
The U.S.-built Sportage Hybrid is expected to reach dealerships in the summer.
The Metaplant will handle production of all Sportage Hybrid variants, including LX, S, EX, X-Line, and SX-Prestige trims.
The 2027 Kia Sportage Hybrid is powered by a 1.6-liter turbocharged four-cylinder engine paired with a 1.49 kWh lithium-ion polymer battery.
Together with its compact electric motor, the Sportage Hybrid's powertrain produces a combined output of 232 horsepower and 271 lb-ft (367 Nm) of torque.
The 2027 Kia Sportage Hybrid is offered in both front-wheel drive and all-wheel drive configurations.
Kia emphasized that launching Sportage Hybrid production in Savannah expands its portfolio of SUVs manufactured for the U.S. market.
The launch further supports Kia's broader strategy of accelerating electrification across its vehicle lineup.
source_title: Kia Launches Sportage Hybrid Production at Georgia Metaplant

facts: VPS (Vehicle Protection Structures) partnered with Kia Georgia to complete a large-scale hail protection and solar canopy system at Kia’s West Point manufacturing facility.
The West Point manufacturing plant includes 3.2 million square feet of automotive hail protection.
The West Point manufacturing plant includes 10 MW of solar capacity.
On Feb. 24, 2026, Kia Georgia team members marked the start of production of the all-new 2027 Kia Telluride at its West Point facility.
Kia Georgia commemorated the production of the state of Georgia’s first-ever HEV on Feb. 24, 2026.
Kia Georgia marked the plant’s 5 millionth vehicle milestone on Feb. 24, 2026.
Eligible Kia owners received $500 in assistance for the purchase or lease of select new Kia vehicles between October 4, 2024, and January 2, 2025.
On October 4, 2024, Kia America and Kia Georgia, Inc., donated $250,000 to the American Red Cross to aid in relief efforts.
The 2025 Kia EV9 is the first electric vehicle assembled in the state of Georgia.
The 2025 Kia EV9 joins the award-winning Telluride, Sorento, and Sportage SUVs currently assembled at Kia’s West Point facility.
On May 30, 2024, Kia Georgia celebrated a historic milestone at its assembly plant in West Point.
The Sportage is the fourth model produced by Kia Georgia, joining the Telluride, Sorento, and K5.
On February 1, 2022, Kia Georgia officially began production of the all-new 2023 Sportage subcompact SUV at its facility in West Point, Georgia.
On Dec. 17, 2021, Kia Georgia, Inc. announced the retirement of President & CEO Jangsoo (Jason) Shin, effective December 31, 2021.
Jangsoo (Jason) Shin led the Georgia facility since 2017 and oversaw the inaugural launches of the Telluride and K5 models.
On August 20, 2021, Kia Georgia, Inc. reopened its online application process for hourly production positions for direct hires for the first time since 2011.
The plant completed a third wave of hiring prior to reopening applications in August 2021.
On March 30, 2021, Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia, Inc. (KMMG) donated $50,000 to the American Red Cross to assist with tornado relief efforts across the region.
KMMG team members raised additional funds for the American Red Cross through an internal channel.
Chairman Mong-Koo Chung was inaugurated as Honorary Chairman of the Group, and Euisun Chung was recognized for successful leadership as Executive Vice Chairman over the past two years.
On September 24, 2020, Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia (KMMG) partnered with the Technical College System of Georgia (TCSG).
KMMG donated 26 quality assessment vehicles to TCSG for their automotive programs as an education tool.
source_title: News Archives - Kia Georgia

facts: Kia Georgia is an automotive manufacturer located in West Point, Georgia.
In 2024, Kia Georgia expanded its operations to include production of two all-electric SUVs.
The expansion added 200 new jobs to the facility's existing workforce of more than 3,000 employees.
Stuart Countess serves as Kia Georgia’s president and CEO.
Countess noted that bringing EV production online required navigating supply chain disruptions and workforce challenges similar to those during the COVID-19 pandemic.
Kia Georgia maintains communication with partners like the GDEcD and the Georgia Ports Authority to manage logistics and supply chain uncertainties.
The company attributes its ability to bring EV production online and weather trade crises to a mindset of nimbleness, outward thinking, and strong internal leadership.

Georgia’s manufacturing companies face uncertainty amid evolving trade policies and tariffs that have disrupted access to foreign markets and increased costs for importing materials or exporting goods.
Georgia operates three Foreign-Trade Zones anchored to Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport and the Ports of Savannah and Brunswick.
Manufacturing facilities using imported inputs can utilize Georgia’s Foreign-Trade Zones to defer customs duties and improve cash flow.
source_title: High Stakes, Global Gains - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Kia will invest more than $200 million at its West Point plant.
The investment will create nearly 200 new jobs.
The expansion is to accommodate assembly of the company’s all-electric 2024 EV9 three-row SUV.
The project involves a production line transformation at the facility.
The EV9 will become the fifth model assembled at Kia Georgia.
Existing models assembled at Kia Georgia include the Telluride, Sorento and Sportage SUVs, and the K5 mid-size sedan.
EV9 assembly is expected to begin in the second quarter of 2024.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia’s longstanding partnership with Kia has led to generational job creation and growth for the West Point area.
Governor Kemp noted that the project will bring more opportunity to hardworking Georgians.
Governor Kemp stated the project will help propel Georgia toward its goal of becoming the e-mobility capital.
Currently, more than 40 percent of Kia vehicles sold in the U.S. are manufactured in West Point.
The addition of the EV9 to the lineup will further increase the facility’s output.
The EV9 is the brand’s first three-row EV SUV.
The EV9 is the first Kia EV to be assembled in North America.
The EV9 serves as the flagship vehicle for Kia’s global Plan S strategy focused on sustainable mobility leadership.
Sean Yoon, President & CEO of Kia Motors North America and Kia Motors America, stated that the EV9 has the potential to be another change catalyst for Kia.
Sean Yoon described the EV9 as the most innovative vehicle they have ever built.
Sean Yoon noted the EV9 will be a standout in the EV market and on the road.
Kia Georgia is a certified Georgia Made manufacturer.
Kia Georgia will continue to utilize the services of Georgia Quick Start.
Georgia Quick Start is described as the nation’s top-ranked workforce development program.
Georgia Quick Start operates the Kia Georgia Training Center.
Quick Start’s comprehensive, customized training services will prepare new employees with skills required for the new assembly line.
Stuart Countess, President & CEO of Kia Georgia, stated that the plant is preparing to begin production of its first EV.
Stuart Countess highlighted partnerships developed statewide with the Governor’s Office, the Department of Economic Development, and Georgia QuickStart.
Stuart Countess highlighted local partnerships with the City of West Point and Troup County.
City of West Point Mayor Steve Tramell stated that Kia has been an outstanding corporate citizen and community partner for seventeen years.
Mayor Tramell noted Kia has helped improve livelihoods in West Point by providing employment opportunities and fostering economic growth.
Amanda Fields, Senior Regional Project Manager, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The GDEcD partnered with the West Point Development Authority, the City of West Point, Troup County, Georgia Center of Innovation’s Manufacturing and Energy teams, and Georgia Quick Start.

Pat Wilson, Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner, stated that since Georgia recruited Kia in 2006, Kia has exceeded expectations as a partner by giving back to its adopted community.
To date, Kia has invested more than $1.9 billion in the state of Georgia.
Kia Georgia is responsible for more than 14,000 plant and supplier jobs in the region.

Governor Kemp and state lawmakers recognized January 31 as “Kia Day” to commend Kia’s vehicle manufacturing and impact since the 2006 announcement of a state-of-the-art vehicle assembly facility in West Point.
From Georgia, Kia ships vehicles to sales markets around the globe.
Kia's global sales markets include the United States, Canada, Mexico, the Caribbean, Middle East and Pacific nations.
Georgia has pursued job creation along the entire EV supply chain.
This pursuit has resulted in more than $22.7 billion in investment and the creation of over 28,400 jobs since 2020.
Kia Georgia, Inc. is the first manufacturing site in North America for Kia Corporation based in Seoul, Korea.
Kia Georgia has an annual capacity of 340,000 units.
Kia Georgia is located on 2,200 acres in West Point, Georgia.
The facility began mass production on November 16, 2009.
Kia America is headquartered in Irvine, California.
Kia serves as the “Official Automotive Partner” of the NBA.
Kia offers a range of gasoline, hybrid, plug-in hybrid and electrified vehicles sold through a network of over 750 dealers in the U.S.
The K5, Sportage, Sorento, and Telluride (excludes HEV and PHEV models) are assembled in the United States from U.S. and globally sourced parts.
The EV9 is expected to be assembled in the United States from U.S. and globally sourced parts.

facts: Around 200 new jobs are coming to Georgia as Kia plans to assemble new electric vehicles, according to an announcement by Governor Brian Kemp on Wednesday.
Kia will be manufacturing new 2024 EV9 SUVs with three rows of seats at Kia Georgia.
The company is investing more than $200 million in this new venture.
Assembly of these new vehicles should begin in the second quarter of 2024, according to Governor Kemp.
This adds to Kia Georgia’s current manufacturing of the Telluride, Sorento and Sportage SUVs, as well as the K5 sedan.
Over 40% of Kias sold in the U.S. are manufactured in West Point.
Kia ships vehicles from Georgia to markets all over the world, including Canada, Mexico, the Caribbean, Middle East and Pacific countries.
The EV9 is Kia’s first three-row EV SUV.
It is also the first electric vehicle Kia has assembled in North America.
Kia has currently invested nearly $2 billion into Georgia.
Kia has added over 14,000 jobs in the region, according to officials in a press release.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia’s longstanding partnership with Kia has led to generational job creation and growth for the West Point area.
Governor Brian Kemp expressed pride in Kia’s continued impact on the Peach State as a national leader in the automotive industry.
Governor Brian Kemp noted that the project will bring more opportunity to hardworking Georgians and help propel the state toward its goal of becoming the e-mobility capital.
source_title: Kia invests $200 million in producing electric vehicles in Georgia - WDEF

facts: Kia announced plans to commence production of its EV9 electric SUV in the United States by May.
The move aims to capitalize on growing U.S. EV demand and qualify for the country’s federal EV tax credit.
The EV9 is Kia’s first three-row electric SUV, which debuted in the U.S. late last year.
Over 3,800 units of the EV9 have been delivered since its launch.
The EV9's starting price is under $55,000.
The EV9 won the 2024 World Car of the Year and World Electric Car awards at the New York Auto Show.
Kia is relocating EV9 production from South Korea to its West Point, Georgia plant.
Production in Georgia is expected to make the EV9 eligible for the full $7,500 federal EV tax credit by early 2025.
James Bell, Kia America’s head of corporate communications, stated that the EV9’s battery is made in South Korea and China.
Moving production to the Georgia plant will require that 50% of the value of battery components be produced or manufactured in North America.
Kia acknowledges the importance of incentives for driving consumer EV adoption and aims to compete with established players like Tesla.
Kia is not deterred by competition from cheaper EVs rolling in from China, viewing new market entrants as a way to accelerate the transition to electric mobility.
Kia is offering a $5,000 customer cash incentive on all 2024 EV9 trims to offset the EV tax credit.
The EV9’s starting price of $54,900 (excluding $1,495 destination fees) is reduced to $51,395 with the incentive.
Kia expects full U.S. production and tax credit eligibility by 2025 to solidify its position in the growing EV landscape.
source_title: Kia Accelerates Plans for EV9 Production in the U.S. For $7,500 EV Tax Credit | EV.com

facts: Kia’s assembly plant in West Point opened in 2008 with more than enough workers available to produce the first cars.
The plant is now struggling to fill 500 new jobs needed to get a new compact SUV off the assembly line due to an affordable housing shortage.

Kia Georgia CEO Stuart Countess testified before a committee of Georgia legislators that many interested applicants for jobs paying $17-$23 per hour are having trouble finding affordable housing near the west Georgia plant.
Kia has been in close communication with Troup County and other government officials about housing development plans.
Countess urged other large employers to collaborate with government and community leaders to prepare for 50,000 manufacturing jobs expected to be created in Georgia in the next decade.
Kia Georgia explored creative solutions by talking to a developer about building workforce housing.
The housing shortage worsened during the pandemic when home development stalled due to supply chain issues, making it harder to buy or rent a house.

Countess stated at Thursday’s meeting held at the Georgia State Capitol: “The housing market creates additional complications for families considering a relocation to pursue employment as we know the housing market is in an exceedingly high demand with most affordable homes in the area lasting hours before asking prices are being offered to buyers.”

Before the new legislative session begins in January, the Georgia House Study Committee on Regulation, Affordability and Access to Housing will have met multiple times to identify solutions for a lack of affordable housing in the state for renters, entry-level homeowners, and middle-class families.
Macon Republican state Rep. Dale Washburn offered support for workforce housing, noting it will take a multilayer approach to address the shortage primarily below $300,000.

Washburn recalled a Macon neighborhood where cotton mill employees once lived in company-owned housing and stated: “Ideally I think for big industries, investing in housing could end up being a good investment.”
Barbara Holmes, president of the Albany Area Chamber of Commerce, noted that south Georgia is working to identify what constitutes affordable housing in the region.
There was a 6% drop in home ownership in Albany and surrounding Dougherty County from 2009 to 2020, and home prices have risen by 17% over the past couple of years.
Holmes said the market presents a hardship for renters, first-time buyers, and middle-class families, and for many people looking to move out of public housing.
It is hard to find nice housing between $100,000 and $150,000 in the Albany area.
Holmes stated: “Bidding wars in Dougherty County’s real estate market is not what I say is (typical) and so you’re seeing prices that are squeezing out homeownership that is truly affordable.”
Holmes added: “What we have is an old stock of homes but nothing newer for our recent college graduates, first-time home buyers.”

Tax credits are being used in south Georgia to build a multifamily workforce housing complex six miles from a Hyundai manufacturing plant planned near Savannah, according to Department of Community Affairs Commissioner Christopher Nunn.
Due to the loss of federal funding, DCA has been unable to provide as many Georgians loans and down payments for homeowners who earn 120% or less than other areas’ median household income.
DCA officials expect a surge in interest in the loan program when the house price limit is increased to $297,000 for most of the state and even higher in Atlanta and Savannah.
The committee discussed how building codes and government design standards drive up developer costs.
Chatsworth Republican state Rep. Jason Ridley asked Nunn if any cost-benefit analyses had been conducted on new regulations.
Dallas Republican state Rep. Martin Momtahan said an international code standard created a major burden for builders in Paulding County and throughout the state.
The DCA is currently involved in a statewide advisory group composed of representatives of general contracting, code enforcement, city and county government leaders, and trade associations.
Nunn stated: “We don’t just take a blanket approach and say Georgia, adopt all these codes as is.”
source_title: Kia plant CEO: Affordable housing shortage hurts worker recruitment - The Current

facts: Kia Georgia celebrated a historic day at its West Point facility with the start of production for the new 2027 Kia Telluride.
The ceremonial vehicle represented the first hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) assembled in the state of Georgia.
The event marked the 5 millionth vehicle produced at Kia Georgia.
Governor Brian P. Kemp, team members, and state and community leaders attended the ceremony.
Sean Yoon, president and CEO of Kia North America and Kia America, stated that the plant is the only one in the world that manufactures the Telluride.

Sean Yoon noted that reaching five million vehicles and presenting the first HEV assembled in Georgia (and the first Kia HEV assembled in the U.S.) testifies to Kia's rich history and future direction.
Since production began in 2009, Kia Georgia has become one of the region's most important employers and a pillar of automotive manufacturing in the Southeast.
The Telluride, launched in 2019, is manufactured exclusively in Georgia and has become one of the most successful vehicles on the Kia lineup.
Stuart Countess, president and CEO of Kia Georgia, emphasized that the day's milestones were made possible by the dedication of the Kia Georgia team members.
Stuart Countess stated that producing gasoline, electric, and world-class hybrid vehicles demonstrates the plant's versatility and talent.

Attendees at the ceremony included Consul General Joon-ho Lee, State Representative Vance Smith, Technical College System of Georgia Commissioner Greg Dozier, Department of Community Affairs Commissioner Chris Nunn, Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson, First Lady Marty Kemp, HATCI President John Robb, Georgia Chamber of Commerce President Chris Clark, and Hyundai Motor Group Washington Office Senior Vice President Drew Ferguson.
The Telluride has become a flagship model for the brand and a symbol of vehicle quality manufactured in West Point.
source_title: Kia Georgia celebra tres hitos importantes, incluido el inicio de la producción del Kia Telluride 2027   - AutoNews-Wire.com

facts: Hyundai plans to build a new electric-vehicle manufacturing plant in the United States.
Hyundai has held discussions with officials in Georgia for the new facility.
The proposed site is located near existing Hyundai and Kia plants in Georgia.
Hyundai Motor confirmed an imminent plan for a new EV plant but declined to comment on details, including site negotiations.
Hyundai stated it is excited to announce a new EV plant plan in the U.S. soon but has no details to share at this stage.
Hyundai has been in advanced discussions with Georgia state officials to build a dedicated EV facility.
Details regarding the projected investment cost and expected job creation numbers are not immediately known.
If finalized, the new Georgia EV facility would serve both Hyundai and Kia brands.
The facility is intended to support the rollout of two fully electric SUVs for the U.S. market: the Ioniq 7 and EV9.
Georgia’s Economic Development Department declined to comment, stating they do not comment on speculation about economic development projects.
The potential investment aligns with the Biden administration's push for more EV and supplier investments to create jobs and drive a clean-energy agenda.
The project would mark a major economic development win for Georgia.
Georgia has been pushing to establish itself as a regional hub for the emerging EV industry.

facts: Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia, Inc. (KMMG) celebrated production of the 500,000th vehicle at the West Point, Ga. automobile manufacturing facility on Feb. 29, 2012.
KMMG began mass production of the Kia Sorento CUV on Nov. 16, 2009.
The Kia Sorento CUV quickly became the brand’s best-selling product in the U.S.
KMMG began production of the Kia Optima mid-size sedan on Sept. 2, 2011.
The Kia Optima led the brand’s U.S. sales in December 2011 and January.
Since opening, KMMG has added two additional shifts of production.
More than 3,000 team members have been hired by the plant to date.
In 2011, KMMG completed a $100 million expansion.
The expansion pushed the plant’s total capacity to 360,000 units per year.
Byung Mo Ahn, Group President and CEO for Kia Motors America and KMMG, said building 500,000 vehicles in just over two years is a milestone for their first U.S. manufacturing plant.
Ahn stated that the commitment from each team member is a big reason they achieved 17 consecutive months of record sales.
Vehicle number 500,000 was a snow white pearl Optima SX.
The vehicle will be allocated to one of KMA’s more than 755 dealerships nationwide.
KMMG is the first manufacturing site in North America for Kia Motors Corporation based in Seoul, Korea.
KMMG is located on 2,200 acres in West Point, Georgia.
Beginning in 2012, the plant has the ability to build 360,000 vehicles annually from U.S. and globally-sourced parts.
