facts: TADs are areas specifically designated by local governments for TIF projects where real estate property taxes gathered above a particular threshold for a set period of time must be used to fund specified improvements.
Local governments consider geographic areas that have potential for development or redevelopment but suffer from blight or other distressed conditions when establishing TADs.

As improvements and redevelopment occur in TADs, the taxable value of property increases, resulting in additional tax revenues held by local governments in special funds to pay debt service on tax allocation bonds (TABs).
The life of a TAD is typically 20-25 years, or sufficient time to repay the TABs.
In Georgia, tax incentive financing for redevelopment is generally referred to as "TAD financing."
Local governments in Georgia are authorized to use TAD financing under the state's Redevelopment Powers Law (O.C.G.A. § 36-44-1 et seq.), adopted by the General Assembly in 1985.
Creating a TAD requires the Redevelopment Law to be made applicable to a county or city via local law passed by the Georgia General Assembly and approved by special election.
A city or county must prepare a redevelopment plan for the proposed TAD and submit it for consent to the political subdivision or board of education required to consent.

The local legislative body must adopt a resolution approving the redevelopment plan, describing the TAD territory, creating the district on December 31 (or a subsequent year), assigning a name, specifying the estimated tax allocation increment base, specifying ad valorem property taxes for computing increments, specifying property pledged for TABs, and finding that the area would not be redeveloped without the plan or contains unpreserved assets.
A certified copy of the authorizing resolution must be submitted to the local legislative body before including ad valorem property taxes or general funds in the tax allocation increment calculation.
Once designated, TABs may be issued by the city or county to pay redevelopment costs for approved projects within the TAD.
TABs are secured by incremental tax revenue increases from redevelopment and may qualify to pay interest exempt from state and federal income taxes.
Over the term of a TAB, the tax increment is set aside to pay debt service; after retirement, the increment goes to general funds at the taxing jurisdiction's discretion.
TABs are not considered general obligations of the issuer.

TAD financing can be structured as front-end sales of TABs for initial infrastructure or land acquisition costs, paid back with tax increment revenues as collected, or on a "pay-as-you-go" basis reimbursed as increments generate.
TAD financing provides funding for redevelopment projects that may otherwise not have been feasible and makes challenging redevelopment sites attractive to developers.
TAD financing allows local governments to leverage tax increments for new public infrastructure and private investment without spending current tax revenues.
TAD financing grants access to redevelopment powers such as eminent domain.

TADs have the potential to create new jobs, eliminate blight, reduce crime, increase property values and tax revenues, decrease poverty, create economic opportunities, and redevelop underperforming neighborhoods and underused commercial parcels.
Community Improvement Districts (CIDs) are authorized by Article IX, Section VII of the Georgia Constitution for any county, city, or combination thereof.
A CID is a unit of government with authority to provide governmental facilities and services like street/road construction, parks, water systems, and other services.
Property owners within CIDs agree to a self-imposed tax used for improving the district.
CIDs can be public bodies or not-for-profit corporations that collect money from property taxes, business licenses, sales taxes, or user fees to fund district services.
A CID's administrative body may incur debt without a voter referendum, backed by the full faith, credit, and taxing power of the CID but not an obligation of the State or other local governments.
Creating a CID requires a resolution consenting to its creation by the governing authority of the county (if unincorporated), municipality (if incorporated), or both.

Creation also requires written consent from a majority of real property owners subject to taxes/fees/assessments and owners constituting at least 75% (by value) of all real property within the CID subject to such levies.
The County Tax Commissioner certifies that required approvals and percentages are satisfied before the County Board of Commissioners and/or City Council pass a resolution consenting to creation.
CIDs are governmental entities administered by a board of directors whose composition is set by local legislation, consisting of elected and appointed members with terms also set by legislation.
For example, Cobb County and Gwinnett County CIDs are governed by boards of 6 members elected by property owners and 1 member appointed by the County.
CID boards raise funds by setting ad valorem millage rates on real property, excluding agricultural, residential, or forestry uses.
The millage rate is constitutionally capped at 2.5% (25 mills) of assessed value, though local laws may limit it further, such as Cobb County's cap of 5 mills.
CID tax revenues are collected by the Tax Commissioner and sent to the CID Board for expenditure strictly on governmental services and facilities within the district.

CIDs create special taxing districts to pay for infrastructure and services including street construction/maintenance, parks/recreation, storm water/sewage collection, terminal/dock facilities, parking, and public transportation.
Numerous CIDs exist in Georgia, with a majority located in and around the Atlanta area.

Examples of existing CIDs include Cumberland (Cobb County), Buckhead (Atlanta), Downtown (Atlanta), Midtown (Atlanta), Town Center (Cobb County), Gwinnett Place (Gwinnett County), Lilburn (Gwinnett County), and Town of Braselton (Braselton).
TIF has been used in DeKalb County, Georgia, and the cities of Atlanta, Macon, College Park, East Point, Marietta, Acworth, and Smyrna.

In 2001, the City of Atlanta issued $67,505,000 in TABs to finance a portion of infrastructure work for the Atlantic Station project, which had a total infrastructure development cost budget of $187,000,000.
Bond proceeds for the Atlantic Station project were used for clearing and grading, utilities, streets and sidewalks, environmental remediation, parking, costs of issuance, and related items.
The Atlanta Atlantic Station TAD contained approximately 119 areas.
For the Atlantic Station project, the incremental tax revenue increase for 2006 over the 2001 base year was estimated at $8,347,772.
In 2003, the Smyrna TAD was established to finance infrastructure for the redevelopment of two obsolete shopping centers along Atlanta Road in a $27,000,000 project.
source_title: Redevelopment Financing Incentives in Georgia | Tax Lawyers | Alston & Bird

facts: The $800 million Aurubis Richmond, LLC plant in the Augusta Corporate Park officially joined the Richmond County business community with a ribbon-cutting ceremony marking the completion of phase one.
Construction on the project has been underway since 2022.
Toralf Haag, CEO of Aurubis AG, described the ribbon-cutting as a landmark day celebrating professionals who worked together to achieve the accomplishment.
Aurubis has spent more than 150 years developing technology and skills as Europe’s leading producer and one of the world’s largest power recyclers.
The plant will recycle copper, precious metals, and non-ferrous materials.
The facility will be part of the growing electric mobility chain in Georgia.
The company will produce materials found in lithium-ion batteries and other electronics.
David Schultheis, President and Managing Director of Aurubis Richmond, stated the facility will process 90,000 tons of recycling materials every year during phase one.
Phase two of the project is already under construction.
With phase two complete, the facility will have the capacity to process over 180,000 tons of materials annually.
Aurubis invested in training and intends to bring more than 230 direct jobs to the Augusta area.
The facility already has 150 staff on site.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp (R) participated in the 2022 groundbreaking ceremony and returned for the ribbon-cutting to mark the plant's opening.

Gov. Kemp stated that projects like this highlight assets making Georgia the number one state to do business for the 11th year in a row, per an Area Development Magazine announcement made a week earlier.
Since taking office over five and a half years ago, Gov. Kemp announced 3,000 new jobs and $2.36 billion in investment in Augusta and Richmond County.
Over the same period, the broader region of the state saw over 6,300 new jobs and over $3.2 billion in investment from projects the state worked on.
These announced figures do not count organic growth happening daily in local communities with local chambers and economic developers.

Inge Hofkens, COO of Multimetal Recycling at Aurubis AG, noted that economic factors over the last two years were heavily influenced by several challenges but emphasized the company's clear vision and strong will to see the project reach full potential.
Hofkens summarized the company's commitment to Richmond County by stating they are here to stay, fully committed, and this is only the beginning.
source_title: Large Augusta manufacturer completes phase one; 80 more jobs to come - Augusta Business Daily

facts: Germany-based metals producer Aurubis AG says the melt shop at its Aurubis Richmond facility in Augusta, Georgia, has started producing recycled-content nonferrous metals.
Aurubis refers to the new site as the first United States multimetal recycling plant.
The facility recovers strategic metals for U.S. energy infrastructure and the defense industry.
The project strengthens Western supply chains for copper, nickel, tin and precious metals.
Aurubis CEO Toralf Haag stated that with Aurubis Richmond, the company is the frontrunner in multimetal recycling and processing in the U.S., a position it already holds in Europe.

Toralf Haag noted that the company will continue expanding its expertise in producing key strategic raw materials in the U.S., citing high availability of recycling material and good local conditions as appealing prospects for the American market.
The company states that demand for strategic metals such as copper is rising steadily worldwide.
U.S. industry currently needs about 1.8 million tons of copper per year.
As of now, the U.S. imports about half of the copper it consumes.
Multimetal recycling can close this supply gap quickly, in part due to the company's $800 million secondary smelter in Georgia.
The plant has been designed to process up to 180,000 tons of end-of-life materials annually.
End-of-life materials processed include printed circuit boards and copper wire and cable.
The facility recovers several nonferrous metals from the processed materials.
A “First Melt” ceremony was held in Augusta the week of Sept. 22.
Participants at the ceremony included U.S. Rep. Rick W. Allen (R-Georgia) and Jens Hanefeld, Germany’s ambassador to the U.S.

U.S. Rep. Rick W. Allen described Aurubis Richmond as a strategic site in Georgia’s 12th District that reinforces America’s ability to supply itself with metals for national security, infrastructure, and technology leadership.
Allen added that Aurubis exemplifies how international partnerships can contribute to solidifying America’s industrial foundation and strengthening economic independence.
Aurubis Richmond President and Managing Director David Schultheis expressed pride in the team that developed and built the state-of-the-art recycling plant.
source_title: Aurubis now producing copper in Georgia - Recycling Today

facts: The Supervisory Board of Aurubis AG approved an additional comprehensive investment package of around € 530 million to accelerate growth and sustainability.
The investment package includes expansion to double the processing capacity of the Aurubis Richmond recycling plant in Georgia, USA.
A second recycling module with an investment volume of around € 250 million is slated to be added to the Aurubis Richmond plant currently under construction in the US state of Georgia.
An additional € 90 million has been approved for a capital expenditure adjustment for infrastructure requirements and inflation at the Georgia site.
Production at the expanded Aurubis Richmond plant is scheduled to begin in 2026.
The expansion will double the throughput volume from the previously planned 90,000 tons of complex recycling materials to 180,000 tons per year.
The additional plant will be built in accordance with the highest environmental standards to allow for environmentally friendly recycling of input materials.
Aurubis anticipates creating around 80 additional jobs in the region due to the expansion.
Once both recycling modules are completed, Aurubis anticipates employing around 200 people at the Richmond site.
Construction is already underway on the first plant at the Richmond site.
Together with the planned expansion approved today, Aurubis anticipates total investments in the Georgia facility of € 640 million.
Starting in 2025/26, Aurubis anticipates additional EBITDA totaling around € 90 million per year from the expansion phase approved today.
The Richmond plant will profit from attractive local electricity and natural gas prices to positively impact cost efficiency.
Aurubis plans to finance the projects approved today primarily out of current cash flow with no need for a capital increase.
Recycling in the USA is growing by 5% every year, resulting in a rapid increase in the availability of complex recycling materials that support the expansion.
source_title: Aurubis AG: Aurubis adopts € 530 million investment package to accelerate growth and sustainability | Corporate - EQS News

facts: Aurubis AG signed an equipment and technology contract with Germany-based SMS Group GmbH for a second plant module at its Richmond, Georgia scrap-fed copper refinery.
The company announced additional capital expenditures for 2023 that included a project designed to double the capacity of the Georgia facility.

The additional equipment will increase the capacity of the metal recycling plant in Richmond, currently under construction, from an annual 90,000 tons of complex metal scrap materials to 180,000 tons per year.

Aurubis CEO Roland Harings stated the expansion delivers on the company’s “Driving Sustainable Growth” strategy while keeping mineral raw materials that are both strategically important and in high demand in the resource cycle.
The SMS modular technology allows for flexible operations and quick growth in the dynamic United States recycling market.

The SMS technology includes a top-blown rotary converter (TBRC), which is state-of-the-art equipment used for processing complex recycling materials so that raw materials like copper, nickel, tin, precious metals and platinum group metals can be recovered in additional downstream stages.
Aurubis CEO Roland Harings noted that bundling SMS Group’s extensive plant know-how with Aurubis’ unique expertise creates an environment for the highly efficient recovery of metals.

Michael Rzepczyk, a member of the SMS executive board, stated the partnership helps Aurubis assume a leading role in multimetal recycling in the U.S. and takes recycling to the next level with the planned doubling of production volume at the new site.
The modular system allows additional components to be added to the Richmond plant to precisely meet any future requirements.
Aurubis CEO Roland Harings added that the modular system enables a prudent market strategy to respond flexibly to U.S. needs and benefit from constant market growth in the country.
Aurubis assigned a total project price tag of about 640 million euros ($692 million) for both phases of the project.
The facility is described as the first secondary smelter specializing in multimetal recycling in the U.S., representing a targeted investment in expanding its recycling business.

In a 2022 interview, Aurubis CEO Roland Harings stated the Richmond plant was initially slated to process 90,000 metric tons of circuit boards, copper cable and other recyclables that contain metal annually to produce 35,000 metric tons of blister copper annually.
Both the annual input scrap volume (90,000 metric tons) and output blister copper volume (35,000 metric tons) are presumed to double when the plant runs at full capacity.
Stage 1 of the Richmond plant is scheduled to go online in the first half of 2024.
The equipment and technology contract was signed in late March, with an anticipated second-stage production startup sometime in 2026.
source_title: Aurubis opts to double capacity at Georgia plant - Recycling Today

facts: Aurubis has started production at its new U.S. copper recycling plant in Richmond.
The facility represents an investment of approximately $800 million.
The plant will process up to 180,000 metric tons of complex waste material annually.
Input materials include printed circuit boards, copper cables, and other metal-bearing products.
The plant will produce 70,000 tons of high-grade blister copper with a purity of 98-99%.
Production outputs also include nickel, tin, and other precious metals.
An Aurubis spokesperson stated the company intends to sell the copper primarily to U.S. customers.
The initiative aims to meet the growing demand for strategic metals such as copper in the U.S. and elsewhere.
The U.S. currently has an annual copper demand of around 1.8 million tons.
Approximately half of the U.S. copper demand is imported.
The Richmond plant aims to address this import gap by providing a faster method to reduce imports.
Blister copper produced at the Richmond facility can be further processed into high-purity copper and copper products at Aurubis' smelting network in Europe.
Integration with European operations allows for efficient distribution and added value to the materials produced.
Aurubis CEO Toralf Haag stated the company may consider additional projects in the United States.
Haag cited the high availability of recycling materials and favorable local conditions as reasons for considering further U.S. expansion.
Haag noted that the American market offers Aurubis appealing prospects that will continue to be considered closely in the future.
The new plant is a significant step for Aurubis to strengthen its presence in the U.S. market.
source_title: Aurubis Starts Production at US Copper Recycling Plant - World-Energy

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the opening of a new smelting and recycling facility in Augusta, Georgia.
The facility will be the very first of its kind in the United States.
The project will create 125 jobs in the Augusta area.
Governor Kemp stated it marks the single-largest investment made by a German company in the State of Georgia.
Aurubis plans to produce materials found in lithium-ion batteries and other electronics from the Augusta facility.
CEO Roland Harings stated the investment sets an example of sustainable growth and positions Aurubis as a forerunner for multi-metal recycling in the U.S.
The facility will be built on 150 acres at Augusta Corporate Park.
Augusta Corporate Park is a “Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified” site.
Operations are expected to begin by the first half of 2024.
The company plans to begin hiring for positions as soon as summer 2022.
Steven Kendrick, Chairman of the Development Authority of Augusta, welcomed Aurubis as a leader in its industry building a world-class advanced manufacturing facility.
Griff Lynch, Executive Director of the Georgia Ports Authority (GPA), noted that exports from the recycling operation will strengthen Georgia’s global position via Savannah port.

Fernanda Kirchner, Senior Project Manager for GDEcD’s Global Commerce division, represented the department in partnership with the Augusta Economic Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, and Georgia Power.
Sérgio Domingues, Managing Director for the State of Georgia’s Europe Office, played a supporting role in attracting Aurubis to Georgia.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson welcomed Aurubis as the first multi-metal recycling plant in the United States located in Richmond County.
Augusta Corporate Park has completed due diligence and specific certification requirements prior to prospective business visits under the GRAD program.
Thousands of Georgia jobs have been created on GRAD sites since the program’s inception in 2008.
Aurubis AG is a leading global provider of non-ferrous metals and one of the largest copper recyclers worldwide.
The company processes complex metal concentrates, scrap metals, organic and inorganic metal-bearing recycling materials, and industrial residues into high-quality metals.
Aurubis produces more than 1 million tons of copper cathodes annually.
From copper cathodes, Aurubis manufactures wire rod, continuous cast shapes, profiles, and flat rolled products made of copper and copper alloys.
Aurubis also produces precious metals, selenium, lead, nickel, tin, zinc, sulfuric acid, and iron silicate.
source_title: Aurubis to Open State-of-the-Art Recycling and Copper Smelting Facility in Augusta - Expansion Solutions

facts: Gov. Brian Kemp came to Augusta on Friday for the ribbon-cutting of a new metal recycling plant in south Augusta.
The project took two years in the making since the groundbreaking occurred a little over two years ago at the Aurubis plant site.
At the time of the June 2022 groundbreaking, the $320 million price tag made it Germany’s all-time largest investment in Georgia.
The facility will bring more than 200 jobs to the Augusta-Aiken area.
The plant will create 235 local jobs paying more than a livable wage and above the average county wage.
Aurubis expects to be fully staffed with every position filled by the middle of 2025.
The company is working with technical colleges in Augusta and Aiken and Richmond County schools, providing nearly 200,000 in scholarships.
Aurubis is also collaborating with local organizations like United Way and Golden Harvest.
The plant will be the first of its kind in the U.S.
From Augusta, the company plans to produce materials found in lithium-ion batteries and other electronics.
The facility will process more than 180,000 tons of complex recycling materials a year.
The recovered metals are used in countless modern-day essentials, from mobile phones and computers to power plants and electric vehicles.
Aurubis is a global provider of non-ferrous metals and one of the largest copper recyclers worldwide.
The company processes complex metal concentrates, scrap metals, organic and inorganic metal-bearing recycling materials and industrial residues into metals.
Aurubis produces more than 1 million tons of copper cathodes annually, which are used to make wire rod, continuous cast shapes, profiles and flat rolled products.
The company also produces precious metals, selenium, lead, nickel, tin and zinc, along with sulfuric acid and iron silicate.
Augusta Mayor Garnett Johnson said, “This site is a result of a lot of hard work.”
Gov. Brian Kemp stated, “We want to bring jobs that are paying above the average county wage. And certainly these jobs are,”
Tonya Bonitatibus of the Savannah Riverkeeper raised concerns about the environmental impact and potential for air pollution.
Bonitatibus said, “I think the potential for recycling companies to harm people, not only is it extremely real, we have a history of it here in Augusta Hyde Park.”
She suggested the company should relocate to Evans or West Augusta instead.
Aurubis states that its production is carried out with very high environmental protection standards to maintain a clean environment.
The American Lung Association reported that this year Augusta is one of the worst places for particle pollution.
source_title: Aurubis recycling plant brings over 200 jobs to the Augusta-Aiken area

facts: The Aurubis Richmond metals recycling plant is ramping up operations in Georgia, strengthening US access to strategic metals.
The facility will recover copper, nickel, tin, and precious metals from complex scrap streams.
The plant is becoming an important pillar for US data centres, energy infrastructure, and defence supply chains.
Aurubis is investing around $800mn in the Richmond facility.
The facility will process up to 180,000 t/yr of recycled material.
The plant will treat printed circuit boards, copper cable, and other complex scrap that traditionally flowed to overseas processors.
The site supports onshore refining of metal units that are essential for electronics and power systems.
Aurubis has signalled an “expansion stage” from 2026 to further increase throughput and product range.
This expansion aligns with rising demand for low-carbon, circular metal supply in North America.
The Georgia facility is expected to help meet growing US demand for “strategic metals” across several high-growth sectors.
Data centres require reliable copper, nickel, and precious metal supply for servers, networking, and cooling systems.
Energy infrastructure and grid upgrades depend on copper-intensive equipment such as transformers and high-voltage cables.
Defence and advanced technology applications need secure access to high-purity metals with traceable provenance.
Turning scrap into refined metal reduces import dependence while lowering the environmental footprint of critical value chains.
Advanced metals recycling is now considered strategic infrastructure rather than just a waste-management activity.
source_title: Aurubis Richmond metals recycling plant boosts US strategic metals supply | The Metalnomist - Raw Material, Metal, Alloy, Scrap News

facts: The Savannah Riverkeeper submitted an argument against an air permit for a company that plans to open a plant in Augusta, Ga.
The $340 million metal recycling and secondary smelting facility by German company Aurubis promises 125 jobs.
Aurubis has filed an air permit request with Georgia’s Environmental Protection Division.

In the Savannah Riverkeeper’s public comment, the organization highlights Aurubis’ request to release what it calls “more than 30 hazardous pollutants into the air that the people of Augusta breathe.”
The list includes things from arsenic to o-Xylene that, if permitted, would be released ‘in and around the facility,’ according to the Savannah Riverkeper.
The American Lung Association’s “State of our Air” report has ranked Augusta 28th worst in the nation for year-round particulate pollution levels.
The city moved up seven spots on that list in just a year.
The Savannah Riverkeeper said its research and advocacy unit uncovered a list of federal Resource Conservation and Recovery Act violations filed against Aurubis.

Those RCRA violations filed against Aurubis’ Buffalo, New York facility indicate that Aurubis’ handling of the toxic chemicals it uses and produces was unsafe, presenting a danger to the local community and Aurubis’ employees.
Aurubis will build its facility on 150 acres at Augusta Corporate Park.
Operations are expected to begin by the first half of 2024.
The company plans to begin hiring as soon as summer 2022.
Aurubis is a global provider of non-ferrous metals and one of the largest copper recyclers worldwide.
The company processes complex metal concentrates, scrap metals, organic and inorganic metal-bearing recycling materials, and industrial residues into metals.
The company produces more than 1 million tons of copper cathodes annually.
From the copper cathodes, Aurubis makes a variety of products such as wire rod, continuous cast shapes, profiles and flat rolled products.
Aurubis produces a number of other metals, including precious metals, selenium, lead, nickel, tin and zinc.
The portfolio also includes additional products such as sulfuric acid and iron silicate.

links.context: Careers page for Aurubis job applications

source_title: Riverkeeper fights air permit for plant that promises 125 jobs

entity_name: Augusta-Richmond County Manufacturing & Supply Chain Sector
facts: Aurubis, a German-based multimetal recycling company, has invested at least $800 million in a first-of-its-kind facility in the U.S.
The company is a global provider of non-ferrous metals used in electric vehicles, power plants, mobile phones, and computers.
Aurubis is one of the largest copper recyclers in the world.
Construction at the Aurubis facility is close to completion, with hopes to start producing product in the fourth quarter.
The Aurubis project will create 240 jobs and currently employs roughly 200 people.
Aurubis is located in the Augusta Corporate Park.
GF Casting Solutions, a Swiss-based auto parts supplier, is expected to begin operations by the end of 2026.
Phase One of the GF Casting facility is almost finished with equipment currently being installed.
The GF Casting investment totals $224 million and will create 343 jobs.
GF Casting Solutions is located in the Augusta Corporate Park, across from Aurubis.
Manufacturing is one of the top industries in the region, with 438 manufacturers employing roughly 25,000 people in the MSA.
The regional manufacturing industry is expected to grow by 2,000 more jobs by 2026.
From 2013 to 2023, the seven-county MSA added 74 new manufacturing operations.
As of the fourth quarter of 2024, manufacturers in the region are estimated to have made $8.1 billion in purchases from suppliers in the U.S.
Of those supplier purchases, 27% or $2.2 billion were made within the Augusta MSA.
Cal Wray, president of the Augusta Economic Development Authority, highlighted the growth and momentum in the regional manufacturing sector and provided key investment data.
House Bill 880 allows military spouses to use an existing professional license in good standing from another state to get a job in Georgia, overcoming a six- to eight-month licensing hurdle.
Military retirees living in Georgia will no longer have to pay income taxes on part of their retirement income, with up to $65,000 exempted beginning with the 2026 tax year.
The Greater Augusta Black Chamber of Commerce has provided $577,000 in grants to small businesses and about $45,000 in loans to address access to capital challenges for local entrepreneurs.

Accelerate Augusta, a microenterprise incubator housed in a city-owned building leased by the Downtown Development Authority, offers boot camps and resources for aspiring entrepreneurs and small businesses.
The Accelerate Augusta center was made possible by a $2.35 million appropriations grant through Sen. Raphael Warnock’s office and a partnership with the city.
source_title: Augusta | Richmond County: From Obstacles to Opportunity - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: Companies looking to lower emissions and reduce carbon footprint have invested over $11.5 billion in Georgia since 2018.
Ascend Elements, formerly known as Battery Resourcers, is investing $43 million to open a commercial-scale lithium-ion battery recycling plant in Georgia.
The Ascend Elements facility will extract critically scarce materials like lithium, cobalt, and nickel from batteries.
The Ascend Elements plant will become the largest of its kind in North America when fully operational later this year (2022).
Ascend Elements selected Georgia primarily because of the growing electric vehicle and battery ecosystem in the state, according to Roger Lin, Vice President of Marketing and Government Relations.
Proximity to partners and customers is essential in the sustainable battery materials industry, notes Roger Lin.
The Southeast is a mecca for the electric vehicle and battery industry, and Georgia offers proximity to factories like SK Battery America, per Roger Lin.
Proximity minimizes transportation costs, provides access to top talent from nearby universities, and offers access to renewable energy, notes Roger Lin.
Korea’s SK Battery America is investing nearly $2.6 billion in two facilities near Atlanta to supply batteries to Ford, Volkswagen, and other EV makers.
Since SK Battery America's first announcement in 2018, more businesses focused on EV sustainability have invested in Georgia.
Plug Power recently announced a new $84 million, zero-emission hydrogen fuel production facility near I-95 and Georgia’s deepwater ports.
Plug Power's fuel cell systems for e-mobility deliver efficiency, fast fueling, and low cost to customers.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp launched the Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance, a statewide initiative between government, industries, electric utilities, nonprofits, and other stakeholders.

The Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance focuses on growing the electric mobility ecosystem in the state and strengthening Georgia’s position in electrification-related manufacturing and innovation.
In 2021, EV manufacturer Rivian announced an investment of $5 billion to build a carbon-conscious facility in Georgia.
Rivian's facility will make vertically connected electric platforms for trucks, sport/utility vehicles, and delivery vans.
Hyundai Motor Group will open its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility in Georgia with a $5.54 billion investment.
Hyundai's Georgia facility operations are slated to begin in the first half of 2025, with an annual capacity of 300,000 units.
Many other Georgia companies are already making parts for EVs.
Companies from Korea, Germany, Turkey, and the Netherlands have announced plans to build EV-related facilities in Georgia.
Site Selection Magazine recently ranked Georgia No. 3 for sustainability in the South Atlantic region.
The Atlanta metro ranked in the Top 10 nationally for sustainability.
Since 2009, Georgia has increased net electricity generation from renewable sources by more than 93%.
Georgia’s electricity mix currently consists of nearly 12% renewables.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that renewable energy capacity installation in Georgia has been driven organically by companies and consumers, accelerated through economic attractiveness rather than mandate.
Smart policies have allowed Georgia to promote renewable power development and offer a comprehensive portfolio of clean energy options for competitive advantage, according to Pat Wilson.
Solar PV is the state’s fastest-growing source of renewable energy, accelerating its appeal to solar panel companies such as NanoPV Solar and Qcells.
NanoPV will manufacture solar panels and equipment in south Georgia’s Sumter County.
Qcells is investing more than $320 million in its solar panel assembly plant in northern Georgia.
Qcells' facility is the largest solar panel assembly plant in the Western hemisphere.
Recent sustainability-related announcements include German copper recycler Aurubis.
Recent announcements include electronic waste recycler Igneo.
Recent announcements include Danimer Scientific, a manufacturer and developer of biodegradable materials.
Recent announcements include recycled furniture maker SoPoly.
In Atlanta, EF Block turns everyday recycled Styrofoam into earth-friendly, stronger, sustainable walls that replace traditional building materials.
With support from GDEcD’s Center of Innovation and the City of Atlanta, EF Block has launched a city-wide Styrofoam recycling initiative.
On Georgia’s coast, NANTRenewables is opening a plant to manufacture biodegradable products like plates, straws, cutlery, trays, and cups.
NANTRenewables provides sustainable products to companies within the food, agriculture, and pharmaceutical industries.

Harold Meherg, a NANT Renewables senior executive, selected SeaPoint Industrial Terminal Complex in Savannah for the new biogenic fillers and bioplastics manufacturing facility due to shared sustainability values and development-ready infrastructure including roads, rail access, sea access, and utility connections.
Companies leading mobility research & development, battery manufacturing and recycling, and electric vehicle production all call Georgia home.
Ascend Elements’ Roger Lin stated that Georgia is in a strategic location with a critical mass of factories locating or operating in the area.
Lin noted that Georgia is a very friendly, welcoming place to open and grow a business with large facilities like theirs.
source_title: Georgia Emerges as Hub for Sustainability - The ATL Airport Chamber

facts: Europe’s largest copper producer Aurubis is looking to increase its sales to the US while there are no import tariffs on the metal.
CEO Toralf Haag told analysts that the company would ramp up its copper recycling smelter in the US this year, with potential for further investment in coming years.
The company’s only US operation is a copper recycling smelter located in Richmond County, Georgia.

CFO Steffen Hoffmann stated that the Richmond multi-metal recycling plant should contribute a lower double-digit million euro amount to the company’s earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization next year.
Higher ramp-up costs for strategic projects, such as its multi-metal recycling plant in the United States, had a negative effect on operating EBT.
Copper was not included in a list of reciprocal tariffs announced by the US in April.
US President Donald Trump ordered a probe into copper imports in February, with findings expected by November 2025 at the latest.
Haag stated that current sales to the US are limited but the operation aims to play a role in the US copper market.
The company confirmed its forecast for the 2024/25 financial year, expecting full-year operating EBT around the midpoint of a 300 million to 400 million euros range.
Half-year core profit beat market expectations on Thursday, helped by higher prices of sulphuric acid and precious metals.
Operating earnings before tax (EBT) fell 5.8% to 229 million euros ($259.2 million) in the six months through March 2025, from 243 million euros a year earlier.
The half-year operating EBT beat analysts’ expectations of 221 million euros.
source_title: Aurubis looks to boost US sales in absence of tariffs - MINING.COM

facts: Georgia Tech President Ángel Cabrera described Tech Square in the 1990s as a brownfield of chain-link fences, broken windows, and abandoned buildings.

Tech Square is now recognized nationally as a premier innovation district with the highest density of startups, corporate innovators, academic researchers, and students in the southeastern United States.
In 2020, Cabrera announced a 10-year strategic plan to turn Atlanta into one of the top five cities for startups in the United States.
More than 140,000 students are enrolled in colleges across Atlanta, with over 13,000 graduating from tech-focused programs each year.

Atlanta hosts corporate innovation centers, incubators, and accelerators including Atlanta Tech Park, the Curiosity Lab, and Atlanta Tech Village, which is the largest tech entrepreneurial hub in the Southeast and the fourth largest in the country.
A Boston Consulting Group (BCG) study found that Atlanta's venture capital funding declined more than 40 percent from 2021 to 2022.
In 2022, Atlanta brought in $1.9 billion in venture capital funding, ranking 12th among the top 14 U.S. cities, falling short of Austin at $3.7 billion and Miami at $5.6 billion.
The Georgia Tech Foundation (GTF) has been investing in private equity since the late 1980s.
In 2019, GTF noticed local startups such as Flock Safety and Stord were seeking funding outside Atlanta due to a lack of local VCs for seed to early-stage companies.
In 2020, GTF committed $20 million to five local venture capital firms and one direct Georgia Tech-related startup.
In 2024, GTF made another $25-$30 million commitment, with a portion going to new firms that have established themselves in Atlanta.
GTF requires its supported VC firms to be based in Atlanta, focus on startups in Atlanta and the Southeast, and maintain a fund size of less than $100 million.
BCG estimates that about 2,000 startups need to be launched annually in Atlanta to create a self-sustaining entrepreneurial flywheel effect.
Last year, Georgia Tech was named a top 20 school for entrepreneurship by The Princeton Review and Entrepreneur magazine.
GTF aims to keep talent in Atlanta and the state of Georgia through its U.S.-centric portfolio strategy.
source_title: Foundation Backs Atlanta Startups | Georgia Tech Foundation

facts: Autonomous vehicles (AVs) are defined as SAE International Levels 4- and 5-capable vehicles.
AVs present a generational opportunity to create safer roads, promote accessibility, ease supply chain challenges, and create new jobs for American workers.
A public policy framework is imperative to support a clear pathway to AV deployment and create jobs across the United States.

Policy measures should take into account supportive actions for investment in training, transition and job creation programs, upskilling, and levers to encourage expansion of investment in AV technology, building, and deployment.
Continued and expanded deployment across all autonomous vehicle modalities is inherent in solving for transitions and inspiring successful investments in the workforce.
Market penetration and scaling of autonomous vehicles will happen over time, with any workforce transitions occurring gradually.

Workforce impacts will not be uniform and will be unique to the type of AV deployed, geographic area, cargo or passenger type, and the operator’s commercial relationships with public and private sector partners.
Enabling rules that address operational and safety issues should be developed on a parallel track with workforce programs or initiatives.
Efforts to address workforce opportunities should be subject to open and transparent processes balancing government, worker, and industry representatives.
States are recommended to establish a workforce innovation committee composed of a balanced group of government, worker, and industry representatives appointed by the state governor.
This committee would serve as a partnership between government, workers, and industry to prepare workforce recommendations as the AV industry advances.

The committee can be used to identify and accomplish workforce-related policy goals, including providing a venue for industry to share information with government and labor partners about existing and upcoming workforce needs.
The committee can support workforce transition programs, including community and technical college programs, transition assistance, best practices, and research.
The committee can advise on funding strategies for workforce programs.
States should conduct and/or fund research projects in coordination with industry to analyze state-specific workforce needs and opportunities.

Research may evaluate how AV jobs will be created due to factors such as geographic location, workforce strengths, access to ports and interstates, community college training programs, existing truck routes, and other special attributes of a state.
States should identify ways to leverage these needs and opportunities to create jobs throughout the AV ecosystem.
States should create new programs, curriculum, and/or enhance existing workforce programs across the state.
States should leverage existing programs and funding to optimize workforce opportunities in the AV industry based on research, deployment timelines, and best practices.
States should establish grant programs for community colleges, universities, and other educational institutions to implement autonomous vehicle-related training programs.
These grant programs aim to ensure workers are taking advantage of high-quality jobs of the future.
source_title: Policy — US AV Jobs Coalition

facts: The initiative aims to enhance transportation efficiency and safety in the region.
The collaboration integrates May Mobility’s self-driving shuttles with T-Mobile’s 5G network to create a seamless and reliable transportation system.
The service will run from 6 a.m. to 9 p.m. on weekdays and from 6 a.m. to 7 p.m. on weekends.
May Mobility is set to launch its first rider-only experience.
The service is scheduled to open to the public on the week of October 7.
Peachtree Corners has partnered with May Mobility to revolutionize urban transportation and create a living lab for AV technology.
The partnership utilizes Multi-Policy De-Policy Technology, a system that allows for seamless integration of multiple transportation policies.
May Mobility partnered with T-Mobile to leverage its robust 5G network for the autonomous vehicle service.
The shuttles integrate Multi-Policy Decision Making (MPDM) systems, which enable real-time learning and adaptation from their environment.
The service is expected to start in the fall and will initially cover a 1.5-mile route between downtown Peachtree Corners and the Peachtree Corners Transit Center.
T-Mobile’s 5G network provides low latency, high speed connectivity, and high bandwidth to enhance the in-cabin experience for riders.
source_title: Georgia's highways await av revolution: mobile team's pioneering mob - EmbarkDrive

facts: Benford Capital Partners and Industrial QC Holdings, LLC announced the acquisition of Avalon Vision Solutions, LLC (AVS).
AVS represents Industrial QC’s fourth acquisition, following LiteSentry, LLC in November 2019, Softsolution GmbH in July 2021, and Strainoptics, LLC in August 2022.
AVS was founded in 1991 and is headquartered in Lithia Springs, Georgia.
AVS is a global leader in machine vision solutions for the injection molding and die casting industries.

The company’s flagship product, MoldWatcher, is a proprietary vision system used by leading manufacturers worldwide to ensure consistent quality, enhance efficiency, and eliminate costly downtime in molding and casting operations.
The addition of AVS expands Industrial QC into the plastics market, supporting its strategy of building a leading quality-control instrumentation platform across diverse applications and end markets.
Industrial QC is actively seeking additional acquisitions of instrumentation and services businesses selling into the glass, plastics, and other industrial markets.
Ed Kachnic is the Founder of AVS.
Brian Staff is an Operating Partner at Benford Capital Partners.
Founded in 2004, Benford Capital is a Chicago-based private equity firm focused on buying and building leading lower middle market companies in partnership with founders and management.
Since inception, Benford Capital has acquired over 60 companies and currently owns 16 platform companies.
BCP’s industry areas of focus include Food, Ingredients & Consumer Products, Industrial Technology & Manufacturing, Specialty Services, and Agricultural Products & Services.
Ed Kachnic stated: “We’re excited to partner with Benford Capital and Industrial QC, whose partnership-oriented approach aligns well with our culture.”
Ed Kachnic stated: “With Industrial QC’s extensive resources and experience in machine vision instrumentation, AVS is well positioned to grow in the years ahead.”
Brian Staff stated: “We are thrilled to add another leading machine vision business to the Industrial QC platform.”
Brian Staff stated: “AVS’s quality control solutions for high-cost-of-failure injection molding and die casting applications are an excellent complement to the systems of Industrial QC.”

links.context: Industrial QC website for add-on acquisition ideas

source_title: Industrial QC Completes 4th Acquisition - Benford Capital Partners

facts: Rivian’s new manufacturing facility in Georgia represents one of the largest greenfield EV factory investments in the United States.
The plant is designed to complement the existing Normal, Illinois facility.
The facility will add capacity for the R2 platform and future vehicle lines that have not yet been announced.
Taha Abbasi views the Georgia plant as Rivian’s clearest signal of its intent to compete at Tesla-level volumes.
The factory represents a multi-billion dollar investment, indicating it is not intended for niche production.
Georgia offers several advantages for EV manufacturing.
Georgia has invested heavily in attracting EV supply chain companies, creating a regional ecosystem that reduces logistics costs.
Proximity to southeastern markets, where truck and SUV demand is highest, reduces delivery costs and times.
The R2’s production ramp will initially occur at Normal.
The Georgia plant will provide overflow capacity if demand exceeds what a single plant can produce.
Rivian is following the Tesla playbook of proving the product at one factory before scaling across multiple facilities.
Site preparation and initial construction for the Georgia plant are underway.
Rivian has been measured in its public communications regarding timeline specifics.
The company has indicated that the Georgia plant will begin production “later this decade.”
This timeline is deliberately vague to preserve flexibility to adjust based on market conditions and R2 demand signals.
Georgia’s lower cost of living compared to Illinois reduces labor costs.
Georgia’s growing reputation as an EV hub helps attract talent.
Building an experienced automotive workforce from scratch takes years.
Taha Abbasi sees the Georgia plant as Rivian’s answer to whether the company can scale.
The Normal plant has a capacity for 215,000 units annually.
Georgia adds the next tier of capacity that Rivian needs to justify its market valuation.
The combination of R2 volume at Normal and future vehicles at Georgia positions Rivian for annual production exceeding 500,000 units.
Only Tesla, BYD, and a handful of Chinese manufacturers currently occupy the territory of producing over 500,000 units annually.
Rivian’s Georgia plant serves as a statement of intent.
Taha Abbasi will continue tracking construction progress as a leading indicator of whether Rivian can transition from a promising startup to a genuine Tesla competitor at scale.
Taha Abbasi is a technology executive, CTO, and applied frontier tech builder.
source_title: Rivian Georgia Plant: Building the Future of American EV Manufacturing | Taha Abbasi — Taha Salahuddin Abbasi

facts: A recent report from the Global Business Alliance reveals that Georgia is a key location for international investment.
Approximately 1,500 international investors employ more than 300,400 people in Georgia.
One-third of the jobs supported by international investors in Georgia are in manufacturing.
Companies from Canada, the U.K., and Japan support the largest number of jobs in Georgia.
Georgia outpaces the national average for the proportion of employment derived from foreign direct investment with 7.2% in Georgia vs. 6% nationally.
Preciball USA is opening a new production facility in Screven County.
The Preciball USA facility represents a $17.6 million investment that will create 65 new jobs over the next several years.
Preciball USA manufactures precision ball bearings for industrial use.
French company Saint-Gobain recently celebrated the conclusion of a $140 million expansion at its CertainTeed roofing facility in Peachtree City, Fayette County.
The Saint-Gobain/CertainTeed expansion doubled the facility's capacity to make roof shingles.

Atlanta Gas Light is building a liquefied natural gas facility in Lowndes County to help strengthen the region’s energy capacity and support more industrial growth, particularly related to food production.
The Atlanta Gas Light LNG facility represents a $50 million investment that will create nearly 100 jobs during construction.
The Atlanta Gas Light project will create several highly skilled positions once operational in 2027.
Pratt & Whitney celebrated a recent expansion that created 400 new jobs.
Pratt & Whitney is investing more than $200 million in yet another expansion in Columbus, initially creating 15 new jobs with more to follow.
Pratt & Whitney designs, manufactures and services aircraft engines and auxiliary power units for military, commercial and civil aviation.
The former CNN Center in Downtown Atlanta is being revived as The Center through a $65 million-plus project.
The Center redevelopment will include a food hall, Maestro’s Ocean Club as an anchor restaurant and the largest bar in the city.
The Center will serve as the home base for the Atlanta Host Committee during the FIFA World Cup 2026 and the event’s volunteer headquarters.
CTR Culture, in partnership with a local community-focused arts group, will feature public art, experiential activations and cultural programing at The Center.
source_title: Economic Development Around the State - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: Manufacturing GDP rose to $86.6 billion in 2024, driven by durable goods such as machinery, aerospace, and fabricated metals.
Manufacturing employment showed mild contraction, down roughly 0.7% year-over-year.
Transportation and Warehousing contributed $37.6 billion in economic output in 2024, highlighting Georgia’s critical logistics position in the southeastern U.S.
Advanced manufacturing sectors such as electric vehicles, aerospace, and automation present strong medium-term growth prospects for Georgia.
Georgia’s existing base in transportation equipment and fabricated metal products can serve as a springboard for advanced manufacturing growth.
There are ongoing opportunities to invest in port infrastructure (Savannah), air freight (Atlanta), and highway expansion to support Georgia’s role as a national logistics center.
Federal and state funds directed toward these logistics assets will be pivotal for regional economic development.
Skills gaps persist, particularly in technology and healthcare. While GDP in these areas is rising, matching labor supply with demand will require concerted investment in workforce development.
source_title: White Paper: The State of Georgia’s Economy in 2025 – Haruka Scott

facts: EV tax credits, charging rebates, and utility programs are available for Georgia residents to save on electric vehicles.
Charging cost in Georgia ranges from $0.10–$0.15/kWh.
Home charger costs range from $700–$2,400.
Annual fuel savings for EVs in Georgia are $800–$1,500 compared to gas vehicles.
Georgia drivers can charge an EV at home for the equivalent of roughly $1.00–$1.50 per gallon of gasoline.
The federal Clean Vehicle Credit (Internal Revenue Code Section 30D) historically offered up to $7,500 for qualifying new electric vehicles.
The Used Clean Vehicle Credit (Section 25E) offered up to $4,000 for qualifying used EVs.
Both federal EV tax credits were terminated for vehicles placed in service after December 31, 2025 under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act.
Georgia residents who purchased an eligible EV before that date may still claim the credit on their 2025 federal tax return.
The Alternative Fuel Vehicle Refueling Property Credit (Section 30C) provided up to $1,000 for home EV charger installation.
Commercial Section 30C credits for businesses installing chargers in low-income or non-urban census tracts ended on the same schedule as the residential credit.
Georgia state-level EV programs vary year to year as legislatures appropriate or sunset funding.

Common Georgia state incentive categories include income tax credits for EV purchase, sales-tax exemptions on vehicles or charging equipment, reduced registration fees, HOV-lane access stickers, and rebates funded through the Volkswagen Diesel Settlement or NEVI corridor programs.
Some states route incentives through their Department of Energy, Department of Environmental Quality, or Department of Transportation rather than through the tax code.
Funding pools for state EV incentives are often capped and close once the budget is exhausted.
Many states fund equity-focused EV rebates that target lower-income households with larger amounts, often $2,500–$7,500.
Public charging in Georgia is expanding rapidly thanks to the federal NEVI (National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure) program.
The NEVI program directs roughly $5 billion across all 50 states over five years to build DC fast-charging stations along Alternative Fuel Corridors.
Georgia's NEVI allocation funds charging stations roughly every 50 miles along designated interstates.
Each NEVI-funded site in Georgia is required to have at least four 150 kW DC fast chargers.
A Level 2 (240V) home charging station typically costs $400–$900 for the unit.
Home charger installation costs range from $300–$1,500 depending on panel capacity, garage wiring distance, and required electrical service upgrades.
Some Georgia utilities offer rebates of $250–$1,000 toward home charger installation.
Utility charger rebates often require customers to agree to off-peak charging or allow demand-response control during grid peaks.
Georgia utilities increasingly offer EV-specific time-of-use (TOU) rate plans that significantly reduce home charging costs.
Overnight electricity on TOU rates is typically priced at half or less of the standard daytime rate, often dropping to $0.05–$0.08 per kWh.
Charging an EV exclusively during off-peak hours can cut effective per-mile fuel cost to $0.02–$0.03.
Other utility programs include managed-charging credits of $50–$150 per year for allowing utilities to delay charging during grid peaks.
Utilities also offer bill credits for installing smart chargers that report usage data.
Pairing rooftop solar with an EV is a cost-effective way for Georgia homeowners to lock in low transportation costs.
A typical 7–10 kW solar array generates enough surplus electricity to cover an additional 10,000–12,000 miles of EV driving per year.
Once installed, the marginal cost of driving an EV charged with rooftop solar is essentially zero.
Combining solar with battery storage and a TOU rate plan can result in a combined payback period of 7–11 years on the full system for Georgia homeowners.
At average Georgia residential electricity rates, charging a typical EV for a full 250-mile range costs roughly $9–$13.
On time-of-use overnight rates, charging for a 250-mile range drops to $4–$7.
Compared to a gasoline vehicle averaging 30 mpg at $3.50/gallon ($29 for the same 250 miles), home charging cuts fuel costs by 50–80%.

Georgia's public charging network is growing through federal NEVI funding, private investment from networks like Tesla Supercharger, ChargePoint, EVgo, and Electrify America, and utility-led infrastructure programs.
DC fast chargers in Georgia are clustered along interstates and in metro areas.
For most Georgia EV owners with daily commutes over 30 miles or a household with two EVs, a Level 2 charger is recommended.
A Level 2 charger cuts charging time from 40+ hours (Level 1) down to 4–8 hours overnight.
Total installed cost for a home Level 2 charger is typically $700–$2,400.
A 7–10 kW solar system in Georgia can offset both home electricity and EV charging, effectively giving free fuel after the system is paid off.
source_title: EV Incentives in Georgia – Electric Vehicle Rebates, Tax Credits & Charging Programs | Net-Zero USA – Energy Incentive Calculator

facts: Big Tex Trailer Manufacturing, Inc. has acquired Circle J Trailers of Caldwell, Idaho.
Circle J Trailers is a 51-year-old manufacturer of steel, aluminum exterior, and all-aluminum horse trailers.
Circle J Trailers is one of the oldest trailer companies in the United States and a respected name in the horse trailer industry.
Operations will continue in Caldwell in the current facility.

Big Tex recognizes the value and reputation of Circle J Trailers and its premium quality trailers continuing to play a leading role in the Western U.S. and Western Canadian markets through its network of loyal and capable dealers.
In addition to the continued manufacture of Circle J trailers, Big Tex trailers will also be produced in Caldwell.
The large facility will serve as a distribution center for CM Trailers and CM Truck Bed products.
Ricky Baker serves as President/CEO of Big Tex.

Ricky Baker stated: "We are really excited about this new opportunity with Circle J Trailers. The name is legendary and it is truly an honor to be associated with the history and reputation of Circle J."

Baker noted that the acquisition will enable serving dealers in the region with other products, offering faster deliveries, lower prices, and the opportunity to purchase less than truckload quantities.
In February, Big Tex announced the purchase of a manufacturing facility in Cordele, Georgia.
The Cordele, Georgia facility has begun distribution of Big Tex, CM, S&H, and CM truck beds.
Manufacturing operations at the Cordele, Georgia facility are scheduled to begin in the fourth quarter.

links.context: Brad Wahlert, Big Tex New Dealer Sales
Mark Osteen, CM Trailers New Dealer Sales

source_title: Big Tex Acquires Circle J Trailers | Trailer Body Builders

facts: Georgia Ports Authority is expanding its rail cargo capacity to move containers across the state and into the Midwest with greater speed and efficiency.

GPA General Manager of Inland Operations Wesley Barrell spoke at the inaugural Greater Hall Chamber of Commerce Logistics Forum on Aug. 6, 2024, regarding development progress of the Blue Ridge Connector near Gainesville, Ga.
Construction of the Blue Ridge Connector is progressing well, with another six weeks of earthwork remaining to meet the rail yard grade for additional engineering.
GPA is working closely with Norfolk Southern on tying its infrastructure into their existing track at the Blue Ridge Connector site.
When complete, the 104-acre Blue Ridge Connector site will feature six tracks for a total of 18,000 feet.
The facility will create 20 direct local jobs.
The inland port will link Northeast Georgia with the Port of Savannah’s 36 global container ship services that call each week.
Norfolk Southern Railroad will provide five-day a week service to the Blue Ridge Connector, which will operate from 7 a.m. to 6 p.m. Monday through Friday.
GPA will provide expedited service from Savannah to the BRC with third day availability.
Local businesses such as poultry producers have shown great interest in the new rail terminal.
The facility will offer refrigerated cargo by rail, with a unique transit from the Blue Ridge Connector to Savannah.
There are five cold storage freezers within five miles of the location, and more than 50 users of those cold storage facilities.
GPA looks to provide services to this customer base.
The $127 million facility is slated to open in 2026.
It will serve an existing customer base, which also includes producers of heavy equipment and forest products.
The BRC will act as an economic catalyst for Northeast Georgia, bringing jobs and opportunity.
Presently, nearly 10,000 Hall County jobs are in industries that rely on Georgia Ports.
An existing, locally owned company recently began operation of a foreign trade zone within 10 miles of the BRC to serve international cargo moving through the rail yard.
The FTZ allows import cargo to be held for storage, assembly or manufacturing without payment of duties until the goods move into the domestic market.
The FTZ will give cargo owners greater flexibility in the timing of their supply chain.
Intermodal service to Savannah will offer a competitive transportation option with reduced carbon footprint through lower emissions per container.
The presence of the inland terminal in Hall County will provide a ready source of empty containers for exports produced in the region.
Customers will no longer need to truck containers through Hall County to the Port of Savannah, because shorter routes to the inland port will become possible.
Every container moved by rail means fewer trucks on state highways moving through the Atlanta area.
The Blue Ridge Connector will have an annual capacity of 200,000 containers.
Georgia Ports’ inland rail connections are supported by the Mason Mega Rail Terminal at the Port of Savannah, the largest on-port intermodal facility in North America.
Mason Mega Rail features 24 miles of on-terminal track.
It is capable of serving six 10,000-foot trains simultaneously.
Mason Mega Rail doubles Georgia Ports’ previous intermodal capacity to 2 million TEUs a year.
The terminal is integral to GPA’s “1,2,3” cargo strategy: one day off the vessel, two days transportation, and third day availability.
Unmatched capacity links Savannah to a broad swath of inland markets through Class I rail service.
By speeding the flow of cargo, Mason Mega Rail is changing how cargo reaches inland destinations from Atlanta and Dallas to Memphis, Chicago and other locations across the Midwest.
Cargo moves from vessel offload to departing rail in Savannah in just 29 hours, the fastest of any major U.S. port.
The Blue Ridge Connector is the latest step in GPA’s strategic rail strategy.
Beyond the Savannah market, the Georgia Ports Authority operates the Appalachian Regional Port in Northwest Georgia.
Customers can take advantage of inland ports to move cargo closer to destination markets by rail, reducing overall supply chain emissions compared to all-truck transit.

In Fiscal Year 2024 (ended June 30), GPA achieved record trade of 36,730 rail lifts at its ARP, a 9 percent increase over FY2023, which was itself a record year for the terminal near Chatsworth in Northwest Georgia.

Georgia Ports has landed major accounts that have located near the Appalachian Regional Port, including GE Appliances, LG and solar technology producer Hanwha Qcells, which is now on its third expansion.
Other goods handled at the ARP include carpet and flooring, automotive manufacturing components, and tires, among other products.
With easy access to Interstate 75 and U.S. 411, target markets include Georgia, Alabama, Tennessee and Kentucky.
A recently added offering is the Carolina Connector service, which links the Port of Savannah to Rocky Mount, N.C., via CSX rail.
The speed of the connection means importers can get access to cargo before it could even be offloaded at another East Coast port.
For exporters, the inland terminal in Rocky Mount provides significant reductions truck transit times.
From the Raleigh-Durham area, a driver can reach the CSX yard in 30 minutes, instead of three hours to the nearest mid-Atlantic port.

Georgia’s ports and inland terminals support more than 561,000 jobs throughout the state annually, contributing $33 billion in income, $140 billion in revenue and $3.8 billion in state and local taxes to Georgia’s economy.
GPA anticipates investing $4.2 billion in the next ten years as part of its port master plan to expand cargo handling capabilities to support future supply chain requirements.
As part of community engagement efforts, $6 million will be donated to communities located near the Port of Savannah to support a multi-year local workforce housing initiative.
CNBC ranked Georgia #1 in the U.S. for infrastructure in America’s Top States for Business in 2023.
Area Development has ranked Georgia as the Top State to Do Business for ten consecutive years.
source_title: Georgia Ports expanding inland connectivity - Georgia Ports Authority

facts: Blue Ridge Manufacturing, Llc (freedom Truck Beds) operates in the trailer and truck industry.
The company manufactures western flatbeds, stake bodies, dump bodies, landscape bodies, contractor bodies, and custom designed truckbeds.
Its products feature powder coating, LED lights, quality designs, and undercoated sub-floors.
Charles Rayside is the listed contact person for the company.
The company's landline phone number is (706) 632-9444.
The company's facility is located at 87 Tom Boyd Rd, Blue Ridge, GA 30513, United States.
Blue Ridge Manufacturing participated in US WTS 2022.
Blue Ridge Manufacturing participated in Natda Ca 2022.
Blue Ridge Manufacturing participated in Natda Us 2021.
Blue Ridge Manufacturing is associated with VIN Vehicle Company.
Blue Ridge Manufacturing is a member of US NTEA.
Blue Ridge Manufacturing participated in US WTW 2024.
Blue Ridge Manufacturing participated in US WTW 2025.

facts: Gov. Brian Kemp announced that hundreds of new manufacturing jobs are heading to east central Georgia.
GF Casting Solutions AG will invest over $184 million for a new facility in Augusta.
The project is expected to create 350 new jobs in Richmond County.
GF Casting Solutions is part of Georg Fischer AG, based in Schaffhausen, Switzerland.
The company develops and creates manufacturing parts from aluminum, magnesium, iron, and super alloys for light vehicles, trucks, aerospace, energy, off-highway vehicles, and industrial uses.

Carlos Vasto, President of GF Casting Solutions, stated that building the high-pressure die casting facility is a very long-term investment to complete their global footprint in the e-mobility market.
The company aims to bring leading technology to the US and create advanced manufacturing jobs for several generations in Georgia.
Carlos Vasto noted that the Augusta location is ideal for addressing the thriving regional automotive industry.
The new facility will be located at a new LEED-certified site at Augusta Corporate Park in Richmond County.
The manufacturing site is expected to produce aluminum parts, with a focus on large structural components for the automotive industry.
Operations are expected to begin in 2027.
GF Casting Solutions will hire for roles in both manufacturing and automation over the coming years.
source_title: Gov. Kemp announces hundreds of e-mobility manufacturing jobs coming to east central Georgia – WSB-TV Channel 2 - Atlanta

facts: Bosch is investing 100 million euros (120 million U.S. dollars) in a new plant in Celaya, Mexico.
The company plans to build a new smart factory for electronic components in the central Mexican city of Celaya by 2020.

Stefan Hartung, member of the Bosch board of management, stated ahead of HANNOVER MESSE: “Bosch is committed to Mexico. The country is and will remain an important market and a hub for our global manufacturing and development network.”
The goal is to create more than 1,200 additional jobs at the new location in Celaya in the coming years.
In total, the Bosch Group employs around 16,000 associates in Mexico.
Bosch is active with all four of its business divisions in Mexico.
In 2016, the company generated sales of 1.1 billion euros in Mexico.
The Bosch Group employs roughly 413,000 associates worldwide as of December 31, 2025.
The company generated sales of 91 billion euros in 2025.
Bosch's operations are divided into four business sectors: Mobility, Industrial Technology, Consumer Goods, and Energy and Building Technology.
Bosch aims to use technology to help shape universal trends such as automation, digitalization, electrification, and artificial intelligence.
Bosch employs some 82,000 associates in research and development.
The company was set up in Stuttgart in 1886 by Robert Bosch (1861-1942) as “Workshop for Precision Mechanics and Electrical Engineering.”
Ninety-four percent of the share capital of Robert Bosch GmbH is held by Robert Bosch Stiftung GmbH, a limited liability company with a charitable purpose.
The remaining shares are held by Robert Bosch GmbH and by a company owned by the Bosch family.
The majority of voting rights are held by Robert Bosch Industrietreuhand KG, which safeguards the company's long-term existence and financial independence.

links.context: Phone for Trix Böhne, Mexico / International media relations

source_title: Manufacturing hub Mexico: Bosch plans smart plant for electronic components - Bosch Media Service

facts: Bosch offers careers in the USA under the #LikeABosch campaign.
The company states it cares for its associates, business, and environment.
Bosch's promise to associates includes growing together, enjoying work, and inspiring each other.
The company provides tailor-made career programs and values diverse perspectives.
Bosch offers opportunities across all phases of a career.

Bosch is an Equal Opportunity Employer that considers all qualified applicants without regard to race, color, religion, sex, sexual orientation, gender identity, marital status, national origin or ancestry, age, disability, veteran status, genetic information, or any other protected class.
source_title: Careers | Bosch in the USA

facts: German automotive supplier Bosch plans to lay off 5,000 employees.
Some 3,800 of the job cuts are to be made in Germany.
The exact number of layoffs will be negotiated in talks with workers' representatives.
Bosch stated it is making significant investments in new technologies.
Bosch manager Stephan Hölzl said, 'We must adapt our structures to the changing market environment and reduce costs sustainably to strengthen our competitiveness.'
Global vehicle production will stagnate this year at around 93 million units, if not decline slightly compared to the previous year.
Manufacturers need significantly fewer parts to make electric vehicles, also making the process less labor-intensive.

Bosch plans to cut up to 1,300 jobs between 2027 and 2030 at its division that makes steering systems for cars and trucks, which is based in Swäbisch Gmünd in the southwestern German state of Baden Württemberg.
The firm had already announced plans in December 2023 to lay off 1,500 workers.
Frank Sell, head of the workers' council for Bosch's automotive division in Germany, called the planned layoffs a 'slap in the face' and vowed resistance to the move.
The crisis has hit a number of other German manufacturers, with ZF, Continental and Webasto also announcing layoffs.
Volkswagen (VW) has said it plans wide-reaching plant closures and layoffs in order to reduce labor costs, citing difficulties breaking into the competitive electric vehicle market.
Workers' representatives urged VW to strike a deal and threatened potential industrial action starting in December if talks fall through.
The European Union increased tariffs on Chinese-imported electric vehicles to as much as 45.3%.
source_title: Germany: Bosch to cut 5,000 jobs with car industry in crisis

facts: As of December 31, 2021, the Bosch Group employs nearly 35,500 associates in more than 100 locations in North America.
In 2021, Bosch generated consolidated sales of $13.5 billion in the U.S., Canada and Mexico.
The company employs roughly 402,600 associates worldwide as of December 31, 2021.
Bosch generated sales of $93.1 billion in 2021.
Operations are divided into four business sectors: Mobility Solutions, Industrial Technology, Consumer Goods, and Energy and Building Technology.
As a leading IoT provider, Bosch offers innovative solutions for smart homes, Industry 4.0, and connected mobility.
Bosch uses its expertise in sensor technology, software, and services to offer customers connected, cross-domain solutions from a single source.
The company's strategic objective is to facilitate connected living with products and solutions that either contain artificial intelligence (AI) or have been developed or manufactured with its help.
The Bosch Group comprises Robert Bosch GmbH and its roughly 440 subsidiary and regional companies in some 60 countries.
Including sales and service partners, the global manufacturing, engineering, and sales network covers nearly every country in the world.
With more than 400 locations worldwide, the company has been carbon neutral since the first quarter of 2020.
At 128 locations across the globe, Bosch employs some 76,100 associates in research and development, of which more than 38,000 are software engineers.
source_title: Bosch announces electric motor production in Charleston and more than $260 million in new investment  - Bosch Media Service US

facts: Robert Bosch founded his first company in 1886 at age 25 with a business partner as a precision mechanics workshop.
The early company fabricated bells, telephones, and sold medical equipment.
Robert Bosch invented magnetic ignition, which was essential for high-revving engines and enabled the birth of the automotive industry.
Bosch established a team of innovation experts in the 1930s to monitor industry trends and suggest areas for investment, invention, and acquisition.
The company diversified its product range early on to cover almost everything powered by a motor, including hair trimmers, power tools, and refrigerators.
Robert Bosch placed great emphasis on the salary, well-being, and training of associates, founded a hospital and sanatorium with 400 beds, and introduced the 8-hour working day.
Bosch has been present in Hungary since 1898 with its products.
After re-establishment as a regional trading company in 1991, Bosch grew into one of Hungary’s largest foreign industrial employers with currently eight subsidiaries.
In fiscal 2023, Bosch Hungary had total net sales of 2.207 billion forints and consolidated sales to third parties on the Hungarian market of 343 billion forints.
The Bosch Group in Hungary employs more than 18,300 associates as of December 31, 2023.
The Bosch Group is a leading global supplier of technology and services that employs roughly 429,000 associates worldwide as of December 31, 2023.
The company generated sales of 91.6 billion euros in 2023.
Operations are divided into four business sectors: Mobility, Industrial Technology, Consumer Goods, and Energy and Building Technology.
Bosch aims to use technology to help shape universal trends such as automation, electrification, digitalization, connectivity, and an orientation to sustainability.
The company uses expertise in sensor technology, software, services, connectivity, and artificial intelligence to develop user-friendly, sustainable products.
The Bosch Group comprises Robert Bosch GmbH and roughly 470 subsidiary and regional companies in over 60 countries.
Including sales and service partners, the global manufacturing, engineering, and sales network covers nearly every country in the world.
At 136 locations across the globe, Bosch employs some 90,000 associates in research and development, of which nearly 48,000 are software engineers.
