facts: A Japanese company announced plans Wednesday for a $150 million plant to make copper foil for electric vehicle batteries in Augusta, Georgia.
The plant location was shifted from a previously announced site near an existing Camden, South Carolina, facility.
Nippon Denkai's American subsidiary said it would hire more than 100 people.
The company could eventually triple production at the Georgia site from an initial 9,500 metric tons (10,500 tons) to 28,500 metric tons (31,500 tons).
Tripling production would raise investment to $430 million and create 250 jobs over the next five years.
Denkai America spokesperson Michael Coll said: "Our goal is to triple the capacity in Augusta; market conditions will determine timeline."
The company raised its initial investment by $10 million from what it had announced in December.
Nippon Denkai told Japanese investors that the 115-acre (47-hectare) Augusta site is better than the South Carolina location because there is room for expansion.
The Augusta site can host an "advanced" equipment layout.
The Augusta site offers a "key cost competitive edge in electricity price."
State and local officials will give the company what could be more than $76 million in tax breaks and incentives.
Nippon Denkai said it will start construction in September.
The company hopes to be shipping samples to manufacturers by the summer of 2024.
The company sees a big opportunity to make copper foil for electric vehicle battery makers, noting many battery plants are being built in the United States but few copper foil plants are.
Companies will want domestic sources to avoid supply chain problems including risks of politically driven supply disruptions.
Nippon Denkai projects an annual 21.4% growth in copper foil demand through 2035.
The ability to triple production in Augusta will allow the company to pursue that projected growth.
Combined with the 30-year-old Camden plant, the company would have an American production capacity of more than 35,000 metric tons.
In June, Denkai announced a smaller $14 million expansion at the 80-worker Camden plant, also related to expansion in the electric battery and vehicle market.
Augusta-Richmond County will give property tax breaks valued at a projected $68.7 million over 25 years.
The company will pay $30 million in taxes, according to the Augusta Economic Development Authority.
The company gets free land valued at $2.25 million, said Cal Wray, the authority's president.
Georgia officials said they will spend an undisclosed amount of money to train workers.
Nippon Denkai could claim various state tax breaks, including an income tax credit allowing it to annually deduct $4,000 per job from state income taxes, up to $5 million over five years.
The income tax credit applies as long as workers make at least $28,000 a year.
Georgia Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson pointed to the announcement as more fruit of the state's focus on what he calls a "generational opportunity" in electric vehicles.
The state's focus includes the $5 billion Rivian Automotive plant east of Atlanta.
The state's focus includes the $5.5 billion Hyundai Motor Group plant near Savannah.
Cal Wray said a $347 million, 125-job copper smelter announced by Aurubis AG in Augusta is another development driven by the growth in electric vehicles.
source_title: Japanese company, Nippon Denkai, shifts location for $150 million copper foil plant from SC to Georgia | Fox Business

facts: Denkai America will build its North American headquarters in Augusta, Georgia.

facts: Denkai America is a leader in the manufacture of high-quality electrodeposited copper foils for printed circuit board (PCB), industrial, and energy storage applications.
The company delivers both conventional cladding and application specific copper foils required by technology leading companies.
Denkai America has over 40 years of experience manufacturing copper foil with a focus on quality and service for domestic and international customers.
Copper foils are manufactured using an electroplating process on revolving titanium drums to achieve a high-quality surface.
Plated foil rolls undergo various surface treating operations tailored to specific end-use requirements.
The manufacturing process deposits copper dendrites onto the foil surface to enhance bonding to resin systems during lamination.
A barrier-layer is applied to promote reliability in thermal applications.
Proprietary organic layers are used to promote chemical adhesion, and foils are stain-proofed to prevent oxidation during shipping, storage, lamination, and customer processing.
Denkai America's estimated annual revenue is $6.7M.
The company has an estimated revenue per employee of $196,000.
Denkai America currently employs 34 people.
The company's employee count grew by 10% in the last year.
Key personnel include a Director and a Materials Manager, though contact information is redacted.

Denkai America spokesperson Michael Coll stated, “Our goal is to triple the capacity in Augusta; market conditions will determine timeline.”

facts: Down 2 Earth Trailers was founded in 1999 by Paul Dubberly and his son Andrew.
The company started in the small town of Baxley, GA.
In its early days, the company focused on single axle utility trailers.
Over time, the company added Equipment, Gooseneck, and Dump trailer lines as commercial use became more common.
Down 2 Earth has been building trailers for more than 23 years.
Each month, more than 1,000 trailers roll out of the Baxley, GA plant.
The company produces over 70 different models at various stages of the build process on any given day.
Down 2 Earth employs around 100 staff members from Appling and surrounding counties.
Employee roles include Welders, Electricians, Carpenters, Powder coat specialists, Salesmen, Logistics, Drivers, Parts specialists, and Office personnel.
In July 2020, the company was acquired by Triple Crown Trailers of Ocala, FL.
Despite the acquisition, the company continues to build the Down 2 Earth brand.
The broader trailer manufacturing industry is a multi-billion dollar industry producing a wide range of trailers for various purposes.
The combined Triple Crown/Down 2 Earth company is now top 5 in the country in trailer manufacturing.
Down 2 Earth trailer dealers are located all over the Eastern side of the United States.

facts: The role of home energy management in the electrification of homes is discussed as part of today's energy transition offering homeowners opportunities to leverage intelligent, connected technologies for sustainable, autonomous, and energy-efficient homes.

Eaton and industry experts from Ample Electric, Rewiring America, and Tesla will discuss opportunities for whole home electrification to reduce homeowner carbon footprints, keep the power on, and help support the electric grid.
The discussion will cover hurdles that stand in the way to home electrification and how intelligent technologies help accelerate the transformation.
Solar-ready meter breakers integrate solar power into residential electrical systems by combining utility and solar PV power in one enclosure.
These breakers offer cost savings, ease of installation, and increased home market value by allowing future solar additions without panel changes.
The breakers maximize PV system potential and meet NEC compliance.
Recent code updates for Arc-Fault Circuit Interrupters (AFCI) require AFCI protection in guest rooms, nursing homes, and sleeping quarters in fire stations and police stations.
Power management companies are extending AFCI technology beyond dwelling units to ensure safety across various locations.

Eaton is helping customers transform homes into strategic energy hubs to take advantage of the new power paradigm, better manage existing electrical infrastructure, maximize electrical safety, and prepare for future energy requirements.
This initiative is called the Home as a Grid strategy, creating flexible energy systems that accelerate decarbonization, boost resilience, reduce energy costs, and exceed the demands of the future.
The Bussmann series CAD configurator provides access to hundreds of products as fully integrated, intelligent 3D CAD models with technical details and data sheets.
Eaton will host the IEEE IAS Electrical Safety Workshop #412, 414, 416, 418 in Jacksonville, FL from Mar 3-7.
Eaton will host the ASHE PDC Summit #1010 in Atlanta, GA from Mar 9-12.
Eaton will host TechAdvantage 2025 in Atlanta, GA from March 10-12.

Additional hosted events include DISTRIBUTECH (Mar 25-27 | Dallas, TX), Automation and technical showcase (March 24 | Dallas, TX), AISTech (May 5-8 | Nashville, TN), and IEEE Cement Conference (May 4-8 | Nashville, TN).
source_title: Staying Current: Home Electrification

facts: Eaton announced significant investments in its North American manufacturing and operations to support rapidly growing demand for electrical solutions.
The majority of these investments will be complete in 2024 and 2025.
Mike Yelton, president, Americas Region, Electrical Sector at Eaton, stated that electrical infrastructure has to work harder and smarter to accelerate decarbonization and electrification.
Eaton is investing more than $500 million in North American manufacturing to support electrification, energy transition and digitalization across industries.
Eaton is adding 200,000-square-feet to its Nacogdoches, Texas manufacturing facility, more than doubling its size.
The project will double production capacity of Eaton’s voltage regulators to help utility customers across North America accelerate grid modernization and resilience projects.
Eaton will continue to produce single-phase, pole-mount and pad-mount transformers in Texas to address the acute supply shortage for these products.
The expansion will create more than 200 skilled manufacturing jobs.
The Texas expansion frees up capacity at the company’s largest Wisconsin manufacturing facility in Waukesha.
Eaton is investing in equipment at its Waukesha, Wisconsin facility to increase manufacturing of three-phase transformers for utility, data center, large commercial and industrial applications.
Eaton will continue to produce regulators, including complex pole and substation voltage regulators, for utility customers in Wisconsin.
Eaton is increasing production of busway to meet rapidly growing demand for this power distribution technology used in commercial, industrial and fleet charging applications.

This expansion includes Eaton’s EV charging busway, an industry-first innovation that dramatically simplifies and expedites infrastructure for fleet charging, while avoiding major infrastructure modifications.
For more than 60 years, Eaton has manufactured hundreds of thousands of miles of busway in South Carolina and continues to expand manufacturing capacity regionally.
Eaton is increasing the manufacturing of its circuit breakers and metering for homes and commercial buildings.

These investments include expanding capacity and diversifying production across various locations in the Americas region to increase the supply of solutions for customers, enhance supply chain efficiency and boost manufacturing resilience.
Eaton is adding capacity to increase supplies of essential power distribution equipment to support infrastructure projects across industries.
The company’s most recent investments enhance its manufacturing facilities for switchgear and switchboards, which provide the power backbone for customers across the Americas.
Eaton is investing in the distribution capacity for its power quality products and connectivity solutions.
The company recently opened its largest regional distribution center in Chicago.
Eaton is expanding its distribution facility in Dallas to serve commercial, data center, industrial, healthcare and communications customers.
source_title: Eaton invests more than $500 million in North American manufacturing to support electrification, energy transition and digitalization across industries

facts: Gov. Nathan Deal announced that Georgia’s business climate has been ranked No. 1 in the nation for the sixth consecutive year by Site Selection.
Georgia is the first state to hold this ranking for six consecutive years under leadership by the same governor.
Governor Deal stated that earning the distinction six consecutive times speaks to the strength of the business-friendly environment and strategic investments made across the state.
The announcement resulted from collaboration between public and private sector partners alongside conservative policies attracting business.
Georgia has cut taxes and made deeper investments in its workforce since first earning the distinction.
Site Selection releases its business climate rankings each November based on a survey of corporate real estate executives and site consultants.

The ranking index uses tax burden criteria from the Tax Foundation and KPMG’s Location Matters analysis, plus qualifying projects from the Conway Projects Database tracking worldwide facility activity.
Georgia has been ranked among the top 10 states throughout the last decade and held the top spot for five years prior to this announcement.
Site Selection editor-in-chief Mark Arend noted a six-year top business climate streak is highly unusual and credits Governor Deal’s early commitment to making Georgia competitive.
Capital investors report that Georgia delivers fiscal soundness, logistics infrastructure, competitive business costs, and required workers.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson, former GDEcD Commissioners Attorney General Chris Carr, and Chris Cummiskey joined Governor Deal at the news conference.
Over the past eight years, Georgia has seen the creation of roughly 750,000 new private sector jobs.
Georgia’s unemployment rate is at 3.7 percent, its lowest point since 2001.

In October 2018 alone, Governor Deal and GDEcD announced the creation of more than 2,500 jobs and over $215 million in private sector investment across Cobb, Emanuel, Fulton, Hall, and Troup counties.
Since 2011, GDEcD has worked on more than 2,900 economic development locations, bringing roughly 214,000 jobs and $39.6 billion in investment to Georgia communities.
GDEcD’s Global Commerce Division helped bring in $5.56 billion in new investments for Georgia during the last fiscal year.
Overall exports from Georgia have increased by 28.8 percent since 2010.
Since 2012, 74 percent of GDEcD location projects were located outside the metro Atlanta area.
When state tax cuts passed earlier this year are fully implemented, Georgia’s corporate and individual income tax rates will be reduced from 6 percent to 5.5 percent.
State tax reforms will double the standard deduction for individuals and families from $3,000 to $6,000.
Federal and state tax reforms could save Georgians up to $5 billion over the next five years.
These tax reforms are estimated to lead to a $4,200 increase in wage and salary income for the average household.
The percent of Georgia’s population living below the poverty line has decreased by 2.1 percent since January 2017, reaching its lowest point since 2006.
Monthly initial claims for unemployment insurance in Georgia recently fell to their lowest level since 1974.
Georgia has maintained a AAA bond rating for 21 consecutive years.
The state’s Rainy Day Fund has topped $2.5 billion, ranking it the highest in the Southeast and one of the top ten highest in the nation.

Site Selection publishes or co-publishes several e-newsletters including The Site Selection Dispatch, Life Sciences Report, Aerospace Report, Energy Report, Site Selection International, The FDI Report, TrustBelt.com, and the OnSITE Travel blog.

facts: Ecoplastic America Corporation is looking to purchase roughly 7.3 acres of Development Authority-owned property located across U.S. Highway 301 South from its Bulloch County manufacturing facility.

The proposed land sale was reviewed during the Development Authority of Bulloch County's June 9 meeting, but a vote to approve the agreement was tabled after questions were raised about potential residential development at the site.
The property is located about a mile and a half north of the Interstate 16 exit onto U.S. Highway 301.

Development Authority CEO Benjy Thompson stated that early conversations with Ecoplastic focused primarily on commercial uses, but company representatives later indicated interest in housing-related uses and sleeping quarters.
Ecoplastic America Corporation manufactures injection-molded plastic automotive body parts for Hyundai Motor Group vehicles.
The company has expressed interest in assigning ownership of the purchased property to an affiliated entity that could pursue commercial or residential projects.
Development Authority board member Tal Johnson noted that Ecoplastic has a need to house workers, including local hires and employees coming from their home country.
Board member Timmy Rushing questioned whether the proposed housing would resemble traditional apartments, company-sponsored workforce housing, or a transient arrangement.
The Statesboro Herald reached out to Ecoplastic about the land sale and housing needs but did not receive a response in time for publication.
Benjy Thompson noted that the housing component was unexpected but not surprising since Hyundai Motor Group announced the Ellabell Metaplant and suppliers began locating throughout the region.
Hyundai suppliers in Bulloch County, including Ecoplastic, Ajin Georgia, and Hanon Systems, have committed to creating about 1,200 job positions.

Many of the 1,200 jobs will be filled by local residents, but Hyundai suppliers have asked local officials about housing options for Korean employees who temporarily relocate to the United States to help establish and operate facilities.

The Development Authority's role in housing issues is generally limited, although it has previously partnered on workforce housing initiatives including support for the Cornerstone Pointe workforce housing development on Highway 67 through a rural workforce housing grant program.
Benjy Thompson stated that commercial development is lacking near the site and could be beneficial due to its proximity to Interstate 16.
The site under consideration is currently zoned for highway commercial, and Ecoplastic would have to request rezoning if their plans do not align with current zoning ordinances.

The property was originally purchased by the Development Authority as part of a long-term industrial recruitment strategy but is not located within an industrial park and has not received site preparation.
SECO Ecoplastic had hoped to close on the property by the end of June, but Benjy Thompson indicated the timeline would be extended to allow sufficient time for due diligence.

The proposed sale agreement includes a provision requiring construction to begin within 24 months, with a repurchase option for the Development Authority at the original sale price between $400,000 and $500,000 if construction does not start within that period.
The repurchase option would be available during a six-month window after the 24-month construction deadline expires.

The agreement references restrictive covenants to govern property use, which the Authority has not finalized but will likely apply requirements similar to those used on other development authority-owned industrial properties.
Any final decision on the land sale will return to the Development Authority board for approval during a public meeting.
source_title: Proposed Ecoplastic land purchase could bring worker housing development to Bulloch County - Statesboro Herald

Ecoplastic Corporation looks forward to contributing to the economic development of the State of Georgia and Bulloch County through the project.
Ecoplastic Corporation appreciates the support of the State and County governments for the initiative.
Ecoplastic has decades of experience as a supplier for Kia and Hyundai.
The company is building the supplier network for the Hyundai metaplant in Bryan County.
Suppliers are spreading out across the region to provide well-paying, high-tech manufacturing jobs for Georgians.
Bulloch County officials welcome Ecoplastic to their community and cite the area's location, workforce, and quality of life as factors that will help them succeed.

Recruiters for three plants currently under construction in Bulloch County that will supply parts to the Hyundai plant will attend the job fair.
The job fair is scheduled for 10 a.m.-4 p.m. on Thursday, April 4.

WorkSource Georgia is partnering with the Metaplant as a sponsor for the job fair.
Approximately 3,500 jobs will be available between the Hyundai EV plant and its local suppliers through 2024.
Career opportunities at the event will include roles in manufacturing, logistics, technology, and administration.
Ecoplastic America Corporation, a supplier of injection-molded plastic automotive body parts to Hyundai Motor Group, is building a $205-million plant off Highway 301 South.
The Ecoplastic America plant will create 456 jobs phased in over eight years.
Ajin USA, a supplier of metal auto body parts to Hyundai, is investing $317 million.

Hanon Systems, a manufacturer of vehicle air conditioning systems, will build a plant in Statesboro’s Gateway Regional Industrial Park off Highway 301 South.
Hanon Systems' $40 million investment is projected to create 160 jobs.

facts: Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp announced that automotive parts manufacturer Ecoplastic Corporation will create 456 jobs and invest about $200 million in a new manufacturing facility.
Ecoplastic Corporation was founded in 1984.
Ecoplastic Corporation is a Korea-based company.
The company manufactures plastic automobile parts for original equipment manufacturers and other suppliers.
The company is a Tier-1 supplier for Hyundai Motor Group and Kia.
The company specializes in the manufacturing of automotive interior and exterior plastic parts, including bumpers, consoles, trims and plastic molds.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that Ecoplastic will be able to serve the Hyundai Metaplant well from Statesboro due to its close proximity to Georgia's ports system.
The company's new facility will be located at 4822 Highway 301 South Register near Statesboro.
The plant will be capable of injection molding and painting.
The plant will produce exterior parts, bumpers and garnishes, interior parts, consoles and trims for electric vehicles.
Benjy Thompson, CEO of the Development Authority of Bulloch County, noted that Ecoplastic is another large Hyundai supplier located in the region to serve the Hyundai meta plant in Bryan County.
The Development Authority of Bulloch County is currently finishing negotiations surrounding the Ecoplastic Corporation project.
This project will be the second Hyundai supplier located in Bulloch County.
Combined with the other supplier, Bulloch County will have a little over 1,000 jobs tied up in Hyundai suppliers.
The region will see hundreds of millions of dollars of capital investment from these suppliers.

Randy Parker, CEO of Hyundai Motor America, stated at a Savannah Economic Development Authority meeting that the Hyundai facility is a $5.5 billion investment in a new EV and battery manufacturer that will create more than 8,100 local jobs.
Ecoplastic Corporation expects to begin operations by October 2024.
The company will be hiring for HR, finance, parts development, quality control, facility maintenance, injection and paint production positions.
source_title: Georgia: Auto parts manufacturer to create nearly 500 jobs - WJCL

facts: Bulloch County Commissioners voted to finalize a 10-year tax abatement for a manufacturer coming to Bulloch County as part of the Hyundai Meta Plant.
The approval comes weeks after commissioners voted to approve the FY 2024 budget and announced that an increase in the county’s millage rate is likely forthcoming.
Hanon Systems Alabama Corp., a South Korea-based operation, announced its intention to come to Bulloch County in May of 2023.
The company proclaimed its new facility will offer 160 jobs to the community.
The facility is located at the Gateway Regional Industrial Park north of I-16.
The project is supposedly a $40 million project.
According to Global Atlanta, the company is a provider of automotive thermal management products and HVAC parts like vents, compressors, cooling fans and condensers.

Documents provided to commissioners said the Development Authority entered into a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with CG Statesboro, LLC and Hanon Systems Alabama Corp. for the location of a project in Bulloch County to supply vehicular HVAC components for Hyundai.
The Development Authority, CG Statesboro, LLC, and Hanon Systems Alabama Corp. are the parties to the MOU.
The Board of Commissioners and Board of Tax Assessors were requested to acknowledge the MOU and agree to applicable provisions.
As an inducement for location of the project in Bulloch County, the MOU includes a 100% abatement of County ad valorem taxes for 10 years.
The tax abatement does not include school taxes or taxes for the County’s fire district.
Wednesday evening, Commissioners voted on a tax abatement of 100% for ten years.
The Bulloch County Development Authority agreed to a discounted land price from $1,700,000 to $850,000 for the bond lease with the Development Authority.
The project will have availability of $17,500,000 for Real Estate Bonds for the Real Estate Assets and $22,500,000 for the Personal Property Bonds for the Personal Property Assets of the project.

The Real Estate Bond lease is the responsibility of CG Statesboro, LLC (out of Alabama) while the Personal Property Bond Lease is the responsibility of Hanon Systems Alabama Corp, though the Development Authority will facilitate both.
The Development Authority promised to apply for a $525,000 grant with the state on behalf of the companies.
There is a commitment to provide $20,000 in training to employees of Hanon Systems via Ogeechee Technical College.
There is a commitment to provide up to $10,000 worth of free office space to Hanon Systems.
Due diligence and geotechnical report costs of $17,625 paid to Terracon Consultants, Inc. will be covered to save Hanon Systems the expense.
The Development Authority will provide concierge services to the companies to assist in setting up utilities, permits, and other project needs.
The contract with the Development Authority lists ‘Year 1’ as no later than 2026.
The only instance in which the company would not be eligible for the tax abatement would be if the investment goal shortfall for jobs was more than 20% of what was promised.
Approval by staff was recommended and commissioners approved the abatement unanimously with no discussion as part of the consent agenda.
source_title: Bulloch Co. Commissioners Approve 10-Year Tax Abatement, Host of Other Incentives for South Korean Manufacturer • The Georgia Virtue

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Ecoplastic Corporation will create 456 new jobs and invest approximately $205 million in a new manufacturing facility in Bulloch County on Thursday, January 5, 2023.
The new facility will be located at 4822 Highway 301 South Register near Statesboro, Georgia.
The plant will be capable of injection molding and painting.
The facility will produce exterior parts, bumpers, garnishes, interior parts, consoles, and trims for electric vehicles.
Ecoplastic expects to begin operations by October 2024.
The company will be hiring for HR, finance, parts development, quality control, facility maintenance, injection, and paint production positions.
Ecoplastic Corporation is a Korea-based company established in June 1984 as Apollo Ind Co., Ltd.
The company established its first Technical Research Center in 1987 and was publicly listed on the Korean Stock Exchange in March 2000.
Ecoplastic is a Tier-1 supplier for Hyundai Motor Group (HMG) and Kia, manufacturing automotive interior and exterior plastic parts including bumpers, consoles, trims, and plastic molds.
The company currently employs more than 770 employees and recorded sales revenue of more than $700 million in 2021.

Han Shang, President and CEO of Ecoplastic, stated the company is honored to establish a production facility for electric vehicle parts in Bulloch County to provide a stable supply of high-quality products to HMGMA.
Development Authority of Bulloch County Chair Billy Allen welcomed Ecoplastic to the community, citing location, workforce, and quality of life as success factors.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that Georgia is building the supplier network for the Hyundai metaplant in Bryan County and spreading suppliers across the region.

Alyce Thornhill, Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development's Global Commerce team on this project in partnership with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia EMC.
Since 2020, more than 30 EV-related projects have announced more than $21 billion in investment and more than 26,300 jobs in Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground on October 25 in neighboring Bryan County for its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Offsite suppliers connected to the Hyundai project were predicted to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around the state, with announced projects already exceeding this amount.

facts: Erdrich Umformtechnik GmbH & Co. KG, a German automotive supplier, will construct a $39 million metal stamping facility in Dublin, Ga.
The new facility will create 178 jobs.
Erdrich is a midsized, family-owned company that produces complex metal parts and subassemblies for the automotive industry.
The company operates two plants in Germany, one in the Czech Republic, and another in China.
Erdrich supplies parts to other automotive supplier companies, as well as to BMW, Mercedes, and Volkswagen.
The new plant will span 130,000 square feet.
This facility will be Erdrich's first U.S. location.
The company expects the Georgia facility to mirror the size and importance of its German operations in supplying its North American customers.
Georgia's international office in Munich developed the relationship with Erdrich to initiate this business opportunity for the state.
The plant opening was announced on January 31, 2012.
source_title: Erdrich Umformtechnik to open plant in Georgia

facts: Michael Weis becomes the new CEO of the Erdrich Group in 2023, forming a new management team with CFO Joachim Schulz.
Franz-Bernd Schenke joins the Management Board in 2020, taking over from Dr. Jürgen Simon, who moves to lead the Technology division.
Georg Erdrich passes company succession to the next generation in 2017, with Nicolas Erdrich, Joachim Schulz, and Dr. Jürgen Simon assuming management.
Erdrich establishes a new plant in Changzhou, China in 2015, where suspension arms and a broad range of formed parts for the auto industry are produced.
Erdrich expands into international markets in 2013 by establishing a new plant in Dublin, Georgia, USA.
In 2011, the SANDER-Firmengruppe merges production facilities Erdrich and SANDER under the brand name ERDRICH Umformtechnik.
The SANDER group of companies establishes a branch in Taicang, China in 2010.
In 2006, the SANDER group builds a plant in Krnov, Czech Republic, developing one of the most modern brake piston plants there.
Due to the success of steel brake pistons, Erdrich establishes a second plant at the Sömmerda site in 2003, doubling capacity.
Sander Automation GmbH becomes an independent part of the SANDER group in 2003.
Erdrich Umformtechnik GmbH & Co. KG becomes independent in 2002 with shareholders Nicolas and Georg Erdrich, remaining part of the SANDER group alongside SANDER Umformtechnik.
Further development of SANDER transfer devices allows their use in warm and hot working of metals for the first time in 1995.
Adolf Hättig retires in 1993, allowing Georg Erdrich to assume sole management.
A subsidiary designated Erdrich Umformtechnik GmbH & Co. is established in Thuringian Sömmerda in 1992.
The first large press is installed at the company in 1987.
SANDER Automation GmbH, which produces transfer units, is established as a subsidiary in Renchen-Ulm in 1979.
The parent company begins developing smaller devices with intermediary speeds for forming sheet metal in 1979.
Georg Erdrich and Adolf Hättig assume management of the company in 1977.
Due to company growth, headquarters are relocated from Oberkirch to Renchen-Ulm in 1975, establishing the company's reputation in the auto industry as an efficient supplier.

Rolf Sander and Adolf Hättig lay the foundation of SANDER Umformtechnik GmbH & Co. KG in Oberkirch in 1962, initially producing cutting and punching tools for chipless sheet forming before expanding to punching and bending parts in series.
source_title: Erdrich Umformtechnik GmbH | Unternehmen | Historie

facts: Fouts Bros Inc. was founded in 1952 and remains family owned and operated.
Fouts Bros Inc. is one of the largest and most diverse commercial truck, van, and automotive equipment manufacturers in the U.S.
The company grew from a single service station in Smyrna, GA into a collection of industrial brands over the last seven decades.
Fouts Defense serves as the newest branch of the family of brands.
Fouts Defense provides American fire tankers, pumpers, wildland trucks, quick attacks, and commercial vans and trucks to U.S. Armed Forces personnel worldwide.
Fouts Defense is led by industry veteran George Bergamini.
Fouts Defense apparatus will be listed on both the DLA and the GSA in the coming weeks.
Fouts Defense operates as a wholly owned subsidiary of Fouts Bros Inc.
Fouts Defense specializes in equipping U.S. military and government agencies with commercial and fire apparatus.
Fouts Bros Inc. is dedicated to the manufacturing, upfitting, and distribution of American work trucks for private and public sectors.

The headquarters facility spans 700,000 square feet.
The company operates a second facility in La Grange, TX.

facts: Fouts Fire is an American fire apparatus manufacturer located in Milledgeville, Georgia.
The company was formerly known as Fouts Bros. Fire Equipment or Fouts Brothers.
It has been family-owned and operated since 1952.

Fouts Fire is a subsidiary of Fouts Bros Inc., a collection of commercial automotive equipment brands including Fouts Commercial Vehicles Inc., Smyrna Truck, Fouts Defense, Big Deal Parts, and Electric Commercial Vehicles Inc.
In 2019, the company moved its headquarters and primary production facility from Smyrna, Georgia, to Milledgeville, Georgia.
The company was founded in 1952 by CJ and Jerry Fouts as a single service station in Smyrna, Georgia.
In 1989, the company opened its fire division with the introduction of the 3000 Gallon Tanker.
The company expanded into a standardized line of fire tankers, pumpers, light rescues, and brush trucks.
In 2024, the fire division was rebranded as Fouts Fire.
The company is currently under third-generation family management.
Fouts Fire is led by Scott Edens.
The company operates out of a 700,000 square foot production facility and headquarters in Milledgeville, Georgia.
The company maintains a second factory in La Grange, Texas.
Fouts Fire builds pumpers, tankers, rescues, and quick attacks on both custom and commercial chassis.
The company produces a limited, standardized line of apparatus to streamline production processes and costs.
The business model relies on keeping stock units of all models readily available for fast delivery nationwide.
Product models include the FB-94 pumper and the Fouts FOUR mini-pumper.

facts: Fouts Bros. Inc. moved its company headquarters to Milledgeville from Smyrna a few years ago.
Scott Edens serves as the president and chief executive officer of the company.
The company was founded in 1952 by C.J. Fouts, who ran day-to-day operations until his passing.
The company manufactures fire and fire rescue trucks.
When Edens first arrived in Milledgeville, the facility was building 150 trucks annually.
Edens set a goal to double annual truck production to 300 as soon as operations got underway.
The company recently announced a local expansion that will bring new jobs and investment to the community.
Sharon Seymour serves as chairwoman of The Development Authority of Milledgeville and Baldwin County (DAMBC).
Jonathan Jackson serves as executive director of DAMBC.
The company is rapidly becoming one of the fastest-growing employers in Milledgeville and Baldwin County.
During a DAMBC board meeting, Jackson shared that the expansion includes $4 million in investment for this year and next.
The company is adding 96 jobs in 2023.
The company forecasts adding 70 more jobs in 2024.
These employment figures will bring the company's total workforce to nearly 500 employees, making it one of Baldwin County's top employers.
The company is currently using only about half of the space at its facility, which was formerly home to Rheem.
Fouts Bros. has plans to build on the back 30 acres of the property due to business expansion.
County commissioners recently toured the facility and observed impressive fire trucks and rescue vehicles being built.
Employees at the facility are making good wages.
The site was previously a Brownfield site requiring well monitoring and sub-surface ventilation due to Rheem's operations.
Through cooperation between Fouts Bros. and Rheem, the company is no longer required to monitor groundwater or perform underground ventilation for the plant.
Fouts Bros. continues to conduct environmental work at the site.
The property was removed from the Georgia Hazardous Sites list, serving as a textbook example of how Georgia's Brownfield program works through the EPD.
Jackson noted that while hiring has been a challenge for the company, the local community is better suited to handle it than their previous location.

facts: Fouts Bros. Inc. remains a leader in building quality fire trucks and is one of the fastest growing industrial employers in Milledgeville and Baldwin County.
The company operates out of the old Rheem plant off Roberson Mill Road in Milledgeville, Georgia.
John Slawson serves as executive vice president of sales and marketing and business development at Fouts Bros. Inc.
Slawson opened the November 13, 2024 event by stating, “Thank you all for coming; this is the Fouts Bros. Inc. Fouts Fire First Home Day.”
Georgia’s 8th District U.S. Rep. Austin Scott attended the company's "Fouts Fire First Home Day" event on November 13, 2024.
Scott Edens serves as president and chief executive officer of Fouts Bros. Inc., while his wife Heather serves as executive vice president.
The organization employs nearly 420 people across its operations.
C.J. Fouts founded the business in 1952; he was a longtime car dealer in Smyrna, Georgia, and operated a Sinclair Gas Station in downtown Smyrna before his death in 2005.
Scott Edens is a third-generation family member who joined the company in his early 20s after graduating from the University of Georgia.

Since relocating to Milledgeville in 2020, Fouts Bros., Inc. has increased its production volume of various fire truck types by more than 100%.
The company's growth and market reach have expanded both domestically and internationally.
Gary Pacilio serves as executive vice president of operations at Fouts Bros., Inc., and concurrently serves as president of the Fire Apparatus Manufacturer’s Association (FAMA).
Pacilio highlighted the critical importance of Assistance to Firefighters Grants (AFG) and SAFER Grants for supporting first responders.
Edens stated that the company currently supports fire services with approximately 4,500 trucks.
Based on industry fire surveys, the fire service sector requires about 6,000 new trucks annually to address population growth and natural disaster response needs.
Earlier in 2024, Edens traveled to Washington, D.C., to meet with Congressional lawmakers, including Rep. Austin Scott, to discuss industry priorities.

Rep. Austin Scott told The Union-Recorder, “They’re doing an amazing job here at their Milledgeville plant and I couldn’t be more proud of a business that’s growing and becoming more and more successful with every passing year.”
Attendees toured the plant and production area, which is scheduled for expansion to increase annual fire apparatus output once construction is completed.

facts: Fox Factory this week announced an overhaul of its operations that will add an expected 800 jobs in northern Georgia.
Fox makes shock absorbers and suspensions for bicycles, motorcycles, off-road vehicles, and snowmobiles.
The company plans to move its headquarters some 2,500 miles from Scotts Valley, Calif., to Georgia by the end of the year.
The company announced its purchase of a 23-acre site in Hall County, Ga., where it will house new manufacturing, warehouse, distribution, and office space.
The first phase of that project is expected to be completed by early 2020.
Over the next five years, Fox expects to invest $50 million in the site.
Fox expects to employ up to 800 workers at the new facility.
Company officials said the new facility would provide additional capacity for growth in its powered vehicles group.
The Scotts Valley location will remain open to house selected corporate functions.
Fox intends to shift its aftermarket bike products operations from Scotts Valley and nearby Watsonville, Calif., to Reno, Nev.
Fox CEO Larry Enterline said the decisions resulted from “extensive research” by the company and would allow it to “better support the needs of our growing business over the next several years.”

facts: Rivian is cutting back on output plans for its future plant in Georgia from 400,000 vehicles per year to 300,000.
The capacity reduction follows a federal loan for the project shrinking by about $2 billion.
Rivian will be able to draw on the funds in 2027, which is earlier than expected.
The company will use the $4.5 billion loan for a single phase with an annual capacity of 300,000 vehicles instead of two phases with a capacity of 200,000 each.
Originally, President Biden's Department of Energy planned to lend Rivian $6.6 billion as part of a program to boost production of its electric trucks and batteries.
The Georgia plant is scheduled to open in late 2028.
The facility will build the R2 midsize crossover and future vehicles on the R2 platform, including the smaller R3 crossover.
CEO RJ Scaringe stated that the strategic decision increased the production capacity of the first phase of the Georgia plant by 50 percent to 300,000 units.
The Georgia factory site near Atlanta has room for future expansion but no immediate plans for an additional phase of construction were announced.
CFO Claire McDonough said Rivian's total production capacity between its two plants would rise to 515,000 vehicles per year.
McDonough declined to say whether Rivian approached the Department of Energy or vice versa regarding changes to the loan terms.
Rivian reported a Q1 2026 net loss that narrowed to $416 million, representing a 23% improvement from the same time last year.
The company's revenue rose 11% to $1.38 billion in Q1 2026.
Rivian reported a 20% gain in Q1 deliveries year-over-year, with 10,365 vehicles delivered between January and March.
The company maintained its 2026 delivery guidance of somewhere between 62,000 and 67,000 vehicles.
source_title: Ford In Talks With Trump Administration About Getting Into The War Business

Fox Factory Holding Corp. experienced strong new design wins in its Power Vehicle segment.
Continued strength in Fox Factory Holding Corp.'s aftermarket business lifted the stock in the quarter.

facts: Gov. Brian Kemp is taking his first overseas trade mission to South Korea, a trip that starts Saturday and runs through Friday.
Kemp told the Atlanta Journal-Constitution in an interview that the trip made a “natural fit” for his first overseas job safari.
South Korea has fast become a major source of investment for Georgia, with $2 billion in projects announced in Georgia over the past year.
Several more major initiatives from South Korean companies are in the pipeline.
Months after taking office in 2003, former Gov. Sonny Perdue trekked to South Korea to talk jobs with overseas executives.
Former Gov. Nathan Deal touched down in Seoul to drum up business for Georgia in his first year as governor.
$1.7 billion in new investments from South Korean companies have poured into Georgia.
Kemp told the AJC: "We want to see how we can bring up their investments."
source_title: Why Georgia looks to South Korea for new jobs

facts: The state of Georgia and local governments are on track to give $2.1 billion in tax breaks and other incentives to Hyundai Motor Group.
Hyundai Motor Group and a partner announced an additional $2 billion investment at an electric vehicle complex in Georgia last month.
Projected incentives will rise by more than $290 million from the $1.8 billion deal originally announced last year.
Only $2.75 million of the incentive increase represents additional cash from the state, with the rest coming from increases in tax breaks.
The deal calls for Hyundai and battery maker LG Energy Solution to invest $7.6 billion in the Georgia plant.
The project will hire 8,500 workers by the end of 2031, up from the original projection of 8,100 jobs.
This is the largest economic development deal and incentive package in Georgia history.
Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated Hyundai is projected to have a direct payroll of $4.7 billion over the next 10 years.
Hyundai has promised to pay workers a yearly average of $58,105 plus benefits.
State leaders and Hyundai plan to invest in local schools, families, and communities as part of the project.
An amended incentive agreement was released by the state on Friday.
Local officials released property tax projections on Tuesday.
The Hyundai package has been described as the largest subsidy package a U.S. state has ever promised a single automotive plant, according to Good Jobs First.
Local governments have agreed to abate property taxes on the assembly plant through 2048.
Local governments have agreed to abate property taxes on the battery plant through 2049.
During the tax abatement period, Hyundai is projected to pay $523 million in taxes while saving $669 million.
The state projects it will waive an additional $81.8 million in sales taxes on construction materials and machinery.
These exemptions bring Hyundai’s total savings from tax exemptions to more than $478 million.
The company is projected to receive an additional $10.5 million in state income tax credits, calculated at $5,250 per job over five years due to the job increase.
This brings Hyundai’s total state income tax savings to $223 million.
If Hyundai did not owe enough state corporate income tax, Georgia would instead provide personal income taxes collected from Hyundai workers.
The state will spend an additional $2.75 million to help fund construction, machinery, and equipment, bringing that total to nearly $53 million.
State and local governments will spend more than $112 million to buy and prepare 2,913 acres for the plant.
The state will spend $175 million on water and sewer facilities.
The state will spend $210 million on road construction and improvements.
More than $153 million will be spent to recruit and train workers.
The deal requires Hyundai to pay back a portion of the incentives if the company falls below 80% of its promised investment or employment targets.
Hyundai plans to start EV production in 2025.
Initial production capacity will be 300,000 vehicles per year.
Production may potentially expand to 500,000 vehicles per year.
Since the announcement of its first U.S. plant solely dedicated to electric vehicles, suppliers have pledged to invest $2.2 billion and hire 5,300 people.
The project is part of an electric vehicle and battery land rush across the United States.
Under the U.S. Inflation Reduction Act, EVs must be assembled in North America to qualify for incentives.
A certain percentage of battery parts and minerals must come from North America or a U.S. free trade partner to qualify for a full $7,500 EV tax credit.
The electric vehicle and battery manufacturing complex is located in Ellabell, Georgia, west of Savannah, in Bryan County.
source_title: New incentives means Hyundai could get $2.1B in aid to make electric cars in Georgia | FOX 5 Atlanta

facts: The Queretaro facility supplies several Japanese engine customers and major automakers with front-wheel drive transmission parts.
Freudenberg-NOK provides oil seals through this business line, which began production in 2014.
Sales for the new business line are expected to grow rapidly alongside overall Mexican auto production.
By the end of 2015, industry sources estimate that Mexico will surpass Japan and Canada to become the top source of imported cars by the U.S.

Gary VanWambeke, general manager of the Global Transmission and Driveline Lead Center at Freudenberg-NOK, stated that increased customer requests for production in Mexico prompted the company to expand its manufacturing there.
Six months after installing machinery for oil seal production, the facility operated 58 pieces of equipment and delivered 2.4 million parts.
During its first year of operation, the Queretaro plant achieved a perfect on-time-delivery score while remaining free from accidents and customer complaints.
Plant manager Luis Pardo credited the facility's success to associates who adapted well to change, highlighting the team's passion, stable performance, quick turnaround times, and incredible morale.
The plant introduced several new operational concepts and a "one company, one team" motto to maintain employee spirit.

Freudenberg-NOK relocated a portion of its automotive sealing production to Mexico based on a global business philosophy to establish a local manufacturing presence and integrate into the host country's business community.
Torsten Maschke, president of automotive sales and marketing at Freudenberg Sealing Technologies, stated that the company does not base regional operations on low-cost labor opportunities.
Instead, the company pursues investments where it can become a local supplier focused on local customer needs and integrate into a country's manufacturing fabric.
Freudenberg-NOK's Queretaro facility has become one of the company's fastest-growing locations due to successful integration into the Mexican industry.
Production numbers at the facility are expected to continue growing as Mexico increases its exporting trend and automakers move production to the country.
source_title: Freudenberg-NOK's Mexico Plant Shifts Into High Gear With New Oil Seals Portfolio - AftermarketNews

facts: Freudenberg-NOK Sealing Technologies will increase its testing capabilities with the addition of a new materials laboratory at its Findlay, Ohio manufacturing plant.
The new laboratory is part of an overall plant expansion taking place in 2015.
The laboratory will provide the company's Fluid Power Division with new material characterization, failure analysis and mechanical property test capabilities.
The laboratory will consolidate and expand an existing engineering test laboratory at the facility.
Findlay's expansion was announced last month and will add 43,000 square feet to the existing factory.
The new lab will account for approximately 2,000 square feet of the total expansion area.
The new laboratory represents an investment of approximately $450,000 in equipment and building expenses.
The entire expansion project represents an investment of approximately $8.6 million in construction and equipment upgrades.
The expansion will create 25 new jobs at the Findlay plant.
The project is expected to be complete by winter, 2015.
The company celebrated the expansion with a groundbreaking ceremony at the plant attended by executives, elected officials, employees and media.

The Findlay facility produces approximately 700 high performance plastic parts for customers including vehicle manufacturers and tier one and two suppliers using state-of-the art manufacturing processes.
The new laboratory will perform analysis and testing of parts made from high performance plastics, including thrust washers, seals and other components used in the automotive industry.
The company is installing a new engineering test rig in the lab that will measure torque loss.
Data from the new test rig will help improve custom engineered sealing solutions that reduce CO2 emissions and improve fuel economy.
The 25 new jobs include chemist, chemical engineer and quality technician positions.
The company worked with the state of Ohio, including the Findlay-Hancock County Economic Development authority, Jobs Ohio and the Regional Growth Partnership on workforce incentives.
Freudenberg-NOK and its Findlay operations are considered a top employer in Hancock County.
The plant was a finalist in the IndustryWeek 2014 Best Plants competition, which annually recognizes the best manufacturing plants in North America.
Findlay maintains nearly perfect statistics in quality, delivery and customer retention.
Findlay has become the company's lead facility in adopting Single Cavity Net Shape Molding, a breakthrough manufacturing approach.

The Single Cavity Net Shape Molding process uses compact, custom-engineered one cavity injection and compression molding machines rather than multi-cavity machines to produce crucial thermoplastic parts.
This new molding process improves part quality and reduces scrap and waste.
The expansion will help the facility increase its compression and injection molding product production, which includes seal rings, thrust washers, pistons and assemblies.
The plant currently manufactures 185 million seals and related components annually.
The company anticipates the new addition will increase production by approximately 25 percent a year.

"Research and development are core values at Freudenberg-NOK and this laboratory investment supports the company's mission, customers and markets," said Dr. Theodore G. Duclos, president, Freudenberg-NOK.

"We continue to invest in our scientific material competencies in order to solve ever more complex global system challenges and to remain an essential leader in material developments and the sealing industry," said Dr. Theodore G. Duclos.

"The addition of the new laboratory and testing capabilities at the Findlay facility will help us meet customer demands for components that function flawlessly in even the most rigorous and even unanticipated environmental conditions," said Roy Schroeder, Lead Center Manager, Fluid Power Automotive, Findlay.
"We are delighted that Freudenberg-NOK is investing in our facility, our people and our community," said Roy Schroeder.
Freudenberg-NOK Sealing Technologies is the Americas joint venture between Freudenberg and Co. in Germany and NOK Corp. in Japan.
Freudenberg-NOK is headquartered in Plymouth, Michigan and operates more than 20 facilities.
source_title: Freudenberg-NOK Expands Material Science Capabilities With Addition Of New Laboratory In Findlay

facts: Freudenberg achieved record sales of €11,902.8 million in fiscal year 2023, up 1.3 percent over the previous year.
Operating profit reached €1,081.6 million in fiscal year 2023, representing a growth rate of about 15 percent and surpassing the €1 billion mark for the first time.
The equity capital ratio climbed to 56 percent in fiscal year 2023.
Investments in research and development grew to €603.6 million in fiscal year 2023, corresponding to 5.1 percent of sales.
Adjusted for acquisitions, investments in intangible and tangible assets as well as real estate held as financial investments totaled €452.7 million in fiscal year 2023.
The Freudenberg e-Power Systems Business Group expanded its manufacturing and assembly capacity for the production of battery systems in the US.
Freudenberg Sealing Technologies moved into a new plant in Parets del Vallès for the production of elastomer products for the auto industry.
In Morinda, India, Freudenberg invested in a new production facility for its Vibracoustic Business Group and its Freudenberg-NOK India joint venture.
Investments in Germany reached €174.1 million in 2023.
Freudenberg Performance Materials launched a new factory to produce gas diffusion layers (key components in fuel cells) in Weinheim.

In Wolfratshausen, EagleBurgmann began building a new research and development center and continued projects to modernize, automate and digitalize the production of mechanical face seals and other products in Eurasburg.
Freudenberg Medical invested in the construction of a new production facility for Hemoteq in Alsdorf.
As of December 31, 2023, the Group employed 52,241 employees in 60 countries.
The Freudenberg Group aims to become CO2-neutral by 2045 and increased its share of electricity from renewable sources from 30 to 36 percent of its total electrical consumption in 2023.
Freudenberg installed its first-ever microgrid in Berlin, which functions as a battery system that operates as a virtual power plant to cover peak demand and stabilize the public grid.
The company's strategic outlook for future investments focuses on the transition in mobility, digitalization, and sustainable solutions.

The announcement of the selection was made on August 25, 2020.
GEDIA Georgia LLC will produce lightweight body-in-white automotive parts and assemblies at the Dalton plant.
GEDIA Georgia LLC will also produce chassis components at the Dalton plant.
The new Dalton facility will be GEDIA’s second U.S. plant.
GEDIA’s first U.S. plant is located in Michigan.
Brandon Darroch, PMP, serves as the Southeast Division Manager for SSOE.
SSOE supports international clients looking to expand or set up manufacturing in the U.S., particularly foreign direct investment (FDI) clients.
SSOE has a long history of supporting European and Asian-based clients.
SSOE holds memberships with TBIC and other organizations that encourage FDI.

facts: Qcells is a clean-energy producer and manufacturer of solar cells that added a factory in Dalton in 2019.
Qcells completed its Dalton factory expansion last year and created 510 additional clean energy jobs in the area.
Qcells began constructing another facility in Cartersville last year, which is expected to be fully operational in 2025.
Together, Qcells’ manufacturing operations in Georgia are projected to generate roughly 4,000 jobs in Bartow and Whitfield Counties.
GEDIA Automotive Group, a German company, invested $85 million in a new manufacturing plant in the Carbondale Business Park.
The GEDIA Automotive Group plant opened in 2022, adding 200 jobs.
Carl Campbell is executive director of the Dalton-Whitfield Joint Development Authority and says his biggest challenge is figuring out how to make room for housing the growing workforce.
Campbell notes that Dalton’s location near the interstate makes it attractive to businesses, and its technical skill development and career progression opportunities help retain talent.
Campbell states that Dalton has multiple facilities with over 1 million square feet and is rich in resources for business growth.
The Northwest Georgia College and Career Academy offers high school students a curriculum integrating core academics with career-technical education to build a local talent pipeline.

Stephani Womack, chief talent officer for the Greater Dalton Chamber of Commerce, conducts bus tours with Campbell to expose nonresidents to career opportunities in advanced manufacturing and other sectors beyond flooring.

Gov. Brian Kemp awarded a $1.5 million grant to Dalton from the Rural Workforce Housing Initiative earlier this year to help add 39 homes to Dalton’s market by funding street and stormwater drainage for the 3-acre South Hamilton Residential Infill Project.
The city of Dalton pledged $622,300 toward infrastructure design and construction for the housing project, which was unanimously approved by the Dalton City Council in September.

Homes on donated city land will range from 850 to 1,400 square feet with one to three bedrooms, priced between $125,000-$290,000 per state mandate to address lower-end and upper-middle income market needs.

Believe Greater Dalton, a strategic plan led by the Greater Dalton Chamber of Commerce, focuses on expanding housing choice, neighborhood revitalization, increasing affordability, and continuing downtown development to attract workers.
Co-chair Anna Adamson says the board uses resources to identify specific housing needs for the local workforce, including multigenerational families and single dwellers.

A $100,000 grant from the Truist Charitable Fund helped Flooring Capital Development Corporation pilot sustainable homes furnished with structurally insulated panels to reduce construction timelines and utility costs.

Dalton State College President John Fuchko III says the college prioritizes preparing students for the workforce through bachelor’s degrees in fields like engineering and accounting, and expects to integrate AI into all programs over time to produce AI-literate graduates for emerging industries leveraging AI.

Roughly half of incoming Dalton State students are first-generation college students, prompting the college to offer peer mentor programs and the six-week Roadrunner Scholars Summer Bridge Program providing intense English and math training at no dorm cost.

The college gives well over a quarter million dollars a year in private scholarships to students, with more than half receiving need-based aid and around nine in 10 receiving any sort of financial aid.

Stephani Womack says Project Purpose was piloted last year as a two-week program connecting high school students with employers, exposing them to industries like advanced manufacturing while training them in communication, conflict management, and financial literacy.

All 18 students who participated in Project Purpose this year were hired after taking industrial maintenance classes at Georgia Northwestern Technical College, receiving professional interview attire, and touring local employers.

Whitfield County Administrator Robert Sivick says his participation in recent training qualifies the county to apply for $50,000 in federal funds to deliver reliable broadband internet coverage to rural areas, benefiting businesses that use cashless payment systems and school-aged children.
source_title: Dalton | Whitfield County: Talent Hub - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: General Motors Co. plans to invest $200 million to expand operations at its Global Powertrain Engineering headquarters in Pontiac, Mich.
The investment will consolidate functions from four other operations into the Pontiac location.
The capital investments are part of GM's previously announced plan to invest $1.5 billion in North American operations.
At the Pontiac site, GM will build a 138,000-sq.-ft. testing center available in the second half of 2014.

Sam Winegarden, GM vice president of Global Engine Engineering, stated that the moves will help the Powertrain team work more effectively to develop powertrain technologies for the world's best vehicles.
In 2008, GM consolidated seven Powertrain Engineering operations at Pontiac from various Michigan locations.
The upcoming consolidation will reduce GM's total footprint by 640,000 sq. ft. and eliminate three leased facilities.
The leased operations being eliminated are located in Wixom, Mich., Castleton, Ind., and Torrance, Calif.
A fourth powertrain operation, GM R&D’s Propulsion Systems Research lab in Warren, Mich., will relocate to the Pontiac campus.
GM previously announced that its hydrogen fuel cell operation would move from Honeoye Falls, N.Y., to Pontiac, with the shift scheduled for this year.
With the new testing center, GM’s Powertrain Development Center in Pontiac will total 450,000 sq.-ft.
GM predicts the consolidated facility will bring engine technologies to market faster and less expensively by reducing development time.

Engineers at the new facility will test engines for durability, reliability, and quality under extreme conditions, including cold ambient temperatures, high RPMs, and repetitive starting and stopping.
Expanding and consolidating powertrain operations at Pontiac will reduce development time for GM's "next-generation advanced propulsion technologies."
GM’s Performance Build Center engine plant in Wixom will relocate early in 2014 to Bowling Green, Ky., where GM assembles Chevrolet Corvettes.
The Wixom Advanced Engineering Lab, including electric motor engineering development and performance engineering, will relocate to Pontiac by mid-2015.
The Torrance Advanced Technology Center’s work on electric motor and power electronics engineering development will shift to Pontiac by the end of 2014.
Heavy-duty transmission, power electronics, hybrid and battery electric drive unit development work done at Castleton will relocate to Pontiac by mid-2014.
GM R&D’s Propulsion Systems Research Lab will relocate to Pontiac during the second half of 2015.
source_title: GM Banking $200M on Powertrain Engineering | American Machinist

facts: Dana Incorporated announced a definitive agreement with Eaton Corporation plc to combine with Eaton’s Mobility business in a transaction valued at approximately $5.1 billion.
The transaction is structured as a Reverse Morris Trust where Eaton shareholders will own at least 50.1% and Dana shareholders will own approximately 49.9% of the combined company at close.
Eaton will receive a cash distribution of approximately $1.1 billion (subject to adjustments for cash and indebtedness), funded through new debt raised prior to closing.
The combined company is expected to have approximately $11 billion in sales on a pro forma 2026 estimated basis.
The combined company is expected to achieve approximately $1.7 billion adjusted EBITDA (approximately 15% adjusted EBITDA margin) on a fully synergized, pro forma 2026 estimated basis.

Eaton Mobility is valued at approximately $5.1 billion on a cash-free, debt-free basis, representing approximately 8.3x estimated 2026 pro forma adjusted EBITDA before synergies, or approximately 5.9x including run-rate synergies.
The combined company is expected to achieve $250 million of run-rate synergies within 24 months following closing.
Synergies are driven by reduced structural costs, purchasing scale, manufacturing optimization, and engineering efficiencies.
Dana’s 2030 strategy targets have been increased to $14–$15 billion in sales, approximately 18% adjusted EBITDA margin, and an 8%–9% adjusted free cash flow margin.
Prior Dana 2030 targets included approximately $10 billion in sales, 14% to 15% adjusted EBITDA margins, and a 6% adjusted free cash flow margin.
R. Bruce McDonald will serve as Executive Chairman of the combined company with responsibility for integration and synergy realization.
Byron Foster will serve as Chief Executive Officer of the combined company.
Timothy Kraus will continue as Chief Financial Officer.
Erin Rowse, Senior Vice President Human Resources at Eaton, will serve as Chief Human Resources Officer at closing.
The combined company’s Board of Directors will include all members of Dana’s Board and three Eaton designees.
New leadership roles are scheduled to begin on July 1, 2026.

The combination integrates Dana’s global powertrain, thermal, and sealing technologies with Eaton Mobility’s commercial vehicle transmissions, engine and emissions products, and advanced electrification capabilities.
The combined entity will serve as a comprehensive supplier for commercial and light vehicle markets, as well as associated aftermarket channels.
The transaction is expected to close in the first quarter of 2027, subject to approval by Dana shareholders, receipt of regulatory approvals, and other customary closing conditions.
The transaction is expected to be tax-free to Dana and Eaton shareholders for U.S. federal income tax purposes.
The combined company is expected to maintain a strong balance sheet with net leverage of approximately 1.2x on a fully synergized pro forma 2026 estimated basis.
Dana is expected to maintain its current credit rating following the transaction.
The combined company will continue to operate under the Dana Incorporated name and retain its listing on the New York Stock Exchange.
Goldman Sachs & Co. LLC is serving as exclusive financial advisor for the transaction.
Kirkland & Ellis LLP is serving as legal counsel to Dana.
Ernst and Young LLP is serving as transaction advisor.
Goldman Sachs Bank USA is serving as sole underwriter of the committed financing for the transaction.
Dana reported sales of $7.5 billion in 2025.
Dana employs 27,000 people across 24 countries on six continents.
Dana is headquartered in Maumee, Ohio, USA.
Dana supplies leading vehicle manufacturers and related aftermarkets with drive systems, electrodynamic technologies, and thermal and sealing solutions.

entity_name: Georgia’s State of Economic Development Presentation 2026
canonical_name: Georgia’s State of Economic Development Presentation 2026
facts: Leaders from across Georgia’s business, government, and economic development sectors gathered on March 18, 2026, at the historic Georgia Freight Depot in downtown Atlanta for the annual State of Economic Development presentation.
The event was co-hosted by the Georgia Chamber of Commerce and the Georgia Economic Developers Association (GEDA).

Chris Clark, President and CEO of the Georgia Chamber, and Grant Cagle, Executive Director of GEDA, emphasized unified economic strategies connecting state leadership, local development authorities, and private-sector partners.

Governor Brian P. Kemp delivered a video message highlighting sustained investment in infrastructure, workforce development, and rural economic opportunities as critical to maintaining Georgia’s edge in site selection and job creation.

Commissioner Pat Wilson of the Georgia Department of Economic Development outlined strong growth in major sectors including advanced manufacturing, logistics, technology, and international investment.
Wilson emphasized sustained public-private collaboration, strategic workforce initiatives, and aggressive recruitment efforts to help the state compete on national and global stages.

A leadership panel titled “Georgia’s Competitive Edge: Leadership Perspectives on Economic Development” featured Commissioner Wilson, Lauren Curry (Chief of Staff for the Office of the Governor), and was moderated by Stuart Countess (President of Kia Georgia and Chair of the Georgia Chamber).
The panel addressed emerging workforce needs, innovation ecosystems, and the evolving demands of corporate expansion projects.
Panelists highlighted Georgia’s strong business infrastructure but noted that continuous adaptation is essential to uphold the state’s high national rankings.
Chris Clark emphasized leveraging collaboration among state agencies, local leaders, and industry partners to address shared challenges such as workforce readiness and regional investment gaps.

Participants engaged in table-based discussions centered on identifying new investment opportunities, strengthening local business ecosystems, and understanding the role of regional assets in attracting industry.

The final panel, “Turning Opportunity Into Investment: Local Perspectives on Georgia’s Growth,” featured Jason Dunn (Executive Director of the Fitzgerald-Ben Hill Economic Development Authority) and Niki Vanderslice (President and CEO of the Fayette County Development Authority).
The final panel was moderated by Stacy Watson (Chairman of GEDA and Director of Economic & Industrial Development for the Georgia Ports Authority).
Panelists discussed strategic planning, infrastructure readiness, community-led workforce efforts, and the importance of leveraging Georgia’s port and logistics assets.
The event adjourned at 1:30 PM with record attendance and a sold-out audience, underscoring statewide commitment to shaping Georgia’s future growth.
Both the Georgia Chamber and GEDA reaffirmed their focus on collaboration, strategic planning, and innovation to position Georgia as a premier destination for business expansion and investment.
source_title: Georgia’s State of Economic Development Presentation 2026 | Waycross - Ware County GA

facts: Hyundai Glovis Georgia, an auto logistics services provider, is moving to a new 310,000-square-foot facility in West Point, Ga.
The company is set to hire 190 employees over four years to support expanded operations.
The sum of the new investment was not disclosed.
The Korean company already has a location in West Point serving the Kia Motors plant there.
Glovis leaders will work with a subcontractor to build the new facility in the Northwest Harris Business Park.
At the new facility, they will design a sequencing and sub-assembly process to distribute components to Kia and Hyundai suppliers spread throughout western Georgia and eastern Alabama.
The company received a $200,000 REBA grant from the state to open the new facility.
Announced investment in Georgia’s automotive sector grew by 149 percent during fiscal year 2014, according to the Georgia Department of Economic Development.
Half of the Korean investment in Georgia during the period was in the auto sector.
