facts: Haering Precision USA LP is located at 50 Anton Haering Street, 30553 Lavonia (GA), USA.
The company manufactures precision parts and assemblies for the automotive industry.
Maximum precision, minimal tolerance and zero errors are standard for precision parts and assemblies for the automotive industry.
Anton Häring KG utilizes comprehensive know-how and mirrored production across four production plants to meet modern automotive industry requirements.
The company operates a comprehensive environmental management system at all its sites and continuously improves its energy efficiency.
source_title: International locations | Anton Häring KG

facts: Haering Precision USA LP hosted its inaugural Skills Invitational in collaboration with North Georgia Technical College.

The Skills Invitational aimed to ignite interest in machining and metals jobs among high school students while providing them a platform to explore diverse career opportunities within the manufacturing industry.
Participants from local high schools teamed up with senior precision machining students from North Georgia Tech in a proam style contest.
The event featured challenges designed to test technical skills, creativity, and teamwork.
Throughout the day-long event, students faced hands-on challenges simulating real-world scenarios encountered in precision machining and manufacturing.
Challenges included interpreting technical drawings and programming CNC machines.
Judges evaluated teams based on criteria such as accuracy, efficiency, innovation, and collaborative problem-solving.
Winners received various prizes, including tools and paid internship opportunities at Haering's US facility.
Franklin County High School senior Logan Turner took first place in the contest.

The event recognized participating high schools, students, faculty, and industry partners from Franklin, Hart, Stephens, and Union counties for their active involvement and commitment to advancing manufacturing education and workforce development initiatives.
Haering plans to expand opportunities for students to explore and pursue careers in precision machining and manufacturing following the success of the inaugural Skills Invitational.
The company looks forward to hosting next year's competition.
source_title: News | Anton Häring KG

facts: Is located in Burton, Ohio.
Continues its renaissance in both organization and leadership composition, with changes that began in 2023.
Names Dubois and Mowery to executive spots.
source_title: Hexpol Compounding Americas names Dubois, Mowery to executive spots - Rubber News

facts: HEXPOL AB has appointed Kenneth Bloom as the interim President of HEXPOL Compounding Americas, effective September 19, 2025.
The appointment follows the departure of Gary Moore.
Bloom previously held the same position from 2020 to 2022 and brings over 20 years of experience in the polymer industry.
Bloom is expected to lead the company through its next phase of growth and value creation until a permanent successor is found.
HEXPOL is a leading global polymers group specializing in advanced polymer compounds, gaskets for plate heat exchangers, and polymer wheels for truck and castor wheel applications.
The company serves system suppliers in industries such as automotive, engineering, building and construction, transportation, energy, consumer goods, and medical equipment.
HEXPOL is organized into two main business areas: HEXPOL Compounding and HEXPOL Engineered Products.
The company reached sales of 20,437 MSEK in 2024.
HEXPOL maintains a workforce of approximately 5,000 employees across fourteen countries.
source_title: Kenneth Bloom Appointed Interim President of HEXPOL Compounding Americas - TipRanks.com

facts: HEXPOL signed an agreement to acquire 80 percent of the shares in Piedmont Resin Supply, LLC from founders Mr. Matt Griffith and Mr. Paul Daniel.
Piedmont was founded in 2013.
Piedmont has grown to become one of the largest independent nylon compounders in the US.
The company is an industry leader in technical nylon compounds on the US market.
Piedmont services customers within the automotive, transportation, and furniture industries.
Piedmont operates a manufacturing facility in Cartersville, GA.
The Georgia facility has approximately 60 employees.
The Georgia facility has further capacity to grow.
The company has a turnover of some 60 MUSD.
The company's profitability is on a similar level to the HEXPOL Group.
Founders Matt Griffith and Paul Daniel stated that they view the merger with HEXPOL as a perfect fit between two successful companies with similar cultures and future ambitions.

Piedmont brings a portfolio of superior nylon compounds and strong market presence, which together with HEXPOL’s complementary product portfolio and financial strength will enable further growth prospects.

Jan Wikström, President of HEXPOL Thermoplastic Compounding, stated that the acquisition adds new capabilities, application know-how, and a new customer base to HEXPOL Thermoplastic Compounding in the US.
Klas Dahlberg, President and CEO of HEXPOL Group, noted that Piedmont has a diverse product portfolio and is a leading independent nylon compounder.
The acquisition broadens the capabilities of the HEXPOL Group on the US market.
The acquisition price amounts to 86 MUSD on a cash and debt-free basis.
The acquisition is funded by a combination of bank facilities and cash.
The closing took place on October 31, 2024.
Pursuant to the agreement, HEXPOL has an option to acquire the remaining shares in Piedmont.
The founders have an option to sell their remaining shares to HEXPOL.
source_title: Hexpol acquires 80 percent of the shares in Piedmont Resin Supply LLC - Rubber World - The Technical Service Magazine for the Rubber Industry

facts: Hexpol Compounding Americas will close its Kennedale, Texas, facility.
Hexpol Compounding Americas will divest its Muscle Shoals facility.
Hexpol Compounding Americas is streamlining its North American operations.
Hexpol Compounding Americas wants to be in the top echelon of rubber suppliers in all the markets it serves.
source_title: Hexpol Compounding streamlines North American operations - Rubber News

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Hyundai Industrial Co., Ltd. will create 100 new jobs and invest more than $24 million in a new manufacturing facility near Newnan in Coweta County.
The announcement was made on November 1.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that partners like Kia and Hyundai are attracting a large network of suppliers, creating jobs and opportunity for hardworking Georgians across the entire state.
Suppliers have now committed to bringing over 5,300 jobs to Georgia since last year's Hyundai Metaplant announcement.
These supplier jobs will mostly go to communities outside of large urban areas.
Hyundai Industrial was established in 1978 and is headquartered in Ulsan, Korea.
The company specializes in manufacturing car seats, arm rests, and head rests.

Mr. Woosuck Kim, Team Leader at HDI America Inc., stated that Hyundai Industrial has more than four decades of experience providing comfortable and safe experiences for drivers and passengers with some of the world’s biggest automotive brands.

Hyundai Industrial expressed excitement to be part of the e-mobility transition happening in Georgia and look forward to working with Coweta County to create mutually beneficial growth for the community and future employees.
Hyundai Industrial has purchased an existing building at 83 Amlajack Way in Coweta County.
The building will be retrofitted to produce arm and head rests for OEMs, including Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Hyundai Industrial will focus on filling manufacturing production roles.
Jobs will be posted on Indeed or workingUS.com when open.

Coweta County Board of Commissioners Chairman John Reidelbach stated that Hyundai joins the county's thriving manufacturing industries and will have close proximity to I-85, making transport to nearby automobile plants effortless.

Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project in partnership with the Coweta County Development Authority and Georgia EMC.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that with Kia in West Georgia and Hyundai on the eastern coast, suppliers like Hyundai Industrial can locate almost anywhere in the state and still be center of at least two major manufacturers.
Companies are able to select a community that’s the right fit for them, and vice versa.

This relationship between Coweta County and Hyundai Industrial is another classic example of Georgia’s partnership approach to economic development, matching a community with an existing asset and plan to a company whose goals aligned.

For over a century, Georgia has fostered healthy industry practices, encouraged collaboration and innovation, and positioned the state as a leader in developing and harnessing emerging technologies for the evolving automotive and mobility industry.
As the electric vehicle market continues to grow, Georgia has pursued the entire supply chain, creating more than $25.1 billion in investments and 29,000 jobs since 2020.
In fiscal year 2023, job creation in the automotive industry increased by 324 percent when compared to fiscal year 2021.
Suppliers attracted by Georgia’s mobility original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) resulted in over $2 billion in investment across the state in FY23.
Hyundai Industry Co., Ltd. aims to manufacture automotive car seats that not only provide comfort but also play a critical role in safety.

Hyundai Industrial creates safer and more comfortable experiences through developing cutting-edge products based on talent and advanced technology while also striving for mutually beneficial growth for its employees and the community.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: Hyundai, Kia Supplier to Invest in Manufacturing Facility in Coweta

facts: Hyundai officially launched its first flagship EV, the IONIQ 9, in South Korea on Thursday.
The IONIQ 9 is the largest Hyundai electric SUV with “best-in-class” interior space and features more driving range than any other EV in its lineup.
The electric SUV is available in six or seven-seat configurations, each with three trim options.
The seven-seater “Exclusive” model starts at 67.15 million won, or around $46,000.
Prices range upward to 79.41 million won ($54,500) for the premium “Calligraphy” six-seater.
The IONIQ 9 can be purchased in AWD or RWD powertrain setups.
With incentives, Hyundai expects the actual purchase price of the base IONIQ 9 (Exclusive trim) to be in the low to mid 60 million won range (around $41,000 to $45,000).
Buyers can choose from four different seating setups.
The six-seater is available with premium relaxation seats, swivel seats, or a dynamic body care seat, while the seven-seater features a 6:4 folding seat.
The electric SUV measures 5,060 mm (199.2″) long, 1,980 mm (78″) wide, and 1,790 mm (70.5″) tall with a wheelbase of 3,130 mm (123″).
The IONIQ 9 is slightly larger than its sibling, the Kia EV9, which measures 5,010 mm long, 1,980 mm wide, and 1,755 mm tall with a wheelbase of 3,100 mm.
Inside, the flagship model features a panoramic curved infotainment setup with dual 12.3″ driver display and navigation screens.
The IONIQ 9 is powered by a 110.3 kWh battery and Hyundai’s E-GMP platform.
The IONIQ 9 is rated for up to 532 km (330 miles) of driving range in Korea.
The IONIQ 9 features a 400/800V dual charging system that can charge from 10% to 80% in just 24 minutes using a 350 kW charger.

During the launch event, a Hyundai Motor official stated, “We plan to provide a new level of electrification experience through the IONIQ 9, Hyundai Motor Company’s first electrified flagship SUV model.”
Following its domestic launch, Hyundai announced it would launch the IONIQ 9 in the US.
Hyundai will build the IONIQ 9 at its new EV plant in Georgia alongside the updated 2025 IONIQ 5.
Like the new IONIQ 5, the 2026 Hyundai IONIQ 9 will have a native NACS port for charging at Tesla Superchargers.
Hyundai’s website states the IONIQ 9 is expected to arrive in the US market in Spring 2025.
The flagship electric SUV is expected to start at around $60,000 in the US, slightly higher than the Kia EV9 which starts at about $55,000.
source_title: Hyundai IONIQ 9 officially launches in Korea, next stop is the US

facts: In 2022, Hyundai Motor Group announced a $7.6 billion electric vehicle and battery manufacturing complex in Bryan County, Georgia.
The complex is located just 25 miles from the Port of Savannah.
The facility is known as the Metaplant.
It is a dedicated electric vehicle manufacturing facility spanning 2,923 acres in Bryan County.
The plant will produce Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis electric vehicles along with their battery packs.
At full capacity, it is expected to produce approximately 300,000 vehicles per year.
It is one of the largest automotive manufacturing plants in the United States.

Automotive manufacturing for the facility requires a vast network of Tier 1 and Tier 2 suppliers providing components including battery cells, electric motors, seats, dashboards, wiring harnesses, and body panels.
Many of these components will be imported through the Port of Savannah.
The supply chain creates massive demand for automotive parts warehousing requiring JIT (Just-In-Time) delivery with precise inventory management.
The supply chain requires parts sequencing and kitting where components arrive at the assembly line in exact order and configuration.
The supply chain requires container drayage with fast, reliable transport from Garden City Terminal.
The supply chain requires transloading to convert ocean containers to domestic trailers optimized for plant delivery schedules.
The supply chain requires customs brokerage to manage duty classifications, FTZ benefits, and import compliance for automotive parts.
The Metaplant is creating a new category of logistics demand in the Savannah region.
Automotive supply chains for the facility require precision timing, quality assurance, and the ability to scale with production schedules.

Third-party logistics providers in Savannah offering kitting and assembly, JIT warehousing, and FTZ services are positioned to capture significant business from Hyundai directly and its supplier network.
Georgia is home to Kia's manufacturing plant in West Point.
Georgia has a growing network of EV battery and component manufacturers.
The Southeast United States has emerged as the new center of gravity for American automotive manufacturing.
Other regional plants include BMW in South Carolina, Mercedes-Benz in Alabama, Volkswagen in Tennessee, and Rivian in Georgia.
Savannah's port and logistics infrastructure serves as the natural import gateway for this regional automotive corridor.
source_title: How Hyundai's Metaplant Is Reshaping Georgia's Supply Chain | Savannah 3PL

facts: The 2026 Hyundai IONIQ 9 will be assembled at Hyundai's all-new Metaplant in Bryan County, Georgia.
Assembly in Georgia allows the vehicle to qualify for a full $7,500 federal tax credit.
It remains unclear whether the Trump administration will continue the federal tax credit program.
The IONIQ 9 is built on Hyundai Group's E-GMP EV platform and shares underpinnings with the Kia EV9.
All versions are powered by a single 110.3-kWh liquid-cooled, lithium-ion battery pack.
Every trim level will offer over 300 miles of range on a full charge, with the RWD model equipped with 19-inch wheels reaching up to 335 miles per EPA projections.
The vehicle can fast charge from 10% to 80% in 24 minutes when connected to a 350-kW charger.
It will come standard from the factory with a Tesla North American Charging Standard (NACS) charge port, enabling compatibility with over 17,000 Supercharger stations across the U.S. and Canada.
Hyundai will provide Combined Charging System (CCS) adapters to allow owners to use legacy fast chargers.
Buyers can choose between rear-wheel drive or dual-motor all-wheel drive configurations, along with 19, 20, or 21-inch wheel options depending on the trim.
The Long Range RWD variant produces 160 kW (215 HP) and 258 lb-ft of torque.
The Long Range AWD variant produces 230 kW combined (308 HP) and 448 lb-ft of torque.
The Performance AWD variant produces 320 kW combined (429 HP) and 516 lb-ft of torque, accelerating to 60 MPH in 4.9 seconds.
With the available tow package, the IONIQ 9 can tow up to 5,000 pounds.
The vehicle measures 199.2 inches in length, 78.0 inches in width, 70.5 inches in height, and has a wheelbase of 123.2 inches, making it Hyundai's largest vehicle to date.
Cargo capacity is 46.7 cubic feet with all seats in place, plus a front trunk (frunk) offering 3.1 cubic feet for RWD models and 1.8 cubic feet for AWD models.
The interior features a large tablet display integrating the digital instrument cluster and infotainment system, along with preserved physical buttons on the lower dashboard.

Hyundai designed a walkable space between the dashboard and sliding center console called Universal Island 2.0, which includes bidirectional armrests, wireless charging, a drive mode selector, and deep storage capable of holding up to 0.20 cubic feet.
The IONIQ 9 made its debut ahead of the LA Auto Show and is expected to arrive in Spring 2025, with final pricing, trims, and specifications to be released closer to launch.
source_title: 2026 Hyundai IONIQ 9 Electric SUV Is Finally Here With 300+ Mile Range On All Trims

facts: Governor Brian Kemp and members from Hyundai Motor Group announced Friday that they would be opening it’s first fully dedicated electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facility in Georgia.
Hyundai will be investing $5.54 billion in opening the U.S. smart factory at the Bryan County Megasite.
Other suppliers are expected to invest $1 billion into the project.
The project is expected to bring around 8,100 new jobs to the Coast of Georgia.
The site itself was bought less than a year ago by the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority and the State.
Construction on the new facility is scheduled to begin in January of 2023.
Full production is expected in the first half of 2025.
The facility will have an annual capacity of 300,000 units.
Wages through the project will be well paying advanced manufacturing positions.
The building of the plant contributes to Georgia’s investment in electric mobility, as well as a developing workforce, and the growth of Bryan County.
As the beginning of the project nears, Hyundai will be looking for local vendors and suppliers.

Governor Brian Kemp stated: “We are proud to welcome Hyundai Motor Group to Georgia as we forge an innovative future together … From initial conversations on my economic development mission to Korea to Georgia’s investment in the Bryan County Megasite, we’ve been preparing for an opportunity like this for a long time. My commitment to hardworking Georgians to make our state the best place to live, work, and raise our families remains steadfast, and with this announcement, which is now the largest economic development project in our state’s history, we will continue working to make Georgia the premier destination for quality companies who are creating the jobs of today, tomorrow, and beyond.”

facts: Federal agents arrested hundreds of people at a sprawling battery plant construction site in Georgia on Sept. 4.
The Department of Homeland Security said agents had a judicial search warrant alleging unlawful employment practices.
The operation was one of the Trump administration’s largest immigration raids.
Agents from the Department of Homeland Security, FBI, DEA, ATF, and Georgia State Patrol participated in the operation at Hyundai’s battery plant in Bryan County, southwest of Savannah.
Agents apprehended about 450 people suspected of being in the country illegally, according to an ATF X post.

DHS stated that Immigration and Customs Enforcement's Homeland Security Investigations executed a judicial search warrant for an ongoing criminal investigation for alleged unlawful employment practices and other federal crimes.
The agency did not clarify how many agents took part in the operation or the charges those detained face.

Steven Schrank, special agent in charge of Homeland Security Investigations Atlanta, stated at a news conference streamed by local news stations: "We are making many arrests of undocumented individuals."
Schrank noted they encountered many lawful employees working there, including United States citizens and lawful permanent residents, who were being released.
Schrank said there were no injuries or uses of force in the operation.
Schrank indicated more details on the raid would be released later.
The facility is the HL-GA Battery Company, a battery plant jointly created by LG Energy Solution and Hyundai Motor Group.
The facility is under construction next to the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America at a site spanning nearly 3,000 acres.
Hyundai’s new $7.6 billion plant in a rural area near Savannah has promised to transform the local economy and workforce.
The facility is estimated to create around 8,500 jobs by 2030.
Hyundai spokesperson Bianca Johnson said in an email there was no impact to the metaplant or its production.
Johnson stated the law enforcement action took place at the HL-GA battery plant's construction site.
Johnson said Hyundai is cooperating with law enforcement and is committed to abiding by all labor and immigration regulations.

Mary Beth Kennedy, a spokesperson for the HL-GA Battery Company, stated in a statement that the company was “cooperating fully with the appropriate authorities regarding activity at our construction site.”
Kennedy said, “To assist their work, we have paused construction,” and added, “We do not have further details at this time."
The Savannah Morning News was told to exit the facility as state troopers blocked access.
A helicopter hovered over the plant site amid the raid, while state patrol cars and unidentified black vehicles entered the site.
Images posted by ATF showed men and women in zip ties being escorted off the site.
Georgia State Patrol Capt. Crystal Zion said the department assisted federal officials with “the service of a criminal search warrant in an operation at the Hyundai Metaplant.”
Zion shared no additional information and directed inquiries to Homeland Security Investigations.

The Georgia raid is one of the largest during President Donald Trump’s second term, as he promises a sweeping immigration crackdown across the country using multiple federal agencies and assistance from local and state law enforcement.
Raids have taken place across the country on farms and construction sites, as well as restaurants and car washes.

During Trump’s first term in August 2019, agents arrested 680 people at seven Mississippi chicken processing plants, which officials at the time called the largest single-state immigration crackdown in a decade.
source_title: Hundreds arrested in immigration raid at Georgia Hyundai plant site

facts: More than 300 South Korean workers detained in a massive immigration raid at Hyundai's battery facility in Georgia have arrived back home.
A chartered Korean Air jet carrying the detained workers and 14 non-Koreans departed Atlanta at midday local time on Thursday (17:00 BST).
One South Korean national reportedly chose to stay in the US to seek permanent residency.
The incident has shaken ties between the US and South Korea, whose companies are investing billions in the States – investment President Donald Trump has pushed to secure.
The workers' departure was delayed by more than a day because of an instruction from the White House, according to South Korean President Lee Jae Myung on Thursday.

President Trump ordered the pause to check whether the detained workers were willing to remain in the US to continue working and training Americans, according to a South Korean foreign ministry official.

US officials last week detained 475 people - more than 300 of them South Korean nationals - who they said were working illegally at Hyundai's battery facility, one of the largest foreign investment projects in Georgia.
The detained workers were taken away in handcuffs and chains to be detained, sparking outrage in their home country.
South Korean President Lee Jae Myung stated companies would be "very hesitant" about investing in the US following the raid.

President Lee noted it is common practice for Korean firms to send workers to help set up overseas factories, adding that if that is no longer allowed, establishing manufacturing facilities in the US will only become more difficult and make companies question whether it is worth doing at all.
On Friday, South Korea's foreign ministry called for the US Congress to support a new visa for Korean firms.
During meetings with US senators in Washington this week, Foreign Minister Cho Hyun reiterated concerns among South Koreans over the arrests.
Hyundai chief executive José Muñoz stated the raid will delay the factory's opening by at least two months because the company needs to find new workers to finish the job.
Mr. Muñoz told US media that the raid will create "minimum two to three months delay [in opening the factory] because now all these people want to get back".
LG Energy Solution, which operates the plant with Hyundai, said that many of its employees who were arrested had various types of visas or were under a visa waiver programme.
A worker at the facility told the BBC that the vast majority of the workers detained were mechanics installing production lines at the site and were employed by a contractor.
South Korea has pledged to invest tens of billions of dollars in America, partly to offset tariffs.
Media in South Korea described the raid as a "shock," with Dong-A Ilbo newspaper warning it could have "a chilling effect on the activities of our businesses in the United States".
Yonhap News Agency published an editorial urging the two countries to "cooperate to repair cracks in their alliance".
The timing of the raid, as the two governments engage in sensitive trade talks, has raised concern in Seoul.
The White House defended the operation at the Hyundai plant, dismissing concerns that the raid could deter foreign investment.
On Sunday, US President Donald Trump referenced the raid in a social media post and called for foreign companies to hire Americans.
Trump said the US government would make it "quickly and legally possible" for foreign firms to bring workers into the country if they respected its immigration laws.
source_title: South Korean workers detained in US raid arrive home

facts: South Korea’s leading car component maker Hyundai Mobis announced on Nov. 8 that it had secured $940 million to build U.S. plants through its first overseas green loan.
The funds will be used for the firm’s plan of investing $1.3 billion through 2030 to build new production hubs for electrification in four locations, including Alabama and Georgia.
The facilities will include battery system assembly lines and power electric system factories aimed at meeting the growing demand for electric cars in the United States.
Hyundai Mobis achieved long-term financing with a maturity of 10 years by liaising with seven international financial institutions.
The Seoul-based company raised the funds through its first overseas green loans, which are funding methods for eco-friendly businesses like EVs and renewable energy.
Hyundai Mobis currently operates electrification production hubs in nine locations globally.
Hyundai Mobis is establishing six new electrification production hubs in South Korea, North America, and Indonesia.

A Hyundai Mobis official stated the company remains committed to enhancing its competitiveness in the electrification business through a strategy of selectivity and focus as it transitions into a future mobility technology specialist.
Observers point out that Hyundai Mobis is expected to further increase its investments in the U.S. market.

Daelim University automotive professor Kim Pil-soo stated that Hyundai Mobis is now the world’s sixth-largest auto part maker and must make its presence felt in the U.S. market for EVs to become a bigger player.
Observers expect the company to channel more funds and energies into the U.S. market down the road than it has already announced.
The share price of Hyundai Mobis went down 0.67 percent on the South Korean Stock Exchange on Nov. 8.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis secures $940 million to build US factories

facts: On Monday, November 13, 2023, MOBIS North America Electrified Powertrain, LLC received the Georgia Economic Developers Association (GEDA) Deal of the Year Award in the Mid-Size Community category.

The GEDA award recognizes outstanding achievement in the location or expansion of new businesses in Georgia that meet community economic development goals and involve significant community collaboration.

MOBIS was recognized alongside community leaders from the Development Authority of Bryan County (DABC), City of Richmond Hill, Bryan County, Raydient Places, and the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD).

GEDA is a non-profit association of professionals and volunteers involved in economic development that aims to increase effectiveness, encourage cooperation, exchange information, upgrade professional skills, and foster closer working relationships among Georgia economic development practitioners.

Jon Seagraves, DABC Chairman, stated that recruiting MOBIS to Belfast Commerce Park at Heartwood was a team effort and thanked MOBIS representatives Mr. Park and Mr. Lee for being instrumental in the company's decision to choose Bryan County.
Hyundai MOBIS is a globally recognized South Korean automotive parts maker founded as Hyundai Precision & Industries Corporation in 1977.
The company forms the "parts and services" arm for South Korean automakers Hyundai Motor Company, Genesis Motors, and Kia Motors.

MOBIS's business scope encompasses key automotive parts, specifically e-powertrain systems (Battery Systems and Power Electric Systems), three major modules (Chassis Modules, Cockpit Modules, and Front-end Modules), and aftermarket sales and service.
Since 2019, the Development Authority of Bryan County has assisted with projects representing over 5,000 new jobs and over $1.7 billion in capital investment.

The DABC's tracked investments exclude the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America project, which involves 8,500 jobs and $7.59 billion in capital investment, making it the largest economic development project in Georgia state history.
The Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America project was recruited to Bryan County by the Savannah Harbor I-16 Corridor Joint Development Authority, of which Bryan County is a member.
Heartwood is a new community being developed in Richmond Hill, Georgia, by Raydient Places + Properties, a taxable subsidiary of real estate investment trust Rayonier Inc.
source_title: MOBIS North America Electrified Powertrain, LLC: Named Georgia Developers Association’s 2023 Deal of the Year for Mid-Size Communities - Heartwood Life

facts: Hyundai Mobis has developed a new battery-cell cooling device dubbed Pulsating Heat Pipe (PHP), designed to transfer heat from between cells to the exterior more efficiently.
The PHP device is composed of aluminum alloy and refrigerant and is positioned between battery cells to dissipate heat during DC fast-charging.
Hyundai Mobis says PHP offers 10 times the heat transfer performance of common aluminum, lowering charging time by permitting faster charging cycles.
The pipes function as metal-tube-shaped thermal conductors that diffuse heat through vibration while circulating refrigerant internally to move heat from the cells to the exterior.
Hyundai Mobis is optimistic about putting PHPs into production with minimal effort at the manufacturing level.
The company applied a press process enabling large-scale continuous production in the manufacturing stage, simplifying the PHP manufacturing process and reducing production costs.
Lower battery temperatures will permit faster charging speeds and reduce the time owners need to spend at the charging station.
PHP technology could address EV battery weight issues by making electrically driven cooling fans unnecessary or less bulky.
Solid-state designs are noted as a potential competitor promising greater thermal safety, but such technology remains largely confined to the lab for now.
Hyundai Mobis aims to secure world-class battery cooling technology and commercialize it to enhance its competitiveness in the future mobility market.
source_title: Will This Device Solve EV Battery Heat Issues?

facts: U.S. Secretary of Energy Chris Wright announced the Department of Energy’s Office of Energy Dominance Financing (EDF) has closed a historic $26.5 billion loan package.
The loan package will deliver over $7 billion in electricity cost savings to millions of customers in Georgia and Alabama.
The investment is funded under President Trump’s Working Families Tax Cut and aligns with the Executive Order "Unleashing American Energy".
The loans support two wholly owned subsidiaries of Southern Company.
The investment aims to lower American energy costs, create thousands of jobs, and increase grid reliability in Georgia and Alabama.
Secretary Wright stated that the Energy Department is lowering energy costs and ensuring access to affordable, reliable, and secure energy for decades to come.
Secretary Wright noted the President's directive to reverse past administrations' "energy subtraction agenda" and add more reliable power generation to the electrical grid.
The two loans will build or upgrade over 16 gigawatts (GW) of firm reliable power to the electrical grid.

The power mix includes 5 GW of new gas generation, 6 GW in nuclear improved through uprates and license renewals, hydropower modernization, battery energy storage systems, and over 1,300 miles of transmission and grid enhancement projects.
These loans represent the largest government investment aimed at directly lowering consumer energy costs and increasing grid reliability.
Once all funds are received through the program, the loans are estimated to reduce Southern Company’s interest expenses by over $300 million per year.
The reduced interest expenses will help expedite lower electricity costs for customers.
Southern Company is among the first utilities working with the DOE and the Trump Administration to restore American energy dominance through common-sense energy investments.
In 2025, Southern Company announced plans to enact multiyear rate freezes.
The DOE remains committed to setting a new standard for government energy financing to ensure loans deliver affordable, reliable, and secure energy.
source_title: Energy Department Announces Largest Loan in Department History, Delivering Over $7 Billion in Electricity Cost Savings for Georgia and Alabama Customers | Department of Energy

facts: JTEKT North America is a subsidiary of global automotive and industrial supplier JTEKT Corporation.
The company completed a $5.8 million expansion of its headquarters on the campus of the Clemson University International Center for Automotive Research (CU-ICAR).
The expansion project began in 2017 to accommodate new jobs in Greenville.
The expansion will create a total of 100 positions, all of which are scheduled to be filled by 2020.
JTEKT manufactures steering systems, driveline components, bearings technologies and precision machine tools, which are critical to the state’s automotive industry.
The company employs more than 6,000 associates at 24 sites across North America.
JTEKT operates four manufacturing facilities located in South Carolina.
In 2015, JTEKT announced the consolidation of a portion of its headquarters to CU-ICAR in Greenville.
The expansion added 19,000 square feet of office and lab space at CU-ICAR’s One Research Drive building.
This complements the 135,000 square feet of space JTEKT already occupies between two buildings on the CU-ICAR campus.
The new space will provide work areas for associates who have relocated to Greenville as well as for new positions currently being filled.

Executive Vice President and Chief Operating Officer Mike Davidson stated that Greenville is an excellent location for the growing headquarters due to its central geography relative to the manufacturing footprint and the availability of talented professionals in business and technical fields.
Mike Davidson noted the expansion facilitates co-location of vital functions, continuation of research and development, better customer service, and an innovative environment for associates.

Governor Henry McMaster stated that creating 100 new jobs through the headquarters expansion is a big win for South Carolina’s automotive industry and demonstrates that the state remains an ideal destination for automobile manufacturers and suppliers.
Secretary of Commerce Bobby Hitt stated that South Carolina’s robust automotive supply chain continues to grow and thrive, citing JTEKT’s expansion as a perfect example of that success.

Greenville County Council chairman and director of Greenville Area Development Corporation H.G. “Butch” Kirven Jr. stated that JTEKT’s decision is a testament to the area's dedicated workforce and strong pro-business environment, highlighting it as an example of leading international companies locating in Greenville.
source_title: JTEKT North America Expands Greenville County Headquarters | S.C. Governor Henry McMaster

facts: On April 8, 2025, General Motors presented HL Mando with a 2024 Supplier of the Year Award at GM's 33rd annual Supplier of the Year event in Phoenix, Arizona.
HL Mando is HL Group's leading Tier 1 automotive Software Defined Vehicle (SDV) company.
HL Mando was selected by General Motors for its 2024 Supplier of the Year award.
HL Mando has been awarded the Supplier of the Year honor for five consecutive years and 11 times in total.
The April 8 recognition event was attended by Head of HL Mando Americas region Jason (Jae-hyuk) Kim, R&D Director Jeff Pontius, and Sales Director Alison Ellis.
A total of 92 companies from 12 countries were recognized as excellent partners to General Motors at the event.
Less than 1% of General Motors' suppliers are awarded the Supplier of the Year Award.
HL Mando was recognized in the Brake Apply & Controls category.
HL Mando's Motor on Caliper (MoC) product received the spotlight and is manufactured at HL Mando's Alabama plant.
General Motors praised HL Mando for its localization efforts and technological innovation.
HL Mando's North American localization journey began in 1996.
The corporation established its first R&D center, Mando Engineering & Sales America (MESA), in Michigan to lay the groundwork for North American expansion.
HL Mando launched its first manufacturing facility, Mando Corporation America in Alabama (MCA).
HL Mando won its first Supplier of the Year award in 2003.
The Alabama plant was completed in 2004.
A second production facility was added in 2012 to meet growing demand for casting components.
This second facility became the MCA Georgia Plant.
In 2013, HL Mando expanded its R&D capabilities by establishing a second R&D center, HL Mechatronics (HM), also in Michigan.
MESA focuses on development activities up to mass production, while HM specializes in future-oriented automotive technologies.
HL Mando has recently invested approximately USD 700 million in its North American operations.
The USD 700 million investment demonstrates HL Mando's commitment to the North American sector and continued focus on cutting-edge technology.

Jeff Morrison, GM's global chief procurement officer, stated that General Motors recognizes top global suppliers annually to spotlight innovation and resiliency, and together they are bringing advanced technology and a broad portfolio of vehicles to market for customers.

Jason (Jae-hyuk) Kim, Head of HL Mando Americas region, stated that three decades of North American localization efforts have led to strong customer satisfaction and expressed a look forward to shaping the future with GM through collaboration in automotive software and autonomous driving.
General Motors' Supplier of the Year awards recognize global suppliers for execution across safety, innovation, and resilience.
Award selections are made by a global cross-functional team based on 2024 performance and alignment to GM's core values and ambitious goals.
source_title: HL Mando Recognized as 2024 Supplier of the Year by General Motors

facts: Any individual or company who converts a vehicle to operate on an alternative fuel must report the conversion to the Arkansas Department of Finance and Administration (DFA) within 10 days of the conversion.
An owner or operator who fails to report such a conversion may be subject to a penalty.
Excise taxes on alternative fuels are imposed on a gasoline gallon equivalent (GGE) basis.
The tax rate for each alternative fuel type is based on the number of motor vehicles licensed in the state that use the specific fuel, not including vehicles the federal government owns or leases.

The Arkansas Department of Finance and Administration (DFA) and the Arkansas State Highways and Transportation Department must prepare an annual report with the number of alternative fuel vehicles licensed in the state and the tax revenue generated.
The DFA must establish the tax rate annually by April 1.
Licensed alternative fuel suppliers must pay alternative fuel taxes for product dispensed, sold to a dealer or user, or used in a motor vehicle owned or operated by the supplier.
Alternative fuel suppliers must prepare a monthly report on the number of GGEs of alternative fuels sold and possess a sufficient number of credits to cover the alternative fuel sales tax.
The governor established the Arkansas Council on Future Mobility (Council) to support advanced mobility technology, including EVs and AVs.
The Council must identify state laws that are prohibitive to EVs and AVs and provide recommendations on policies and incentives to promote their advancement.
The Council must identify future tasks and goals related to EV and AV education, workforce training, and economic development.
The Council published a report of their recommendations in December 2022.
Recommendations include increasing the EV annual registration fee.
Recommendations include increasing funding for existing state EV programs.
Recommendations include considering creating tax credits to fund the expansion of the state’s direct current fast charger (DCFC) network.
Recommendations include creating EV and automated vehicle career technical education training programs for high school and community college students.
HEV and EV owners must pay an annual fee in addition to other registration fees.
The annual fee is $200 for all-electric vehicles, $100 for plug-in hybrid electric vehicles, and $50 for HEVs.
Fees contribute to the State Highway and Transportation Department Fund.
After July 1, 2024, the fees will also go to the County Aid Fund and the Municipal Aid Fund.
Vehicles registered with a military service and veterans special license plate with permanent decal are exempt from the annual fee.
A person or corporation that supplies electricity to the public exclusively to charge electric vehicles is not defined as a public utility in Arkansas.
Investor-owned utilities (IOUs) may install and own public direct current fast charging stations.
IOUs must allow site hosts to install a metering device for measuring electricity sold at the EV charging station.
Site hosts must choose the EV charging station equipment vendor and network service from the utility’s pre-qualified list.
IOUs must submit an annual report to the Public Service Commission on the construction and operation of the public EV charging stations.

The U.S. Department of Transportation’s (DOT) NEVI Formula Program required the Arkansas Department of Transportation (ARDOT) to submit an annually updated EV Infrastructure Deployment Plan for fiscal years 2022 through 2026.
The plan must describe how the state intends to distribute NEVI funds and their physical and cybersecurity strategies, including a Community Engagement Outcomes Report.
The Arkansas Division of Environmental Quality (DEQ) Office of Air Quality provides grants to protect air quality.
Grants include U.S. Environmental Protection Agency Diesel Emissions Reduction Act funding for projects that reduce emissions from medium- and heavy-duty vehicles.

Funding is available for projects that reduce diesel emissions by employing exhaust controls, engine upgrades, idling reduction technologies, engine replacements, or vehicle and equipment replacements.

facts: Georgia's Freight Plan serves as the statewide, long-term strategy to guide freight investments and strengthen economic competitiveness.
Designed as a forward-looking and actionable roadmap, the plan advances freight mobility and drives sustained economic growth across the state.
A new 2027 State Freight Plan will be completed in early 2027 to position GDOT to develop and guide future investments.
Georgia developed its most recent State Freight Plan, which includes strategies built around insights on logistics patterns and business requirements following the pandemic.
The Freight Plan is required to be updated every four years.
As required by federal law, the plan includes a fiscally constrained list of projects to be funded through the National Highway Freight Program.

facts: Attorney General Chris Carr joined a coalition of 50 attorneys general in announcing a $149,673,750 settlement with Mercedes-Benz USA and Daimler AG.

The settlement addresses violations of state laws prohibiting unfair or deceptive trade practices by marketing, selling, and leasing vehicles equipped with illegal and undisclosed emissions defeat devices.

Beginning in 2008 and continuing to 2016, the states allege Mercedes manufactured, marketed, advertised, and distributed nationwide more than 211,000 diesel passenger cars and vans equipped with software defeat devices.
The defeat devices optimized emission controls during emissions tests while reducing those controls outside of normal operations.
The defeat devices enabled vehicles to far exceed legal limits of nitrogen oxides (NOx) emissions, a harmful pollutant that causes respiratory illness and contributes to the formation of smog.

Mercedes allegedly engaged in this conduct to achieve design and performance goals, such as increased fuel efficiency and reduced maintenance, that it was unable to meet while complying with applicable emission standards.
Mercedes concealed the existence of these defeat devices from state and federal regulators and the public.
At the same time, Mercedes marketed the vehicles to consumers as “environmentally-friendly” and in compliance with applicable emissions regulations.
The settlement requires Mercedes-Benz USA and Daimler AG to pay $120 million to the states immediately upon the effective date of the settlement.
An additional $29,673,750 will be suspended and potentially waived pending completion of a comprehensive consumer relief program.
Georgia will receive $4,385,953 through today’s settlement.
Approximately 10,005 of impacted vehicles were sold or registered in Georgia.
The consumer relief program extends to the estimated 39,565 vehicles which, as of August 1, 2023, had not been repaired or permanently removed from service in the United States.
Mercedes must bear the cost of installing approved emission modification software on each of the affected vehicles.
The company must provide consumers with an extended warranty and will pay consumers $2,000 per subject vehicle.

The company must comply with reporting requirements and reforms to their practices, including a prohibition on any further engagement in unfair or deceptive marketing or sale of diesel vehicles or misrepresentations regarding emissions compliance.

Today’s settlement follows similar settlements reached previously between the states and Volkswagen, Fiat Chrysler and German engineering company Robert Bosch GmbH over its development of the cheat software.
Automaker Fiat Chrysler and its subsidiaries paid $72.5 million to the states in 2019.
Bosch paid $98.7 million in 2019.
Volkswagen reached a $570 million settlement with the states in 2016.

Joining Carr in this settlement are the attorneys general of Alabama, Alaska, Arkansas, Colorado, Connecticut, Delaware, the District of Columbia, Florida, Hawaii, Idaho, Illinois, Indiana, Iowa, Kansas, Kentucky, Louisiana, Maine, Maryland, Massachusetts, Michigan, Minnesota, Mississippi, Missouri, Montana, Nebraska, Nevada, New Hampshire, New Jersey, New Mexico, New York, North Carolina, North Dakota, Ohio, Oklahoma, Oregon, Pennsylvania, Rhode Island, South Carolina, South Dakota, Tennessee, Texas, Utah, Vermont, Virginia, Washington, West Virginia, Wisconsin, Wyoming and Puerto Rico.
source_title: Carr Announces Nearly $150 Million Settlement With Mercedes-Benz USA and Daimler AG Over Emissions Fraud | Georgia Attorney General's Consumer Protection Division

facts: Transitioning to electric vehicles (EVs) is transforming the automotive supply chain, requiring new innovations in battery storage technology.

Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL), the Tennessee Valley Authority, and the Tennessee Department of Economic & Community Development collaborated to drive home why EV battery firms should locate their innovation operations in Tennessee.
Tennessee is the location of nearly 40 percent of the nation's EV jobs and investments.
The collaboration team targeted a dozen automotive and EV battery companies.
A key win included a $2.3 billion commitment by GM-LG Energy Solution JV to build a battery cell plant in Spring Hill, TN.
The Spring Hill facility will be the second battery cell plant for the JV in the country.
The investment will bring 1,300 new jobs to Tennessee.
Jesse Smith and Jeffrey Cornett received the Economic Development award for leading this project.
The project is titled 'ORNL's and Partners' $2B Win Is Largest in Tennessee's History'.
source_title: Winners of the 2022 Technology Transfer Working Group Awards! | Department of Energy

facts: Hyundai Mobis presented its expansion strategy in the North American market with highly value-added technologies at the NAIAS 2022 Press Conference.
The company is planning to expand production facilities worldwide, including a new EV components plant for prompt supply to North American automakers.
Hyundai Mobis aims to reach about 30-40% CAGR in the North American market by 2030 with future mobility technologies.

Hyundai Mobis (KRX 012330), the sixth largest global automotive supplier, introduced its expansion strategy in the North American market during a press conference at the North American International Auto Show (NAIAS) on Sept. 14, 2022.
Axel Maschka, Executive Vice President and head of Hyundai Mobis Global OE Sales, stated that the two keywords of the company's growth strategy are Plant Expansion and Value Expansion.
The company plans to expand its production facilities across the globe for prompt and timely supply to global automakers.

Axel Maschka said that Hyundai Mobis will construct more EV dedicated sites in the region and invest in EV components and battery system assemblies to allow rapid and environmentally optimized local production.
Hyundai Mobis currently operates 44 production bases, with North American facilities located in Alabama, Georgia, Ohio, and Michigan.
North America leading the electric vehicle shift to achieve carbon neutrality and green mobility is integral to the company's electrification production expansion plan.
The company believes having a strong production capacity will be key to winning sales orders as the EV market continues to grow.
A new manufacturing site, combined with over ten years of expertise in producing high-quality electrification components, will allow Hyundai Mobis to see continued growth in the market.
Hyundai Mobis revealed a value expansion plan to increase its sales orders in the North American market with value-added technologies optimized specifically for electric and autonomous vehicles.
The company presented future mobility technologies including the electric Complete Chassis Platform Module (eCCPM) and next-generation AR HUD.
Axel Maschka stated that Hyundai Mobis has a wide range of innovations in its portfolio, is ready for the future of mobility, and aims to bring the future to the present and make it more valuable.

The company highlighted its successful mass-production of key electrification components, including the battery system for the e-GMP platform used in Hyundai and Kia electric vehicles, and the PE module.
Value-added products including the eCCPM module, electrification platform, AR HUD, and lighting grilles are drawing attention from clients in North America for their versatility and innovation.
During the presentation, Hyundai Mobis announced its goal of driving a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 36% in the North American market through the year 2030.
The company reported steady growth in total order volumes over the last few years, with $660 million USD in 2020, $1.4 billion USD in 2021, and $1.7 billion USD in the first half of 2022.
Hyundai Mobis is the global no. 6 automotive supplier, headquartered in Seoul, Korea.
The company has outstanding expertise in sensors, sensor fusion in ECUs and software development for safety control.
Hyundai Mobis's products include various components for electrification, brakes, chassis and suspension, steering, airbags, lighting and automotive electronics.
The company operates an R&D headquarter in Korea, with four technology centers in the United States, Germany, China and India.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis takes a new step with future mobility through further expansion in the North American market

facts: Rivian will expand its manufacturing operations, locating its second U.S. plant in the state of Georgia.
A carbon-conscious campus is planned east of Atlanta, in Morgan and Walton Counties.
The project represents a $5-billion site development and manufacturing investment.
The plant will eventually employ more than 7,500 workers.
Once ramped, the Georgia facility will be capable of producing up to 400,000 vehicles per year.
Construction on the facility is expected to begin in summer 2022.
The start of production is slated for 2024.
Site considerations included logistics, environmental impact, renewable energy production, availability and quality of talent, and fit with Rivian company culture.
Rivian Chief People Officer Helen Russell stated the company is happy to partner with Georgia on the new manufacturing site to build next-generation products important for scaling the business.
Helen Russell noted the partnership is driven by a compelling purpose and rooted in building a highly collaborative environment that creates a true sense of belonging.
Helen Russell expressed confidence that alongside Illinois, Georgia is the place for Rivian to continue to scale and thrive.
Rivian’s parcel for the site will be almost 2,000 acres and include abundant natural space.
Rivian will develop community engagement and workforce training programs in the area, similar to its facility in Normal.
Rivian is also scaling capacity at its existing plant in Normal, Illinois.
The Normal plant was recently approved for a 623,000-square-foot expansion, bringing its total footprint to approximately 4 million square feet.
There are further plans to extend warehouse, storage, and production capacity onsite at the Normal facility.
Rivian plans to hire an additional 800-1,000 employees in Normal by the second quarter of 2022.
Rivian and Georgia are planning 2022 town hall events near the manufacturing site so area residents can learn more about the company and the site plan.
Rivian offers industry-leading compensation, generous benefits, and equity in the company for every team member to drive a real sense of ownership.
New Normal and Georgia-based positions were posted on December 16, 2021.

source_title: Rivian to site second manufacturing plant in Georgia

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced on June 22, 2023, that automotive parts manufacturer NVH Korea will establish a new facility in Locust Grove.
The project will create over 160 new jobs and bring $72 million in investment to Henry County.
This announcement marks the second economic development project Governor Kemp has announced during his week-long trip to the Paris Air Show.
Governor Brian P. Kemp stated that Georgia's reliable infrastructure network and great workforce enable job creators to find success in every community.
Governor Brian P. Kemp highlighted the incredible growth of Georgia's e-mobility and battery industries as a testament to the state's ability to support large suppliers like NVH Korea.
NVH Korea is an automotive parts manufacturer based in the City of Ulsan, South Korea, and was established in 1984.
The company specializes in automotive noise, vibration, and heat-control parts, which it currently produces in Korea, Europe, and India.
NVH Korea already operates a manufacturing facility in Georgia that produces floor mats, cargo mats, and cargo trays.
The new Locust Grove facility will supply battery parts for electric vehicles (EVs) in the United States for manufacturers including Hyundai Mobis, Kia Georgia, and Hyundai Motor Group.
Ja Kyum Koo, Chairman and CEO of NVH Korea, stated that Georgia was chosen as the company's first EV battery component manufacturing site.
Cooperation between state agencies and NVH Korea staff helped guide the project from selection through planning.
The new facility will be located at Gardner Logistics Park on Colvin Drive in Locust Grove.
The facility will produce EV battery system components that protect, connect, and sense the performance of battery cells.
Operations at the new facility are expected to begin in the second quarter of 2024.
Ja Kyum Koo noted that this project is the first step in NVH Korea's effort to contribute to the electrification of the mobility industry.
Mayor Robert Price of Locust Grove stated that NVH's investment will increase the city's manufacturing jobs base and integrate it into Georgia's electric vehicle ecosystem.
Carlotta Harrell, Chair of the Henry County Board of Commissioners, stated that the announcement will add quality manufacturing jobs for citizens and diversify the local economy.
Project Manager Jacob Lee represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on the project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Henry County Development Authority, Georgia EMC, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that NVH Korea is establishing its first U.S. or e-mobility manufacturing presence.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that manufacturers creating jobs of the future are driving an economic transformation across Georgia's communities.
From July 1 through December 31, 2022, the automotive industry was the second-largest source of new jobs and investment in Georgia.
Since 2020, Georgia has attracted more than $22.7 billion in investments and created over 28,400 jobs along the electric vehicle supply chain.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: EV Parts Supplier NVH Korea to Create Over 160 Jobs, Invest $72M in Henry County | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

canonical_name: U.S. Department of Energy Vehicle Technologies Office Fiscal Year 2024 Batteries Funding Opportunity Announcement
facts: The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) announced $43 million in funding for projects that will advance research, development, demonstration, and deployment (RDD&D) in several areas critical to the future of advanced batteries.
The funding will drive innovations in low-cost electric vehicle (EV) battery electrode, cell, or pack manufacturing.
The funding aims to improve battery safety and reduce cascading failures.
The funding aims to strengthen the domestic supply chain of inexpensive and abundant battery materials.

DOE’s Vehicle Technologies Office (VTO) will supply the funding and build on the Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy’s leadership in RDD&D of new technologies leading to efficient and affordable mobility options.

Topic 1, Improved 12 Volt Lead Acid Batteries for Safety-Critical Electric Vehicle Applications, is focused on improving the service life and performance requirements to meet critical safety features while reducing cost ($10 million).

Topic 2, Develop Vehicle or Structural Level Strategies to Reduce the Likelihood of the Cascading Effects of Electric Vehicle Fires, is focused on university-led teams conducting research at the cell, pack, and vehicle level ($3.9 million).
Topic 3, Battery Electrode, Cell, and Pack Manufacturing Cost Reduction, is focused on developing improved manufacturing technologies for EV battery electrodes, cells, and packs ($12.5 million).

Topic 4, Silicon-Based Anodes for Lithium-Ion Batteries, is focused on researching, fabricating, and testing lithium battery cells implementing silicon electrodes with a commercially available cathode technology to achieve cell and cost performance targets (more than 350 Wh/kg of usable energy with a cell cost target of less than $70/kWh) ($12.5 million).

Topic 5, High Energy Density Conversion Cathodes, is focused on developing high energy density battery cells containing metal chalcogenide, oxide, or halide cathodes by solving key challenges for the cathode, electrolyte, electrode integrity, or safety ($4.05 million).
The FOA issue date was August 8, 2024.
The submission deadline for concept papers is September 9, 2024, at 5:00 p.m. ET.
The submission deadline for full applications is October 30, 2024, at 5:00 p.m. ET.
The expected date for EERE selection notifications is March 2025.
The expected timeframe for award negotiations is March-June 2025.
The FOA number is DE-FOA-0003383.
The total FOA amount is $42,950,000.
source_title: Funding Notice: Fiscal Year 2024 Vehicle Technologies Office Batteries Funding Opportunity Announcement | Department of Energy

facts: LPO has launched a new portfolio projects dynamic map that showcases its clean energy and advanced technology vehicle manufacturing projects, including active conditional commitments and loans, across the United States.
The Office of Energy Dominance Financing announced the launch on September 21, 2023.

Visitors can use search filters to track and dynamically search all of LPO’s publicly announced projects by state, loan type (e.g., direct loan, loan guarantee, partial loan guarantee), technology sector, and loan status (e.g., active, conditional, repaid).
The map's project details feature connects search results to every LPO project while summarizing statistics by customized sub-portfolio searches.

The map provides geographic visualization that gives a dynamic geographic representation of LPO’s projects, enabling visitors to see the distribution of initiatives across the nation and identify regional trends and opportunities.
Visitors can consult the map for the latest information on LPO’s portfolio, including new conditional commitment and financial close announcements.
source_title: Introducing the LPO Portfolio Projects Dynamic Map | Department of Energy

facts: Georgia is home to some of SK’s most significant investments, including manufacturing sites for advanced batteries that are part of U.S. “battery belt.”
SK Battery America is the first U.S. battery manufacturing site of SK On.
SK Battery America currently operates two EV battery plants in Commerce, Georgia.
SK Battery America's facility address is 1760 SK Boulevard, Commerce, GA 30529.
SK Inc. C&C is an advanced IT company providing local support in Georgia for the SK Battery America EV battery site.
SK Inc. C&C's location is at 2915 Premiere Parkway, Duluth, GA 30097.
Hyundai Motor Group and SK On formed a joint venture to establish an EV battery cell plant in Bartow County, Georgia.
The Hyundai Motor Group and SK On joint venture EV battery cell plant is expected to be operational by the end of 2025.
The joint venture facility location is Cartersville, GA.
SK tes, a subsidiary of SK Ecoplant, is an electronic waste management company and global leader in e-waste recycling, IT asset disposition, and battery recycling.
SK tes has a location at 5238 Royal Woods Pkwy, Suite 110, Tucker, GA 30084.
SK On Tennessee is an electric vehicle battery manufacturing facility in Stanton, Tennessee.
SK On Tennessee is expected to start production in 2028.
GridFlex is a joint venture between SK Gas and SK Eternix that invests in energy storage and renewable energy projects across the US.
Key Capture Energy identifies, develops, constructs and operates energy storage solutions (ESS) to foster a greater deployment of renewable energy.
SK Enmove Americas Inc., a subsidiary of SK Enmove, is a supplier of auto transmission oil, engine oil, and other lubricants for the automotive industry.
SK Gas is expanding beyond its legacy LPG business as it invests in new sustainable solutions like hydrogen charging stations and hydrogen fuel cells.
SK Signet is a global leader in smart, convenient charging solutions for all types of electric vehicles - with a special emphasis on fast and ultra-fast EV chargers.
Solid Power is an industry-leading developer of next-generation all-solid-state battery technology for electric vehicles.
Group14 Technologies is a global provider of silicon-carbon composite materials for lithium-ion-powered devices and vehicles.
SK Siltron CSS produces silicon carbide (SiC) wafers for the semiconductor industry that can be used for sustainable solutions in EVs, renewable energy, and telecommunications.

PassKey, a subsidiary of SK innovation E&S, is a U.S.-based energy transition business with investments in technologies such as EV charging, energy storage and advanced software solutions support the move toward net-zero carbon emissions.
SK innovation E&S, traditionally focused on natural gas, is pursuing renewable energy, clean hydrogen, and other alternative energy solutions in the transition to a carbon-neutral LNG value chain.
SK Geo Centric is global chemical company accelerating its transition into eco-friendly materials and recycling solutions.
source_title: Discover Our U.S. Investment and Operations Map | SK

facts: Global corporations throughout the clean-energy sector are spending billions to build facilities to manufacture a wide array of solar, e-mobility, and innovative battery products.
The State of Georgia ranks as a top-choice destination for these projects.
These projects are literally creating tens of thousands of jobs in the state.
Georgia Quick Start is a 56-year-old program that provides company-specific workforce training.
The program has been routinely named the top state workforce development program in the country.
Quick Start has helped prepare more than 1.8 million Georgians for new jobs since its creation in 1967.
Companies benefiting from Quick Start include Rivian, Hyundai Motor Group, and SK Battery America.
Quick Start customizes training to replicate the actual processes that will be used on-site.
The program creates a workforce ready to start production on Day One.
Quick Start offers free-of-charge job training for qualified companies.
While utilized by a diverse group of manufacturing sectors, the majority of its recent clients are clean energy companies and their suppliers.
In June (2023), the U.S. Secretary of Education spoke at Agnes Scott College.
The U.S. Secretary of Education praised Quick Start as one of Georgia’s unique workforce-training assets.
An article by Alison F. Takemura for Canary Media detailed Quick Start's impact in the clean energy space.
Robert Howey was hired as a 'tabber operator' on the production line at the Hanwha Qcells solar manufacturing plant in Dalton.
His role involved watching over machines that solder silicon wafers together, an early stage in making a solar module.
He initially did not understand how his task fit into the overall process and felt nervous about doing his job well.
Georgia Quick Start provided him guidance during a week of on-ramping.
Trainers walked him through all the steps of making a solar panel, giving him confidence to start his new career.
Howey described Quick Start as 'a lifesaver.'
Quick Start currently operates five training centers.
The program is expanding its advanced manufacturing center.
Quick Start is designing three new facilities for clean energy manufacturers and suppliers.
One of the new facilities will be located in Bryan County.
Hyundai Motor Group is investing $7.59 billion in an electric vehicle (EV) and battery manufacturing 'metaplant' with aggressive production timelines.
The state-of-the-art Quick Start facility for Hyundai will be able to train 300 to 400 new Hyundai hires a week.
source_title: Quick Start: Meeting the Demands of Georgia’s Clean Energy Boom | Georgia

facts: Stryten Energy LLC is a U.S.-based energy storage solutions provider headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia.
The company added to its in-house battery testing and analysis capabilities with a new laboratory space at its new corporate headquarters.
The facility includes dedicated space for testing lead, lithium, and vanadium batteries and materials.
A ribbon-cutting celebration for the battery lab opening was held on August 26.

Ribbon-cutting participants included Roger Miksad (President & Executive Director, Battery Council International), Tim Vargo (Chairman of the Board, Stryten Energy), Mike Judd (CEO and President, Stryten Energy), Nathan Barker (Legislative Director for U.S. Representative Rich McCormick, GA-06), and Ronnie Joyner (Senior Manager, Laboratory, Stryten Energy).
Event attendees included staff members from the offices of Senator Jon Ossoff, Senator Raphael Warnock, Representative Rich McCormick, Representative Andrew Clyde, and local community leaders.
Attendees toured the new battery lab and an onsite working solar plus energy storage microgrid.
Lieutenant Governor Burt Jones issued a proclamation recognizing the celebration.

In his proclamation, Lieutenant Governor Burt Jones stated that Stryten Energy is helping shape the future of energy storage technology in Georgia important for national U.S. energy independence and security, while forging economic development and innovation in the state.
Mike Judd, CEO and President of Stryten Energy, stated that achieving long-term U.S. energy resiliency and security requires a range of solutions across battery chemistries.

Mike Judd noted that the expanded lab will allow the company to stress test lead and lithium batteries, achieve market readiness for newer chemistries like vanadium, and collaborate with customers to find additional applications for these chemistries.

The lab supports Stryten Energy’s engineering, research and development, and quality assurance departments, as well as manufacturing facilities, in new product development, technology evaluations, warranty investigations, and root cause analysis.

The Corporate Materials Lab performs tests on battery materials including electrolyte, polypropylene, lead, and lead alloys for automotive, heavy-duty commercial, industrial, lithium, and vanadium redox flow batteries.

The Lead Battery Test Lab performs electrical performance and teardown testing on automotive, industrial, and heavy-duty commercial batteries, including enhanced flooded (EFB), conventional flooded, and absorbed glass mat (AGM) batteries.
The Lithium Battery Test Lab is the newest addition to the facility and focuses on non-destructive functional charge/discharge, environmental, and UL-related testing on lithium battery products.
The new lab will serve as a model to other local energy technology companies, further strengthening Georgia's claim as a key power player in the clean energy sector.
Stryten Energy provides energy storage solutions across the Essential Power, Motive Power, Transportation, Military, and Government sectors.
The company's stored energy technologies include advanced lead, lithium, and vanadium redox flow batteries, intelligent chargers, and energy performance management software.
Stryten Energy powers applications ranging from submarines to subcompacts, microgrids, warehouses, distribution centers, cars, trains, and trucks.
source_title: Stryten Energy Opens Expanded, State-of-the-Art Battery Testing Lab

facts: Stryten Energy is headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia.
The company partners with some of the world’s most recognized companies to meet the growing demand for reliable and sustainable energy storage capacity.
Stryten's stored energy technologies include advanced lead, lithium and vanadium redox flow batteries.
The company produces intelligent chargers and energy performance management software that keep people on the move and supply chains running.
Stryten powers everything from submarines to subcompacts, microgrids, warehouses, distribution centers, cars, trains and trucks.
