The company focuses on the development and sales of passenger cars, vans, and other vehicle-related services.
The corporation is one of the world’s largest manufacturers of high-end passenger cars.
MBUSA will continue to offer drivers a diverse lineup.
The U.S. is an important market for the company with sales of 374,100 vehicles last year.
The company is responsible for 11,100 direct jobs in the U.S., including corporate roles and customer service roles in dealerships around the country.

Jason Hoff, CEO of Mercedes-Benz North America, stated: “We thank the State of Georgia for its support in deepening Mercedes-Benz’s roots in the Atlanta area as we bring even more talented team members to this world-class city.”
Jason Hoff stated: “This strengthens our position for continued growth and reinforces our established commitment to the U.S. market.”
Jason Hoff stated: “Bringing our teams closer together will enable us to be more agile, increase speed to market, and ensure the best customer experience.”

The Sandy Springs facility originally opened in 2018 housing sales teams and has since been expanded to house financial services and corporate arms.
The relocation and development are expected to take up to a year, with all jobs and facilities open by August of next year.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia continues to lead in mobility and technical innovation, attracting world-class automotive companies.

facts: Mercedes-Benz announced it will relocate its corporate offices to metro Atlanta.
New Jersey served as the headquarters for Mercedes-Benz's U.S. operations for over 50 years.
New Jersey lawmakers made a last-minute incentive effort to retain Mercedes-Benz.
Governor Chris Christie's spokesman stated New Jersey could not effectively compete with Georgia for the relocation.
Mercedes-Benz USA cited the high cost of doing business and tax environment as the primary reason for leaving New Jersey.
Mercedes-Benz pledged $93 million in capital costs to build a new headquarters in Georgia.
The nonpartisan Tax Foundation rated New Jersey as having the worst overall business tax climate, literally 50th out of 50 states.
Georgia ranked 36th overall in the Tax Foundation's state business climate index, besting New Jersey in every category except unemployment insurance.
Georgia's corporate tax rate is 6 percent, ranking 8th nationally, while New Jersey's corporate tax rate is 9 percent, ranking 41st.
The borough of Montvale charges Mercedes-Benz $21.81 per $1,000 of assessed value for local, county and school taxes.
Sandy Springs, expected to be Mercedes' temporary or permanent home, would charge a property tax rate of $35.38 per thousand of assessed value.
Mercedes-Benz pays property taxes on full assessed value in New Jersey, with last year's taxes on its 37-acre campus valued at about $37 million totaling $916,700.
In Georgia, residential and commercial property is assessed at only 40 percent of its value rather than the full value.
For every $1 million in property value, Mercedes-Benz pays $21,810 in taxes in New Jersey but would pay $14,154 in Georgia.
Expected exemptions from the Fulton County Development Authority would cut Mercedes' Georgia tax bill in half for the first year and increase it just 5 percent for a decade.
Fulton County Tax Administrator Matt Buff stated that what looks like a higher rate in Georgia is actually 60 percent off initially, with even more savings after exemptions.
Site selection consultant John Boyd of the Boyd Company Inc. predicted the relocation to Fulton County would reduce Mercedes-Benz's costs, including labor and property taxes, by about 20 percent.
Mercedes-Benz corporate communications manager Donna Boland stated that company research shows a cost of living differential from the move in the 20 percent to 30 percent range.
Mercedes-Benz CEO Steve Cannon downplayed the role of incentives in the relocation decision.
Neither state has made public what it offered, but Georgia's incentive package could be valued at $23 million, not including local incentives.
Mercedes-Benz expects additional savings from labor costs in a right-to-work state like Georgia.
The relocation targets lower housing costs in the Southeast and a push to find employees and buyers among millennials.
Kelley Blue Book senior analyst Karl Brauer noted that auto executives see millennials as key to avoiding the appearance of being outdated.
Karl Brauer indicated a likely new manufacturing plant for Mercedes-Benz in the near future, located somewhere in the Southeast.
The planned manufacturing plant would follow Kia, Toyota and other automakers to the region, benefiting from lower business costs.
Brauer speculated that Mercedes has mapped out expansion options 50, 100, or 250 miles from an Atlanta headquarters.
John Boyd described the Southeast as becoming the "center of gravity" for the auto industry.
Rutgers University professor Joe Seneca observed that both Georgia and New Jersey have had a tepid post-recession job recovery.
Seneca characterized the state competition for investment as "intense economic development warfare" indicating a "Darwinian nature" where more is to be expected.
PolitiFact concluded that the move will save Mercedes-Benz on its tax bill and expert analysis shows cost savings of about 20 percent on factors such as labor expenses.
source_title: PolitiFact | New Jersey's taxes, cost of living paid off for Georgia with Mercedes

Section 179 is a business tax code that allows deducting the cost of equipment like vehicles partially or completely for the current tax year if put into use before December 31.

facts: Factory workers are aging and manufacturers are worried there won’t be enough young people with the technical skills to do their jobs.
10 states have embraced Germany’s apprenticeship model to help fill these manufacturing positions.
In Georgia, high school students are training for factory jobs in a German-style apprenticeship program.
Seventeen-year-old Northgate High School student Cole McKeehan wakes up at 3 a.m. on Fridays to get to his job at E.G.O. North America.
E.G.O. is a radiant heating elements and electronics factory, which mostly makes the parts inside electric stoves, in Newnan, about an hour’s drive south of Atlanta.
The state of Georgia wants more teens like McKeehan to get exposure to manufacturing while they’re in high school by expanding the Georgia Consortium for Advanced Technical Training (CATT).
Students start the three-year program when they’re 15 and are matched with a nearby technical college and a factory where they learn technical skills firsthand.
McKeehan splits his time between his studies at his high school, classes at the technical college and work experience shaping metal at E.G.O., where he makes $10 an hour.
This year, he expects to graduate with a high school diploma, an associate’s degree in precision manufacturing and a full-time job offer.
McKeehan is currently one of 27 students in the apprenticeship program.
It began with just 10 students in the fall of 2016 in Coweta County, Georgia.
Georgia Lt. Gov. Casey Cagle said the program is a great win-win for not only the student but obviously for industry.
The state launched the program in 2016 after a German company, Grenzebach, which makes manufacturing equipment for other factories, had trouble finding workers.
A report from The Manufacturing Institute and Deloitte found U.S. manufacturers need to fill more than 3.5 million jobs over the next decade.

Starting with Tennessee in 2011, the German American Chamber of Commerce helped set up German-style apprenticeship programs in ten states: Georgia, Illinois, Wisconsin, Michigan, Indiana, Virginia, North Carolina, South Carolina, Alabama and Tennessee.
There are plans underway to help expand the program to six more states.

Tony Wilding, a manager of the Michelin Tread Technologies plant in Covington, Georgia, said they have a hard time filling manufacturing or reliability technician jobs and have to go outside of the area.
Wilding’s company recently recruited a 15-year-old high school sophomore.
Companies train and mentor high school students, but also commit to $25,000 to fund student salaries.
In Georgia there are 20 manufacturers that are part of the program.
Kenny Adkins with the Technical College System of Georgia said hundreds of students apply and the state wants to accept more of them.
The state relaunched Georgia Piedmont Technical College’s manufacturing program in Covington with a new Advanced Manufacturing Center.
It includes five labs and more than $1 million of new equipment.
It provides training in machine tools, electronics, mechanical training, automation and process control, as well as industrial wiring and motor controls.
Adkins said it is part of the state’s effort to update the labs at the technical colleges.
Training at the technical colleges is a key part of the program since child labor laws in the U.S. prevent the apprentices from working at the manufacturing plants until they turn 16.
Lt. Gov. Cagle said he expects the number of high school students in the Georgia CATT program will double in 2018.
The state of Georgia aims to offer this kind of opportunity to every high school student by 2020.
Cagle set a bold goal so that by 2020 every student in Georgia will have access to a College and Career Academy, with all high school students eventually becoming GA CATT apprentices.
source_title: Georgia embraces Germany’s apprenticeship model to overcome skill shortages – Skill Reporter

facts: Michelin has fully deployed Michelin TreadEye, a proprietary technology designed to enhance the retread process and deliver measurable value to fleets.
Michelin Retread Technologies has completed a phased rollout and activated TreadEye across its U.S. franchise network.
TreadEye serves as a tread depth assessment tool that helps fleets get the most from every tire.
The system collects 1,200 measurement points, offering insight into tread wear and casing condition.
Key advantages include identifying pull points by fleet location, maximizing rubber on the road, and reducing casing damage.
It also helps fleets avoid DOT violations and minimize downtime from late pulls.

Nate Kirian, VP of sales and distribution for commercial business lines at Michelin, stated that TreadEye is a game-changer for retread operations and fleets, highlighting Michelin's innovation benefiting customers.
source_title: Michelin TreadEye Enhances Retread Efficiency

Page assembles 5 consecutive years of EPA Toxics Release Inventory Form R filings for Tread Technologies Co, spanning 2019–2023.
Each annual total aggregates on-site air emissions, surface-water discharges, land disposal, and off-site transfers reported by the facility under EPCRA Section 313.
Reported releases opened at 8.3K in 2019 and closed at 6.1K in 2023 – a decrease of 25.7% over the window.
The top contributor by cumulative mass is Zinc compounds at 33.5K.
In 2019, total releases were 8.3K lbs involving 1 chemical.
In 2020, total releases were 7.3K lbs involving 1 chemical, a -11.8% year-over-year change.
In 2021, total releases were 6.2K lbs involving 1 chemical, a -14.9% year-over-year change.
In 2022, total releases were 5.6K lbs involving 1 chemical, a -10.1% year-over-year change.
In 2023, total releases were 6.1K lbs involving 1 chemical, a +10.0% year-over-year change.

TRI figures are estimated, not metered, so year-over-year deltas can reflect methodology revisions, ownership changes, or shifts in what a facility classifies as reportable, not only physical emission changes.
A decrease in reported releases does not necessarily indicate improved environmental performance, as it may reflect production cutbacks, facility closures, or changes in reporting requirements.
Increases may reflect expanded production rather than degraded environmental practices.
Release quantities are self-reported estimates.
source_title: Tread Technologies Co - 5-Year Emissions Trend (2019–2023)

facts: Milliken & Company unveiled its seventh annual sustainability report on April 16, 2025.
The report showcases performance against the 2025 Sustainability Goals and 2030 net-zero commitments.
Halsey Cook, president and CEO of Milliken, stated that sustainability is at the core of Milliken’s business strategy and highlighted progress made with associates, customers, and partners.
Milliken successfully reduced lost-time incidents in 2024 by prioritizing essential safety measures.
The company achieved its target of 100,000 volunteer hours a year ahead of schedule, surpassing its initial 2025 volunteer goal by an additional 31,000 hours.
In 2024, Milliken conducted sustainability assessments on 100% of new products, an increase from a 22% baseline in 2018.
Milliken became the first textile manufacturer to provide non-PFAS materials for all three layers of firefighter turnout gear.
The company launched its N/XT Life™ program alongside a partnership with The Recycling Partnership’s Center for Sustainable Behavior and Impact to support circular economy principles.
Milliken exceeded its 2025 renewable energy target in 2022 and continues seeking solutions to enhance renewable energy usage to minimize emissions.
Ongoing efforts include thermal drying biosolids from wastewater treatment and removing coal ash from steam generation to reduce waste.
In 2024, Milliken optimized wastewater treatment processes and targeted various waste streams across its manufacturing sites, decreasing waste intensity compared to its 2018 baseline.
The company achieved a 42% reduction in scope 1 and 2 emissions from its 2018 baseline in 2024, approaching its 2030 goal of a 50% reduction.
Since 2018, Milliken has invested over $35 million in coal elimination initiatives, energy efficiency improvements, increased renewable electricity usage, and manufacturing modernization.
Efforts to reduce scope 3 emissions involve engaging suppliers, enhancing product sustainability data, and sourcing lower-carbon materials.
Milliken reaffirmed its dedication to the U.N. Global Compact, committing to uphold Ten Principles concerning human rights, labor standards, environmental protection, and anti-corruption.
Kasel Knight, SVP, Chief Legal Officer, and head of sustainability at Milliken, remarked that the report reflects progress and a commitment to driving positive change.
source_title: Milliken & Company Unveils Its 2024 Sustainability Report

facts: Kia America is recalling 427,407 Kia Telluride SUVs over concerns that a shaft misalignment could cause the vehicles to move while parked and cause a crash.
The recall covers all 2020-2023 Telluride SUVs and some 2024 models.
The intermediate shaft and right front driveshaft in each affected vehicle may not fully mate due to suspected improper assembly by the supplier.
Affected vehicles were manufactured between January 9, 2019 and October 19, 2023.
The recalled vehicles represent approximately 1% of all 2020 to 2024 Kia Telluride SUVs manufactured during the same period.
Kia identified the affected vehicles by examining production records from both Kia and its supplier.
The supplier responsible for the suspected improper assembly is MOBIS Alabama, LLC, based in West Point, Georgia.
Dealers will install the latest electronic parking brake (EPB) software to prevent movement of parked vehicles.
Dealers will also replace any damaged intermediate shafts found during the recall service.
Kia has asked vehicle owners to move the gear shifter to the ‘P’ (Park) position before exiting the vehicle and to always use the manual EPB until the recall is complete.
Kia has promised to refund vehicle owners who may have paid to resolve the issue independently.

The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) published a document stating that damaged shaft splines can cause the vehicle to move unintentionally while parked if the parking brake is not engaged.

Morgan Corp. maintains offices in Spartanburg, Charlotte, Savannah, and Raleigh to serve the entire Southeast region.
The company focuses on safety, people, and relationships, maintaining a competitively low incident rate, low EMR, and extensive work hours without a lost-time injury.

The company is recruiting for a Quality Control Inspector position responsible for monitoring and evaluating construction activities to ensure compliance with project specifications, industry standards, and quality requirements.

The Quality Control Inspector role involves conducting inspections, documenting findings, working with field teams to address deficiencies, reviewing project plans and specifications, identifying construction codes and regulatory requirements, and developing Project Specific Inspection and Test Plans (ITPs).
The position requires a minimum of 5 years of experience managing QA/QC efforts in Industrial Construction.
Candidates must possess a strong grasp of construction codes and standards such as ASME, ACI, NECA, ASTM, etc., along with the ability to research them to develop work plans.
The role requires willingness to travel, work outside in various environmental conditions or operating process facilities, and strong computer skills with Word and Excel.
Employees receive benefits including a 401K with company match, PTO, holidays, paid winter shutdown, professional development and career growth opportunities, and a cell phone allowance.

facts: Kinder Morgan has proposed the $3 billion South System Expansion 4 project.
The project aims to add 1.3 billion cubic feet per day of natural gas capacity across 279 miles in the Southeast.
The expansion is intended to enhance energy supply for homes, businesses, and manufacturing.
The project could be operational by 2028 or 2029, pending approval.
An open house event for the initiative was held at Bethel Missionary Baptist Church in Tuskegee.
The proposed route spans 279 miles, incorporating new pipelines alongside existing ones and enhancing compressor stations to maintain gas flow.
Alabama Power is a part of this project and has indicated a need for additional natural gas capacity for the next 20 to 30 years.
Allen Fore, Kinder Morgan’s vice president of public affairs, confirmed Alabama Power's involvement and needs.
Kinder Morgan oversees approximately 3,000 miles of pipeline infrastructure across Alabama.
The expansion will include parallel pipelines, known as loop lines, and upgrades to existing infrastructure.
Landowners impacted by the project will receive compensation, with no relocations required.

facts: Rivian has a planned electric vehicle factory located an hour east of Atlanta.
The Georgia state government and a local development authority are seeking more than $540,000 in legal fees.
These fees accumulated from two unsuccessful lawsuits filed by six Morgan County property owners who challenged the factory’s zoning.
State agencies say they are trying to recoup taxpayer-funded litigation costs for what they called frivolous lawsuits intended to delay construction.
The residents argue their lawsuits raised legitimate legal concerns and that the fee request is merely government intimidation.
Governments have a right to pursue litigation costs in Georgia when lawsuits are deemed frivolous, a distinction determined by a judge.
First Amendment advocates say this sum of money is unprecedented in a lawsuit that pits government agencies against citizens.

Richard T. Griffiths, a former CNN executive and Georgia First Amendment Foundation board member, said the state has never gone after attorneys fees for citizens’ First Amendment protected right to advocate in courts.
The agencies pursuing the litigation costs are the Georgia Department of Economic Development and the Joint Development Authority of Jasper, Morgan, Newton and Walton counties.
The JDA denied trying to stifle free speech and said it is pursuing legal fees from only two of seven lawsuits filed against the Rivian project involving the same pool of litigants.

The JDA board wrote in a Covington News op-ed that weaponizing the legal system to block a perfectly legal project is not legitimate and that the lawsuits were designed to delay progress, costing taxpayers and the community.
A Morgan County judge previously ruled that the state and JDA must pay their own legal fees, but the state is appealing.
source_title: Georgia government agencies seek $540K in legal fees from Rivian critics

facts: Murata Electronics North America is located in Smyrna, GA.
The company recently announced the addition of the PRF and NCP series to its temperature sensing thermistor line.
Both series were developed to meet design flexibility, reliability, standardization, and cost savings needs expected by engineers in the automotive market.
The PRF series consists of PTC (positive temperature coefficient) thermistors ideal for high accuracy overheat sensing.
The NCP series consists of NTC (negative temperature coefficient) thermistors created for temperature measurement with high accuracy over wide temperature ranges.
The PRF series offers quick and easy design for repeatable overheat sensing and provides resettable protection in the circuit because there is no hysteresis.
The NCP series offers the same resistance and B constant in several different package sizes that allows for convenient redesign and cost reduction.
Both series are surface mount series with operating temperatures in the range of -40°C to +125°C for commercial grade and up to +150°C for automotive grade.
Both series are suitable for lead-free soldering.
Both series meet ELV/UL and AEC-Q200 requirements.
Both series utilize a very sturdy construction that makes them resistant to mechanical vibrations and shock.

Mitch Nozaki, Product Manager, stated that as the inventor of ceramic thermistors, Murata continuously develops fine ceramic materials and firing processes to produce products meeting designers' needs.
Mitch Nozaki noted that Murata has provided the automotive design community with products needed to regulate detrimental temperature variances.
Mitch Nozaki stated that Murata has secured the most market share in providing these products.
source_title: Murata Unveils Latest Additions To Its Temperature Sensing Thermistor Line

facts: Since its founding in 1940, Murata has followed a strategy of organic growth using research and development to build capacitors, filters, resistors, microwave parts, and other components.
The prolonged industry downturn forced Murata to trim its workforce and lower capital investment.
Increasing OEM pressure has forced Murata to continue reducing commodity part prices.
Murata is leveraging core ceramic expertise to develop higher-margin products such as Bluetooth modules and higher-performance capacitors for emerging applications.
The company is increasing its presence in China.
John Denslinger, senior vice president of Murata Electronics North America Inc., Smyrna, Ga., stated: “Everything is changing in terms of what people do and where.”
Murata remains the world's leading manufacturer of multilayer ceramic capacitors (MLCCs), accounting for more than 20% of the global market based on revenue.
The company considers itself a market leader in SAW filters with a 20% share, and dielectric filters with a 40% share.
Murata remains vertically integrated, forming its own ceramic materials and building its own machines, eschewing outsourcing trends.
The company consistently reinvests 5% to 8% of its revenue in R&D.
Murata's last notable acquisition was in 1981.
For fiscal year 2001 ended March 31, 2002, Murata's revenue was $2.97 billion, down 32.4% from the previous year.
Net income for fiscal 2001 was $394 million, down 66% from the prior year.
Murata projects revenue and earnings for fiscal 2002 to be roughly flat with that of fiscal 2001.
Capital investment for fiscal 2002 is projected at $200 million, down from $260 million in fiscal 2001.
Denslinger expects Murata's revenue to slowly improve over the next year, citing price erosion in commodity parts as the main hurdle to recovery.
A lot of OEMs now negotiate price contracts on a quarterly rather than yearly basis.
Capacitors have experienced 12 consecutive quarters of price reductions.
Denslinger expects prices to erode 10% the first half of 2003.
Murata's largest market segment is communications products, but its percentage of revenue from this sector slid to 37% in fiscal 2001 from 41% in fiscal 2000.

Jiro Miyazaki, vice president of marketing for Murata Electronics North America, expects the company to supply EMC filters, thermistors, and DC/DC converters to OEMs building broadband, home networking, and digital access equipment.
Capacitors account for 40% of Murata's revenue.
The company is expanding offerings in capacitors with low-equivalent-series resistance as well as capacitance values exceeding 1 microfarad.
Murata hopes to target the RF market and applications in which tantalum capacitors now dominate.
Denslinger expects the focus on advanced technology to fend off low-cost Far East suppliers like Taiwan's Yageo and Korea's Samsung.
Murata is leveraging low-temperature co-fired ceramic technology to manufacture integrated Bluetooth modules.
Last week, Murata announced a new Bluetooth module measuring 9.3 x 7.8 x 1.8mm, which is 25% smaller than the previous version introduced in August.
The Bluetooth module is for wireless handset and PDA applications and sells for $15 to $20.
Scott Klettke, Murata's group product manager, said the higher price is justified since the module speeds time-to-market for designers of Bluetooth products.
Murata expects its percentage of revenue from China, 14% in fiscal 2001, to increase as more OEM and EMS customers locate there.
Last month, the company began mass production of capacitors, isolators, and thermistors at a plant in Wuxi.
Murata's other China plants are located in Beijing, Suzhou, and Shenzhen.
source_title: Murata banks on ceramic technology - EE Times

facts: Murata Electronics North America is located in Smyrna, GA.
The company announced the addition of the PRF and NCP series to its temperature sensing thermistor line.
Both series were developed to meet the design flexibility, reliability, standardization and cost savings needs expected by engineers in today's automotive market.
The PRF PTC (positive temperature coefficient) thermistors are ideal for high accuracy overheat sensing.
The NCP NTC (negative temperature coefficient) thermistors were created for temperature measurement with high accuracy over wide temperature ranges.
The PRF series offers a quick and easy design for repeatable overheat sensing and provides resettable protection in the circuit because there is no hysteresis.
The NCP series offers the same resistance and B constant in several different package sizes that allows for convenient redesign and cost reduction.
Both series are surface mount with operating temperatures in the range of -40°C to +125°C for commercial grade and up to +150°C for automotive grade.
Both series are suitable for lead free soldering.
Both series meet ELV/UL and AEC-Q200 requirements.
Both series utilize a very sturdy construction that make them resistant to mechanical vibrations and shock.

Mitch Nozaki, Product Manager at Murata Electronics North America, stated the company is continuously developing fine ceramic materials and firing processes to produce the best products that meet designers' needs of today and tomorrow.
Nozaki noted the company has provided the automotive design community with products needed to regulate detrimental temperature variances and secured the most market share in doing so.

facts: Top passive component suppliers are investing in new-product development and information technology to automate communications with their supply chain partners despite weak demand, continued price pressure, pockets of excess inventory, and increased competition from China.

Passives makers implemented cost-reduction initiatives including plant closings, factory consolidation, manufacturing shifts to low-cost-labor regions in China and Eastern Europe, layoffs, and reduced capital spending.

Glyndwr Smith, assistant to the chief executive and senior vice president at Vishay Intertechnology Inc., Malvern, Pa., stated cost reduction involves examining purchased materials through to component insertion, working with customers on logistics, vendor-managed inventory, global support, and introducing new technologies.
Inventory levels remain higher than needed for current consumption; some suppliers claim they bottomed out while others cite spot ordering trends and poor EMS channel visibility forcing excess stock.
Sandy Beck, vice president of sales and marketing at Kemet Corp., Greenville, S.C., noted inventory levels are not as high as before but excess remains.
A hand-to-mouth environment with four to eight week lead times makes recovery forecasting difficult for OEMs who have no need to queue up before placing production orders.
Delivery times for specialty products range from about eight to 10 weeks.
Capacitor and resistor prices have dropped steadily over the past 12 months, with some tags approaching manufacturing costs.
Pockets of growth exist in automotive, game consoles, cell phones, and laptop computers end markets, while telecommunications and Internet-related markets lag recovery by several months.

AVX Corp., Myrtle Beach, S.C., posted net sales of $294.9 million and net income of $1.3 million for its fiscal first quarter ended June 30; unit volume sales exceeded expectations as price pressure slowed with increased production capacity utilization.

Kemet reported $124 million in net sales (a 5% sequential increase) and $3.4 million in net earnings for the June quarter; unit shipments increased for three consecutive quarters while average selling prices declined approximately 5%.
Kemet plans to close plants in Greenwood, S.C., and Matamoros, Mexico, and lowered raw materials inventories by $11 million and finished goods stores by $7.9 million during the quarter.
Kemet reduced its workforce from a high of about 16,000 to 6,500 over the past two years.
Vishay expected aggregate unit shipments for its passives, semiconductors, and optoelectronic devices to rise 3% to 5% compared with the March quarter.
Vishay will drop capital expenditures, including R&D, to approximately $100 million from $160 million last year.
Taiwan-based Yageo Corp. is expected to cut its capital budget to just $30 million, down from $160 million in 2001.

iSuppli Corp., El Segundo, Calif., reported about 30% of the EMS industry's total production is manufactured in low-cost-labor centers including China, Asia, Eastern Europe, and Mexico; top-tier players aim to migrate more than 70% of total production to those regions by 2004.
The manufacturing shift to low-cost regions has caused declining spending in North America and Europe, leaving the United States as a center for design and new-product development.

Chinese passives makers are targeting U.S., Europe, and Japan markets; expected to achieve $1.6 billion in resistor and capacitor sales in 2003, capturing an 8.9% share of the global market per iSuppli.
Key Chinese players to watch include Chengdu, Fenghua, Man Yue, Nanyang, and Shenzhen, according to iSuppli analyst Shawn A. Wood.

Global suppliers retain a competitive edge because China's passives vendors lack advanced products, manufacturing tools, global support services, and brand recognition, though this may change as China gains logistical advantages for local OEMs and EMS providers.

John Denslinger, senior vice president of marketing at Murata Electronics North America Inc., Smyrna, Ga., stated the company is well-equipped to handle expansion in China with a global structure for sales, marketing, and supply chain to navigate customer moves.
Rohm Co. Ltd. shifted manufacturing to China and is making investments to increase production capacity.

AVX is entrenched in China via sales offices, warehouses, and parent company Kyocera Corp., Kyoto, Japan; it is establishing a facility in China that will come online in the third quarter to support multinational companies moving to the region.
Willie King, division vice president of product marketing at AVX, stated operations are moving to low-cost-labor centers while actively supporting design efforts in North America and Europe.

Kemet has no manufacturing presence in China but maintains distribution hubs in Asia including China; it expects a future Asian manufacturing presence to support existing customers moving to China and engage local customers.

Epcos A.G., Munich, Germany, is moving production to low-cost areas in both China and Eastern Europe, including Hungary and the Czech Republic, per Denny Salmang, president and chief executive of Epcos Inc., Iselin, N.J.
Leading passive component manufacturers share a strategy of developing new technology to bolster earnings even in down markets.
KOA Speer, Vishay, and Yageo utilize acquisition strategies; Vishay acquired Tansitor and Mallory last year to build its passives portfolio.
Vishay plans to roll out 200 new products this year, of which 60 will be passive devices.

Yageo acquired two passive component divisions from Royal Philips Electronics N.V. in 2000, marketed under Ferroxcube and Phycomp; about 8% of its sales are derived from North America, and it seeks to expand U.S. presence to guide customer designs.

KOA Corp. of Japan acquired specialty resistor manufacturer Tama Electric Co. Ltd. in 2001 and broadened its filtering product line to include metal oxide varistors, resettable fuses, three-pole capacitors, inductors, and ferrite beads to tap into emerging gigahertz designs.

Jeff Rice, vice president of sales and marketing at KOA Speer Electronics Inc., Bradford, Pa., noted the new product lines provide access to emerging gigahertz designs not available with commodity resistors.

Kemet differentiates itself with a full complement of surface-mount capacitor technologies including solid-state aluminum, ceramic, and tantalum dielectrics; it expanded high-frequency tantalum and high-capacitance ceramic products and generated significant revenue from new-product introductions while targeting the medical sector.
Yageo's new-product development focuses on lead-free components, a new line of miniaturized solid-state antennas, and X2Y attenuators primarily used for filtering in high-frequency applications.
Suppliers are expanding vendor-managed inventory programs and creating customer and supplier Web portals to facilitate business.
AVX, Kemet, and Vishay enable distributors to check inventory via the Web.

Taiyo Yuden (U.S.A.), Schaumburg, Ill., launched an inventory check function on its website in July for franchised distributor inventory information updated daily, allowing users to view quantities, update dates, download contacts, send RFQs, and place orders.
Suppliers offer online design support; Vishay provides an MLCC modeling program for capacitor/circuit behavior and a parametric search engine, while AVX and Kemet provide SPICE models and software.
Vishay supported its EMS customers with senior account managers to coordinate component requirements.

Murata Electronics North America's strategy focuses on design issues and strengthening engineering and product management as business shifts from North America and Eastern Europe to Asia; it also bolstered its EMS organization to address the production shift to China.

Kemet endorses systems for supply chain visibility and uses RosettaNet Partner Interface Process 3A1 with distributor TTI Inc., Fort Worth, Texas, for automated quoting to reduce cycle time and increase information accuracy.
source_title: 'Passive' but not inert - EE Times

facts: Murata Electronics North America announced the High Frequency (HF) Active Filter on December 13, 1999.
The HF Active Filter is a leaded surface mounted device for use in next-generation electronics products.
The component employs high-speed op-amps and advanced thick-film technology.
The HF Active Filter reduces the size of a passive filter solution by 50%.
The HF Active Filter is laser-tuned in an automated process.

facts: Murata Electronics North America is located in Smyrna, Ga.
The company announced its latest pyroelectric infrared (PIR) sensor as the world's smallest with a 5.0mm x 4.7mm x 2.4mm footprint.
The new sensor carries the global part number IRS-B210ST01-RI and is intended for use in compact, low profile electronic equipment and energy-saving devices.
According to Murata, the IRS-B210ST01-RI is the only surface mount PIR sensor available on the market today.
The sensor provides high sensitivity and signal to noise ratio, high stability against temperature changes and white light, and enhanced radio frequency interference (RFI) immunity.
The PIR sensor includes dual 0.85mm x 1.2mm electrodes with a field of vision of ±70° (horizontal) and ±50° (vertical).
Its operating temperature range is -40 to +70°C and the device is driven by supply voltage in the range of 2V to 15V.
The sample price for the sensor is $1.88.
The lead-time for availability is approximately 12 weeks.
source_title: PIR sensor touts tiny footprint - EDN

facts: Murata Electronics North America Inc. is located in Smyrna, Ga.
The company developed a Bluetooth Module using Low Temperature Co-Fired Ceramics technology.
The module integrates both active devices (ICs) and passive components onto a single surface-mount ceramic chip that supports Universal Serial Bus, UART and pulse-code modulation interfaces.
Applications for the module include cellular phones, PDAs, notebook and desktop PCs, wireless printers, mouses and keyboards.
The module measures 14.5 x 11.9 x 2.3 mm.
Murata integrated the passives and matching circuits within the substrate of the chip, making it both a carrier of the IC and a functional device.
Scott Klettke serves as Murata's marketing-segment manager.
Sourcing and inventory costs can be reduced because the all-in-one device eliminates the line item cost of managing several part numbers on a bill of materials.
Approximately 60 components are embedded in the module.
The module includes a radio-frequency/baseband IC that is mounted in a cavity on the bottom surface of the substrate.
Murata mounts a power amplifier to increase the output power and a low-noise amplifier to improve sensitivity on the surface of the substrate.
Several parts and functions are integrated within the substrate, such as a bandpass filter, a balun and other passives.
The module has achieved Logo Certification of Version 1.0b plus CE.
The module will acquire version 1.1 certification shortly.
The module is available for sampling at $100 each.
source_title: All-in-one Bluetooth module - EDN

facts: US-based Novelis is an aluminum rolling company with headquarters in Atlanta.
The company has operations on four continents, including 25 facilities in 11 countries.

Novelis is currently a subsidiary of Hindalco Industries Limited, which in turn is the flagship company of the Aditya Birla Group.
In Brazil, Novelis operates two aluminum rolled product facilities in Santo André and Pindamonhangaba, which is also the largest aluminum rolling and recycling facility in the region.
In October 2014, Novelis announced the closure of its Ouro Preto primary aluminum smelter in Brazil to focus operations on its core business of aluminum rolling and recycling.

Novelis released its second Green Bond report on April 21, 2023.
The report highlights sustainability-focused investments funded by a €500 million Euro Green Bond issued in March 2021.
Investments aim to increase aluminum recycling capacity and reduce primary aluminum use across the U.S., Brazil, South Korea, the United Kingdom, and Germany.
These investments are expected to reduce carbon emissions by more than three million tons annually once fully operational.
Featured projects include the construction of a $365 million Automotive Recycling Center in Guthrie, Kentucky.
Featured projects include a new recycling plant in Bay Minette, Alabama.
Featured projects include a $1.2 million investment to install nearly 1,000 photovoltaic solar panels across 14 UBC collection centers in Brazil.
Steve Fisher serves as President & Chief Executive Officer of Novelis Inc.

Steve Fisher stated that the projects demonstrate Novelis's purpose of 'Shaping a Sustainable World Together' by funding investments that lower manufacturing carbon emissions and increase recycled content in products to industry-leading levels.

facts: On 12 January, 2022, Novelis Inc. announced it will invest $365 million to build a highly advanced recycling centre for automotive in North America.
The new facility will have an annual casting capacity of 240 kt of sheet ingot.
The facility is expected to reduce the company's carbon emissions by more than one million tonnes each year.
The new recycling facility will be built adjacent to Novelis' existing automotive finishing plant in Guthrie, Kentucky.
The centre will enable Novelis to grow its closed-loop-recycling programs with more automotive customers in North America.
Through closed-loop recycling, the company takes back the aluminum remaining after automotive parts are stamped from sheets and remakes it into the same product for new vehicle production.
The centre will also have the capability to process aluminum from vehicles at the end of their lifecycle.
Using recycled aluminum as input material requires only 5 per cent of the energy used to make primary aluminum, thus avoiding 95 per cent of the carbon emissions associated with production.

Steve Fisher, President and CEO of Novelis, Inc., stated that Novelis aims to be the world's leading provider of low-carbon, sustainable aluminum solutions that advance business, industry and society toward the benefits of a circular economy.
Steve Fisher noted that through this investment, the company will continue to increase the amount of recycled content in its products, reducing CO2 emissions and moving closer to carbon neutrality.
The new recycling centre will be equipped with industry-leading processes and capabilities, including advanced shredding and sorting technology, as well as energy-efficient innovations.
Novelis has a sustainability goal to reduce energy intensity by 10 per cent by 2026.
Groundbreaking for the new facility is scheduled for early 2022, with commissioning expected in 2024.
When fully operational, the expansion will add approximately 140 new jobs in Guthrie.
Novelis held the grand opening of its automotive finishing plant in Guthrie in October, which currently employs 150 people and will grow to 190 over the next two years.

Tom Boney, Executive Vice President and President of Novelis North America, stated that the investment will help Novelis achieve sustainability goals and support the carbon-reduction targets of automotive customers.
Tom Boney noted that the Commonwealth of Kentucky has been a great business partner with a strong, highly skilled workforce to meet the growing need for high-strength, low-carbon aluminum.

Kentucky Gov. Andy Beshear stated that Novelis is committed to creating quality job opportunities for Kentucky families and praised the company's leadership decision to quickly move forward with expansion plans.
Gov. Beshear noted that Novelis is proving to be a great fit for Todd County and the surrounding area, and expressed eagerness to see the company continue to grow for years to come.
Novelis has a 37-year history of creating jobs in Kentucky and investing in local communities, currently employing 1,600 people in the state.
In addition to the Guthrie automotive finishing facility, Novelis operates an aluminum beverage can recycling plant in Berea and the Logan Aluminum joint venture in Russellville.
Novelis Inc. is a global leader in the production of innovative aluminum products and solutions and the world's largest recycler of aluminum.

Novelis aims to be the leading provider of low-carbon, sustainable aluminum solutions and achieve a fully circular economy by partnering with suppliers and customers in aerospace, automotive, beverage can and specialties industries throughout North America, Europe, Asia and South America.
Novelis had net sales of $12.3 billion in fiscal year 2021.

Novelis is a subsidiary of Hindalco Industries Limited, an industry leader in aluminum and copper, and the metals flagship company of the Aditya Birla Group, a multinational conglomerate based in Mumbai.

The press release includes forward-looking statements regarding plans to invest approximately $365 million, expectations to reduce carbon emissions by more than one million tons each year, and the creation of 140 new jobs at Guthrie.

Novelis cautions that forward-looking statements involve risk and uncertainty and references important risk factors in its Form 10-K filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission for the fiscal year ended March 31, 2021.
source_title: Novelis to build $365 million recycling centre to support North American Automotive customers and reduce carbon emissions by more than one million tonnes a year

facts: On Wednesday, September 8, Novelis Inc., the world leader in aluminum rolling and recycling, declared that it expanded its research partnership with the Georgia Institute of Technology (Georgia Tech) Novelis Innovation Hub.

The expansion of partnership will work on accelerating the pace of innovation via high throughput development methodologies and artificial intelligence and developing new generations of electric vehicle batteries using the higher amount of recycled aluminum.

Philippe Meyer, Senior Vice President and Chief Technology Officer of Novelis Inc., said: "Our collaboration provides an exciting opportunity to accelerate our R&D and to boost the performance of EV batteries (energy density) and recyclability, while lowering costs for automakers and battery manufacturers."
Meyer added that they want to go beyond and partner closely with Georgia Tech to help transform their industry to reach committed objectives toward sustainability.

The renewed partnership will also carry on with technical research, while initiating new strategic programmes including circular economy solutions or de-carbonization of the industrial footprint, promoting entrepreneurship, and incubation of new ventures.
This will further accelerate commercialisation of new products.

Raj Gopalaswamy, Global Technology Director of New Domains, Novelis Inc., said: "To keep advancing the aluminum industry toward the circular economy, we must increase the pace of innovation and develop new solutions that demonstrate aluminum's superior sustainability benefits."

Gopalaswamy added: "Through research partnerships with world-leading institutions like Georgia Tech, we can fulfill the growing needs for aluminum applications that help our customers meet their sustainability goals faster and more efficiently."

Shreyes Melkote, Executive Director of the Novelis Innovation Hub, Georgia Tech, said: "Collaboration between academia and industry will help speed the development of transformative solutions to meet the sustainability challenges we face today."

Melkote further said: "We look forward to expanding our partnership with Novelis to address its critical scientific, technological and workforce needs by leveraging the wide-ranging expertise and talent of Georgia Tech faculty and students in sustainable technologies, advanced materials and manufacturing, artificial intelligence, data sciences and robotics."

According to Julia Kubanek, Vice President for Interdisciplinary Research at Georgia Tech, Novelis is a critical partner for Georgia Tech that can together accelerate product innovation across multiple sectors.

Kubanek stated: "As an Institute, we remain committed to our vision to foster world-class partnerships as we collaborate with Novelis. Our mutual interests in sustainability, clean energy and creative design of materials make this research partnership especially exciting for us."
source_title: Novelis expands research partnership with Georgia Tech aiming at enhancing innovations using recycled aluminium; Aluminium Extrusion, Profiles, Price, Scrap, Recycling, Section

facts: Novelis Inc. is the world’s largest manufacturer of rolled aluminum and the global leader in aluminum recycling.
Novelis has U.S. manufacturing plants in Georgia, Indiana, Kentucky, New York, Ohio, and West Virginia.
Novelis VP and Chief Sustainability Officer John Gardner stated that delivering technologically advanced, low-carbon solutions is a source of competitive advantage for Novelis and its customers.
Novelis supports the principles of the EPA Clean Power Plan as an important step in providing greater energy choices and helping the U.S. achieve needed emissions reductions.
223 companies announced support for the EPA’s proposed carbon standard for electric power plants via a letter to the EPA, Obama Administration, and congressional leaders.
The letter was coordinated by the nonprofit sustainability advocacy organization Ceres.
Signatory companies include industry giants such as IKEA, Mars Inc., VF Corporation, Nestlé, JLL, and Levi Strauss & Co.

Dan Probst, Chairman of Energy and Sustainability Services at JLL, stated that achieving proposed carbon reductions through energy efficiency retrofits will reduce greenhouse gas emissions, lower costs for power companies and consumers, and help drive the economy by creating jobs.

Tim Brown, President and CEO of Nestlé Waters North America, commented that Nestlé places great importance on environmental responsibility and focuses on efficiency and renewable energy incorporation aligned with the Clean Power Plan.
IKEA has set a goal to use 100 percent renewable energy for its global facilities by 2020.
IKEA recently announced the purchase of a 165-megawatt Texas wind farm, boosting its total wind production capacity to nearly 1,000 gigawatts of electricity per year.
This IKEA wind capacity is equivalent to the annual electricity consumption of about 90,000 American households.
JLL manages more than 3 billion square feet of building space globally.
JLL has helped commercial real estate clients reduce greenhouse gas emissions by 12 million metric tons while saving them $2.5 billion in energy costs over the past seven years.

The EPA Clean Power Plan is the nation’s first comprehensive effort to reduce carbon pollution from existing electric power plants, which are the single largest source of global warming pollution in the U.S.
EPA estimates the plan will reduce carbon emissions from power plants by 30 percent by 2030, from a 2005 baseline.
The public comment period for the proposed standard closed on December 1, 2014.
EPA is expected to announce a final rule in June 2015.
More than 270 companies and investors have expressed support for the EPA Clean Power Plan.
In June 2014, 49 investors managing $800 billion in assets sent letters of support to the Obama Administration and congressional leaders.
The sign-on letter was open to energy users only, though some large electric power utilities also support the rule.

Tom King, US President of National Grid, stated that National Grid supports EPA’s efforts to reduce emissions through sensible regulation and looks forward to working with the Obama Administration and EPA Administrator Gina McCarthy to reduce emissions from the energy sector.
Ceres directs the Investor Network on Climate Risk (INCR), a network of over 100 institutional investors with collective assets totaling more than $13 trillion.
Ceres also directs Business for Innovative Climate and Energy Policy (BICEP), an advocacy coalition of nearly 30 businesses committed to working with policymakers on energy and climate legislation.
Burton President Donna Carpenter stated that the company backs the EPA’s Clean Power Plan due to experiencing climate change effects personally.

Letitia Webster, director of global sustainability at VF Corporation (a North Carolina-based apparel company), stated that the EPA clean power plan provides long-term certainty for VF to continue investing in clean energy solutions.

Nicole Peill-Moelter, Senior Director of Environmental Sustainability at Akamai Technologies, stated that Akamai is committed to driving energy efficiency and decarbonizing electricity consumption, viewing EPA guidelines as a good start to accelerating industry-wide innovation for clean energy.

Andrew Lemley, Government Affairs Representative with New Belgium Brewing, stated the company has committed to protecting its future by saving energy and investing in renewable sources, pleased to see U.S. steps toward a clean energy economy.

Amy Davidsen, Executive Director of The Climate Group North America, stated that power plants account for nearly 40 percent of US carbon emissions and commended the Administration's step to make them cleaner and safer.

Lance Pierce, President of CDP North America, stated that companies supporting the plan are successful, competitive, bottom-line oriented firms that have analyzed why supporting low-carbon power makes good sense for their business and consumers.

facts: Novelis Inc. is a leading sustainable aluminium solutions provider and the world leader in aluminium rolling and recycling.
Novelis successfully tested using hydrogen fuel to power a recycling furnace at its UK plant in Latchford, Warrington.
The tests were carried out as part of a UK government decarbonization programme and in collaboration with Progressive Energy, an independent UK energy company.
The project required the installation of new burners, regenerators, and furnace lining material.
Using hydrogen instead of the same amount of natural gas when operating a melting furnace can reduce CO2e emissions by up to 90%.

Emilio Braghi, Executive Vice President of Novelis Inc. and President of Novelis Europe, stated that exploring renewable energy sources like hydrogen, making first mover investments, and reducing energy intensity are part of the company's 3×30 vision to advance aluminium as the material of choice with circular solutions.
The expansion of local recycling capacity at the Latchford site is transforming it into a prototype for high-recycled content and decarbonized aluminium production.
Safety was the top priority during the hydrogen pilot project, with several series of tests conducted by blending different percentages of hydrogen with natural gas ranging from 30% to 100%.
The trial campaign involved remelting and casting several hundred tonnes of 3000 series scrap aluminium alloy into sheet ingots.
All relevant parameters were measured during the trial to assess any impact on the product, process, operating environment, and environmental emissions.

Further downstream processing, including rolling and finishing, will be completed at other Novelis plants in Europe to establish the real end-to-end parameters of a hydrogen-based recycled alloy production process.
A report detailing the full post-trial evaluation and assessments will be released as part of the UK government’s Industrial Fuel Switching programme later this year.

Allan Sweeney, Plant Manager at Novelis Latchford, noted that the use of hydrogen is not common in the aluminium industry today and expressed pride in being one of the pioneers to test this new fuel at an industrial scale in a real-world environment.
The results from the Latchford trial will drive further research into the potential deployment of hydrogen in Novelis' recycling operations worldwide.
The demonstration project is part of the UK government’s Industrial Fuel Switching Competition programme and is supported with a grant of £4.6 million.
The grant funding comes as part of the £1 billion Net Zero Innovation Portfolio and the wider regional HyNet project.
The programme is designed to support industry in decarbonising operations through a switch from natural gas to low carbon hydrogen.

HyNet aims to decarbonise industry across the North West and North Wales through the production, transportation, and storage of low-carbon hydrogen, and by capturing industry’s carbon dioxide emissions through Carbon Capture Solutions.
Novelis has been a partner in the HyNet project since 2017 and is supporting the development of the regional infrastructure project.
Novelis is conducting its own technical feasibility studies into using hydrogen as a direct replacement for natural gas.
Novelis research and development teams worldwide continue to investigate the possibility of using plasma and electricity to supply energy to its production plants.
In July 2024, Novelis announced an investment of approximately $90 million to double the recycling capacity for used beverage cans (UBCs) at its Latchford plant.
The UBC recycling expansion project will increase the facility’s capacity by 85 kilotonnes per year and decrease Novelis Europe’s CO2e by more than 350,000 tonnes annually.
The Latchford UBC recycling expansion project is expected to begin commissioning in December 2026.
As part of its 3×30 vision, Novelis has set sustainability targets to achieve by the end of 2030, including increasing recycled content to 75% from today’s 63%.
Another 2030 target is reducing carbon emissions to less than 3 tonnes CO2e per tonne of aluminium shipped.
Novelis aims to continue leading the industry to circularity through first-mover investments and achieve carbon neutrality by 2050 or sooner.
Novelis is a global leader in the production of innovative aluminum products and solutions, and the world’s largest recycler of aluminum.
The company partners with suppliers and customers in the aerospace, automotive, beverage can, and specialties industries throughout North America, Europe, Asia, and South America.
Novelis reported net sales of $16.2 billion in fiscal year 2024.

Novelis is a subsidiary of Hindalco Industries Limited, an industry leader in aluminum and copper, and the metals flagship company of the Aditya Birla Group, a multinational conglomerate based in Mumbai.
Novelis Latchford in Warrington, UK, is one of Europe’s largest aluminium used beverage cans recycling plants.
The Latchford facility is Europe’s largest closed-loop recycling operation for automotive aluminium rolled products with an annual recycling capacity of up to 195,000 tonnes.
The plant has enough capacity to recycle every aluminium beverage can sold in the UK.
Novelis collaborated with Jaguar Land Rover (JLR) to create the first closed-loop system in Europe for automotive aluminium rolled products.
Novelis operates across borders in well-established and efficient supply chains due to the high level of economic integration and geographical proximity between the EU, UK, and Switzerland.

This interconnectedness creates important economies of scale that benefit value chains across Europe and enables Novelis to maximize the use of recycled inputs for low-carbon, sustainable aluminum solutions.

facts: Novelis North America is headquartered in Atlanta, Georgia.
Novelis North America operates eight aluminum rolled products facilities, including two fully dedicated recycling facilities and one facility with recycling operations.

Novelis manufactures a broad range of aluminum sheet and light gauge products for end-use markets that include beverage and food cans, containers and packaging, automotive and other transportation applications, architectural and other industrial applications.
The majority of Novelis North America’s volumes are currently directed toward the beverage can sheet market.
Three facilities in North America re-melt post-consumer aluminum and recycled process material as part of the manufacturing process.
Most recycled material comes from used beverage cans (UBCs) and automotive scrap, and is cast into sheet ingot at plants in Greensboro, Georgia; Berea, Kentucky; and Oswego, New York.

The Logan Aluminum joint venture facility in Russellville, Kentucky manufactures aluminum sheet products for the can stock market and operates modern and high-speed equipment for ingot casting, hot-rolling, cold-rolling, and finishing.

Novelis expanded its Oswego, New York facility by constructing three automotive finishing lines and supporting automotive scrap recycling capabilities in response to the lightweighting trend in the automotive industry.
Novelis Europe operates ten aluminum rolled product facilities, including two fully dedicated recycling facilities and two facilities with recycling operations.

Novelis Europe manufactures sheet and foil products for end-use markets including beverage and food can, automotive, architectural and industrial products, foil and technical products, and lithographic sheet.
Beverage and food can represent the largest end-use market in terms of shipment volume for Novelis Europe.
Novelis holds a 50% joint venture interest in Aluminium Norf GmbH (Alunorf), which is the world’s largest aluminum rolling and remelt facility.
Alunorf supplies high quality can stock, foilstock and feeder stock for finishing at Novelis's other European operations.
Novelis built a fully integrated recycling facility at its Nachterstedt, Germany plant that commissioned in fiscal 2015 and is the largest aluminum recycling facility in the world.
A second automotive finishing line at the Nachterstedt, Germany facility was commissioned in fiscal 2016 to expand production of aluminum automotive sheet products in Europe.
Novelis Asia operates five aluminum rolled product facilities, including three facilities with recycling operations.
End-use markets for Novelis Asia include beverage and food cans, electronics, architectural, automotive, foil, industrial and other products.
Beverage can represents the largest end-use market in terms of volume for Novelis Asia.
Novelis has recycling facilities at its Ulsan and Yeongju, South Korea plants.
Novelis operates a facility in Binh Duong, Vietnam that handles the collection and processing of used beverage cans (UBCs).
Novelis expanded its aluminum rolling operations beginning in fiscal 2014 and its recycling operations in fiscal 2013 in South Korea to bring high-quality aluminum rolling capacity to market.
The South Korea expansion includes a state-of-the-art recycling center primarily for UBCs and a casting operation.
Novelis South America operates two aluminum rolled product facilities, including one facility with recycling operations.
Main markets for Novelis South America include beverage and food can, specialty, industrial, foil and other packaging and transportation end-use applications.
source_title: Novelis Inc (NVLCN) Quarterly and Annual Segment Results by Country and Region - CSIMarket

facts: Novelis is the world's leading provider of low-carbon, recycled aluminum.
Aluminum-intensive body-in-white structures weigh up to 45% less than steel-intensive vehicles, enabling automakers to improve performance and increase payload capacity.
Reduced vehicle weight using aluminum extends battery range for electric vehicles (EVs), which is a critical factor for consumer adoption.
Lighter vehicles require fewer batteries and smaller engines, suspension systems, and brakes, translating to additional weight and cost savings for automakers.
For internal combustion engine vehicles, lighter vehicles produce fewer emissions and require less fuel during operation.

For EVs, using fewer batteries or smaller battery packs reduces the vehicle's carbon footprint by consuming less energy while driving, producing less CO2 from electricity generation, and lessening the need for resource-intensive mining of critical minerals.
Aluminum's infinite recyclability creates opportunities for a more circular economy through the recovery of aluminum in body and battery enclosure systems at the end of the life of the vehicle.
Reusing recovered aluminum scrap significantly reduces the need for primary aluminum production, leading to substantial environmental benefits.
Recycling aluminum consumes only about 5% of the energy required for primary production, resulting in up to a 95% reduction in energy usage.
The aluminum recycling process also achieves a similar percentage reduction in greenhouse gas emissions.
Aluminum's versatility supports modular design and manufacturing efficiencies, helping automakers streamline production across vehicle segments.
Using aluminum results in fewer total parts, reduced robots, tools, and joints, improving assembly efficiency and lowering factory emissions.
Using aluminum reduces the complexity of material grades compared to steel while achieving similar functionality.
These efficiencies reduce downstream supply chain requirements, simplify manufacturing complexity, and create value for automakers.
High-strength aluminum alloys deliver exceptional crash performance, protecting occupants while still allowing for vehicle weight reduction.
Aluminum demonstrates better energy absorption, allowing vehicle architectures to be engineered for improved occupant protection and high marks in 5-star crash safety testing.
Aluminum's lightweight properties enhance vehicle dynamics, improving handling, braking, and acceleration.
Novelis positions itself as a partner to automakers with unmatched expertise, innovative solutions, and commitment to sustainability.
The content is a blog post authored by Daniel Kern, Vice President, Global Automotive Development at Novelis.
source_title: Lightweighting for a Sustainable Future: Unlocking the Aluminum Advantage for Automakers :: Novelis Inc.

facts: Novelis Inc., an aluminum products maker owned by Hindalco Industries Ltd., is seeking to complete a planned US initial public offering as soon as early June.

The company may target a valuation of about $18 billion.
Details of the offering, including timing, could change.
A representative for Novelis declined to comment on the plans.
Novelis is working with Morgan Stanley, Bank of America Corp., and Citigroup Inc. on the potential New York Stock Exchange listing.
The stock will be offered by a Hindalco subsidiary.
Novelis will not receive any proceeds from the sale.

facts: Governor Kemp’s office announced on Thursday morning, June 22, that auto parts manufacturer NVH Korea will be locating in Locust Grove.
The company produces battery parts for electric vehicles.
The announcement represents Henry County’s first addition in EV manufacturing.
Electric vehicle production is a growing sector of new manufacturing in Georgia.
Multiple new vehicle plants are coming to the state.
NVH Korea will invest $72 million dollars and create over 160 new jobs in Locust Grove.
They will be located at Gardner Logistics Park off Colvin Drive.
The facility expects to begin operations in the second quarter of 2024.
The new facility will supply battery parts for electric vehicles in the United States for manufacturers such as KIA and Hyundai Motor Group.
The facility will produce the EV battery system components that protect, connect and sense the performance of battery cells.
NVH Korea is an automotive parts manufacturer based in the City of Ulsan and established in 1984.
The company specializes in automotive noise, vibration, and heat-control parts.
It has current production facilities in Korea, Europe and India.
No stranger to the Peach State, the company operates a manufacturing facility in Columbus that produces floor mats, cargo mats and cargo trays.

“We are pleased to announce that we have chosen the State of Georgia as our company’s first EV battery component manufacturing site. Cooperation between state agencies and our company staff has helped guide us throughout the project,” said Ja Kyum Koo, Chairman and CEO of NVH Korea.
“It is the first step of our company’s effort to contribute to the electrification of the mobility industry, and we look forward to joining the community of Locust Grove and Henry County.”
The announcement by NVH Korea is the latest in a series of electric vehicle manufacturing coming to Georgia.
Since 2020, the state has attracted more than $22.7 billion in investments and the creation of over 28,400 new jobs in the EV sector.
Headlining the announcements are two new vehicle plants — Rivian near Social Circle and Hyundai near Savannah.
Each represents several billion dollars in investment and thousands of jobs.
Several new battery plants are also building in various parts of the state.
source_title: Auto Parts Manufacturer NVH Korea to Locate in Locust Grove

facts: South Korea's foreign minister is considering a trip to the U.S. to meet with the Trump administration after hundreds of South Korean nationals were arrested in Georgia at an electric vehicle battery plant.
Foreign Minister Cho Hyun expressed deep concern and a heavy sense of responsibility over the arrests of South Korean nationals during an emergency meeting in Seoul.

South Korean President Lee Jae Myung called for all-out efforts to swiftly resolve the matter, stressing that the rights and interests of South Korean nationals and the business operations of South Korean companies investing in the U.S. must not be infringed upon.

U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents arrested 475 people, most of them South Korean nationals, in a raid Thursday at an electric vehicle battery plant under construction in Ellabell, Ga., near Savannah.
The plant is a joint venture between Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution.
On Sunday, Presidential chief of staff Kang Hoon-sik said that South Korea and the U.S. had finalized negotiations on the workers' release.
South Korea plans to send a charter plane to bring more than 300 workers home as soon as remaining administrative steps are completed.

ICE released video of what officials say is the largest single-site enforcement operation in the history of the Homeland Security Investigations, showing officers shackling workers and loading them onto Georgia prison buses.
Steven Schrank, special agent in charge of homeland security investigations for Georgia and Alabama, stated the operation underscores a commitment to protecting jobs for Georgians and Americans.
Schrank said the operation was not simply rounding up people but the result of a months-long investigation.
According to Schrank, the workers were either illegally present in the U.S. or working unlawfully.
The arrests add to tension with a key ally at a time when President Trump has been pushing for trade deals and more South Korean investment in the U.S.
Hyundai also has an auto plant in Montgomery, Ala., and has announced plans to invest more than $5 billion in a steel plant in Louisiana.
President Trump said when asked about the raid at the White House on Friday: "I would say that they were illegal aliens, and ICE was just doing its job."

Democratic U.S. Sen. Raphael Warnock of Georgia says the raid raises questions about both the president's immigration strategy and how the firms were able to allegedly employ workers without proper documentation.
Warnock stated: "Companies should not be able to get rich in Georgia by exploiting immigrant workers and putting them at risk of politically motivated raids and violations of due process."
Hyundai Motor Co. says it's working to understand the specific circumstances of the arrests.
Hyundai Motor Co. stated that based on their current understanding, none of those detained is directly employed by Hyundai Motor Company.
Hyundai Motor Co. promised an internal investigation into employment compliance and stated it has zero tolerance for those who don't follow the law.
LG Energy Solution suspended all business trips to the U.S. and told workers visiting the U.S. to stay in their accommodations or return to South Korea immediately.
LG Energy Solution's chief human resources officer is heading to the U.S. to address the issues on site.
LG Energy Solution stated it will make every effort to promptly resolve the situation, including ensuring the speedy and safe return of all employees.
Georgia officials have touted the Hyundai Metaplant America site as a game changer for the state.
Republican Gov. Brian Kemp has said the plant has provided "historic benefits" and new opportunities for workers.

A spokesperson for Gov. Kemp's office stated that Georgia will always enforce the law, including all state and federal immigration laws, and noted that the state Department of Public Safety coordinated with ICE to support the operation.
The Georgia National Guard is providing administrative support to ICE.

Republican U.S. Senate candidate and Congressman Buddy Carter, whose district includes the plant site in Ellabell, Ga., applauded the Trump Administration and law enforcement officers for taking bold action to put American workers first.
source_title: South Korea charters plane to fly home over 300 workers detained by ICE at Georgia Hyundai plant : NPR

Ja Kyum Koo, Chairman and CEO of NVH Korea, stated that Georgia is the company's first EV battery component manufacturing site.
Ja Kyum Koo noted that cooperation between state agencies and company staff helped guide the project.
The new facility will be located at Gardner Logistics Park on Colvin Drive in Locust Grove.
The facility will produce EV battery system components that protect, connect, and sense the performance of battery cells.
Operations are expected to begin in the second quarter of 2024.
Mayor Robert Price of the City of Locust Grove stated that NVH's investment will increase the manufacturing jobs base in Locust Grove.
Carlotta Harrell, Chair of the Henry County Board of Commissioners, stated that the announcement will add quality manufacturing jobs for Henry County citizens.
Project Manager Jacob Lee represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
Partners include the Henry County Development Authority, Georgia EMC, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that NVH Korea is establishing its first U.S. or e-mobility manufacturing presence.
From July 1 through December 31, 2022, the automotive industry was the second-largest source of new jobs and investment in Georgia.
Since 2020, Georgia has pursued job creation along the entire EV supply chain, resulting in more than $22.7 billion in investments and the creation of over 28,400 jobs.
source_title: EV Parts Supplier NVH Korea to Create Over 160 Jobs, Invest $72M in Henry County - AllOnGeorgia

facts: A South Korean company will open a factory in suburban Atlanta to make parts for electric batteries.
NVH Korea plans to invest $72 million in a plant located in Locust Grove, south of Atlanta.
The new plant will hire 140 workers.
The facility will manufacture sensors, connections, and protectors for electric vehicle batteries.
Operations at the new plant are scheduled to begin in 2024.
Chairman and CEO Ja Kyum Koo stated that this marks NVH Korea’s first venture into manufacturing components for electric batteries.
Based in Ulsan, South Korea, NVH Korea is the 10th supplier to locate in the region.
Hyundai announced in 2022 that it would build a $5.5 billion plant to assemble electric vehicles and batteries in Ellabell, Georgia, near Savannah.
The Hyundai plant site could eventually grow to employ 8,100 workers.
The Hyundai plant is slated to begin producing vehicles in 2025.
Suppliers for the region's EV projects have pledged to invest nearly $2.1 billion.
Suppliers for the region's EV projects have pledged to hire nearly 4,900 people.
This facility will be NVH Korea’s second plant in Georgia.

NVH Korea’s subsidiary, AFS America, is located in Columbus and manufactures floor mats and cargo trays for the Hyundai plant in Montgomery, Alabama, the Kia plant in West Point, Georgia, and General Motors.
NVH Korea operates 19 plants worldwide across South Korea, North America, China, India, Europe, and Russia.
NVH Korea manufactures sound-dampening parts and materials, floor mats, headliners, insulators, and components for managing engine and cooling system temperatures.
The state of Georgia will fund the training of NVH Korea’s workers at the new facility.
The company could qualify for $1.2 million in state income tax credits, calculated at $1,750 per job over five years, provided workers earn at least $31,300 annually.
The company could qualify for additional incentives, including property tax breaks from the city of Locust Grove and Henry County.
source_title: South Korean firm plans $72M Georgia plant to make battery parts, hiring 140 – WSB-TV Channel 2 - Atlanta

facts: Apollo Funds announced a strategic partnership with Panasonic Automotive Systems to leverage technological advancements and boost growth opportunities.
The alliance aims to accelerate the growth trajectory of Panasonic Automotive while ensuring Panasonic Holdings Corporation maintains a significant minority stake in Panasonic Automotive.
Specific details regarding the terms and financial aspects of the agreement have not been disclosed publicly.

Panasonic Automotive Systems is recognized as a leading global supplier of advanced automotive technologies, including infotainment systems, electrification solutions, and connected vehicle platforms.
Panasonic Automotive's products play a pivotal role in the industry’s transition towards electric and autonomous vehicles.
Through the strategic partnership, Panasonic Automotive aims to tap into Apollo Funds' extensive network and financial capabilities to drive innovation and bolster its market position.
Apollo Funds is a prominent investment manager focused on driving growth and value creation across various sectors.
The collaboration strengthens Apollo Funds’ position in the automotive industry as it seeks to leverage emerging trends and capitalize on the transition towards electric and autonomous vehicles.

The synergy between Apollo Funds and Panasonic Automotive Systems has the potential to revolutionize the automotive sector by combining cutting-edge technologies, capital resources, and industry expertise.

The partnership could accelerate the development and deployment of advanced in-vehicle technologies, enhance the driving experience, and contribute to broader sustainability and electrification goals.
