facts: Denso Corporation (TSE: 6902) is the world's second-largest automotive components supplier by revenue and Toyota's primary auto parts supplier.

Originally spun off from Toyota Motor in 1949, Denso supplies thermal management systems, powertrain control systems, safety components, ADAS sensors, and vehicle electrification hardware to Toyota, Lexus, Mazda, Honda, and over 200 other automakers worldwide.
Toyota Group entities collectively own approximately 24% of Denso, cementing its keiretsu anchor role within Japan's industrial group.
Denso has committed to deploying over 50% of its capital expenditure and R&D investment toward electrification-related businesses through 2025.

Electrification investments span electric motor systems for EVs and hybrids, high-voltage power electronics (inverters, converters), battery thermal management, on-board charger systems, and hydrogen fuel cell components in collaboration with Toyota.
Denso co-founded MIRISE Technologies with Toyota to design proprietary power semiconductor chips for EVs, reducing dependence on external suppliers like Rohm and Infineon.
In-house automotive-grade power semiconductor development creates cost advantages and supply chain security for the Toyota Group ecosystem amid tight global supply.
Denso generates revenues of approximately ¥7 trillion, ranking #2 globally among auto parts suppliers behind Germany's Bosch.

Approximately 80% of Denso's revenues are generated outside Japan, with major exposure to North American operations and natural hedging through local manufacturing in the US and Europe following customer plants.

Denso has set an aggressive internal carbon neutrality goal for its manufacturing operations by 2035, requiring significant capex in renewable energy procurement, factory electrification, and manufacturing process redesign.

EV component revenue mix is a key forward indicator; as EV component revenue rises above 30% of total, analysts expect operating margin compression from transition costs followed by margin recovery as EV platforms scale.

China's emergence as the world's largest and most competitive EV market creates risks for Denso's China operations, with Chinese EV manufacturers increasingly sourcing components from domestic suppliers to reduce foreign dependence.

Primary supply chain and operational risks include Toyota's EV strategy pace, China EV competition displacing Denso components at Chinese OEMs, raw material cost inflation for copper and rare earths, power semiconductor supply constraints limiting EV component production, and US auto tariff policy changes affecting North American production economics.
source_title: Denso (6902) Stock Analysis: Toyota's Giant Supplier and the EV Transformation | Kabu Prediction

facts: Georgia governor’s office announced February 4 that Dongwon Autopart Technology Co. will build a new auto parts factory near Swainsboro, Georgia, in Emanuel County.
The Korean auto parts manufacturer will spend $30 million on the plant and create 200 new jobs in the coming years.
Hiring for the site is expected to begin this year, but no timeline was given for when the plant would become optional.
The new factory will expand Dongwon’s auto components business, which produces door frames, seat frames, and battery frames for automotive OEMs.
Dongwon’s new factory will add capacity alongside the company’s existing site in Hogansville, Georgia.
The project adds to the company’s existing Southeastern workforce of about 500 employees.

Governor Brian Kemp stated Georgia’s thriving automotive industry is creating new opportunities for Georgians statewide regardless of zip code.
Governor Brian Kemp expressed excitement for the project to bring new jobs to Emanuel County and impact the community for years to come.

facts: Fictiv has created a strategic partnership with EVCO Plastics to deliver end-to-end injection molding supply chain solutions.
The collaboration aims to tariff-proof manufacturing for U.S. OEMs by combining Fictiv's digitally connected global supply chain with EVCO's manufacturing capabilities.
EVCO Plastics provides over 1 million square feet of manufacturing space and hundreds of injection molding machines, including presses as big as 4,400 tons.
The partnership leverages deep expertise in design for manufacturability (DFM), AI, human-powered materials data and insights, program management, and global logistics.
The alliance aims to mitigate tariff concerns and deliver a highly resilient, fully managed supply chain to OEMs across multiple industries.
Fictiv addresses complexities in Mexico's emerging ecosystem for highly engineered products by streamlining tooling and specialized secondary processes.
Innovative solutions like Fictiv GlobalFlex integrate tooling inserts produced in China with next-generation manufacturing facilities in the U.S. and Mexico.
This integration enhances gross margins, accelerates production timelines, and elevates customer experiences.
The collaboration supports the entire product lifecycle, encompassing 3D printing, injection molding, assembly, testing, and packaging.
Advanced manufacturing capabilities include overmolding, gas-assist molding, in-mold decorating, and liquid silicone rubber (LSR) production.
The partnership holds ISO 9001:2015, ISO 13485, IATF 16949, and FDA compliance certifications to ensure quality and reliability throughout the supply chain.
Dave Evans, Co-Founder and CEO of Fictiv, stated that the partnership marks a crucial step in simplifying global manufacturing supply chains.

Carlos Gonzales, head of EVCO Plastics de Mexico, noted that Fictiv's capabilities allow EVCO to concentrate on manufacturing excellence while Fictiv tackles complex workflow and supply chain challenges.
The collaboration underscores a shared commitment to innovation, quality, and sustainability in the manufacturing sector.
Fictiv operates four global manufacturing centers located in India, Mexico, China, and the U.S.
To date, Fictiv has delivered more than 35 million commercial and prototype parts for early-stage companies and large enterprises.
EVCO Plastics was founded in 1964, employs over 2,100 people, and operates 270 injection molding machines ranging from 28 to 4,400 tons across 13 manufacturing facilities worldwide.
source_title: Fictiv and EVCO Plastics Announce Strategic Partnership to Deliver End-to-End Injection Molding Supply Chain Solutions

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the fourth round of grants from the Rural Site Development Initiative on June 30, 2026.
The fourth round awards $7.8 million through the OneGeorgia Authority to support ten local projects across Georgia.
Since the program launched in fiscal year 2025, Georgia has invested a total of $28 million in rural site development projects.
Grants are designed to help communities prepare sites for future business investment, job creation, and long-term economic growth.
Grant funds may be used for site improvement projects or to assist with costs related to Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) certification.
Communities provide matching funds at various levels based on program criteria.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that over 64 percent of new jobs created have gone to parts of the state outside the metro Atlanta region since he took office.
Governor Brian Kemp credited the General Assembly for funding and promoting the program and expressed hope for more jobs and investment in rural areas.
Lt. Governor Burt Jones described the grants as a key component of Georgia’s economic development strategy, essential to growth, productivity, and sustainability.
Lt. Governor Burt Jones commended Governor Kemp for leading the charge to fund and promote the program.
Speaker Jon Burns emphasized the importance of the funding for rural communities, particularly in Southeast Georgia as the region responds to changes in the forestry industry.
Speaker Jon Burns stated that the House is proud to support Rural Site Development Grants to bring good-paying jobs to hardworking Georgians across the state.
Speaker Jon Burns noted pride in investments being made in Southeast Georgia as it navigates unprecedented changes in the forestry industry.
Georgia Department of Community Affairs Commissioner Christopher Nunn said GRAD sites are important to local strategies for attracting employers and investment in Georgia’s communities.
Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that shovel-ready sites remain a major advantage in the state’s economic development portfolio.
Commissioner Pat Wilson reported that over the last several years, GRAD sites have helped attract an estimated 10,000 jobs to communities across the state.
The Effingham County Development Authority was awarded $1.8 million for site readiness improvements at the GRAD-certified Savannah Portside International Park.
Effingham County's project includes construction of a sewer pump station with total estimated costs of $2.5 million.
The Development Authority of Columbus was awarded $2 million for site improvement work at the GRAD-certified Muscogee Technology Park.
Planned improvements for the Columbus-Muscogee County project include clearing and grading, with total estimated costs of $10 million.
The McIntosh County Industrial Development Authority was awarded $1.5 million for road, water, and sewer infrastructure improvements at the GRAD-certified Tidewaters Industrial Park.
Total estimated costs for the McIntosh County project are $2.8 million.
This marks McIntosh County’s second site development award, following a previous GRAD-certification scholarship.

The Thomaston-Upson County Industrial Development Authority was awarded $2 million to support water, sewer, and transportation infrastructure at the GRAD-certified Central Georgia Business and Technology Park.
Total estimated costs for the Thomaston-Upson County project are $2.3 million.
source_title: Gov. Kemp Announces $7.8M in Rural Site Development Grants Across Georgia - Expansion Solutions

facts: Rivian renegotiated a major loan agreement with the U.S. Department of Energy, reducing the total amount while reshaping how and when the funds will be deployed.
The revised agreement reduces Rivian’s federal loan from an initial $6.57 billion to $4.5 billion.
The updated terms allow the company to begin drawing on the loan earlier than originally planned, improving liquidity during a critical expansion phase.
Under the previous structure, the loan supported a two-phase production plan; the new agreement simplifies this into a single phase.
Rivian now expects to begin utilizing the funds in 2027, approximately one year ahead of its earlier timeline.
This shift is expected to support faster ramp-up of manufacturing operations and reduce near-term financial pressure.
The Georgia plant was initially designed to support an annual capacity of 400,000 vehicles across two phases.
Under the new plan, total capacity has been reduced to 300,000 units annually.
The updated strategy emphasizes stronger early-stage production output by focusing on a single phase to improve operational efficiency and bring vehicles to market faster.
Rivian confirmed that production of the R2 model is still scheduled to begin in late 2028 at the Georgia plant.
The R2 platform targets a more affordable segment of the EV market.
The Georgia facility will play a central role in scaling this platform, complementing Rivian’s existing manufacturing operations in Illinois.
Any additional expansion beyond the revised Georgia plant capacity is expected to be funded through Rivian’s own resources rather than government loans.
Rivian has been actively securing partnerships and external funding to support its long-term plans.
Collaborations with major global players, including automotive and mobility partners, are helping Rivian strengthen its financial position while sharing development costs.
Rivian reported first-quarter revenue of $1.38 billion, representing an increase from $1.24 billion in the same period last year and slightly exceeding market expectations.
Net losses narrowed to $416 million, compared to $541 million a year earlier.
Gross profit came in at $119 million, though this marked a decline from the previous year.
The automotive segment recorded a loss of $62 million.
The software and services division generated a strong $181 million profit.
A significant factor behind weaker automotive performance was a roughly $100 million drop in revenue from regulatory credit sales, combined with lower production volumes during the quarter.
Rivian’s adjustments reflect broader EV industry trends including moderating demand growth, intensifying competition, and pricing pressure squeezing margins.
Policy uncertainty around government incentives and funding programs is influencing long-term planning for automakers.
source_title: Rivian Restructures $4.5 Billion Federal Loan as Georgia EV Plant Plans Shift Amid Demand Uncertainty | MarketSpy

facts: FREYR Battery has announced that it has chosen and completed the purchase of a site in Coweta County, Georgia for its planned Giga America battery plant.

The company selected the Bridgeport Industrial Park site after a rigorous evaluation process based on key operational, logistical, and financial criteria, including the feasibility of co-locating modular upstream and downstream projects with the Giga America cell manufacturing facility.

The initial phase of the Giga America project is set to be a cell production module of approximately 34 GWh based on the next-generation of 24M’s U.S.-based SemiSolidTM production platform at an initial projected capital cost of $1.7 billion.

The company is carrying out an assessment of value accretive upstream and downstream modules as well as additional cell production lines which are expected to bring total capital investments to more than $2.6 billion through 2029.
When all of the contemplated construction phases are fully completed, the Giga America complex is expected to be one of the largest battery cell manufacturing developments in the world.

FREYR’s Co-Founder and Chief Executive Officer Tom Einar Jensen stated that the landmark announcement underscores FREYR’s ambition to develop a very strong and near-term operational footprint in the United States.

Expanding into the U.S. has been a foundational aspect of FREYR’s long-term strategy from its inception, and with the recent passage of the Inflation Reduction Act, the company expects U.S. demand for ESS, passenger EV and other electric mobility applications to grow rapidly over the next decade.
Governor Brian Kemp welcomed the deal, noting that job creators and innovators bring operations to Georgia because they know they will have success there.

Governor Brian Kemp expressed pride in welcoming FREYR to the Peach State as the latest company to bring transformational investments and opportunity to communities, calling them a valued addition to the No. 1 state for business and looking forward to their growing impact on Coweta and the surrounding region.

Coweta County Board of Commissioners Chairman Bob Blackburn welcomed FREYR Battery to the community, stating that Coweta County is quickly becoming a premier destination for top-tier manufacturing industries that support the U.S. economy and beyond.

In accordance with ambitions to accelerate development of Giga America in parallel with the ongoing construction of Giga Arctic in Mo i Rana, Norway, the company is intensifying and broadening its financing efforts.

The total package of financial incentives for Giga America includes robust assistance collectively provided by The State of Georgia and Coweta County in addition to expected production tax credits associated with the recently passed Inflation Reduction Act.
FREYR intends to apply with the U.S. Department of Energy for packages that could include a grant and/or direct loans to assist with the development of Giga America.
The U.S. energy storage market is already the largest in the world and is expected to experience exponential growth over the next several years.
According to Rystad Energy, to satisfy a 1.6-degree scenario, cumulative collective storage demand of 4.5 TWh from the U.S. and Europe will be required through 2030.

Under the same scenario, Rystad expects annual collective storage demand in the U.S. and Europe to reach 1.4 TWh by 2030, representing a 34-fold increase from projected corresponding annual 2022 demand.

FREYR is deeply committed to decarbonizing the transportation and energy sectors and expects to make meaningful investments to spur job creation and develop localized, decarbonized supply chains in the U.S. to enhance energy security and economic activity.
The Giga America project will be based on the SemiSolidTM technology of FREYR’s U.S.-based licensing partner, 24M.
The SemiSolidTM platform enables capital and energy efficient production of lithium-ion batteries at scale.
FREYR is currently evaluating clean power supply solutions for the Giga America project with U.S. Independent Power Producers (IPPs).
The company’s long-term ambition is to replicate the strategy employed at Giga Arctic to produce next-generation batteries that are among the cleanest anywhere in the world.

facts: FREYR is planning to initiate detailed project engineering of Giga America in the coming months.

The initial phase of Giga America is planned to be a cell production module of approximately 34 GWh based on the next-generation of 24M’s U.S.-based SemiSolid™ production platform at an initial projected capital cost of $1.7 billion.

The company is also evaluating value accretive upstream and downstream modules as well as additional cell production lines that are expected to bring total capital investments to more than $2.6 billion through 2029.
Upon completion of all the contemplated construction phases, the Giga America complex is expected to be one of the largest battery cell manufacturing developments globally.
FREYR’s Co-Founder and Chief Executive Officer Tom Einar Jensen stated that expanding into the U.S. has been a foundational aspect of FREYR’s long-term strategy from its inception.
With the recent passage of the Inflation Reduction Act, FREYR expects U.S. demand for ESS, passenger EV and other electric mobility applications to grow rapidly over the next decade.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp stated that job creators and innovators from all over the world bring their operations to Georgia because they know they will have success here.

Governor Kemp expressed pride in welcoming FREYR to the Peach State as the latest company to bring transformational investments and opportunity to communities, noting Georgia is the No. 1 state for business.
Governor Kemp looks forward to FREYR's growing impact on Coweta and the surrounding region.
Coweta County Board of Commissioners Chairman Bob Blackburn said they are thrilled to welcome FREYR Battery to the Coweta County community.
With the addition of FREYR, Coweta County is quickly becoming a premier destination for top-tier manufacturing industries that support the U.S. economy and beyond.
Coweta County looks forward to the innovation, opportunities, and success that FREYR will generate.

In accordance with ambitions to accelerate development of Giga America in parallel with the ongoing construction of Giga Arctic in Mo i Rana, Norway, the company is intensifying and broadening its financing efforts.

According to Rystad Energy, to satisfy a 1.6-degree scenario, cumulative collective storage demand of 4.5 TWh from the U.S. and Europe will be required through 2030.

Under the same scenario, Rystad expects annual collective storage demand in the U.S. and Europe to reach 1.4 TWh by 2030, which represents a 34-fold increase from projected corresponding annual 2022 demand.

facts: FREYR Battery is a Norwegian developer of battery cells that is entering solar panel manufacturing by acquiring the U.S. manufacturing operations of Trina Solar.
The company previously sought to serve the energy storage and EV markets and was pursuing a gigawatt-scale battery factory in Coweta County, Georgia.
FREYR will take control of Trina’s 5-GW solar panel assembly facility in Wilmer, Texas, paying $340 million for its U.S. manufacturing assets.
An investor presentation details that FREYR is establishing an operations and intellectual property agreement between Trina Solar and FREYR.
The transaction closing is expected before the end of 2024, at which point FREYR will begin efforts on domestic cell manufacturing.
Site selection for the new facility is underway, with construction targeted to begin in the second quarter of 2025.
The new cell manufacturing outfit is expected to support 1,800 jobs and begin production in 2026.
Newly appointed CEO Daniel Barcelo stated that the transaction will immediately position the company as one of the leading solar manufacturing companies in the U.S.

Barcelo noted that domestic manufacturing capacity for solar and batteries is essential for energy transition and job creation, and added that the U.S. was once the global leader in solar and can be again.
FREYR has secured a U.S. polysilicon supply with Hemlock for its eventual cell production, establishing a platform for its U.S. solar and battery storage strategy.
The company announced it has terminated its battery technology license with 24M Technologies, but no further news was released regarding its potential Georgia battery factory.
Upon closing of the Trina transaction, Trina employees Mingxing Lin will join FREYR as chief strategy officer and Dave Gustafson will join as chief operating officer.
Solar panels produced at the Texas plant will continue to carry the Trina brand, and both companies will jointly market the panels to the U.S. market.
source_title: FREYR buys Trina solar panel factory in Texas for $340 million

facts: FREYR Battery, a Europe-based producer of next-generation battery cells, will build a manufacturing plant in Coweta County.
The project represents a $2.57 billion investment that will create 723 jobs during the next seven years.
Gov. Brian Kemp announced the project on Friday, November 11, 2022.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated: “Job creators and innovators from all over the world bring their operations to Georgia because they know they will have success here.”
Gov. Brian Kemp added: “We’re proud to welcome FREYR to the Peach State as the latest company to bring transformational investments and opportunity to our communities.”
FREYR is dedicated to supporting a domestic supply chain for renewable power sources.
The company’s battery cells can be used for stationary energy storage, electric mobility, and additional applications.
Tom Einar Jensen, co-founder and CEO of FREYR, stated: “At FREYR, we are deeply committed to the ambition we share with our U.S. partners to decarbonize the transportation and energy sectors.”

Tom Einar Jensen noted that as they advance their U.S. expansion plan in cooperation with key stakeholders, they expect to make meaningful investments to spur job creation and the eventual development of localized, decarbonized supply chains in the U.S. to enhance energy security and economic activity.
The announcement comes as Georgia ramps up to become a leading producer of electric vehicles and EV batteries.
During the past year, Rivian announced plans for a huge EV manufacturing plant near Covington.
During the past year, Hyundai announced plans for a huge EV manufacturing plant near Savannah.
The Rivian and Hyundai projects are described as the two largest economic development projects in Georgia’s history.
SK Innovation has built two EV battery manufacturing facilities in Northeast Georgia.
FREYR’s new facility will be called Giga America.
The planned first phase production of the Giga America facility will be about 34 gigawatt hours.
The company is currently evaluating clean power supply solutions for the new plant, including the potential use of renewable energy from solar installations.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team worked to land the project in partnership with the Coweta County Development Authority.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team worked to land the project in partnership with the Metro Atlanta Chamber.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team worked to land the project in partnership with Georgia EMC.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team worked to land the project in partnership with the state Department of Labor’s Quick Start program.
source_title: $2.57 billion Freyr battery plant will bring 723 jobs to Coweta County

facts: According to state and local economic development sources FREYR Battery plans to invest $2,570,000,000.00 to build out new space in Newnan, Georgia.
The company plans to occupy the new space at Bridgeport Industrial Park in Newnan on or about July 1, 2025.
According to the company website FREYR intends to install 50 GWh of battery cell capacity by 2025, 100 GWh annual capacity by 2028 and 200 GWh of annual capacity by 2030.
The company aims to position itself as one of Europe's largest battery cell suppliers.

Facilities will be located in the Mo i Rana industrial complex in Northern Norway, leveraging Norway's highly skilled workforce and abundant, low-cost renewable energy sources from hydro and wind in a crisp, clear and energized environment.
FREYR will supply safe, high energy density and cost competitive clean battery cells to the rapidly growing global markets for electric vehicles, energy storage, and marine applications.

FREYR is committed to supporting cluster-based R&D initiatives and the development of an international ecosystem of scientific, commercial, and financial stakeholders to support the expansion of the battery value chain in the region.
source_title: FREYR Battery to spend $2.57 Billion to expand into new space in Newnan Georgia. - intelligence360

facts: PJM Interconnection, the nation’s largest grid operator, has greenlit 51 generation projects totaling 9,361 MW of unforced capacity under its one-time Reliability Resource Initiative (RRI).
The approved project mix includes a blend of gas (combined and simple cycle), battery storage, nuclear, coal, and a whisper of wind.
39 of the approved projects are capacity uprates at existing plants.
12 of the approved projects are new builds, consisting of 6 gas, 5 battery, and 1 nuclear facility.
Nearly all approved projects are expected to be online by 2030.
PJM’s shift from a 'first-come' to 'first-ready' interconnection queue has already yielded 18 GW of signed agreements.
The American Clean Power Association and industry executives announced a $100 billion commitment to U.S.-based battery manufacturing by 2030.
This fivefold jump in domestic investment could, in theory, cover 100% of U.S. storage demand without relying on Chinese supply chains.
Tesla is building a refinery near Corpus Christi and an expanded Megapack facility in Nevada.
Form Energy is constructing an iron-air battery plant in West Virginia.
Multiple LG and Fluence facilities are located across the South and Midwest.
The IRA supercharged storage economics with generous credits, but policy stability is now uncertain due to legal limbo and looming tariffs.
ACP CEO Jason Grumet stated: 'Best investment conditions in a generation, highest uncertainty in a generation.'
E2 reported that nearly $8 billion in clean energy projects were canceled or downsized in Q1 2025.
Kore Power’s $1.2B Arizona battery plant was canceled.
Freyr Battery’s $2.6B Georgia facility was canceled.
Multiple solar, wind, and EV investments were halted or reversed during this period.
Cancellations were driven by the Trump administration freezing IRA funds pending a federal review, increasing tariffs, and signaling hostility toward EV and solar subsidies.
Courts temporarily reinstated some IRA funds, but investor sentiment has turned cautious.
$1.6B in clean energy investments were announced in March alone.
Freyr rebranded as T1 Energy and pivoted to solar despite canceling its battery plant.
Quanta Services (PWR) is positioned as the dominant contractor for grid buildouts, benefiting from the need for new or upgraded transmission lines for PJM's fast-tracked generation projects.
Utilities are shifting from speculative renewables to shovel-ready gas and nuclear, which will likely cause Quanta's backlog of lucrative, long-duration projects to balloon.
Freyr just pulled the plug on its $2.6B Georgia battery plant due to the IRA freeze and tariff threats gutting expected incentives.
Unlike Tesla or LG, Freyr lacks U.S. manufacturing scale or political leverage, and its semi-solid lithium technology remains unproven at scale.
Freyr is now months behind its peers and hemorrhaging investor confidence, representing a retreat rather than a pivot.
source_title: PJM Adds 9.3 GW to Meet Rising Demand // $100B Storage Investment // Clean Energy Cancellations

facts: A clean energy company is abandoning a plan to build a giant electric battery factory in Atlanta's suburbs after shifting to buy a solar panel plant in Texas.
Freyr Battery told officials in Newnan on Thursday it would not build a $2.6 billion plant that was supposed to hire more than 700 people.
Freyr sent a Jan. 21 letter to the Coweta County Development Authority announcing its plans to end the project.
The factory would have built batteries to store electricity produced by renewable sources and release it later.
It would have been the second-largest battery factory worldwide when announced in 2023.
Freyr is a startup founded in 2018 that never began construction on the 368-acre (149-hectare) site.
Freyr moved its corporate headquarters from Norway to Newnan in part to maximize eligibility for U.S. tax benefits of President Joe Biden's climate law.
Freyr is shifting focus to a newly opened solar panel factory bought last year for $340 million from top Chinese solar panel maker Trina Solar.
Freyr spokesperson Amy Jaick wrote that the company is grateful for support in Coweta County and Georgia but is focusing on the solar module manufacturing facility in Texas.
Newnan Times-Herald first reported the story regarding Freyr senior vice president of business development Jason Peace meeting with local officials on Thursday.
Peace told Coweta County Development Authority board members the decision was driven by rising interest rates, falling battery prices, a change in company leadership, and a shift in goals.
Authority President Sarah Jacobs wrote an email Friday confirming the meeting and reasons for the project's end.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development conveyed a $7 million grant to buy a site for Freyr in Newnan, about 35 miles (55 kilometers) southwest of Atlanta.
Department spokesperson Jessica Atwell said the state and company are working together to ensure the grant money is repaid expeditiously.
Freyr may also owe money to Coweta County.
Georgia's incentives process protects taxpayers, ensuring discretionary incentives are repaid when a company's plans change, according to Atwell.
Planning for the project made Coweta County a stronger candidate for future projects, according to Jacobs.
The company had planned battery factories in Norway and Finland but said in November it will try to sell its European business.
Freyr is terminating its license for technology to make batteries, paying $3 million to the company it was licensed from.
Tom Einar Jensen, then CEO, told investors in August that raising money to make batteries had grown difficult due to a surplus of Chinese batteries produced at lower costs.
The company is switching its strategy into businesses that would allow it to raise cash, including solar panel manufacturing.
Freyr saw its cash on hand fall from $253 million at the end of 2023 to $182 million on Sept. 30.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp has targeted recruitment of the electric vehicle industry.
Korean firm SK Innovation built a $2.6 billion battery plant in Commerce, northeast of Atlanta and hired 3,000 workers, but later laid off or furloughed some workers.
Hyundai Motor Group has started production at a $7.6 billion electric vehicle and battery plant near Savannah, with plans to hire 8,500 workers.
Electric truck maker Rivian revived its plans to build a plant east of Atlanta after a $6.6 billion loan from the Biden administration.
source_title: Battery firm abandons plan for a $2.6 billion plant in Georgia

facts: U.S. markets witnessed steep declines on Thursday due to Trump administration tariffs and recessionary fears.
Goodyear Tire & Rubber Co. rallied nearly 12% on Thursday, making it the day’s top gainer among the highlighted stocks.
Deutsche Bank analyst Edison Yu considers Goodyear a 'relative winner' of Trump's trade wars because most U.S. demand is filled with domestic manufacturing, reducing exposure to import tariffs.
Goodyear's focus on replacement tires accounts for 85% of its revenue and is set to benefit from auto tariffs as consumers keep their vehicles longer.
Goodyear stock was upgraded to a 'BUY' rating with a $13 price target by Investing.com.
First Solar Inc. rallied nearly 5% on Thursday as an American-made solar panel manufacturer that avoids the new tariffs impacting competitors.
Chinese-made solar panels face a considerable disadvantage due to the imposition of a massive 54% tax on imports.
Investors are seeking to avoid the 54% tariff on Chinese panels while pursuing Inflation Reduction Act tax credits, providing strong impetus to First Solar.
Dollar General Corp. spiked 4% as investors pursued it as a recession-proof stock with nearly 20,000 stores, adding to an 18% rally since early March.
Motley Fool writer Billy Duberstein notes that Dollar General faced challenges from inflation reducing low-income customers' purchasing power and rising theft during the post-pandemic period.
American Water Works Company Inc. provides water and wastewater solutions, remains insulated from global trade and tariffs, and offers a 2% annualized yield as a safe-haven stock.

facts: Goodyear Tire & Rubber Co. is executing a two-year transformation plan called "Goodyear Forward" that ends in December.
The plan aims to double operating income margin to 10%.
The plan includes enacting top-line and cost reductions of $1.5 billion.
The plan targets bringing in gross proceeds of $2 billion from business asset sales.
The plan reduces the company's debt load by $1.5 billion, net of approximately $1.1 billion for restructuring.
Goodyear is investing in and deploying artificial intelligence technologies and 3D-printing for components such as tread teeth.
The company uses simulation technology to speed the development and production of its products.
CEO Mark Stewart stated that Goodyear is ahead of schedule for its benchmarks, including upping cost cuts by $200 million.
Goodyear has reported five consecutive quarters of margin growth and its best retail performance in more than 20 years.
Shares of Goodyear received a 17% boost after the company reported its 2024 and fourth-quarter results.
Elliott Investment Management revealed a stake in the company in 2023 and retains a roughly 9% stake, adding three directors to its board.
Goodyear employs about 68,000 people globally.
The company manufactures its products in 53 facilities across multiple countries, with major operations in North and South America, Asia-Pacific, and Europe.
Goodyear operates eight tire plants in the U.S., two plants in Canada, two in Mexico, and one plant each in Brazil, Chile, Colombia, and Peru.
Manufacturing operations in the Americas represented roughly half of Goodyear's tire sales in 2024.
Goodyear is investing an unspecified amount into a facility in Oklahoma to expand production by 10 million units annually and modernize the plant.
Goodyear's Asia-Pacific segment operating income increased by 37% last year to $277 million, with an operating margin of 11.4%.
Low-cost imported tires from Southeast Asia grew 11% in the U.S. last year, undercutting Goodyear ahead of potential tariffs.
Goodyear's largest global competitors are France-based Michelin, Japan-based Bridgestone Corp., and Germany-based Continental.
The company is expanding automation to its U.S. facilities, contrasting with traditional processes at its historic Akron headquarters.
Goodyear invested millions in a simulation machine at its headquarters to cut R&D costs and time while improving product profits.
The simulation facility features vehicle cockpits for a hatchback and pickup truck surrounded by 270 degrees of screens for virtual tire testing.
Goodyear has worked with major automakers on virtual development, including Ferrari.

Goodyear is expanding its top-tier products to include massive tires for off-road vehicles like the Jeep Wrangler, Ford Bronco, Tesla Cybertruck, and large SUVs with 22-inch or 24-inch wheels like the Cadillac Escalade.
CEO Mark Stewart plans to get aggressive about growth in retail and service, as well as high-end tires.
Goodyear inked a high-profile business deal for its first launch in decades on a Ferrari sports car.
The company is evaluating the potential impact of President Donald Trump's tariffs on Canada and Mexico for automakers and suppliers on its North American operations.
CFO Christina Zamarro stated during the quarterly earnings call that low-end imports outperformed the U.S. industry last year and grew 11%.
Argus analyst Bill Selesky upgraded Goodyear to buy in a Feb. 14 investor note, citing fourth quarter 2024 results as an inflection point.
Nine analysts compiled by FactSet rate Goodyear's stock as overweight with a target price of $11.47 a share.
source_title: Goodyear Tire's transformation plan is underway — in the sky and on the ground

facts: Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company's ElectricDrive Sustainable-Material (EDS) tire won the Environmental Achievement of the Year – Tire Design award at the 2025 Tire Technology International (TTI) Awards for Innovation and Excellence.
The award was presented at the Tire Technology Expo held at Deutsche Messe in Hannover, Germany.

Matt Ross, editor-in-chief of TTI and chairman of the Tire Technology Awards for Innovation and Excellence, stated that judges were particularly impressed by Goodyear's EDS tire for its unique combination of sustainability and tire design.
The EDS tire was unveiled at the 7th China International Import Expo (CIIE).
The EDS tire represents a major step in Goodyear's pursuit of developing the industry's first 100% sustainable-material tire by 2030.
The EDS tire is China's first ISCC mark retail tire with over 70% sustainable materials and is engineered for electric vehicles.
Nathaniel Madarang, Goodyear Asia Pacific President, stated that the EDS tire provides EV drivers an excellent option of high-performing tires with ISCC-certified sustainable materials exceeding 70%.
Goodyear is committed to introducing the industry's first 100% sustainable-material tire by 2030.
Goodyear's Better Future framework is an important enabler to realizing its strategic vision to be #1 in tires and service in Luxury, SUV and EV across Asia Pacific.
The EDS tire is designed to meet the growing demand for sustainable and high-performing tires in the electric vehicle sector.
Engineered for the unique demands of EVs, the EDS tire delivers superior wet braking, enhanced handling, lower rolling resistance, and reduced noise.
Goodyear has integrated corporate responsibility into its company culture through its Corporate Responsibility framework, known as Goodyear Better Future.
The Goodyear Better Future framework's pillars are Sustainable Sourcing, Responsible Operations, Advanced Mobility and Inspiring Culture.
These pillars continue to guide Goodyear's global operations.
Goodyear is one of the world's largest tire companies.
Goodyear employs about 68,000 people.
Goodyear manufactures its products in 53 facilities in 30 countries around the world.
Goodyear operates two Innovation Centers in Akron, Ohio, and Colmar-Berg, Luxembourg.
The innovation centers strive to develop state-of-the-art products and services that set the technology and performance standard for the industry.
source_title: GOODYEAR EDS TIRE WINS 2025 TIRE TECHNOLOGY INTERNATIONAL AWARDS-Ginco Hotel Shanghai-Tourism News

facts: Great Dane employs more than 400 people at its Statesboro plant.
Great Dane brings more than $25 million in payroll to the Bulloch County area each year.
Great Dane was named Georgia Manufacturer of the Year in the medium category, which covers plants with 150-499 employees.
Gov. Nathan Deal presented the awards Thursday during the Manufacturing Appreciation Week luncheon at the Georgia International Convention Center in Atlanta.
Great Dane was nominated for the award by Ogeechee Technical College, which nominates at least one manufacturer from its three-county service area each year.
Great Dane was founded in Savannah in 1900.

The company developed its first refrigerated trailer in 1940, which was also the first refrigerated trailer in the industry according to Brian Sage, Great Dane's current vice president of manufacturing.
Great Dane opened its 450,000-square-foot Statesboro factory in Bulloch County's Gateway Industrial Park in April 2012.

Brian Sage stated that Great Dane has deep roots in Georgia and chose the Statesboro location because of community support, the partnership with Ogeechee Technical College, and its strategic location.
Great Dane is an international leader in the trailer industry and is now headquartered in Chicago while maintaining a corporate campus in Savannah.

Representatives from Ogeechee Tech, the Statesboro-Bulloch Chamber of Commerce, the Bulloch County Development Authority, Bulloch County government, the city of Statesboro, and the Evans County Industrial Development Authority attended the luncheon.
Several agencies contributed letters supporting Ogeechee Tech's nomination of Great Dane.

Previous Manufacturer of the Year winners nominated by Ogeechee Tech were engine manufacturer Briggs & Stratton (large category, 2002) and architectural glass fabricator Viracon (medium category, 2005).
Both previous winning companies' Georgia plants are located in Bulloch County.
Ogeechee Tech and Bulloch County had a first-time winner in the Manufacturing Appreciation Week student design contest this year.
Bethany Boyer, a seventh-grader at William James Middle School, won statewide first place among middle school artists (grades six through eight).
Ogeechee Technical College President Lori Durden praised Boyer for competing against hundreds of state middle schoolers and her insight into manufacturing in Georgia.
Boyer received a $500 scholarship check presented by the governor.
The 2017 Manufacturer of the Year winner in the large manufacturer category (over 500 employees) was King's Hawaiian bakery in Flowery Branch, near Gainesville.
The winner with fewer than 150 employees was Grenzebach Corporation, an international maker of specialized equipment with a plant in Newnan.
Award categories are based on the workforce of the nominated Georgia-based location instead of overall company size.
OTC Vice President for Economic Development Jan Moore, who is also Statesboro's mayor, called it a wonderful day for the community.
Manufacturing and its economic impact are vital to Statesboro and Bulloch County, according to Moore.
Great Dane employs a number of Ogeechee Tech graduates.
Aspects of Ogeechee Tech's welding program have been geared to meet Great Dane's needs.

Lori Durden noted that Ogeechee Tech and Great Dane have had a great partnership since day one, with Great Dane utilizing office space on the college campus before opening its plant and thousands crowding the campus to apply for positions.
Great Dane has contributed to the college, including giving a trailer for use in the Commercial Truck Driving program.
Gov. Nathan Deal's proclamation designated April 24-28 as Manufacturing Appreciation Week.
The proclamation stated that manufacturing makes a tremendous contribution to Georgia's economic well-being.
About 10,059 manufacturing facilities are located in Georgia.
These facilities provide roughly 386,600 jobs, generate nearly $20.3 billion in wages, and contribute more than $100 billion to the state's economy each year.
Gov. Deal acknowledged and honored the award-winning manufacturers at the luncheon.
The event was cohosted by the Technical College System of Georgia and the Georgia Department of Economic Development.
The 2017 awards selection and luncheon constituted Georgia's 23rd annual observance of Manufacturing Appreciation Week.

Sponsors included the Georgia Association of Manufacturers, the Georgia Manufacturing Extension Partnership with the Georgia Institute of Technology, Georgia Power, and the accounting and business advisory firm Aprio (previously HA&W).
Ogeechee Tech and schools in Bulloch County participate in a national Manufacturing Day each fall.
Local industries that hosted about 100 eighth-graders for tours in October were Briggs & Stratton, Viracon, and Great Dane.
source_title: Great Dane tops in Georgia - Statesboro Herald

canonical_name: Bulloch County Schools Career Technical & Agricultural Education (CTAE) Program
facts: An Economic Development Partnership team from the Georgia Department of Education (GaDOE) visited Bulloch County Schools on Friday, January 21.

Patrick Ledford, an economic development liaison with the GaDOE’s Career, Technical, and Agricultural Education division, described the visit as a fantastic experience and highlighted strong stakeholder relationships in the district.
The eight-hour site visit was a key part of a year-long process for Bulloch County Schools to seek a district-wide designation from the state’s Economic Development Partnership program.
The designation is endorsed by the Georgia Economic Developers Association.

The GaDOE team visited to learn about the school district’s Career Technical & Agricultural Education (CTAE) program and its collaboration with local post-secondary institutions, businesses, industries, economic developers, and workforce development partners.

The school district was admitted into the Economic Development Partnership program in 2021 after an application process that required input and support from local partners including the Development Authority of Bulloch County and Great Dane.

Bethany Gilliam, the school district’s CTAE director, stated the district believes it has a strong chance of obtaining the designation, which would benefit the community by helping compete for new business and industry.
The GaDOE team toured career pathway classes and labs at Portal Middle High School, Southeast Bulloch High School, and Statesboro High School to visit with teachers and students.
The team met one-on-one with multiple school district partners and heard presentations about the district’s Workforce Development Plan and opportunities within its CTAE program.
Gilliam noted the GaDOE team was very complimentary of the work in CTAE classrooms and labs, as well as the district-wide approach to employability skills training.

The Economic Development Partnership Designation program is a district-wide process that prepares school districts to adopt best practices supporting economic development, strengthen programs, and align education with key regional industries.
In terms of economic development, the Partnership elevates the capability of school systems to fill a key role in business recruitment and expansion and talent development for their communities.

Fostering strong relationships between education, business and industry, and economic development stakeholders ensures districts continue offering high-quality industry-relevant programs equitable for all students and supportive of local talent pipeline development.

Dr. Julie Chance, executive director of Program Improvement for Bulloch County Schools, said it is an honor to be accepted into the 2021-2022 Economic Development Partnership Designation program and highlighted ongoing collaboration with local partners to prepare students for successful careers and build an educated, skilled workforce for economic growth.
Bulloch County Schools’ CTAE program helps prepare students for college enrollment, military enlistment, or employment in in-demand, high-wage careers after high school.
The district’s state-approved career pathway courses leverage partnerships with industry and higher education to ensure students acquire skills needed for immediate and future employment.
The program offers students courses in 13 different career clusters and 23 high school career pathways and three middle school career pathways.
Work Based Learning opportunities are offered for students at all three of the school district’s high schools.
source_title: State leaders visit Bulloch County Schools • The Georgia Virtue

facts: Great Dane delivers high-quality trailers in Flowery Branch, Georgia (GA) built for durability, safety, and efficiency.
Great Dane trailers are crafted for long-haul trucking and heavy-duty loads.
Dry van trailers feature reinforced frames for maximum strength.
Reefer trailers are designed for temperature-sensitive cargo.
Flatbed trailers are designed for versatile hauling needs.
Trailers include precision suspension systems for smoother rides and fuel efficiency.
Great Dane provides professional trailer maintenance and repair services through trained technicians.
Routine inspections and preventive maintenance help truckers avoid costly downtime, extend equipment life, and ensure deliveries stay on schedule.
Great Dane service centers are equipped with the latest diagnostic tools and replacement parts, capable of handling everything from minor adjustments to major overhauls.
Great Dane was one of the first trailer manufacturers to implement a nationwide network of certified service centers.
Trucker Guide is a platform that helps locate truck parts and service centers for Great Dane trailers in Flowery Branch, Georgia (GA).

facts: Leslie Doggett Industries through its newly acquired Truck Enterprises Group has acquired the privately-held Great Dane Trailer distributor/dealer for El Paso, Laredo and McAllen/Pharr, Texas.
The dealership territory stretches to Las Cruces, New Mexico.
Doggett purchased this business from El Paso’s legendary trucking magnate and International Truck dealer, Royal Jones.
The purchase price was undisclosed.
This acquisition follows Doggett’s acquisition of El Paso’s Truck Enterprises two weeks ago.
It also follows Doggett’s acquisition of the Freightliner dealerships throughout the State of Arkansas last year.
The addition of Great Dane complements Doggett’s existing Freightliner and Western Star dealerships in El Paso, Laredo and McAllen/Pharr.
Doggett will now sell and service flatbeds, dry freight and refrigerated vans with their over-the-road truck offerings.

Paul Burk, SVP of the Doggett Truck Group, stated that adding Great Dane products to Doggett’s world class lineup allows further partnership with trucking companies and truck dealers on a measurable and meaningful basis.
Burk expressed pride in partnering with Great Dane and expected extraordinary growth with their products alongside Freightliner, John Deere, Toyota, LinkBelt, and Ford businesses.

Great Dane’s history dates back to 1900, having established itself as the transportation solutions leader of today delivering innovative products and exceptional services for trucking customers and employees.
Great Dane engineers and builds the industry’s premier high-quality trailers and truck bodies.
Great Dane manufactured 49,000 trailers in 2018.
Great Dane’s headquarters are in Savannah, Georgia with manufacturing plants throughout North America.
Doggett is a Houston-based diversified heavy equipment dealer for seven first-tier manufacturers that are either number 1 or 2 in their respective industries.

Doggett's dealership portfolio includes John Deere construction and forestry equipment across 17 dealerships, Toyota Industrial Equipment forklifts & material handling across 7 dealerships, Freightliner and Western Star on-highway and vocational trucks across 9 dealerships, Link Belt Cranes across 3 dealerships, a Ford auto and truck dealership, and now Great Dane Trailers across 3 dealerships.
Leslie Doggett Industries was founded by Leslie Doggett and Brady Carruth in 1993 with 17 employees.
The Houston Chronicle ranked Doggett in 2019 as Houston’s 12th largest private company.
Doggett reported annual sales exceeding US $1 billion.
Doggett employs over 1400 full-time staff, including more than 500 factory trained and certified, highly professional technicians.
Doggett operates as a family-owned heavy equipment, highway truck and automotive dealership group without outside investment of any kind.
source_title: Doggett Industries Acquires Great Dane Trailer Dealership

facts: Great Dane Limited Partnership has announced plans to build a new refrigerated trailer plant on a 118-acre site in Statesboro, Ga., northwest of Savannah.
Construction is expected to get under way later this year to bring the plant into production in 2012.
The plant meets the company’s and the state's joint goal of keeping this facility close to Great Dane's heritage.
The trailer manufacturer was founded more than 100 years ago in Savannah.
The plant, in its first phase, will encompass 450,000 square feet, including office and show room space.
The facility will have the capacity to produce 5,000-plus trailers annually.
At that level of production, the facility will employ more than 400 people.
After more than a year-long search for the ideal location, Statesboro, Ga., was chosen as the future home of the new refrigerated trailer plant.
Many different sites throughout the Southeast region were considered before the decision was made.
The company believes the Statesboro location best meets its strategic and corporate needs.
Phill Pines serves as President and Chief Operating Officer for Great Dane Limited Partnership.
The new reefer plant will serve the traditional Great Dane customer base.
Manufacturing processes will feature the latest technology in foaming methods and automation of key production operations.

Phill Pines stated that the company was impressed with the State of Georgia and Bulloch County's ability and willingness to effectively address their needs, noting they were professional and made the company feel welcome and wanted.
The new plant will be located less than an hour's drive from Great Dane's Division Offices in Savannah, Ga.
Great Dane's Division Offices in Savannah, Ga., will remain home to its sales, marketing, engineering, and state-of-the-art research and development center.
source_title: Great Dane To Build New Reefer Plant in Statesboro, Georgia | Trailer Body Builders

facts: Great Dane Trailers is headquartered in Savannah, Ga.
Great Dane Trailers announced the addition of All Points Great Dane in Dearborn, Mich., to its North American distribution network.
The new full-service dealer location was established to better serve Detroit and Eastern Michigan customers.
Chris Hammond serves as vice president of Dealer Sales at Great Dane Trailers.
All Points Great Dane is located at 12851 Prospect St. just off I-94 in Dearborn.
The facility is staffed with 32 employees, including four salespeople, 15 mechanics, and two parts and service people.
The All Points Great Dane facility spans 22,000 square feet.
The site offers eight drive-through service bays, one of which is a paint bay.
Three trucks provide mobile service at the location, including a parts liner supported by a salesman.
source_title: Great Dane Trailers Announces Dearborn Dealship | Heavy Duty Trucking

facts: Great Dane Trailers announced plans to close its original manufacturing facility in Savannah, Ga., effective Jan. 9.

The company cited the effect of the extended economic downturn on the transportation industry coupled with the need for more efficient production as the key factors behind closing the refrigerated trailer plant.
About 270 plant hourly and office employees will be affected by this closing.

Corporate office roles unaffected include sales and marketing, design engineering, manufacturing engineering, research and development, retail accounting, MIS, corporate quality, and customer service.
The Savannah plant was built in 1919 for the expanding Savannah Blowpipe Co. before becoming the starting ground for the first Great Dane trailers.

Phillip Pines, chief operating officer of Great Dane Limited Partnership, stated that the closing is a sad day affecting many family and friends, and emphasized that the plant has remained pivotal in the company's history.

Pines noted that the closing is no reflection on the caliber of the people or their work, but rather that the company has exhausted the ability of this plant to produce refrigerated trailers at a competitive price.
Plans for replacing the lost capacity are underway to meet customer expectations while remaining price-competitive.

Advancements in technology for trailer design and production have placed increased demands on manufacturing, necessitating a modernized facility to maintain Great Dane’s position as the leader in the refrigerated trailer market.
Until a new plant is established, the Savannah plant’s production will be transferred to Great Dane’s two other refrigerated plants in Wayne, Neb., and Brazil, Ind.
source_title: Great Dane Trailers to close manufacturing facility in Savannah, Ga. | Commercial Carrier Journal
