facts: Invest Georgia, the state-funded investment program, has received an additional $10 million in the FY 2019 state budget.
This brings the program’s total funding up to $40 million available to spur economic activity by investing in Georgia-based venture capital and private equity funds.

Governor Nathan Deal stated that Invest Georgia furthers one of his top priorities as governor: building Georgia’s reputation as the best place to start and grow a business so that every Georgian who wants a good job can get a good job right here at home.
Governor Nathan Deal added that the money will plant seeds of economic growth that will continue to bear fruit for Georgians long after his time in office has come to an end.
Invest Georgia was instituted by the legislature in 2013 and funded in 2015.
The program is structured as a “fund of funds,” investing directly in investment firms, rather than individual companies.
The goal of the program is to generate more local capital for early-stage companies.
In 2015, Georgia venture capital deals represented just 1.4 percent of all deals in the U.S.
In 2015, 86 percent of investors in Georgia deals were located outside the state.

The program's initial $20 million has been allocated into six early and growth-stage investment firms including TTV Capital, Mosley Ventures, TechSquare Labs, Engage Ventures and two additional firms still finalizing their capital raises.

Katie Kirkpatrick, chief policy officer for the Metro Atlanta Chamber, stated that directing resources to Invest Georgia will build confidence in Georgia’s startup scene and show the strength of the venture capital system in Georgia.
Previous investments from the program have helped attract additional interest and resources from other funds located outside of the state.
In October 2017, Invest Georgia’s Executive Director Knox Massey said the money allocated into just three firms at the time had helped support 36 companies and developed about 800 jobs.
Along with Massey, Invest Georgia’s Board of Directors and a consultancy firm help choose the firms that receive funding.
Industries strong in Georgia including health IT, fintech, cybersecurity and SaaS are favored by the program.
Massey says they will largely invest the additional $20 million allocation into funds they have not previously worked with.
The legislature has capped Invest Georgia’s potential allocation at $100 million.
source_title: Invest Georgia Allocated Another $10M to Support Georgia's Venture Funds - Hypepotamus

facts: Hanon Systems, a global provider of automotive thermal and energy management solutions, will create 160 new jobs and invest more than $40 million in a new manufacturing facility in Bulloch County.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the investment while on an economic development trip to Israel on May 23, 2023.
Hanon Systems will construct its new facility at Gateway Regional Industrial Park in Bulloch County.
The new facility will be used to assemble automotive thermal solutions.
Operations at the Hanon Systems facility are expected to begin in May 2024.
Governor Brian P. Kemp stated that Georgia’s automotive and e-mobility industries continue to grow as the state welcomes Hanon Systems.
Governor Kemp noted that Georgia is furthering its position as the EV capital of America, with new jobs and opportunities coming to hardworking Georgians statewide.
Min Sung, President and Chief Executive Officer of Hanon Systems, said the company is pleased to contribute to Bulloch County’s local economy through job creation.

Min Sung thanked the State of Georgia and its cooperating agencies for guiding the project, noting it aligns with Hanon Systems’ strategy to support the industry shift toward electrification while growing its North America footprint.
Billy Allen, Chair of the Development Authority of Bulloch County, said Statesboro-Bulloch County is honored by Hanon Systems’ decision to locate there.
Billy Allen highlighted that the new facility will work closely with the Hyundai Metaplant and the electric vehicle industry.
Griff Lynch, Executive Director of the Georgia Ports Authority (GPA), stated the announcement further solidifies Georgia’s position as a growing hub for auto manufacturing.
Griff Lynch noted that GPA supports the operations of Hyundai Motor Group and its suppliers to deliver jobs and opportunity for Georgia residents.
Alyce Thornhill, Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy at GDEcD’s Global Commerce team, represented the department on this competitive project.
The GDEcD project was completed in partnership with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that suppliers like Hanon Systems successfully support larger industry ecosystems through partnership-driven solutions ranging from workforce training to logistics systems efficiencies.

Pat Wilson noted that Bulloch County is part of the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority and has been part of Georgia’s growing success story in automotive manufacturing.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry, building on assets that make its automotive industry successful.
Since 2020, over 40 EV-related projects have announced more than $22.7 billion in investment and 28,400 jobs in Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground on October 25 in neighboring Bryan County for its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Hanon Systems was formed in 1986 and has grown into a leading supplier of automotive thermal and energy management solutions for electrified and conventional vehicles.
Hanon Systems’ offerings include heating ventilation and air conditioning, powertrain cooling, compressors, fluid transport, and electronics and fluid pressure solutions.
source_title: Automotive Supplier Hanon Systems to Create 160 New Jobs in Bulloch County  | Nanjing Conveyor Co.,Ltd

facts: Hanon Systems will invest more than $40 million for a manufacturing facility in Bulloch County.
The investment is expected to create 160 jobs.
Hanon Systems specializes in automotive climate control systems.
Hanon Systems is a supplier for Hyundai.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp’s office announced the investment on Tuesday.
The new facility is located in the Savannah area of Georgia.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that Georgia’s automotive and e-mobility industries continue to grow as they welcome Hanon Systems to the state.
Gov. Brian Kemp noted that as Georgia furthers its position as the EV capital of America, new jobs and opportunities are coming to hardworking Georgians.
source_title: Hanon Systems investing more than $40 million in manufacturing facility in Bulloch County | AllSides

facts: Hanon Systems is a major South Korean provider of automotive thermal management systems.
Hanon Systems released a report estimating the amount of trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) generated in Europe by emissions of refrigerants HFC-134a and HFO-1234yf from car cooling and heating systems.
The report describes the opportunity to limit TFA creation via the use of CO2 (R744) and propane (R290) as alternative refrigerants.
Hanon calculated a cumulative TFA load of approximately 68,000 metric tons through 2025 in Europe.
Using three separate regulatory scenarios, the company projected a cumulative TFA load from 2028 through 2058 of 240,000 metric tons under a no-regulations scenario.
The company projected a cumulative TFA load of 143,000 metric tons under a regulations-with-exemptions scenario.
The company projected a cumulative TFA load of 102,000 metric tons under a regulations-without-exemptions scenario.
The report is titled “TFA contaminations caused by fluorinated gases inside mobile air-conditioning systems and already available alternatives”.
The report was authored by Dr. Roman Heckt, Global Chief Engineer for Natural Refrigerant Systems, and Dr. Henning Freitag, Natural Refrigerant Systems Development Engineer.
Hanon’s office in Germany is responsible for natural refrigerant-based thermal management components.
Hanon operates factories in Portugal, Bulgaria, and the Czech Republic.
The report outlines a model devised by Hanon to calculate and evaluate TFA created in the atmosphere from refrigerant leakages in passenger car cooling and heating systems.
The model assumes an average refrigerant charge per vehicle of 500g.
The model focuses on passenger cars in Europe, comprising the 27 EU member states, the European Free Trade Association (EFTA) countries, and the United Kingdom.
The model covers internal combustion engine vehicles (ICE), hybrid electric vehicles (HEV), plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEV), and battery electric vehicles (BEV).
The report noted that annual TFA emissions are rising strongly and will continue for the next ten years at least unless a PFAS restriction leads to an early switch to natural refrigerants.
For systems with electric compressors, the model used an annual leakage rate of 3% of the nominal refrigerant charge.
For systems with mechanical compressors, the model uses a 5% annual leakage rate.
The model includes a further 2% leakage due to crash events, assuming the release of the full charge.
The main goal of the report is to quantify current and future emissions and to show the impact of different future regulatory scenarios.

The EU’s European Chemical Agency (ECHA) is currently considering a sweeping restriction of PFAS that would include certain f-gas refrigerants and TFA, with a possible enactment of regulations in 2028.
Thermal management systems using CO2 are already established in battery electric vehicles.
Designs using R290 are under active development and validation.
Compliance with stricter PFAS regulation is technically achievable and already available for the mass market, according to the report.
TFA is an ultrashort-chain example of PFAS formed in the atmosphere by the breakdown of certain f-gases.
HFC-134a undergoes a 7–20% decomposition over approximately 14 years.
HFO-1234yf undergoes a 100% decomposition over 12 days.
Over the past 30 years, HFC-134a and HFO-1234yf have been introduced as refrigerants in car air-conditioning systems.
HFC-134a is now being phased down as a high-GWP greenhouse gas.
TFA formed in the atmosphere is delivered to Earth in rainfall and is also created by the breakdown of pesticides, pharmaceuticals, and other sources.
TFA has proliferated in the global environment, accumulating as a “forever chemical” in surface water and entering drinking water, beverages, foods, and ultimately human blood serum.
TFA has a short half-life in the body of about 32 days.
The effect of TFA on human health is being investigated, with a 2025 study at the University of Milan pointing to an impact on the immune system.
Germany has recommended to the EU that TFA be classified as reproductively toxic.
The chemical industry asserts TFA is not harmful, citing reports from the UN Environment Programme (UNEP).
In its report, Hanon calculated that the use of HFC-134a, starting in the mid-1990s in air-conditioned vehicles, led to the creation of roughly 2,000 metric tons per year of TFA by 2010.
After 2014, a steady transition to HFO-1234yf took place as a result of the EU MAC Directive, which fully banned HFC-134A as of 2017.
By 2025, annual TFA emissions exceed 6,000 metric tons per year.
R1234yf accounts for more than 80% of the total emissions but only 44% of the vehicle fleet, according to the report.
About half of the cumulative TFA load through 2025 derives from historical R134a use, while the other half has accumulated during the brief but rapidly expanding era of R1234yf.
Under the baseline scenario with no PFAS-related EU restriction for passenger car refrigerants, emissions rise until 2036 and exceed 9,000 metric tons per year.
At that time, the full EU car fleet is transferred to R1234yf, except for a smaller number of cars built by the Volkswagen Group that continue to use R744.

After the peak in 2036, TFA emissions decrease slightly because of fewer leakages due to an increasing number of electric compressors replacing mechanical compressors and the corresponding lower leakage rate.
The overall car fleet volume is assumed to decrease after the peak.
Over the 30-year baseline evaluation period, cumulative TFA emissions reach 240,000 metric tons.
This would be 3.6 times more than the cumulative TFA load caused by the EU passenger car fleet over the previous 50 years.
The report considered a second scenario model, Restriction Option 1 (RO1), described in the ECHA PFAS restriction proposal documents.
RO1 assumes a full PFAS ban after an 18-month transitional period.
Only the service of existing vehicles would continue with R1234yf after the ban date under RO1.
Under RO1, annual TFA emissions do not rise above 9,000 metric tons per year because the restriction becomes active before the full vehicle fleet has transitioned to R1234yf.

Even under this strict restriction option, an accumulated TFA load of 102,000 metric tons would result during the evaluation period, which is 1.5 times more than the cumulative TFA load caused by the EU passenger car fleet over the previous 50 years.

The third scenario is Restriction Option 2 (RO2), for which the PFAS ban has derogation periods of five years for BEV passenger cars and a derogation period of 12 years applied to all other passenger cars.
Because of the derogations granted under RO2, annual TFA emissions continue to rise until 2036 even though the regulations enter into force in 2028.
Under RO2, the TFA peak is nearly as high as in the baseline scenario without regulation.
Cumulative TFA emissions over the relevant 30-year period reach 143,000 metric tons under RO2, which is 2.1 times higher than during the previous 50 years.

A key finding in the report is the need to convert as many vehicles as possible to alternative refrigerants before the entire fleet is converted to R1234yf by 2036 to avoid the enormous TFA emissions peak of more than 9,000 metric tons per year.

The report noted that its model is not a full life-cycle model and does not include aspects such as production of refrigerants, leakages during vehicle manufacturing, detailed servicing procedures, end-of-life refrigerant recovery, or a detailed representation of atmospheric processes.
The rapidly increasing electrification of the EU vehicle fleet will cause a growing share of vehicles to be equipped with heat pumps, which often require considerably higher refrigerant charges.
This means that the expected annual TFA peak may in reality be significantly higher than 9,000 metric tons per year, and the present model carries a risk of underestimating future TFA emissions.
Passenger car thermal management systems based on PFAS-free CO2 refrigerant are ready for widespread application across the mobile air-conditioning market, except for pure ICE vehicles.
This includes all hybrid vehicles using an electric refrigerant compressor.
In 2020, Volkswagen introduced a thermal management system using CO2 on its first all-electric vehicle platform, the Modular Electrification Toolkit (MEB).
The MEB system also integrated a heat pump, well-suited to battery electric vehicles that lack waste heat from a combustion engine.
In winter conditions, the heat pump significantly improves heating efficiency, helping to reduce vehicle range losses.
There are now more than one million VW MEB cars using CO2 refrigerant on the road in Europe.
About 10–15% of newly registered battery electric vehicles are equipped with CO2 thermal management systems.
Hanon is also developing and optimizing systems and components using R290 as a refrigerant.
Due to its flammability, R290 was not initially considered during the early development phase of natural refrigerant systems for MAC applications.
This solution is at an earlier stage of development compared to CO2.
Hanon has found R290 suitable for thermal management of battery electric vehicles, including battery conditioning.
A potential start of production with key R290 components is planned for 2029.
The extent to which R290 is suitable for use in vehicles with hybrid drive systems would still need to be investigated.

According to Benedikt Schauder, Thermal Management Development Lead at German automotive supplier ZF, automakers are showing high interest in R290-based thermal management systems for electric vehicles.
source_title: Hanon Systems Estimates TFA Levels

facts: Hella GmbH & Co. KGaA is an internationally operating German automotive parts supplier headquartered in Lippstadt, North Rhine-Westphalia.
The company develops and manufactures lighting, electronic components, and systems for the automotive industry.
Hella operates one of the largest trade organizations for automotive parts, accessories, diagnostics, and services within Europe.
It is one of the top 50 global automotive suppliers and one of the 100 largest industrial companies in Germany.
The company has approximately 37,000 employees at over 125 locations worldwide.
Hella generated consolidated sales of €8.1 billion in fiscal year 2024.
In 2022, the company merged with French global automotive supplier Faurecia, forming the merged business named Forvia.
The two divisions of the merger are formally called Forvia Hella and Forvia Faurecia.
Products of Hella GmbH & Co. KGaA continue to be sold under its own brand following the merger.
Sally Windmüller founded the company in 1899 under the name Westfälische Metall-Industrie Aktien-Gesellschaft (WMI) to produce ball horns, candles, and kerosene lamps for carriages.
Hella's name first appeared in 1908 as a trademark for acetylene headlights.
In 1923, the manufacturing family Lüdenscheider Hueck took over the majority of the shares.
The name "Hella" was included in the company name in 1986.
The Hella brand is attributed to founder Sally Windmüller honoring his wife Helen and using the German word heller (brighter).
After World War II, expansion led to the first subsidiary company being founded in Wembach in 1951.
Hella has six production manufacturers in Germany located in Lippstadt, Bremen, Recklinghausen, Hamm (Bockum-Hövel), Nellingen, and Wembach.
Early internationalization in the 1960s resulted in one of the largest distribution networks in the industry.
The first foreign production plant was founded in Mentone, Australia in 1961.
Hella operates more than 100 locations in over 35 countries across Europe, NAFTA/South America, Africa, and Asia/Pacific.
Since the early 1990s, Hella has been part of several Joint Ventures with other automotive part suppliers to develop new competencies.
The company cooperates with partners including Mahle Behr, Plastic Omnium, Samlip, Leoni, Mando, TMD Friction, and innocent.
Joint Ventures include HBPO and BHTC located in Lippstadt.
A holding named "Hella Stanley Ltd Pty" with Stanley Electric was founded in 2002 and is located in Melbourne.
In 2008, Hella started a joint venture with Gutmann Messtechnik to focus on diagnostic specialists.
Also in 2008, the company announced it would pay more attention to items outside the traditional automotive domain.
In 2008, Hella sold its Danish subsidiary Holger Christiansen A/S to the automotive supplier Bosch.
Due to strong economic fluctuations, Hella recorded a loss in fiscal year 2005/06.
Management implemented a program to lower costs and increase efficiency, recording an operational gain in fiscal year 2006/07.
The operational gain increased again in fiscal year 2007/08.
Hella recorded its second highest consolidated sale of €4.4 billion in the following fiscal years.
Constant growth continued, reaching €5.3 billion in 2013/14.
Since 2017, Hella has spun off Brighter AI Technologies and Yptokey from its incubator.
In 2021, French automotive supplier Faurecia agreed to acquire the Hueck family's 60% majority stake in Hella for €3.4 billion.
Faurecia announced an offer for remaining shares at €60 per share, valuing the company at a total of €6.7 billion.
The takeover agreement for the 60% stake was concluded on 15 August 2021.
Faurecia purchased half of the remaining shares in August 2022, bringing its stake to 80%.
Hella's main businesses are divided into three segments: Automotive, Aftermarket, and Special Applications.
The Automotive Segment develops, produces, and markets lighting and electronic components and systems for vehicle manufacturers and other suppliers.
The Aftermarket Segment develops, produces, and markets products for the independent aftermarket and garages.
The Special Applications Segment serves construction machinery producers, boat builders, municipalities, and energy suppliers with innovative lighting and electronic products.
The lighting division develops and manufactures headlamps, rear lamps, and interior lighting.
Recent innovations include headlights that adapt to prevailing driving and weather situations.
Headlamps with LEDs as light sources for low beam and high beam are already produced in series.
An example of LED headlamp application is the beams in the Cadillac Escalade Platinum.
As daytime running lights, these are included as standard in many middle- and upper-class vehicles.
The electronic product portfolio includes data-enabled electronic control units, roof modules, and vehicle access and driving authorization modules.
Modules for power management optimize the energy balance of the electrical system and improve battery charge balance.
Innovation and growth areas include electronic driver assistance systems using infrared (lidar), 24-GHz radar, camera, and ultrasonic sensors.
Other important business segments include position sensors, actuators, vacuum pumps, and wash water systems.
Hella sells automotive parts and accessories with its own sales companies and partners in more than 100 countries.
The company supplies the automotive aftermarket and garages with parts covering lighting, electrical, electronics, and thermal management.
In addition to civilian car accessories, Hella manufactures special signaling for official emergency vehicles including flash beacons, Visual Warning Systems, and sound combinations.
These emergency vehicle products include compact roof structures combining blue lights, sirens, and additional issues for police, fire departments, and technical aid agencies.
The Industries segment transfers automotive technologies to new target groups, including a pilot project providing new street lights to cities in China.
Another project involves LED-airport beacons found at airports in Luxembourg, Paderborn/Lippstadt, and Stavanger.
In 2012, Hella started a cooperation with Chinese automobile manufacturer BAIC to develop and produce light systems designed for the Chinese market.
In 2017, Hella partnered with Air-Quality Data Company BreezoMeter.
As of 31 December 2023, shareholder structure includes Forvia SE (80.59%), Elliott Investment Management LP (Paul Singer) (9.91%), Artisan Partners LP (2.76%), and Public (6.74%).
The company's revenue was €7,954,140,000 in 2023, with total assets of €7,061,640,000 in 2023.
Forvia is listed as the owner with an 81.6% stake.
source_title: Hella (company) - Wikipedia

facts: Hitachi Astemo, Ltd. (Astemo) and GlobalLogic Inc. (GlobalLogic) have been strengthening their collaboration to lead the trends of software-defined vehicles.
Hitachi Astemo President & CEO Kohei Takeuchi and GlobalLogic CEO Nitesh Banga discussed the future of mobility through digital transformation.

Astemo acts as a global mega-supplier providing tangible value in the In-Car domain, including internal vehicle technology such as software that enables advanced vehicle functions like autonomous driving.
Astemo focuses on the Out-Car domain, utilizing cloud and communications technology to expand functionality and improve performance outside of vehicles.
Astemo established Cypremos, Ltd. (Cypremos) on November 1 to further strengthen support for the Out-Car field.
The Astemo Group operates as a Tier 0.5 supplier developing technologies and providing value for future mobility.
Vehicle OEMs are rapidly transforming into tech companies and developing SDV architecture to provide over-the-air updates and generate additional revenue streams throughout the vehicle lifecycle.
GlobalLogic enables this industry transformation by creating in-vehicle and around-the-vehicle platform technologies that generate revenue streams for OEMs and the broader mobility ecosystem.
GlobalLogic has formed partnerships with major automobile manufacturers, parts suppliers, hyperscalers, semiconductor companies, fleets, and automotive retail.
GlobalLogic is focusing on the development of electric vehicle propulsion systems.
GlobalLogic's work contributes to next-generation sustainable transportation and enhanced vehicle safety, comfort, and connectivity.

Kohei Takeuchi stated that Astemo's vision involves providing more than just safety and peace of mind by removing barriers like time and space to empower people worldwide with greater freedom and quality of life.
Takeuchi identified software and digital transformation as the keys to achieving Astemo's vision.
The new company Cypremos will provide software and cloud services aimed at expanding support for SDVs and realizing new mobility services.
Cypremos will improve car intelligence and mobility evolution linked to the cloud.
Astemo continues to accelerate future mobility technology development in partnership with companies like GlobalLogic.

Nitesh Banga stated GlobalLogic's purpose is to create a positive impact for society and the planet by engineering digital products, platforms, and services designed for desirability, curated for intelligence, and built for excellence.
GlobalLogic drives business transformation through intelligent, sustainable solutions in collaboration with clients across healthcare, energy, finance, mobility, and other sectors.

Banga noted that GlobalLogic shares Astemo's vision and will build a future where technology elevates daily life and promotes meaningful, sustainable change for the mobility industry and communities worldwide.
Astemo will accelerate efforts together with GlobalLogic and various partners to develop the mobility of the future for sustainable growth.
GlobalLogic is a leader in digital engineering that helps brands design and build innovative products, platforms, and digital experiences for the modern world.
GlobalLogic integrates experience design, complex engineering, and data expertise to help clients transition into digital businesses.
GlobalLogic is headquartered in Silicon Valley and operates design studios and engineering centers globally.
GlobalLogic serves customers in the automotive, communications, financial services, healthcare and life sciences, manufacturing, media and entertainment, semiconductor, and technology industries.
GlobalLogic is a Hitachi Group Company operating under Hitachi, Ltd. (TSE: 6501).
Hitachi, Ltd. contributes to a sustainable society with a higher quality of life by driving innovation through data and technology as the Social Innovation Business.
source_title: Hitachi Astemo CEO Takeuchi and GlobalLogic CEO Nitesh discuss the future of mobility

facts: The Dublin-Laurens County Development Authority maintains a structured network of economic development partners to support regional industrial and supply-chain projects.
City of Dublin leadership includes Mayor Joshua Kight and City Manager Josh Powell.
Laurens County Board of Commissioners is led by Chairman Jeff Davis and County Administrator Bryan Rogers.

State of Georgia officials involved in regional economic development include Governor Brian P. Kemp, Lieutenant Governor Burt Jones, Secretary of State Brad Raffensperger, GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson, Senator Larry Walker III (District 20), and Representative Matt Hatchett (District 150).

Regional state agencies supporting industrial development include the Department of Community Affairs, OneGeorgia Authority, Georgia Tech Enterprise Innovation Institute, and Heart of Georgia Altamaha Regional Commission.

Local economic development is coordinated by the Dublin-Laurens County Chamber of Commerce under President & CEO Heath Taylor, alongside the Downtown Dublin Development Authority with directors Glenda Berry and Miriam Lewis.
Workforce development partners for manufacturing and industrial training include Oconee Fall Line Technical College, QuickStart, and the Georgia Department of Labor.
Transportation infrastructure partners supporting logistics and supply chain include Genesee & Wyoming, Inc. and the Georgia Ports Authority.
Utility partners available for industrial site development include Georgia Power Company, Georgia EMC, Oconee EMC, Little Ocmulgee EMC, Tri-County EMC, and Electric Cities of Georgia.

facts: Valdosta State University has partnered with Hwashin Georgia to promote entrepreneurship and small business development by supporting the creation of ventures conceived, managed, and owned by students.
Yohan Yeon serves as supply chain manager for Hwashin Georgia.
Sungkee Kim is the chief executive officer of Hwashin Georgia.
Hwashin Georgia donated $10,000 to support the J. Donald Lee Center for Entrepreneurship’s annual Business Plan Competition.
The Business Plan Competition is open to all VSU students, regardless of major or business experience.
Participating students develop their understanding of a successful business plan and receive expert feedback from industry leaders.
Students have the opportunity to win seed money to help turn their ideas into reality.

Dr. Byeonghwa Park, assistant professor of management for VSU’s Harley Langdale Jr. College of Business Administration and director of the J. Donald Lee Center for Entrepreneurship, stated that the competition helps students gain real-world experience, enhances entrepreneurial skills, and provides a chance to win financial support for their ventures.
The competition promotes student engagement with industry leaders and potential future employers like Hwashin.
The J. Donald Lee Center for Entrepreneurship will begin accepting applications for this year’s Business Plan Competition in November.
Students have nearly three months to register for the event and submit their initial business ideas.
The final round of the competition takes place in April, when finalists present their full business plans to a panel of judges.
Hwashin is a specialized manufacturer of chassis and body parts for automobiles.
Hwashin was established in Korea in 1975.
Following its successful expansion into Greenville, Alabama, in 2003, the company is now making a significant new investment in Dublin, Georgia.
Hwashin aims to begin mass production by December 2025 as part of its Dublin, Georgia expansion.
Sungkee Kim stated that Hwashin looks forward to a strong partnership with Valdosta State University and anticipates positive outcomes from the Business Plan Competition supported by Hwashin.
Sungkee Kim stated that the company aspires to be a valuable contributor to the economic growth of the Georgia region.
VSU’s Harley Langdale Jr. College of Business Administration opened the J. Donald Lee Center for Entrepreneurship in early 2023.
The center is located in Pound Hall on the Rea and Lillian Steel North Campus at Valdosta State University.
The center brings emerging student entrepreneurs, faculty, staff, and industry leaders together to ignite innovation and foster connections across the university, region, and world.
The center offers a wide range of resources, technology, equipment, and mentorship needed to unlock the potential of the next startup generation.

links.context: J. Donald Lee Center for Entrepreneurship website

source_title: VSU, Hwashin Collaboration Helps Innovative Ideas Become Sustainable Businesses - Valdosta State University

facts: South Korean auto parts maker Hwashin Co. will invest USD 176 million in a new Georgia plant to supply Hyundai's electric vehicle facility.
The investment will create 460 jobs.
Hwashin is investing more than USD 176 million and hiring more than 460 employees for the new Georgia facility.
The company plans to build its plant in the town of Dublin, located in the middle of Georgia.
Production at the new Georgia plant is planned to start in 2025.
The facility will produce metal pieces for auto chassis.
Chassis parts will be supplied to the new Hyundai Motor Group plant being built in Ellabell.
Parts will also be supplied to sister company Kia's facility in West Point.
Hwashin is the latest supplier to announce a plant for Hyundai's USD 7.6 billion EV and battery assembly plant in Ellabell, near Savannah.
Hyundai's Ellabell plant was announced last year.
The Hyundai plant could grow to 8,500 employees.
The Hyundai plant could begin producing vehicles as early as 2024.
Suppliers to the Hyundai Ellabell plant have collectively pledged to invest more than USD 2.4 billion.
These suppliers have pledged to hire nearly 6,300 people.
Hwashin is headquartered in Yeongcheon, South Korea.
Hwashin previously established a plant in Greenville, Alabama, to supply Hyundai in 2003.
The Alabama plant has expanded multiple times and currently employs 750 people.
Hwashin supplies parts to other manufacturers, including Volkswagen AG in Chattanooga, Tennessee.
The state of Georgia will pay for worker training at the new facility.
Hwashin could qualify for USD 9.2 million in state income tax credits.
The tax credits are valued at USD 4,000 per job over five years.
To qualify for the tax credits, workers must earn at least USD 31,300 a year.
Local officials could grant additional property tax breaks to the company.
The company is acquiring 60 acres (24 hectares) for the new plant.
The land is part of a larger site that local officials have already cleared and graded.
source_title: Hyundai Supplier Hwashin to Build USD 176M Georgia Plant: Rediff Moneynews

facts: Georgia Gov. Brian P. Kemp announced that Hwashin, an automotive parts company, will establish a new manufacturing facility in Dublin.
The project will create over 460 new jobs.
The project involves more than $176 million in investment in Laurens County.
Governor Brian Kemp stated the goal to become the e-mobility capital of the nation is bringing opportunity to communities across the state.
Hwashin is the latest company to join Georgia's growing ecosystem of companies bringing good jobs in this industry.
Hwashin stands as a Tier-1 supplier specializing in crafting chassis and body parts for Hyundai Motor Group, Kia, and international original equipment manufacturers (OEMs).
Hwashin operates from its headquarters in Korea.
Hwashin has established production facilities spanning the United States, Asia, and South America.
Seo Jin Jung is the CEO of Hwashin.
Hwashin aspires to shape a future global city focused on electric vehicles (EVs), drawing from its 50-year expertise and collaboration with Georgia and Laurens County.
The alliance seeks to position the region at the forefront of the evolving automobile industry.
Hwashin will take on the role of a reliable partner to customers and an engaged contributor to the local community.
Hwashin believes in its capacity to contribute to the prosperity of communities and the American auto parts industry.
Hwashin extends gratitude to the State of Georgia and Laurens County for their ongoing cooperation and support throughout this project.
Hwashin's new Georgia facility is scheduled to begin production in late 2025.
The facility will be located at the Georgia Highway 257 Rail Served Site.
This site is the first Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Select site in the state.
A “GRAD Certified Select” status indicates a site has met or exceeded more rigorous certification requirements.
The facility will be primarily focused on manufacturing chassis components for OEMs, including Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America and Kia Georgia.
Dublin Mayor Joshua Kight welcomed Hwashin to the community.
The staff and board of the Dublin-Laurens County Development Authority showed Hwashin why Dublin-Laurens County is a great place to do business.
The company's manufacturing facility in Dublin will become an economic engine for the region, leading to more employment opportunities and quality growth.
Jeff Davis is Chairman of the Laurens County Board of Commissioners.
Hwashin is the seventh international company to call Dublin and Laurens County home.
The project demonstrates the partnership and 'can-do attitude' between Laurens County, the City of Dublin, and the Dublin-Laurens County Development Authority.
The project will change the landscape for Dublin-Laurens County, providing new career opportunities for area residents.
The project will develop increased and far-reaching opportunities in areas such as housing and retail development.
Griff Lynch is President and CEO of the Georgia Ports Authority.
Significant investments from Hwashin and other automotive suppliers are delivering career opportunities that will allow Georgians to thrive right here at home.
The Georgia Ports Authority supports economic development throughout the state and welcomes Hwashin as the newest member of its manufacturing community.
The authority's mission is to efficiently connect companies like Hwashin to international markets.
Alyce Thornhill is Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy at the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team.
GDEcD worked on this competitive project in partnership with the Dublin-Laurens County Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia EMC, and Georgia Quick Start.
Pat Wilson is a GDEcD Commissioner.
From their chosen site, Hwashin will have direct interstate access to at least two long-term automotive partners.
Hwashin will be able to pursue an accelerated construction timeline by locating on a GRAD Select site.
Decades of elected leadership have supported infrastructure and workforce development in Georgia.

For over a century, Georgia has fostered healthy industry practices, encouraged collaboration and innovation, and positioned the state as a leader in developing emerging technologies for the automotive and mobility industry.
In fiscal year 2023, job creation in the automotive industry increased by 324 percent when compared to FY21.
Suppliers attracted by Georgia’s mobility OEMs resulted in over $2 billion in investment across the state.
Since 2020, Georgia has pursued the entire EV supply chain, creating more than $25.1 billion in investments and 29,000 jobs.
source_title: GA: Auto Parts Supplier Hwashin to Bring 460 Jobs to Middle Georgia With New Facility in Dublin | Trade and Industry Development

facts: Dublin-Laurens County is known throughout Georgia as one of the most pro-business communities in the state.
The community has experienced a resurgence of manufacturing and logistics jobs.
The region has one of the nation’s lowest costs of living, which translates into lower costs for companies.
Laurens County has the third lowest property tax rate out of 159 counties in Georgia.
The community provides a 100% exemption on Freeport inventory that includes a company’s inventory and work in process materials.
Millions of dollars are being invested in the region’s utility infrastructure to meet future demands while maintaining very competitive rates.
The City of Dublin has over 1.5 million gallons per day of excess permitted water and wastewater capacity with significant pressure.
The City has one of the most reliable natural gas networks in the country along with significant excess gas capacity.
Customers in Georgia with over a 900 kilowatts per hour connected load can require electric companies to bid for their service.
Georgia Power, five Electric Membership Corporations, and the City of Dublin provide electricity and natural gas throughout the region.
These utility providers are in the midst of developments designed to significantly increase power generation capacity over the next decade.
Companies in Middle Georgia typically enjoy electric rates priced at 15%-20% below the U.S. national average.
Laurens County is designated as a Tier One county by the Georgia Department of Community Affairs.
Tier One communities receive full state statutory incentives, including a per job state income tax credit equivalent to $4,000 per job over five years.
Companies creating jobs in Laurens County can apply the $4,000 per job credit against 100% of their State of Georgia corporate income tax liability.
Any remaining credits, up to $3,500 per job, can be applied to the company’s withholding tax liability and sent as a cash reimbursement to the company.
Dublin-Laurens County is located halfway between Atlanta and Savannah with direct access to major highways, interstates, two international airports, and the fastest-growing port in North America.
The strategic location allows businesses to transport goods and materials to 80% of the consumer market in the United States in less than a 2-hour plane ride or a 48-hour truck drive.
Dublin is governed by a mayor and a 7-person city council, while Laurens County is governed by a 5-person county commission board.
Matt Hatchett, a small business owner in the community, represents Dublin-Laurens County as a state representative in the Georgia House of Representatives.
State Senator Larry Walker, III represents the interests of Dublin-Laurens County in the other chamber of the Georgia General Assembly.
The area offers a low cost/high quality of life, small-town lifestyle, good schools, major healthcare providers, and availability of large industrial job opportunities.
source_title: Competitive Advantages | Dublin-Laurens County Development Authority

canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Joint Venture
facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution (LGES) have formed a $4.3 billion joint venture to build an EV battery cell manufacturing plant in Bryan County, Georgia.
The Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) of 2022 offers a $7,500 tax credit for eligible vehicles.
To qualify for the IRA tax credit, 60% of a vehicle's battery components must be assembled in North America, and critical minerals must come from the U.S. or its trade partners.
The Georgia plant is set to begin production by late 2025.
The facility will have an annual capacity of 30 gigawatt-hours (GWh), enough to power 300,000 EVs.
The facility will supply battery packs to Hyundai's U.S. plants.
The IRA tax credit is projected to generate $41.7 billion in investments and 72,300 jobs in facilities already producing eligible vehicles.
An additional $20.2 billion in future investments is tied to upcoming projects related to the IRA.
Hyundai's Georgia plant is part of a $61.9 billion surge in IRA-driven investments.
Tax-credit-eligible EVs are expected to command 60% of U.S. sales by 2030.
The U.S.-China trade war has exposed vulnerabilities in global supply chains, particularly in the EV battery sector.
In January 2025, the Pentagon added CATL to its “Chinese military companies” list.
The IRA's sourcing requirements are forcing automakers to diversify their supply chains.
The partnership aligns with the Trump administration's aggressive tariff policies.
Volkswagen, BMW, and Ford are also shifting battery production to North America.
Hyundai and LGES already collaborate on battery production in Indonesia and South Korea.
The Georgia facility is part of Hyundai's broader $21 billion U.S. investment plan from 2025 to 2028.
The plant will supply battery packs to the Hyundai Metaplant America, which is set to produce 1.2 million EVs annually.
The Hyundai Metaplant America will produce vehicle models including the IONIQ 5 and IONIQ 9.
Hyundai Mobis, Hyundai's parts subsidiary, will assemble battery packs on-site at the Georgia facility.
The project is expected to create 3,000 direct jobs by 2030.
The project is expected to create over 8,500 indirect jobs by 2030.
The project reinforces Georgia's emergence as the “e-mobility capital” of the U.S.
Hyundai's U.S. EV sales are projected to grow from 150,000 units in 2024 to 500,000 by 2030.
The IONIQ 5 and IONIQ 9 are expected to qualify for the full $7,500 tax credit.
LGES is expanding in the U.S. with a $5.5 billion Arizona plant.
The EV battery market is growing at 15% annually.
By 2026, IRA criteria will require 80% of critical minerals to be sourced domestically.
source_title: Strategic EV Battery Partnerships: How Hyundai and LG Energy Solution's $4.3 Billion JV Positions Them for IRA-Driven Growth

canonical_name: Georgia Electric Vehicle and Battery Manufacturing Sector
facts: The country’s clean energy economy surged thanks to President Biden’s Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) and growing demand for clean energy technologies, adding nearly $100 billion in new investment and more than 80,000 new jobs.
The IRA was the largest-ever investment in U.S. climate, clean energy, electrification, and energy efficiency.

Combined with the CHIPS Act, Bipartisan Infrastructure Act, and other federal actions, the IRA is supercharging the economy and jumpstarting a new clean industrial revolution through onshoring domestic manufacturing of EVs, batteries, solar panels, wind turbines, heat pumps, and other clean energy technologies.

Since the IRA was signed into law in August 2022, 142 projects have been announced totaling $98 billion in investment dollars and more than 80,000 jobs, including nearly $60 billion and 50,000 jobs in 2023 alone.
EV and battery manufacturing facilities make up nearly 90% of the total $98 billion invested, with 19 out of the top 20 largest investments going to batteries and EVs.
Georgia led states in attracting investment, securing $15.4 billion.
South Carolina secured more than 10,000 new clean energy and electrification manufacturing jobs.
North Carolina attracted $13.7 billion in invested capital.
Arizona, Nevada, Indiana, Illinois, Louisiana, New Mexico, and Michigan each attracted $1 billion in investment during 2023.

Georgia now boasts four multi-billion dollar investments: Hyundai/SK ($5 billion), Freyr Battery ($2.6 billion), Hanwha Qcells ($2.5 billion), and Hyundai/LG Energy Solution ($2 billion more added this year).
South Carolina welcomed two multi-billion investments in 2023: Scout Motors in Blythewood ($2 billion) and battery manufacturer Albemarle in Chester County ($1.3 billion).

Toyota announced three additional investments for its North Carolina battery plant after the IRA's passage, including an additional $8 billion in October 2023 that brings the total investment to nearly $14 billion and creates over 5,000 jobs for U.S. autoworkers.
Among all states, Georgia, South Carolina, and North Carolina rank highest in total new investment dollars at $40.7 billion combined.
Georgia leads the way with over 13,000 new manufacturing jobs.
South Carolina ranks third with over 10,000 jobs.
North Carolina ranks sixth with over 4,000 jobs.
Toyota, Hyundai, Honda, Tesla, Stellantis, General Motors, Ford, and Scout Motors have all made billion-plus dollar investments into American-made batteries and/or EV assembly since the IRA passed.

LG Energy Solution nearly quadrupled its initial $1.4 billion investment into an Arizona battery plant and committed $5.5 billion in March 2023, which was at the time the largest investment for a single American battery manufacturing facility before being passed by Toyota’s North Carolina battery plant.

LGES stated that its decision to increase investment in cylindrical EV battery production in North America comes from rising demand from EV makers for locally manufactured high-quality, high-performance batteries to satisfy the Inflation Reduction Act’s EV tax credits.
Tesla announced in January 2023 that it was investing $3.6 billion into its growing Gigafactory Nevada, adding 3,000 new jobs.
Redwood Materials committed $1.1 billion and 701 jobs to an expanded Storey County facility one month prior to Tesla's announcement.

J.B. Straubel, co-founder and CEO at Redwood Materials, stated that the Inflation Reduction Act has really accelerated even what was already a fast process of companies locating or expanding battery production in the U.S.

Stellantis resurrected a shuttered assembly plant in Belvidere, Illinois at the beginning of 2023, committing over $3 billion to a new battery plant and rehiring many previously laid off workers with help from the IRA, state and local leaders, and the United Auto Workers.
Michigan added the second-most jobs since the IRA passed (10,685) and ranks in the top-eight in total investment.
Ohio added the fourth-most jobs (5,785) and also ranks in the top-eight in total investment.
Illinois landed the Stellantis investment and $2 billion from battery manufacturer Gotion in Manteno, which will create 2,600 jobs.
Between the two facilities and a few smaller announced investments, Illinois will see a total of $5.25 billion in new investments and over 4,000 jobs created.
Indiana attracted two multi-billion investments: a $3 billion joint venture from GM and Samsung SDI in New Carlisle and a $3.2 billion joint venture from Stellantis and Samsung SDI.
Canadian Solar made an $800 million investment in October to Jeffersonville, creating 1,200 jobs.
Indiana statewide investments from 2023 totaled up to $7.2 billion and 4,500 jobs.
Collectively, Michigan, Ohio, Illinois, and Indiana compose 30% of all investment dollars and 25,000 of the jobs created in investments tracked since the IRA passed.
Governor Brian Kemp (R-GA) stated he intends for Georgia to be recognized as the electric mobility capital of America.

Governor Kemp and Senator Jon Ossoff (D-GA) have successfully recruited businesses to Georgia through bipartisan collaboration, including multiple international trips that helped land major Hyundai investments.

These recruitment efforts are bolstered by a workforce program called Georgia Quick Start that helps support workforce training and recruitment efforts for new EV, battery, and solar manufacturing plants.

Governor Gretchen Whitmer (D-MI) created the Office of Future Mobility and Electrification to make Michigan more attractive to investments and support the future workforce for transportation electrification.
Governor McMaster (R-SC) signed an executive order supporting South Carolina’s aim to recruit EV business and prepare its workforce for these jobs.

The U.S. just eclipsed one million EVs sold in 2023, a first in the country’s history, with demand continuing to grow for batteries, solar panels, wind turbines, heat pumps, and other clean technologies.

States that adopt favorable policies, build strong workforce programs, and capitalize on new federal grant programs can tap billions in IRA-spurred investment to attract economic development opportunities, high-paying jobs, and secure the domestic clean energy economy.
source_title: In 2023, The United States Started Building Big Again, Thanks To Biden’s Inflation Reduction Act

facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution are investing another $2 billion in the planned “Metaplant” west of Savannah.
The Metaplant is already the largest economic development project in Georgia history.
The two companies announced an expansion of the original $5.5 billion electric vehicles and battery cell manufacturing joint venture on Thursday.
The expansion brings the total investment to nearly $7.6 billion.
The expansion adds 400 jobs on top of the 8,100 originally planned for the next eight years.
Gov. Brian Kemp said the massive project will go a long way toward making Georgia the e-mobility capital of the U.S.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated: “These types of major investments ultimately go to hardworking Georgians in the form of paychecks, improved schools and infrastructure, and more.”

José Muñoz, president and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America, stated: “This incremental investment in Bryan County reflects our continued commitment to create a more sustainable future powered by American workers.”
When fully built out, the Metaplant will turn out 300,000 EVs per year.
Hyundai Mobis will assemble battery packs using cells from the plant.
Hyundai Mobis will supply the assembled battery packs to Hyundai Motor Group’s U.S. manufacturing facilities for the production of Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis EV models.
The state Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team worked on the project in partnership with the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority.
The Georgia Ports Authority worked on the project in partnership with the state Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team.
Georgia Power worked on the project in partnership with the state Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team.
The Technical College System of Georgia’s Quick Start program worked on the project in partnership with the state Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team.
source_title: Hyundai, LG Energy investing another $2B in Metaplant |

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Joint Venture
facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution announced they would build a $4.3 billion electric vehicle battery plant in the United States.
South Korea’s Hyundai Motor Group is the world’s third-largest automaker in terms of vehicle sales.
LG Energy Solution (LGES), headquartered in Seoul, South Korea, is ranked second in the world based on battery sales.
LGES and Hyundai Motor Group will each own 50% of the joint venture.
Hyundai Motor Group houses Hyundai Motor, Kia, and auto parts maker Hyundai Mobis Co Ltd.
LGES supplies car manufacturers such as Tesla Inc and General Motors Co.
LGES CEO Youngsoo Kwon stated: “Two strong leaders in the automotive and battery industries have partnered, and together we are poised to drive America’s electric vehicle transition.”
In October 2022, Hyundai opened its $5.4 billion electric vehicle and EV battery plant in Bryan County, 30 minutes west of Savannah, Georgia.
The company planned to begin commercial production at the new plant by the first half of 2025 with an annual capacity of 300,000 units.
Garrison Douglas, spokesman for Republican Gov. Brian Kemp, said the 3,000-job battery plant would be among 8,100 overall jobs.
The $4.3 billion investment will be part of the previously announced total of $5.4 billion.
The Hyundai/LG plant is expected to supply batteries for 300,000 electric vehicles per year.
This battery capacity matches the planned initial production of the nearby vehicle assembly plant.
Hyundai said the Georgia plant could later expand to build 500,000 vehicles a year.

Governor Brian Kemp stated: “This is exactly what we envisioned when Georgia landed the Hyundai Metaplant in May of last year, and this project is the latest step on Georgia’s path to becoming the nation’s electric vehicle capital.”
Under the Biden Inflation Reduction Act (IRA), electric vehicle buyers can receive a tax credit of up to $7,500 if the vehicle meets certain criteria.

To qualify for the IRA tax credit, the vehicle must be assembled in North America and a certain percentage of its battery parts and minerals must be sourced from North America or a US free trade partner.
Currently, no Hyundai or Kia vehicle is eligible for the IRA tax credit.
Democratic U.S. Senator Jon Ossoff of Georgia stated: “The IRA’s manufacturing incentives continue to bring jobs and investment to Georgia.”
Senator Ossoff's goal remains to make Georgia the world leader in peak energy generation.
Hyundai Motor finalized a $5 billion electric vehicle battery joint venture in Cartersville, Georgia, with SK On in April.
SK On is a battery unit of SK Innovation Co Ltd.
This marks Hyundai’s second battery factory.
Georgia state and local governments have pledged US$1.8 billion in tax relief and other incentives for the project.
Greg LeRoy, executive director of Good Jobs First, noted that this is the largest subsidy package a US state has ever promised an auto plant.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy to build $4.3 billion electric vehicle battery factory in the United States - ExBulletin

facts: IMS Gear develops and produces specific drive solutions for international customers with an industry focus on Automotive, industry, and e-mobility concepts.
The company employs around 3,100 people worldwide.
IMS Gear is organized into 29 entrepreneurial business units, each maintaining a closed value chain.
The company maintains locations in Gainesville, Georgia, and Virginia Beach, Virginia within the southeastern United States.

Employees at the U.S. locations benefit from proximity to major metropolitan areas, natural settings like the Appalachian Mountain chain and Chesapeake Bay, as well as good airport connections and shipping options.

IMS Gear provides a comprehensive benefits package that includes medical, dental, and vision insurance, vacation and paid time off, holidays, performance bonus participation, a 401(k) plan, tuition reimbursement, disability and life insurance, a wellness program, and an employee referral program.
The company offers competitive pay within its industry and geographical locations.
IMS Gear provides two-year apprenticeship programs for a defined career path as an alternative to a four-year college degree program.
Apprentices receive hands-on career training in an apprenticeship lab under the guidance of talented, industry professionals.
Apprentices earn a competitive, guaranteed hourly rate while working and participate in a competitive benefits package.
Apprentices receive job-specific classroom instruction with free tuition and books, resulting in zero student debt.
Upon successful completion of the apprenticeship, participants receive a Journey-Worker Certificate presented by the Department of Labor and Industry.

IMS Gear offers global opportunities for employees to work at production facilities in the USA, Germany, Mexico, and China to understand the global marketplace and immerse themselves in different cultures.
The company's wellness program provides access to e-learning programs and events related to wellness.
Employees can receive a discount on medical insurance premiums when they complete certain annual and preventative screenings.
IMS Gear offers committed students the opportunity each semester to work on exciting projects and gain practical experience in gear and transmission technology.
Direct entry newcomers and graduates participate in a trainee program where a mentor is responsible for looking after them from the start to help build a network among colleagues.
source_title: Working at IMS Gear | IMS Gear SE & Co. KGaA

facts: JAC Products Inc., based in Pontiac, MI, announced on Oct. 31 the sale of the company to Tulsa-based Argonaut Private Equity LLC.
The sale was effective Oct. 28, 2016.

JAC Products is a Tier 1 designer, manufacturer and global supplier of original equipment roof racks, cargo management systems, running boards and step rails to leading original equipment manufacturers.
The company has more than 1,500 employees.
JAC Products was previously owned by Wynnchurch Capital LLC.
Terms of the sale were not disclosed.
The management team at JAC is remaining with the company to drive implementation of its strategic growth plans.
JAC is diversifying its product mix and expanding globally into new markets.
CEO Mike Wood will continue his leadership role at JAC.

CEO Mike Wood stated: “JAC is in a major growth phase and this new ownership is committed to the implementation of our strategic plan. It is an exciting time for JAC and we look forward to strengthening our relationships with our customers by providing new, innovative products in the markets they serve. This is great news for our customers, our employees and all of our stakeholders.”
source_title: Automotive supplier JAC Products changes hands

facts: State-owned JAC (Anhui Jianghuai Automobile Group Corp) has returned to the Australian market with a dual-cab 4x4 ute and plans for battery electric and plug-in hybrid models.
The company has manufactured products since the 1960s but only began vehicle production in 2007.
JAC sold 592,500 vehicles globally last year, an increase of 18 percent over 2022.
Export sales accounted for more than 250,000 units in 2023, a rise of 60 percent year-on-year.
JAC exports to more than 132 sales regions and operates six overseas subsidiaries alongside 19 factories overseas.
Group chair Xiang Xingchu identified Australia as a strategic expansion test-case export market for the brand.
Xiang Xingchu stated that Chinese auto brands will have increased involvement in the Australian market and JAC aims to become a mainstream automotive brand there.
Xiang Xingchu observed that post-epidemic consumer preferences now prioritize affordable pricing, five-star safety ratings, intelligent features, richer configurations, and reliable quality.
Danny Lenartic, deputy managing director of Australian distributor LTS Auto, described the new vehicle as having kerbside appeal, excellent features, and approaching European fit and finish.
JAC must navigate the upcoming National Vehicle Emissions Standard, expected to take effect on January 1, 2025, which targets single-model sellers with CO2 emissions above 200 grams per kilometre.
Lenartic expressed confidence that JAC will not face issues with the new emissions legislation.
Jiang stated that JAC plans to introduce the T9 EV 4x4 and T9 PHEV 4x4 in the future.
Distributor LTS Auto has invested more than $40 million in setup costs, including assembling an Australian management team and establishing aftersales operations.
The T9 dual-cab ute is scheduled to arrive at 53 dealerships (and counting) during April.
The Northern Territory is currently the only region without JAC representation, with major dealers like Eagers handling sales elsewhere.
A dedicated JAC parts and accessories warehouse has been established in NSW’s Lane Cove to guarantee parts availability for business owners.
Lenartic stated the company's mission is to align its policies to be the easiest OEM to deal with in the industry.
Lenartic emphasized focusing on process fundamentals, marketing investment, team building, customer support, and product quality rather than dictating specific sales targets.
Lenartic noted that starting from scratch without a legacy allows the company to establish correct practices from the beginning.
source_title: JAC sets up shop in Australia | GoAuto

facts: Anhui Jianghuai Automobile Co., Ltd (JAC) is seeking a US partner to bring an electric car to the United States.
JAC partnered with GreenTech Automotive, a maker of neighborhood electric vehicles (NEVs) led by a group of politicians and political advisers.
GreenTech Automotive describes itself as a "U.S.-based automotive manufacturer dedicated to developing and producing environmentally friendly, energy-efficient vehicles."
GreenTech currently does not manufacture vehicles for US roads and highways; it only produces a limited-use, 2-seat NEV.
GreenTech was founded by former Democratic National Committee Chairman Terry McAuliffe.
Terry McAuliffe co-chaired Bill Clinton’s presidential run and chaired Hillary Clinton’s 2008 presidential run, and is running for Governor of Virginia.
JAC has been in business since 1964 and claims the capacity to build 700,000 vehicles annually.
The partnership aims to produce an affordable, 5-passenger electric sedan for sale in the US.
The base vehicle for the new sedan will be the JAC Rejoice, which JAC calls "An electric car platform."
The sedan's drivetrain will use GreenTech’s electric drive system featuring a 19kWh battery pack.
The vehicle will have an approximate range of 100 miles.

Charles Wang, Chairman and CEO of GreenTech Automotive, stated that JAC is recognized worldwide for manufacturing caliber and quality, and that GTA's innovative electric powertrain fits JAC's EV platform.
Marianne McInerney, executive vice president of sales and marketing at GTA, commented that the five-passenger sedan complements their two-passenger MyCar product line.

Marianne McInerney is a former Bush administration press officer for the Peace Corps, past President of the American International Automobile Dealers Association, and former VP of Sales at another EV company.
Cerberus Capital Management, the former US based owner of Chrysler, included former US Vice President Dan Quayle as chairman of its Global Investments Division.
US President Bill Clinton has been an advisor to GreenTech Automotive.
GreenTech lists green technology, affordability, and job creation as its core values.
The new vehicle is planned to be assembled at GreenTech’s Horn Lake, Mississippi facility.
The partnership was announced at the Shanghai International Automobile Industry Exhibition.
The car was slated for sale in late 2013.
The timeline is considered ambitious given US testing and regulatory requirements for vehicles sold in the marketplace.
The vehicle may be a Chinese-made car with an electric drive from GreenTech and a battery from a supplier, possibly Flux Power.
If realized, the collaboration would be the first production Chinese EV sold in North America.
source_title: China's JAC will launch its first electric car in the US

facts: SRC and Ajin USA have enjoyed a strong relationship for more than a decade.
All projects pursued by SRC with Ajin USA have been located in Rural Targeted Employment Areas (TEAs).
The latest project with Ajin USA is the Joon Georgia, Inc. factory in Bulloch County, Georgia.
The Joon Georgia, Inc. factory is located in a Rural TEA, qualifying it for the Rural TEA visa set-aside pool under the EB-5 Program.
Twenty percent of the entire annual EB-5 visa allotment (2,000 visas) may only be issued for projects located in Rural TEAs.
This Rural TEA visa feature was passed in the Reform and Integrity Act of 2022.
The Joon Georgia, Inc. factory is a critical supplier to Hyundai’s new electric vehicle plant in next door Bryan County, Georgia.

Hyundai located its USD 5.5 billion plant in America because the country’s executive and legislative branch has signaled the importance of electric vehicles in the Inflation and Reduction Act of 2022, which provides tax credits for purchases of US-built EVs.
Hyundai must have Joon Georgia’s output to produce its EVs.
Ajin USA has been one of Hyundai’s most important US suppliers for more than 40 years.
Ajin USA's product quality control is described as state-of-the-art.
SRC and Ajin’s loan model features proprietary elements that have stood the test of time regarding investment safeguards and terms.
USCIS sometimes gives preference to projects viewed as critical to US national interests, such as large-scale manufacturers of clean-energy technology.

facts: Kumho Tire has been selected by the Volkswagen Group as an original equipment (OE) supplier for the new 2018 Atlas SUV.
The 2018 Atlas is a seven-passenger family-ready SUV and the newest and largest member of the Volkswagen portfolio.
The Atlas will be available with the Kumho Crugen Premium KL33 in a 245/60R18 fitment.
The Atlas will also be equipped with standard Kumho Crugen HP91 tires in 245/60R18 and 255/50R20 fitments.
The North American market will offer Crugen Premium KL33 equipped Atlas SUVs.
The overseas market will offer the Crugen HP91 equipped vehicles.
Kumho’s Crugen Premium KL33 and Crugen HP91 tires will be supplied to Volkswagen’s factory in Chattanooga, Tennessee.
Volkswagen's Chattanooga factory will assemble the spacious and multi-trim level Atlas SUV.
The Atlas features advanced interior technology and a unique Automatic Post-Collision Braking System.
Harry Choi is the CEO of Kumho Tire USA.
Harry Choi stated that Kumho is elated to be associated with Volkswagen’s new entry into the SUV category by supplying the 2018 Atlas with their Crugen Premium tires.
The collaboration with Volkswagen demonstrates Kumho's strategic and steadfast focus on expanding its global OE business.

The Kumho Crugen Premium KL33 features an upgraded silica compound and noise-canceling tread that reduces road noise and vibration for exceptional all-weather performance and a smooth, comfortable, quiet ride.
